This journal is published at frequent intervals to bring out new contributions to mathematics.
It is a policy of the journal to publish papers within four months of acceptance.

# Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Robert Friedman\* and Francesco Scattone\*\*

Columbia University, Department of Mathematics, New York, NY 10027, USA

# Introduction

The starting point for the investigation of semistable degenerations of K3 surfaces is the theorem of Kulikov and Persson-Pinkham [16, 21]:

(0.1) Theorem.
Let $\pi' \colon X' \to \Delta$ be a semistable degeneration of K3 surfaces, with all components of the central fiber $X_0' = \pi'^{-1}(0)$ algebraic.
Then there is a semistable modification $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ of $\pi'$ such that the canonical divisor of the total space $X$ is trivial.

Here $\Delta \subset \mathbb{C}$ is a disk containing the origin and a degeneration $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ is called a modification of $\pi' \colon X' \to \Delta$ if there exists a birational map $\varphi \colon X' \to X$ which commutes with the projections and is an isomorphism over $\Delta - \{0\}$ .
Recall that $X$ is semistable if it is smooth and the central fiber is a reduced divisor with local normal crossings.

A semistable degeneration $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ with $K_X \equiv 0$ will be called a Kulikov degeneration.
The central fibers of such degenerations have been classified:

(0.2) Theorem.
(Persson [20], Kulikov [16]).
If $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ is a Kulikov degeneration of K3 surfaces, then the central fiber $X_0$ is one of the following three types:

(I) $X_0$ is a smooth K3 surface.
(II) $X_0$ is a chain of elliptic ruled surfaces, with rational surfaces on either end.
(III) $X_0$ is a union of rational surfaces; the double curves on each component form a cycle of rational curves and the dual graph of $X_0$ is a triangulation of $S^2$ .

A degeneration of Type I, II or III will always mean a Kulikov degeneration, with the types as listed above.
If a surface $X_0 = \bigcup V_i$ occurs as the singular fiber of such a degeneration, its components are subject to various combinatorial constraints, namely:

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

1. for each component $V_{i}$ of $X_{0}$, $D_{i} \in |-K_{\tilde{V}_{i}}|$, where $\tilde{V}_{i}$ is the normalization of $V_{i}$ and $D_{i}$ is the preimage on $\tilde{V}_{i}$ of the double curves in $V_{i}$;

2. the triple point formula: if $C \subset V_i \cap V_j$ is an irreducible double curve,

$$
(C)_{\tilde{V}_i}^2 + (C)_{\tilde{V}_j}^2 = \begin{cases} 0 &amp; \text{if } C \text{ is a nodal curve} \\ -2 &amp; \text{if } C \text{ is smooth.} \end{cases}
$$

There is in addition an analytic condition, $d$-semistability: this simply means an isomorphism of sheaves (see [7])

$$
T_{X_0}^1 \underset{\text{def}}{=} \operatorname{Ext}_{\tilde{V}_{X_0}}^1(\Omega_{X_0}^1, \mathcal{O}_{X_0}) \cong \mathcal{O}_{(X_0)_{\text{sing}}}.
$$

It turns out that the conditions above essentially characterize the central fibers of Kulikov degenerations, which motivates the following terminology.
A compact analytic surface $X_0 = \bigcup V_i$ with local normal crossings is called a combinatorial K3 surface if it satisfies one of the descriptions of type in (0.2), along with the Conditions 1) and 2) above.
If the type is specified, $X_0$ is referred to as a combinatorial Type II (or Type III) K3 surface.

The converse of Theorem (0.2) can now be formulated as follows: Given a combinatorial K3 surface $X_0$, there exists a $d$-semistable $X_0'$ with the same combinatorial data (e.g. self-intersections of the double curves), which may be taken to be a locally trivial deformation of the original $X_0$ over a smooth connected base ([7], 5.14). Moreover, if $X_0$ is $d$-semistable, it is smoothable within a one-variable (semistable) degeneration $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$, where $X$ is itself smooth, and the versal deformation space of $X_0$ can be explicitly described ([7], 5.10).

Because of the nature of threefold birational geometry, Kulikov models of degenerations are never unique; it is always possible to perform so-called elementary modifications of Type 0, I, II ([11], pp.
12–15).
They are the only birational operations that one knows, and grounds for suspecting that they are the only ones are given by

(0.3) Theorem.
([25], Corollary 3.1). Let $X \to \Delta$, $X' \to \Delta$ be two birationally equivalent Kulikov degenerations of K3 surfaces, and suppose that there exists a line bundle $L$ on $X$ such that $L|X_t$ is ample for $t \neq 0$ (i.e. $X \to \Delta$ is polarized).
Then the birational map $X \to X'$ is composed of elementary modifications of Types 0, I, and II.

To understand Kulikov models, then, we seek for invariants of the combinatorics which are preserved by elementary modifications, and then hope that these will give a complete set of combinatorial invariants.

Such invariants are easy to describe in the case of Type II degenerations, where the number of elliptic ruled components is a complete combinatorial invariant.
Precisely, this means that if $X_0$ and $Y_0$ are Type II $\tilde{d}$-semistable K3 surfaces with the same number of elliptic ruled components, then there is a locally trivial deformation of $X_0$ to some $X_0'$ (over a smooth connected base) and a sequence of abstract Type I modifications $X_0' \to Y_0$.
(We use the terminology abstract modification when we think of an elementary modification as being performed on the combinatorial surface $X_0$, as opposed to the total

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

space $X$ of a degeneration: For example, if $C \subset X_0$ is an exceptional curve intersecting one irreducible double curve transversally, the elementary modification of Type I along $C$ can be described as in the following picture:

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

![img-1.jpeg](img-1.jpeg)

![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)
Fig.
A

and the effect of a Type II modification can be similarly described just in terms of blowups and blowdowns on $X_0$ .)

To be more precise about the moduli of $X_0$ , write $X_0 = \bigcup_{i=0}^{k} V_i$ where $V_1, \ldots, V_{k-1}$ are the elliptic ruled components and $V_0, V_k$ the rational ones; then one can arrange after Type I modifications that $V_1, \ldots, V_{k-1}$ are minimal.
In this case $V_0 \cup V_k$ , glued along the double curve by the obvious isomorphism, is again $d$ -semistable ([8], 2.1). Moreover, the deformation theory of the original surface $X_0$ can be precisely related to that of $V_0 \cup V_k$ (compare [8]).
Roughly speaking, smoothings of $X_0$ arise by taking one-parameter smoothings of $V_0 \cup V_k$ which are tangent of order $k$ to the discriminant locus in the versal deformation (or are a base change of order $k$ ) and computing semistable models.

One final observation about Type II degenerations is that the arithmetic reflects the combinatorics: if $T$ is the monodromy action on $H^2$ of the general fiber then $N = \log T$ is an integral matrix and we can write $N = kN'$ , where $N'$ is a primitive integral matrix and $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ ; the point is that this $k$ is the same $k$ as above, equal to the number of elliptic ruled components plus one.

We now turn to Type III degenerations.
A Type III K3 surface is in minus-one-form if, for all irreducible double curves $C$ , $C^2 = -1$ on both components on which it lies, unless $C$ is a singular nodal curve, in which case $C^2 = 1$ on the smooth component which contains it and $(\tilde{C})^2 = -1$ on the normalization of the other component.
Miranda and Morrison have proved

(0.4) Theorem.
[18].
If $X_0$ is a Type III K3 surface, then there exists a sequence of abstract modifications of Types I and II, $X_0 \to X_0'$ such that $X_0'$ is in minus-one-form.

The argument of [18] provides, in fact, an effective algorithm.
On the other hand, minus-one-forms are not unique, and an attempt to understand their combinatorics leads rapidly to difficult and partially unsolved questions on polyhedral decompositions of $S^2$ .

The aim of this paper is to classify the combinatorics of Type III degenerations, up to elementary modifications, via the arithmetic of the monodromy.

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

Let $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ be a Type III degeneration, with monodromy $T$ acting on $H^2(X_t; \mathbf{Z})$ .
The arithmetic of $N = \log T$ is studied in §1.
In particular we show

(0.5) Theorem.

1. $N$ is an integral matrix.

2) Let $N = kN'$ , where $N'$ is a primitive integral matrix and $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ .
   Further, let $t = t(X_0)$ be the $t$ -invariant of $X_0$ (defined in (1.4)).
   Then $t$ is a positive even integer and $\{k, t\}$ is a complete set of arithmetic invariants for $N$ (modulo the orthogonal group of the K3 lattice).

We define $k$ to be the index of $X_0$ and call $X_0$ primitive if $k = 1$ .
We remark that both invariants $k$ and $t$ can be determined from the combinatorics of the surface $X_0$ : the $t$ -invariant just counts the number of triple points (7.1), and the index can be read from the dual graph of an appropriate modification of $X_0$ (as in (6.5)), indeed any such which puts $X_0$ in minus-one-form.

We can now state our main result:

(0.6) Theorem.

1. For any $t \in 2\mathbb{Z}^+$ there exist primitive Type III degenerations $X \to \Delta$ with $t(X_0) = t$ .

2) Suppose $X_0$ and $Y_0$ are primitive Type III $d$ -semistable K3 surfaces with $t(X_0) = t(Y_0)$ .
   Then there exists a locally trivial family $\pi \colon X \to S$ of $d$ -semistable varieties where $S$ is smooth and connected, points $0, s \in S$ with $\pi^{-1}(0) = X_0$ , $\pi^{-1}(s) = X_s$ , and a sequence of abstract Type I and II modifications

$$
X _ {s} \rightarrow Y _ {0}.
$$

3. Let $X_0$ be a Type III $d$ -semistable K3 surface.
   If $X_0$ has index $k$ , there exists a sequence of abstract Type I and II modifications

$$
X _ {0} \rightarrow X _ {0} ^ {\prime}
$$

such that $X_0'$ is in minus-one-form and has special $k$ -bands of hexagons (hence $X_0'$ is the "abstract base change" of order $k$ of a primitive Type III surface $\bar{X}_0'$ ).

Recall that a Type III K3 surface $\bar{X}_0$ in minus-one-form has special $n$ -bands of hexagons if its dual graph $\bar{\Gamma}$ can be obtained as the refinement of another semisimplicial triangulation $\Gamma$ of $S^2$ , by subdividing each 2-simplex $\sigma$ of $\Gamma$ into $n^2$ 2-simplices as depicted in Fig.
B:

![img-3.jpeg](img-3.jpeg)
Fig.
B

Note in particular that, when $k = 1$ , (0.6.3) simply means that $X_0$ can be put into minus-one-form.

The consequences for the deformation theory of $X_0'$ are as described above for the Type II case; see also [9] (4.1) and [11], p.
24.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

We proceed to describe the strategy of proof of (0.6) and the further contents of this paper.
In §2, we give explicit examples of Type III degenerations of certain standard types; these constructions will be used in §6 and §7.
The mixed Hodge structure of a Type III K3 surface is studied in §3, via Carlson's formalism of extensions of Hodge structures.
We also give a general mechanism for computing the differential of the period map for the variation of mixed Hodge structure on families of varieties with normal crossings.
In §4, we prove a statement on the surjectivity of the period map for Type III K3 surfaces; this is a relatively elementary argument, based on the theory of regular singular points.
We also study the relationship between the limiting mixed Hodge structure and the mixed Hodge structure on the central fiber in a Type III degeneration.
We consider polarized mixed Hodge structures in §5, and construct partial compactifications $\overline{\Gamma\setminus\mathfrak{D}}$ of polarized period space $\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ , along the lines of Mumford [1]; these partial compactifications are adapted to the arithmetic of $N$ .

Armed with these preliminaries, the proof of (0.6) is given in §6.
For example, here is the sketch of the proof of (0.6.2).
Via the surjectivity result of §4, deform $X_0$ until it acquires a polarization.
By [7], we may smooth $X_0$ , along with the polarization.
Given $Y_0$ of the same arithmetic type, we may deform $Y_0$ until it acquires the same limiting mixed Hodge structure as $X_0$ .
Via simple Hilbert scheme arguments, we show that there exists a smooth algebraic curve $C$ and algebraic threefolds $X$ and $Y$ , with morphisms,

![img-4.jpeg](img-4.jpeg)

together with a marked point $p \in C$ , satisfying:

1.  $\pi_1^{-1}(p) = X_0$ , $\pi_2^{-1}(p) = Y_0$ .
2.  $X$ and $Y$ are smooth near $X_0, Y_0$ respectively.
3.  The induced period maps $\eta_{i}\colon C\to \Gamma \backslash \mathfrak{D}$ coincide, where $\eta_{i}$ is the period map induced from $\pi_{i}$ .

By global Torelli for K3 surfaces, we must have $\pi_1^{-1}(t) \cong \pi_2^{-1}(t)$ for general $t$ .
From standard descent theory, it is not hard to show that, in fact, $X$ and $Y$ are birational if the limiting mixed Hodge structure of $X_0$ is chosen sufficiently general, and we conclude by (0.3).

Various applications of (0.6) are given in §7.
We show how to derive the minus-one-theorem for smoothable $d$ -semistable $X_0$ .
(However, the proof of (0.6) used the smoothability of $d$ -semistable Type III K3 surfaces, and hence implicitly the minus-one-theorem.
Compare [7], (5.9).) As a corollary of our results, we also show that any Type III degeneration has a birational modification (possibly singular) whose central fiber is an irreducible rational surface with a minimally elliptic cusp singularity, and various consequences are given.
This result was conjectured by Morrison.

Similar techniques could most likely prove a very similar result for abelian surfaces.
In this case, however, it is possible to start with a central fiber in

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

minus-one-form and make a direct combinatorial analysis (compare [18], p.
176).
In particular, the analogue of (0.6) may be proved by giving explicit sequences of Type II modifications.
We also mention the fact that similar methods to those in this paper (and in particular an application of the global Torelli theorem) will prove a version of the Kulikov-Persson-Pinkham theorem in the algebraic case.

## § 1. Arithmetic of Type III degenerations

Throughout this section we suppose that $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ is a Type III degeneration of K3 surfaces over the unit disk $\Delta \subset \mathbb{C}$.
Alternatively, we might think of having a given Type III $d$-semistable K3 surface $X_0$, in which case $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ will stand for a smoothing of $X_0$, with smooth total space $X$ (which always exists, by [7]).

Select a point $s \in \Delta - \{0\}$ and let $T \in \operatorname{Aut}(H^2(X_s; \mathbb{Z}))$ denote the Picard-Lefschetz transformation on the fiber $X_s = \pi^{-1}(s)$.
Under our assumptions, $T$ is unipotent of index 3 and we can consider its logarithm

$$
N = \log T = (T - 1) - \frac {1}{2} (T - 1) ^ {2}.
$$

We remark that, since the discriminant locus in the versal deformation space of $X_0$ is smooth ([7], 5.10), the arithmetic character of the monodromy $T$ does not depend on the choice of a smoothing of $X_0$, and is unchanged under small locally trivial deformations of $X_0$ which preserve $d$-semistability.

$N$ is a rational nilpotent endomorphism of $H^{2}(X_{s};\mathbb{Q})$ and the corresponding monodromy weight filtration has the form

$$
H ^ {2} \left(X _ {s}; \mathbb {Q}\right) = W _ {4} \supset W _ {2} \supset W _ {0}
$$

where $W_0 = \operatorname{Im} N^2$ has dimension one, $W_2 = \operatorname{Ker} N^2 = (W_0)^\perp$, and $NW_i \subseteq W_{i-2}$.
These formulas are well known if the degeneration is Kähler (cf.
[20], 2.7.1 and 2.7.2). In general, the above serves to define the spaces $W_i$.
The statement about $W_0$ follows from the topological invariance of the monodromy and the first paragraph of the proof of (7.2); the arguments there are logically independent of this section.
Note that $N$ is skew-symmetric as it is the logarithm of the isometry $T$.

In this section we study the arithmetic properties of $N$ and their relationship with the combinatorial configuration of the central fiber $X_0$.
We begin by proving that $N$ is in fact integral and by determining two invariants for its action on $H^2(X_s; \mathbb{Z})$.
The notations we introduce here will remain fixed for the rest of the section.

Recall that the K3 lattice $\Lambda = H^{2}(X_{s};\mathbb{Z})$, endowed with the bilinear symmetric form $(\cdot)$ given by cup product, is isometric to the orthogonal direct sum $H\oplus H\oplus H\oplus (-E_8)\oplus (-E_8)$ of three copies of the hyperbolic plane $\mathbb{Z}^2$ with form $\left( \begin{array}{cc}0 &amp; 1\\ 1 &amp; 0 \end{array} \right)$ and two copies of the lattice $\mathbb{Z}^8$ with form given by the negative of the Cartan matrix of the root system $E_8$.
In particular, $\Lambda$ is an even unimodular lattice.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Choose an integral generator $\gamma$ of $W_0$, $\gamma$ primitive in $\Lambda$; this can be accomplished since $W_0 \cap \Lambda$ is a primitive sublattice of $\Lambda$.
By unimodularity, there is an integral vector $\gamma' \in W_4$ such that $(\gamma' \cdot \gamma) = 1$.
Let $\delta = N\gamma' \in W_2$.
Then we can write the following formula for $N$:

(1.1) Lemma.
With the notations introduced above we have, for any $x \in H^2(X_s; \mathbb{Q})$,

$$
N x = (x \cdot \gamma) \delta - (x \cdot \delta) \gamma .
$$

Proof.
Consider first an element $y \in W_2$; then $Ny \in W_0$ is of the form $Ny = a\gamma$ where $a = (Ny \cdot \gamma') = -(y \cdot N\gamma') = -(y \cdot \delta)$.
Therefore

$$
(*) \quad N y = - (y \cdot \delta) \gamma , \quad \text{for} \quad y \in W_2.
$$

Next, note that $(\gamma' \cdot N\gamma') = -(N\gamma' \cdot \gamma') = -(\gamma' \cdot N\gamma')$, hence $(\gamma' \cdot \delta) = 0$.
The formula now follows by writing a vector $x \in W_4$ as a sum $x = (x \cdot \gamma)\gamma' + y$ and applying $(*)$ to $y = x - (x \cdot \gamma)\gamma' \in (W_0)^\perp = W_2$.

(1.2) Proposition.
Let $\pi \colon X \to \Lambda$ be a Type III degeneration of K3 surfaces.
Then the logarithm $N$ of the monodromy is integral.

Proof.
In view of (1.1) it suffices to prove that $\delta$ is an integral vector.
As $(T - 1)^2 = N^2$, we can write $\delta = N\gamma' = (T - 1)\gamma' - \frac{1}{2}N^2\gamma'$ and we must show that $\frac{1}{2}N^2\gamma'$ is integral.
From (1.1) we obtain

$$
N^2 \gamma' = N \delta = - (\delta \cdot \delta) \gamma . \tag{1.3}
$$

Moreover $(\delta \cdot \delta)\in 2\mathbb{Z}$, since

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\delta \cdot \delta) &amp;= ((T - 1) \gamma' - \frac{1}{2} (T - 1)^2 \gamma' \cdot (T - 1) \gamma' - \frac{1}{2} (T - 1)^2 \gamma') \\
&amp;= ((T - 1) \gamma' \cdot (T - 1) \gamma')
\end{aligned}
$$

and $\Lambda$ is an even lattice, q.e.d.

Note that the class $\delta$ constructed above is well defined (up to sign) only modulo $W_0$; it is easily seen however that its square $(\delta \cdot \delta)$ is independent of all choices, hence an invariant of $X_0$.

(1.4) Definition.
The number $(\delta \cdot \delta)$ will be called the $t$-invariant of $X_0$, denoted by $t(X_0)$.

We remark that, by the proof of (1.2), $t(X_0)$ is an even integer.
As a consequence of Theorem (0.6) and the constructions in §2, we will show in §7 that $t(X_0)$ is in fact equal to the number of triple points of $X_0$.

We associate to $X_0$ another invariant: since $N$ is an integral matrix, we can write $N = kN'$ for some primitive integral matrix $N'$ and $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$.

(1.5) Definition.
The integer $k = k(X_0)$ is called the index of $X_0$; we say that $X_0$ is primitive when $k = 1$.

The formula in (1.1) shows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_0 &amp;\text{is primitive} \Leftrightarrow N \text{is primitive} \Leftrightarrow \text{the class of } \delta \\
&amp;\text{in } (W_2 / W_0)_{\mathbb{Z}} \text{is primitive}.
\end{aligned}
$$

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

Finally we remark that if $X_0'$ is obtained from $X_0$ by a base change of order $n$, then $k(X_0') = n \cdot k(X_0)$ and $t(X_0') = n^2 \cdot t(X_0)$.

We now proceed to show that the index and the $t$-invariant of the central fiber $X_0$ form a complete set of invariants for $N$.
We shall need the following result of James (see also [17], Theorem 2.4):

(1.6) Theorem.
[15].
Let $M$ be an even lattice of signature $(p, n)$, $L$ an even unimodular lattice of signature $(P, N)$.
If $p + n &lt; \min(P, N)$ there exists a unique primitive embedding $M \hookrightarrow L$, up to isometries of $L$.

(1.7) Proposition.
The endomorphism $N$ is completely determined by the invariants $k(X_0)$ and $t(X_0)$, up to conjugation by isometries of $\Lambda$.

Proof.
We shall only consider the case in which $k(X_0) = 1$ (i.e., $N$ is primitive); the general case then follows without difficulty.

It is possible to put the matrix of $N$ in a "standard form" with respect to a normalized basis of $\Lambda = H^{2}(X_{s};\mathbb{Z})$, that is a basis in which the intersection form on $H^{2}(X_{s};\mathbb{Z})$ presents it as the lattice $H\oplus H\oplus H\oplus (-E_8)\oplus (-E_8)$.
Notice that in the construction of the classes $\gamma ,\gamma^{\prime},\delta$ at the beginning of this section we could have chosen $\gamma^\prime \in \varLambda$ with the additional property that $(\gamma^{\prime}\cdot \gamma^{\prime}) = 0$, by adding to it a suitable multiple of $\gamma$.
Therefore we have an orthogonal decomposition $\varLambda=\langle \gamma,\gamma^{\prime}\rangle\oplus L$ with $\langle \gamma ,\gamma^{\prime}\rangle \cong H$ and $L$ an even unimodular lattice of signature (2,18).
The class of $\delta$ is now well defined (and by hypothesis determines a primitive vector) in $L\cong (W_2 / W_0)_{\mathbb{Z}}$; in fact, by (1.6) applied to $M = \mathbb{Z}\delta$, $\delta$ is uniquely determined in $L$ up to isometries.
In other words, given any primitive vector $\eta \in L$ with $(\eta \cdot \eta) = (\delta \cdot \delta)$, there is an isometry of $L$ mapping $\delta$ to $\eta$.

Thus we can choose a normalized basis of $L = H \oplus H \oplus (-E_8) \oplus (-E_8)$ such that, if $\{\varepsilon, \varepsilon'\}$ is the basis of one of the hyperbolics, $\delta = \varepsilon + \frac{(\delta \cdot \delta)}{2} \varepsilon'$.

In conclusion, we have an orthogonal decomposition of the K3 lattice $\varLambda=\langle\gamma,\gamma^{\prime}\rangle\oplus\langle\varepsilon,\varepsilon^{\prime}\rangle\oplus L^{\prime}$, with $\delta=\varepsilon+\frac{(\delta\cdot\delta)}{2}\varepsilon^{\prime}$.
Recalling the formula in (1.1), we obtain a standard form for the matrix of $N$, which depends only on the number $(\delta\cdot\delta)=t(X_0)$, q.e.d.

We conclude this section with a discussion of the invariant $t(X_0)$ introduced above and its connection to the combinatorial configuration of the central fiber $X_0$.
We need to recall some results on the action of the monodromy, described by Clemens in [4] (see also [20]).

Let $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ be a semistable degeneration of surfaces, with central fiber $X_0$.
Let $c_s \colon X_s \to X_0$ denote the collapsing map; for a triple point $p \in X_0$, $c_s^{-1}(p)$ has the homotopy type of an oriented real 2-torus, whose class we denote by $\tau_p \in H_2(X_s; \mathbb{Z})$.
The cycle $\tau_p$ will be referred to as the vanishing cycle associated to the triple point $p$.
According to Clemens, if $B$ stands for the set of triple points in $X_0$, we have ([4], Theorem 4.4):

$$
N ^ {2} \alpha = - \sum_ {p \in B} (\alpha \cdot \tau_ {p}) \tau_ {p}, \quad \text {for any} \quad \alpha \in H _ {2} \left(X _ {s}; \mathbb {Q}\right). \tag {1.8}
$$

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

This formula can be simplified in our situation, thanks to the following

(1.9) Lemma.
Let $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ be a semistable degeneration of surfaces, $C$ an irreducible double curve of $X_0$ containing two triple points $p, q$.
If $C$ is smooth of genus 0, then the vanishing cycles $\tau_p$ and $\tau_q$ associated to $p$ and $q$ are homologous.

Proof.
Consider the fibration $\tilde{C} = c_s^{-1}(C - \{p, q\}) \to C - \{p, q\}$.
We can think of $\tilde{C}$ as the boundary of a regular tubular neighborhood in $X$ of $C - (\Delta_p \cup \Delta_q)$, where $\Delta_p, \Delta_q$ are disks in $C$ around the triple points $p$ and $q$.
Therefore $\tilde{C}$ has the homotopy type of an $S^1$-bundle over $C - \{p, q\}$.
Moreover, if $\delta_p$ denotes a loop in $C$ around $p$, the 2-cycle $\tau_p$ is homologous to the product of $\delta_p$ with the fiber of $\tilde{C} \to C - \{p, q\}$.

The main remark is now that, since the $S^1$-bundle $\tilde{C}$ is topologically trivial, $\tau_p$ is homologous to $\tau_q$ as soon as the loops $\delta_p$ and $\delta_q$ are homologous in $C - \{p, q\}$.
This happens exactly when $C$ is a smooth rational curve.

A rigorous proof of the details, especially with regard to the correct orientations, can be easily given in local coordinates, along the lines of [20], §2.2.
Note however that the orientations of the cycles $\tau_p$ are irrelevant in (1.8).

(1.10) Proposition.
Let $X_0$ be the central fiber of a Type III degeneration of K3 surfaces, $t = t(X_0)$ the $t$-invariant defined in (1.4), and $v$ the number of triple points of $X_0$.
Then

$$
t = a^2 v, \quad \text{for some integer } a \geq 1.
$$

In particular, $t$ is a positive even integer.

Proof.
In a Type III K3 surface $X_0$, any two triple points can be connected by smooth rational double curves, hence by (1.9) all vanishing cycles $\tau_p$, $p \in \{\text{triple points of } X_0\}$, are homologous.
If $\tau$ denotes their class, we can rewrite (1.8) as

$$
N^2 \alpha = -v(\alpha \cdot \tau) \tau.
$$

This shows that $\tau$ generates the image of $N^2$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, hence its dual class in $H^2(X_s; \mathbb{Z})$ is equal to some multiple $a\gamma$ of the element we denoted earlier by $\gamma$ (a generator of $(W_0)_{\mathbb{Z}} = \operatorname{Im} N^2 \cap \Lambda$).
Thus (1.3) becomes

$$
-(\delta \cdot \delta) \gamma = N^2 \gamma' = -v(\gamma' \cdot a\gamma) a\gamma = -a^2 v\gamma
$$

establishing the proposition.

We can be more precise about the integer $a$ in (1.10).
Let's recall some notation and results from [20], §2.5.
If $X_0 = \bigcup V_i$ has double curves $C_{ij} = V_i \cap V_j$ and triple points $T_{ijk} = V_i \cap V_j \cap V_k$ we let $\bar{V}_i = V_i \cup$ (tubular neighborhood of the double curves on $V_i$), $\bar{C}_{ij} = \bar{V}_i \cap \bar{V}_j$, $\bar{T}_{ijk} = \bar{V}_i \cap \bar{V}_j \cap \bar{V}_k$ and we put

$$
\widehat{\bar{V}}_i = c_s^{-1}(\bar{V}_i), \quad \widehat{C}_{ij} = c_s^{-1}(\bar{C}_{ij}), \quad \widehat{T}_{ijk} = c_s^{-1}(\bar{T}_{ijk})
$$

where $c_s \colon X_s \to X_0$ is the Clemens collapsing map.
Then $\hat{V}_i$ is homotopic to $V_i$ - (double curves on $V_i$), $\hat{C}_{ij}$ retracts to an $S^1$-bundle over $C_{ij}$ - (triple points on $C_{ij}$), and $\hat{T}_{ijk}$ is the product of a 2-torus by a 2-simplex

$$
\widehat{T}_{ijk} = \tau_{ijk} \times \omega_{ijk}
$$

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

where $\tau_{ijk}$ is the vanishing cycle associated to the triple point $T_{ijk}$ (cf.
[4], §3, where $\hat{T}_{ijk}$ is denoted by $X_s \cap Y\{i,j,k\}$, $\omega_{ijk} = \sigma_0$, $\tau_{ijk} = \eta_{1,2}$).
Moreover, viewing the $\omega_{ijk}$ as 2-chains in $X_s$, a consistent choice in orientations gives $\tau_{ijk} \cdot \omega_{ijk} = 1$ by [4], (3.3).

The homology of $X_{s} = \bigcup \hat{V}_{i}$ is computed by the Mayer-Vietoris homology spectral sequence ([20], 2.5.1), which has $E^{1}$ terms

$$
{}^q \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} \oplus H _ {q} (\hat {V} _ {i}) &amp; \oplus H _ {q} (\hat {C} _ {i j}) &amp; \oplus H _ {q} (\hat {T} _ {i j k}) \\ &amp; &amp; p. \end{array} \right.
$$

Here all homology is with $\mathbb{Z}$-coefficients, and $E_{p,q}^{1} \Rightarrow H_{p+q}(X_{s};\mathbb{Z})$.

(1.11) Lemma.
Let $A = \text{Coker} \{d_{1,1}^{1} \colon \oplus H_{1}(\hat{C}_{ij}) \to \oplus H_{1}(\hat{V}_{i})\}$.
Then $A = \mathbb{Z}/a\mathbb{Z}$, where $a$ is the integer appearing in (1.10).

Proof.
We have exact sequences $H_{2}(X_{s};\mathbb{Z}) \to E_{2,0}^{\infty} \to 0$ and $0 \to E_{2,0}^{\infty} \to E_{2,0}^{2} \to E_{0,1}^{2} \to E_{0,1}^{\infty} = 0$.
Since $E_{0,1}^{2} = A$ and

$$
E _ {2, 0} ^ {2} = \operatorname {K e r} \left\{\oplus H _ {0} \left(\hat {T} _ {i j k}\right)\rightarrow \oplus H _ {0} \left(\hat {C} _ {i j}\right)\right\} = H _ {2} (| \Gamma |; \mathbb {Z}),
$$

where $\Gamma$ is the dual graph of $X_0$, we obtain an exact sequence

$$
H _ {2} \left(X _ {s}; \mathbb {Z}\right) \xrightarrow {\psi} H _ {2} (| \Gamma |; \mathbb {Z}) = \mathbb {Z} \to A \to 0.
$$

Let $\tau = \tau_{ijk}$ denote the vanishing cycle associated to a triple point $T_{ijk}$ and recall that, by (1.9), its class in $H_2(X_s;\mathbb{Z})$ is independent of $\{i,j,k\}$.
Also note that each $T_{ijk}$ can be naturally thought of as a 2-simplex of $|\Gamma|$.
Going through the identifications that lead to the exact sequence above, we can interpret the generator $\omega = \sum T_{ijk}$ of $H_2(|\Gamma|;\mathbb{Z})$ as locally giving a class dual under intersection to $\tau$.
More precisely, a cycle $\eta \in H_2(X_s;\mathbb{Z})$ maps to $\psi(\eta) = n\omega$ if, locally in $\hat{T}_{ijk} = \tau_{ijk} \times \omega_{ijk}$, $\eta$ is of the form $n\omega_{ijk}$ (and hence $(\eta \cdot \tau) = n$).
Here locally means that we look at the image of $\eta$ via

$$
H _ {2} \left(X _ {s}\right)\rightarrow H _ {2} \left(X _ {s}, X _ {s} - \hat {T} _ {i j k}\right) = \mathbb {Z} \omega_ {i j k}.
$$

Thus the map $\psi \colon H_2(X_s) \to H_2(|\Gamma|) = \mathbb{Z}\omega$ is given by $\psi(\eta) = (\eta \cdot \tau)\omega$.

Recall now that we had constructed primitive vectors $\gamma, \gamma' \in H^2(X_s; \mathbb{Z})$ and, if $\hat{\gamma}$ and $\hat{\gamma}'$ denote their dual classes in $H_2(X_s; \mathbb{Z})$, the integer $a$ in (1.10) was defined by the relation $\tau = a\hat{\gamma}$.
Since $(\gamma \cdot \gamma') = 1$ and $\gamma'$ generates $H^2(X_s; \mathbb{Z}) / \langle \gamma \rangle^\perp$, the image of $\psi$ is generated by $\psi(\hat{\gamma}') = (\hat{\gamma}' \cdot \tau)\omega = a\omega$, and the lemma follows.

§ 2. Construction of degenerations

In this section we discuss the existence and construction, for all positive even integers $t$, of primitive Type III degenerations with $t$ triple points.
To establish the existence of such degenerations it suffices, as will be explained in (6.6), to exhibit Type III K3 surfaces in minus-one-form without any special $n$-bands of hexagons.
By the results of [7], this amounts to drawing appropriate polygonal

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

decompositions of $S^2$.
This method is used when $t \equiv 2 \pmod{4}$; in case $t \equiv 0 \pmod{4}$, instead, we are able to construct directly certain elementary examples originating from degenerations of abelian surfaces.

We shall also prove that, for all the surfaces constructed in this section, the $t$-invariant is equal to the number of triple points.
Thus, as a consequence of Theorem (0.6), we shall be able to extend this characterization of the $t$-invariant to all Type III K3 surfaces (see (7.1)).

(2.1) The case $t \equiv 0$ (mod 4).

Let $\rho \colon \mathcal{A}^* \to \Delta^* = \Delta - \{0\}$ be a proper smooth family of abelian surfaces, with a holomorphic section $0 \colon \Delta^* \to \mathcal{A}^*, 0(t) = \text{zero in } A_t = \rho^{-1}(t)$.
The canonical involutions $\iota_t \colon A_t \to A_t$ on the fibers induce an involution $\iota \colon \mathcal{A}^* \to \mathcal{A}^*$ which has 16 disjoint fixed curves (traced by the points of order two in the fibers).
If $f \colon \tilde{\mathcal{A}}^* \to \mathcal{A}^*$ is the blowup of these curves, $\iota$ lifts to an involution $\tilde{\iota}$ of $\tilde{\mathcal{A}}^*$ and the quotient $X^* = \tilde{\mathcal{A}}^* / \tilde{\iota}$ is a smooth threefold.
The fibers of the natural map $\pi \colon X^* \to \Delta^*$ are in fact Kummer surfaces as, for each $t \in \Delta^*$, $X_t = \pi^{-1}(t)$ is the desingularization of the quotient $A_t / \iota_t$.

By completing the family $\mathcal{A}^*$ to a semistable degeneration $\rho \colon \mathcal{A} \to \Delta$ we correspondingly obtain a degeneration of Kummer surfaces $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$.
However, the latter is not in general semistable, as singularities worse than normal crossings might be introduced in the central fiber.

This happens in fact exactly when two fixed curves of the involution $\iota \colon \mathcal{A}^* \to \mathcal{A}^*$ come together in the central fiber $A_0$.
The point of intersection of their closures lies then necessarily in a double curve of $A_0$ and gives rise to a pinch point in the singular fiber $X_0$ of $X$.
A semistable model of $X$ can only be obtained in this case after a base change of order 2. (Alternatively, this can be seen from the action of the monodromy $T \colon H^2(X_t) \to H^2(X_t)$: each fixed curve $F_j$ of the involution of $\mathcal{A}^*$ determines in the obvious way a class $e_j \in H^2(X_t; \mathbf{Z})$; now if $F_1$ and $F_2$ come together in $A_0$ we must have $Te_1 = e_2$, $Te_2 = e_1$ and, in particular, $T$ cannot be unipotent).

The examples we are interested in arise from families of reducible abelian surfaces.
For each integer $n &gt; 1$, let $E(n) \to \Delta$ be a semistable family of elliptic curves degenerating to a cycle of $n$ smooth rational curves, and consider the product families

$$
\mathcal{A}'(n, m) = E(n) \times_{\Delta} E(m) \to \Delta, \quad n \geq m \geq 1.
$$

A semistable model for $\mathcal{A}' = \mathcal{A}'(n, m)$ is easily determined.
The singular locus $(E(n)_0)_{\mathrm{sing}} \times (E(m)_0)_{\mathrm{sing}}$ of $\mathcal{A}'$ consists of isolated ordinary double points (locally around one of them, $\mathcal{A}'$ has the form $\{xy = zw\}$); each can be resolved by one blowup followed by the contraction of the resulting exceptional divisor $E \cong \mathbf{P}^1 \times \mathbf{P}^1$ along either one of its rulings.
Therefore, up to choosing in a consistent fashion the rulings to be contracted, we obtain a semistable model $\mathcal{A}(n, m)$ of $\mathcal{A}'$ whose central fiber $A_0(n, m)$ is an “$n \times m$ torus” in minus-one-form: the nondual graph of $A_0(n, m)$ is a decomposition of $S^1 \times S^1$ into $n \cdot m$ hexagons, as in Fig.
1 (see also [19], Ch.
II).

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

![img-5.jpeg](img-5.jpeg)
Fig.
1

Thus $A_0(n, m) = \bigcup V_{(p, q)}, (p, q) \in \mathbb{Z} / n\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} / m\mathbb{Z}$, and each component $V_{(p, q)}$ is isomorphic to $\mathbf{P}^1 \times \mathbf{P}^1$ blown up at the points $(0, 0)$ and $(\infty, \infty)$.

The canonical involution of the family over $\Delta^{*}$ extends to the central fiber, and acts there by

$$
\iota \colon (z, w) _ {(p, q)} \rightarrow \left(\frac {1}{z}, \frac {1}{w}\right) _ {(n - p, m - q)} \tag {2.2}
$$

where $(z,w)_{(p,q)}$ denote inhomogeneous coordinates on $V_{(p,q)}$.

In particular, some fixed points of the involution will lie on double curves – and hence the induced degeneration of Kummer surfaces will not be semistable – unless both integers $n$ and $m$ are even.
Therefore we shall restrict our considerations to that case.

(2.3) Proposition.
Let $X(a, b) \to \Delta$ be the semistable degeneration of (special) Kummer surfaces induced by $\mathcal{A}(2a, 2b)$ as in (2.1).
Let $X_0(a, b)$ be the singular fiber and let $k, v$ denote respectively the index and the number of triple points of $X_0(a, b)$.
Then

1. $X_0(a, b)$ is in minus-one-form and its nondual graph consists of 4 triangles and $2ab - 2$ hexagons, arranged as in Fig.
   2 (compare [14], p.
   281).
2. $v = 4ab$
3. $k = \gcd(a, b)$.

Proof.
Denote as before by $\iota$ the involution on the central fiber $A_0(2a, 2b)$; its action is described in (2.2), with the indices there referring to Fig.

1.

In our situation $\iota$ restricts to an involution on each of the four components in position $(0,0)$ $(a,0)$ $(0,b)$ and $(a,b)$, fixing 4 points on each and identifying the double curves in pairs.
Any other component $V_{(p,q)}$ is mapped isomorphically onto $V_{(2a - p,2b - q)}$.
The desingularization of the quotient $A_0(2a,2b) / \iota$ is therefore a Type III K3 surface in minus-one-form with nondual graph as described in 1).

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

![img-6.jpeg](img-6.jpeg)
Fig.
2

The number of triple points can now be calculated by inspection or by using the formula $v = 2f - 4$ which relates the number of vertices and the number of faces of any trivalent polygonal decomposition of the sphere.

Finally, 3) can be proved by a direct arithmetic argument, via an explicit calculation of the monodromy of the family $X(a,b)$ .
Or, using the results of §6, one can argue as in (6.6) and observe that $X_0(na,nb)$ is obtained as a base change of order $n$ of $X_0(a,b)$ and that $X_0(a,b)$ is primitive whenever $\gcd(a,b) = 1$ since in this case the graph of Fig.
2 cannot admit any special $n$ -bands of hexagons.

We also remark that the $t$ -invariant $(\delta \cdot \delta)$ can be explicitly calculated to be $4ab$ , proving that $t(X_0) = \#$ (triple points of $X_0$ ) in this case.
A different and more general argument is given below, in (2.6).

(2.4) Corollary.
For each integer $a \geq 1$ , the family $X(a, 1)$ provides an example of primitive Type III degeneration with $t(X_0) = 4a$ .
The central fiber is in minus-one-form, with nondual graph as in the figure:

![img-7.jpeg](img-7.jpeg)
Fig.
3

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

The reader may find it amusing to verify directly that, if $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ , there exists a birational map $X_0(a, b) \xrightarrow{\sim} X_0(ab, 1)$ composed of abstract Type I and II modifications, as guaranteed by (0.6).
In fact, just Type II modifications are sufficient in this case.

(2.5) The case $t \equiv 2$ (mod 4).

If we write $t = 4a + 2$ we can use, among numerous possibilities, central fibers in minus-one-form with the following nondual graphs:

![img-8.jpeg](img-8.jpeg)

![img-9.jpeg](img-9.jpeg)
Fig.
4

The graphs here consist of 2 digons (polygons with two sides), 2 quadrilaterals and $2a - 1$ hexagons, except for the case $a = 0$ when all three faces are digons.

Notice that each one of these graphs contains at least two adjacent nonhexagonal faces, hence the corresponding Type III K3 surfaces do not have any special $n$ -bands of hexagons.

(2.6) Lemma.
Let $X_0$ be one of the Type III K3 surfaces depicted in Fig.
3 or Fig.
4. Then $t(X_0) = \# (\text{triple points of } X_0)$ .

Proof.
By (1.10) and (1.11) it suffices to prove that, with the notations introduced there, $A = (0)$ .
Recall that

$$
A = \operatorname {C o k e r} \left\{d _ {1, 1} ^ {1}: \oplus H _ {1} \left(\hat {C} _ {i j}; \mathbf {Z}\right)\rightarrow \oplus H _ {1} \left(\hat {V} _ {i}; \mathbf {Z}\right)\right\},
$$

$\hat{C}_{ij}$ is homotopic to an $S^1$ -bundle over $C_{ij} - (\text{triple points on } C_{ij})$ and $\hat{V}_i$ is homotopic to $V_i - (\text{double curves on } V_i)$ .

Denote by $\gamma_{ij} \in H_1(\hat{V}_i)$ a loop in $\hat{V}_j$ encircling $C_{ij}$ and by $\alpha = \alpha_{ij} \in H_1(\hat{C}_{ij})$ the class corresponding to the fiber of $\hat{C}_{ij} \to C_{ij} - (\text{triple points})$ .
Then it is not difficult to see, using local coordinates and the explicit descriptions in [20], §2.2, that the image of $\alpha$ in the natural map $H_1(\hat{C}_{ij}) \to H_1(\hat{V}_i)$ is $\pm \gamma_{ij}$ ; therefore, up

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

to a convenient choice of orientations,

$$
d _ {1, 1} ^ {1} (\alpha) = \gamma_ {i j} + \gamma_ {j i} \in \oplus H _ {1} (\hat {V} _ {i}),
$$

and we conclude that $\{\gamma_{ij} + \gamma_{ji},\mathrm{all}i,j\} \subseteq \mathrm{Im}d_{1,1}^{1}$

Next we remark that since $X_0$ is in minus-one-form, $H_{1}(\hat{V}_{i}) = 0$ unless $V_{i}$ is a hexagon (cfr.
[7] (5.9), proof of Claim 2).
If $V_{i}$ is a hexagon, $\hat{V}_i = \mathbb{C}^*\times \mathbb{C}^*$ and $H_{1}(\hat{V}_{i}) = \mathbb{Z}\oplus \mathbb{Z}$ is generated by any two loops $\gamma_{ij},\gamma_{ik}$ corresponding to adjacent double curves.
A simple direct analysis based on the explicit picture of $X_0$ will now show that $\oplus H_{1}(\hat{V}_{i})\subseteq \{\gamma_{ij} + \gamma_{ji},\mathrm{all}i,j\}$ , concluding the proof.

For future reference, we collect some of the results proved in this section.

(2.7) Proposition.
For every $t \in 2\mathbb{Z}^+$ , there exists a $d$ -semistable Type III K3 surface $X_0$ satisfying the following properties:

(i) $t = t(X_0) = \# (\text{triple points of } X_0)$ .
(ii) $X_0$ is in minus-one-form.
(iii) The nondual graph of $X_0$ does not admit special $n$ -bands of hexagons for any $n &gt; 1$ .

It will be shown in (6.6) that all these $X_0$ are primitive.

We conclude with a result that will be used in the last section.

(2.8) Lemma.
Let $X_0$ be a base change of order $n \geq 1$ of one of the Type III K3 surfaces in Fig.
3 or Fig.
4. Then there exists, up to Type I and II modifications, a component of $X_0$ whose double curve is negative definite.

(We say that a curve $D$ on a smooth surface is negative definite when the intersection matrix for the irreducible components of $D$ is such.)

Proof.
Suppose first that $X_0 = X_0(a, 1)$ .
Then we can arrange a negative definite double curve on any of the triangular components, as an obvious sequence of standard modifications of Type I puts $X_0$ in the following form:

![img-10.jpeg](img-10.jpeg)
Fig.
5. All unmarked double curves have self intersection $-1$

It is clear that analogous Type I modifications also work if $X_0$ is a base change of $X_0(a, 1)$ .

Similarly, for the degenerations of Fig.
4, appropriate Type I modifications lead to the following configuration, with a negative definite digonal component:
