6

![img-12.jpeg](img-12.jpeg)

# § 3. The mixed Hodge structure on the singular fiber

We fix notation for the rest of this section.
Let $X_0$ be a Type III K3 surface.
For us, this shall mean that $X_0$ is a surface with normal crossings $X_0 = \bigcup V_i$ , where $\tilde{V}_i$ is smooth rational (here $\nu_i \colon \tilde{V}_i \to V_i$ is the normalization map),

$$
v _ {i} ^ {- 1} \left(D _ {i}\right) = v _ {i} ^ {- 1} \left[ V _ {i} \cap \left(\bigcup_ {j \neq i} V _ {j}\right) \right] \in | - K _ {\tilde {V} _ {i}} |
$$

and the dual graph $\Gamma$ is a triangulation of $S^2$ .
We shall not assume that $X_0$ is $d$ -semistable, as this assumption is irrelevant to the constructions of this section.

Let $H^{2}(X_{0};\mathbb{C})$ be the cohomology of $X_0$ , equipped with its natural mixed Hodge structure.
For this section, $F^{\prime} = \{F^{0}\supseteq F^{1}\}$ and $W_{\bullet} = \{W_{0} = W_{1}\subseteq W_{2}\}$ will denote the Hodge and weight filtrations on $H^{2}(X_{0})$ .
By the Mayer-Vietoris spectral sequence [12] there is an exact sequence of free $\mathbb{Z}$ -modules

$$
0 \to (W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} \to (W _ {2}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} \to (W _ {2} / W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} \to 0,
$$

where $(W_{2} / W_{0})_{\mathbb{Z}} = \ker (H^{2}(\tilde{X}_{0};\mathbb{Z})\to H^{2}((\tilde{X}_{0})_{\mathrm{sing}};\mathbb{Z}))$ and $(W_0)_{\mathbb{Z}} = H^2 (|\Gamma |;\mathbb{Z})$

Set $L = (W_{2} / W_{0})_{\mathbb{Z}}$ .
Carlson ([2] and [3]) has defined an intrinsic invariant of the mixed Hodge structure on $H^{2}(X_{0})$ , the extension homomorphism $\psi$ ; $\psi$ is simply a homomorphism

$$
\psi \colon L \to J ^ {0} W _ {0} = \mathbb {C} ^ {*}.
$$

To define $\psi$ , choose a (complex) section $s$ of the projection $p\colon W_2\to W_2 / W_0$ which preserves the Hodge filtration, and a retraction $r\colon W_2\to W_0$ of the inclusion $W_{0}\subseteq W_{2}$ which is defined over $\mathbb{Z}$ .
In particular,

$$
(r, p) \colon (W _ {2}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} \stackrel {{\sim}} {{\to}} (W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} \oplus (W _ {2} / W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {Z}}.
$$

Then

$$
\psi = r \circ s \bmod (W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} \colon L \to (W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {C}} / (W _ {0}) _ {\mathbb {Z}} = \mathbb {C} / \mathbb {Z} = \mathbb {C} ^ {*}
$$

is independent of the choices made.

Carlson gives a geometric interpretation of $\psi$ in [3], §7.
He proves:

(3.1) Proposition.
If $h \in L$ and $\psi(h) = 1$ , there is a unique Cartier divisor $H \in \operatorname{Pic}(X_0)$ inducing $h$ .

We shall deduce (3.1) from the following result.

(3.2) Proposition.
Let $h \in \ker \psi$ .
Then there is a unique $\tilde{h} \in F^1 \cap (W_2)_\mathbb{Z}$ such that $\tilde{h} \bmod W_0 = h$ .

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Proof.
To find $\hat{h}$ , set $\hat{h} = s(h) \in F^1$ .
Since $p(s(h))$ is integral and $r(s(h)) \in (W_0)_{\mathbb{Z}}$ by hypothesis, $s(h)$ is integral.
If now $\hat{h}_1, \hat{h}_2$ are two lifts of $h$ , we have

$$
\hat {h} _ {1} - \hat {h} _ {2} \in W _ {0} \cap F ^ {1} = 0, \quad \text {q . e . d .}
$$

(3.3) Remark.
A similar statement (with similar proof) holds in the Type II case.

Proof of (3.1).
Given $h \in L$ with $\psi(h) = 1$ , lift $h$ to $\hat{h} \in F^1 \cap (W_2)_{\mathbb{Z}}$ as in (3.2).
By the exponential sheaf sequence

$$
0 = H ^ {1} \left(X _ {0}; \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0}}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {1} \left(X _ {0}; \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0}} ^ {*}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0}}\right)
$$

it suffices to check that the image of $\hat{h}$ in $H^{2}(X_{0};\mathcal{C}_{X_{0}})$ is zero.
But an easy verification (cf.
[7] (1.5)) shows that the map $H^{2}(X_{0};\mathbb{Z})\to H^{2}(X_{0};\mathcal{C}_{X_{0}})$ is induced by composition from the natural maps

$$
H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {C}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {C}\right) / F ^ {1} = H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0}}\right).
$$

Since $\hat{h} \in F^1$ , we are done.

The next result is another corollary of Carlson's work and gives a different description of $\psi$ .
We shall not use it in the sequel.
To state (3.4), we begin with the following resolution of $\mathcal{C}_{X_0}^*$ [7] (1.5):

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0}} ^ {*} \rightarrow \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 0 ]}} ^ {*} \rightarrow \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 1 ]}} ^ {*} \rightarrow \mathcal {C} _ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 2 ]}} ^ {*} \rightarrow 0
$$

where

$$
X _ {0} ^ {[ 0 ]} = \coprod_ {i} \tilde {V} _ {i} = \tilde {X} _ {0}
$$

$$
X _ {0} ^ {[ 1 ]} = \coprod_ {i &lt;   j} (\widetilde {V _ {i} \cap V _ {j}}) = (\widetilde {X _ {0}}) _ {\text {s i n g}}
$$

$$
X _ {0} ^ {[ 2 ]} = \coprod_ {i &lt;   j &lt;   k} \left(V _ {i} \cap V _ {j} \cap V _ {k}\right).
$$

There is a spectral sequence converging to $H^{*}(X_{0},\mathcal{C}_{X_{0}}^{*})$ with $E_{1}$ term

| H1(C\*X0[0]) | H1(C\*X1[1]) |              |
| ------------ | ------------ | ------------ |
| H0(C\*X1[1]) | H0(C\*X1[1]) | H0(C\*X2[1]) |

Now $\ker (H^{1}(\mathcal{C}_{X_{0}^{[0]}}^{*})\to H^{1}(\mathcal{C}_{X_{0}^{[1]}}^{*})) = \ker (H^{2}(X_{0}^{[0]};\mathbb{Z})\to H^{2}(X_{0}^{[1]};\mathbb{Z})) = L$ and therefore $d_{2}$ induces a map

$$
d _ {2} \colon L \to H ^ {2} (| \Gamma |; \mathbb {C} ^ {*}) = \mathbb {C} ^ {*}.
$$

(3.4) Proposition [3].
There exists an identification of $H^2(|\Gamma|; \mathbb{C}^*)$ with $J^0 W_0$ such that $d_2 = \psi$ .

Since $\operatorname{Pic}(X_0) = H^1(X_0; \mathcal{C}_{X_0}^*) = \ker d_2$ , (3.4) easily implies (3.1).

The remaining topic in this section is the calculation of the differential of an appropriate variation of mixed Hodge structures.
To do so, we will rapidly describe a general formalism for varieties with normal crossings of any dimension.

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

Let $X_0 = \bigcup V_i$ be a variety with global normal crossings (there are obvious modifications in case $X_0$ has local normal crossing, which will be left to the reader for the sake of notational simplicity).
Define

$$
X _ {0} ^ {[ p ]} = \coprod_ {i _ {0} &lt;   \dots &lt;   i _ {p}} \left(V _ {i _ {0}} \cap \dots \cap V _ {i _ {p}}\right)
$$

and let $a_{p} \colon X_{0}^{[p]} \to X_{0}$ be the natural map.
Let $\Omega_{X_0}^1$ be the sheaf of Kähler differentials on $X_0$, $\Lambda^q\Omega_{X_0}^1 = \Omega_{X_0}^q$, and set $\tau_{X_0}^q =$ the subcomplex of $\Omega_{X_0}^q$ consisting of those sections with support on $(X_0)_{\mathrm{sing}}$.

(3.5) Lemma.
([7] (1.5)).

1. The sequence

$$
0 \to \Omega_ {X _ {0}} ^ {q} / \tau_ {X _ {0}} ^ {q} \to (a _ {0}) _ {*} \Omega_ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 0 ]}} ^ {q} \to (a _ {1}) _ {*} \Omega_ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 1 ]}} ^ {q} \to \dots
$$

is exact.

2. There is a spectral sequence converging to $H^{*}(X_{0};\mathbb{C})$ with

$$
E _ {1} ^ {p, q} = H ^ {q} \left(X _ {0}; \Omega_ {X _ {0}} ^ {p} / \tau_ {X _ {0}} ^ {p}\right).
$$

3. If each $V_{i}$ is compact Kähler, the spectral sequence in 2) degenerates at $E_{1}$ and the resulting filtration $F^{\bullet}$ on $H^{*}(X_{0};\mathbb{C})$ is the Hodge filtration in the natural mixed Hodge structure.

We shall need a $C^\infty$ analogue of $\Omega_{X_0}^p / \tau_{X_0}^p$.
In general, if $M$ is a smooth manifold, let $A_M^p$ be the sheaf of $C^\infty$ $p$-forms on $M$.
If $X_0$ is a variety with normal crossings, define

$$
A _ {X _ {0}} ^ {p} = \ker \left(\left(a _ {0}\right) _ {*} A _ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 0 ]}} ^ {p} \rightarrow \left(a _ {1}\right) _ {*} A _ {X _ {0} ^ {[ 1 ]}} ^ {p}\right).
$$

Arguing exactly as in [7] (1.4) and (1.5), there is a canonical isomorphism

$$
\mathbb {H} ^ {k} \left(X _ {0}; A _ {X _ {0}} ^ {\bullet}\right) \cong H ^ {k} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {C}\right).
$$

Since the sheaves $A_{X_0}^p$ admit partitions of unity, we have moreover

$$
\mathbb {H} ^ {k} \left(X _ {0}; A _ {X _ {0}} ^ {\bullet}\right) = H ^ {k} \left(\Gamma A _ {X _ {0}} ^ {\bullet}\right),
$$

where $\Gamma$ denotes the global sections.
Finally, from the natural inclusion of sheaves

$$
\Omega_ {X _ {0}} ^ {p} / \tau_ {X _ {0}} ^ {p} \rightarrow A _ {X _ {0}} ^ {p}
$$

and the above remarks, we have canonical isomorphisms

$$
\mathbb {H} ^ {k} \left(X _ {0}; \Omega_ {X _ {0}} ^ {\bullet} / \tau_ {X _ {0}} ^ {\bullet}\right) \cong H ^ {k} \left(\Gamma A _ {X _ {0}} ^ {\bullet}\right) \cong H ^ {k} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {C}\right).
$$

Now suppose that $\pi \colon X \to S$ is a flat proper family, where $S$ is smooth, $0 \in S$, $\pi^{-1}(0) = X_0$ and $\pi^{-1}(s) = X_s$, say.
We assume in addition that $\pi$ is locally trivial: locally on $X$, $X$ is a product.
Intuitively, we obtain $X_s$ from $X_0$ by deforming the $V_i$, keeping the double varieties $V_i \cap V_j \subseteq V_i$, and in addition deforming the gluing maps

$$
V _ {i} \cong (V _ {i} \cap V _ {j}) \xrightarrow {\sim} (V _ {i} \cap V _ {j}) \subseteq V _ {j}
$$

(in particular, these must remain isomorphic throughout the deformation).

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Given $\pi \colon X \to S$ as above, there is an associated Kodaira-Spencer map

$$
\theta \colon T_{S, 0} \to H^{1}(X_{0}; T_{X_{0}}^{0}),
$$

where $T_{S,0}$ is the tangent space to $S$ at 0 and $T_{X_0}^0$ is the sheaf of derivations of $X_0$.

Just as for smooth varieties, the differential of the corresponding variation of mixed Hodge structure can be read off from the Gauss-Manin connection acting on $R^k \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ (cf.
[5], [12] §3).
There are obvious relative objects compatible with base change

$$
\Omega_{X/S}^{p}, \Omega_{X/S}^{p} / \tau_{X/S}^{p}
$$

and $R^k \pi_*(\mathbb{C}) \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ is filtered by subbundles $F^p$ with

$$
F^{p} / F^{p+1} = R^{q} \pi_*(\Omega_{X/S}^{p} / \tau_{X/S}^{p}) \quad (p + q = k).
$$

(3.6) **Theorem.** 1) Let $V$ be the Gauss-Manin connection on $R^k \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ and $\xi$ a holomorphic tangent vector on $S$.
If $\sigma$ is a holomorphic section of $F^p$, then $V_\xi \sigma$ is a holomorphic section of $F^{p-1}$.

2. The map, induced via 1),

$$
\bigoplus_{p + q = k} H^{q}(X_{0}; \Omega_{X_{0}}^{p} / \tau_{X_{0}}^{p}) \to \bigoplus_{p, q} H^{q+1}(X_{0}; \Omega_{X_{0}}^{p-1} / \tau_{X_{0}}^{p-1})
$$

is cup product with $\theta(\xi) \in H^{1}(X_{0}; T_{X_{0}}^{0})$.

**Proof.** The proof given in [5] (7.7) when $\pi$ is a smooth map applies unchanged in this situation, given that we have constructed the $C^\infty$ differentials $A_{X_0}^*$.

(3.7) **Corollary.** If $X_0$ is a Type III K3 surface and $\pi \colon X \to S$ is a germ of the universal locally trivial deformation of $X_0$, the differential of the period map is an isomorphism.

**Proof.** Note that $S$ is smooth by [7] (5.8.4).
It is enough to show that

$$
H^{1}(T_{X_{0}}^{0}) \otimes H^{1}(\Omega_{X_{0}}^{1} / \tau_{X_{0}}^{1}) \to H^{2}(\mathcal{O}_{X_{0}}) = \mathbb{C}
$$

is a perfect pairing.
But, by [7] (2.10), the map

$$
H^{1}(T_{X_{0}}^{0}) \otimes H^{1}\left(\left(\Omega_{X_{0}}^{1} / \tau_{X_{0}}^{1}\right) \otimes \omega_{X_{0}}\right) \to H^{2}(\omega_{X_{0}})
$$

given by cup product is a perfect pairing, where $\omega_{X_0}$ is the dualizing sheaf.
Since $\omega_{X_0} = \mathcal{O}_{X_0}$, we are done.

(3.8) **Remark.** If $X_0$ is any variety with normal crossings of dimension $n$ such that $\omega_{X_0} = \mathcal{O}_{X_0}$ and the base of the versal locally trivial deformation space of $X_0$ is smooth, the above proof applies unchanged to show that the variation of mixed Hodge structure on $H^n(X_0; \mathbb{C})$ is injective.

(3.9) We mention the link between (3.7) and Carlson's extension homomorphism, defined at the beginning of this section.
If

$$
\pi \colon X \to S
$$

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

is a locally trivial family of Type III K3 surfaces, the cohomology bundle $R^2\pi_{\star}\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{C}}\mathcal{O}_S$ is filtered by Hodge subbundles $F^0\supseteq F^1$, which are holomorphic, and weight subbundles $\mathcal{W}_0\subseteq \mathcal{W}_2$, which are horizontal.
Repeating the construction of $\psi$, in small open subsets of $S$, we may split the map of bundles $F^0\to F^0 /F^1$ and find (flat) integral retractions $\mathcal{W}_2\rightarrow \mathcal{W}_0$ locally.
If there is no monodromy on $\mathcal{W}_2 / \mathcal{W}_0$ (which may always be arranged in the Application (4.3)) we may define, globally, a map

$$
\Psi \colon S \to \operatorname {H o m} (L, \mathbb {C} ^ {*})
$$

after identifying $\mathcal{W}_2 / \mathcal{W}_0$ with $L\otimes_{\mathbb{C}}\mathcal{O}_S$, and $\Psi$ is easily seen to be holomorphic and to satisfy $\Psi_s = \psi$, Carlson's invariant described above.

By the results of [2], $\Psi$ is locally equivalent to the data of $F^1 \subseteq R^2\pi_{\star}\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{C}}\mathcal{O}_S$, so that (3.7) yields an isomorphism statement for the differential of $\Psi$.

## § 4. Surjectivity of the period map

We keep the notations of the previous section.
In particular, $X_0$ will denote a not necessarily $d$-semistable Type III K3 surface, $F'$ and $W$, refer to the Hodge and weight filtrations on $H^2(X_0; \mathbb{C})$, and $\psi, L$ have the same meaning as in §3.
The period space for $X_0$ is then simply

$$
Z = \operatorname {H o m} (L, \mathbb {C} ^ {*}) \cong (\mathbb {C} ^ {*}) ^ {n}.
$$

If $\pi \colon X \to S$ is any family, then $\pi^{-1}(s)$ will, as usual, be denoted $X_{s}$.
Our goal in this section is to prove:

(4.1) **Theorem.** Let $z \in Z$.
Then there is a locally trivial deformation $\pi \colon X \to S$ over a smooth connected base $S$ and points $0, t \in S$ such that $X_0$ is a given Type III K3 surface and $X_t$ has the mixed Hodge structure corresponding to $z \in Z$.

Thus, (4.1) roughly means that the period map is surjective for Type III K3 surfaces.
The proof of (4.1) is as follows.
First, we will construct a parameter space $S$ for all deformations of $X_0$, where $S = (\mathbb{C}^*)^m$ for some $m$.
$S$ may be quite far from being a coarse moduli space for varieties of type $X_0$, but the Kodaira-Spencer map will be surjective near 0.

Now, the period map (3.9)

$$
\rho \colon S \to Z
$$

is a holomorphic map: $(\mathbb{C}^{*})^{m}\to (\mathbb{C}^{*})^{n}$.
We then use

(4.2) **Theorem.** (Rosenlicht [23]).
If $\rho \colon (\mathbb{C}^{*})^{m}\to (\mathbb{C}^{*})^{n}$ is an algebraic map with $\rho(1) = 1$, then $\rho$ is a homomorphism.

We give a brief sketch of the proof of (4.2).
Clearly, we may assume $n = 1$.
View $\rho$ as a meromorphic function on $(\mathbf{P}^1)^m$.
$(\mathbf{P}^1)^m$ has $2m$ natural divisors $D_0^i$ and $D_{\infty}^{i}$, $i = 1,\dots,m$, defined by setting the $i$th coordinate $\zeta_i$ equal to 0 or $\infty$.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Moreover, the divisor $(\rho)$ clearly satisfies

$$
(\rho) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} n_i D_0^i + \sum_{i=1}^{m} m_i D_\infty^i;
$$

since $(\rho)$ is homologous to 0, we must have $n_i = -m_i$.
Consider the character $\chi: (\mathbb{C}^*)^m \to \mathbb{C}^*$ defined by

$$
\chi(\zeta_1, \dots, \zeta_m) = \zeta_1^{n_1} \dots \zeta_m^{n_m}.
$$

Then $(\chi) = (\rho)$, so that $\chi$ and $\rho$ differ by a nonvanishing constant.
As $\rho(1) = \chi(1)$, $\rho = \chi$, q.e.d.

The bulk of this section will then be devoted to showing that the period map $\rho$ is algebraic.
Once we have done so, and applying (4.2), $\rho$ is surjective $\Leftrightarrow$ the differential of $\rho$ is surjective at one point.
Thus, (4.1) will follow immediately from (3.9).

That $\rho$ is an affine map presumably follows from the results of Carlson sketched in §3.
Since this approach seems a bit messy to write down carefully, we shall argue that $\rho$ is algebraic via the theory of regular singular points.

## (4.3) Construction of $S$.

### (4.4) Definition

An _anticanonical pair_ $(V, D)$ consists of a smooth rational surface $V$ and a section $D \in |-K_V|$, where $D$ is a cycle of smooth rational curves meeting transversally (possibly of length 1).

A _deformation of the pair_ $(V, D)$ consists of a family $\pi: \mathcal{V} \to S$ and a divisor $\mathcal{D} \subset \mathcal{V}$ where $\pi$ is smooth and proper and $\mathcal{D}$ is a relative curve with normal crossings over $S$, whose normalization is smooth over $S$, and a marked point $0 \in S$ such that $\pi^{-1}(0) = V$, $V \cap \mathcal{D} = D \subseteq V$.
In particular, $\mathcal{D} \cap \pi^{-1}(s)$ is an anticanonical divisor on $\pi^{-1}(s)$ for all $s \in S$, and the topological type of $(V, D)$ is unchanged under deformation.

### (4.5) Remark

1. The anticanonical pairs $(V, D)$ where $V$ is $\mathbf{P}^2$ or $\mathbf{F}_N$ are listed in [10], Lemma (3.2).

2. Let $(V, D)$ be any anticanonical pair.
   We may find a birational morphism $f: V \to V' = \mathbf{P}^2$ or $\mathbf{F}_N$ such that $(V', f(D))$ is an anticanonical pair, by the following procedure:

a) Successively contract exceptional curves $E$ on $V$ which are not contained in the support of $D$.
As part of this procedure, we may have chains of rational curves, all of which have self-intersection $-2$ except an end curve, which has self-intersection $-1$.

b) When there are no more such $E$ to contract, successively contract exceptional curves $E$ in the cycle $D$.
Arguing as in [10], (3.3) and (3.4), the end result will be $f: V \to V'$ as desired.

3. The inverse construction to 2) is as follows: start with an anticanonical pair $(V', D')$ where $V'$ is $\mathbf{P}^2$ or $\mathbf{F}_N$.
   Blow up points in $(D')_{\mathrm{sing}}$ a certain number of times — this construction has no moduli.
   Let $(V'', D'')$ be the resulting anticanonical pair.
   Now blow up a certain number of points (possibly infinitely

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

near) on $(D^{\prime \prime})_{\mathrm{reg}}$.
The construction is clearly parametrized by a $(\mathbb{C}^{*})^{a} = S_{V,D}$ and leads to a “relative anticanonical pair” $(\mathcal{V},\mathcal{D})\to S_{V,D}$ where $\mathcal{V}$ is a scheme and $\mathcal{D} = \bigcup \mathcal{D}_i$ is a relative curve with normal crossings over $S_{V,D}$.

We shall need one additional lemma:

(4.6) Lemma.
If $(V, D)$ is an anticanonical pair and $V = \mathbf{P}^2$ or $\mathbf{F}_N$, then the deformations of the pair $(V, D)$ as an anticanonical pair may be parametrized by a $(\mathbb{C}^*)^b$.

Proof.
For many of the entries in the table of (3.2) of [10], (4.6) is clear because the pair $(V, D)$ has no moduli, e.g., there is only one nodal cubic in $\mathbf{P}^2$ up to projective equivalence.

We give a sample of the argument in one of the remaining cases: $V = \mathbf{F}_0$ or $\mathbf{F}_2$ and $D$ is irreducible, $D^2 = 8$.
Start with a nodal cubic in $\mathbf{P}^2$; as remarked above, these have no moduli.
Blow up two smooth points on it, possibly infinitely near.
Contracting the line between them (or tangent line, if we made an infinitely near blowup), we obtain $\mathbf{F}_0$ or $\mathbf{F}_2$ with $D$ as desired, and all such anticanonical pairs arise in this way.
The construction is parametrized by the choice of two points in the smooth locus of a nodal cubic, i.e., by $\mathbb{C}^* \times \mathbb{C}^*$.

Given $X_0 = \bigcup V_i$ we may use the arguments of (4.5) and (4.6) to find tori $(\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}$ and schemes $(\mathcal{V}_i, \mathcal{D}_i)$ over $(\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}$ which parametrize the deformations of the pair $(V_i, D_i)$.
We must still account for the gluings $V_i \supseteq (V_i \cap V_j) = D_{ij} \cong D_{ji} = (V_i \cap V_j) \subseteq V_j$.
Note that $D_{ij}$ and $D_{ji}$ will remain isomorphic (along with the marked points on them) in any small deformation.
Moreover, the $\mathbf{P}^1$ bundle $\mathcal{D}_{ij} \to (\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}$ leads to a line bundle, since there are marked sections $\infty$ and $0$, and every algebraic line bundle over $(\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}$ is algebraically trivial.
Choosing such trivializations for each $i$, we set

$$
G = \prod_{\substack{i &lt; j\\ V_{i}\cap V_{j}\neq \emptyset}}\operatorname {Aut}(\mathbb{C}^{*}) = (\mathbb{C}^{*})^{c}
$$

and $S = \Pi_i(\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}\times G$ with projection $p_1\colon S\to \Pi_i(\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}$, $\pi_i\colon S\to (\mathbb{C}^*)^{a_i}$.

Over $S$, we have schemes $\pi^{[0]}: X^{[0]} \to S$, $\pi^{[1]}: X^{[1]} \to S$, $\pi^{[2]}: X^{[2]} \to S$, where

$$
X ^ {[ 0 ]} = \coprod_ {i} \pi_ {i} ^ {- 1} (\mathcal {V} _ {i}) \quad (\text {pullback of families})
$$

$$
X ^ {[ 1 ]} = \left(p _ {1}\right) ^ {- 1} \left(\coprod_ {i &lt;   j} \mathcal {D} _ {i j}\right)
$$

$X^{[2]} =$ the scheme of (relative) triple points.

Using the data of $G$, there are moreover gluing morphisms

$$
g ^ {[ 0 ]} = \left(g _ {i j} ^ {[ 0 ]}\right): X ^ {[ 1 ]} \xrightarrow {\sim} X ^ {[ 1 ]},
$$

and similarly for $g^{[1]} (= \mathrm{Id})$.
Hence there is a glued family $\pi \colon X \to S$ which is a surface with normal crossings over $S$; however, the fibers of $\pi$ will not in general be projective (cf.
[7] (2.12)).
It is clear that $X \to S$ is versal for locally trivial deformations of $X_0$, in the sense that any locally trivial deformation of $X_0$ is pulled back from $X \to S$.
In particular, the Kodaira-Spencer map is surjective at $0 \in S$.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

In this situation, there are flat vector bundles $R^i \pi_*^{[j]} \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ over $S$, which have a Gauss-Manin connection.
Via the $\mathfrak{g}^{[j]}$, we have maps of the local systems $\partial^{[j]}$ (an appropriate alternating sum of restrictions, followed by $(\mathfrak{g}^{[j]})^*$, e.g.

$$
\partial^{[0]} = \operatorname{res}_{V_i} - (\mathfrak{g}_{ij}^{[0]})^* \operatorname{res}_{V_j} \text{ for } i &lt; j): R^i \pi_*^{[j]} \mathfrak{C} \xrightarrow{\partial^{[j]}} R^i \pi_*^{[j+1]} \mathfrak{C}.
$$

In particular, $R^i \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ is the abutment of a spectral sequence $E_r^{p,q}$ which degenerates at $E_2$ [12] (4.2), and all the terms in $E_1$ are flat vector bundles and the $d_1^{p,q}$ are horizontal maps.

(4.7) Lemma 1) The Gauss-Manin connection on $R^i \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ has regular singular points.

2. The weight and Hodge subbundles of $R^i \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ are algebraic subbundles.

Proof.
We shall only prove 1), as the proof of 2) is based on similar ideas.
To prove 1), use:

(4.8) Lemma.
([5] II (1.13)).
If $M$ is a complex manifold, $D \subseteq M$ a divisor with normal crossings, and

$$
0 \to E' \to E \to E'' \to 0
$$

a horizontal sequence of flat vector bundles over $M - D$, then the connection on $E$ has regular singular points along $D \Leftrightarrow$ those of $E'$ and $E''$ do.

Returning to the proof of (4.7), by the above lemma all of the terms in $E_2$ for the spectral sequence abutting to $R^i \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ have regular singular points.
Since $R^i \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ is filtered by a filtration whose associated graded terms have regular singular points, $R^i \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ does as well, again by repeated application of (4.8), q.e.d.

We are now able to prove (4.1).
By the discussion at the beginning of this section, it suffices to prove

(4.9) Proposition.
The period map $\rho: S \to Z$ is algebraic.

Proof.
Write $Z = \operatorname{Hom}(L, \mathbb{C}^*) = (\mathbb{C}^*)^n$, by choosing a basis $\{e_1, \ldots, e_n\}$ for $L$.
If $\rho_i$ is the $i$th coordinate function of $\rho$ (i.e. $\rho_i(s) = \rho(s)(e_i)$), it suffices to prove that $\rho_i$ has a meromorphic extension to $(\mathbf{P}^1)^m$.
For notational simplicity, choose coordinates $\zeta_1, \ldots, \zeta_m$ on $S$.
We shall show that $\rho_i$ has a meromorphic extension at the generic point of the divisor $\{\zeta_1 = 0\}$; the proposition follows from this.
We follow the notation of (3.9).

The monodromy $T$ of the vector bundle $R^2 \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ along $\{\zeta_1 = 0\}$ is easily seen to be unipotent; indeed, $T$ is the identity on the natural subbundles $\mathcal{W}_0$ and $\mathcal{W}_2 / \mathcal{W}_0$.
If $N$ is the logarithm of $T$, $N^2 = 0$, as $N(\mathcal{W}_2) \subseteq \mathcal{W}_0$ and $N(\mathcal{W}_0) = 0$.
In particular, every multivalued horizontal section of $R^2 \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ grows like $O(\log |\zeta_1|)$ [5], II (5.2).

The bundle $F^1 \subseteq R^2 \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ is an algebraic subbundle and hence may be extended to the canonical extension (in the sense of regular singular points) of $R^2 \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_S$ to $(\mathbf{P}^1)^m$ in such a way that the extension, which we again denote by $F^1$, is locally free at a generic point of $\{\zeta_1 = 0\}$.
Choosing local sections of

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

$F^1$ which do not vanish at a generic point of $\{\zeta_1 = 0\}$ and identifying $J^0 W_0$ with $\mathbb{C} / \mathbb{Z} = \mathbb{C}^*$ via $\exp (2\pi i\cdot)$, we see that $\rho_{i}$ has a growth in $\zeta_{1}$ like

$$
| \text{holomorphic function in } \zeta | \times \exp (2 \pi i C \log | \zeta_ {1} |) = O (| \zeta_ {1} | ^ {C})
$$

and hence $\rho_{i}$ is meromorphic as $\zeta_1\to 0$, q.e.d.

For the remainder of this section, we assume that $X_0 = \bigcup_{i=1}^{f} V_i$ is $d$-semistable, and discuss the modification of (4.1) that can be made in this case.
First, $X_0$ has additional Cartier divisors $\xi_i$, $1 \leq i \leq f$, which may be defined as follows: let $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ be a smoothing of $X_0$, where $X$ is a smooth threefold (which exists by [7] (5.10)).
Then set

$$
\xi_ {i} = \mathcal {O} _ {X} (V _ {i}) | _ {X _ {0}}. \tag {4.10}
$$

More intrinsically, it is easy to check that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi_ {i} \mid V _ {i} = \mathcal {O} _ {V _ {i}} (- D _ {i}) \\ \xi_ {i} \mid V _ {j} = \mathcal {O} _ {V _ {j}} (V _ {i} \cap V _ {j}), \quad i \neq j, \\ \end{array}
$$

defines a Cartier divisor on $X_0$, agreeing with the previous definition.
Note the relation $\xi_1 + \ldots + \xi_f = \mathcal{O}_{X_0}(V_1 + \ldots + V_f) = 0$.

Given $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$, set $X^{*} = X - X_{0}$.
The following sequences are exact over $\mathbb{Z}$ (without any Kähler assumption on $X$) [12] $10(b)$:

$$
0 = L H ^ {1} \left(X _ {0}\right) = H ^ {1} \left(X _ {t}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X ^ {*}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {t}; \mathbb {Z}\right) \xrightarrow {I - T} H ^ {2} \left(X _ {t}; \mathbb {Z}\right) \tag {4.11}
$$

(Wang sequence)

$$
H ^ {1} \left(X ^ {*}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H _ {4} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X ^ {*}; \mathbb {Z}\right)\rightarrow H _ {3} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right) \tag {4.12}
$$

(exact sequence of the pair $(X, X^{*})$ plus Poincaré-Alexander duality).
In what follows, we shall always mean $\mathbb{Z}$ coefficients and shall write $H^{2}(X_{t}; \mathbb{Z}) = LH^{2}(X_{0})$.

We claim that $H^{2}(X_{0})$ maps onto $H^{2}(X^{*})$ over $\mathbb{Z}$, i.e. that $H_{3}(X_{0};\mathbb{Z}) = 0$.
It is easy to see that $H_{3}(X_{0})$ is torsion free: by the universal coefficient theorem, $H_{3}(X_{0})_{\mathrm{tors}} = H^{4}(X_{0})_{\mathrm{tors}}$, and an examination of the Mayer-Vietoris spectral sequence for $H^{*}(X_{0})$ shows that $H^{4}(X_{0}) = \mathbb{Z}^{f}$.
We shall deduce the statement about the rank of $H_{3}(X_{0})$ in §7 via the Clemens-Schmid exact sequence, in the section on automorphisms (Proposition (7.2)); the argument there is logically independent of what follows.
If the rank of $H_{3}(X_{0}) = \dim H^{3}(X_{0};\mathbb{C}) = 0$, it follows then that $H_{3}(X_{0};\mathbb{Z}) = 0$.

Via the exact sequence (4.12) and (4.10), it is easy to see that the image of the fundamental class $[V_i] \in H_1(X_0)$ is $\xi_i$.
On the other hand, from

$$
L H ^ {0} \left(X _ {0}\right) = \mathbb {Z} \xrightarrow {0} \mathbb {Z} \xrightarrow {\sim} H ^ {1} \left(X ^ {*}\right)\rightarrow 0,
$$

and the fact that $\Sigma \xi_{i} = 0$, the kernel of the map $H_{4}(X_{0})\to H^{2}(X^{0})$ is precisely $\Sigma [V_i]$ (which is a primitive vector).

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Since $H^{2}(X_{0})$ maps onto $H^{2}(X^{*})$, the following is exact over $\mathbb{Z}$:

$$
0 \rightarrow \bigoplus_{i} \mathbb{Z} \xi_{i} / (\Sigma \xi_{i}) \rightarrow H^{2}(X_{0}; \mathbb{Z}) \rightarrow L H^{2}(X_{0}) \xrightarrow{N} L H^{2}(X_{0}), \tag{4.13}
$$

where the replacement of $I - T$ by $N$ is justified since they have the same kernel over $\mathbb{Q}$ and $LH^{2}(X_{0})$ is torsion free, so that the kernel of either $I - T$ or $N$ is automatically primitive.

We now consider the deformation theory of $X_0$.
The classes $\xi_1, \ldots, \xi_f \in \operatorname{Pic}(X_0)$ satisfy the single relation $\Sigma \xi_i = 0$.
As they satisfy no additional relations in $H^2(X_0)$, they generate a subspace of $\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)$ of rank $f - 1$.
In particular, looking at $\operatorname{Hom}(L, \mathbb{C}^*)$ as in §3 and using (3.1) and (3.7), it is $(f - 1)$ conditions on the versal locally trivial deformation of $X_0$ to keep $\xi_1, \ldots, \xi_f$ Cartier.
An easy argument shows, however, that all the $\xi_i$ are Cartier $\Leftrightarrow X_0$ is $d$-semistable.

Alternatively, note that $X_0$ $d$-semistable $\Rightarrow$ all the $\xi_i$ are Cartier.
Moreover, by [7] (5.10), the subspace of the versal locally trivial deformation of $X_0$ consisting of those deformations for which $X_0$ remains $d$-semistable has codimension $= \dim \operatorname{Pic}((X_0)_{\mathrm{ling}})$.
But, using the resolution $(D = (X_0)_{\mathrm{ling}}, e = \# \text{ of double curves}, v = \# \text{ of triple points})$

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{D}^{*} \rightarrow \hat{\mathcal{C}}_{D}^{*} \rightarrow \hat{\mathcal{C}}_{D}^{*} / \mathcal{C}_{D}^{*} \rightarrow 0
$$

and the fact that, at a triple point $p$, $\hat{\mathcal{C}}_D^* / \mathcal{C}_D^* \cong (\mathbb{C}_p^*)^2$, we obtain

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\dim \operatorname{Pic}(D) = 2v - e + 1 = 2v - e + 2 - 1 \\
= 2v - e + (v - e + f) - 1 = 3v - 2e + f - 1 = f - 1,
\end{array}
$$

via Euler's theorem $v - e + f = 2$ and the relation $3v = 2e$.
Hence, the locus where the $\xi_i$ are Cartier is precisely the locus where $X_0$ remains $d$-semistable.

Replacing $\operatorname{Hom}(L, \mathbb{C}^*)$ by the subtorus $Z_1 = \{\psi \in \operatorname{Hom}(L, \mathbb{C}^*): \psi(\xi_i) = 1 \text{ for all } i\}$, we obtain the following corollary of (4.1)

(4.14) Proposition.
If $z \in Z_1$, then there is a deformation $\pi: X \to S$ as in (4.1), satisfying all of the conclusions of (4.1) and such that $X_s$ is in addition $d$-semistable for all $s \in S$.

(4.15) Remark.
Let $(V, D)$ be an anticanonical pair.
Then a surjectivity result on the periods of $H^2(V - D)$, with its natural mixed Hodge structure, along the lines of (4.1) is also true.
The proof is simpler than that of (4.1).
This remark has applications to the study of smoothings of cusp singularities.

Finally, we need a result on the mixed Hodge structure of $LH^{2}(X_{0})$ itself, not just on that of $H^{2}(X_{0})$.

(4.16) Proposition.
The mixed Hodge structure on $H^{2}(X_{0})$ determines the mixed Hodge structure on $LH^{2}(X_{0})$, modulo the nilpotent orbit $\{\exp (\lambda N): \lambda \in \mathbb{C}\}$.

Proof.
The natural map $\alpha \colon H^{2}(X_{0}) \to LH^{2}(X_{0})$ is a morphism of mixed Hodge structures.
In particular,

$$
\alpha \left(F^{1} H^{2}(X_{0})\right) \subseteq F^{1} L H^{2}(X_{0}).
$$

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

Let $A = \alpha (F^{1}H^{2}(X_{0}))^{\perp}$ in $LH^{2}(X_{0})$.
Since $\alpha (H^{2}(X_{0}))$ has codimension 2 in $LH^{2}(X_{0})$, $A$ has dimension three in $LH^{2}(X_{0})$.
The choice of a line $F^{2} = \mathbb{C}v$ in $A$ is a choice on the conic $v^{2} = 0$ in $\mathbf{P}(A)$.
There is an additional non-degeneracy condition $F^{2} \cap W_{2}LH^{2}(X_{0}) = 0$ which in this case simply means $F^{2}$ does not lie on the points of intersection of the conic $\{v^{2} = 0\}$ with the line $\mathbf{P}(A \cap W_{2}LH^{2}(X_{0})) \subseteq \mathbf{P}(A)$.
Hence $\{F^{2}\}$ is isomorphic to a conic minus one or two points, i.e. either $\mathbb{C}$ or $\mathbb{C}^*$.

A straightforward argument shows that the additive group

$$
\{\exp (\lambda N): \lambda \in \mathbb {C} \}
$$

operates freely on the set of all $F^2$'s.
This gives rise to an inclusion $j\colon \mathbb{C}\to \mathbb{C}$ or $\mathbb{C}\subseteq \mathbb{C}^*$.
Since any nonconstant morphism of $\mathbb{C}$ to itself is surjective, the set of $F^2$'s is $\cong \mathbb{C}$ and $\{\exp (\lambda N)\colon \lambda \in \mathbb{C}\}$ operates transitively on it.
Hence, all possible choices of $F^2$ yield the same nilpotent orbit of mixed Hodge structures, q.e.d.

## §5.

Polarized mixed Hodge structures

We begin by describing briefly certain partial compactifications of polarized period space $\Gamma \backslash \mathfrak{D}$ along the lines of [1].

Let $\varLambda$ be as in §1 and fix a primitive element $h \in \varLambda$ with $h^2 = 2k &gt; 2$.
Let $\varLambda_0 = \{h\}^\perp \subseteq \varLambda$.
Define

$$
\varLambda_{\mathbb{C}} = \varLambda_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{C},\quad (\varLambda_{0})_{\mathbb{C}} = (\varLambda_{0})_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{C}.
$$

Let $\mathfrak{D} =$ the set of lines $F^2 = \mathbb{C} \cdot v \subseteq (\varLambda_0)_{\mathbb{C}}$ such that

(I) $v^{2} = 0$
(II) $v\cdot \bar{v} &gt;0$

We shall view the points of $\mathfrak{D}$ either as lines or as filtrations

$$
F ^ {\prime} = \left\{F ^ {2} \subseteq F ^ {1} = \left(F ^ {2}\right) ^ {\perp} \subseteq F ^ {0} = \left(\Lambda_ {0}\right) _ {\mathbb {C}} \right\}.
$$

Therefore, $\mathfrak{D}$ is the set of Hodge filtrations on $\varLambda_{\mathbb{C}}$ for which $h$ is of Type (1, 1); it is a Hermitian symmetric space for $G_{\mathbb{R}} = SO(2,19)_{\mathbb{R}}$, and its compact dual $\mathfrak{D}$, the set of lines $F^2$ satisfying (I) only, is a compact Hermitian symmetric space for $G_{\mathbb{C}} = SO(2,19)_{\mathbb{C}}$.
Define

$$
\Gamma = \{\gamma \in O (\Lambda) _ {\mathbb {Z}}: \gamma (h) = h \}.
$$

Then $\Gamma \backslash \mathfrak{D}$ is the classifying space for periods of K3 surfaces having a primitive polarization of degree $2k$.

Assume in addition that $N$ is a primitive integral matrix with $N^3 = 0$, $N^2 \neq 0$, and such that $N$ is skew with respect to the intersection pairing.
Let $W_*$ be the weight filtration induced on $\Lambda$ by $N$.
An easy argument shows (with the notations of §1 and using (1.1))

(5.1) Lemma.
$N(h) = 0 \Leftrightarrow h \in W_2$ and $h \cdot \delta = 0$.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

We assume that either of the conditions of (5.1) is satisfied.
Then $N$ induces a nilpotent endomorphism of $\Lambda_0$ with weight filtration $W_{\bullet} \cap \Lambda_0$.
For the remainder of this section, $W_{\bullet}$ will refer to the induced filtration on $\Lambda_0$, and $N$ will arise as in §1.
This will simply mean that, in the language of §1, $\delta^2 &gt; 0$ and that $N$ is integral.

Let

$$
T = \exp (N),
$$

$$
U _ {\mathbb {C}} = \left\{\exp (\lambda N): \lambda \in \mathbb {C} \right\} \quad \text{and} \quad U _ {\mathbb {Z}} = U _ {\mathbb {C}} \cap \Gamma ;
$$

note that $U_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is generated by $T \Leftrightarrow N$ is a primitive matrix.
Also, $U_{\mathbb{Z}} \subseteq \Gamma$ and $U_{\mathbb{C}} \subseteq G_{\mathbb{C}}$.
In particular, the set

$$
\tilde {\mathfrak {D}} = U _ {\mathbb {C}} \cdot \mathfrak {D} \subseteq \tilde {\mathfrak {D}}
$$

has meaning, and $U_{\mathbb{C}}$ acts on $\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}$

(5.2) Lemma.
$\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}$ is the set of filtrations $F' \in \tilde{\mathfrak{D}}$ such that $(F', W_{\bullet})$ is a mixed Hodge structure on $(\Lambda_0)_{\mathbb{C}}$.
Moreover, $U_{\mathbb{C}}$ acts freely on $\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}$, so that $\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}$ is a principal $U_{\mathbb{C}}$ bundle over $\mathfrak{D}' = \tilde{\mathfrak{D}} / U_{\mathbb{C}}$.

Proof.
The proof is essentially contained in [1] and [24] and is, in any case, straightforward.

We now sketch the toroidal construction briefly [1].
The inclusion

$$
U _ {\mathbb {Z}} \backslash \mathfrak {D} \subseteq U _ {\mathbb {Z}} \backslash \tilde {\mathfrak {D}}
$$

realizes $U_{\mathbb{Z}} \backslash \mathfrak{D}$ as an open subset of $U_{\mathbb{Z}} \backslash \tilde{\mathfrak{D}}$, a principal bundle over $\mathfrak{D}'$ with structure group

$$
T = U _ {\mathbb {Z}} \backslash U _ {\mathbb {C}} = \mathbb {C} / \mathbb {Z} = \mathbb {C} ^ {*},
$$

a one-dimensional algebraic torus.
For appropriate choice of a partial compactification $\mathbb{C}^* \subseteq \mathbb{C}$, we obtain a partial compactification $\widetilde{T}$ of $T$, and the action of $T$ on itself by multiplication extends to an action of $T$ on $\widetilde{T}$.
Define

$$
\tilde {\mathfrak {D}} ^ {\prime \prime} = \left(U _ {\mathbb {Z}} \backslash \tilde {\mathfrak {D}}\right) \stackrel {T} {\times} \tilde {T}
$$

(associated $T$-bundle) and let $\mathfrak{D}''$ be the interior of the closure of $U_{\mathbb{Z}} \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ in $\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}''$.
Then $\mathfrak{D}''$ is set-theoretically essentially $(U_{\mathbb{Z}} \setminus \mathfrak{D}) \cup \mathfrak{D}'$, where $\mathfrak{D}'$ is identified with the smooth divisor $(U_{\mathbb{Z}} \setminus \tilde{\mathfrak{D}}) \stackrel{\mathrm{T}}{\times} \{0\}$ in $\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}''$.

There is an appropriate arithmetic group $\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ which acts on $\mathfrak{D}''$ (essentially, those elements of $\Gamma$ which preserve $N$), although we do not need to know the exact nature of $\Gamma_0$.
We may then glue $\Gamma_0 \setminus \mathfrak{D}''$ to $\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ in the natural way, by identifying along the image of $\mathfrak{D}$ in each one.
Call the result $\overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$.
Then:

(5.3) Theorem.

1. $\overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$ is an algebraic space and a separated $V$-manifold.

2) $\Gamma \backslash \mathfrak{D}$ is a Zariski open subset of $\overline{\Gamma \backslash \mathfrak{D}}$.

3) $\overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}} - \Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ is, modulo finite group actions, a smooth divisor.

4) If $f \colon \Delta^n \times \Delta^* \to \Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ is a locally liftable map with unipotent monodromy whose logarithm is conjugate, via $\Gamma$, to $kN$, then $f$ extends to a

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

map $\bar{f}\colon \varDelta^{n + 1}\to \overline{\varGamma\setminus\mathfrak{D}}$ and $\bar{f} (x,0)$ is the limiting mixed Hodge structure of Schmid [24].

5. In the situation of 4), if $N$ is a primitive integral matrix, the induced map on normal spaces

$$
\left(\bar {f}\right) _ {\Phi}: N _ {\Delta^ {n} \times \{0 \} \subseteq \Delta^ {n + 1}} \rightarrow N _ {\overline {{\Gamma \mathfrak {D}}} - \Gamma \backslash \mathfrak {D} \subset \overline {{\Gamma \mathfrak {D}}}}
$$

is an isomorphism at a general point of $\Delta^n\times \{0\} \Leftrightarrow k = 1$

The proof of (5.3) may be found in [1] and [24].
The point of 5) is that $k$ measures how many times $f_{\Phi}(\gamma)$ turns around $\overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}} - \Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ , where $\gamma$ is a loop in $\Delta^{n+1}$ which turns once around $\{z_{n+1} = 0\}$ .

(5.4) Lemma.
Let $\varphi \in \Gamma$ be an integral automorphism of the polarized mixed Hodge structure $(F', W)$ , where $F' \in \mathfrak{D}$ .
If $F'$ is general and $\varphi$ is of finite order, $\varphi = \operatorname{Id}$ .

Proof.
By hypothesis, $\varphi$ induces an automorphism of the mixed Hodge structure on $W_{2}$ .
As $W_{2}$ is an extension of Hodge structures via the exact sequence

$$
0 \to W _ {0} \to W _ {2} \to W _ {2} / W _ {0} \to 0,
$$

exactly as in §3, $W_{2}$ is classified by a (functorial) extension homomorphism $\psi \colon L_{0} = (W_{2} / W_{0})_{\mathbb{Z}} \to J^{0}W_{0} = \mathbb{C}^{*}$ .
If $\psi$ is general, we may assume $\psi$ is an embedding.
As the only automorphisms of $\mathbb{C}^*$ are $\zeta \mapsto \zeta^{\pm 1}$ , and by the commutativity of

![img-13.jpeg](img-13.jpeg)

either $\varphi = \mathrm{Id}$ on $W_{0}$ and $W_{2} / W_{0}$ and hence on $W_{4} / W_{2}$ , since this is paired with $W_{0}$ via cup product, or $\varphi = -\mathrm{Id}$ on $W_{0}$ , $W_{2} / W_{0}$ , and $W_{4} / W_{2}$ .
As $\varphi$ is semi-simple, $\varphi = \pm \mathrm{Id}$ on $W_{4} = \varLambda_{0}$ .
But $\varphi$ is the restriction of some $\tilde{\varphi}$ on $\varLambda$ with $\tilde{\varphi}(h) = h$ .
By [8] (1.4), either $\varphi = \mathrm{Id}$ or $h^{2} = 2$ .
As we have assumed $h^{2} = 2k &gt; 2$ , $\varphi = \mathrm{Id}$ , q.e.d.

The final topic in this section is the extension of the period map.
Let $X_0$ be a $d$ -semistable K3 surface with $N =$ the logarithm of the monodromy on $LH^2(X_0)$ .
Let

$$
h \in W _ {2} H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right) / W _ {0} H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0}; \mathbb {Z}\right) = L
$$

with $\psi(h) = 1 \in \mathbb{C}^*$ (notation as in §3).
Then there is a unique $H \in \operatorname{Pic}(X_0)$ inducing $h$ , by (3.1), and by (3.2) there is a unique element in $H^2(X_0; \mathbb{Z}) \cap F^1H^2(X_0)$ , which we shall again denote by $h$ , which comes from $H$ and induces $h$ on $L$ .
From the Clemens-Schmid exact sequence (4.13), $h \in \ker N$ via the natural map $\alpha: H^2(X_0) \to LH^2(X_0)$ .

(5.5) Lemma.
The smoothing component of the polarized versal deformation space of the pair $(X_0, H)$ is smooth of dimension 19. The locally trivial $d$ -semistable polarized deformations of $(X_0, H)$ form a smooth divisor in the smoothing component.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Proof.
Without the statement on polarizations, (5.5) is [7] (5.10).
To keep the polarization $H$, we argue as in [8] (4.3): Yoneda pairing

$$
\operatorname{Ext}^{1}\left(\Omega_{X_{0}}^{1}; \mathcal{C}_{X_{0}}\right) \otimes H^{1}\left(X_{0}; \Omega_{X_{0}}^{1}\right) \to H^{2}\left(\mathcal{C}_{X_{0}}\right) = \mathfrak{C}
$$

is a perfect pairing, as it is dual to cup product

$$
H^{0}\left(X_{0}; \mathcal{C}_{X_{0}}\right) \otimes H^{1}\left(X_{0}; \Omega_{X_{0}}^{1}\right) \to H^{1}\left(X_{0}; \Omega_{X_{0}}^{1}\right)
$$

via Serre duality and since $\omega_{X_0} = \mathcal{C}_{X_0}$.

The rest of the argument goes as in [8] (4.3).

We sketch another argument.
Let $V$ be a germ of the smoothing component of the (unpolarized) versal deformation of $X_0$, $D \subseteq V$ the smooth divisor of locally trivial $d$-semistable deformations of $X_0$, and $\pi \colon \mathcal{X} \to V$ the versal deformation itself.

Choose a local section $\omega$ of $R^0 \pi_* \omega_{\mathcal{X}/V}$.
Let $h \in H^2(X_0)$ be as constructed in (3.2).
Since $h$ is invariant under monodromy on $LH^2(X_0)$, $h$ extends to a cohomology class on $\mathcal{X}$ (possibly after shrinking $V$), say $\tilde{h}$.
More precisely, $\tilde{h}$ is naturally a section of $R^2 \pi_* \mathfrak{C}$.
We can pair $\tilde{h}$ and $\omega$:

$$
\langle \omega, \tilde{h} \rangle \in R^{0} \pi_* \omega_{\mathcal{X}/V} \otimes R^{2} \pi_* \mathfrak{C} \to R^{2} \pi_* \omega_{\mathcal{X}/V} = \mathcal{C}_{V},
$$

using $R^2 \pi_* \mathfrak{C} \to R^2 \pi_* \mathcal{C}_{\mathcal{X}}$ and duality.

An argument along the lines of the proof of (3.1) but replacing $X_0$ by $\mathcal{X}$ and using the Leray spectral sequence shows that $H$ extends to a line bundle on the locus $\{\langle \omega, \tilde{h} \rangle = 0\} \subseteq V$.
An easy local calculation shows that $\{\langle \omega, \tilde{h} \rangle = 0\}$ is smooth and meets $D$ transversally, q.e.d.

Let $\widetilde{U}$ denote a germ of the smoothing component of the polarized versal deformation space of $(X_0, H)$, and $U \subseteq \widetilde{U}$ be the open set corresponding to smooth K3 surfaces with a primitive polarization of degree $2k$.
Thus, we have the period map $\eta: U \to \Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$.

(5.6) Lemma.
$\eta$ extends to a map $\bar{\eta} \colon \widetilde{U} \to \overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$.

Proof.
This follows from (5.3.4).

In the next section, we will also use the following variant of the global Torelli theorem for polarized K3 surfaces [22, 17, 8].

(5.7) Theorem.
Via the period map, the space $\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ is a coarse moduli space for polarized K3 surfaces $(S,h)$, where $h \in \operatorname{Pic}(S)$ is a primitive polarization of degree $2k$.

§6.
Proof of Theorem (0.6)

In this section, we will present the proof of Theorem (0.6).
We begin with the proof of (0.6.2):

(6.1) Theorem.
Let $X_0$ and $Y_0$ be two primitive $d$-semistable Type III K3 surfaces with $t(X_0) = t(Y_0)$.
Then there exists a locally trivial family $\pi: X \to S$ of $d$

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

semistable varieties over a smooth connected base $S$ and points $0, s \in S$ with $\pi^{-1}(0) \cong X_0$, and a sequence of abstract Type I and II modifications

$$
X_s \xrightarrow{\sim} Y_0.
$$

Proof.
The proof will occupy most of this section.
We begin by noting that it suffices to prove the existence of two locally trivial families of $d$-semistable varieties $\pi: X \to S$ and $\pi': Y \to S'$, with $S, S'$ smooth and connected, and pairs of points $0, s \in S$ and $0, t \in S'$, such that there exists a sequence of abstract modifications of Types I and II

$$
X_s \xrightarrow{\sim} Y_t.
$$

Indeed, this result implies (6.1) immediately via the stability of exceptional curves and induction on the number of operations of Types I and II used in the modification $X_s \dashrightarrow Y_t$.

Next, under the hypothesis of (6.1) and using the arithmetic classification of the monodromy (1.7), there is an integral isomorphism

$$
\begin{array}{c}
\varphi \colon LH^2(X_0) \xrightarrow{\sim} LH^2(Y_0) \\
\Big\| \Big\| \\
\varLambda \varLambda
\end{array}
$$

such that $\varphi \circ N = N \circ \varphi$.
We will always use $\varphi$ to identify $LH^2(X_0)$ and $LH^2(Y_0)$.
Choose a primitive $h \in \varLambda$ with $h^2 = 2k &gt; 2$.
Via $\varphi$ we will view $h$ either as a class in $LH^2(X_0)$ or in $LH^2(Y_0)$.
We may suppose that $Nh = 0$.

By the surjectivity of the period map (4.1), we may deform $X_0$ as in the statement of (6.1) to some $d$-semistable variety $X_s$ with $\psi(h) = 1$, in the notation of §3.
We may further assume that, subject to the condition $\psi(h) = 1$, the mixed Hodge structure of $X_s$ is suitably generic; in particular, by (5.4), we may assume that the limiting mixed Hodge structure of $X_s$ has no automorphisms.
Deform $Y_0$ as well in a locally trivial family $T$ of $d$-semistable varieties over the smooth, connected base $T$, to some $Y_t$ which has the same limiting mixed Hodge structure as $X_s$; this is again possible by (4.1).
We will show that $Y_t$ may be obtained from $X_s$ via Type I and II modifications.
For simplicity, relabel $X_s = X_0$, $Y_t = Y_0$.

Referring to (5.6), let $\widetilde{U}$ be a germ of the smoothing component of the polarized versal deformation space of $(X_0, H)$, where $H \in \operatorname{Pic}(X_0)$ is the Cartier divisor corresponding to $h$ and

$$
\tilde{\eta} \colon \widetilde{U} \to \overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}
$$

the extended period map.

(6.2) Lemma.
$\tilde{\eta}$ is étale.

Proof.
It suffices to check (6.2) at points of $\widetilde{U} - U$, where $U \subseteq \widetilde{U}$ is the locus of smooth K3 surfaces.
At a point of $\widetilde{U} - U$, the differential $(\tilde{\eta})_*$ is nonzero in directions tangent to $\widetilde{U} - U$, by (3.7), and $\tilde{\eta}$ is unramified along $\widetilde{U} - U$ by (5.3.5) and the assumption of primitivity, q.e.d.

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

Let $v \colon \mathcal{X} \to \widetilde{U}$ be the polarized family of K3 surfaces above $\widetilde{U}$ , with $\mathcal{L}$ the corresponding line bundle.
By Shepherd-Barron's results [25] Theorem 1, we may, after Type 0, I and II modifications, assume that $\mathcal{L}|X_0$ is numerically effective.
After possibly shrinking $\widetilde{U}$ , we may further assume that $\mathcal{L}$ is numerically effective.
By [25] Theorem 2, if $n \geq 4$ , $R^0 v_*\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ generates $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ at all points and defines a contraction

![img-14.jpeg](img-14.jpeg)

where, over $U$ , $\bar{\mathcal{X}} = \mathcal{X}$ except possibly for contracting some smooth rational curves in the fibers $X_{u}$ .
Hence, $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ defines a holomorphic map (after trivializing $R^0 v_*\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ )

$$
w \colon \widetilde {U} \to \operatorname {H i l b} (\mathbf {P} ^ {M})
$$

for an appropriate $M$ .
Let $0 \in \widetilde{U}$ be the point corresponding to $X_0$ and $p = w(0) \in \mathrm{Hilb}(\mathbb{P}^M)$ .
Since $\mathrm{Hilb}(\mathbb{P}^M)$ is a scheme, we may write an affine neighborhood of $p$ in $\mathrm{Hilb}(\mathbb{P}^M)$ as $\operatorname{Spec} R_0$ for some ring $R$ , finitely generated over $\mathbb{C}$ .
Let $\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U},0}$ be the local ring of holomorphic functions at $0$ and $i: R_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U},0}$ the natural homomorphism.
If $R = i(R_0)$ is the image, $R$ is again finitely generated over $\mathbb{C}$ , and we let $V = \operatorname{Spec} R$ .
Hence, we have a diagram (of analytic spaces)

![img-15.jpeg](img-15.jpeg)

Here, $\mu$ is a rational, generically finite map.
Choose a curve $C_0 \subseteq \overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$ and a point $p_0 \in C_0$ such that $C_0$ meets $\overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}} - \Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}$ transversally at $p_0 = \bar{\eta}(X_0, H)$ ; this is possible if $\bar{\eta}(X_0, H)$ is chosen sufficiently general, as $\overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$ is an algebraic space, hence isomorphic to a quasi-projective variety after removing a subset of codimension two.
By hypothesis, $\bar{\eta}^{-1}(C_0)$ meets $\tilde{U} - U$ transversally near $X_0$ .

By lifting $C_0$ to the scheme $V$ , we may thus find an algebraic space $X$ and a map $\pi_1 \colon X \to C_1$ such that

(6.3.1) $\pi_1\colon X\to C_1$ has generic fiber a K3 surface.
(6.3.2) The period map induces a finite cover $f_1 \colon C_1 \to C_0$ which is unramified at some point $p_1 \in f_1^{-1}(p_0)$ .
(6.3.3) $X_{p_1} \cong X_0$ and $X$ is smooth in a neighborhood of $X_{p_1}$ .

Indeed, the only point to check is (6.3.3), which follows from the deformation theory [7] (2.5).

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

Repeating the above construction with $Y_0$ , we obtain a curve $C_2$ and a family $\pi_2 \colon Y \to C_2$ satisfying (6.3.1)-(6.3.3).
The picture is as follows:

![img-16.jpeg](img-16.jpeg)

Let $\tilde{C} = C_1 \times_{C_0} C_2$ and let $C$ be the component of $\tilde{C}$ containing $(p_1, p_2)$ ; note that $\tilde{C}$ is already smooth at $(p_1, p_2)$ since $f_i$ is étale at $p_i$ .

Finally, then, we have two families of algebraic K3 surfaces, which we will again denote by $\pi_i$ :

![img-17.jpeg](img-17.jpeg)

where $f \colon C \to C_0$ is induced from the period map of either family.
We would like to conclude that $X$ and $Y$ are birational, using (5.7).

To this end, let $k(C)$ be the function field of $C$ , and view $X$ and $Y$ as K3 surfaces over $k(C)$ :

$$
X _ {k (C)} = X \times_ {C} \operatorname {S p e c} k (C)
$$

$$
Y _ {k (C)} = Y \times_ {C} \operatorname {S p e c} k (C)
$$

via the inclusion $\operatorname{Spec} k(C) \subseteq C$ .
Note that $X_{k(C)}$ has a polarization $H_1$ , and $Y_{k(C)}$ has a polarization $H_2$ .
By construction, the two $k(C)$ -valued points of $\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D} \times_{\operatorname{Spec} \mathfrak{C}} k(C)$ corresponding to $(X_{k(C)}, H_1)$ and $(Y_{k(C)}, H_2)$ are the same.

By global Torelli (5.7), then, $X_{k(C)}$ and $Y_{k(C)}$ are isomorphic over $k(C)$ , by an isomorphism sending $H_1$ to $H_2$ .
Now if we knew that they were isomorphic over $k(C)$ , we would have a birational morphism $X \to Y$ and a commutative diagram

![img-18.jpeg](img-18.jpeg)

Therefore, by [25] (3.1) (which is a corollary of Shepherd-Barron's results on extending polarizations), near $p = (p_1, p_2) \in C$ , $Y$ would be obtained from $X$ by a sequence of modifications of Types 0, I and II, which would prove (6.1).

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

By standard descent theory, to descend the isomorphism over $\overline{k(C)}$ to one over $k(C)$, it suffices to prove that

$$
\operatorname{Aut}\left(X_{\overline{k(C)}}, \bar{H}_1\right) = \{\operatorname{Id}\},
$$

where $\bar{H}_1$ is the line bundle induced on $X_{\overline{k(C)}}$ by $H_1$ (see e.g. [13] B.I.
Théorème 2).
Thus, to prove (6.1), we are reduced to proving

(6.4) Lemma.
If the mixed Hodge structure $LH^2(X_0)$ has no integral automorphisms, then $\operatorname{Aut}(X_{\overline{k(C)}}, \bar{H}_1) = \{\operatorname{Id}\}$.

Proof.
The hypothesis of (6.4) is insensitive to replacing $C$ by a finite cover.
Since any automorphism of $X_{\overline{k(C)}}$ is actually definable over some finite extension of $k(C)$, it suffices to prove (6.4) after replacing $X_{\overline{k(C)}}$ by $X_{k(C)}$.

Let $\alpha \in \operatorname{Aut}(X_{k(C)}, H_1)$, and suppose $\alpha \neq \operatorname{Id}$.
In any case, $\alpha$ is of finite order as it preserves a polarization.
Let $C^0$ be a Zariski open subset of $C$ where there exists a model $\pi: X^0 \to C^0$ such that $\alpha$ is actually a morphism on $X^0$; we may suppose in addition that $\pi$ is smooth over $C^0$.
Thus $\alpha$ acts on $R^2 \pi_*\mathbb{Z}$ and on all Hodge bundles and on their canonical extensions, in the sense of regular singular points.
Moreover, by [17] (7.5) or [22] §2, Prop.
2, if $\alpha \neq \operatorname{Id}$, $\alpha$ must act nontrivially on some fiber of $R^2 \pi_*\mathbb{Z}$ and hence on all fibers; in particular, $\alpha$ acts nontrivially on $R^2 \pi_* \mathbb{C} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C$.
Hence, $\alpha$ acts nontrivially on the Hodge structure $LH^2(X_0)$, and is integral because it comes from the action on $R^2 \pi_* \mathbb{Z}$, a contradiction, q.e.d.

We have proved (6.1).

We next prove a partial statement along the lines of (0.6.3).
We need the extra hypothesis as we have not yet proved (0.6.1).

(6.5) Theorem.
Let $X_0$ be a Type III K3 surface of index $k$.
Suppose that there exists a primitive Type III K3 surface in minus-one-form, $Y_0$, with $t(X_0) = k^2 t(Y_0)$.
Then, after Type I and II modifications, $X_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} X_0'$ where $X_0'$ is in minus-one-form and has special $k$-bands of hexagons.

Proof.
The argument will be very similar to that of (6.1), and we shall be very sketchy.
As in the proof of (6.1), deform $X_0$ until it acquires a polarization $h$, $h^2 = 2k &gt; 2$, and such that the limiting mixed Hodge structure is suitably general subject to $\psi(h) = 1$.
If $\bar{U}$ denotes the smoothing component of the polarized versal deformation of $X_0$, we again get a map $\bar{U} \to \overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$.

By hypothesis, there is an integral isometry $\varphi \colon LH^2(X_0) \xrightarrow{\sim} LH^2(Y_0)$ such that $\varphi \circ N = (kN) \circ \varphi$, where the left hand $N$ is on $LH^2(X_0)$ and the right hand $N$ is on $LH^2(Y_0)$.
Identifying $LH^2(X_0)$ and $LH^2(Y_0)$ via $\varphi$, deform $Y_0$ until it has the same limiting mixed Hodge structure as $X_0$.
Via the arguments of (6.1), there exists an algebraic curve $C$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to \overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$ and two algebraic spaces $\pi_1 \colon X \to C$, $\pi_2 \colon Y \to C$ satisfying:

1. there exists a smooth point $p \in C$ with $\pi_1^{-1}(p) = X_0$ and $\pi_2^{-1}(p) = Y_0$;
2. the generic fiber of $\pi_i$ is a smooth K3 surface and the period map from either $X$ or $Y$ coincides with $f$;

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

3. $X$ is smooth near $\pi_1^{-1}(p)$, and, locally around $\pi_2^{-1}(p)$, $Y$ has the form

$$
\{x y z = t ^ {k} \},
$$

where $t$ is a local coordinate on $C$ at $p$.

Here, the only additional comment to be made is on 3).
The point is that the map $\widetilde{U} \to \overline{\Gamma \setminus \mathfrak{D}}$ is ramified to order $k$ along $\widetilde{U} - U$, whereas the map from the smoothing component of the polarized versal deformation space for $Y_0$ is étale.
Hence, when we perform the construction of (6.1), the family $Y \to C$ induces a map of a neighborhood of $p \in C$ to the versal deformation of $Y_0$ which is tangent to order $k$ to the discriminant locus (or ramified of order $k$); hence, we obtain the local description of 3).

As in the proof of (6.1), $X$ and $Y$ are birational over $k(C)$.
Finally, use [9] (1.2) to resolve the singularities of $Y$ near $\pi_2^{-1}(p)$; this gives a semistable degeneration $\tilde{Y}$ (near $p$) with central fiber $\tilde{Y}_0$ with special $k$-bands of hexagons as in [9] (1.2.2) and in minus-one-form by [9].
The argument concludes by [25] (3.1) as before, q.e.d.

Thus, to complete the proof of (0.6), it suffices to prove (0.6.1).

(6.6) Theorem.
Primitive Type III K3 surfaces $X_0$ exist for every value of $t = t(X_0) \in 2\mathbb{Z}^+$; moreover, $X_0$ may be assumed to be in minus-one-form.

Proof.
The proof is by induction on $t$ and uses the models described in (2.7).
For $t = 2$, (6.6) is obvious by (2.5) and (2.6).
For $t &gt; 2$, we let $X_0$ be as constructed in §2, with $t(X_0) = t$.
Suppose that $X_0$ has index $l &gt; 1$, so that $l^2$ divides $t$.
By induction, there exists $Y_0$ which is primitive and in minus-one-form with $t = l^2 t(Y_0)$.
By (6.5), $X_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} X_0'$ where $X_0'$ has special $l$-bands of hexagons.
By the main theorem of [9], $X_0'$ has special $l$-bands of hexagons $\Leftrightarrow X_0'$ has special $\mu_l$ action $\Leftrightarrow X_0$ has special $\mu_l$ action $\Leftrightarrow X_0$ has special $l$-bands of hexagons, which is a contradiction.

We have thus proved (0.6).

§ 7. Applications

(1) Calculation of $(k, t)$

We complete the description of the monodromy $N$ by proving the following result, valid for any $X_0$:

(7.1) Proposition.
Let $X_0$ be a $d$-semistable Type III K3 surface.
Then $t(X_0)$ is the number of triple points of $X_0$.

Proof.
Assume first that $X_0$ is primitive.
By (6.1), $X_0$ is birational, via a sequence of abstract Type I and II modifications, to $X_0'$, where $X_0'$ is as described in §2.
In particular, by (2.6), $t(X_0') = \text{the number of triple points of } X_0'$.
Since both numbers are invariant under Type I and II modifications, the same is true for $X_0$.

In general, if $X_0$ is the abstract base change of order $k$ of $\widetilde{X}_0$ (i.e., the dual graph of $X_0$ is a refinement of that of $\widetilde{X}_0$ as described in [9] (3.3)), then

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

$$
t(X_0) = k^2 t(\bar{X}_0)
$$

and the number of triple points of $X_0$ equals $k^2$ times the number of triple points of $\bar{X}_0$.
(7.1) now follows from (0.6.3).

The number $k$ may also be calculated from $X_0$, using [9] (3.4).
If we use the algorithm of Miranda-Morrison to put $X_0$ in minus-one-form, $k$ is simply the largest integer such that $X_0$ has special $k$-bands of hexagons, and may be determined immediately from the dual graph.

## (2) Non-existence of vector fields

Let $T_{X_0}^0$ be the sheaf of derivations of $X_0$.
By Serre duality and [7] (2.10),

$$
H^0(T_{X_0}^0)^* \cong H^2(\Omega_{X_0}^1/\tau_{X_0}^1).
$$

Using the exact sequence (3.5)

$$
0 \to \Omega_{X_0}^1/\tau_{X_0}^1 \to (a_0)_* \Omega_{X_0[0]}^1 \to (a_1)_* \Omega_{X_0[1]}^1 \to 0,
$$

we obtain

$$
H^2(\Omega_{X_0}^1/\tau_{X_0}^1) \cong \operatorname{coker}(H^2(X_0^{[0]};\mathbb{C}) \to H^2(X_0^{[1]};\mathbb{C})).
$$

In particular, $H^0(T_{X_0}^0) = 0 \Leftrightarrow$ the above cokernel is 0, a fact which was mentioned in §4.
In [9], it was shown that $H^0(T_{X_0}^0) = 0$ via the minus-one-theorem.
We give another proof, based on the ideas of §4; the reader can easily check that the argument is not circular.

## (7.2) Proposition.

The natural map $H^2(X_0^{[0]};\mathbb{C}) \to H^2(X_0^{[1]};\mathbb{C})$ is surjective.

**Proof.** The statement is purely topological, and hence we may prove it after deforming $X_0$ in a locally trivial (connected) family.
Thus, we may assume, using (4.1) and the argument preceding (4.14), that $X_0$ is polarized and $d$-semistable.
Hence, arguing as in (5.5), there exists a smoothing of $X_0$, say $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$, which is semistable and polarized.
In particular, we may apply the Clemens-Schmid exact sequence to $X$: the sequence

$$
0 \to LH^0(X_0) \to H_4(X_0) \to H^2(X_0) \to LH^2(X_0) \xrightarrow{N} LH^2(X_0)
$$

is exact over $\mathbb{Q}$.
Since $\dim \operatorname{Im} N = 2$, by the results of §1, and $\dim H_4(X_0) = f = \text{the number of components of } X_0$, we obtain

$$
\dim H^2(X_0) = 19 + f.
$$

Consider the weight filtration on $H^2(X_0)$.
We have by Mayer-Vietoris,

$$
\dim W_0 H^2(X_0) = 1
$$

$$
W_2 H^2(X_0) = H^2(X_0)
$$

$$
H^2(X_0)/W_1 H^2(X_0) = \ker\left(H^2(X_0^{[0]})\to H^2(X_0^{[1]})\right)
$$

$$
0 = H^1(X_0^{[1]}) \to W_1 H^2(X_0)/W_0 H^2(X_0).
$$

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

Hence

$$
\dim \ker \left(H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0} ^ {(0)}\right)\rightarrow H ^ {2} \left(X _ {0} ^ {(1)}\right)\right) = 18 + f.
$$

To prove (7.2), it suffices to prove that

$$
h ^ {2} \left(X _ {0} ^ {(0)}\right) - h ^ {2} \left(X _ {0} ^ {(1)}\right) = 18 + f \tag {7.2.1}
$$

as well, for then $h^2(X_0^{(0)}) - h^2(X_0^{(1)}) = \dim \ker(H^2(X_0^{(0)}) \to H^2(X_0^{(1)}))$ , in which case the cokernel must be zero.

Let $e =$ the number of double curves of $X_0$ and $v =$ the number of triple points.
Thus,

$$
v - e + f = 2
$$

and

$$
2 e = 3 v.
$$

We must show that $h^2(X_0^{(0)}) = 18 + f + e$ .
To this end, let $r_i$ be the number of irreducible components of the double curve $D_i$ on $V_i$ and define [10] (3.1)

$$
Q _ {i} = Q (\tilde {V} _ {i}, D _ {i}) = 12 - (K _ {\tilde {V} _ {i}}) ^ {2} - r _ {i}.
$$

The principle of "conservation of charge" [10] (3.7) is then

$$
\Sigma_ {i} Q _ {i} = 24.
$$

Now $h^2(\tilde{V}_i) = 10 - (K_{\tilde{V}_i})^2$ .
Hence

$$
\begin{array}{l} h ^ {2} \left(X _ {0} ^ {(0)}\right) = \Sigma_ {i} \left(10 - \left(K _ {\tilde {V} _ {i}}\right) ^ {2}\right) = \Sigma_ {i} \left(12 - \left(K _ {\tilde {V} _ {i}}\right) ^ {2} - r _ {i}\right) + \Sigma_ {i} \left(r _ {i} - 2\right) \\ = \Sigma_ {i} Q _ {i} + \Sigma_ {i} r _ {i} - 2 f = 24 + 2 e - 2 f \\ = 24 + 2 (v - 2) = 20 + 2 v \\ = 20 + 3 v - v = 20 + 2 e - v \\ = 20 + e - v + e = 20 + f - 2 + e = 18 + f + e, \\ \end{array}
$$

q.e.d.

## (3) A proof of the minus-one-theorem

Let $X_0$ be a Type III $d$ -semistable K3 surface.
If we knew a priori that all such were smoothable, which is proven in [7] §5, we could deduce the minus-one-theorem from (0.6) and the fact that the standard models constructed in §2 are in minus-one-form.

Unfortunately, the minus-one-theorem is used in the proof of smoothability in [7] (5.10), so the reasoning as it stands is circular.
On the other hand, the minus-one-theorem is used simply to show that

$$
H ^ {0} \left(X _ {0}, \Lambda_ {X _ {0}} ^ {1}\right) = 0,
$$

where $\Lambda_{X_0}^1$ is the abstract log complex defined in [7] §3.
The vanishing of this sheaf cohomology group is a purely combinatorial problem, and this problem seems less deep than the proof of the minus-one-theorem.

If we start out with $X_0$ which is assumed smoothable, we may still use (0.6) to prove the minus-one-theorem for $X_0$ , noting that any $Y_0$ in minus-one-form

Type III degenerations of K3 surfaces

is smoothable by [7] (5.10).
Indeed, a glance at the argument in §6 shows that it is sufficient to prove:

(7.3) Proposition.
If $X_0$ is smoothable, and $\pi \colon X \to S$ is a locally trivial family of $d$ -semistable varieties over a smooth connected base $S$ with $0 \in S$ such that $\pi^{-1}(0) \cong X_0$ , then $\pi^{-1}(s)$ is smoothable for any $s \in S$ .

Proof.
One easily constructs a relative log complex $\Lambda_{X / S}^{1}$ as in [7] §3 in our situation.
It suffices to prove that the set

$$
\{s \in S: H ^ {0} \left(X _ {s}, \Lambda_ {X _ {s}} ^ {1}\right) = 0 \}
$$

is open and closed.
It is clearly open, by upper semi-continuity.
To prove that it is closed, we use

(7.4) Lemma.
If $X_0$ is a Type III $d$ -semistable K3 surface, the following are equivalent:

1.  $H^0 (X_0;\varLambda_{X_0}^1)=0;$
2.  $X_0$ is a central fiber in a one-variable semistable degeneration;
3.  $X_0$ is smoothable.

Sketch of proof.
$1) \Rightarrow 2)$ by [7] (5.10).
$2) \Rightarrow 3)$ is obvious.
To prove that $3) \Rightarrow 1)$ .
let $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ be a not necessarily semistable smoothing of $X_0$ .
It is not difficult to show that the singularities of $X$ have the local form

$$
\{x y z = t ^ {k} \}
$$

and hence the method of [9] §1 gives a semistable resolution of singularities

![img-19.jpeg](img-19.jpeg)

Here, $\tilde{X}_0$ has special $k$ -bands of hexagons and is related to $X_0$ as in [9] (1.2).
Since $\tilde{X}_0$ is semistably smoothable, $H^{1}(\tilde{X}_{0},\varLambda_{X_{0}}^{1})=0$ , by [26].

A routine if slightly involved calculation now shows that, if $H^{1}(\tilde{X}_{0},\varLambda_{X_{0}}^{1})=0$ , then $H^{1}(X_{0},\varLambda_{X_{0}}^{1})=0$ as well, q.e.d.

To complete the proof of (7.3), note that openness of versality implies that the smoothability of $X_{s}$ is a closed condition, q.e.d.

(4) Applications to cusp singularities
(7.5) Theorem.
Let $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$ be a Type III degeneration of K3 surfaces.
Then there exists a modification $X \to X'$ and a morphism

![img-20.jpeg](img-20.jpeg)

R.
Friedman and F.
Scattone

such that

1. $p$ is birational except at $t=0$ and at worst contracts $A$, $D$, or $E$ configurations of rational curves in the general fiber;
2. $\bar{X}_0$ is irreducible and normal, and aside from possible rational double points, has a unique minimally elliptic cusp singularity.

Proof.
This follows immediately from Theorem 2A of [25], (6.1), and (2.8) for primitive $X_0$.
The general case follows immediately from the primitive case, by (6.5) and (6.6).

In view of (7.5), one might ask which cusp singularities occur in central fibers of modifications of a given $\pi \colon X \to \Delta$.
It is easy to see, by stability of exceptional divisors, that the possible analytic types are specified by $(k, t)$.

It seems more fruitful to invert this question: given a cusp singularity whose minimal resolution $E$ is a section of $-K_V$ for some smooth rational surface $V$, for which $(k, t)$ does there exist a smoothing of $\tilde{V} = (V \text{ contracted along } E)$ which has a semistable model with the invariants $(k, t)$? (Not all cusps have the property that their resolution is a section of $-K_V$ for a smooth rational surface $V$-briefly, we say that the cusp is rational - and Looijenga has recently made some beautiful conjectures related to necessary and sufficient conditions for smoothability of a cusp, having to do with the rationality of the so-called dual cusp.
For more details on the relationship between semistable degenerations of K3 surfaces and smoothings of cusps, we refer to [10].)

It is possible to prove some weak results concerning the arithmetic of $N$ and the arithmetic of embeddings of the dual cusp; in particular, the formula (7.1) "explains" why some of the central fibers given in [10] are forced to be much more complicated than others.
We can formulate some general conjectures when the multiplicity $m = \max(-E^2, 2)$ is as large as possible, relating $(k, t)$ to the arithmetic of the dual cusp.
Details will appear elsewhere.

Acknowledgement.
We would like to thank Jim Carlson for giving us access to his unpublished work, and Dave Morrison for several stimulating suggestions.
