# Explicit $p$-adic theta functions\*

Peter Norman

Department of Mathematics and Statistics, University of Massachusetts, Amherst, MA 01003, USA

Let $k$ be an algebraically closed field of characteristic $p &gt; 2$; $W(k)$, the ring of Witt vectors over $k$.
Let $B$ be a $W(k)$-algebra.
We say $B$ is $p$-adic provided it is closed and separated in the $p$-adic topology.
Let $E$ be an elliptic curve over a $p$-adic $B$ such that $\hat{E} \cong G_m \times B$; let $\mathcal{L}$ be an ample, totally symmetric line bundle on $E$; $s$, a section of $\mathcal{L}$.
Then we may associate to $s$ a theta function, $\theta_s$, on the formal group $\hat{E}$ of $E$ [2, 8].
If $B = W(k)((q))$ and $E$ is the Tate curve, then $\theta_s$ can be written simply and explicitly; for example, for appropriate $\mathcal{L}$ and $s$,

$$
\theta_s = \sum_{n = -\infty}^{n = \infty} q^{n^2} w^n
$$

where $w$ is a multiplicative parameter on $E$.
The problem we attack is: Exhibit these theta functions explicitly in the case of other elliptic curves; in particular, in the case of the universal lifting of an ordinary elliptic curve.

We begin by representing $\theta_s$ as an integral: there is a finite abelian group $D$ and a measure on $D \times \mathbb{Q}_p$ so that

$$
\theta_s = \int_{m \in D \times \mathbb{Q}_p} f(m) w^m d\mu(m)
$$

where $f$ is locally constant with compact support.
(We assume that the degree of $\mathcal{L}$ is prime to $p$).
We identify $d\mu$ with Mumford's measure ([6], II, p.
112).

The second step is to prove $\theta_s$ satisfies a heat equation.
The main difficulty is to make sense out of this; after all $\theta_s$, as defined in [2, 6] or [8], is only defined up to an element of $B^*$, while the heat equation relates the behavior of $\theta$ along a fiber to its behavior along the base space.
We construct rigidified theta functions by using a compactification of the moduli space.
This enables us to single out "rigid" sections.
The heat operator comes from the Kodaira-Spencer map.
For a rigid section $s$, to show $\theta_s$ satisfies the heat equation we use the $q$-expansion principle for $p$-adic modular forms.

P.
Norman

By combining the integral formula for theta functions with the heat equation we obtain the following formula for theta: Let $E_0$ be an ordinary elliptic curve over $k$ and let $E / W(k)[[q]]$ be its universal lifting.
Given an isomorphism $\hat{E} \cong G_m \times W(k)[[q]]$, let $w$ be a multiplicative parameter on $E$; let $q$ be the corresponding Serre-Tate parameter.
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a line bundle of degree 4 on $E$; then, after adjoining the fourth root of unity to $W(k)$, there is a section $s \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L})$ so that

$$
\theta_{s} = \int_{m \in \mathbb{Z}_{p}} (1 + q)^{m^{2}} w^{-2^{n}} d\mu(m).
$$

This raises the questions

(1) Let $E$ be the canonical lifting of an elliptic curve in characteristic $p &gt; 2$.
Can you compute $d\mu$ explicitly?
(2) Can you generalize this result to abelian varieties? Using the results in Chai's thesis [1] this seems well within reach.

## § I.

Theta as an integral

Let $A$ be an abelian scheme of dimension $g$ over a $p$-adic $W(k)$-algebra $B$.
Assume $\hat{A} = \hat{G}_m^g$.
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be an ample, totally symmetric line bundle on $A$ and let $s \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L})$.
We can associate to $s$ a power series theta function $\theta_B$.
The aim of this section is to write $\theta_B$, not as an infinite sum, but rather as an integral using Mumford's measure ([6], II).
We proceed as follows: Let $K$ be the fraction field of $B$.
Let $\theta_K$ denote the theta function associated to $s$ on the $p$-adic Tate group of $A_K$.
Then $\theta_K$ can be expressed as an integral; furthermore $\theta_B$ interpolates $\theta_K$ on the part of the $p$-adic Tate group that comes from the connected part of the $p$-divisible group of $A$.
Putting these two facts together gives us our expression of $\theta_B$ as an integral.

We begin by recapitulating part of Mumford's theory of theta functions.
We state these results for $K$ an algebraically closed field for the sake of brevity.

**Definition.** The $p$-tower $\mathcal{X}$ of $(A, \mathcal{L})$ is the inverse system $(A_{\alpha}, \mathcal{L}_{\alpha})$ of abelian varieties and line bundles indexed by a partial ordered set $\mathcal{S}$ which satisfies:

(o) $(A,\mathcal{L})\in \mathcal{X}_0$
(i) If $\alpha, \beta \in \mathcal{S}, \alpha &gt; \beta$, then there is given an isogeny whose degree is a power of $p$:

$$
\pi_{\alpha \beta} \colon A_{\alpha} \to A_{\beta}
$$

such that $\mathcal{L}_{\alpha} = \pi_{\alpha \beta}^{*}\mathcal{L}_{\beta}$

(ii) If $\alpha &gt;\beta &gt;\gamma$, then $\pi_{\alpha \gamma} = \pi_{\beta \gamma}\circ \pi_{\alpha \beta}$
(iii) If $\alpha &gt;\beta_{1},\beta_{2}$, and $K_{i} = \ker (A_{\alpha}\to A_{\beta_{i}})$, then $K_{1}\subseteq K_{2}$ if and only if $\beta_{1}\geq \beta_{2}$
(iv) a) If $A_{\alpha} \to B$ is an isogeny of degree $p^n$, then there is a $\beta \in \mathcal{S}$ and a diagram

![img-21.jpeg](img-21.jpeg)

Explicit $p$-adic theta functions
43

(iv) b) If $B \to A_{\alpha}$ is degree $p^n$, there is a diagram

![img-22.jpeg](img-22.jpeg)

Let $A_{\alpha}(n)$ denote the group of points of order $p^n$ in $A_{\alpha}$.
Let

$$
\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L}) = \{x \in A \mid T_x^* \mathcal{L} \cong \mathcal{L}\}.
$$

Since $\mathcal{L}$ is ample, $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L})$ is finite; we assume that the degree of $\mathcal{L}$ is prime to $p$.
Let $H_{\alpha}$ be the canonical lifting of $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L})$ to $A_{\alpha}$.
Write $H = \mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L})$.

Definition.
$X = X(\mathcal{X}) = \lim_{\substack{\rightarrow \\ \alpha}} \lim_{n} (A_{\alpha}(n) \oplus H_{\alpha})$.
Note that $X \cong \mathbb{Q}_p^{2\pi} \oplus H$.

Definition.
Let $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ be the set of sequences $(\phi_{\alpha}, x_{\alpha})$ such that for all sufficiently large $\alpha$

$$
\phi_{\alpha} \colon \mathcal{L}_{\alpha} \xrightarrow{\sim} T_{x_{\alpha}}^* \mathcal{L}_{\alpha};
$$

and $\pi_{\alpha \beta}^* \phi_{\beta} = \phi_{\alpha}$.

We give $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ a group structure by setting

$$
(\phi_{\alpha}, x_{\alpha}) * (\psi_{\alpha}, y_{\alpha}) = (\rho_{\alpha}, z_{\alpha})
$$

with

$$
\rho_{\alpha} \colon \mathcal{L}_{\alpha} \xrightarrow{\psi_{\alpha}} T_{y_{\alpha}}^* \mathcal{L}_{\alpha} \xrightarrow{\hat{z}_{\alpha} \phi_{\alpha}} T_{x_{\alpha} + y_{\alpha}} \mathcal{L}_{\alpha}
$$

and $(z_{\alpha}) = (x_{\alpha} + y_{\alpha})$.
There is an exact sequence

$$
1 \to K^* \to \mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X}) \to X \to 0
$$

making $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ into a central extension of $X$ by $K^*$.
Let $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_{\mathcal{X}}$ denote the skew form on $X$ induced by the commutator in $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$.
Since $K^*$ is exactly the center of $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$, the form $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_{\mathcal{X}}$ is non-degenerate.

Pullback induces an inclusion

$$
\pi_{\beta \alpha}^* \Gamma(\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(\mathcal{L}_{\beta})
$$

if $\beta &gt; \alpha$ in $\mathcal{S}$.
Let $\Gamma(\mathcal{X})$ denote $\lim_{\rightarrow} \Gamma(\mathcal{L}_{\alpha})$.
The group $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ acts on $\Gamma(\mathcal{X})$ by

$$
U_{(\phi_{\alpha}, x_{\alpha})} \mathfrak{s} = T_{-x_{\alpha}} \phi_{\alpha}(s_{\alpha})
$$

where $(s_{\alpha}) = s$.
This makes since $\phi_{\alpha}(s_{\alpha}) \in T_{x_{\alpha}}^* \mathcal{L}_{\alpha}$ and so $T_{-x_{\alpha}}^* \phi_{\alpha}(s_{\alpha}) \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L}_{\alpha})$; furthermore the sequence $T_{-x_{\alpha}}^* \phi_{\alpha} s_{\alpha}$ patches together.
The action of $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ on $\Gamma(\mathcal{X})$ allows us to associate to $s \in \Gamma(\mathcal{X})$ a function $\theta_s$ on $X$ in a canonical fashion:

Proposition 1. There is a canonical section $\sigma$:

$$
\mathcal{G} \underset{\sigma}{\longrightarrow} X.
$$

P.
Norman

Let $e_{\alpha}$ denote the identity point of $A_{\alpha}$ and $\lambda = (\lambda_{\alpha})$ an isomorphism of $\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}(e_{\alpha})$ with $K$ induced via pullback by a single evaluation of $\mathcal{L}(e)$ with $K$.
Let $(\phi_{\alpha}, x_{\alpha}) = \sigma(x_{\alpha})$.
We have, for $\alpha$ sufficiently large, the diagram

![img-23.jpeg](img-23.jpeg)

This gives us a map from $\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}(x_{\alpha})$ to $K$ and allows us to evaluate $s_{\alpha}(x_{\alpha})$.
For a fixed $s = (s_{\alpha})$ these evaluations patch together.
In terms of the action of $\Gamma(\mathcal{X})$ on $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ this evaluation is

$$
\theta_{s}(x) = \lambda \circ U_{\sigma(-x)} s.
$$

We now state the basic theorems which allow us to express $\theta_{s}$ as an integral.
Equip $\mathbb{Q}_p^g\oplus \mathbb{Q}_p^g$ with the standard symplectic form $\langle ,\rangle_{p}$.
Choose an inclusion of groups

$$
\chi \colon \mathbb{Q}_{p} / \mathbb{Z}_{p} \hookrightarrow K^{*}.
$$

Now $\chi \circ \langle ,\rangle_{p}$ gives $\mathbb{Q}_p^{2g}$ a multiplicative symplectic pairing.
Let $D$ be a finite abelian group and $D^{*}$ its dual group, so $E = D\oplus D^{*}$ has a multiplicative symplectic form, $\langle ,\rangle_{E}$.
Let $\{\, \}$ denote the product of $\chi \circ \langle ,\rangle_{p}$ and $\langle ,\rangle_{E}$, a multiplicative symplectic form on $\mathbb{Q}_p^{2g}\oplus E$.

Definition.
A symplectic structure on $X$ is an isomorphism

$$
(X, \langle , \rangle_{\mathcal{X}}) \rightarrow (\mathbb{Q}_{p}^{2g} \oplus E, \{\, \}).
$$

Let $G = K^{*}\oplus (\mathbb{Q}_{p}^{2g}\oplus E)$ with multiplication

$$
(r, x_{1}, x_{2}) * (s, y_{1}, y_{2}) = (r s \{x_{2}, y_{1} \}, x_{1} + y_{1}, x_{2} + y_{2}).
$$

Here $x_{1},y_{1}\in \mathbb{Q}_{p}^{g}\oplus D$ and $x_{2},y_{2}\in \mathbb{Q}_{p}^{g}\oplus D^{*}$

Since $\mathcal{L}$ is totally symmetric we are able to define a canonical automorphism, $\delta$, of $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$ of period two; the map $\delta$ induces the map $x$ goes to $-x$ on $X$.
The group $G$ also has an automorphism of order two:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
D \colon G \to G, \\
(r, x_{1}, x_{2}) \to (r, -x_{1}, -x_{2}).
\end{array}
$$

Definition.
A theta structure on $\mathcal{X}$ is an isomorphism of $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ with $G$ that takes $\delta$ to $D$.

Theorem 2 (Stone-Von Neumann).
There is a unique irreducible representation of $G$ with $K^*$ acting by multiplication.

Explicit $p$-adic theta functions

A model for this representation is the space, $\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{g}}$, of locally constant functions with compact support on $\mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \times D$; $G$ acts by

$$
(W_{(r, x_1, x_2)} f)(y) = r\{x_2, y\}_s f(y - x_1).
$$

Here $\{, \}_s$ denotes the multiplicative pairing from $(\mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus D) \times (\mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus D^*)$ into $K^*$.

**Proposition 3.** $\Gamma (\mathcal{X})$ is an irreducible $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$-module.

**Corollary.** Given a theta structure on $(A, \mathcal{L})$ there is a unique, up to an element in $K^*$, isomorphism

$$
\beta \colon \Gamma (\mathcal{X}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{g}}.
$$

We can now describe $\theta_s$ in terms of measures.
Choose a theta structure for $(A, \mathcal{L})$.
Let $V$ denote the space of $K$-valued functions on $X \cong (\mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus D) \times (\mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus D^*)$.
We obtain a $G - \mathcal{G}(\mathcal{X})$ diagram:

![img-24.jpeg](img-24.jpeg)

Define a linear functional

$$
\begin{array}{c}
\varLambda \colon \mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{g}} \longrightarrow K \\
f \longrightarrow \beta^{-1} f \xrightarrow{\lambda} K.
\end{array}
$$

This functional is given by a finitely additive measure, $d\mu$, on $\mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \times D$.
Knowledge of $\theta_s(e) = \varLambda \beta(s)$ for all $s$ allows us to compute $\theta_s$ on all of $X$:

$$
\theta[f](x_1, x_2) = \theta_s(x_1, x_2) = \int_{u \in \mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \times D} \{x_1, x_2/2\}_s \{-x_2, u\}_s rf(u - x_1) d\mu(u) \tag{1}
$$

where $(r_1, x_1, x_2) = \sigma(x_1, x_2)$ and $x_1 \in \mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \times D$, $x_2 \in \mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \times D^*$.

We now work out this formula in a special case.
Assume that

$$
D = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{\mathrm{g}} [(1/n_i) \mathbf{Z}/n_i \mathbf{Z}] \quad \text{with} \quad n_1 | n_2| \dots n_g.
$$

For $a, b \in \bigoplus (1/n_i) \mathbf{Z}/\mathbf{Z}$ define a function $\begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix} \in \mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{g}}$ by

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left[ \begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (x) = 0 \quad \text{if } x \notin a + (\mathbf{Z}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus (\bigoplus \mathbf{Z}/n_i \mathbf{Z})) \\
= \{x, b\}_s \quad \text{if } x \in a + (\mathbf{Z}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus (\bigoplus \mathbf{Z}/n_i \mathbf{Z})).
\end{array}
$$

We restrict formula (1) to $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 \in \mathbb{Q}_p^{\mathrm{g}}$.
We obtain

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (0, x_2) = \int_{u \in \mathbf{Z}_p^{\mathrm{g}} \oplus \bigoplus \mathbf{Z}/n_i \mathbf{Z}} \{-x_2, u\}_s \{a, b\} \{u, b\} \cdot d\mu(u) \tag{2} \\
= \sum_{\bar{u} \in \bigoplus \mathbf{Z}/n_i \mathbf{Z}} \int_{u \in \mathbf{Z}_p^{\mathrm{g}}} \{-x_2, u\}_s \{a, b\}_s \{\bar{u}, b\} d\mu_{\bar{u}}(u).
\end{array}
$$

Here $d\mu_{\bar{u}}$ denotes the measure $d\mu$ restricted to $(\mathbf{Z}_p^{\mathrm{g}}, \bar{u})$.

P.
Norman

Let $B$ be a $p$-adic $W(k)$-algebra with sufficiently many roots of unity.
Let $K$ be its fraction field and $\bar{K}$ the algebraic closure of $K$.
Let $(A, \mathcal{L})$ be an abelian scheme over $B$ with degree $\mathcal{L}$ prime to $p$.
Let $\mathcal{X}$ be its $p$-tower and assume $\mathcal{X}$ has a theta structure over $K$ with $D$ as above.
Let $s \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L})$ so that $s \times K$ correspond to $\left[ \begin{array}{c}a \\ b \end{array} \right] \in \mathcal{H}_g$.
We now show how to write $\theta_s$ as an integral.

We begin by establishing some compatibilities.
The theta structure induces a map

$$
\mathbb {Q} _ {p} ^ {2 g} / \mathbb {Z} _ {p} ^ {2 g} \hookrightarrow A (\bar {K}).
$$

Assume this has been chosen so that $0 \times (\mathbb{Q}_p^g / \mathbb{Z}_p^g)$ maps to the points of $A(\bar{K})$ corresponding to the connected component of the $p$-divisible group of $A$.
Choose a map

$$
\left(\mathbb {Q} _ {p} / \mathbb {Z} _ {p}\right) ^ {g} \rightarrow \hat {G} _ {m} ^ {g} (\bar {K}) \cong \hat {A} (K)
$$

compatible with the choice above.

We make some notation.
Let $\rho_{i}$ be a primitive $n_i^2$-th root of unity, so $\{a,b\}_{s} = \prod_{i}\rho_{i}^{a,b,n_{i}^{2}}$ and $\{\bar{u},b\} = \prod_{i}\rho_{i}^{\bar{u},b,n_{i}^{2}}$.
Write this as $\rho^{ab}$, $\rho^{\bar{u}b}$ the sentence: Let $x\in (1 / p^n)\mathbb{Z}_p^g$; $x = (x_{1}\ldots x_{g})$.
Choose a $(p^n)$-th root of unity so that under the isomorphisms above, $x$ corresponds to $(\zeta^{x_1},\dots,\zeta^{x_g})$.
Notice that $\{x,u\}_{s}$ is constant on cosets of $p^n\mathbb{Z}_p^g$ as a function of $u$.
Now formula (2) applied to the section $s\times K$ corresponding to $\left[ \begin{array}{c}a\\ b \end{array} \right]$ is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \theta_ {s \times K} (0, x) = \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (0, x) = \sum_ {\bar {u}} \int_ {u \in \mathbb {Z} _ {p} ^ {g}} \rho^ {(a + \bar {u}) b} \rho^ {- x u} d \mu_ {\bar {u}} (u) \\ = \sum_ {\bar {u}, u} \rho^ {(a + \bar {u}) b} \rho^ {- x u} \mu ((\bar {u}, u) + p ^ {n} \mathbb {Z} _ {p} ^ {g}). \\ \end{array}
$$

Write $(\mu_{p^n})^g = \operatorname{Spec}(B[y_{n1},\ldots ,y_{ng}] / (\ldots ,y_{ni}^{p^n} - 1,\ldots))$.
The above formula says that $\theta_s$ (which interpolates $\theta_{s\times K}$ since the formation of theta functions commutes with base extension) is given by

$$
f _ {n} \left(y _ {n 1}, \dots , y _ {n g}\right) = \sum_ {\bar {u}, u} \rho^ {(a + \bar {u}) b} y ^ {- u} \mu \left(\left(\bar {u}, u\right) + p ^ {n} \mathbb {Z} _ {p} ^ {g}\right))
$$

when restricted to $(\mu_{p^n})^g$.
Since $\theta_s$, the power series theta function associated to $s$, is the limit of the $f_n$ we have

$$
\theta_ {s} \left(y _ {1}, \dots , y _ {g}\right) = \theta_ {s} (y) = \sum_ {\bar {u} \in \oplus \mathbb {Z} / n _ {i} \mathbb {Z}} \int_ {u \in \mathbb {Z} _ {p} ^ {g}} \rho^ {(a + \bar {u}) b} y ^ {- u} d \mu_ {\bar {u}} (u).
$$

## § II.

Rigid theta functions

Classically theta functions satisfy a heat equation; for example, the classical Jacobi theta function

$$
\theta (z, \tau) = \sum_ {n \in \mathbb {Z}} \exp (\pi i n ^ {2} \tau + 2 \pi \mathrm {i n z})
$$

Explicit $p$-adic theta functions
47

with $\tau \in H$, the upper half plane, satisfies

$$
(4\pi i \partial / \partial \tau - \partial^2 / \partial z^2)(\theta) = 0.
$$

In terms of the family of elliptic curves over $H$, the heat equation relates the behavior of $\theta$ in each fiber to its behavior along the parameter space $H$.
If

$$
\pi: \mathcal{E} \rightarrow \mathcal{S} \tag{3}
$$

is a family of elliptic curves and $\mathcal{L}$ is a line bundle on $E$ with $s \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L})$ such that we can construct a power series theta function following [2] or [7], then, as it now stands, it is meaningless to ask if $\theta_s$ satisfies a heat equation: $\theta_s$ is only determined up to a non-vanishing function on $\mathcal{S}$.
The object of this section is to produce rigid theta functions, i.e., to produce a $\theta$ unique up to $\mathbb{Z}_s[1/2n]^*$ with appropriate $n$.

We begin by noting the sources of ambiguity in constructing power series theta functions.
Let $e: \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{E}$ denote the identity section of our family of elliptic curves; for simplicity assume $\pi_*\mathcal{L}$ and $e^*\mathcal{L}$ are both trivial bundles.
To construct a theta function we need a section of $\mathcal{L}$.
By fixing a divisor we can fix a section up to a non-vanishing function on $\mathcal{S}$.
This is the first source of ambiguity.
The second source is the choice of an evaluation map

$$
\lambda: e^*\mathcal{L} \to \mathbb{A}^1 \times \mathcal{S}
$$

where $\mathbb{A} \times \mathcal{S}$ denotes the trivial bundle on $\mathcal{S}$; again this is unique only up to a non-vanishing function on $\mathcal{S}$.

We overcome the obstacle, the plethora of non-vanishing functions on $\mathcal{S}$, by using the fact that if

$$
\mathcal{T} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}[1/2n]
$$

is proper, then there are very few non-vanishing functions on $\mathcal{T}$.
We write our family (3) as the pull back of a family over a proper base space.
There we can make a choice of section and evaluation maps unique up to $\mathbb{Z}[1/2n]^*$.
We obtain a rigid theta by pulling these choices back to (3).

We begin by recalling some definitions and theorems from [6].
We limit ourselves to stating these for an abelian variety over an algebraically closed field $K$.
The global versions are in ([6] II, §6).
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a very ample, totally symmetric line bundle on an abelian variety $A$ of dimension $g$.
Assume that the degree of $\mathcal{L}$ is prime to the characteristic of $K$.
Let $\Gamma(\mathcal{L})$ denote the global sections of $\mathcal{L}$ and let $T_x$ denote translation by $x \in A(K)$.

**Definition.** Let $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$ be the group scheme whose points are

$$
\{(\phi, x) \mid \phi: \mathcal{L} \to T_x^*\mathcal{L}\},
$$

and whose multiplication is

$$
(\phi, x) * (\psi, y): \mathcal{L} \xrightarrow{\psi} T_y^*\mathcal{L} \xrightarrow{\xi\phi} T_{x+y}^*\mathcal{L}.
$$

P.
Norman

Let $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L}) = \{x\in A|\exists \phi \colon \mathcal{L}\cong T_x^*\mathcal{L}\}$.
Let $G_{m}$ denote the multiplicative group scheme; then $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$ is a central extension of $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L})$ by $G_{m}$:

$$
1 \rightarrow G _ {m} \rightarrow \mathcal {G} (\mathcal {L}) \rightarrow \mathcal {H} (\mathcal {L}) \rightarrow 0.
$$

We define a skew pairing on $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L})$ with values in $G_{m}$ by setting $\langle x,y\rangle_{\mathcal{L}}$ equal to the commutator of $(\phi ,x)$ and $(\psi ,y)$ in $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$.
This pairing is non-degenerate since $G_{m}$ is exactly the center of $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$.

Define an action of $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$ on $\Gamma (\mathcal{L})$ by

$$
U _ {\phi} (s) = T _ {- x} ^ {*} \phi (s)
$$

for $s \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L})$.
This makes sense: $\phi(s) \in \Gamma(T_x^* \mathcal{L})$, so $T_{-x}^*(s) \in \Gamma(\mathcal{L})$.

Theorem 4. $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$ acts irreducibly on $\Gamma (\mathcal{L})$.

We rigidify.
Let $(\delta_1, \ldots, \delta_q) \in \mathbb{Z}^q$, let $K(\delta) = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{q} \mathbb{Z} / \delta_i \mathbb{Z}$ with $\delta_1 | \delta_2 | \ldots | \delta_q$.
Let $K(\delta)^*$ denote the Cartier dual of $K(\delta)$, and let $\langle , \rangle_f$ denote the skew pairing on $H(\delta) = K(\delta) \oplus K(\delta)^*$.
Let $G(\delta) = G_m \times H(\delta)$ as schemes and give $G(\delta)$ a group structure by defining

$$
\left(r _ {1}, \left(x _ {1}, y _ {1}\right)\right) * \left(r _ {2}, \left(x _ {2}, y _ {2}\right)\right) = \left(r _ {1} r _ {2} \langle y _ {2}, x _ {1} \rangle_ {f}, x _ {1} + x _ {2}, y _ {1} + y _ {2}\right).
$$

A symplectic structure on $(A,\mathcal{L})$ is an isomorphism

$$
\left(\mathcal {H} (\mathcal {L}), \langle , \rangle_ {\mathcal {L}}\right)\rightarrow \left(H (\delta), \langle , \rangle_ {f}\right).
$$

Since $\mathcal{L}$ is totally symmetric we can define an automorphism $\delta$ of period two on $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$ which induces the map

$$
a \mapsto - a
$$

on $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L})$ (5, I, p.★308).
The group $G(\delta)$ also has an automorphism of period two, namely

$$
D \colon (r, x, y) \mapsto (r, - x, - y).
$$

A theta structure or $\delta$-theta structure on $(A,\mathcal{L})$ is an isomorphism

$$
\mathcal {G} (\mathcal {L}) \rightarrow G (\delta)
$$

taking $\delta$ to 0. Given a symplectic structure on $(A,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 2})$ there is a unique compatible theta structure on $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{L})$.

Theorem 5 (Stone-Von Neumann).
The group $G(\delta)$ has a unique irreducible representation on which $K^*$ acts by multiplication.

One model for this representation is the space, $V(\delta)$, of $K$-valued functions on $K(\delta)$ with $G(\delta)$ acting by

$$
\left(W _ {\left(r, x _ {1}, x _ {2}\right)} f\right) (y) = r \langle x _ {2}, y \rangle_ {f} f (y + x _ {1}).
$$

Explicit $p$-adic theta functions

Corollary.
Given a theta structure on $(A, \mathcal{L})$, there is a unique (up to a non-zero constant) isomorphism

$$
\Gamma (\mathcal {L}) \rightarrow V (\delta).
$$

Upon choosing a basis for $V(\delta)$ we get an embedding of $A$ in projective space (if $\mathcal{L}$ is very ample):

$$
A \to \operatorname {I P} (\Gamma (\mathcal {L})) \to \operatorname {I P} (V (\delta)) \to \operatorname {I P} ^ {n}.
$$

The last map comes from a choice of basis.

We now define our moduli functor.

Definition.
An abelian scheme over $S$ with a $\delta$-marking consists of:

(i) an abelian scheme $A$ of dimension $g$ over $S$,
(ii) a relatively ample, totally symmetric line bundle

$$
\pi \colon \mathcal {L} \to A, \quad \text{and}
$$

(iii) a theta structure

$$
\lambda \colon \mathcal {G} (\mathcal {L}) \xrightarrow {\sim} G (\delta) \times S;
$$

furthermore assume $\mathcal{L}$ is normalized so that

$$
\pi_ {*} \mathcal {L} \cong V \otimes \mathcal {O} _ {\mathcal {L}}.
$$

Theorem 6 (Mumford).
The functor $\mathcal{M}_{\delta}$ which associates to a scheme $\mathcal{S}$ the set of all abelian schemes over $S$ with $\delta$-markings modulo isomorphisms is representable if $\delta = (\delta_{1},\ldots ,\delta_{g})$ has all $\delta_{i}$ divisible by 8. There exists a universal abelian scheme with $\delta$-markings

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\delta} \rightarrow \mathcal {A} _ {\delta} \rightarrow \mathcal {M} _ {\delta}.
$$

Theorem 7 (Chai [1]).
Let $d$ be the least common multiple of $\delta_1, \ldots, \delta_g$.
Then there is a smooth compactification $\bar{\mathcal{M}}_{\delta}$ of $\mathcal{M}_{\delta} \otimes \mathbb{Z}[1/2d]$; furthermore we have a compactification $\bar{\mathcal{A}}_{\delta}$ of $\mathcal{A}^{\delta} \otimes \mathbb{Z}[1/2d]$ and an extension of $\mathcal{L}_{\delta}$:

![img-25.jpeg](img-25.jpeg)

The action of $H(\delta)$ on $\mathcal{A}_{\delta}$ and $G(\delta)$ on $\pi_*\mathcal{L}$ extends to $\bar{\mathcal{A}}_{\delta}$ and $\bar{\pi}^*\bar{\mathcal{L}}_{\delta}$.

Remark.
If $g = 1$ we may replace 8 by 4 in Theorem 7 [3, 6].

We are now in a position to define "rigid" sections.
Let $(A, \mathcal{L})$ be an abelian scheme over a $\mathbb{Z}[1/2d]$-scheme $S$ with totally symmetric line bundle $\mathcal{L}$.
Assume $A, \mathcal{L}$ has a $\delta$-theta structure.
By Theorem (6) $(A, \mathcal{L})$ is the pullback of $(\mathcal{A}_{\delta}, \mathcal{L}_{\delta})$ for an appropriate map from $S$ to $\mathcal{M}_{\delta}$.
Notice that $\bar{\pi}_{*} \bar{\mathcal{L}} \cong V_{\delta} \otimes \mathbb{Z}[1/2d] = T_{\delta}$.

P.
Norman

Definition.
A rigid section of $(A, \mathcal{L})$ is the restriction of an element of $T_{\delta}$.

For use in calculating $q$-expansions we now show that for elliptic curves $T_{\delta} \otimes \mathbb{C}$ is spanned by classical theta functions.
More precisely, let $\delta = 4n^{2}$ and let $a, b \in \{0, \frac{1}{2n}, \frac{2}{2n}, \dots, \frac{2n - 1}{2n}\}$.
Let

$$
\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) = \sum_ {m \in \mathbb {Z}} \exp \pi i (m + a) ^ {2} \tau + 2 \pi i (m + a) (z + b).
$$

We claim that the $\mathbb{C}$-linear span of $\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c}a\\ b \end{array} \right](nz,\tau)$ as $a,b$ run over $\{0,\frac{1}{n},\frac{2}{n},\ldots ,\frac{n - 1}{n}\}$ is $T_{\delta}\otimes \mathbb{C}$.
We begin by working out the case $\delta = 4$ (the Jacobi family) and using this as a stepping stone to treat the case $\delta = 4n^2$.

Let $H$ be the upper half plane; we define three maps: First

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\alpha \colon H \to \mathbb {P} _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {2} \\
\tau \to \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] ^ {2} (0, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] ^ {2} (0, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1 / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] ^ {2} (0, \tau)\right).
\end{array}
$$

The image of $\alpha$ is a dense open subscheme of the scheme $A$ defined by

$$
A _ {0} ^ {2} = A _ {1} ^ {2} + A _ {2} ^ {2}.
$$

Secondly

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\beta \colon \mathbb {C} \times H \mapsto \mathbb {P} _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {2} \\
(z, \tau) \mapsto \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 z, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 1 \end{array} \right] (2 z, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 z, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 1 \end{array} \right] (2 z, \tau)\right).
\end{array}
$$

The image of $\beta$ is a dense open subscheme of the scheme $F_{0}$ defined by

$$
X _ {0} ^ {4} + X _ {3} ^ {4} = X _ {1} ^ {4} + X _ {2} ^ {4}.
$$

Lastly

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\gamma \colon H \to \mathbb {P} _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {2} \\
\tau \to \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right]\right) (0, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau), \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1 / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau).
\end{array}
$$

The image of $\gamma$ is an open dense subscheme of the scheme $B$ defined by

$$
Y _ {0} ^ {4} = Y _ {1} ^ {4} + Y _ {2} ^ {4}.
$$

Between these varieties we have maps

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\psi_ {1} \colon F _ {0} \to A \\
\left(X _ {0}, X _ {1}, X _ {2}, X _ {3}\right) \mapsto \left(X _ {1} ^ {4} + X _ {2} ^ {4}, X _ {0} ^ {2} X _ {1} ^ {2} - X _ {2} ^ {2} X _ {3} ^ {2}, X _ {0} ^ {2} X _ {2} ^ {2} + X _ {1} ^ {2} X _ {3} ^ {2}\right)
\end{array}
$$

Explicit
p
-adic theta functions

and

$$
\psi_ {2} \colon B \to A
$$

$$
\left(Y _ {0}, Y _ {1}, Y _ {2}\right) \mapsto \left(Y _ {0} ^ {2}, Y _ {1} ^ {2}, Y _ {2} ^ {2}\right).
$$

The fibers of $\psi_{1}$ are the Jacobi curves, but $F_{0}$ is not a family of elliptic curves since there is no identity section.
Upon base extending by $\psi_{2}$ we obtain the family

$$
\bar {\pi} \colon F = F _ {0} \times_ {A} B \to B.
$$

The identity section is

$$
e \colon B \to F \times_ {A} B
$$

$$
e \colon (Y _ {0}, Y _ {1}, Y _ {2}) \mapsto ((Y _ {0}, Y _ {1}, Y _ {2}, 0), (Y _ {0}, Y _ {1}, Y _ {2})).
$$

The variety $F \times_A B$ is $\mathcal{M}_{\delta} \otimes \mathbb{C}$ with $\delta = 4$ .
Note that the pullback to $H$ of $T_4 \otimes \mathbb{C}$ is spanned by $\left\{\theta \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix} (2z, \tau)\right\}$ for $a, b \in \{0, 1/2\}$ .

Remark.
It can be checked that $F / B$ gives $\bar{\mathcal{M}}_{\delta} \otimes \mathbb{Z}[1/2, i]$ .
For details see [7].

We now treat the case $\delta = 4n^2$ .
For the universal abelian variety $\mathcal{A}_{4n^2}$ with its bundle $\mathcal{L}_{4n^2}$ we have $\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{L}_{4n^2}) \cong (\mathbb{Z}/4n^2\mathbb{Z})^2$ .
The subgroup $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{H}$ is level, i.e., the symplectic form $\langle , \rangle_{\mathcal{L}_{4n^2}}$ is trivial there; therefore, the line bundle $\mathcal{L}_{4n^2}$ descends to the quotient of $\mathcal{A}_{4n^2}$ by $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})$ .
Let $[n]$ denote multiplication by $n$ on $\mathcal{A}_{4n^2}$ .
We obtain the diagram:

![img-26.jpeg](img-26.jpeg)

This diagram extends to the compactifications $\bar{\mathcal{M}}_4$ , $\bar{\mathcal{A}}_4$ , $\bar{\mathcal{L}}_4$ , etc. over $\mathbb{Z}[1/2n]$ .
We conclude that $[n]^* T_4 \hookrightarrow T_{4n^2}$ ; in particular

$$
\left\{\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (2 n z, \tau) | a, b = 0, 1 / 2 \right\}
$$

is in $T_{4n^2} \otimes \mathbb{C}$ .
Since $T_{4n^2} \otimes \mathbb{C}$ is irreducible for the action of $G(4n^2)$ , we conclude that

$$
\left\{\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (2 n z, \tau) | a, b = 0, 1 / 2 n, 2 / 2 n, \ldots , 2 n - 1 / 2 n \right\}
$$

spans $T_{4n^2}\otimes \mathbb{C}$

P.
Norman

The second step in constructing rigid thetas is to find a rigid evaluation map.
We restrict to $g = 1$.
Let $4|\delta$ and write $\bar{\mathcal{L}} = \bar{\mathcal{L}}_{\delta}$; let $\Omega$ denote the sheaf of relative differentials of $\bar{\mathcal{A}}_{\delta}$ over $\bar{\mathcal{M}}_{\delta}$.
Let

$$
e \colon \bar {\mathcal {M}} _ {\delta} \to \bar {\mathcal {A}} _ {\delta}
$$

be the identity section.
Our aim is to show

$$
\left(e ^ {*} \bar {\mathcal {L}}\right) ^ {\otimes 2} \cong \bar {\pi} _ {*} \Omega . \tag {4}
$$

This implies that given a holomorphic differential, we get a map, unique up to $\mathbb{Z}[1 / \delta]^*$:

$$
(e ^ {*} \bar {\mathcal {L}}) ^ {\otimes 2} \rightarrow \mathbb {A} ^ {1} \times \bar {\mathcal {M}} _ {\delta}.
$$

Note that if we show (4) for $\delta_{1}$ and $\delta_1|\delta_2$, then (4) holds for $\delta_{2}$; thus we assume $\delta = 4$, and study the Jacobi family $F / B$.

Consider

(i) the section $Y_0^2 = a_0$ of $(e^{*}\mathcal{L})^{\otimes 2}$, and
(ii) the section

$$
\omega = \left[ d \left(X _ {0} / X _ {3}\right) \right] \left(X _ {3} ^ {2} / X _ {1} X _ {2}\right)
$$

of $\bar{\pi}_{*}\Omega$.

It is an exercise to check that they have identical divisors.
We conclude that (4) indeed holds for $\delta = 4$.

The motivation comes from the classical case: Begin by writing $dz$ in terms of theta functions.
Let $w = 1/2 + \tau/2$ and set $f = p(w, \tau)$.
Then since the divisor of $p(z, \tau) - f$ is $(-2 \cdot e + 2(1/2 + \tau/2))$ we have

$$
p (z, \tau) - f = c _ {1} \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau)\right) ^ {2} / \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau)\right) ^ {2}
$$

with

$$
c _ {1} = \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1 / 2 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] \theta (0, \tau) / \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau).
$$

Similarly we can write

$$
\begin{array}{l}
p ^ {\prime} (z, \tau) = d p / d z = \left(c _ {2} \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau)\right) / \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau)\right) ^ {3} \\
c _ {2} = - 2 \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] ^ {\prime} (0, \tau) / \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau)\right).
\end{array}
$$

We conclude

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d z = d p / p ^ {\prime} = \\
c _ {3} \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau)\right) ^ {- 2} \left[ d \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) / \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau)\right) \right] / \left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 1 / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \theta \left[ \begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 1 / 2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau)\right) \tag {5}
\end{array}
$$

where $c_{3} \in \mathbb{C}$.
This calculation uses Jacobi's derivative formula.

Explicit $p$-adic theta functions
53

We sketch how this calculation leads to an isomorphism of $(e^{*}\mathcal{L})^{\otimes 2}$ and $\pi_{*}\Omega$ on $H$.
Over $H$ both of these line bundles are trivial.
The classical constructions have inherent trivializations: $\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c}a\\ b \end{array} \right](0,\tau)$ is a function of $\tau$, and, on the other hand, $\tau \mapsto dz_{\tau}$ is a non-vanishing section of $\pi_{*}\Omega$ on $H$.
We have the isomorphism

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathbb{A}^{1} \times H \rightarrow (\pi_{*} \Omega)/H \\
1 \mapsto dz_{\tau}. \tag{6}
\end{array}
$$

By the calculation (5)

$$
dz_{\tau} = c_{3} \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{-2} \omega
$$

with

$$
\omega = \left[ d \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \left/ \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1/2 \\ 1/2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \right) \right) \right] \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1/2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 1/2 \end{array} \right] (z, \tau) \right).
$$

Consequently the isomorphism (6) maps

$$
\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau)^{2} \rightarrow \omega.
$$

This is the algebraic isomorphism we gave above in (4) tensored with $\mathbb{C}$ and pulled back to $H$!

We go one step further.
We compute the evaluation map induced by the isomorphism (4) pulled back to $H$ using the differential $dz_{\tau}$.
We have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
(e^{*} \mathcal{L})^{\otimes 2} \otimes (\pi_{*} \Omega)^{-1} \rightarrow \mathbb{A}^{1} \times H \\
\left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{2} \otimes \omega^{-1} \mapsto 1,
\end{array}
$$

and so

$$
\left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{2} \otimes dz = \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{2} \otimes \left( \omega \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{-2} \right)^{-1} \rightarrow \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{2}; \tag{7}
$$

furthermore,

$$
\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{2} \otimes (dz)^{-1} \rightarrow \left( \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (0, \tau) \right)^{2} \tag{8}
$$

for $a, b \in \{0, 1/2\}$.

Let $T$ be the Tate curve over $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]((q))$ with multiplicative parameter $w$ and canonical differential $dw/w$.
Over $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]/((q^{1/8}))$, $T$ has a $\delta$-theta structure, $\delta = 4$.
We calculate the power series theta functions for a rigid section using the evaluation map induced by the choice, $dw/w$, of differential.
Up to an element in $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]((q^{1/8}))^*$, the rigid theta functions are given by

$$
\sum q^{(n + a)_{2}} w^{2(n + a)} (-1)^{2b(n + a)} \tag{9}
$$

P.
Norman

(see [8]).
To remove the ambiguity we have to evaluate our rigid sections along the identity and see that we get the same formula as (9).
We tensor with $\mathbb{C}$.
The calculations (7) and (8) show that, indeed, we do have agreement.

The general case $\delta = 4n^2$ works similarly.
The map

$$
(e^* \mathcal{L})^2 \to \Omega
$$

when pulled back to $H$ maps

$$
\left(\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (0, \tau)\right)^2 \mapsto \theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right]^2 (0, \tau) dz
$$

for $a, b \in \{0, 1/2n, \ldots\}$, so the module of rigid theta functions on the Tate curve is spanned by

$$
\theta \left[ \begin{array}{c} a \\ b \end{array} \right] (w, q) = \sum_{m \in \mathbb{Z}} q^{(m + a)^2} w^{n(m + a)} \zeta^{(m + a)b}
$$

where $\zeta$ is a $(2n)^{\mathrm{th}}$ root of unity.

## § III.

The heat equation

Fix $\Lambda$, a $p$-adic ring over $w(k)$, and let $B$ be a $p$-adic $\Lambda$-algebra.
By a trivialization of an elliptic curve over $B$ we mean an isomorphism

$$
\phi \colon \hat{E} \xrightarrow{\sim} \hat{G}_m \times B.
$$

Let $\delta \in \mathbb{Z}$ be divisible by 4 and relatively prime top.
Given the data

$$
* \left\{ \begin{array}{l} E / B, \\ \text{a trivialization of } E, \text{ and} \\ \text{a } \delta\text{-theta structure on } E, \end{array} \right.
$$

we obtain

(i) the rigid sections $T_E$ of $\mathcal{L}_\delta$ (a $\Lambda$-submodule of $\Gamma(\mathcal{L}_\delta)$) such that $T_E \otimes B = \Gamma(\mathcal{L}_\delta)$,

(ii) a canonical evaluation map

$$
e^* \mathcal{L}^2 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbb{A}^1 \times B
$$

induced by the differential $\phi^*(dw/w)$ where $w$ is the multiplicative parameter on $\hat{G}_m$, and consequently

(iii) the squares of power series theta on $\hat{E}$, unique up to $\Lambda^*$.

The object of this section is to show that these power series theta functions satisfy a heat equation.
Assume that the $\delta$-th roots of unity are in $\Lambda$.
We begin by using the Kodaira-Spencer map to define a heat operator.
Secondly we show how to get at $\theta_s$ from $\theta_s^2$.
The last step is to state our problem in terms of $p$-adic modular forms and apply the $q$-expansion principle to get the solution.

Explicit $p$-adic theta functions

We define the heat operator.
Given data $(\ast)$, we get $D_{E}$, the derivation dual to $(dw / w)$, and thus $D_{E}^{2}$, and even $p(D_{E})$ for a polynomial $p$.
Let $\omega$ denote the sheaf of invariant differentials on $E$, and $\omega^t$, that of the dual to $E$.
Let $\Omega$ denote the sheaf of relative differentials of $B$ over $\Lambda$.
We have the Kodaira-Spencer map

$$
K \colon \omega \otimes \omega^ {t} \to \Omega ,
$$

and, via the canonical isomorphism of $E$ with $E^t$,

$$
K \colon \omega \otimes \omega \to \Omega .
$$

We say the family $E$ is modular provided $K$ is an isomorphism.
For a modular family, $K((dw / w)\otimes (dw / w))$ is a non-vanishing section of $\Omega$.
Let $D_B$ denote the derivation dual to $K((dw / w)\otimes (dw / w))$.
Since $E$ is trivialized, $D_B$ acts on $\tilde{E}\cong \tilde{G}_m\times B$.
Consequently, for any polynomial $p$ in two variables, we can define the action of $p(D_E,D_B)$ on $\theta_s^2$ for a rigid section $s$.
For $c\in \Lambda$ we can define a heat operator: $D_{E}^{2} - cD_{B}$ provided we have data $(\ast)$ on a modular family.

We now show how to reduce the proof of the heat equation for $\theta_{s}$ to a result about $\theta_{s}^{2}$.
First we calculate the differential operator acting on $\theta_{s}^{2}$ that corresponds to the heat operator acting on $\theta_{s}$.
Write $g = \theta_{s}^{2}$; let $c\in \Lambda$.
We have

$$
\left(D _ {E} - c D _ {B}\right) g ^ {1 / 2} = g ^ {- 1 / 2} \left[ - \left(D _ {E} g\right) ^ {2} / 4 g + \left(D _ {E} ^ {2} g\right) / 2 - c D _ {B} g / 2 \right] = g ^ {- 1 / 2} D _ {c} (g).
$$

(The last line is notation.) Thus to show that $\theta_{s}$ satisfies the heat equation we must show:

(a) Given data $(\ast)$, we can define $\theta_{s}$ if we allow degree two étale covers of $B$.

(b) $D_{c}(\theta_{s}^{2})\equiv 0$ for an appropriate $c\in \Lambda$

Start with (a).
Giving a non-vanishing invariant differential $\omega$ on $E$ gives an isomorphism

$$
\lambda (\omega) \colon e ^ {*} \left(\mathcal {L} ^ {2}\right) \to \mathbb {A} ^ {1} \times B;
$$

thus given a section $s$ of $\mathcal{L}$, to define $\theta_{s}$ we must give a trivialization $\rho$ on $e^{*}(\mathcal{L})$ such that $\rho \otimes \rho = \lambda (\omega)$.
Starting with a arbitrary trivialization $\rho$ we have $\lambda (\omega)\otimes \rho^{-2}$ is an invertible element of $B$; to get the sought for trivializations we have to adjoin, if necessary, the square root of $\lambda (\omega)\otimes \rho^{-2}$ to $B$.

The last step is to prove (b); this is done using $p$-adic modular functions.
Recall the definition [5]: Let $N \in \mathbb{Z}$, $(N, p) = 1$.
A $\Gamma(N) - p$-adic modular function is a rule which assigns an element of $B$ to a triple $(E, \phi, \beta)$ consisting of an elliptic curve $E / B$ with trivialization $\phi$ and level $N$ structure $\beta$.
This rule is to depend only on the isomorphism class of $(E, \phi, \beta)$ and is to commute with extension of scalars.

Remark.
Since a level $2\delta$ structure induces a unique $\delta$-thets structure, the data $(E,\phi ,\beta)$ with $N = 2\delta$ gives the data $(\ast)$.

We now define our $p$-adic modular functions.

P.
Norman

Assume $\delta = 4n^2$.
We need to fix an element in $V_{\delta} \otimes \Lambda$.
Start with $(E, \phi, \beta)$ with $N = 2\delta$.
Now $(E, \phi, \beta)$ is the pullback of a modular family $(E'/B', \phi' \beta')$, at least locally.
From $(E'/B', \phi', \beta')$ and our fixed element in $V_{\delta} \otimes \Lambda$ we get

(i) $\theta_s^2$, unique up to an element in $\Lambda^{*}$,
(ii) $D_{c}$, and
(iii) $D_{c}\theta_{s}^{2} / \theta_{s}^{2}$

Pull $D_{c}\theta_{s}^{2} / \theta_{s}^{2}$ back to $B$; this is independent of the choice of $(E' / B', \phi', \beta')$.
The coefficients of $(w - 1)$ in the expansion of $D_{c}\theta_{s}^{2} / \theta_{s}^{2}$ are now $p$-adic modular functions.

We claim: For an appropriate choice of $c$ these $p$-adic modular functions are identically zero.
By the $q$-expansion principle it suffices to verify that they vanish on the Tate curve.
There, by a calculation of Katz [4], $K((dw / w) \otimes (dw / w)) = dq / q$; hence by formula (9), $D_{c}\theta_{s}^{2} = 0$ with $c = 4n^2$.

We have not shown that the heat operator annihilates rigid theta functions if $\delta = 4m$ and $m$ is not a square.
To deal with this note we have a diagram

![img-27.jpeg](img-27.jpeg)

for a suitable $n$.
Pull the rigid sections of $\mathcal{L}_{4m}$ up to $\mathcal{L}_{4n^2}$; they satisfy the heat equation there.
To show they satisfy a heat equation as a section of $\mathcal{L}_{4m}$ we only need to compute how the heat operator behaves under isogeny:

**Lemma.** Let

$$
f \colon E _ {1} \to E _ {2}
$$

be an isogeny of elliptic curves over $B$ of degree $d$.
Let $K_{1}, K_{2}$ denote the Kodaira-Spencer maps of $E_{1}, E_{2}$, and $\omega_{1} = f^{*}\omega_{2}$, $\omega_{2}$ non-zero invariant differentials.
Then

$$
K _ {1} \left(\omega_ {1} \otimes \omega_ {1}\right) = d \cdot K _ {2} \left(\omega_ {2} \otimes \omega_ {2}\right).
$$

**Proof.** Just consider the diagram

![img-28.jpeg](img-28.jpeg)

From this it is easy to find a constant $c \in \Lambda$ so $D_{c}(\theta_{s})^{2} = 0$ for rigid sections $s$ of $\mathcal{L}_{4m}$.

**Final Formulas.** Let $\delta = 4n^2$; let $\Lambda$ be a $p$-adic $W(k)$-algebra that contains the $\delta$-th roots of unity and all the $p^n$-th roots of unity.
Let $a, b \in (1/2n)\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}$.
Let $s_0$ be the section of $V_{\delta} \otimes \Lambda$ corresponding $\theta \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix}$.
Let $\zeta = a$ fixed $(2n)$-th root of unity.

Explicit
p
-adic theta functions

Let $E_0$ be an ordinary elliptic curve over $k$ with level $2\delta$ structure $\beta_0$, and trivialization $\phi_0$.
Let $(E, \phi, \beta)$ be the universal lifting of $(E_0, \phi_0, \beta_0)$ to $\Lambda((q))$.
If $w$ is multiplicative parameter on $\hat{E}$ induced by $\phi$, we let $q$ be the corresponding Serre-Tate parameter (so $K((dw/w) \otimes (dw/w)) = dq/(1 + q)$).
Let $s$ be the rigid section of $E$ corresponding to $s_0$.
Then

$$
\theta_{s}(w) = \sum_{\boldsymbol{m} \in \mathbb{Z}/2\,n\mathbb{Z}} \int_{\boldsymbol{m} \in \mathbb{Z}_{p}} (1 + q)^{m} \zeta^{b(\boldsymbol{m} + a)} w^{-2\,nm} d\mu_{\boldsymbol{m}}(m).
$$

In particular if $a = b = 0$, then

$$
\theta_{s}(w) = \sum_{\boldsymbol{m} \in \mathbb{Z}/2\,n\mathbb{Z}} \int_{\boldsymbol{m} \in \mathbb{Z}_{p}} (1 + q)^{m^2} w^{-2\,nm} d\mu_{\boldsymbol{m}}(m).
$$

# $L^p$ bounds for Hilbert transforms along convex curves

A.
Cordoba$^{1}$, A.
Nagel$^{\star 2}$, J.
Vance$^{3}$, S.
Wainger$^{\star 2}$, and D.
Weinberg$^{4}$

$^{1}$ Division de Matematicas, Universidad Autonoma, Cantoblanca, Madrid 28049, Spain
$^{2}$ Department of Mathematics, University of Wisconsin, Madison, WI 53706, USA
$^{3}$ Department of Mathematics, Wright State University, Dayton, OH, USA
$^{4}$ Department of Mathematics, Texas Tech, Lubbock, TX, USA

## § 1. Introduction

Let $\Gamma \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^n$ be a curve in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with $\Gamma(0) = 0$, $n \geq 2$.
To $\Gamma$ we associate the Hilbert transform operator $\mathcal{H}$ defined by the principal-value integral

$$
\mathcal{H}f(x) = \text{p.v.} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x - \Gamma(t)) \frac{dt}{t} \quad (x \in \mathbb{R}^n). \tag{1}
$$

($f$ denotes an arbitrary function in an appropriate class; say, $f \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)$.) It is of substantial interest to determine for which curves $\Gamma$, and which indices $p$, one has the $L^p$ bound

$$
\left\| \mathcal{H}f \right\|_p \leq A_p \| f \|_p \tag{2}
$$

for a constant $A_p$ depending only on $\Gamma$ and $p$, not $f$.
See [SW] for a survey of this problem's history through 1977. More recent results are found in [Ne, Wn, NSW2, NVWW1, NVWW2, Ch].

To clarify the purpose of this paper, let us recall the main strategy up until this time for obtaining estimates of the form

$$
\left\| \mathcal{H}f \right\|_p \leq A_p \| f \|_p. \tag{2}
$$

First, since $\mathcal{H}$ is a convolution operator, it follows easily that

$$
\widehat{\mathcal{H}f} = m \cdot \hat{f} \tag{3}
$$

where $\hat{f}$ denotes the Fourier transform and the "Fourier multiplier" $m$ is the function

$$
m(\xi) = \text{p.v.} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp(i\xi \cdot \Gamma(t)) \frac{dt}{t} \quad (\xi \in \mathbb{R}^n). \tag{4}
$$

So, to prove the estimate (2) for $p = 2$, it suffices to show that $m(\xi)$ is a bounded function on $\mathbb{R}^n$.

- Supported in part by NSF grants at the University of Wisconsin-Madison

A.
Cordoba et al.

To prove the estimate (2) for $p \neq 2$, one has had to take advantage of "good properties" of the Fourier transform of a measure or distribution supported on the curve $\Gamma$.
These "good" properties might be expressed in terms of certain smoothness of the function $m(\xi)$ in (4), (derivatives of $m$ must decay at $\infty$), or the decay of the Fourier transform of measures supported on $\Gamma$, or the boundedness of certain "worse" functions - i.e. worse than $m(\xi)$.
For example, in the case of a plane curve (i.e. $n = 2$) $\Gamma(t) = (t, \gamma(t))$, one might try to prove that

$$
m_{\varepsilon}(\xi, \eta) = \mathrm{p.v.} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (1 + \eta^2 \gamma^2(t))^{\varepsilon} \cdot \exp(i\xi t + i\eta \gamma(t)) \frac{dt}{t} \tag{5}
$$

is a bounded function on $\mathbb{R}^2$ for some positive $\varepsilon$.
The idea is then to show that an "improved" operator such as that corresponding to the multiplier $m_{-\delta}(\xi, \eta)$ is bounded on $L^p(\mathbb{R}^2)$, $1 &lt; p &lt; \infty$, for some positive $\delta$, and then finish by applying Stein's analytic interpolation theorem [SWe], p.
205.

The results of [NVWW1] show that estimate (2) holds for $p = 2$ for curves on which the "good" properties described above fail.
For example, $\mathcal{H}$ is bounded on $L^2(\mathbb{R}^2)$ for certain plane curves $\Gamma(t) + (t, \gamma(t))$ where $\gamma(t)$ is linear on intervals $a_j \leq t \leq b_j$ with $b_j = 2a_j$ and $a_j \to 0$ as $j \to \infty$.
By considering points $(\xi, \eta)$ orthogonal to these straight line portions of $\Gamma$, one can see that the "good" properties alluded to above fail.

In this paper, we see that the above method of obtaining $L^p$ estimates can still be used for these curves if the "bad parts" of the curve are "cut out" with appropriate Paley-Littlewood decompositions.
(The "bad parts" of the curve are treated separately via a maximal function.) We shall use the lacunary Paley-Littlewood decomposition of [CF] and [NSW1].
See §3.1 for details of this decomposition.
A similar idea of cutting out "bad" directions was previously used in the estimation of a certain maximal function; see [NSW1].

## §2.

Statement of results

Theorem.
Suppose $\Gamma(t) = (t, \gamma(t))$ ($t \in \mathbb{R}$) is a continuous plane curve with $\gamma: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ convex for $t \geq 0$, $\gamma(0) = 0$, $\gamma'(0)^+ = 0$, and $\gamma$ either even or odd.
Suppose also that $\gamma'$ has bounded doubling time: there exists a constant $C &gt; 1$ with

$$
\gamma'(C t)^+ \geq 2 \gamma'(t)^- \quad \text{for } t \geq 0. \tag{6}
$$

Then, $\mathcal{H}$ is bounded on $L^p(\mathbb{R}^2)$ (2) for $4/3 &lt; q &lt; 4$.
[The convexity hypothesis means that $[\gamma(C) - \gamma(B)]/(C - B) \geq [\gamma(B) - \gamma(A)]/(B - A)$ for $0 &lt; A &lt; B &lt; C$.]

Our theorem not only broadens the class of curves for which $L^p$ estimates ($p \neq 2$) are known, but it also extends the range of $p$ obtained for example in [NW].
Moreover, if our theorem is combined with the results of [NVWW1], we see that for any $p$ with $4/3 &lt; p &lt; 4$, the problem of $L^p$ boundedness of the Hilbert transform for even convex plane curve (with $\gamma(0) = \gamma'(0)^+ = 0$) is completely solved:

L^p
bounds for Hilbert transforms along convex curves

Corollary.
Let $\Gamma(t) = (t, \gamma(t))$ be a continuous plane curve with $\gamma \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ convex for $t \geq 0$, $\gamma(0) = \gamma'(0)^+ = 0$, and $\gamma$ an even function.
Let $4/3 &lt; p &lt; 4$.
Then, $\mathcal{H}$ is bounded on $L^p(\mathbb{R}^2)$ (2) if and only if $\gamma'$ has bounded doubling time (6).

## § 3. Proof of the theorem

We give the proof in the case that $\gamma$ is an even function; the odd case is somewhat easier.
Let us also assume that $\gamma \in C^2(0, \infty)$.
Thus, $\gamma''(t) \geq 0$ for $t \neq 0$.
The treatment of a general convex $\gamma$, which contains no substantive additional ideas, will be indicated briefly in § 3.5. Finally, a note about rigor: one should focus upon truncated operators

$$
H_{\varepsilon, N} f(x, y) = \int_{\varepsilon \leq |t| \leq N} f(x - t, y - \gamma(t)) t^{-1} dt;
$$

however, this entails so much additional notation, etc., that we choose to ignore the truncation and proceed instead in the simpler "limit operator" setting.

Throughout our proof, $C_0 &gt; 1$ will be a constant so that

$$
\gamma'(C_0 t) \geq 8 \gamma'(t) \quad \text{for } t \geq 0 \tag{7}
$$

e.g. $C_0 = C^3$, $C$ as in (6).

Some of our estimates will require the following lemma.

**Lemma A.** Van der Corput Lemma.
(See [Z], p.
197.) Suppose $\phi \colon [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is in $C^1[a,b]$, $\phi'$ is monotone, and there is a $\lambda &gt; 0$ with $|\phi'(t)| \geq \lambda$ for $a \leq t \leq b$.
Then $\left| \int_{a}^{b} \exp(i\phi(t)) dt \right| \leq B / \lambda$ for a constant $B$ independent of $a, b, f$, and $\lambda$.

## § 3.2. A Paley-Littlewood decomposition

Partition the plane into sectors $R_k$ ($k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$), each sector symmetric about both coordinate axes, as shown below.

Specifically, let

$$
R_k = \left\{ (\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : \tan(2^{-k-2} \pi) \leq |\eta/\xi| \leq \tan(2^{-k-1} \pi) \right\} \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \dots,
$$

and

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_k &amp;= \left\{ (\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : (\eta, \xi) \in R_{-k} \right\} \\
&amp;= \left\{ (\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : \tan(2^{k-2} \pi) \leq |\xi/\eta| \leq \tan(2^{k-1} \pi) \right\} \quad \text{for } k = -1, -2, \dots. \tag{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

Write $(\mathcal{T}_k^f)^* = X_{R_k} \cdot \hat{f}$.
($X_S$ denotes the characteristic function of the set $S$.) We have

**Theorem B.** (See [NSW1], Corollary 2.) For $1 &lt; p &lt; \infty$ there exists positive constants $A_p$ and $B_p$ with $A_p \| f \|_p \leq \| (\sum_k |\mathcal{T}_k^f|^2)^{1/2} \|_p \leq B_p \| f \|_p$ for each $f \in L^p(\mathbb{R}^2)$.

A.
Cordoba et al.

![img-29.jpeg](img-29.jpeg)
Fig.

1. The sectors $R_{k}$ of the Paley-Littlewood decomposition

## § 3.2. The splitting of $\mathcal{H}$

For $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ we write $\mathcal{H} = H_k + L_k$ where

$$
H f _ {k} (x, y) = \int_ {| t | \in I _ {k}} f (x - t, y - \gamma (t)) \frac {d t}{t}, \quad (x, y) \in \mathbb {R} ^ {2}. \tag {9}
$$

$I_{k} \leqslant (0, \infty)$ — to which corresponds the part of the curve “bad” for $(\xi, \eta)$ in sector $R_{k}$ — is an interval of the form $I_{k} = [\alpha_{k} / C_{0}, \alpha_{k} \cdot C_{0}]$, and $\alpha_{k}$ is chosen as follows.
Suppose $R_{k} = \{(\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} : r_{k} \leq |\xi / \eta| \leq \rho_{k}\}$; then, let $\alpha_{k}$ be any positive number with $\gamma'(\alpha_{k}) = r_{k}$.

If we use Theorem B and then the triangle inequality, we obtain

$$
A _ {p} \| \mathcal {H} f \| _ {p} \leq \| \sum_ {k} \left(\left| T _ {k} H _ {k} f \right| ^ {2}\right) ^ {1 / 2} \| _ {p} + \| \left(\sum_ {k} \left| T _ {k} L _ {k} f \right| ^ {2}\right) ^ {1 / 2} \| _ {p} \tag {10}
$$

for $1 &lt; p &lt; \infty$.
We therefore need to dominate each of the above terms by a constant multiple of $\| f\| _p$.
For the second term, we will see in §3.4 that the bounded doubling time of $\gamma^\prime$ guarantees oscillation in multiplier integrals for $T_{k}L_{k}$; this facilitates an argument via the Marcinkiewicz multiplier theorem [S], p.
109, and analytic interpolation.
The first term in (10) we control by appropriate maximal functions in §3.3.

L^p
bounds for Hilbert transforms along convex curves

# § 3.3. Estimate for $\| (\sum_{k}|T_{k}H_{k}f|^{2})^{1 / 2}\|_{p}$

For each $k$ , $T_{k}$ and $H_{k}$ commute since they are both multiplier operators.
So, if we can prove that

$$
\left\| \left(\sum_ {k} \left| H _ {k} g _ {k} \right| ^ {2}\right) ^ {1 / 2} \right\| _ {p} \leq C _ {p} \left\| \left(\sum_ {k} \left| g _ {k} \right| ^ {2}\right) ^ {1 / 2} \right\| _ {p} \quad (4 / 3 &lt;   p &lt;   4) \tag {11}
$$

for a constant $C_p$ independent of the arbitrary measurable functions $q_k$ ( $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ ), then we can apply (11) and Theorem B to obtain

$$
\left\| \left(\sum_ {k} \left| T _ {k} H _ {k} f \right| ^ {2}\right) ^ {1 / 2} \right\| _ {p} \leq B _ {p} \cdot C _ {p} \| f \| _ {p} \quad (4 / 3 &lt;   p &lt;   4).
$$

Toward a proof of (11), we define a maximal operator $M$ by

$$
M f (x, y) = \sup  _ {j \in \mathbb {Z}} | M _ {j} f (x, y) |,
$$

$$
M _ {j} f (x, y) = \left[ 2 \left(C _ {0} ^ {j + 1} - C _ {0} ^ {j}\right) \right] ^ {- 1} \int_ {J _ {j}} f (x - t, y - \gamma (t)) d t,
$$

$$
J _ {j} = \left\{t \in \mathbb {R}: C _ {0} ^ {j} \leq | t | &lt;   C _ {0} ^ {j + 1} \right\}.
$$

We see easily that $|H_{k}f(x,y)| \leq C \cdot M(|f|)(x,y)$ for a constant $C$ independent of $k, f$ , and $(x,y)$ .
Moreover, comparison via a $g$ -function to (essentially) the strong maximal function shows that $M$ is bounded on $L^{2}(\mathbb{R}^{2})$ ; see Lemma C below.
Now, we consider the inequalities

$$
\left\| \left(\sum_ {k} \left| H _ {k} g _ {k} \right| ^ {q}\right) ^ {1 / q} \right\| _ {p} \leq C _ {p} \left\| \left(\sum_ {k} \left| g _ {k} \right| ^ {g}\right) ^ {1 / q} \right\| _ {p}. \tag {13}
$$

For $p = 2$ , $q = \infty$ , we verify (13) using the positivity and $L^2$ -boundedness of $M$ .
For $p = q &gt; 1$ , (13) holds simply because each $H_{k}$ is convolution with a measure of mass $4\ln(C_0)$ .
(13) follows by interpolation (see [BP], Theorem 2) for $q = 2$ , $4/3 &lt; p \leq 2$ , and by duality for $q = 2$ , $2 \leq p &lt; 4$ .
This proves (11).

Let us now prove that $M$ is bounded on $L^2(\mathbb{R}^2)$ .
For this purpose we consider for each of the average $M_j f$ a companion average $N_j f$ , defined as follows:

$$
N _ {j} f (x, y) = \left[ 2 \left(C _ {0} ^ {j + 1} - C _ {0} ^ {j}\right) \right] ^ {- 1} \int_ {J _ {j}} \left[ \gamma \left(C _ {0} ^ {j}\right) \right] ^ {- 1} \left[ \int_ {0} ^ {\gamma \left(C _ {0} ^ {j}\right)} f (x - t, y - s) d s \right] d t, \tag {14}
$$

i.e. $N_{j}$ is convolution with the (normalized) characteristic function of the shaded set in Fig.
2.

![img-30.jpeg](img-30.jpeg)
Fig.
2

A.
Cordoba et al.

Evidently, $\sup_{j} |N_j f|$ is dominated by a constant multiple of the strong maximal function (the operator $f^*$ of [Z] p.
306, e.g.).
Thus, the $L^2$ bound for $M$ will from

**Lemma C.** Define

$$
g(f)(x, y) = \left[ \sum_{j = -\infty}^{\infty} |M_j f x, y) - N_j f x, y)|^2 \right]^{1/2}.
$$

Then $\| g(f) \|_2 \leq C \| f \|_2$ for a constant $C$ independent of $f$.

**Proof.** By the Plancherel theorem, it suffices to show that the function

$$
\sigma(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{j = -\infty}^{\infty} |m_j(\xi, \eta) - n_j(\xi, \eta)|^2, \quad (\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \tag{15}
$$

is (essentially) bounded on $\mathbb{R}^2$, where

$$
n_j(\xi, \eta) = [2(C_0^{j+1} - C_0^j)]^{-1} \int_{J_j} \exp(i\xi t) \cdot \left[ \frac{\exp(i\eta\gamma(C_0^j)) - 1}{i\eta\gamma(C_0^j)} \right] dt \tag{16}
$$

and

$$
m_j(\xi, \eta) = [2(C_0^{j+1} - C_0^j)]^{-1} \int_{J_j} \exp(i\xi t) [\exp(i\eta\gamma(t))] dt \tag{17}
$$

are the Fourier multipliers for the operators $N_j$ and $M_j$.
($J_j$ is defined in (12).)

Given $(\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^2$, consider first those integers $j$ with $|\eta| \gamma (C_0^{j+1}) \leq 1$; let us write $j \in I$.
If $j \in I$ and $t \in J_j$, we see easily that for the quantities $\alpha = [\exp(i\eta\gamma(C_0^j)) - 1] / [i\eta\gamma(C_0^j)]$ and $\beta = \exp(i\eta\gamma(t))$ appearing in (16) and (17), we have $|\alpha - \beta| &lt; |\alpha - 1| + |\beta - 1| \leq 3|\eta| \gamma (C_0^{j+1})$.
Thus for $j \in I$, $|n_j(\xi, \eta) - m_j(\xi, \eta)|^2 \leq 9|\eta\gamma(C_0^{j+1})|^2$.
Since $\gamma$ is convex, this latter quantity decreases geometrically as $j \to -\infty$.
Hence $\sum_{j \in I} |m_j(\xi, \eta) - n_j(\xi, \eta)|^2$ is bounded by a constant independent of $(\xi, \eta)$.

Next we consider those integers $j$ for which $|\eta| \gamma (C_0^j) &gt; 1$; let us write $j \in \Pi$.
But $|n_j| \leq 2 / |\eta \gamma (C_0^j)|$ for all $j$, so by again comparing with a geometric series we see that $\sum_{j \in \Pi} |n_j(\xi, \eta)|^2 \leq C$, $C$ independent of $(\xi, \eta)$.
As for $m_j$, we consider those $j \in \Pi$ for which $|\xi| \geq 2|\eta \gamma'(t)|$ whenever $t \in J_j$ — we write $j \in \Pi A$ — and those $j \in \Pi$ for which $|\eta \gamma'(t)| &gt; 2|\xi|$ whenever $t \in J_j$ — we write $j \in \Pi B$.
Put $\phi(t) = \xi t + \eta \gamma(t)$.
For $t \in J_j$ and $j \in \Pi A$ we have $|\phi'(t)| &gt; |\xi| / 2 \geq |\eta \gamma'(C_0^j)|$.
For $t \in J_j$ and $j \in \Pi B$ we have $|\phi'(t)| \geq |\eta \gamma'(t)| / 2 \geq |\eta \gamma'(C_0^j)| / 2$.
Thus, Lemma A plus convexity of $\gamma$ gives

$$
|m_j(\xi, \eta)| &lt; \frac{a}{(C_0^{j+1} - C_0^j) |\eta \gamma'(C_0^j)|} &lt; \frac{a}{(C_0 - 1) |\eta \gamma(C_0^j)|} \tag{18}
$$

for $j \in \Pi A \cup \Pi B$, a independent of $j$ and $(\beta, \eta)$.
Therefore both

$$
\sum_{j \in \Pi A} |m_j(\xi, \eta)|^2 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{j \in \Pi B} |m_j(\xi, \eta)|^2
$$

are suitably bounded.

L^p
bounds for Hilbert transforms along convex curves

Finally, there is only one $j$ in $\mathbb{Z} \setminus (1 \cup \mathrm{II})$, and (7) shows that there are at most three $j$ in $\mathrm{II} \setminus (\mathrm{II}A \cup \mathrm{II}B)$.
This is satisfactory since $|m_j(\xi, \eta)| \leq 1$ and $|n_j(\xi, \eta)| \leq 1$ for all $j$ and $(\xi, \eta)$.

## § 3.4. Estimate for $\| (\sum_{k} |T_{k}L_{k}f|^{2})^{1 / 2}\|_{p}$

For this estimate, we intend to use the Marcinkiewicz multiplier theorem [S], p.
109. However, the multiplier $\chi_{R_k}$ of the operator $T_{k}$ is not smooth.
Our first step is therefore

### § 3.4.1 Replacing $T_{k}$ by a “smooth” operator $S_{k}$.

We shall presently define operators $S_{k}$ so that $T_{k} = T_{k}S_{k}$.
Then we will be able to “replace” $T_{k}$ by $S_{k}$:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left\| \left(\sum_{k} \left| T_{k} L_{k} f \right|^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \right\|_{p} = \left\| \left(\sum_{k} \left| T_{k} S_{k} L_{k} f \right|^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \right\|_{p} \\
\leq C_{p} \left\| \left(\sum_{k} \left| S_{k} L_{k} f \right|^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \right\|_{p} \quad \text{for } 4 / 3 &lt; p &lt; 4 \tag{19}
\end{array}
$$

where $C_p$ is independent of $f$.
The inequality in (19) follows from [CF], p.
425, specifically from the estimate $\| (\sum_j |P_j f_j|^2)^{1/2} \|_p \leq C_p \| (\sum_j |f_j|^2)^{1/2} \|_p$ in which $P_j$ is the operator whose multiplier is the characteristic function of a half-plane with boundary line $\eta = \tan(2^{-j})\xi$.

Now, we choose $S_{k}$ to be the operator such that

$$
(S_{k} f)^{A} = \omega_{k} \hat{f} \tag{20}
$$

where the Fourier multiplier $\omega_{k}$ is defined as follows.
Let $\omega \colon \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus (0,0) \to [0,1]$ be a $C^\infty$ function, homogeneous of degree 0, even in each variable, $\omega(\xi, \eta) = 1$ if $\pi/4 \leq |\eta/\xi| \leq 2$, $\omega(\xi, \eta) = 0$ if $|\eta/\xi| \geq \pi$ or $|\eta/\xi| \leq 1/2$; then, for $k = 1,2,3,\ldots$ put $\omega_{k}(\xi, \eta) = \omega(\xi, 2^{k}\eta)$, and for $k = -1, -2, -3,\ldots$ put $\omega_{k}(\xi, \eta) = \omega_{-k}(\eta, \xi)$.
We verify that

$$
\omega_{k} \equiv 1 \quad \text{on} \quad R_{k} \tag{21}
$$

so that $T_{k} = T_{k}S_{k}$ and (19) holds.

Two other properties of $\omega_{k}$ will be important.
First, their support are nearly disjoint in that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text{each } (\xi, \eta) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \setminus (0, 0) \text{ is in the support} \\
\text{of at most ten of the functions } \omega_{k}.
\end{array} \tag{22}
$$

Second, for $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ and $(\xi, \eta) \in \operatorname{supp}(\omega_k)$, if $|t| \leq \alpha_k / C_0$ then $|\xi| &gt; 2|\eta \gamma'(t)|$ and if $|t| &gt; \alpha_k \cdot C_0$ then $|\eta \gamma'(t)| &gt; 2|\xi|$.
($\alpha_k$ is as defined in § 3.2.) Thus,

$$
(\xi , \eta) \in \operatorname{supp} \left(\omega_{k}\right)
$$

and

$$
t \notin I_{k} = \left[ \alpha_{k} / C_{0}, \alpha_{k} \cdot C_{0} \right] \Rightarrow | \xi + \eta \gamma'(t) | \geq \frac{1}{2} \max (| \xi |, | \eta \gamma'(t) |).
$$

### § 3.4.2. An analytic family of operators $T_{z}$.

By (19), it remains to dominate $\| (\sum_{k} |S_{k}L_{k}f|^{2})^{1/2}\|_{p}$ by a constant multiple of $\| f\|_{p}$, $4/3 &lt; p &lt; 4$.
An application

A.
Cordoba et al.

of the Rademacher functions (e.g., see [S], p.
104) shows that it is enough to prove that the operator $T$

$$
T f = \sum_{k} \pm S_{k} L_{k} f \tag{24}
$$

is bounded on $L^p(\mathbb{R}^2)$, $4/3 &lt; p &lt; 4$, with a bound independent of the choice of $\pm$ signs.
To do this, we introduce a complex parameter $z$ as follows:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
(L_{k, z} f)^{A} = m_{k, z} \cdot \hat{f} \\
m_{k, z}(\xi, \eta) = \text{p.v.} \int_{|t| \notin I_{k}} \exp(i \xi t + i \eta \gamma(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{z} \frac{dt}{t} \tag{25} \\
T_{z} f = \sum_{k} \pm S_{k} L_{k, z} f \\
\end{array}
$$

so that $T = T_{0}$ and $L_{k} = L_{k,0}$.

Now, fix an arbitrarily small $\varepsilon &gt; 0$.
In §3.4.3 we shall show that $T_{z}$ is bounded in $L^{2}(\mathbb{R}^{2})$ for $\operatorname{Re}(z) = 1 - \varepsilon$, and in §3.4.4 that $T_{z}$ is bounded on $L^{p}(\mathbb{R}^{2})$ for $\operatorname{Re}(z) = -1 - \varepsilon$ and $1 &lt; p &lt; \infty$.
Our estimates will be independent of the choice of $\pm$ signs and will grow at most polynomially in $|z|$.
The required estimate for $T$ will then follow by analytic interpolation [SWe], p.
205, and the proof of our theorem will be complete.

§ 3.4.3. $L^2$-boundedness of $T_{z}$, $\operatorname{Re}(z) = 1 - \varepsilon$.
We will show that the multiplier for $T_{z}$, $\sum_{k} \pm \omega_{k}(\xi, \eta) \cdot m_{k,z}(\xi, \eta)$, is (essentially) bounded on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
Since $|\omega_{k}| \leq 1$ and in view of (22), it suffices to show that $m_{k,z}$ is bounded (independent of $k$) on the support of $\omega_{k}$.
So, we fix $(\xi, \eta) \in \operatorname{supp}(\omega_{k})$ and write $\phi(t) = \xi t + \eta \gamma(t)$.

Estimate 1. Suppose $0 &lt; a &lt; b$ and $|\eta \gamma(t)| \leq 1$ for $a \leq |t| \leq b$.
Then

$$
\left| \int_{a \leq |t| \leq b} \exp(i \phi(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{z} t^{-1} dt \right| \leq C \cdot (1 + |z|). \tag{26}
$$

Estimate 2. Suppose that for $a \leq t \leq b$ we have

$$
|\eta \gamma(t)| \geq 1 \quad \text{and} \quad |\phi'(t)| &gt; |\eta \gamma'(t)| / 2. \tag{27}
$$

Then

$$
\left| \int_{a}^{b} \exp(i \phi(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{z} t^{-1} dt \right| \leq C (1 + |z|). \tag{28}
$$

(In both (26) and (28), $C$ is independent of $a, b, \xi, \eta$, and $\operatorname{Im}(z)$.)

Proof of Estimate 1. Let $\Phi(t) = \int_{a}^{t} \exp(i\phi(s) - i\phi(-s))s^{-1}ds$.
Then, since $\gamma$ is even, the integral to be estimated equals

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{a}^{b} \phi'(t) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{z} dt \\
= \Phi(t) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{z} \bigg|_{a}^{b} - z \int_{a}^{b} \Phi(t) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{z-1} |\eta| \gamma'(t) dt.
\end{array}
$$

L^p
bounds for Hilbert transforms along convex curves

By the $L^2$ theory of [NVWW1], Theorem 2, $|\varPhi(t)|\leq K$ for a constant $K$ depending only on the curve $\gamma$.
Thus, each boundary term in the above integration by parts is at most $2K$, and the integrated term is at most

$$
K | z | \int_{a}^{b} [1 + |\eta \gamma (t)|]^{-\varepsilon} |\eta | \gamma' (t) \, dt \leq K | z | \cdot |\eta| (\gamma (b) - \gamma (a)) \leq K | z |.
$$

Proof of Estimate 2. We use the inequalities

$$
2 | t \phi'(t)| \geq |\eta t \gamma'(t)| \geq |\eta \gamma(t)| \geq 1 \quad \text{for } a \leq t \leq b, \tag{29}
$$

and

$$
| [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z | &lt; 2 |\eta \gamma(t)|^{1 - \varepsilon} \quad \text{for } a \leq t \leq b, \tag{30}
$$

which follow from (27) and the convexity of $\gamma$.
The integral to be estimated is $\int_{a}^{b} [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z \cdot [t \phi'(t)]^{-1} \cdot d[\exp(i \phi(t))]$.
We integrate by parts in the indicated way.
For the boundary terms, we have

$$
| \exp [i \phi (t) ] \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma (t)|]^{z} / [t \phi'(t)] | \leq 4 |\eta \gamma (t)|^{1 - \varepsilon} / |\eta \gamma (t)| \leq 4.
$$

Two of the three integrated terms are

$$
\int_{a}^{b} \exp (i \phi (t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma (t)|]^{z} \cdot [t \phi'(t)]^{-1} \cdot [t \eta \gamma'(t)]^{-1} \cdot \eta \gamma'(t) \, dt
$$

and

$$
z \int_{a}^{b} \exp (i \phi (t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma (t)|]^{z - 1} \cdot [t \phi'(t)]^{-1} \cdot \eta \gamma'(t) \, dt.
$$

Both are dominated by

$$
4 (1 + | z |) \int_{a}^{b} |\eta \gamma (t)|^{-1 - \varepsilon} |\eta \gamma'(t)| \, dt \leq 4 (1 + z) \int_{1}^{\infty} u^{-1 - \varepsilon} \, du.
$$

For the third integrated term,

$$
\int_{a}^{b} \exp (i \phi (t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma (t)|]^{z} \cdot t^{-1} \cdot [\phi'(t)]^{-2} \cdot \eta \gamma''(t) \, dt,
$$

we assume with no loss of generality that $0 &lt; a &lt; b$.
This term is dominated by

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 \int_{a}^{b} |\eta \gamma(t)|^{1 - \varepsilon} \cdot t^{-1} \cdot |\eta \gamma'(t)|^{-2} \cdot |\eta| \gamma''(t) \, dt \\
&amp;\leq 8 \int_{a}^{b} |\eta t \gamma'(t)|^{1 - \varepsilon} \cdot t^{-1} \cdot |\eta \gamma'(t)|^{-2} |\eta| \gamma''(t) \, dt \\
&amp;\leq 8 |\eta|^{-\varepsilon} a^{-\varepsilon} \int_{a}^{b} |\gamma'(t)|^{-1 - \varepsilon} \gamma''(t) \, dt \leq 8 \varepsilon^{-1} (|\eta| a \gamma'(a))^{-\varepsilon} \leq 8 \varepsilon^{-1} (|\eta| \gamma(a))^{-\varepsilon} \leq 8 \varepsilon^{-1}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus Estimate 2 holds.

A.
Cordoba et al.

Property (23) and Estimates 1 and 2 imply the required bound for $m_{k,z}$ on $\operatorname{supp}(\omega_k)$.
Specifically, define $\tau &gt; 0$ by $|\eta| \gamma(\tau) = 1$.
If $\tau &lt; \alpha_k / C_0$, we treat $\int_{-\tau}^{\tau} \exp(i\phi(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z t^{-1} dt$ with Estimate 1 and $\int_{\tau}^{\tau}, \int_{\alpha_k C_0}^{\alpha_k C_0}, \int_{-\alpha_k / C_0}^{\alpha_k C_0}$, and $\int_{-\infty}^{-\alpha_k C_0}$ with Estimate 2. If $\tau \geq \alpha_k / C_0$, a singular splitting of the integral for $m_{k,z}$ suffices.

§ 3.4.4. $L^p$-boundedness of $T_z$, $\operatorname{Re}(z) = -1 - \varepsilon$, $1 &lt; p &lt; \infty$.
We will apply the Marcinkiewicz multiplier theorem [S], p.
109. For the multiplier $m$ of $T_z$, $m = \sum_k \pm \omega_k(\xi, \eta) \cdot m_{k,z}(\xi, \eta)$, we need to show that the functions $m, \xi, \frac{\partial m}{\partial \xi}, \eta, \frac{\partial m}{\partial \eta}$ and $\xi \eta, \frac{\partial^2 m}{\partial \xi \partial \eta}$ are bounded.
By (22), it suffices to verify these four estimates for $\omega_k \cdot m_{k,z}$, with bounds independent of $k$.
The form of the functions $\omega_k(\xi, \eta)$ - all dilates of $\omega(\xi, \eta)$ or of $\omega(\eta, \xi)$, $\omega$ homogeneous of degree 0 and smooth on $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus (0,0)$ - shows that we need only check the four estimates for $m_{k,z}(\xi, \eta)$ with $(\xi, \eta) \in \operatorname{supp}(\omega_k)$.
So, we again fix $(\xi, \eta) \in \operatorname{supp}(\omega_k)$ and write $\phi(t) = \xi t + \eta \gamma(t)$.

Bound for $\xi \eta \frac{\partial^2 m_{k,z}}{\partial \xi \partial \eta}$

There are two terms,

$$
\alpha = \int_{|t| \notin I_k} \exp(i\phi(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z \xi \eta \gamma(t) dt
$$

and

$$
\beta = z \int_{|t| \notin I_k} \exp(i\phi(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z^{-1} \xi \eta \gamma(t) dt.
$$

Evidently, we need only estimate $\alpha$.

Write

$$
\alpha = \int_0^{\alpha_k / C_0} \int_{-\alpha_k / C_0}^{\infty} +\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} +\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} -\alpha_k C_0.
$$

For the first of these, let $\Phi(t) = \int_0^t \exp(i\phi(s)) ds$.
By the convexity of $\gamma$, (23) and Lemma A, $|\Phi(t)| \leq 2B / |\xi|$ for $0 \leq t \leq \alpha_k / C_0$.
Integration by parts gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{\alpha_k / C_0} \exp(i\phi(t)) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z \xi \eta \gamma(t) dt \\
= \xi \Phi(t) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z \eta \gamma(t)|_0^{\alpha_k / C_0} + \text{two integrated terms}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The boundary terms are suitably bounded since $\operatorname{Re}(z) \leq -1$.
For one integrated term, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left| z \int_0^{\alpha_k / C_0} \xi \Phi(t) [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^z^{-1} \eta \gamma(t) |\eta| \gamma'(t) dt \right| \\
\leq 2B |z| \int_0^{\alpha_k / C_0} |\eta| \gamma(t) \cdot [1 + |\eta \gamma(t)|]^{-2 - \varepsilon} |\eta| \gamma'(t) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

L^p
bounds for Hilbert transforms along convex curves

which is satisfactory by comparison to $\int_0^\infty u\cdot [1 + u]^{-2 - \varepsilon}du$ .
The other integrated term,

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {\alpha_ {k} / C _ {0}} \xi \Phi (t) \cdot [ 1 + | \eta \gamma (t) | ] ^ {z} \cdot \eta \gamma^ {\prime} (t) d t,
$$

is likewise compared to $\int_0^\infty [1 + u]^{-1 - \varepsilon}du$ .
Thus, $\int_0^{a_k / C_0}dt$ is appropriately bounded, as is $\int_{-\alpha_k / C_0}$ by the same reasoning.

As for $\int_{\alpha_k C_0}^{\infty}\left(\text{and}\int_{-\infty}^{-\alpha_k C_0}\right)$ , we begin by writing

$$
\Phi (t) = \int_ {\alpha_ {k} \cdot C _ {0}} ^ {t} \exp (i \Phi (s)) d s, \quad | \Phi (t) | \leq 2 B / | \xi |,
$$

and proceed in the same way.

Bound for $\xi \frac{\partial m_{k,z}}{\partial\xi}$

This equals $\int_{|t|\notin I_k}\exp (i\Phi (t))[1 + |\eta \gamma (t)|]^z\xi dt$ , which we split into four integrals and estimate exactly as above.

Bound for $\eta \frac{\partial m_{k,z}}{\partial\eta}$

There are two terms,

$$
\alpha = \int_ {| t | \notin I _ {k}} \exp (i \Phi (t)) [ 1 + 1 | \eta \gamma (t) | ] ^ {z} \eta \gamma (t) t ^ {- 1} d t
$$

and

$$
\beta = z \int_ {| t | \notin I _ {k}} \exp (i \Phi (t)) \cdot [ 1 + | \eta \gamma (t) | ] ^ {z - 1} \eta \gamma (t) t ^ {- 1} d t.
$$

By the evenness and convexity of $\gamma$ , we have

$$
| \alpha | \leq \int_ {| t | \notin I _ {k}} [ 1 + | \eta \gamma (t) | ] ^ {- 1 - \varepsilon} | \eta \gamma^ {\prime} (t) | d t \leq \int_ {- \infty} ^ {\infty} [ 1 + | u | ] ^ {- 1 - \varepsilon} d u.
$$

$\beta$ is evidently easier.

Bound for $m_{k}(\xi ,\eta)$

This is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \text {p . v .} \int_ {| t | \notin I _ {k}} \exp (i \Phi (t)) [ 1 + | \eta \gamma (t) | ] ^ {z} t ^ {- 1} d t \\ = \left(\int_ {0} ^ {\alpha_ {k} / C _ {0}} + \int_ {\alpha_ {k} \cdot C _ {0}} ^ {\infty}\right) \left[ \exp (i \phi (t)) - \exp (i \phi (- t)) \right] \cdot [ 1 + | \eta \gamma (t) | ] ^ {z} t ^ {- 1} d t \\ \end{array}
$$

by the evenness of $\gamma$

A.
Cordoba et al.

For $\int_0^{\alpha_{\mathrm{R}} / C_0} dt$, we write $\Phi(t) = \int_0^t [\exp(i\phi(s)) - \exp(i\phi(-s))]s^{-1}ds$.
By the $L^2$ theory of [NVWW1], Theorem 2, $|\Phi(t)| \leq C = a$ constant depending only on the curve $\gamma$.
We now rewrite $\int_0^{\infty} dt$ using integration by parts in the way indicated by our choice of $\Phi$.
The resulting boundary terms are bounded because $\operatorname{Re}(z) &lt; 0$, and the integrated term is dominated by $C|z|\int_0^{\infty}[1 + u]^{-2 - \varepsilon}du$.

For $\int_{\alpha_{\mathrm{R}} / C_0}^{\infty} dt$, we write

$$
\Phi(t) = \int_{\alpha_{\mathrm{R}} / C_0}^t [\exp(i\phi(s)) - \exp(i\phi(-s))]s^{-1}ds
$$

and proceed in the same way.

## § 3.5. Extension to a general convex curve

Nearly all of our proof stands as is in the case of a general convex curve, provided one indicates one-sided derivatives, etc., as necessary.
Lemma A holds for a convex or concave $\phi$, rather than $\phi \in C^1$, with the hypothesis $|\phi'(t)| \geq \lambda$ replaced by $|\phi(s) - \phi(t)| / |s - t| \geq \lambda$.
Also, the various integrations by parts are valid, since one can check that appropriate functions are absolutely continuous.
There is one exception to this last comment: the proof of Estimate 2 in § 3.4.3, in which $\gamma''$ appears.
There, one approximates the convex curve $\gamma(t)$ by $\gamma_j(t) \equiv \psi_j * \gamma(t) - \psi_j * \gamma(0)$, where $\{\psi_j\}_{j=1}^{\infty}$ is an appropriate smooth approximate identity, each $\psi_j$ an even function with support in the interval $[-1/j, 1/j]$.
The proof given for Estimate 2 applies to $\gamma_j$ for $\int_{a+1/j}^{b-1/j} dt$.
Finish by letting $j \to \infty$.

# On the projective normality of complete linear series on an algebraic curve

Mark Green\* and Robert Lazarsfeld\*\*

Department of Mathematics, University of California, Los Angeles, CA 90024, USA

# Contents
