# Introduction

Let $X$ be a smooth irreducible complex projective curve of genus $g \geq 2$ , and let $L$ be an ample line bundle on $X$ , generated by its global sections.
Then $L$ defines a morphism

$$
\phi_ {L} \colon X \to \mathbb {P} (H ^ {0} (L)) = \mathbb {P} ^ {r},
$$

and following Mumford [18] one says that $L$ is normally generated if $L$ is very ample and if $\phi_L$ embeds $X$ as a projectively normal curve.
Equivalently, $L$ is normally generated if and only if the natural maps $S^m H^0 (L)\to H^0 (L^m)$ are surjective for all $m\geq 0$ .
Several classical results address the question of when a given line bundle is normally generated.
Most notably, a well-known theorem of Noether (c.f.
[18] or [16]) asserts that the canonical bundle defines a projectively normal embedding unless $X$ is hyperelliptic, and a result of Castelnuovo [3], Mattuck [16] and Mumford [18] states that any line bundle of degree at least $2g + 1$ is normally generated.
More recently, Lange and Martens [10] have shown that a general line bundle of degree $2g$ on a non-hyperelliptic curve is normally generated, and Arbarello, Cornalba, Griffiths and Harris [1]

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

have proven that if $X$ is a sufficiently general curve of genus $g$, then a general line bundle of degree $[\frac{3}{2} g + 2]$ or greater defines a projectively normal embedding.
The diversity of these results leads one to ask if one can obtain a unified and strengthened statement by taking into account in some quantitative way the intrinsic geometry of the curve.
Our first purpose here is to show that this is indeed the case, the invariant in question being the Clifford index of $X$.

Recall that the Clifford index of a line bundle $A$ on $X$ is defined by

$$
\operatorname{Cliff}(A) = \deg(A) - 2 \cdot r(A),
$$

where $r(A) = h^0(A) - 1$.
The Clifford index of $X$ itself is taken to be

$$
\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = \min \left\{ \operatorname{Cliff}(A) \mid h^0(A) \geq 2, h^1(A) \geq 2 \right\}
$$

(c.f.
[15, 13, 14], and (0.3) below).
This gives a rough indication of how special $X$ is in the sense of moduli.
Thus Clifford's theorem says that $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) \geq 0$ with equality if and only if $X$ is hyperelliptic, and similarly $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = 1$ if and only if $X$ is either trigonal or a smooth plane quintic.
At the other extreme, if $X$ is a general curve of genus $g$ then $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = \left\lfloor \frac{g - 1}{2} \right\rfloor$, and in any event $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) \leq \left\lfloor \frac{g - 1}{2} \right\rfloor$.

Our first result, which applies when $h^1(L) \leq 1$, generalizes the various theorems stated above:

**Theorem 1.** Let $L$ be a very ample line bundle on $X$, with

$$
\deg(L) \geq 2g + 1 - 2 \cdot h^1(L) - \operatorname{Cliff}(X)
$$

(and hence $h^1(L) \leq 1$).
Then $L$ is normally generated.

To deduce Noether's theorem, for instance, one uses the fact that $X$ is nonhyperelliptic if and only if $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) \geq 1$.
Or again, if $X$ is a general curve then $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = \left\lfloor \frac{g - 1}{2} \right\rfloor$, and so one recovers the result of Arbarello et al.

The inequality above is in general the best possible.
In fact, let us say that a very ample line bundle $L$ on $X$ is _extremal_ if

$$
\deg(L) = 2g - 2 \cdot h^1(L) - \operatorname{Cliff}(X),
$$

and if $L$ fails to be normally generated.
To get a picture of the situations in which Theorem 1 is optimal, and in the hope of finding further geometry in these questions, it is natural to look for examples of curves having a given Clifford index, but arbitrarily large genus, on which there exist extremal line bundles.
We classify all such infinite families, and at least for extremal bundles with $h^1 = 1$ the conclusion seems somewhat amusing:

**Theorem 2.** There exists an explicit constant $N(e)$ such that if $X$ is a curve of Clifford index $e$ and genus $g &gt; N(e)$, then:

(a) $X$ always carries an extremal line bundle $L$ with $h^1(L) = 0$, but never one with $h^1(L) \geq 2$;

On the projective normality of complete linear series

(b) $X$ carries an extremal line bundle $L$ with $h^1(L) = 1$ if and only if $e = 2f \geq 4$ is even, and $X$ is a two-sheeted branched covering

$$
\pi \colon X \to Y \subseteq \mathbb{P}^2
$$

of a smooth plane curve $Y$ of degree $f + 2$.

In fact, although we shall not go into all the details here, one can give a quite precise description of the extremal bundles.

Theorems 1 and 2 are consequences of a general result on the projective normality of very ample complete linear series of degree roughly $\frac{3}{2}g$ or greater.
The theme is that the failure of normal generation is accounted for by the existence of special configurations of points on $\phi_L(X) \subseteq \mathbb{P}^r$.
Specifically, consider a very ample line bundle $L$ on $X$ with $\deg(L) = 2g + 1 - k$.
Assume that $2k + 1 \leq g$ if $h^1(L) = 0$, or that $2k - 3 \leq g$ if $h^1(L) \neq 0$, and consider the embedding

$$
X \subseteq \mathbb{P}(H^0(L)) = \mathbb{P}^r
$$

defined by $L$.

Theorem 3. Under the hypotheses just stated, $L$ fails to be normally generated if and only if there exists an integer $1 \leq n \leq r - 2$, and an effective divisor $D$ on $X$ of degree at least $2n + 2$ such that

(a) $H^1(X, L^2(-D)) = 0$ and
(b) $D$ spans an $n$-plane $\Lambda \subseteq \mathbb{P}^r$ in which $D$ fails to impose independent conditions on quadrics.

Concerning assertion (b), we mean that $H^1(\Lambda, I_{D/\Lambda}(2)) \neq 0$, where $I_{D/\Lambda}$ is the ideal sheaf of $D$ in $\Lambda$.
Note that when $n = 1$ this condition is automatic, but for $n \geq 2$ it is not enough that $X$ simply have a $(2n + 2)$-secant $n$-plane.
Theorem 1 follows immediately by computing the Clifford index of the linear series cut out on $X$ by hyperplanes through $\Lambda$.
As for Theorem 2, the essential point is to combine a slight strengthening of the result just stated with Castelnuovo's bound on the genus of a space curve; our use of Castelnuovo's bound here was inspired by the techniques of Martens in [13].
The proof of Theorem 3, which occupies §1, is simple but perhaps a bit surprising.
The idea is to interpret the failure of normal generation in terms of extensions of line bundles.
One arrives at a rank two vector bundle $E$ on $X$, with determinant $\Omega_X^1$, having a large number of sections.
The required secant plane then pops out from the existence of a line sub-bundle of $E$ of suitably high degree.

It seems very likely that Theorem 3 and its corollary Theorem 1 should be the first cases of a much more general picture of how the geometry of $X$ influences the whole chain of syzygies associated to a complete linear series.
For instance, we conjecture that if $L$ is a very ample line bundle with $\deg(L) \geq 2g + 2 - 2 \cdot h^1(L) - \operatorname{Cliff}(X)$, then the homogeneous ideal of $X$ in $\mathbb{P}(H^0(L))$ is generated by quadrics unless $\phi_L$ embeds $X$ with a tri-secant line; if $\deg(L) \geq 2g + 3 - 2 \cdot h^1(L) - \operatorname{Cliff}(X)$, then the first module of syzygies among these quadrics should be generated by relations with linear coefficients unless $X$ has a 4-secant 2-plane; and so on.
Precise statements are given in §3.
They generalize an earlier conjecture of the first author [7] to the effect that one

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

should be able to read off the Clifford index of a curve from the minimal resolution of its canonical ring.
In view of the role of the Clifford index in these conjectural generalizations of several classical results, it seems of interest to understand this invariant more clearly than one does at the moment.
Hence we have also included in §3 some open problems pertaining to the Clifford index.

# § 0. Notation and conventions

(0.1) We work throughout over the complex numbers.
However the results of §1 remain valid over an algebraically closed ground field of arbitrary characteristic.

(0.2) $X$ will always be a smooth irreducible projective curve of genus $g \geq 2$.
We denote by $\Omega$ the canonical bundle on $X$.
If $F$ is a coherent sheaf on $X$ we write $H^i(F)$ instead of $H^i(X, F)$ so long as no confusion can arise; as usual, $h^i(F)$ is the dimension of the corresponding cohomology group.
We freely use Riemann-Roch and Serre duality on $X$ without explicit mention.

(0.3) The definition of $\operatorname{Cliff}(X)$ presupposes that $W_{g-1}^{1}(X) \neq \emptyset$, and hence that $g \geq 4$.
Our results remain valid for curves of genus $g \geq 2$ if we adopt the convention that $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = 0$ for $X$ of genus 2 or hyperelliptic of genus 3, and that $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = 1$ if $X$ is non-hyperelliptic of genus 3. Concerning the inequalities in the definition of $\operatorname{Cliff}(X)$, observe that the existence of a line bundle $A$ of small degree says nothing about the curve $X$ unless $h^0(A) \geq 2$.
On the other hand, $\operatorname{Cliff}(A) = \operatorname{Cliff}(\Omega \otimes A^*)$ for any line bundle $A$.
So the inequalities in the definition may be explained as simply a device for preventing such examples from contributing to the computation of $\operatorname{Cliff}(X)$.

(0.4) We will several times use the observation that if $A$ is any line bundle on $X$, then

$$
h^{0}(A) + h^{0}(\Omega \otimes A^{*}) = g + 1 - \operatorname{Cliff}(A).
$$

In fact, suppose that $A$ has degree $d$ and that $h^0(A) = r + 1$.
Then $h^0(\Omega \otimes A^*) = h^1(A) = g - d + r$, whence the stated formula.

(0.5) We say that a short exact sequence

$$
(*) \quad 0 \to F' \to F \to F'' \to 0
$$

of sheaves on $X$ is exact on global sections if $(*)$ induces an exact sequence $0 \to H^0(F') \to H^0(F) \to H^0(F'') \to 0$, or equivalently if the connecting homomorphism $H^0(F'') \to H^1(F')$ determined by $(*)$ is zero.

# § 1. Normal generation of complete linear series

This section is devoted to the proofs of Theorems 1 and 3 stated in the Introduction.
Assuming the statement of Theorem 3, we start with the

On the projective normality of complete linear series

Proof of Theorem 1. Suppose to the contrary that $L$ fails to be normally generated.
If $A$ is any line bundle of degree $2g - e$, then $\operatorname{Cliff}(A) = e - 2 \cdot h^1(A)$.
Thus the inequality in the hypothesis is equivalent to the assumption that

$$
(*) \quad \operatorname{Cliff}(L) &lt; \operatorname{Cliff}(X).
$$

Furthermore, $h^0(L) \geq 2$ since $L$ is very ample, and in view of the definition of $\operatorname{Cliff}(X)$ it follows from $(*)$ that $h^1(L) \leq 1$.
Recalling that $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) \leq (g - 1)/2$, one then finds that $L$ satisfies the numerical hypotheses of Theorem 3. Let $D$ be the divisor of degree $\geq 2n + 2$ whose existence is guaranteed by that theorem.
Since $D$ spans an $n$-plane in $\mathbb{P}(H^0(L))$, one has

$$
\operatorname{Cliff}(L(-D)) \leq \operatorname{Cliff}(L).
$$

Moreover $h^0(L(-D)) \geq 2$ and $h^1(L(-D)) \geq 2$ thanks to the fact that $1 \leq n \leq r(L) - 2$, and consequently $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) \leq \operatorname{Cliff}(L(-D))$.
Thus we have a contradiction to $(*)$, and this proves the theorem.
$\square$

We turn next to Theorem 3. Recall the assumptions: $L$ is a very ample line bundle of degree $2g + 1 - k$, where $2k + 1 \leq g$ if $h^1(L) = 0$ or $2k - 3 \leq g$ if $h^1(L) \neq 0$.

Proof of Theorem 3. Suppose first that a divisor $D$ as described exists.
Then thanks to (a) the homomorphism

$$
H^1(\mathbb{P}^r, I_{X/\mathbb{P}^r}(2)) \to H^1(\mathbb{P}^r, I_{D/\mathbb{P}^r}(2))
$$

is surjective.
Since in any event $h^1(\mathbb{P}^r, I_{D/\mathbb{P}^r}(2)) = h^1(\varLambda, I_{D/\varLambda}(2))$, it follows from (b) that $X \subseteq \mathbb{P}^r$ is not projectively normal.

Assume conversely that $L$ fails to be normally generated.
Note to begin with that the multiplication maps

$$
\mu_m \colon H^0(L) \otimes H^0(L^m) \to H^0(L^{m+1})
$$

are surjective for $m \geq 2$.
If $L$ is non-special this is a well-known general fact (c.f.
[17, Lecture 14]), while for special $L$ it follows in the degree range at hand from [7, Theorem (4.e.1)].
Hence $\mu_1$ - which we call simply $\mu$ - cannot be surjective.
Equivalently, its transpose

$$
\mu^* \colon H^0(L^2)^* \to H^0(L)^* \otimes H^0(L)^*
$$

has a non-zero kernel.

On the other hand, $H^0(L^2)^* = \operatorname{Ext}^1(L, \Omega \otimes L^*)$ classifies extensions of $L$ by $\Omega \otimes L^*$, and $\mu^*$ is identified (up to multiplication by a non-zero scalar) with the map

$$
\operatorname{Ext}^1(L, \Omega \otimes L^*) \to \operatorname{Hom}(H^0(L), H^1(\Omega \otimes L^*))
$$

which sends an extension of $L$ by $\Omega \otimes L^*$ to the connecting homomorphism it determines.
Hence there exists a non-trivial extension

$$
0 \to \Omega \otimes L^* \to E \to L \to 0 \tag{1.1}
$$

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

which is exact on global sections.
Note that $\det E = \Omega$, and that

$$
h^{0}(E) = h^{0}(L) + h^{0}(\Omega \otimes L^{*}) = g + 1 - \operatorname{Cliff}(L)
$$

(c.f.
(0.4)).

The next step is to produce a sub-bundle of $E$ of suitably large degree.
To this end we use a theorem of Ghione [5, 6], which implies that a rank two vector bundle $F$ on $X$ of degree $d$ has a line sub-bundle $A \subseteq F$ of degree $\geq a$ provided that $2a \leq d - g + 1$.
In view of the numerical hypotheses of the theorem, it follows that $E$ has a line sub-bundle $A$ with

$$
\deg(A) \geq k \quad \text{if} \quad h^{1}(L) = 0
$$

or

$$
\deg(A) \geq k - 2 \quad \text{if} \quad h^{1}(L) \neq 0.
$$

Fixing such a sub-bundle, the situation is summarized by the following diagram, in which (1.1) appears as the horizontal sequence:

(1.2)

![img-31.jpeg](img-31.jpeg)

Since $\deg(A) &gt; \deg(\Omega \otimes L^{*}) = k - 3$, the indicated homomorphism $A \to L$ is non-zero.
Hence

$$
A = L(-D)
$$

for some effective divisor $D$ on $X$, and the map $\alpha_{D}$ in (1.2) is just the natural inclusion $L(-D) \to L$ determined by $D$.
The goal now is to show that $D$ has the properties asserted in the statement of the theorem.

Let $n$ be the dimension of the linear space $\Lambda \subseteq \mathbb{P}(H^0(L)) = \mathbb{P}^r$ spanned by $D$.
We begin by proving that $\deg(D) \geq 2n + 2$ and that $1 \leq n \leq r - 2$.
Recalling that $A = L(-D)$, it is equivalent (as in the proof of Theorem 1) to verify that

(i) $\operatorname{Cliff}(A) \leq \operatorname{Cliff}(L)$ $[= k - 1 - 2 \cdot h^{1}(L)]$

and

(ii) $2 \leq h^{0}(A) \leq h^{0}(L) - 2.$

On the projective normality of complete linear series

But (i) follows immediately from the vertical sequence in (1.2), which gives:

$$
g + 1 - \operatorname {C l i f f} (L) = h ^ {0} (E) \leq h ^ {0} (A) + h ^ {0} (\Omega \otimes A ^ {*}) = g + 1 - \operatorname {C l i f f} (A).
$$

In particular, the lower bounds on $\deg(A)$ then force $\deg(A) &gt; \operatorname{Cliff}(A)$ , and consequently $h^0(A) \geq 2$ .
For the other inequality in (ii) observe to begin with that $D \neq 0$ , or else the inclusion $A = L(-D) \to E$ would split (1.1).
Since $L$ is generated by its global sections, it follows that $h^0(A) = h^0(L(-D)) \leq h^0(L) - 1$ .
Thus thanks to (i), $D$ must have degree at least two.
But this being so, if $h^0(L(-D)) = h^0(L) - 1$ then $L$ fails to be very ample.
Therefore $h^0(L(-D)) \leq h^0(L) - 2$ , and (ii) is proved.

We next show that $D$ fails to impose independent conditions on quadrics in $\Lambda$ or - what is the same thing - in $\mathbb{P}(H^0 (L))$ .
Denoting by $\mu_{D}$ the composition of the natural maps

$$
H ^ {0} (L) \otimes H ^ {0} (L) \xrightarrow {\mu} H ^ {0} (L ^ {2}) \xrightarrow [ D ]{\text {e v a l u a t i o n o n}} H ^ {0} (L ^ {2} \otimes \mathcal {O} _ {D}),
$$

it is equivalent to show that $\mu_{D}$ fails to be surjective.
But $\alpha_{D}$ gives rise to an inclusion $H^{0}(L(-D))\subseteq H^{0}(L)$ ; and setting $W_{D} = H^{0}(L) / H^{0}(L(-D))$ , evaluation on $D$ yields a homomorphism

$$
\rho_ {D} \colon H ^ {0} (L) \otimes W _ {D} \to H ^ {0} \left(L ^ {2} \otimes \mathcal {O} _ {D}\right)
$$

having the same image as $\mu_{D}$ .
So the question is in turn equivalent to proving that $\rho_{D}$ is not surjective.

Consider to this end the following commutative diagram of exact sequences:

![img-32.jpeg](img-32.jpeg)

Let $e \in \ker(\mu^*)$ be the element corresponding to the extension (1.1).
We claim that $e$ maps to zero in $H^0(L^2(-D))^* = \operatorname{Ext}^1(L(-D), \Omega \otimes L^*)$ .
In fact, by construction $\alpha_D$ lifts to a homomorphism $\tilde{\alpha}_D \colon L(-D) \to E$ and hence (1.1) induces the trivial extension of $L(-D)$ by $\Omega \otimes L^*$ .
By the diagram, there thus exists a non-zero element $\tilde{e} \in \ker(\rho_D^*)$ mapping to $e$ .
Consequently $\rho_D$ is not surjective, and this completes the proof of assertion (b) of the Theorem.

It remains only to show that $H^{1}(L^{2}(-D)) = 0$ .
But this is clear, since $L^{2}(-D) = A \otimes L$ , and

$$
\deg (A \otimes L) \geq (k - 2) + (2 g + 1 - k) = 2 g - 1. \quad \square
$$

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

(1.3) Remark.
Observe for later reference that by varying the numerical hypotheses of Theorem 3 one can obtain somewhat stronger bounds in the conclusion.
Suppose, for instance, that $e$ is a fixed non-negative integer and that $L$ is a very ample line bundle of degree $2g + 1 - k$ which fails to be normally generated.
If

$$
(*) \quad 2k + 4e + 1 \leq g,
$$

then the conclusion of the theorem holds now with

$$
(**) \quad 1 \leq n \leq r(L) - 2 - e.
$$

In fact, by virtue of $(*)$ one can assume that the line bundle $A$ constructed in the proof of Theorem 3 has degree $\geq k + 2e$.
Then

$$
k + 2e - 2 \cdot r(A) \leq \operatorname{Cliff}(A) \leq \operatorname{Cliff}(L) \leq k - 1,
$$

and it follows that $h^0(A) \geq e + 2$.
But $n = r(L) - h^0(A)$, so this gives $(**)$.

We conclude this section with two simple applications of these results and techniques.

(1.4) Corollary.
Let $L$ be a very ample line bundle of degree $2g$ on $X$, defining an embedding $X \subseteq \mathbf{IP}^g$.
Then $L$ fails to be normally generated if and only if $X$ is hyperelliptic.

Proof.
In view of Theorem 1, we need only check that if $X$ is hyperelliptic, then $L$ cannot be normally generated.
But the hyperelliptic pencil sweeps out a two-dimensional rational normal scroll $S \subseteq \mathbf{IP}^g$ containing $X$, and $S$ lies on enough quadrics to force $H^{1}(\mathbf{IP}^{g}, I_{X / \mathbf{IP}^{g}}(2)) \neq 0$.
(Compare [10, §3] for a more general result proved along these lines.) $\square$

It was suggested by Lange and Martens [10] that the result of the Corollary should hold.
(C.f.
also [8] and [9].)

(1.5) Remark.
If $L$ is a general line bundle of degree $2g$ on a hyperelliptic curve $X$, giving rise to an embedding $X \subseteq \mathbf{IP}^g$, then $X$ cannot have a $(2n + 2)$-secant $n$-plane for any $n &lt; (g - 2)/2$.
Hence the least value of $n$ for which Theorem 3 applies can be arbitrarily large.

(1.6) Corollary.
Let $L$ be a very ample line bundle on $X$ of degree $2g - 1$, with $g \geq 4$.
Then $L$ fails to be normally generated if and only if either:

(a) $X$ is hyperelliptic;

(b) $X$ is trigonal and $L = \Omega \otimes B^{*}(D)$, where $B \in W_3^1(X)$ and $D$ is an effective divisor of degree 4; or

(c) $X \subseteq \mathbf{IP}^2$ is a smooth plane quintic with embedding line bundle $B \in W_5^2(X)$, and $L = B \otimes N$ for some line bundle $N \in W_6^1(X)$ generated by its global sections.

In case (b) $\phi_L$ embeds $X$ with a 4-secant line; in case (c) $L$ defines an embedding $X \subseteq \mathbf{IP}^5$ in which $X$ has $\infty^1$ 6-secant conics.
We leave the proof of (1.6) to the interested reader.

On the projective normality of complete linear series

# $\S 2$ .

Classification of extremal line bundles on curves of large genus

Let $X$ be a smooth curve of genus $g$ and Clifford index $e$ , and let $L$ be a very ample line bundle on $X$ of degree

$(\star)$ $\deg (L) = 2g - 2i - e,$

where $i = h^{1}(L)$ is the index of speciality of $L$ .
Motivated by Theorem 1, we say as in the Introduction that such a line bundle is extremal if it fails to be normally generated.
This section is devoted to the classification of extremal line bundles on curves for which $g &gt; N(e)$ , where

$$
N (e) \underset {\text {d e f}} {=} \max  \left\{\binom {e + 3} {2}, 1 0 e + 6 \right\}.
$$

Specifically, we will prove

(2.1) Theorem.
Let $L$ be a very ample line bundle satisfying $(\star)$ .
Assume that $g &gt; N(e)$ and that $X$ is neither hyperelliptic nor elliptic-hyperelliptic.
Then $L$ fails to be normally generated if and only if the pair $(X, L)$ falls into one of the three families listed in the following Table (2.2):

(2.2) Table

|      | X                                                                              | h1(L) | φL                                                  |
| ---- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| I.   | Has a g1e+2                                                                    | 0     | Embeds X with a 4-secant line                       |
| II.  | e=2f≧4;X is a double coveringπ: X→Y⊆IP2of a smooth plane curveY of degree f+2. | 1     | Embeds X with a 4-secant line                       |
| III. | as in II                                                                       | 0     | Embeds X with a 6-secant conic,but no 4-secant line |

Concerning (I) we note that according to a theorem of Martens [13], any curve of Clifford index $e$ and genus $g &gt; (e + 3)(e + 2)/2$ carries a $g_{e + 2}^{1}$ .
We remark also that each of the families described in (2.2) does in fact occur for every $g &gt; N(e)$ ; the construction of the extremal bundles is sketched in (2.5) below.
As for the two classes of curves excluded from the theorem, the hyperelliptic case is covered by Corollary 1.4 and the observation that no special line bundle on a hyperelliptic curve is very ample.
The situation on elliptic-hyperelliptic curves is discussed in (2.6).
Taken together, these results constitute the proof of Theorem 2.

Theorem (2.1) depends on the following elementary consequence of Castelnuovo's bound on the geometric genus of a non-degenerate space curve.
Martens has used this bound in a similar way in [13].
Recall that a line

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

bundle $B$ on $X$ is birationally very ample if $B$ is generated by its global sections and the corresponding map $\phi_B$ is birational onto its image.

(2.3) Lemma.
Let $X$ be a curve of genus $g$, and let $B$ be a line bundle on $X$ with $r(B) = r$ and $\deg(B) = 2r + e$ for some $e \geq 0$.
If $B$ is birationally very ample, then either

$$
r \geq g - 2e - 1 \quad \text{or} \quad g \leq (e + 3)(e + 2)/2.
$$

We omit the proof.

Proof of Theorem (2.1).
It is immediate from Theorem 3 that if $L$ is a very ample bundle of the stated degree for which $\phi_L$ has one of the properties described, then $L$ cannot be normally generated.

Assume conversely that $L$ fails to define a projectively normal embedding.
Since $L$ is very ample and $g &gt; (e + 3)(e + 2)/2$, it follows from Lemma (2.3) that

$$
r(L) = g - i - e \geq g - 2e - 1,
$$

i.e. that $i \leq e + 1$.
Then thanks to the fact that $g &gt; N(e) \geq 10e + 6$ we are in the situation of Remark (1.3).
Thus Theorem 3 gives the existence of an integer

$$
1 \leq n \leq r(L) - 2 - e,
$$

and an effective divisor $D$ of degree $\geq 2n + 2$ which spans an $n$-plane in $\mathbb{P}(H^0(L))$.

Since $\operatorname{Cliff}(L) = \operatorname{Cliff}(X)$, one must in fact have $\deg D = 2n + 2$, and hence $h^1(L(-D)) = n + i + 1$.
Setting

$$
B = \Omega \otimes L^*(D), \tag{2.4}
$$

this means that $\deg B = 2n + 2i + e$ and $r(B) = n + i$.
In particular $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = \operatorname{Cliff}(B)$, and so $B$ is generated by its global sections.

If $r(B) = 1$ then $i = 0$, $B$ is a $g_{e+2}^1$, and we are in case (I).
Assuming that $r(B) \geq 2$, consider the map

$$
\phi_B \colon X \to \mathbb{P}^{n + i}.
$$

We claim, and this is the main point, that $\phi_B$ cannot be birational onto its image.
Indeed, if on the contrary $B$ were birationally very ample, then since $g &gt; (e + 3)(e + 2)/2$ Lemma (2.3) would give the inequality

$$
n + i = r(B) \geq g - 2e - 1.
$$

But this contradicts our upper bound on $n$.

The upshot is that $\phi_B$ factors through a branched covering $\pi \colon X \to Y$ of degree $m \geq 2$, where $Y$ is a smooth curve mapped birationally onto its image in $\mathbb{P}^{n + i}$ by a line bundle $B_0$ with $r(B_0) = r(B) = n + i$, and $B = \pi^*(B_0)$:

![img-33.jpeg](img-33.jpeg)

On the projective normality of complete linear series

If $m \geq 3$ then $\operatorname{Cliff}(\pi^*(B_0(-y))) &lt; \operatorname{Cliff}(B) = \operatorname{Cliff}(X)$ for any $y \in Y$.
But $h^0(\pi^*(B_0(-y))) \geq 2$ and $h^1(\pi^*(B_0(-y))) \geq 2$, so this is impossible.
Therefore $m = 2$, $e = 2f$ is even, and $\deg(B_0) = n + i + f$.
In fact, for the same reasons of Clifford index, $B_0$ must be very ample and $Y \subseteq \mathbb{P}^{n + i}$ cannot have any $(s + 2)$-secant $s$-planes if $s \leq n + i - 2$.
But for $s \geq 1$, any smooth non-degenerate curve $Y \subseteq \mathbb{P}^{s + 2}$ of degree at least $s + 4$ has an $(s + 2)$-secant $s$-plane; this is standard if $s = 1$, and one may reduce to this case by projecting from general points of $Y$.
Hence if $n + i \geq 3$, then $Y$ must be rational or elliptic normal, and $X$ in turn is hyper-elliptic or elliptic-hyperelliptic.
So we must have $n + i = 2$, and

$$
Y \subseteq \mathbb{P}^2
$$

is a smooth plane curve of degree $f + 2 \geq 4$.
If $n = 1$ then $i = 1$ and we are in case (II); case (III) occurs if $n = 2$ and $i = 0$.
In either event it follows from Theorem 3 that $\phi_L$ must have the stated properties, except that in (III) it might happen that $D = F + D'$ for some effective divisors $F$ and $D'$ of degree respectively 2 and 4, where $\phi_L$ maps $D'$ to a line.
But then $B' = B(-F)$ is a $\mathbf{g}_{e+2}^1$, and we revert to case I.

(2.5) Remark.
The three families described in Theorem (2.1) do in fact occur for all $g &gt; N(e)$, and we indicate here how the extremal bundles arise.
To this end, note that the previous proof shows exactly what the line bundle

$$
B \in W_{2n+2i+e}^{n+i}(X)
$$

of (2.4) must be in each of the three cases, and then

$$
L = \Omega \otimes B^*(D) \tag{*}
$$

for some effective divisor $D$ of degree $2n + 2$ on $X$.
The extremal bundles are determined via $(*)$ by the following recipes:

Case I.
Take $X$ to be a curve of Clifford index $e$ and genus $g &gt; N(e)$ with a $g_{e+2}^{1}$ but no $g_{e+4}^{2}$; for instance $X$ could be a general $(e+2)$-gonal curve of the appropriate genus.
Then take

$$
B \in W_{e+2}^{1}(X) \quad \text{and} \quad D = x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4,
$$

where the $x_i$ are distinct points of $X$, no two of which lie in the same fibre of $\phi_B$.

For cases II and III one starts with a smooth plane curve

$$
Y \subseteq \mathbb{P}^2,
$$

of degree $f + 2 \geq 4$, with embedding line bundle $B_0 \in W_{f+2}^2(Y)$.
Set $e = 2f$, and let $X$ be any curve of genus $g &gt; N(e)$ arising as a double covering

$$
\pi: X \to Y \subseteq \mathbb{P}^2;
$$

one checks that $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = e$.

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

Case II.
Take

$$
B = \pi^ {*} \left(B _ {0}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad D = x _ {1} + x _ {2} + x _ {3} + x _ {4}
$$

where the $x_{i}$ are distinct points on $X$ mapping via $\pi$ to four distinct collinear points of $Y$.

Case III.
Take

$$
B = \pi^ {*} \left(B _ {0}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad D = x _ {1} + \dots + x _ {6}
$$

where the $x_{i}$ are distinct points on $X$ mapping to six distinct points of $Y$ which lie on a conic in $\mathbb{P}^2$, but with no four collinear.

In each case the resulting line bundle

$$
L = \Omega \otimes B ^ {*} (D)
$$

is very ample, with the appropriate index of speciality; the divisor $D$ spans a 4-secant line in cases I and II, and a 6-secant conic in case III.
Moreover, if $D$ is reduced these are the only possibilities.
(For general $D$ the descriptions become a bit more involved, and in case I if $X$ has a $g_{e+4}^2$ then there are some additional restrictions on $D$.)

(2.6) Remark.
To complete the picture, it remains to describe extremal line bundles on an elliptic-hyperelliptic curve $X$, i.e. a curve which admits a degree two covering $\pi \colon X \to Y$, with $Y$ elliptic.
Assume that $X$ has genus $g &gt; N(2) = 26$.
First of all, arguing from the proof of Theorem (2.1), one easily shows:

(a) There is no extremal line bundle $L$ on $X$ with $h^1 (L)\neq 0$.

By contrast, there are at least two irreducible families of extremal non-special line bundles on $X$:

(b) Let $L$ be a very ample line bundle of degree $2g - 2$ on $X$, with $h^1(L) = 0$.
Then $L$ fails to be normally generated if and only if either $\phi_L$ embeds $X$ with a four-secant line, or else

$$
(*) \quad \det  (\pi_ {*} L) = \det  (\pi_ {*} \Omega).
$$

In the former case $(\ast)$ is not generally satisfied, and hence the family of extremal non-special line bundles on $X$ has one irreducible component of dimension 5 (parametrizing embeddings with 4-secant lines), and also one or more $(g - 1)$-dimensional components defined by $(\ast)$.

In an earlier version of this paper we included a rather lengthy proof of (b) based on the results and techniques of §1; however Lange and Martens pointed out to us that the result can be deduced (for a wider range of genera) from the proofs in their paper [11].

§ 3. Conjectures and open problems

We discuss in this section a number of open questions related to the above circle of ideas.

On the projective normality of complete linear series

# § 3a.

Higher syzygies

Results on normal generation frequently go hand in hand with analogous statements giving conditions under which a curve is cut out by quadrics (c.f.
[18] and [22]).
Until recently, however, the corresponding questions for higher syzygies seem not to have been addressed.
But as some of the results and conjectures of [7] suggest, it is in fact very reasonable to expect that "lower order" results will extend in a natural way to higher syzygies, and that moreover these extensions may shed new light on the classical situations.
We present and discuss here some conjectures along these lines.

We start with some notation.
With $X$ and $L$ as in the Introduction, set

$$
R = R (L) = \bigoplus_ {n} H ^ {0} \left(L ^ {n}\right).
$$

Thus $R(L)$ is a Cohen-Macaulay module of dimension two over the homogeneous coordinate ring $S = \operatorname{Sym}(H^0(L))$ .
Let $E_{\cdot} = E_{\cdot}(L)$ be a minimal graded free resolution of $R(L)$ over $S$ :

$$
0 \rightarrow E _ {r - 1} \rightarrow E _ {r - 2} \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow E _ {1} \rightarrow E _ {0} \rightarrow R \rightarrow 0, \tag {3.1}
$$

where each $E_{i} = E_{i}(L)$ is a direct sum of twists of $S$ , and $r = r(L)$ .
As we are dealing with complete linear series, one has:

(i) $E_0 = S \oplus E_0'$ , with $E_0' = \oplus S(-a_{0j})$ and all $a_{0j} \geq 2$ ; and, for $i \geq 1$ ,
(ii) $E_{i} = \oplus S(-a_{ij})$ ，where all $a_{ij}\geq i + 1$

The natural way to extend the various classical results alluded to above is to ask when the first few terms in $E_{\cdot}(L)$ are as simple as possible.
Specifically, we ask whether, for a given integer $p \geq 0$ , $L$ enjoys the following property:

$$
\begin{array}{l l} \mathrm {(N _ {p})} &amp; E _ {0} (L) = S \quad \text {a n d} \\ &amp; E _ {i} (L) = \oplus S (- i - 1) \quad (\text {i . e . a l l} a _ {i j} = i + 1) \text {f o r} 1 \leq i \leq p. \end{array}
$$

Thus, very concretely:

$(\mathrm{N}_0)$ holds for $L$ iff $L$ is normally generated;
$(\mathrm{N}_1)$ holds for $L$ iff $L$ is normally generated, and the homogeneous ideal of $X$ in $\mathbb{P}(H^0 (L))$ is generated by quadrics;
$(\mathrm{N}_2)$ holds for $L$ iff $(\mathrm{N}_0)$ and $(\mathrm{N}_1)$ do, and the module of syzygies among quadratic generators $Q_{i}\in I_{X / \mathbb{P}^{r}}$ is spanned by relations of the form

$$
\sum L _ {i} Q _ {i} = 0,
$$

where the $L_{i}$ are linear polynomials;

and so on.
Property $(\mathrm{N}_1)$ is what Mumford calls "normal presentation" in [18]; in the terminology of [7], $(\mathrm{N_p})$ is equivalent to the vanishing of the Koszul cohomology groups $K_{i,j}(L)$ for $i\leq p$ and $j\geq 2$

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

The theorem of Castelnuovo-Mattuck-Mumford on projective normality was generalized to higher syzygies by the first author in [7]:

(3.2) If $\deg(L) \geq 2g + 1 + p$ , then $L$ satisfies property $(\mathbf{N}_{\mathfrak{p}})$ .

In particular, one recovers a result of Fujita [4] and St.
Donat [23] to the effect that if $\deg(L) \geq 2g + 2$ , then $X$ is cut out by quadrics in $\mathbb{P}(H^0(L))$ .
The examples for which (3.2) is optimal have been classified by the authors, and one finds:

(3.3) If $\deg(L) = 2g + p$ , then property $(\mathbf{N}_{\mathfrak{p}})$ fails for $L$ if and only if either:

(a) $X$ is hyperelliptic; or
(b) $\phi_L$ embeds $X$ with a $(p + 2)$ -secant $p$ -plane, i.e. $H^0 (L\otimes \Omega^*)\neq 0$

(When $p = 0$ , the statement in (b) should be interpreted to mean that $\phi_L$ is not, in fact, an embedding: compare Corollary (1.4) above.) The proof will appear elsewhere.

In light of Theorem 1, the natural generalization of (3.2) and (3.3) is the following:

(3.4) Conjecture.
Assume that $L$ is very ample, with

$$
\deg (L) \geq 2 g + 1 + p - 2 \cdot h ^ {1} (L) - \operatorname {C l i f f} (X).
$$

Then property $(\mathbf{N}_{\mathfrak{p}})$ holds for $L$ unless $\phi_L$ embeds $X$ with a $(p + 2)$ -secant $p$ -plane.

In fact, one would hope for a more precise statement along the lines of Theorem 3.

A particularly interesting case of (3.4) is when $L = \Omega$ .
Then the conjecture states that the canonical bundle satisfies $(\mathbf{N}_{\mathfrak{p}})$ for $p &lt; \operatorname{Cliff}(X)$ .
On the other hand, it was shown in the appendix to [7] that $(\mathbf{N}_{\mathfrak{p}})$ does not hold for $\Omega$ if $p \geq \operatorname{Cliff}(X)$ .
Hence (3.4) contains as a special case the "Noether-Enriques-Babbage-Petri" conjecture of the first author:

(3.5) Conjecture ([7, Conjecture (5.1)].
The Clifford index of $X$ is equal to the least integer $p$ for which property $(\mathbf{N}_{\mathfrak{p}})$ fails for the canonical bundle $\Omega$ .

In other words, knowing the Clifford index of $X$ should be equivalent to knowing the degrees of the terms in the minimal resolution of its canonical ring.
The conjecture would generalize – and clarify – Petri's theorem [20] that the homogeneous ideal of a non-hyperelliptic canonical curve $X$ is generated by quadrics unless $X$ is trigonal or a smooth plane quintic, i.e. unless $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = 1$ .
The conjecture has been verified by Schreyer [21] for $g \leq 8$ .

In a somewhat different direction, there is a conjecture which would give a complete picture of the grading of the resolution (3.1) when $\deg(L)$ is large compared to $2g$ .
In particular $L$ will be non-special, and then it is elementary that there are only two degrees in which generators of $E_i(L)$ might occur.
Specifically, so long as $h^1(L) = 0$ one has:

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c} &amp; S &amp; \oplus S (- i - 1) \\ E _ {0} = &amp; \oplus &amp; \text {a n d} &amp; E _ {i} = \oplus \\ &amp; \oplus S (- 2) &amp; &amp; \oplus S (- i - 2) \end{array} \qquad \text {i f} i \geq 1,
$$

On the projective normality of complete linear series

although it is not necessarily the case that summands of both degrees are actually present.
In fact, always assuming that $h^1(L) = 0$, one finds from (3.2) that

$$
E _ {0} = S \text{ if } 0 \leq (r - 1) - g \text{ and } E _ {i} = \bigoplus S (- i - 1) \text{ for } 1 \leq i \leq (r - 1) - g,
$$

where as usual $r = r(L)$.
On the other hand, Schreyer observed that if $\deg(L) \geq 3g - 2$ then for every $i \geq r - g$ the module $E_i(L)$ must have some $S(-i - 2)$ summands (this follows, e.g., from (3.3)).
This leaves undetermined only whether or not, for a given integer $1 \leq q \leq g$, $E_{r - q}(L)$ has generators in more than one degree.
More precisely, we ask whether $L$ satisfies the following property for some $1 \leq q \leq g$:

$$
\left(\mathrm {M} _ {\mathrm {q}}\right) \quad E _ {i} (L) = \bigoplus S (- i - 2) \quad \text{for } i \geq r - q.
$$

In the terminology of [7], $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{q}})$ is equivalent to the vanishing of $K_{i,j}(L)$ for $i\geq r - q$ and $j\neq 2$.

To interpret property $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{q}})$ somewhat more geometrically, recall that by Serre duality $\tilde{E} \cdot (-r - 1)$ is a minimal resolution of the $S$-module

$$
W = W (L) = \bigoplus_ {n} H ^ {0} (\Omega \otimes L ^ {n}).
$$

(This module was studied by Arbarello and Sernesi in [2].) Hence if $L$ is non-special, then:

$(\mathbf{M}_1)$ holds for $L \Leftrightarrow$ the map $H^0 (L) \otimes H^0 (\Omega) \to H^0 (L \otimes \Omega)$ is surjective;

$(\mathbf{M}_2)$ holds for $L \Leftrightarrow (\mathbf{M}_1)$ does, and if $\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_g \in H^0(\Omega)$ is a basis, then all relations of the form

$$
\sum P _ {i} \omega_ {i} = 0 \in W (L), \quad \text{where } P _ {i} \in S ^ {k} H ^ {0} (L),
$$

are already generated by those relations in which the $P_{i}$ are linear polynomials;

and so on.
The “$K_{p,1}$ Theorem” of [7] gives a geometric criterion, in a more general setting, for the failure of $(\mathbf{M}_1)$ or $(\mathbf{M}_2)$.
In particular, one finds:

(3.6) If $\deg(L) \geq 2g + 1$, then $(\mathbf{M}_1)$ fails for $L$ if and only if $X = \mathbf{IP}^1$; If $\deg(L) \geq 2g + 2$, then $(\mathbf{M}_2)$ fails for $L$ if and only if $X$ carries a $g_2^1$.

This suggests:

(3.7) Conjecture.
If $\deg(L)$ is sufficiently large compared to $2g$, then property $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{q}})$ fails for $L$ if and only if $X$ carries a $g_{q}^{1}$, i.e., a line bundle $A$ with $\deg(A) = q$ and $r(A) = 1$.

Ideally $\deg(L) \geq 2g + q$ (and hence also $\deg(L) \geq 3g$) would do; one certainly wants a bound independent of $q$.
Then (3.7) would have the surprising consequence that one could read off the "gonality" of a curve from the minimal resolution of any one line bundle of sufficiently large degree.
One implication in the statement is known: it is elementary that if $X$ carries a $g_q^1$, then no line bundle of degree $\geq 2g + q$ can satisfy $(\mathbf{M}_q)$.
However we remark that of the three conjectures presented here, this is the one of which we are the least convinced.

M.
Green and R.
Lazarsfeld

# § 3b.

The Clifford index

Our results for projective normality, and the conjectures just stated, suggest that the Clifford index is an important invariant of a curve for questions of an algebraic nature involving complete linear series.
Hence it seems worthwhile to understand this invariant as closely as possible.
Martens [13, 14] has taken some basic steps in this direction, but much remains to be learned.
To begin with, it is not known that there even exist curves of arbitrary Clifford index $0 \leq e \leq \left[\frac{g - 1}{2}\right]$ for a given genus $g$.
The obvious thing to expect here is:

(3.8) Conjecture.
Given an integer $0 \leq e \leq \left[\frac{g - 1}{2}\right]$, if $X$ is a sufficiently general $(e + 2)$-gonal curve of genus $g$, then $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) = e$.

The issue of course is to rule out the possibility that $\operatorname{Cliff}(X) &lt; e$; this is elementary if $g$ is reasonably large compared to $e$ (e.g. $g &gt; 4e$).

Define the Clifford dimension of a curve $X$ to be the integer

$$
r (X) = \min  \left\{r (A) \left| \begin{array}{l} A \text{ is a line bundle on } X, \\ \text{with } h ^ {0} (A) \geq 2, h ^ {1} (A) \geq 2, \\ \text{s.t. } \operatorname {Cliff} (X) = \operatorname {Cliff} (A) \end{array} \right. \right\}.
$$

In other words, the Clifford dimension of $X$ is the least dimension of a linear series which computes the Clifford index of $X$.
For instance Martens' theorem [13] states that if $X$ has Clifford index $e$ and genus $g &gt; (e + 3)(e + 2)/2$ then $r(X) = 1$, while if $X$ is a smooth plane curve then $r(X) = 2$.
Martens has pointed out that it is very rare to find a curve with Clifford dimension $\geq 3$.
For if $r(X) = r \geq 3$, and if $X \subseteq \mathbb{P}^r$ is embedded by a line bundle computing $\operatorname{Cliff}(X)$, then evidently $X$ cannot have any $(2r - 2)$-secant $(r - 2)$-planes.
But a formula of Castelnuovo counting the number of such planes (c.f.
[14] or [1]) then puts severe restrictions on the possible genus and Clifford index of $X$.
Martens uses these ideas to prove the remarkable fact [14]:

(3.9) $r(X) = 3$ if and only if $X$ is the complete intersection of two cubic surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^3$.

In a letter to the authors, Martens also points out that the example in (3.9) should be merely the first in an infinite family:

(3.10) Problem (G.
Martens).
Show that for each $r \geq 3$ there exist smooth curves

$$
\begin{array}{c}
X \subseteq \mathbb {P} ^ {r} \\
\text{with:} \\
\deg (X) = 4 r - 3 \qquad r (X) = r, \\
g (X) = 4 r - 2, \quad \operatorname {Cliff} (X) = 2 r - 3.
\end{array}
$$

As Martens notes, one would expect such curves to be half-canonically embedded and projectively normal.
In fact, it seems very likely that the desired curves will exist on suitable $K3$ surfaces $S \subseteq \mathbb{P}^r$, but it's not immediately clear how to verify that the candidates have the stated Clifford index and dimension.

On the projective normality of complete linear series

(3.11) Problem.
Is it true that any curve $X$ with Clifford dimension $r(X) \geq 3$ is in one of the families specified in (3.10)?

This might seem like too much to expect, but in view of (3.9) there doesn't appear to be any reason not to hope for a simple picture.
Furthermore, a home computer was used to make numerical calculations based on Castelnuovo's secant-plane formula.
It turned out that for $r = 4$ and 5, and $g \leq 100$ , there were no other possibilities except for a few with $\deg(X) = g - 1$ , some of which could be ruled out for non-numerical reasons.
An affirmative solution to (3.11) together with a proof of (3.5) would yield an essentially complete list of all curves for which the minimal resolution of the canonical ring has a given grading.
Presumably a positive solution to (3.11) would also imply Conjecture (3.8), at least if one had a good enough understanding of the linear series on the curves in (3.10).

# The Atiyah-Singer index theorem for families of Dirac operators: Two heat equation proofs

Jean-Michel Bismut

Département de Mathématique, Université Paris-Sud, F-91405 Orsay, France

Summary.
The purpose of this paper is to give two heat equation proofs of the Index Theorem of Atiyah-Singer for a family of Dirac operators.

b) The Dirac operator in the fibers 105

J.
M.
Bismut

# Introduction

In [8], Atiyah and Singer gave a proof of the Index Theorem for elliptic operators.
They showed that on a compact manifold $X$, as morphisms from $K(TX)$ into $Z$, the analytic and topological indexes coincide.

In [9], they extended the methods of [8] to a family of elliptic operators $D_y$ acting on a fiber bundle $M$ over a compact space $B$.
They proved that as morphisms taking values in $K(B)$, the analytic and topological indexes coincide.
As a consequence, they could determine the Chern character of the difference bundle $\operatorname{Ker}D - \operatorname{Coker}D$.
The proof of the Index Theorem for families given in [9] was a simple extension of the proof in [8] for the Index Theorem.
The results of [9] extended and generalized earlier results by Shih [34].

The purpose of this paper is to give two heat equation proofs of the Index Theorem for a family of Dirac operators $D_y$.
More precisely, we determine the Chern character $ch(\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D)$.

Let us recall that Atiyah et al.
[6], extending previous results of Patodi [32] and Gilkey [28, 29], had shown how to obtain the index for the Dirac operator $D$ acting on a twisted spin complex. They used the formula for $t &gt; 0$

$$
\operatorname{Ind} D = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t D^* D}{2}} \right] - \operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t D D^*}{2}} \right] \tag{0.1}
$$

to show that $\operatorname{Ind}D$ can be expressed as

$$
\operatorname{Ind} D = \int J_t(x) \, dx \tag{0.2}
$$

Using Gilkey's theory of invariants, they proved by algebraic arguments that as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, $\lim J_t(x)$ exists and they showed that the limit $J(x)$ could be expressed in terms of certain characteristic classes using Chern-Weil's theory.
So the heat equation method proved the Index Theorem for Dirac operators.
Also Atiyah et al.
[6] showed how to obtain the general Index Theorem for elliptic operators by using the periodicity Theorem of Bott.

More recently, Witten [38], Alvarez-Gaumé [1], Friedan and Windey [25] gave a supersymmetric interpretation of formula (0.1).
This led Getzler [26] to give a proof of the Index Theorem for Dirac operators on twisted spin complexes using pseudo-differential operators.
In Bismut [18], we gave a probabilistic proof of the Index Theorem for Dirac operators using a probabilistic representation of the heat kernel [16] and we proved the Lefschetz fixed point formulas of Atiyah and Bott [5], Atiyah and Singer [8].
Another proof of the Index Theorem and of the fixed point formulas for Dirac operators was provided by Berline and Vergne [12] using the heat equation in the bundle of orthonormal frames.
Still another proof was given by Getzler [27] using a detailed analysis on the Clifford algebra.
The proofs of [12, 18, 26, 27] use in a fundamental way the formula of Lichnerowicz [30] which calculates the square of the Dirac operator on the spin complex

$$
D^2 = -\Delta^H + \frac{K}{4}, \tag{0.3}
$$

where $\Delta^H$ is the Bochner Laplacian, and $K$ the scalar curvature.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

In Bismut [20a], we gave a heat equation proof of the infinitesimal Lefschetz formulas, which calculate the trace of certain representation of groups as an integral over the considered manifold.
The proof in [20a] involves an extension of formula (0.3).
In fact if $Y$ is a Killing vector field, acting on spinors by Clifford multiplication, if $L_{Y}$ is the Lie derivative operator lifted to spinors, we showed in [20a] that for $t &gt; 0$

$$
- \frac {t}{2} \left(D + \frac {Y}{2 t}\right) ^ {2} - L _ {Y} = \frac {t}{2} \Delta^ {- \frac {Y}{2 t}, H} - \frac {t K}{8}. \tag {0.4}
$$

In (0.4), $\Delta^{-\frac{Y}{2t},H}$ is the Bochner Laplacian associated with the connection on spinors $V. - \left\langle \frac{Y}{2t}, \cdot \right\rangle$.
Formula (0.4) permitted us in [20a] to obtain the infinitesimal Lefschetz formulas in exactly the same way as the ordinary index [18], the analysis of the singular term $\frac{Y}{2t}$ (for $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$) being very easy by probabilistic arguments.

As pointed out in Atiyah and Singer [9], the Index Theorem for families is a consequence of the Lefschetz fixed point formulas in the special case where the holonomy group of the fibration $M_{\overline{X}^*}B$ can be taken to be compact.
In this case, (0.1), (0.3) can be generalized in a straightforward way using (0.4).

So in this paper we are essentially interested in the case where the holonomy group is not compact.
We now briefly explain the main steps of the proofs.

# 1. Extension of formula (0.1): Chern-Weil's theory in infinite dimensions

To start with, we obviously need an analogue of formula (0.1) which will give us an expression for $ch(\operatorname{Ker}D - \operatorname{Coker}D)$.

First note that the fibers $G_{y}$ of the fibering $M_{\overline{X}^{*}}B$ can be naturally endowed with a connection $\tilde{V}$ by defining a horizontal lift of $TB$ in $TM$.
So there is a geometric way of putting a connection on the infinite dimensional bundle $H^{\infty}$ of the $C^\infty$ sections over $G$ of a given finite dimensional vector bundle.
However the difficulty is now that in general $\mathrm{Ker}D$, $\mathrm{Coker}D$ are not parallel for $\tilde{V}$.

This difficulty is overcome by extending to infinite dimensions an idea of Quillen [33].
In fact in [33], Quillen has considered the case of a $Z_{2}$-graded finite dimensional vector bundle $E = E_0 \oplus E_1$ endowed with an "odd" linear mapping $u$ exchanging $E_0$ and $E_1$.
By introducing certain $Z_{2}$ graded - or super - algebras, he showed how to construct a family of differential forms representing $ch(E_0 - E_1)$ which take into account the mapping $u$.
Note that in [33], Quillen was thinking explicitly of extending his formalism to treat the case of the Index Theorem for families.

This is exactly the program which we follow in Sect.
2. We consider $\tilde{V} + D$ as a superconnection on $H^{\infty}$ whose curvature $(\tilde{V} + D)_y^2$ is an elliptic operator over $H_y^\infty \otimes \Lambda_y(B)$.
We then show that if $\mathrm{Tr}_s$ is the supertrace which is well-defined on $C^\infty$ kernels

$$
\operatorname {T r} _ {s} e ^ {- \frac {(\tilde {V} + D) ^ {2}}{2}} \tag {0.5}
$$

94
J.
M.
Bismut

represents in cohomology $\overline{ch}(\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D)$ (which is the Chern character of $\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D$ using different weights).
The proof that (0.5) is a closed differential form over the base $B$ is not difficult.
It is much harder to prove it represents $\overline{ch}(\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D)$.
This is done by modifying the connection $\vec{V}$ into a connection $\vec{V}'$ which is defined using pseudodifferential operators along the fiber, for which it is almost "obvious" that

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_{s} e^{-\frac{(\vec{V}' + D)^2}{2}} \tag{0.6}
$$

represents $\overline{ch}(\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D)$.

Section 2 uses the machinery of pseudodifferential operators and is quite technical.
Of course the results of Sect.
2 are obvious when the infinitesimal Lefschetz formulas can be directly applied.

## 2. The first proof: The Levi-Civita superconnection

The idea is then to have a parameter $t$ in (0.5) decrease to 0. However for the limit to exist, we need to make the right choice of a superconnection.
In Sect.
3, we introduce the Levi-Civita superconnection on the fibers $G$.
The analogue of (0.4) is proved in Theorem 3.5. The obtained formula includes (0.4) as a special case.

## 3. The first proof: The heat equation in the fibers

Using the extension of (0.4), the Index Theorem of Atiyah and Singer for families is proved in Sect.
4 by studying the heat equation in a given fiber.
The singularities in the analogue of (0.4) are much more severe and are eliminated using elementary properties of Heisenberg algebras.
Probability theory seems to play a still more fundamental role than in our proof of the Index Theorem [18] because of the importance of integration by parts on flat loops in the computations.
Also the $Z_{2}$ grading is much more essential than in [18].

## 4. The second proof: Two formulas for a classical index

The idea of the second proof is to make the expression in (0.5) appear explicitly in the computations of the $Z$-valued index of a Dirac operator defined on the manifold $M$.
(0.5) appears by using formula (0.1) and by changing the metric only in the horizontal directions.
By comparing the obtained formula with the classical formula of Atiyah and Singer for the index, we are able to compute (0.5) explicitly.
The second proof is only briefly sketched in Sect.
5. The technically difficult part – i.e. the a priori estimates – are obtained by using the partial Malliavin calculus of Bismut and Michel [22].

The paper is organized in the following way.
In Sect.
1, we give certain algebraic preliminaries.
Also various standard connections are introduced on finite and infinite dimensional bundles, and their elementary properties are derived.
In

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Sect.
2, the ideas in Quillen [33] are briefly described, and formula (0.5) is proved.
In Sect.
3, the Levi-Civita superconnection is introduced and the analogue of formula (0.4) is proved.
In Sect.
4 we give a first proof of the Index Theorem for families.
In Sect.
5, a second proof is briefly sketched.

We want also to point out a recent proof by Boutet de Montvel and Malgrange [23, 24] of the Index Theorem for families, where the index is taken in the sense of $D$-modules.

We refer to our paper [18] for a more complete list of references.
The probabilistic background which is needed to read Sect.
4 is also briefly described in [17] and [18].
The estimates in our second proof necessitate some familiarity with the Malliavin calculus, and the partial Malliavin calculus.
We refer to Malliavin [31], Bismut [14, 15], Bismut and Michel [22].

The results contained in this paper have been announced in [20b].
Also a detailed interpretation of the proofs in terms of the cohomology of the loop space is given by us in [21].

## I.

Some geometric properties of a fiber bundle

In this section, we introduce the fiber bundle $M_{\overline{X}^*}B$ and describe several connections on finite and infinite dimensional vector bundles over $M$ and $B$.
The simple properties of $M$ which are given here are crucial in proving the Index Theorem for families.

In a), we give a few algebraic preliminaries.
In b), the fiber manifold $M_{\overline{X}^*}B$ is introduced, and is endowed with a smooth connection $V$.
In c), a connection on $TM$ is constructed, whose torsion and curvature are calculated.
In d), the simple case where the holonomy group of the fiber bundle $M$ is compact is considered.
In e), the connection on $TM$ is extended to spinors.
Finally in f), a connection on an infinite dimensional vector bundle over $B$ is introduced and its curvature is calculated.

### a) Algebraic preliminaries

$n = 2l$ is an even integer.
$R^n$ is the canonical oriented Euclidean space of dimension $n, e_1, \ldots, e_n$ is its canonical oriented orthonormal base.

$c(R^n)$ is the Clifford algebra of $R^n$.
It is generated by 1, $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ with the commutation relations

$$
e_i^2 = -1, \quad e_i e_j + e_j e_i = 0, \quad i \neq j. \tag{1.1}
$$

$c(R^n)$ carries a natural grading.
It is isomorphic (as a vector space) to the exterior algebra $\Lambda(R^n)$, and the gradings of $c(R^n)$ and $\Lambda(R^n)$ correspond.

In particular $c(R^n)$ has a natural $Z_2$ grading.

$\mathcal{A}$ denotes the Lie algebra of the real $(n,n)$ antisymmetric matrices.
We identify each $A \in \mathcal{A}$ with the element of $\Lambda^2(R^n)$

$$
X, Y \in R^n \rightarrow \langle X, A Y \rangle. \tag{1.2}
$$

J.
M.
Bismut

If $A = (a_{i}^{j})$, the corresponding element in $\Lambda^2(R^n)$ is given by

$$
\frac {1}{2} \sum_ {i, j} a _ {j} ^ {i} d x ^ {i} \wedge d x ^ {j}. \tag {1.3}
$$

Similarly we also identify $A = (a_{i}^{j})\in \mathcal{A}$ with the element of $c(R^n)$

$$
\frac {1}{4} \sum_ {i, j} a _ {i} ^ {j} e _ {i} e _ {j}. \tag {1.4}
$$

The correspondence which to $A \in \mathcal{A}$ associates (1.4) is a Lie algebra homomorphism.
In particular the set of element of $c(R^n)$ of length 2 can be canonically identified with $\mathcal{A}$.

For a given integer $m \in N$, we do the same constructions for $R^m$.

$f_{1}, \ldots, f_{n}, \ldots, f_{m}$ denotes its canonical oriented Euclidean base, $dy^{1}, \ldots, dy^{n}, \ldots, dy^{m}$ the corresponding dual base.

Definition 1.1. $\mathcal{E}$ denotes the graded tensor product of the $Z_{2}$ graded algebras $c(R^n)$ and $\Lambda(R^m)$, i.e.

$$
\mathcal {E} = c \left(R ^ {n}\right) \hat {\otimes} \Lambda \left(R ^ {m}\right). \tag {1.5}
$$

We will use no $\otimes$ sign to indicate the product in $\mathcal{E}$.
For instance $e_i dy^s$ is a well defined element of $\mathcal{E}$, and of course

$$
e _ {i} d y ^ {s} + d y ^ {n} e _ {i} = 0. \tag {1.6}
$$

Note that $\mathcal{E}$ carries a natural grading, and also a $Z_{2}$ grading.

Definition 1.2. $\mathcal{E}^2$ denotes the subspace of $\mathcal{E}$ spanned by the elements of total length 2.

$\mathcal{E}^2$ is linearly spanned by the products

$$
e _ {i} e _ {j}, \quad i \neq j; \quad e _ {i} d y ^ {s}; d y ^ {s} d y ^ {\beta}, \quad \alpha \neq \beta .
$$

$\mathcal{E}^2$ then admits a natural decomposition

$$
\mathcal {E} ^ {2} = \mathcal {A} \oplus \left(R ^ {n} \hat {\otimes} R ^ {m}\right) \oplus \Lambda^ {2} \left(R ^ {m}\right). \tag {1.7}
$$

$\mathcal{F}$ is the subspace of $\mathcal{E}^2$ generated by the

$$
e _ {i} d y ^ {s}, d y ^ {s} d y ^ {\beta} \quad \alpha \neq \beta
$$

so that

$$
\mathcal {E} ^ {2} = \mathcal {A} \oplus \mathcal {F}. \tag {1.8}
$$

For $c, d \in \mathcal{E}$, we can define the commutator $[c, d]$ by

$$
[ c, d ] = c d - d c. \tag {1.9}
$$

We now have the following result:

Theorem 1.3. $\mathcal{E}^2$ is a Lie algebra, $\mathcal{F}$ is an ideal of $\mathcal{E}^2$, and also

$$
[ \mathcal {E} ^ {2}, R ^ {n} \hat {\otimes} R ^ {m} ] = R ^ {n} \hat {\otimes} R ^ {m}. \tag {1.10}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

The center of $\mathcal{E}^2$ is exactly $\Lambda^2(R^m)$.
$\mathcal{F}$ is a Heisenberg algebra, with center $\Lambda^2(R^m)$ and the commutation relations

$$
[e_i dy^{\alpha}, e_j dy^{\beta}] = 2\delta_i^j dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta}. \tag{1.11}
$$

Proof.
(1.11) is trivial.
$\Lambda^2(R^m)$ obviously commutes with the elements of $\mathcal{E}^2$.
Also for $i \neq j$, and $k \neq i$, $k \neq j$

$$
[e_i e_j, e_i dy^{\alpha}] = 2e_j dy^{\alpha} \tag{1.12}
$$

$$
[e_i e_j, e_k dy^{\alpha}] = 0.
$$

$\mathcal{E}^2$ is a Lie algebra, and (1.10) is proved.
$\square$

Also recall (see [7], [18, Sect. 1]) that $\operatorname{Spin}(n)$ acts unitarily on the $2^i$ dimensional complex vector space of spinors $S$ and that $c(R^n) \otimes_R C$ can be identified with $\operatorname{End}(S)$, so that $\operatorname{Spin}(n) \subset \operatorname{End}(S)$.
Set

$$
\tau = i^l e_1 \dots e_n. \tag{1.13}
$$

Then $\tau$ acts unitarily on $S$ and also

$$
\tau^2 = 1. \tag{1.14}
$$

Set

$$
S_+ = \{s \in S; \tau s = +s\}; \quad S_- = \{s \in S; \tau s = -s\}. \tag{1.15}
$$

Then $S_+$, $S_-$ are $2^{l-1}$ dimensional vector subspaces of $S$ such that

$$
S = S_+ \oplus S_-. \tag{1.16}
$$

$\operatorname{Spin}(n)$ acts unitarily and irreducibly on $S_+$, $S_-$.

## b) Description of the fiber manifold

$B$ now denotes a $m$ dimensional connected compact Riemannian manifold.
The scalar product in $TB$ is noted $g_B$.

$X$ is a connected compact orientable manifold of even dimension $n = 2l$.
We assume that $X$ is a spin manifold, so that $w_2(X) = 0$.

As shown in Atiyah et al.
[6], the usual geometric complexes can be constructed using the spin complex. As in [6, 18], global spin structures can be replaced by local spin structures (which always exist) when dealing with complexes for which no global spin structure is needed.

$M$ is a $n + m$ dimensional compact connected manifold.
We assume that $\pi$ is a submersion of $M$ onto $B$, which defines a fibration of $M$ with fiber $X$.

Namely we assume that $B$ admits an open covering $\mathcal{U}$ so that for $U \in \mathcal{U}$, there is a $C^\infty$ diffeomorphism $\varphi_U: \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times X$, and also if $U \cap V \neq \emptyset$, $\varphi_U \circ \varphi_V^{-1}: (U \cap V) \times X \to (U \cap V) \times X$ is given by $(y, x) \to (y, f_{U,V}(y, x))$ where $f_{U,V}(y,.)$ is a $C^\infty$ diffeomorphism of $X$ which is jointly $C^\infty$ in $(y, x) \in (U \cap V) \times X$.

98
J.
M.
Bismut

For $y \in B$, $\pi^{-1}(y)$ is then a submanifold $G_y$ of $M$.
$\pi$ defines a fibration $G$ of $M$.

$TG$ denotes the $n$-dimensional vector bundle on $M$ whose fiber $T_xG$ is the tangent space at $x$ to the fiber $G_{\pi(x)}$.

We assume that the bundle $TG$ is oriented (which is a stronger assumption than the orientability of $X$).

By using any Riemannian structure on $M$, we can construct a $m$-dimensional smooth subvector bundle $T^H M$ of $TM$ such that

$$
TM = T^H M \oplus TG. \tag{1.17}
$$

In particular for each $x \in M$, under $\pi_*$, $T_x^H M$ and $T_{\pi(x)}B$ are isomorphic.

Recall that $B$ is Riemannian.
The Euclidean scalar product in $TB$ can be lifted to $T^H M$.
Also we assume that $TG$ is endowed with a smooth scalar product $g_G$.

The scalar products on $T^H M$ and $TG$ can be extended to $TM$, by assuming that $T^H M$ and $TG$ are orthogonal.
$M$ is now a Riemannian manifold.
The scalar product in $TM$ is noted $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$.

Let $O$ be the $SO(n)$ bundle of oriented orthonormal frames in $TG$.

We now do the assumption that the bundle $TG$ is spin.
Namely we assume that the $SO(n)$ bundle $O_{\overrightarrow{e}^*}M$ can be lifted to a $\mathrm{Spin}(n)$ bundle $O'$ so that the projection $O'_{\overrightarrow{a}^*}O$ induces the covering projection $\mathrm{Spin}(n) \to SO(n)$ on each fiber.

Let $F, F_+$, $F_-$ be the bundle of spinors

$$
F = O' \times_{\operatorname{Spin}(n)} S \tag{1.18}
$$

$F_{\pm} = O' \times_{\operatorname{Spin}(n)} S_{\pm}.$

$F, F_{\pm}$ are Hermitian bundles on $M$ and also

$$
F = F_+ \oplus F_-.
$$

Recall that if $e \in TG$ is such that $\|e\| = 1$, $e$ acts unitarily by Clifford multiplication on $F$, and interchanges $F_+$ and $F_-$.

In the sequel, we will use the notations of a) in a different context.
Namely if $x \in M$, $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ will denote an oriented orthonormal base of $T_xG$; if $y \in B$, $f_1, \ldots, f_m$ is an oriented orthonormal base of $T_yB$, $dy^1, \ldots, dy^m$ the corresponding dual base.
Similarly $c(T_xG)$ is the Clifford algebra of $T_xG$.
$\Lambda(B)$ will denote the exterior algebra of $T^*B$.
In the sequel, the indices $i, j, \ldots$ will be used for vertical variables (like $e_i$), and $\alpha, \beta \ldots$ for horizontal variables like $f_\alpha$, $dy^\alpha \ldots$.

Finally, we set the following definition.

**Definition 1.4.** $\mathcal{E}$ is the bundle over $M$ defined by

$$
\mathcal{E}_x = c(T_xG) \hat{\otimes} \Lambda_{\pi(x)}(B). \tag{1.19}
$$

By identifying $R^n$ with $T_xM$ and $R^m$ with $T_{\pi(x)}B$, we can also define the bundles $\mathcal{E}^2$ and $\mathcal{F}$ over $M$.

**c) Connections on TM**

$B$ being a Riemannian manifold, we endow $TB$ with the Levi-Civita connection of $B$.
Let $\mathcal{V}^B$ be the corresponding covariant differentiation operator, let $R^B$ be the curvature tensor of $B$.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

If $x \in M$, $Y \in T_{\pi(x)}B$, $Y^H$ will denote the lift of $Y$ in $T_x^H M$, so that

$$
Y^H \in T_x^H M, \quad \pi_* Y^H = Y. \tag{1.20}
$$

We now define a natural connection on the bundle $T^H M$.

**Definition 1.5.** $V^H$ denotes the connection on $T^H M$ defined by the following relations:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text{if } Y \in TB, \quad Z \in TB \quad V_{Y^H}^H Z^H = (V_Y^B Z)^H \\
\text{if } Y \in TG, \quad Z \in TB \quad V_Y^H Z^H = 0.
\end{array}
\tag{1.21}
$$

Since $T^H M$ inherits its scalar product from $TB$, it is obvious that $V^H$ preserves the scalar product of $T^H M$.

Let $V^L$ be the Levi-Civita connection on $TM$.
$P_G$ (resp.
$P_H$) denotes the orthogonal projection operator of $TM$ on $TG$ (resp.
$T^H M$).

We then define a connection on $TG$.

**Definition 1.6.** $V^G$ denotes the connection on $TG$ defined by the following relation

$$
\text{if } Y \in TM, \quad Z \in TG \quad V_Y^G Z = P_G[V_Y^L Z]. \tag{1.22}
$$

$V^G$ obviously preserves the scalar product in $TG$.

We finally define a natural connection on $TM$.

**Definition 1.7.** $V$ denotes the connection on $TM$ which coincides with $V^H$ on $T^H M$ and with $V^G$ on $TG$.

$V$ is a connection on $TM$ which preserves the scalar product in $TM$.
In particular, the splitting of $TM$ described in (1.17) is parallel for $V$.

Note that in the sequel, the choice of $V^H$ and the fact that $V^G$ is obtained by projecting $V^L$ on $TG$ are of critical importance.

**Definition 1.8.** $S$ denotes the tensor defined by

$$
V^L = V + S. \tag{1.23}
$$

$T, R$ are the torsion and curvature tensors of the connection $V$.
$R^L$ is the curvature tensor for the connection $V^L$.

We now give some elementary properties of the connection $V$.

**Theorem 1.9.** For any $Y, Z \in TM$, $T(Y,Z) \in TG$.
Moreover if $V, W \in TG$, then

$$
T(V, W) = 0. \tag{1.24}
$$

For any $Y, Z \in TM$, $R(Y,Z)$ sends $T^H M$ into $T^H M$ and $TG$ into $TG$.

When restricted to $T_xG \times T_xG \times T_xG$, $R_x$ coincides with the curvature tensor of the Levi-Civita connection of the fiber $G_{\pi(x)}$.
Also if $Y \in TB$, $V, W \in TM$, then

$$
R(V, W) Y^H = [R^B(\pi_* V, \pi_* W) Y]^H. \tag{1.25}
$$

If $Y \in TM$, $V \in TG$, $S(Y)V \in T^H M$.
If $Y, Z \in TB$, $S(Y^H)Z^H \in TG$.

Finally the connection $V^G$, the torsion tensor $T$, and the $(3,0)$ tensor $\langle S(. .) \ldots \rangle$ do not depend on $g_B$.

100
J.
M.
Bismut

Proof.
Note that if $V \in TG$, $Z \in TB$ are two smooth vector fields, then

$$
[ \mathrm {V}, \mathrm {Z} ^ {H} ] \in TG. \tag {1.26}
$$

Using the second line in (1.21), it is clear that $T(V, Z^H) \in TG$.

Since the connection on $TG$ is obtained by projecting the Levi-Civita connection of $TM$ on $TG$, (1.24) is obvious.
Also since $V^B$ is the Levi-Civita connection of $B$, $T(Y^H, Z^H)$ is necessarily in $TG$.

Since the splitting (1.17) is parallel for $V$, it is stable under action of $R$.
Also since $T^H M$ inherits the connection of $TB$, (1.25) is obvious.

Also, using Definition 1.6, it is clear that if $Y \in TM$, $Z \in TG$

$$
P _ {G} S (Y) Z = 0. \tag {1.27}
$$

Now let us recall the well-known relation stating that if $X, Y, Z \in TM$

$$
2 \langle S (X) Y, Z \rangle + \langle T (X, Y), Z \rangle + \langle T (Z, X), Y \rangle - \langle T (Y, Z), X \rangle = 0. \tag {1.28}
$$

Since $T$ takes its values in $TG$, it follows that $S(Y^H)Z^H \in TG$.

Let $g_B'$ be any other metric on $TB$, $V'^B$ the corresponding Levi-Civita connection on $TB$.
We lift $V'^B$ into a connection $V'^H$ on $T^H M$ as in (1.21).
The connection $V'^H \oplus V^G = V'$ on $TM$ preserves the metric $g_B' \oplus g_G = g'$.
It is easy to see that its torsion is still equal to $T$.
Also if $V'^L$ is the Levi-Civita connection for the metric $g_B' \oplus g_G$, if $V'^L = V' + S'$, since $T$ takes its values in $TG$, (1.28) shows that if $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle'$ is the scalar product for $g'$, $\langle S'(X)Y, Z \rangle' = \langle S(X)Y, Z \rangle$.
If $Y \in TM$, $Z \in TG$, it follows that $S'(Y)Z \in T^H M$, and so $V_Y^G Z = P_G V_Y'^L Z$.
(1.22) is still verified.

d) A simple example

We now briefly develop a simple example, which corresponds to the case considered in [20a] for the proof of the infinitesimal Lefschetz formulas.

Namely assume that $P_{\overrightarrow{A}} B'$ is a principal fiber bundle whose structure group $A$ is a compact connected group.
We also assume that $P$ is endowed with a smooth connection.

Finally, we suppose that $A$ acts isometrically on the compact connected Riemannian manifold $X'$.
Set

$$
M ^ {\prime} = P \times_ {A} X ^ {\prime}.
$$

The projection $M' \xrightarrow[\pi]{\longrightarrow} B'$ defines a fibration of $M'$ whose typical fiber $G'$ is isomorphic to $X'$.
Also the fibers of $G'$ inherit naturally a metric from the metric of $X'$ and also the Levi-Civita connection of $X'$ (which is invariant under $A$).
Moreover the connection on $P$ defines naturally a smooth connection on the fibration $G'$ so that $T^H M'$ is naturally defined.

Assuming that $B'$ is a Riemannian manifold endowed with the Levi-Civita connection, we can define $V'^H$ as in Definition 1.5. Also if $Y \in TB'$, $V \in TG'$, set

$$
V _ {Y ^ {H}} ^ {G ^ {\prime}} V = \left[ Y ^ {H}, V \right]. \tag {1.29}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Obviously $V_{Y^H}^G V \in TG'$.
Finally if $V, W \in TG'$, $V_V^{G}W \in TG'$ is naturally defined using the Levi-Civita connection of $X$.

It is trivial to verify that if $Y, Z \in TB'$, $U, V \in TG'$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
T \left(Y ^ {H}, Z ^ {H}\right) = - P _ {G ^ {\prime}} \left[ Y ^ {H}, Z ^ {H} \right] \\
T \left(Y ^ {H}, U\right) = 0 \tag {1.30} \\
T (U, V) = 0.
\end{array}
$$

It immediately follows from (1.28), (1.30) that the fibers $G'$ are totally geodesic in $M'$, and that (1.22) holds.

Also, an obvious computation shows that besides (1.25), if $Y, Z \in TB'$, $V, W \in TG'$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
R \left(Y ^ {H}, Z ^ {H}\right) V = V _ {V} T \left(Y ^ {H}, Z ^ {H}\right) \tag {1.31} \\
R \left(Y ^ {H}, V\right) W = 0.
\end{array}
$$

(1.31) is directly connected with the fact that $A$ acts isometrically on $X'$ and preserves the Levi-Civita connection of $X'$.
(1.31) and (1.25) show that

$$
R \left(Y ^ {H}, V\right) = 0. \tag {1.32}
$$

It is the absence of $R(Y^H, V)$ which simplifies the computation of the Index of families of Dirac operators.

e) Connections on spinors and an auxiliary bundle

The connection $V^G$ defines a natural connection on the bundle of frames $O$.
It lifts naturally as a connection on $O'$.

Also let $\xi$ be a $k$-dimensional complex Hermitian bundle over $M$.
We assume that $\xi$ is endowed with a unitary connection.
Let $L$ be its curvature tensor.

$F\otimes \xi ,F_{\pm}\otimes \xi$ are then naturally endowed with a connection.

From now on, we will note $V$ the covariant differentiation operator on $TM$, $F\otimes \xi$, $F_{\pm}\otimes \xi$, keeping the notation $V^{L}$ for the Levi-Civita connection of $M$.

f) Connections on infinite dimensional bundles

$H^{\infty}, H_{\pm}^{\infty}$ denote the set of $C^\infty$ sections of $F \otimes \xi$, $F_{\pm} \otimes \xi$ over $M$.

Obviously, we may regard $H^{\infty}, H_{\pm}^{\infty}$ as being the set of $C^\infty$ sections over $B$ of infinite dimensional fiber bundles, which we still note $H^{\infty}, H_{\pm}^{\infty}$.
The fibers $H_y^{\infty}, H_{\pm, y}^{\infty}$ are the sets of $C^\infty$ sections over $G_y$ of $F \otimes \xi$, $F_{\pm} \otimes \xi$.

Also note that since $F \otimes \xi$ is an Hermitian bundle, and since the fibers $G_y$ carry a natural volume element $dx$, if $h, h' \in H_y^\infty$, we can define the scalar product

$$
\langle h, h ^ {\prime} \rangle = \int_ {G _ {y}} \langle h (x), h ^ {\prime} (x) \rangle d x. \tag {1.33}
$$

We now define a connection on $H_{\pm}^{\infty}$.

102
J.
M.
Bismut

Definition 1.10. $\tilde{V}$ denotes the connection on $H_{\pm}^{\infty}$ such that if $Y \in TB$, $h \in H_{\pm}^{\infty}$, then

$$
\tilde{V}_Y h = V_{Y^H} h. \tag{1.34}
$$

We finally compute the curvature $\tilde{R}$ of $\tilde{V}$.

Let $R^G$ be the curvature tensor of $TG$ for the connection $V^G$.
$R^G$ is the restriction of $R$ to $TG$.
Also, using (1.4), we can identify $R^G$ with the curvature tensor of $F$.

Proposition 1.11. If $Y, Z \in T_yB$, $\tilde{R}_y(Y,Z)$ is the first order differential operator on $H_y^\infty$

$$
\tilde{R}_y(Y,Z) = R^G(Y^H, Z^H) \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes L(Y^H, Z^H) - V_{T(Y^H, Z^H)}. \tag{1.35}
$$

Proof.
This follows from the obvious relations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{R}(Y,Z) &amp;= V_{Y^H} V_{Z^H} - V_{Z^H} V_{Y^H} - V_{[Y,Z]^H} \\
&amp;= R^G(Y^H, Z^H) \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes L(Y^H, Z^H) + V_{P_G[Y^H, Z^H]} \\
T(Y^H, Z^H) &amp;= - P_G[Y^H, Z^H].
\end{aligned}
$$

## II.

Chern-Weil's theory in infinite dimensions: A heat equation formula for the index

In this section we show how to extend the results of Quillen [33] on superconnections on finite dimensional $Z_2$ graded vector bundles to the infinite dimensional case.
We establish that (0.5) represents in cohomology the scaled Chern character of the difference bundle $\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D$ (where $D$ is the Dirac operator along the fibers).

In a), we briefly recall Quillen's formalism [33] in the finite dimensional case.
In b), we define the Dirac operator $D$ along the fibers $G$.
In c), we define the superconnection $\tilde{V} + D$ on the $Z_2$-bundle $H^\infty$, and we compute its curvature $I$ which is a second order differential operator.
In d), we prove the fundamental formula which gives an infinite dimensional representation of $\overline{ch}(\mathrm{Ker}D - \mathrm{Coker}D)$.
We use pseudodifferential operators techniques in the proof (see Treves [36, Chap. III]).

For the development of the formalism of superconnections from a Physicist's point of view, see Wess and Bagger [37].

### a) Construction by Quillen of the Chern character of a difference bundle

We first briefly describe the construction by Quillen [33] of the Chern character of a finite dimensional difference bundle.

Let $V = V^0 \oplus V^1$ be a super (or $Z_2$ graded) complex vector space.
Let $\varrho$ be the involution defining the grading $\varrho(e) = (-1)^{\deg e} e$.

The space $\operatorname{End} V$ of endomorphisms of $V$ is a super vector space, the even (resp.
odd) elements of $\operatorname{End} V$ commuting (resp.
anticommuting) with $\varrho$.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

The supertrace $\operatorname{Tr}_s k$ of $k \in \operatorname{End} V$ is defined by

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_s k = \operatorname{Tr} \varrho k. \tag{2.1}
$$

Let $H$ be a Grassmann algebra.
$H$ is naturally $Z_2$ graded.
$K$ is the graded tensor product

$$
K = \operatorname{End} V \hat{\otimes} H. \tag{2.2}
$$

$K$ possesses a natural $Z_2$ grading.
Also $\operatorname{Tr}_s$ can be naturally extended to $K$ and takes its values in $H$, so that if $h \in H$, $k \in K$,

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_s [h \ k] = h \operatorname{Tr}_s [k].
$$

In what follows, the commutator $[k, k']$ of two elements of $K$ is always taken in the graded algebra $K$.

We first have the elementary result.

**Proposition 2.1.** Let $k, k'$ be two elements of $K$, and assume that $k$ is even.
Then

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_s [k, k'] = 0. \tag{2.3}
$$

**Proof.** We assume that

$$
k = \alpha \eta, \quad \alpha \in \operatorname{End} V, \quad \eta \in H
$$

$$
k' = \alpha' \eta', \quad \alpha' \in \operatorname{End} V, \quad \eta' \in H.
$$

A trivial computation shows that since $k$ is even

$$
[k, k'] = (-1)^{\deg \eta \ \deg \alpha'} \left( \alpha \alpha' - (-1)^{\deg \alpha \ \deg \alpha'} \alpha' \alpha \right) \cdot \eta \eta'. \tag{2.4}
$$

In the r.h.s.
of (2.4), we recognize the supercommutator of $\alpha, \alpha'$ in the sense of [33] and it is known that $\operatorname{Tr}_s$ vanishes on supercommutators.
$\square$

Let $N$ be a manifold.
Let $E = E^0 \oplus E^1$ be a supervector bundle on $N$, whose fiber $E = E^0 \oplus E^1$ is modelled on $V = V^0 \oplus V^1$.

Let $X^0, X^1$ be the bundles of frames in $E^0, E^1$.
Assume that $X^0, X^1$ are endowed with connection 1-forms $\omega^0, \omega^1$ taking their values in $\operatorname{End} V^0$, $\operatorname{End} V^1$.

$X^0 \boxtimes X^1$ denotes the bundle over $N$ whose fiber at $y$ is $X_y^0 \times X_y^1$.
$X^0 \boxtimes X^1$ is then naturally endowed with a connection.

We here take $H = \varLambda(X^0 \boxtimes X^1)$, so that

$$
K = \operatorname{End} V \hat{\otimes} \varLambda (X^0 \boxtimes X^1). \tag{2.5}
$$

$\omega = (\omega^0, \omega^1)$ can be considered as an odd element of $K$.

$d$ denotes the exterior differentiation operator acting on $\varLambda(X^0 \boxtimes X^1)$.
Note that $d$ can be extended to $K$ in the obvious way, by setting for $\alpha \in \operatorname{End} V$, $\eta \in \varLambda(X^0 \boxtimes X^1)$

$$
d(\eta \alpha) = (d \eta) \alpha.
$$

$d$ is an odd operator acting on $K$ in the sense that if $k, k' \in K$

$$
d(k \ k') = (d k) k' + (-1)^{\deg k} k \, dk'. \tag{2.6}
$$

J.
M.
Bismut

Let $B$ be an odd element in $\operatorname{End} E\hat{\otimes}\Lambda(N)$ whose equivariant representation in $K$ is denoted $\beta$.
$\gamma = \omega + \beta$ is called a superconnection.

Definition 2.2. The curvature $\Gamma$ is the even element in $K$

$$
\Gamma = (d + \gamma)^2. \tag{2.7}
$$

As for usual connections, $\Gamma$ is the equivariant representation of an even element $I$ in $\operatorname{End} E\hat{\otimes}\Lambda(N)$.

In this formalism, the Bianchi identities are given by the obvious relation

$$
[d + \gamma, \Gamma] = 0. \tag{2.8}
$$

Using (2.6), we see that (2.7) writes

$$
d\Gamma + [\gamma, \Gamma] = 0. \tag{2.9}
$$

We now have the following result by Quillen [33].

Theorem 2.3. The differential form on $X^0 \boxtimes X^1$

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_s \exp\left[-\frac{\Gamma}{2i\pi}\right] \tag{2.10}
$$

projects onto $N$ as a differential form which is a representative (in cohomology) of $\operatorname{ch}(E^0 - E^1)$.

Proof.
Since we will use similar arguments repeatedly, we reprove the Theorem.
If $\beta = 0$, the result is obvious.
We first prove that (2.10) is closed.

Since $\Gamma$ is even, we find using Proposition 2.1, that

$$
d\operatorname{Tr}_s \left[\exp\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2i\pi}\right]\right] = \operatorname{Tr}_s \left[-\frac{d\Gamma}{2i\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2i\pi}\right]\right]. \tag{2.11}
$$

Using (2.9) and Proposition 2.1 again, we get

$$
d\left[\operatorname{Tr}_s \exp\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2i\pi}\right]\right] = \operatorname{Tr}_s \left[-\gamma, \exp\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2i\pi}\right]\right] = 0. \tag{2.12}
$$

(2.10) is then closed.
Replacing $\omega + \beta$ by $\omega + s\beta$ ($s \in \mathbb{R}$), the fact that the cohomology class of (2.10) does not depend on $s$ is now an extension of what has been done before.
The fact that (2.10) projects on $N$ is obvious using the equivariance of $\operatorname{Tr}_s$.

Let $\bar{V}$ be the covariant differentiation operator for the connection $\omega$.
$\bar{V}$ acts on the sections of $E\otimes \Lambda (N)$ by setting for $e\in E$, $\varphi \in \Lambda (N)$

$$
\bar{V} \cdot \varphi e = d \varphi e + (-1)^{\deg \varphi} \varphi \wedge \bar{V} \cdot e. \tag{2.13}
$$

$\operatorname{End} E\hat{\otimes}\Lambda(N)$ acts naturally on $E\otimes \Lambda (N)$.
Namely if $h\in \operatorname{End}E$, $e\in E$, $\eta ,\eta^{\prime}\in \Lambda (N)$, we set

$$
(h \eta) (e \eta^ {\prime}) = (-1)^{\left(\deg \eta\right) \deg e} (h e) \eta \eta^ {\prime}. \tag{2.14}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

As shown in Quillen [33], (2.14) permits the identification of $\operatorname{End} E\hat{\otimes}\Lambda(N)$ with the set of $u \in \operatorname{End}(E\otimes \Lambda (N))$ such that

$$
u \left[ (e \eta) \eta^ {\prime} \right] = \left[ u (e \eta) \right] \eta^ {\prime}. \tag {2.15}
$$

Set

$$
\vec {V} ^ {\prime} = \vec {V} + B.
$$

Then $I$ (whose equivariant representation is $\Gamma$) is given by

$$
I = \left(\vec {V} ^ {\prime}\right) ^ {2}. \tag {2.16}
$$

As shown in Quillen [33], if $B$ is an odd element in $\operatorname{End} E$, if $\bar{R}$ is the curvature for $\bar{V}$, then

$$
I = \bar {R} + \bar {V} \cdot B + B ^ {2}. \tag {2.17}
$$

## b) The Dirac operator in the fibers

We now use the conventions of Sects.
1a) and b).
In particular $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ still denotes an oriented orthonormal base of $TG$.

**Definition 2.4.** $D$ denotes the operator acting on $H^\infty$

$$
D = \sum_ {1} ^ {n} e _ {i} V _ {e _ {i}}. \tag {2.18}
$$

Of course the operator $D$ acts fiberwise in the fibers $G$.

For $y \in B$, $D_y$ denotes the restriction of $D$ to the fiber $H_y^\infty$.
$D$ interchanges $H_+^\infty$ and $H_-^\infty$.
$D_+$, $D_-$ are the restrictions of $D$ to $H_+^\infty$, $H_-^\infty$.

By Atiyah and Singer [9], the difference bundle over $B$

$$
\operatorname {K e r} D _ {+, y} - \operatorname {K e r} D _ {-, y} \tag {2.19}
$$

is well defined in the sense of $K$-theory.
