The purpose of this paper is to calculate the Chern character of (2.19).

## c) A superconnection on $H^\infty$

The fiber bundle

$$
F \otimes \xi = (F _ {+} \otimes \xi) \oplus (F _ {-} \otimes \xi)
$$

possesses a natural $Z_{2}$ grading, in which $F_{+} \otimes \xi$ are the even elements, and $F_{-} \otimes \xi$ the odd elements.

Recall the definition of $\tau$ in (1.13), which can be transferred to $F\otimes \xi$.
The involution defining the grading is here given by $\tau \otimes 1$.

Similarly, the infinite dimensional vector bundle $H^{\infty}$ over $B$ has a natural $Z_{2}$ grading

$$
H ^ {\infty} = H _ {+} ^ {\infty} \oplus H _ {-} ^ {\infty}. \tag {2.20}
$$

106
J.
M.
Bismut

We will apply the construction of Quillen [33] to $H^\infty$.
First recall that the connection $\tilde{V}$ is a standard connection on $H_+^\infty$ and $H_-^\infty$.
Moreover we can write

$$
D = \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 0 &amp; D_- \\ D_+ &amp; 0 \end{array} \right] \tag{2.21}
$$

so that $D$ is an odd element in $\operatorname{End} H^\infty$.

Also recall that $TB$ is endowed with the Levi-Civita connection.
This implies that when restricted to $\Lambda(B)$, the exterior differential operator $d$ coincides with $\sum dy^{\alpha} \wedge V_{f_{\alpha}}^{B}$.

We then find that in the sense of (2.13), the action of $\tilde{V}$ on $H^\infty \otimes \Lambda(B)$ is given by

$$
\tilde{V} = \sum dy^{\alpha} \wedge \tilde{V}_{f_{\alpha}} = \sum dy^{\alpha} \wedge V_{(f_{\alpha})^H}, \tag{2.22}
$$

where $V_{(f_{\alpha})^H}$ now acts on the whole bundle $H^\infty \otimes \Lambda(B)$ in the obvious way.

In what follows, to simplify the notations, we will use the following conventions

- All the summation signs will be omitted.
- The subscripts $\alpha, \beta$ will be used for horizontal variables and the subscripts $i, j$ for the vertical ones (i.e. the variables in $TG$).
- We omit $H$ in $(f_{\alpha})^{H}$, i.e. we identify the orthonormal base $f_{1}, \ldots, f_{m}$ of $T_{y}B$ and its lift in $T_{x}^{H}M$ (for $x \in G_{y}$).
  Also $dy^{1}, \ldots, dy^{m}$ are now considered as differential forms on $M$.
- We omit the exterior product sign $\wedge$.

It is then clear that

$$
\tilde{V} + D = e_i V_{e_i} + dy^{\alpha} V_{f_{\alpha}}. \tag{2.23}
$$

$\tilde{V} + D$ then defines a superconnection on $H^\infty$ in the sense of Quillen [33].
Recall that $R^G$ is the curvature tensor of the bundle $F$ for the connection $V$.

We first compute the curvature of $\tilde{V} + D$.
We have

**Theorem 2.5.** Let $I$ be the curvature of the superconnection $\tilde{V} + D$ given by

$$
I = (\tilde{V} + D)^2. \tag{2.24}
$$

Then $I$ is the even element in $\operatorname{End} H^\infty \hat{\otimes} \Lambda(B)$ given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &amp;= D^2 - \frac{1}{2} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} V_{T_{(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta})}} - dy^{\alpha} e_i V_{T_{(f_{\alpha}, e_i)}} \\
&amp;\quad + \frac{1}{2} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} \left[ R^G(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes L(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) \right] \\
&amp;\quad + dy^{\alpha} e_i \left[ R^G(f_{\alpha}, e_i) \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes L(f_{\alpha}, e_i) \right]. \tag{2.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof.** First observe the essential fact that by Definition 1.5,

$$
V_{e_i} dy^{\alpha} = 0. \tag{2.26}
$$

This makes that the anticommutation rule in $\operatorname{End} H^\infty \hat{\otimes} \Lambda(B)$

$$
\{D, dy^{\alpha}\} = 0 \tag{2.27}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

is automatically verified when using purely local computations in terms of $V$.
Also note that the rule (2.27) will be verified by assuming that $e_i$ and $dy^{\alpha}$ anticommute, i.e. by doing the computations in the graded algebra $\mathcal{E}$ described in Definition 1.4.

Using (2.27) it is clear that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
I = D^{2} + e_{i} dy^{\alpha} V_{e_{i}} V_{f_{\alpha}} + dy^{\alpha} (V_{f_{\alpha}} e_{i}) V_{e_{i}} + dy^{\alpha} e_{i} V_{f_{\alpha}} V_{e_{i}} + dy^{\alpha} (V_{f_{\alpha}} dy^{\beta}) V_{f_{\beta}} \\
+ dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} V_{f_{\alpha}} V_{f_{\beta}}
\end{array}
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
I = D^{2} + dy^{\alpha} e_{i} \left[ R(f_{\alpha}, e_{i}) \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes L(f_{\alpha}, e_{i}) + V_{\{f_{\alpha}, e_{i}\}} \right] \\
+ \frac{1}{2} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} \left[ R(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes L(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) + V_{\{f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}\}} \right] \\
+ dy^{\alpha} (V_{f_{\alpha}} e_{i}) V_{e_{i}} + dy^{\alpha} (V_{f_{\alpha}} dy^{\beta}) V_{f_{\beta}}. \tag{2.28}
\end{array}
$$

Also since $f_{1}, \ldots, f_{m}$ is an orthonormal base, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
V_{f_{\alpha}} f_{\beta} = a_{\alpha, \beta}^{\gamma} f_{\gamma} \tag{2.29} \\
a_{\alpha, \beta}^{\gamma} = -a_{\alpha, \gamma}^{\beta}.
\end{array}
$$

It then obviously follows that

$$
\frac{1}{2} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} V_{\{f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}\}} + dy^{\alpha} (V_{f_{\alpha}} dy^{\beta}) V_{f_{\beta}} = -\frac{1}{2} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} V_{T(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta})}. \tag{2.30}
$$

Similarly, using the fact that $V_{e_i}f_{\alpha} = 0$, we get easily

$$
dy^{\alpha} e_{i} V_{\{f_{\alpha}, e_{i}\}} + dy^{\alpha} (V_{f_{\alpha}} e_{i}) V_{e_{i}} = -dy^{\alpha} e_{i} V_{T(f_{\alpha}, e_{i})}. \tag{2.31}
$$

Observe that by (1.25), the action of $R(f_{\alpha}, e_{i})$ on $\Lambda(B)$ is 0. Also the action of $R(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta})$ on $\Lambda(B)$ is given by

$$
- \langle R^{B}(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) f_{\gamma}, dy^{\delta} \rangle dy^{\gamma} i_{f_{\delta}} \tag{2.32}
$$

and by Bianchi's identities

$$
dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} dy^{\gamma} \langle R^{B}(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) f_{\gamma}, dy^{\delta} \rangle i_{f_{\delta}} = 0. \tag{2.33}
$$

Note that (2.33) essentially expresses the fact that $d^{2} = 0$ on $\Lambda(B)$.

We can then replace everywhere $R$ by $R^{G}$ is (2.28).
The proof is finished.

Remark 1. According to Theorem 1.9, $T$ takes its values in $TG$.
This plays an essential role here, since the curvature $I$ must operate fiberwise.
To do the computations, we also could have used Proposition 1.11 and formula (2.17).

Finally recall that if $\Delta^H$ is the horizontal Laplacian in the fiber (for the Levi-Civita connection of the fiber) and if $K$ is its scalar curvature, then by a formula of Lichnerowicz [30; 18, Theorem 1.9]

$$
D^{2} = -\Delta^{H} + \frac{K}{4} + \frac{1}{2} e_{i} e_{j} \otimes L(e_{i}, e_{j}). \tag{2.34}
$$

108
J.
M.
Bismut

d) A heat equation formula for the Chern character

$dx$ will denote the Riemannian volume element in a given fiber $G_y$.
All the kernels which we consider are calculated with respect to $dx$.

Let $I_y$ be the restriction of $I$ to $G_y$.
For a given $y \in B$, the operator $I^y$ acts on $H_y^\infty \otimes \varLambda_y(B)$ as indicated in (2.14).
We will omit the sign $\otimes$ in the sequel.

By (2.15), if $h \in H_y^\infty$, $\eta \in \varLambda_y(B)$

$$
I^ {y} (h \eta) = \left(I ^ {y} h\right) \eta . \tag {2.35}
$$

Using standard results on elliptic equations (see Treves [36, Chapter III.1]), we can construct the "heat equation" semi-group $e^{-\frac{tI^y}{2}}$ which also acts in the fiber.
For any $t &gt; 0$, $e^{-\frac{tI^y}{2}}$ is given by a kernel $P_1^y (x,x')$ (for $x,x^{\prime}\in G_{y}$), which is $C^\infty$ in $(t,x,x^{\prime})\in ]0, + \infty [\times G_y\times G_y$.

Of course we could directly invoke probability theory results to get all the regularity which is needed in $(x, x', y)$.
However since we will have to consider more general pseudodifferential operators, we leave probability outside of the problem for the moment.

Since the fibration $M_{\mathfrak{R}^*}B$ is locally trivial, there is an open neighborhood of $y$ in $B$ such that $\pi^{-1}(U)$ is diffeomorphic to $U\times X$.
In what follows, we will not distinguish $\pi^{-1}(U)$ and $U\times X$.

In particular, since $I^y$ is a smooth family of second order elliptic differential operator, it is not difficult to prove that $P_i^y (x,x')$ is $C^\infty$ in $(t,x,x',y)\in ]0, + \infty [\times X\times X\times U$.
This will be briefly shown in the proof of Proposition 2.8.

For $x, x' \in G_y$, $P_i^y(x, x')$ is a linear mapping from $(F \otimes \xi)_{x'}$ into $(F \otimes \xi)_x \otimes \varLambda_y(B)$.

Using (2.35), we see that if $h \in H_y^\infty$, $\eta \in \varLambda_y(B)$

$$
\left(e ^ {- \frac {t I ^ {y}}{2}} h \eta\right) (x) = \int_ {G _ {y}} P _ {i} ^ {y} \left(x, x ^ {\prime}\right) h \left(x ^ {\prime}\right) d x ^ {\prime} \eta . \tag {2.36}
$$

Let $\tau_{x}$ be the involution defining the grading in $(F\otimes \xi)_{x}$.
Then $\mathrm{Hom}((F\otimes \xi)_{x'};(F\otimes \xi)_x)$ has a natural grading, the even elements being the $k$ such that

$$
\tau_ {x} k - k \tau_ {x ^ {\prime}} = 0
$$

and the odd elements being the $k^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\tau_ {x} k ^ {\prime} + k ^ {\prime} \tau_ {x ^ {\prime}} = 0.
$$

$P_{i}^{y}(x,x^{\prime})$ is then an even element of the graded tensor product $\mathrm{Hom}((F\otimes \xi)_{x'};(F\otimes \xi)_x)\widehat{\otimes}\varLambda_y(B)$.

In particular $P_{i}^{y}(x,x)$ is an even element in the graded algebra $\operatorname{End}(F\otimes \xi)_x\widehat{\otimes}\varLambda_y(B)$.
$\operatorname{Tr}_xP_i^y (x,x)$ is then an even element in $\varLambda_y(B)$.

For technical reasons, we temporarily change the normalization constants in the definition of the Chern character.
Namely, for a vector bundle $V$ with

Families index theorem and the heat equation

connection form $\mu$ and curvature $C$, we set

$$
\overline{ch} V = \operatorname{Tr} \exp \left\{- \frac {C}{2} \right\} \tag {2.37}
$$

(the difference with $chV$ is by the factor $-\frac{i}{\pi}$).

We devote the remainder of the section to the proof of the fundamental result.

**Theorem 2.6.** The $C^\infty$ element of $\Lambda(B)$

$$
\int_ {G _ {y}} \operatorname{Tr} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {s} (x, x) \right] d x \tag {2.38}
$$

is a representative (in cohomology) of $\overline{ch}(\operatorname{Ker}D_{+} - \operatorname{Ker}D_{-})$.

**Proof.** Since the proof is rather long, we will divide it into several steps.

We will first prove the Theorem under the following simplifying assumption.

H1: For any $y \in B$,

$$
\operatorname{Ker} D _ {-, y} = \{0 \}. \tag {2.39}
$$

Later on, we will see how a standard argument in $K$-theory permits us to lift assumption H1.

For $s \in R, y \in B$, let $H_{\pm, y}^{s}$ be the set of sections of $F_{\pm} \otimes \xi$ over $G_{y}$ which belong to the $s$-Sobolev space.
The Dirac operator $D_{+, y}$ is then a Fredholm operator from $H_{+, y}^{0}$ into $H_{-, y}^{-1}$.
In what follows, we will consider $D_{+, y}$ as being defined on $H_{+, y}^{0}$ with values in $H_{-, y}^{-1}$.

Since $D_{+,y}$ depends continuously on $y \in B$, the index $\operatorname{Ind} D_{+,y}$ is constant over the connected manifold $B$.
Since $\operatorname{Ker} D_{-,y} = \{0\}$, it follows that

$$
\operatorname{Im} D _ {+, y} = H _ {-, y} ^ {- 1}.
$$

Also $\operatorname{Ker} D_{+,y}$ has constant dimension over $B$.
A standard argument (see Atiyah [3, p. 104]) shows that $\operatorname{Ker} D_{+,y}$ is a smooth vector bundle over $B$.

The general idea of the proof of the Theorem is

- to prove that (2.38) is closed: this is Proposition 2.9.
- to show that the cohomology class of (2.38) does not change when the connection $\tilde{V}$ is modified into a connection which is constructed using certain pseudodifferential operators.
  This is done in Proposition 2.10.
- to construct one modified connection for which the identification of (2.38) with $\overline{ch}(\operatorname{Ker}D_{+} - \operatorname{Ker}D_{-})$ is "obvious".
  This is achieved in Proposition 2.17.

We first have the elementary

**Lemma 2.7.** The following relation holds

$$
[ \tilde {V} + D, I ] = 0. \tag {2.40}
$$

**Proof.** Since $I = (\tilde{V} + D)^2$, this result is obvious.
$\square$

Let $(y'^{1},\ldots ,y'^{m})$ be a local system of coordinates in $U$, which we assume to be diffeomorphic to an open ball of center $y$ in $R^m$.
In the next lemma, we identify

110
J.
M.
Bismut

explicitly $\pi^{-1}(U)$ with $U \times X$.
In particular $dx$ will temporarily denote a fixed volume element in $X$.
Of course $P_{t}^{y'}(x, x')$ will temporarily denote the kernel of $e^{-\frac{tI^{y'}}{2}}$ with respect to this volume element $dx$ (which is in general different from the volume element in $G_{y'}$).
However since (2.38) is independent of the volume element, this is entirely irrelevant.

Also by using parallel transportation over the ray connecting $y$ and $y' \in U$ in the fibers of $G$ and also in the bundles $F_{\pm}, \xi$, we can pull back everything on fixed bundles over the manifold $X$.
In particular $P_t^{y'}$ is now acting on a fixed bundle over $X$.
We can also form the product of the kernels $P_{t'}^{y'}$ and $P_{t'}^{y''}$ (for $y', y'' \in U$).
We can also make sense of the derivative $\frac{\partial I^{y'}}{\partial y'^2}$.
Note that since we use the parallel transport trivialization over rays starting from $y$, at $y$,

$$
\frac{\partial I^{y}}{\partial y'^2} = \tilde{V}_{f_{\pi}} I^{y}. \tag{2.41}
$$

**Proposition 2.8.** For any $t &gt; 0$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial y^{a}} \int_{X} \operatorname{Tr}_{s} \left[ P_{t}^{y}(x, x) \right] dx = - \frac{t}{2} \int_{X} \operatorname{Tr}_{s} \left[ \frac{\partial I_{x}^{y}}{\partial y^{a}} P_{t}(x, x') \right]_{x' = x} dx. \tag{2.42}
$$

_Proof._ Let $I^{y*}$ denote the adjoint of $I^{y}$ (calculated with respect to $dx$).
For any $x, x' \in X, t &gt; 0$, we have

$$
\frac{\partial P_{t}^{y}}{\partial t}(x, x') = - \frac{I_{x}^{y}}{2} P_{t}^{y}(x, x') = - \frac{I_{x'}^{y*}}{2} P_{t}^{y}(x, x'). \tag{2.43}
$$

It then follows that for $0 &lt; t' &lt; t$, $y \in U$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t'} \int_{X} P_{t'}^{y'}(x, u) P_{t - t'}^{y}(u, x') du = \int_{X} P_{t'}^{y'}(x, u) \left[ \frac{I_{u}^{y} - I_{u}^{y'}}{2} \right] P_{t - t'}^{y}(u, x') du. \tag{2.44}
$$

Now for $0 \leq t' \leq \frac{t}{2}$, $P_{t - t'}^{y}(u, x')$ is $C^\infty$ in $(t', u)$.
Since as $t' \downarrow 0$, for one given $x \in X$, $P_{t'}^{y'}(x, .)$ converges in distribution sense to $I \otimes \delta_{\{x\}}$, we find that

$$
\lim_{t' \downarrow 0} \int_{X} P_{t'}^{y'}(x, u) P_{t - t'}^{y}(u, x') du = P_{t}^{y}(x, x').
$$

Similarly

$$
\lim_{t' \uparrow t} \int_{X} P_{t'}^{y'}(x, u) P_{t - t'}^{y}(u, x') du = P_{t}^{y'}(x, x').
$$

For the same reason, the r.h.s.
of (2.44) can be integrated in $t'$ on the interval $[0, t]$.
We get

$$
P_{t}^{y}(x, x') - P_{t}^{x}(x, x') = \int_{0}^{t} dt' \int_{X} P_{t'}^{y'}(x, u) \left( \frac{I_{u}^{y} - I_{u}^{y'}}{2} \right) P_{t - t'}^{y}(u, x') du. \tag{2.45}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Now for a given $t &gt; 0$, the bounds in Treves [36, III, Theorem 1.1] show that for $t' \geq \frac{t}{2}$, $(y, u, x') \in U \times X \times X$, and any multi-index $k, k'$

$$
\left| \frac {\partial^ {| k + k ^ {\prime} |}}{\partial x ^ {k} \partial u ^ {k ^ {\prime}}} P _ {t ^ {\prime}} (x, u) \right|
$$

remains uniformly bounded.
Note here that the uniform ellipticity of $I^y$ plays an essential role.
Also for $t' \leq t$, $P_t^{y'}(x,.)$ remains bounded uniformly in the set of pseudodifferential operators of order 0. Finally, $I^y$ depends continuously on $y$ in the obvious way.
It follows that

$$
\left| \int_ {0} ^ {t / 2} d t ^ {\prime} \int_ {X} P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y ^ {\prime}} (x, u) \left(\frac {I _ {u} ^ {y} - I _ {u} ^ {y ^ {\prime}}}{2}\right) P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u, x ^ {\prime}) d u \right| = 0 (| y ^ {\prime} - y |).
$$

By considering the adjoints $I_u^{y*}, I_u^{y'*}$ a similar argument gives the same result for the integral $\int_{t/2}^{t}$.
We finally get that

$$
\left| P _ {t} ^ {y ^ {\prime}} \left(x, x ^ {\prime}\right) - P _ {t} ^ {y} \left(x, x ^ {\prime}\right) \right| = 0 \left(\left| y ^ {\prime} - y \right|\right). \tag {2.46}
$$

This already shows that $P_{t}^{y}(x,x^{\prime})$ depends continuously on $t, y, x, x^{\prime}$.
In particular as $y^\prime \to y$, $P_{t'}^{y'}$ converges to $P_{t'}^{y}$ in the sense of zero order pseudodifferential operators, while staying uniformly bounded in this set for $t^{\prime}\leq t$.
This shows that for $0 &lt; t^{\prime}\leq \frac{t}{2}$, $h\in T_yB$

$$
\lim  _ {s \downarrow \downarrow 0} \int_ {X} \frac {P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y + s h} (x , u) \left(I _ {u} ^ {y + s h} - I _ {u} ^ {y}\right) P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u , x ^ {\prime}) d u}{s} = \int_ {X} P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) \left[ \frac {\partial I _ {u} ^ {y}}{\partial y} h \right] P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) d u \tag {2.47}
$$

and the convergence takes place boundedly.

An obvious symmetry argument shows that (2.47) also holds for $\frac{t}{2} \leq t' &lt; t$.
Using (2.45) and (2.47), we find that $y \to P_t^y(x, x')$ is $C^1$ and moreover

$$
\frac {\partial P _ {t} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} (x, x ^ {\prime}) = - \frac {1}{2} \int_ {0} ^ {t} d t ^ {\prime} \int_ {X} P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) \frac {\partial I _ {u} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u, x ^ {\prime}) d u. \tag {2.48}
$$

Note incidently that by iteration, we find easily that as indicated at the beginning of Sect.
2d), $P_{t}^{y}(x,x^{\prime})$ is smooth in $(t,x,x^{\prime},y)$.

We then get

$$
\frac {\partial}{\partial y ^ {a}} \int_ {X} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {t} ^ {y} (x, x) \right] d x = - \frac {1}{2} \int_ {0} ^ {t} d t ^ {\prime} \int_ {X \times X} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) \frac {\partial I _ {u} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u, x) \right] d u d x. \tag {2.49}
$$

Now recall that $P_{t'}^y(x, u)$ is even.
An obvious extension of Proposition 2.1 shows that

$$
\operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) \frac {\partial I _ {u} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u, x) \right] = \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ \frac {\partial I _ {u} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} P _ {t - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u, x) P _ {t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) \right] \tag {2.50}
$$

J.
M.
Bismut

so that

$$
\frac {\partial}{\partial y ^ {a}} \int_ {x} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {i} ^ {y} (x, x) \right] d x = - \frac {1}{2} \int_ {0} ^ {t} d t ^ {\prime} \int_ {x \times x} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ \frac {\partial I _ {x} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} P _ {i - t ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (x, u) P _ {i ^ {\prime}} ^ {y} (u, x) \right] d u d x. \tag {2.51}
$$

Using the semi-group property of $P_{i}^{y}$ , we see that (2.51) is obviously equal to the r.h.s.
of (2.42).

Remark 2. (2.48) and the estimates in Treves [36, III, Theorem 1.1] show that as $t$ varies in a bounded set, $\frac{\partial P_i^y}{\partial y^a}$ remains uniformly bounded in the set of pseudodifferential operators of order 0. By iteration in (2.48), this is also true for the derivatives of higher order in the variable $y$ .
Of course, this result also follows directly from the construction of the parametrix in Treves [36, III] by the pseudodifferential calculus.

We now prove

Proposition 2.9. The $C^\infty$ form over $B$

$$
\int_ {G _ {y}} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {i} ^ {y} (x, x) \right] d x \tag {2.52}
$$

is closed.

Proof.
From (2.42), we find that

$$
d \int_ {x} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {i} ^ {y} (x, x) \right] d x = - \frac {t}{2} \int_ {x} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ d y ^ {a} \frac {\partial I _ {x} ^ {y}}{\partial y ^ {a}} P _ {i} ^ {y} (x, x ^ {\prime}) \right] _ {x ^ {\prime} = x} d x. \tag {2.53}
$$

Since we have used the parallel transport trivialization of the various bundles at $y$ , (2.41) show that (2.53) is also equal to

$$
- \frac {t}{2} \int_ {G _ {y}} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ \left(\vec {V} I _ {x} ^ {y}\right) P _ {i} ^ {y} \left(x, x ^ {\prime}\right) \right] _ {x ^ {\prime} = x} d x. \tag {2.54}
$$

Since $I^y$ is even, clearly

$$
\left(\vec {V} I ^ {y}\right) = \left[ \vec {V}, I ^ {y} \right]. \tag {2.55}
$$

Using Lemma 2.7, we see that

$$
d \int_ {G _ {y}} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {i} ^ {y} (x, x) \right] d x = \frac {t}{2} \int_ {G _ {y}} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left(\left[ D, I ^ {y} \right] _ {x} P _ {i} ^ {y} \left(x, x ^ {\prime}\right)\right) _ {x ^ {\prime} = x} d x. \tag {2.56}
$$

The operator $De^{-\frac{t}{2} I^y}$ is clearly regularizing, and its kernel is given by $D_xP_i^y (x,x')$ .

Let $J$ be a regularizing pseudodifferential operator in $\operatorname{End} H_x^\infty \hat{\otimes} \Lambda_y(B)$ , which we assume to be even.
Let $J(x, x')$ denote its kernel, which is then even.

Since $J(x, x')$ is even, an obvious extension of Proposition 2.1 shows that

$$
\operatorname {T r} _ {s} [ J (x, u) D _ {u} P _ {i} ^ {y} (u, x) ] = \operatorname {T r} _ {s} [ D _ {u} P _ {i} ^ {y} (u, x) J (x, u) ]. \tag {2.57}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

By integrating with respect to $x, u$ in $G_y \times G_y$, we see that

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[(JDP_t^y)(x, x)\right] dx = \int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[(DP_t^y J)(x, x)\right] dx \tag{2.58}
$$

which can also be written

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[J, DP_t^y\right](x, x) dx = 0. \tag{2.59}
$$

Now $I^y$ can be approximated in the set of pseudodifferential operators of order 2 by a family of regularizing operators.
Since $DP_t^y$ is regularizing, it is clear from (2.59) that

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[I^y, DP_t^y\right](x, x) dx = 0. \tag{2.60}
$$

Moreover

$$
\left[I^y, DP_t^y\right] = \left[I^y, D\right] P_t^y + D \left[I^y, P_t^y\right]. \tag{2.61}
$$

Using (2.43), we find that

$$
\left[I^y, P_t^y\right] = 0 \tag{2.62}
$$

so that (2.60) reads

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[\left[I^y, D\right]_x P_t^y(x, x')\right]_{x' = x} dx = 0. \tag{2.63}
$$

Using (2.56), (2.63), we find that

$$
d \int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[P_t^y(x, x)\right] dx = 0. \tag{2.64}
$$

The proof is finished.
$\square$

We now will show that the cohomology class of (2.52) does not depend on the connection $\tilde{V}$.
However for our special needs, we will have to consider connections of a more general type than the previously considered ones.

More precisely, for each $y \in B$, $Y \in T_yB$, we assume that $C_y(Y)$ is a pseudodifferential operator acting on the sections over $G_y$ of $F_+ \otimes \xi$, depending linearly on $Y \in T_yB$.
We do the following assumption:

For any smooth section $Y$ of $TB$, $C_y(Y(y))$ is a smooth family of zero-order pseudodifferential operators.

Also we assume that $C_y(Y)$ acts as the zero mapping on the sections of $F_- \otimes \xi$.
So $C_y(Y)$ acts now linearly on the sections of $F \otimes \xi$.

For $s \in R$, $Y \in T_yB$, set

$$
\tilde{V}_Y^s = \tilde{V}_Y + sC(Y). \tag{2.65}
$$

$\tilde{V}_-^s$ also defines a connection on $H_{\pm}^{\infty}$.
$\tilde{V}_-^s + D$ is then a superconnection.
Its curvature $I^s$ is given by

$$
I^s = (\tilde{V}^s + D)^2 \tag{2.66}
$$

114
J.
M.
Bismut

or equivalently

$$
I ^ {s} = (\vec {V} + s C + D) ^ {2}. \tag {2.67}
$$

An obvious computation shows that

$$
\begin{array}{l} I ^ {s} = I + s d y ^ {\alpha} [ C (f _ {\alpha}), D ] \\ + \frac {s d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta}}{2} \left[ \left(\vec {V} _ {f _ {\alpha}} C\right) \left(f _ {\beta}\right) - \left(\vec {V} _ {f _ {\beta}} C\right) \left(f _ {\alpha}\right) \right] + \frac {s ^ {2}}{2} d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta} \left[ C \left(f _ {\alpha}\right), C \left(f _ {\beta}\right) \right]. \tag {2.68} \\ \end{array}
$$

$[C(f_{\alpha}), D]$ is of order 1, $[C(f_{\alpha}), C(f_{\beta})]$ is of order 0. Using a local trivialization of $G$ , we also find that $\vec{V}_{f_{\alpha}}C$ is a linear combination of terms in which $\frac{\partial C}{\partial y^{\alpha}}$ and $\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ , which are of order 0 and 1 appear, so that

$$
\frac {s d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta}}{2} \left[ \left(\vec {V} _ {f _ {\alpha}} C\right) \left(f _ {\beta}\right) - \left(\vec {V} _ {f _ {\beta}} C\right) \left(f _ {\alpha}\right) \right] \tag {2.69}
$$

is of order 1 (in fact, as we shall see in (2.90), it is of order 0).
It is then clear that $I^s$ has the same principal symbol as $I$ .

$I^{y,s}$ denotes the restriction of $I^s$ to $G_{y}$ .
For $t &gt; 0$ , let $P_i^{y,s}(x,x')$ be the smooth kernel associated to the regularizing operator $e^{-\frac{tI^{y,s}}{2}}$ .

We now have the extension of Proposition 2.9.

**Proposition 2.10.** For any $t &gt; 0$ , $s \in R$ , the $C^\infty$ form on $B$

$$
\int_ {G _ {y}} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {i} ^ {y, s} (x, x) \right] d x \tag {2.70}
$$

is closed, and its cohomology class is independent of $s$ .

**Proof.** The proof that (2.70) is closed is a word for word repetition of the proof of Proposition 2.9. In particular the fact that the principal symbol of $I^s$ does not depend on $s$ permits us to proceed in the proof of Proposition 2.9 to show that $P_i^{y,s}(x,x')$ is smooth in $(t,x,x',s,y) \in ]0, +\infty[ \times X \times X \times R \times B$ .

We now prove that the cohomology class of (2.70) does not depend on $s$ .
We consider $B \times R$ instead of $B$ , and we extend all the bundles over $B$ as bundles over $B \times R$ in the obvious way.
We also replace $\vec{V}$ by

$$
\vec {V} + d s \frac {\partial}{\partial s}.
$$

Also over $B \times R$ , we consider the superconnection calculated at $(y, s) \in B \times R$

$$
\vec {V} + d s \frac {\partial}{\partial s} + D + s C.
$$

A trivial computation shows that its curvature (on $B \times R$ ) is given by

$$
J = I ^ {s} + d s d y ^ {\alpha} C \left(f _ {\alpha}\right). \tag {2.71}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

If $Q_{t}^{y,s}(x,x')$ is the kernel of $e^{-\frac{tJ}{2}}$, we find from the previous result that

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[Q_t^{y,s}(x, x)\right] dx \tag{2.72}
$$

is closed on $B \times R$.
(2.72) is given by

$$
\alpha_t(y, s) + ds \wedge \beta_t(y, s) \tag{2.73}
$$

where $\alpha_t(y,s)$, $\beta_t(y,s)$ are $C^\infty$ forms on $B$.
Moreover clearly (by making $ds = 0$) we find that

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[P_t^{y,s}(x, x)\right] dx = \alpha_t(y, s). \tag{2.74}
$$

Since (2.73) is closed, we find that

$$
\frac{\partial \alpha_t(y, s)}{\partial s} = d_B \beta_t(y, s). \tag{2.75}
$$

It is then clear that for $s &lt; s'$

$$
\alpha_t(y, s') - \alpha_t(y, s) = d_B \int_s^{s'} \beta_t(y, h) \, dh. \tag{2.76}
$$

The proposition is proved.
$\square$

**Remark 3.** More precisely, it can be easily proved that

$$
\beta_t(y, s) = - \frac{t}{2} \int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[C P_t^{y,s}(x, x)\right] dx. \tag{2.77}
$$

We now construct the natural inverse of $D_-$.
In fact our purpose is to find the right $C$ which will permit us to prove Theorem 2.6.

**Definition 2.11.** $V_y$ denotes the closed vector subspace of $H_{+,y}^0$ which is the orthogonal of $\operatorname{Ker}D_{+,y}$.

Now $D_{-,y}$ is a Fredholm operator from $H_{-,y}^1$ into $H_{+,y}^0$ which has 0 kernel.
It follows that $D_{-,y}$ is continuous and one to one from $H_{-,y}^1$ onto $V_y$.

**Definition 2.12.** $K_y$ denotes the linear operator from $H_{+,y}^0$ into $H_{-,y}^1$ which is 0 on $\operatorname{Ker}D_{+,y}$ and which is equal to the inverse of $D_{-,y}$ on $V_y$.

The closed graph theorem shows that $K_y$ is a continuous operator.

We now have the technical result, for which I acknowledge discussions with N.
Lerner and J.
Sjöstrand.

**Proposition 2.13.** The orthogonal projection operator $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}$, which maps $H_{+,y}^0$ on $\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}$, is a regularizing operator which depends smoothly on $y$.
Also $K_y$ is a smooth family of pseudodifferential operators of order $-1$.

**Proof.** $\operatorname{Ker}D_{+,y}$ is a smooth bundle over $B$.
Moreover for a given $y \in B$, since $D_{+,y}$ is elliptic, $\operatorname{Ker}D_{+,y}$ is made of $C^\infty$ sections along the fiber, so that $P_{\operatorname{Ker}D_{+,y}}$ is

116
J.
M.
Bismut

trivially regularizing.
We now prove that as a regularizing operator $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}$ depends smoothly on $y$.

Using elementary results on parametrices with parameters, we know that since $D_{+,y}^{y}$ is elliptic of order 1 and depends smoothly on $y$, we can find a parametrix $Q_{+,y}$ acting fiberwise, which is a smooth family of pseudodifferential operators of order $-1$.
In particular a smooth family of regularizing operator $R_{+,y}$ exists such that if $I_{\pm}$ is the identity on $F_{\pm} \otimes \xi$

$$
Q_{+,y} D_{+,y} = I_{+} + R_{+,y}. \tag{2.78}
$$

If $D_{+,y}h = 0$, we find from (2.78) that

$$
h = - R_{+,y} h. \tag{2.79}
$$

Now since $\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}$ is a bundle over $B$, over any small enough open set $U$ in $B$, we can find a finite family $h_1(y), \ldots, h_k(y)$ of independent sections of $\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}$ which span $\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}$ and are such that $y \to h_{j,y} \in H_{+,y}^{0}$ is smooth.
Also we assume that $\pi^{-1}(U) \simeq U \times X$.

By (2.79), we have

$$
h_{j,y} = - R_{+,y} [ h_{j,y} ]. \tag{2.80}
$$

Since $h_{j,y}$ is (locally) bounded in $H_{+,y}^{0}$, since $R_{+,y}$ is regularizing, the derivatives $\frac{\partial^{|k|} h_{j,y}(x)}{\partial x^k}$ (calculated along the fiber) are bounded.
Also since $y \to h_{j,y}$ is smooth in the $L_2$ sense, (2.80) shows that the derivatives $\frac{\partial^{|l|} h_{j,y}(x)}{\partial y^l}$ are also bounded.
It immediately follows that $h_{j,y}(x)$ is smooth in $(x,y)$.

It is now clear that $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}$ is a smooth family of regularizing operators.

Let $Q_{-,y}$ be a parametrix for $D_{-,y}$.
$Q_{-,y}$ is a smooth family of pseudodifferential operators of order $-1$.
A smooth family of regularizing operators $R'_{+,y}$, $R'_{-,y}$ exists such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{-,y} D_{-,y} &amp;= I_{-} + R'_{-,y} \\
D_{-,y} Q_{-,y} &amp;= I_{+} + R'_{+,y}. \tag{2.81}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also note that on $H_{-,y}^{+1}$

$$
K_{y} D_{-,y} = I_{-} \tag{2.82}
$$

and that on $H_{+,y}^{0}$

$$
D_{-,y} K_{y} = I_{+} - P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}. \tag{2.83}
$$

For a given $y \in B$, the linear mapping

$$
(h, u) \in H_{-,y}^{+1} \oplus \mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y} \xrightarrow{\varphi_y} D_{-,y} h + u \in H_{+,y}^{0} \tag{2.84}
$$

is clearly one to one and onto.
Moreover $\varphi_y$ is a smooth family of continuous operators.
Also it is clear that

$$
K_{y} = \varphi_{y}^{-1} \left(I - P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}\right) \tag{2.85}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

so that $K_y$ depends smoothly on $y$ in an operator theoretic sense.

We now omit the subscripts $y$.
We first get from (2.81) and (2.83) on $H_{+,y}^{0}$

$$
Q_{-}D_{-}K = Q_{-} - Q_{-}P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+}}
$$

or equivalently

$$
K = Q_{-} - Q_{-}P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+}} - R_{-}^{\prime}K. \tag{2.86}
$$

$P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}$ is a smooth family of regularizing operators, and $K_y$ is smooth in $y$ (as a family of operators).
By proceeding the way we did after (2.79), we find easily from (2.86) that if $\psi(y,x)$ is $C^\infty$ in $(y,x) \in U \times X$, $K_y\psi(y,.)$ is also $C^\infty$ in $(y,x)$.

Also from (2.81), (2.82) we get

$$
KD_{-}Q_{-} = Q_{-}
$$

or equivalently

$$
K = Q_{-} - KR_{+}'. \tag{2.87}
$$

$(KR_{+}')_{,y}$ is now obviously a smooth family of regularizing operators.

From (2.87), we find that $K_y$ is indeed a smooth family of pseudodifferential operators of order $-1$.
$\square$

We now will define a special family of pseudodifferential operators $C_y$ which verifies the assumptions of Proposition 2.10.

**Definition 2.14.** For $y \in B$, $Y \in T_yB$, $C_y(Y)$ is the operator acting on the sections of $F_{+} \otimes \xi$ over $G_y$

$$
C_y(Y) = D_{-}(\vec{V}_Y K) + P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}(\vec{V}_Y P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}). \tag{2.88}
$$

$P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}}(V, P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_{+,y}})$ is obviously a smooth family of regularizing operators.

Also by using a trivialization of $G$, $F \otimes \xi$ over the open set $U$ in $B$, we find that if $V^{\pm}$ is the restriction of $V$ to the sections of $F_{\pm} \otimes \xi$

$$
V_{(\frac{\partial}{\partial y^{\alpha}})}^{\pm} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y^{\alpha}} + X_{\alpha}^{\beta}(x, y) \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{\beta}} + \Gamma^{\pm}(x, y) \tag{2.89}
$$

where $\Gamma^{\pm}(x,y)$ is a matrix valued smooth function.

By definition

$$
\vec{V}_{\frac{\partial}{\partial y^{\alpha}}}K = \frac{\partial}{\partial y^{\alpha}}K_y + \left[ X_{\alpha}^{\beta}(x, y) \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{\beta}}, K_y \right] + \Gamma^{-}(x, y)K_y - K_y\Gamma^{+}(x, y). \tag{2.90}
$$

Now $X_{\alpha}^{\beta}(x,y) \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{\beta}}$ is a scalar operator of order 1, and $K_y$ is of order $-1$.
By Treves [36, I, Corollary 4.2], the commutator $\left[X_{\alpha}^{\beta}(x,y) \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{\beta}}, K_y\right]$ is of order $-1$, and so (2.90) is of order $-1$.

$D_{-}(\vec{V}_Y K)$ is obviously of order 0. $C_y(.)$ has then all the required properties.

We still extend $C_y$ to the sections of $F \otimes \xi$ by assuming $C$ is 0 on $F_{-} \otimes \xi$.

118
J.
M.
Bismut

Definition 2.15. $\tilde{V}'$ is the connection on $H^\infty$

$$
\tilde{V}' = \tilde{V} + C. \tag{2.91}
$$

We now have the key result.

Proposition 2.16. For any smooth section $h(y)$ of $H_+^\infty$, for $Y \in TB$

$$
\tilde{V}_Y' h = P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \tilde{V}_Y' [P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} h] + D_- \tilde{V}_Y' (Kh). \tag{2.92}
$$

$\mathrm{Ker}D_+$, $V \cap H_+^\infty$ are stable under $\tilde{V}'$.
Also

$$
\tilde{V}' (D_-) = 0. \tag{2.93}
$$

Proof.
Clearly

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \tilde{V}_Y' [P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} h] + D_- \tilde{V}_Y' (Kh) &amp;= (P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \tilde{V}_Y P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}) h + P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \tilde{V}_Y' h \\
&amp;\quad + D_- K \tilde{V}_Y' h + D_- (\tilde{V}_Y K) h. \tag{2.94}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now by definition

$$
D_- K = I - P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \tag{2.95}
$$

(2.92) is proved.
Also if $k \in H_-^1$, we have by (2.92)

$$
\tilde{V}_Y' (D_- k) = D_- \tilde{V}_Y' (K D_- h). \tag{2.96}
$$

Now $K D_-$ is the identity of $H_-^1$, so that

$$
\tilde{V}_Y' (D_- k) = D_- \tilde{V}_Y' k. \tag{2.97}
$$

Also $\tilde{V}_Y' k = \tilde{V}_Y' k$.
Using (2.97), (2.93) follows.

Also if $h \in \mathrm{Ker}D_+$ then

$$
Kh = 0.
$$

Moreover if $h \in V$

$$
P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} h = 0.
$$

Noting that $D_-$ takes its values in $V$, we find that $\mathrm{Ker}D_+$ and $V \cap H_+^\infty$ are stable under $\tilde{V}'$.
$\square$

Let $\tilde{R}'$ denote the curvature of the ordinary connection $\tilde{V}'$.
Proposition 1.11 and (2.68) (with $D$ replaced by 0) show that $\tilde{R}_y'$ takes its values in the set of pseudodifferential operators of order 1, and depends smoothly on $y$.
Since $\tilde{V}'$ coincides with $\tilde{V}$ on $F_- \otimes \xi$, $\tilde{R}'$ coincides with $\tilde{R}$ on $H_-^\infty$.

Also $\mathrm{Ker}D_+$ and $V \cap H_+^\infty$ are stable by $\tilde{R}'$.
In particular the restriction of $\tilde{R}'$ to $\mathrm{Ker}D_+$ — which we note $\tilde{R}'^{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ — is the curvature of the connection $\tilde{V}'$ restricted to $\mathrm{Ker}D_+$.

We now use the notations in Proposition 2.10 for the special choice $C$ given by (2.88).

We have the final step in the proof of Theorem 2.6 under assumption H1.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

**Proposition 2.17.** The following equality holds

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[ P_t^{y,1}(x, x) \right] dx = \operatorname{Tr} \exp \left[ -\frac{t \tilde{R}'^{\text{Ker}D_{+}, y}}{2} \right]. \tag{2.98}
$$

**Proof.** Let $\tilde{R}_{+}^{\prime},\tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime}$ be the restrictions of $\tilde{R}^{\prime}$ to $H_{+}^{\infty},H_{-}^{\infty}$.
Since $\tilde{V}.D_{-} = 0$, it is clear that

$$
\tilde{R}_{+} D_{-} = D_{-} \tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime}. \tag{2.99}
$$

Using the fact that $D_{-}K$ is the orthogonal projection operator $P_V$ on $V$, we see that

$$
D_{-} \left[ \tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime} + D_{+} D_{-} \right] K = \left(\tilde{R}_{+} + D_{-} D_{+}\right) P_V. \tag{2.100}
$$

We now compute $I^1$ (given by (2.67) for $s = 1$).

Quillen's formula (2.17) remaining formally valid, we have

$$
I^{1} = \tilde{R}^{\prime} + \tilde{V}.D + D^{2}. \tag{2.101}
$$

We now write $I^1$ in matrix form on $H_{+}^{\infty} \oplus H_{-}^{\infty}$.
Recalling that $\tilde{V}.D_{-} = 0$, we get

$$
I^{1} = \begin{bmatrix} \tilde{R}_{+} + D_{-} D_{+} &amp; 0 \\ \tilde{V}.D_{+} &amp; \tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime} + D_{+} D_{-} \end{bmatrix}. \tag{2.102}
$$

The kernel $P_{t}^{y,1}(x,x')$ of $e^{-\frac{t}{2} I^{y,1}}$ can be also written in matrix form

$$
P_{t}^{y,1}(x, x') = \begin{bmatrix} A_{t}^{y}(x, x') &amp; B_{t}^{y}(x, x') \\ B_{t}^{\prime y}(x, x') &amp; A_{t}^{\prime y}(x, x') \end{bmatrix}. \tag{2.103}
$$

Recall that

$$
\frac{\partial P_{t}^{y,1}(x, x')}{\partial t} = -\frac{I_{x}^{y,1}}{2} P_{t}^{y,1}(x, x'). \tag{2.104}
$$

Using (2.102), we find immediately that $A_{t}^{y}(x,x')$ is the kernel of the operator acting on $H_{+}^{\infty}$ $e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\tilde{R}_{+}^{\prime} + D_{-}D_{+})}$.
Also by (2.102), (2.104)

$$
\frac{\partial B_{t}^{y}(x, x')}{\partial t} = -\frac{(\tilde{R}_{+} + D_{-} D_{+})_{x}}{2} B_{t}^{y}(x, x'). \tag{2.105}
$$

Since $B_0^y = 0$, we see that $B_t^y (x,x') = 0$.

We then find that

$$
\frac{\partial A_{t}^{\prime y}(x, x')}{\partial t} = -\frac{(\tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime} + D_{+} D_{-})_{x}}{2} A_{t}^{\prime y}(x, x') \tag{2.106}
$$

so that $A_{t}^{\prime y}(x,x')$ is the kernel acting on $H_{-}^{\infty}$ of $e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime} + D_{-}D_{+})}$.

Also by definition

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[ P_{t}^{y,1}(x, x) \right] dx = \int_{G_y} \left( \operatorname{Tr} A_{t}^{y}(x, x) - \operatorname{Tr} A_{t}^{\prime y}(x, x) \right) dx \tag{2.107}
$$

where $\operatorname{Tr} A_t^y(x,x)$, $\operatorname{Tr} A_t^{\prime y}(x,x)$ take their values in $\Lambda_y(B)$.

120
J.
M.
Bismut

To simplify the notations, we will write

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr} A_t^y(x, x) \, dx = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} \right] \tag{2.108}
$$

and use a similar notation for any regularizing operator.
Of course (2.108) is a definition and is not related directly to the trace of $e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)}$ in a functional sense.

Now since $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ and $P_V$ are pseudodifferential operators of order 0, we have

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} \right] = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \right] + \operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_V \right]. \tag{2.109}
$$

$\left(e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}\right.$ and $\left.e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_V\right.$ are regularizing).
Also $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}^2 = P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ and moreover

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}^2 \right] = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \right] \tag{2.110}
$$

(commuting operators is feasible since $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ is regularizing).

Now $\mathrm{Ker}D_+$ and $V \cap H_+^\infty$ are stable under $\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_-D_+$.
Clearly

$$
P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+) P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} = P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \bar{R}_+^\prime P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \tag{2.111}
$$

so that

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \right] = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} e^{-\frac{t}{2} \bar{R}_+^\prime} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \right]. \tag{2.112}
$$

Now $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ has finite dimensional rank, and it is trivial to verify that

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} e^{-\frac{t}{2} \bar{R}_+^\prime} P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+} \right] = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} \bar{R}_+^\prime \mathrm{Ker}D_+} \right] \tag{2.113}
$$

(where the l.h.s.
is defined as in (2.108) using kernels).

Also $P_V^2 = P_V$ and moreover

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_V^2 \right] = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ P_V e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_V \right] \tag{2.114}
$$

(here commutation is possible because $P_V = I - P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ and $P_{\mathrm{Ker}D_+}$ is regularizing).

Also using (2.100), we find that

$$
P_V e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_V = D_- e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_+ D_-)} K. \tag{2.115}
$$

We get

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ P_V e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_- D_+)} P_V \right] = \operatorname{Tr} \left[ D_- e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\bar{R}_+^\prime + D_+ D_-)} K \right]. \tag{2.116}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

By commuting $D_{-}$ – which is possible using an approximation argument as in (2.59), (2.60) because $e^{-\frac{t}{2} (\tilde{R}_{-}^{\prime} + D_{+}D_{-})}$ is regularizing, – since $KD_{-} = I_{-}$, we get

$$
\operatorname {Tr} \left[ D _ {-} e ^ {- \frac {t}{2} \left(\tilde {R} _ {-} ^ {\prime} + D _ {+} D _ {-}\right)} K \right] = \operatorname {Tr} \left[ e ^ {- \frac {t}{2} \left(\tilde {R} _ {-} ^ {\prime} + D _ {+} D _ {-}\right)} \right]. \tag {2.117}
$$

From (2.107)-(2.117), we finally get (2.98).

This finishes the proof of the Theorem.
In fact $\mathrm{Tr}\left[\exp -\frac{\tilde{R}^{\prime}\mathrm{Ker}D_{+}}{2}\right]$ represents in cohomology $\overline{ch}\mathrm{Ker}D_{+}$.
The Theorem then follows from Proposition 2.10.

We now briefly show how to lift assumption H1.

By using a standard argument in $K$-theory (see Atiyah and Singer [9, Proposition 2.2]), we know that $q \in N$ and $C^\infty$ sections $s_1 \ldots s_q$ of $F_{-} \otimes \xi$ over $M$ exist such that if $\bar{D}_{+,y}$ is the operator

$$
(h, \lambda) \in H _ {+, y} ^ {s} \oplus C ^ {q} \rightarrow \bar {D} _ {+, y} (h, \lambda) = D _ {+, y} h + \sum_ {1} ^ {q} \lambda_ {i} s _ {i} \in H _ {-} ^ {s - 1} \tag {2.118}
$$

then for any $y \in B$, $\bar{D}_{+,y}$ is onto (this property does not depend on $s$).

Putting on $C^q$ its canonical Hermitian structure and assuming that $C^q$ and $H_{+,y}^{s}$ are orthogonal, we find that the formal adjoint $\bar{D}_{-,y}$ of $\bar{D}_{+,y}$ is given by

$$
h ^ {\prime} \in H _ {-, y} ^ {s} \rightarrow \bar {D} _ {-, y} h ^ {\prime} = \left(D _ {-} h ^ {\prime}, \left(\langle h ^ {\prime}, s _ {1} \rangle , \dots , \langle h ^ {\prime}, s _ {q} \rangle\right)\right). \tag {2.119}
$$

$\mathrm{Ker}\bar{D}_{-,y}$ is now reduced to $\{0\}$.
By definition (see [9, Proposition 2.2]), in the sense of $K$-theory

$$
\operatorname {K e r} D _ {+, y} - \operatorname {K e r} D _ {-, y} = \operatorname {K e r} \bar {D} _ {+, y} - C ^ {q}. \tag {2.120}
$$

$\bar{D}_{-}\bar{D}_{+}$ is given in matrix form (on $H_{+}^{s}\oplus C^{q}$) by

$$
\bar {D} _ {-} \bar {D} _ {+} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} D _ {-} D _ {+} &amp; D _ {-} s _ {i} \\ \langle ., D _ {-} s _ {j} \rangle , &amp; \langle s _ {i}, s _ {j} \rangle \end{array} \right]
$$

and $\bar{D}_{+}\bar{D}_{-}$ is given (on $H_{-}^{s}$) by

$$
\bar {D} _ {+} \bar {D} _ {-} = D _ {+} D _ {-} + \sum_ {1} ^ {q} s _ {i} \langle ., s _ {i} \rangle .
$$

There is no difficulty in reproducing for $\bar{D}_{+}$, $\bar{D}_{-}$, what has been done before for $D_{+}, D_{-}$, since in the expression of $\bar{D}_{+}\bar{D}_{-}$ and $\bar{D}_{-}\bar{D}_{+}$, except $D_{+}D_{-}$ and $D_{-}D_{+}$, the other operators are regularizing or bounded.

The essential point is to observe that if for $\varepsilon &gt; 0$, $\bar{D}_{+}$ is replaced by $\bar{D}_{+}^{\varepsilon}$ given by

$$
\bar {D} _ {+} ^ {\varepsilon} (h, \lambda) = D _ {+, y} h + \varepsilon \sum_ {1} ^ {q} \lambda_ {i} s _ {i}.
$$

$\bar{D}^{\varepsilon}$ still has the same properties $\bar{D}$.
Also in the analogue of Proposition 2.10, it is easy to prove (by similar techniques) that the corresponding cohomology class

122
J.
M.
Bismut

does not depend on $\varepsilon$.
It is then feasible to make $\varepsilon \downarrow \downarrow 0$ in such formulas.
When $\varepsilon \downarrow \downarrow 0$, if the trivial connection is considered on $C^q$, we get

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[P_l^s(x, x)\right] dx + q - q = \int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[P_l^y(x, x)\right] dx
$$

$(+q$ comes from the trace evaluated on $C^q$, $-q$ from (2.120)).
The Theorem is then proved.
$\square$

## III.

The Levi-Civita superconnection: A simple formula for the curvature

In the heat equation proof of the ordinary Index Theorem for Dirac operators acting on twisted spin complexes [6, 12, 18, 26, 27], it is well known that the method works only if the Dirac operator is calculated using the Levi-Civita connection on the base manifold, although the Dirac operator calculated with any other connection has the same index. The importance of the Levi-Civita connection appears clearly in the very simple formula of Lichnerowicz given in (0.3).

In our proof of the Index Theorem for families, we have one more degree of arbitrariness i.e. the choice of the superconnection, since in principle, all the superconnections produce representatives in cohomology of $\overline{ch}(\mathrm{Ker}D_+ - \mathrm{Ker}D_-)$.

It turns out that the superconnection $\tilde{V} + D$ considered in Sect.
2 is not adequate for an asymptotic determination of the Chern character.
For reasons which are already apparent in [4, 19, 20a] and in formula (0.4) and should appear more clearly in our paper [21], we must introduce an extension of the Levi-Civita connection on $M$, which is the Levi-Civita superconnection.
The new operator $\tilde{V}^L + D$ differs from $\tilde{V} + D$ by terms of degree 1 and 3 in $\mathcal{E}$, which are of utmost importance when studying the asymptotics of the formula for the Chern character.

Incidentally, note that the full strength of Quillen's formalism [33] should appear here, since we go beyond the simple case considered in (2.17), of which (2.25) was the infinite dimensional analogue.

In a), the Levi-Civita superconnection is introduced, and the analogue of Theorem 2.6 is briefly proved.
In b), a remarkably simple formula for the curvature $I^L$ is proved, which extends Lichnerowicz's formula (0.3) and our formula (0.4).
Finally in c), a scaling parameter $t &gt; 0$ is introduced.
By making $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, we will obtain the Atiyah-Singer formula in Sect.
4.

### a) The Levi-Civita superconnection

We now modify the construction of Sect.
2. Recall that $S$ has been defined in Definition 1.8.

**Definition 3.1.** $E$ denotes the odd element in $\mathcal{E}$ given by

$$
E = e_i\left[\frac{1}{2}\langle S(e_i)e_j, f_\alpha \rangle e_j dy^\alpha + \frac{1}{4}\langle S(e_i)f_\alpha, f_\beta \rangle dy^\alpha dy^\beta\right] + \frac{1}{2}\langle S(f_\alpha)e_i, f_\beta \rangle dy^\alpha e_i dy^\beta. \tag{3.1}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Note that by Theorem 1.9,

$$
\langle S(e_i) e_j, e_k \rangle = \langle S(f_\alpha) e_j, e_k \rangle = \langle S(f_\alpha) f_\beta, f_\gamma \rangle = 0.
$$

The rationale for the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ is formula (1.4).
$\frac{1}{2}$ naturally appears for mixed terms of the type $e_i dy^a$.

$E$ obviously defines an odd element in $\operatorname{End} H^\infty \hat{\otimes} \Lambda(B)$.

Also note the important fact that by Theorem 1.9, $E$ does not depend on $g_B$.

**Definition 3.2.** The Levi-Civita superconnection $\tilde{V}^L$ on $H^\infty$ is given by

$$
\tilde{V}^L = \tilde{V} + E. \tag{3.2}
$$

We first have the obvious result:

**Proposition 3.3.** The following identity holds

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{V}^L + D &amp;= e_i \left[ V_{e_i} + \frac{1}{2} \langle S(e_i) e_j, f_\alpha \rangle e_j dy^a + \frac{1}{4} \langle S(e_i) f_\alpha, f_\beta \rangle dy^a dy^\beta \right] \\
&amp;\quad + dy^a \left[ V_{f_\alpha} + \frac{1}{2} \langle S(f_\alpha) e_i, f_\beta \rangle e_i dy^\beta \right] \tag{3.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\tilde{V}^L + D$ does not depend on $g_B$.

**Proof.** (3.3) is obvious.
Since $E$ does not depend on $g_B$, $\tilde{V}^L + D$ does not depend on $g_B$.
$\square$

The denomination “Levi-Civita superconnection” should now be justified.
Indeed if the $dy^a$ are replaced by the corresponding Clifford variables, $\tilde{V}^L + D$ becomes exactly the Dirac operator on $M$ calculated with the ordinary Levi-Civita connection of $M$.
This observation plays a key role in the proof of Theorem 3.5.

Let $I^L$ be the curvature of $\tilde{V}^L + D$, i.e.

$$
I^L = (\tilde{V}^L + D)^2. \tag{3.4}
$$

Still $I^L$ is an elliptic second order differential operator, so that for $t &gt; 0$, $e^{-\frac{tI_L^L}{2}}$ is given by a smooth kernel $P_t^{L,y}(x,x')$.

We first have the obvious extension of Theorem 2.6.

**Theorem 3.4.** The $C^\infty$ element of $\Lambda(B)$

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s \left[ P_1^{L,y}(x,x) \right] dx \tag{3.5}
$$

is a representative in cohomology of $\overline{ch}(\operatorname{Ker} D_{+,y} - \operatorname{Ker} D_{-,y})$.

**Proof.** The proof of Proposition 2.10 shows that if the 0 order odd $C$ is replaced by the 0 order $\varepsilon E$, the corresponding cohomology class does not depend on $\varepsilon$.
For $\varepsilon = 0$, we get (2.38) and for $\varepsilon = 1$, we obtain (3.5).
The Theorem is proved.
$\square$

**b) A formula for $I^L$**

In the sequel, if $A(Y)$ is any 0 order operator depending linearly on $Y \in TM$, we define the operator

$$
(V_{e_i} + A(e_i))^2
$$

124
J.
M.
Bismut

as follows: if $(e_1(x), \ldots, e_n(x))$ is any (locally defined) smooth orthonormal base of $TG$, then

$$
\left(V _ {e _ {i}} + A \left(e _ {i}\right)\right) ^ {2} = \sum_ {1} ^ {n} \left(V _ {e _ {i} (x)} + A \left(e _ {i} (x)\right) ^ {2} - V _ {\sum_ {j} V _ {e _ {j}} e _ {j}} - A \left(\sum_ {j} V _ {e _ {j}} e _ {j}\right)\right). \tag {3.6}
$$

Recall that $K$ is the scalar curvature of the fiber $G$.

We now have the following remarkable identity.

**Theorem 3.5.** The curvature $I^L$ of the superconnection $\tilde{V}^L + D$ is given by

$$
\begin{array}{l} I ^ {L} = - \left(V _ {e _ {i}} + \frac {1}{2} \langle S (e _ {i}) e _ {j}, f _ {\alpha} \rangle e _ {j} d y ^ {\alpha} + \frac {1}{4} \langle S (e _ {i}) f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta} \rangle d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta}\right) ^ {2} \\ + \frac {K}{4} + \frac {1}{2} e _ {i} e _ {j} \otimes L \left(e _ {i}, e _ {j}\right) + \frac {1}{2} d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta} \otimes L \left(f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta}\right) + e _ {i} d y ^ {\alpha} \otimes L \left(e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha}\right). \tag {3.7} \\ \end{array}
$$

**Proof.** We give a short proof of (3.7) based on Lichnerowicz's formula (2.34).

We know that $\tilde{V}^L + D$ does not depend on the metric on $B$.
Since the computation of $I^L$ is purely local, we can as well assume that $B$ is equal to the Euclidean space $R^m$ with its flat metric $g_{R^m}$.

Let $F'$ be the Hermitian space of spinors associated with the spin representation of $\operatorname{Spin}(m)$.
$M$ is then obviously spin, and the bundle of spinors over $M$ is exactly $F \otimes F'$.

Let $D^c$ be the Dirac operator acting on the sections of $F \otimes F' \otimes \xi$, which is calculated using the Levi-Civita connection $V^L$ of $M$ and the connection $V$ on $\xi$.
$V^L$ denotes the corresponding connection on $F \otimes F' \otimes \xi$.
Then $D^c$ is given by

$$
D ^ {c} = e _ {i} V _ {e _ {i}} ^ {L} + f _ {\alpha} V _ {f _ {\alpha}} ^ {L}.
$$

By Lichnerowicz's formula (2.34), if $K^M$ is the scalar curvature of $M$

$$
\begin{array}{l} (D ^ {c}) ^ {2} = - \left(V _ {e _ {i}} ^ {L}\right) ^ {2} - \left(V _ {f _ {\alpha}} ^ {L}\right) ^ {2} + \frac {K ^ {M}}{4} + \frac {1}{2} e _ {i} e _ {j} \otimes L \left(e _ {i}, e _ {j}\right) + \frac {1}{2} f _ {\alpha} f _ {\beta} \otimes L \left(f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta}\right) \\ + e _ {i} f _ {\alpha} \otimes L \left(e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha}\right). \tag {3.8} \\ \end{array}
$$

Take $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ and replace the flat metric $g_{R^m}$ by $\frac{g_{R^m}}{\varepsilon}$ while leaving $g_G$ unchanged.
The new metric $g_\varepsilon$ on $TM$ is still parallel for $V$.
Let $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\varepsilon$ be the new scalar product in $TM$ and let $V^{L,\varepsilon}$ be the Levi-Civita connection on $TM$ for the metric $g_\varepsilon$.

Set

$$
S ^ {\varepsilon} = V ^ {L, \varepsilon} - V. \tag {3.9}
$$

By Theorem 1.9, the $(3,0)$ tensor $\langle S^{\varepsilon}(.)\ldots \rangle_{\varepsilon}$ coincides with $\langle S(.)\ldots \rangle$.
It immediately follows that if $X,Y\in TM$

$$
\begin{array}{l} P _ {G} S ^ {\varepsilon} (X) Y = P _ {G} S (X) Y \\ P _ {H} S ^ {\varepsilon} (X) Y = P _ {H} \varepsilon S (X) Y. \tag {3.10} \\ \end{array}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

$\sqrt[3]{\varepsilon} f_{1}, \ldots, \sqrt[3]{\varepsilon} f_{m}$ is an orthonormal base of $TR^{m}$ for $\frac{\theta_{R^{m}}}{\varepsilon}$.
It follows that if $D^{c,\varepsilon}$ is the Dirac operator associated with $V^{L,\varepsilon}$, then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
D^{c,\varepsilon} = e_{i} \left(V_{e_{i}} + \frac{1}{2} \langle S(e_{i})e_{j}, f_{\alpha} \rangle e_{j} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} + \frac{1}{4} \langle S(e_{i})f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta} \rangle \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\beta}\right) \\
+ \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} \left(V_{f_{\alpha}} + \frac{1}{2} \langle S(f_{\alpha})e_{i}, f_{\beta} \rangle e_{i} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\beta}\right). \tag{3.11}
\end{array}
$$

Now when acting on $C^\infty$ sections of the vector space $F'$ over $R^m$, $V_{e_i}$ is 0 and $V_{f_\alpha}$ is equal to $\frac{\partial}{\partial f_\alpha}$.
It follows that $V_{e_i}, V_{f_\alpha}$ are of degree 0 in the Clifford variables $f_\alpha$.
It is then clear that by (3.11), each Clifford variable $f_\alpha$ is multiplied by the scaling factor $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$.

Now by (3.10), since $S(e_i)e_i \in T^H M$

$$
S^{\varepsilon}(e_{i})e_{i} = \varepsilon S(e_{i})e_{i}. \tag{3.12}
$$

Also by (3.11), since $S^{\varepsilon}(\sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha})\sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\beta} \in TG$

$$
S^{\varepsilon}(\sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha}) \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\beta} = \varepsilon S(f_{\alpha}) f_{\beta}. \tag{3.13}
$$

Let $K^{M,\varepsilon}$ be the scalar curvature of $M$ for $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_{\varepsilon}$.

Recall that we use the convention (3.6).
By formula (3.8), we get

$$
\begin{array}{l}
(D^{c,\varepsilon})^{2} = - \left(V_{e_{i}} + \frac{1}{2} \langle S(e_{i})e_{j}, f_{\alpha} \rangle e_{j} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} + \frac{1}{4} \langle S(e_{i})f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta} \rangle \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\beta}\right)^{2} \\
- \left(\sqrt{\varepsilon} V_{f_{\alpha}} + \frac{1}{2} \langle S(f_{\alpha})e_{i}, f_{\beta} \rangle e_{i} \varepsilon f_{\beta}\right)^{2} + V_{\varepsilon S(e_{i})e_{i} + \varepsilon S(f_{\alpha})f_{\alpha}} \\
+ \frac{1}{2} \langle S(S(e_{i})e_{i} + S(f_{\alpha})f_{\alpha})e_{j}, f_{\beta} \rangle e_{j} \varepsilon^{3/2} f_{\beta} \\
+ \frac{1}{4} \langle S(S(e_{i})e_{i} + S(f_{\alpha})f_{\alpha})f_{\beta}, f_{\gamma} \rangle \varepsilon^{3/2} f_{\beta} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\gamma} \\
+ \frac{1}{2} e_{i} e_{j} \otimes L(e_{i}, e_{j}) + \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\beta} \otimes L(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}) \\
+ e_{i} \sqrt{\varepsilon} f_{\alpha} \otimes L(e_{i}, f_{\alpha}) + \frac{K^{M,\varepsilon}}{4}. \tag{3.14}
\end{array}
$$

In the r.h.s.
of (3.14), we define the weight in the Clifford variables $f_{\alpha}$ of a given monomial to be the length (in $c(TB)$) of this monomial.
For instance 1 has length 0, $f_{\alpha}f_{\beta}$ has length 2 if $\alpha \neq \beta$, length 0 if $\alpha = \beta$ etc. ...
Now in $(D^{c,\varepsilon})^2$, each monomial in the variables $f_{\alpha}$ is multiplied by a certain power of $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$, this power being at least equal to the corresponding length.
The power of $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$ is exactly equal to the length when no contractions have occurred in the Clifford algebra $c(TB)$.
It follows that if in the r.h.s.
of (3.14) we only select those monomials in the $f_{\alpha}$ of length $k$, which are multiplied by the factor $(\sqrt{\varepsilon})^k$, and make the other monomials equal to 0, we get an identity in which the Clifford variables can be replaced again by the Grassmann variables $dy^{\alpha}$.

For instance $V_{e_i}$ is of length 0 and so should be kept in the r.h.s.
of (3.14), while $\sqrt{\varepsilon} V_{f_\alpha}$ has length 0 and should be erased.
Similarly $\frac{1}{2} \langle S(f_\alpha)e_i, f_\beta \rangle e_i \varepsilon f_\beta$ has length 1 and should also be erased as well as $V_{\varepsilon S(e_i)e_i + \varepsilon S(f_\alpha)f_\alpha}$.

126
J.
M.
Bismut

$K^{M,\varepsilon}$ is a scalar, so we should only replace $K^{M,\varepsilon}$ by $\lim_{\varepsilon \downarrow 0} K^{M,\varepsilon}$.
Let $R^{L,\varepsilon}$ be the curvature tensor of $V^{L,\varepsilon}$.
If $\check{D}$ is the horizontal exterior differentiation operator for $V$, we know that

$$
R^{L,\varepsilon} = R + \check{D}S^{\varepsilon} + [S^{\varepsilon}, S^{\varepsilon}]. \tag{3.15}
$$

Since $\check{D}S(e_i, e_j)e_k \in T^H M$, we get

$$
\langle R^{L,\varepsilon}(e_i, e_j)e_i, e_j \rangle_{\varepsilon} = \langle R(e_i, e_j)e_i, e_j \rangle + \langle [S^{\varepsilon}(e_i), S^{\varepsilon}(e_j)]e_i, e_j \rangle_{\varepsilon}. \tag{3.16}
$$

Now $S^{\varepsilon}(e_i)e_j \in T^H M$, so we find easily using (3.10) that the last term in (3.16) is factor of $\varepsilon$.

Also $\langle R^{L,\varepsilon}(\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\alpha},\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\beta})\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\alpha},\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\beta}\rangle_{\varepsilon}$ is $0(\varepsilon)$.

Finally

$$
\langle R^{L,\varepsilon}(\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\alpha}, e_i)\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\alpha}, e_i \rangle_{\varepsilon} = \langle R^{L,\varepsilon}(\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\alpha}, e_i)\sqrt{\varepsilon}f_{\alpha}, e_i \rangle. \tag{3.17}
$$

Using (3.15), we find that the r.h.s.
of (3.17) is $0(\varepsilon)$.

We have proved formula (3.7).
$\square$

**Remark 1.** Although a direct computation is possible, it is not really pleasant.
Also note that some of the ideas in our proof of (3.7) are used in our second proof in Sect.
5, in particular in introducing the Clifford variables $f_{\alpha}$.

**Remark 2.** Let us temporally assume that we are under the assumptions of Sect.
1.d). In this case, using (3.3), (1.28), and (1.30), we find easily that

$$
\tilde{V}^L + D = D - \frac{1}{4}T + \tilde{V} \tag{3.18}
$$

where in (3.18) $T$ is identified with the element of $\mathcal{E}$

$$
\frac{1}{2}\langle T(f_{\alpha}, f_{\beta}), e_i \rangle dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} e_i. \tag{3.19}
$$

It is easy to prove that in this situation, both $D$ and $T$ are parallel with respect to $\tilde{V}$.

By Proposition 1.11 and (1.31), we find that if $\xi$ is the trivial bundle, $\tilde{R}(\cdot,\cdot)$ is exactly the Lie derivative operator $L_{-T(\cdot,\cdot)}$ associated with the Killing vector field of the fiber $-T(\cdot,\cdot)$.

Using Quillen’s formula (2.17), we find that

$$
I^L = (D - \frac{1}{4}T)^2 - L_T. \tag{3.20}
$$

Now Theorem 3.5 shows that

$$
I^L = - \left(V_{e_i} + \frac{1}{4}\langle T, e_i \rangle\right)^2 + \frac{K}{4} \tag{3.21}
$$

so that we get the identity

$$
\left(D - \frac{1}{4}T\right)^2 - L_T = - \left(V_{e_i} + \frac{1}{4}\langle T, e_i \rangle\right)^2 + \frac{K}{4}. \tag{3.22}
$$

Observe that $dy^{\alpha}dy^{\beta}$ is in the center of $\mathcal{E}$, so that in (3.22), we can do as if $T$ was any Killing vector field on the fiber acting on the left by Clifford multiplication.
(3.22) is then exactly the identity (0.4) which we proved in [20a, Theorem 1.6].

Families index theorem and the heat equation

## c) Scaling of the metric in the fiber

We now change the metric on the fiber bundle $TG$, replacing the initial $g_{G}$ by $\frac{g_{G}}{t}$ (with $t &gt; 0$), while we keep unchanged the metric of $TB$.
This defines a new metric $g_{t}^{\prime}$ on $TM$.

We claim that $V$ has the same properties with respect to the new metrics $\frac{g_G}{t}$ and $g_t'$ as before.
Namely, let $V^{L,t}$ be the Levi-Civita connection on $TM$ for $g_t'$.
Set

$$
V^{L, t} = V. + S^{\alpha}. \tag{3.23}
$$

Let $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_t$ be the scalar product $g_t'$ in $TM$.

Formula (1.28) and the fact that $T$ takes its values in $TG$ shows that if $X, Y, Z \in TM$

$$
\langle S^{\alpha}(X) Y, Z \rangle_t = \frac{\langle S(X) Y, Z \rangle}{t}. \tag{3.24}
$$

In particular if $X \in TM$, $Y \in TG$, $S(X)Y \in T^H M$ so that

$$
V_Y^G Z = P_G V_Y^{L, t} Z \tag{3.25}
$$

i.e. the conditions of Definition 1.6 are still verified.

In the scaling process, the orthonormal base $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ is replaced by $\sqrt[t]{t} e_1, \ldots, \sqrt[t]{t} e_n$, but of course the $e_i$ are unchanged in $c(TG)$, since they still must verify the commutation relations (1.1).
For instance $D$ is now replaced by $D_t = \sqrt[t]{t} D$.

Let $I^{L,t}$ be the curvature of $\tilde{V}^{L,t} + D_t$, i.e.

$$
I^{L, t} = (\tilde{V}^{L, t} + D_t)^2. \tag{3.26}
$$

We now have

**Theorem 3.6.** $I^{L,t}$ is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
I^{L, t} &amp;= -t \left( V_{e_i} + \frac{1}{2t} \langle S(e_i) e_j, f_\alpha \rangle \sqrt[t]{t} e_j dy^{\alpha} + \frac{1}{4t} \langle S(e_i) f_\alpha, f_\beta \rangle dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} \right)^2 \\
&amp;\quad + \frac{tK}{4} + \frac{1}{2} t e_i e_j \otimes L(e_i, e_j) + \frac{1}{2} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta} \otimes L(f_\alpha, f_\beta) + \sqrt[t]{t} e_i dy^{\alpha} \otimes L(e_i, f_\alpha). \tag{3.27}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $P_1^{L,t,y}(x,x')$ be the $C^\infty$ kernel over $G_y$ of $e^{-\frac{tL^2t}{2}}$.

Then

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s\left[ P_1^{L, t, y}(x, x) \right] dx \tag{3.28}
$$

is a $C^\infty$ form over $B$ which is a representative of $\overline{ch}(\operatorname{Ker} D_{+,y} - \operatorname{Ker} D_{-,y})$.

**Proof.** First note that expressions like $\langle S(e_i) e_j, f_\alpha \rangle$ become $\frac{\langle S(\sqrt[t]{t} e_i) \sqrt[t]{t} e_j, f_\alpha \rangle}{t}$ because of (3.24) and of the rescaling of $e_i, e_j$ in $TG$.
Also observe that $D_+$, $D_-$ are

128
J.
M.
Bismut

changed into $\sqrt[t]{t} D_{+}, \sqrt[t]{t} D_{-}$ and so $\operatorname{Ker}\sqrt[t]{t} D_{+}, \operatorname{Ker}\sqrt[t]{t} D_{-}$ do not change with $t$.
The Theorem is obvious by (3.24), and Theorems 2.6 and 3.4. ☐

**Remark 3.** The heat equation method will now consist in making $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$ in (3.28).

## IV.

The asymptotics of the supertrace: The index theorem for families

In this section, we now study the asymptotics of (3.28) as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$.
We use the asymptotic representation of Brownian bridges which we gave in [16] in an essential way.

In a), we give a few probabilistic preliminaries.
In b), we obtain an a priori estimate which is necessary to control the singularity $\frac{1}{t}$ in (3.27).
In c), using our results of [16], we give an asymptotic representation of $P_{1}^{L,t,y}(x_{0},x_{0})$ using a Brownian bridge in $T_{x_0}G$.
In d), as in [18, 20a] an auxiliary Brownian motion is introduced, which will simplify some computations.
In e), the full strength of the Heisenberg algebra property of $\mathcal{F}$ is used to explicitly integrate a differential equation over the paths of Brownian motion.
This plays a key role in eliminating the singularity $\frac{1}{t}$ in (3.27).
In f), we compute the limit of $\mathrm{Tr}_s[P_1^{L,t,y}(x_0,x_0)]$ in terms of a flat Brownian bridge.
In g), using a formula of P.
Lévy, we obtain the Atiyah-Singer theorem for families.

We suggest the reader to first consider the case where $\xi$ is the trivial line bundle, for which there is no need to introduce an auxiliary Brownian motion.
A brief look at [18, Sect. 3] might also be helpful.

As far as the stochastic calculus is concerned, the reader should keep in mind that Stratonovitch differentials which are denoted $d$ (like $dw_{s}, dx_{s}^{t}$) behave exactly like their deterministic counterpart.
Some more care has to be given to Itô differentials which are noted $\delta$ (like $\delta \gamma_{s}$).
For these questions, we refer to [17].
Also the reader will have to distinguish standard differentials (like $dw_{s}, dx_{s}^{t}$) from the Grassmann variables $dy^{s}$.

The proof given here includes what is done in [18] for the $Z$ valued Index Theorem, and also in [20a] for the infinitesimal Lefschetz formulas.
On the contrary, the proof of Sect.
5 uses the $Z$ valued Index Theorem, and identifies the integrand in Atiyah-Singer's formula in an indirect way.

### a) Probabilistic preliminaries

We now fix $y \in B$.
Everything which we will now construct will be done on the fixed fiber $G_y$, endowed with its given metric $g_{G_y}$.

We will now essentially use the notations of our papers [18, 20a].

Take $x_0 \in G_y$, $u_0' \in O_{x_0'}'$.
Set

$$
u_0 = \sigma u_0'.
$$

Let $W = \mathcal{C}(R^{+}; R^{n})$ be the probability space of the continuous functions $w_{t} = (w_{t}^{1}, \ldots, w_{t}^{n})$ defined on $R^{+}$ with values in $R^{n}$.
$P$ denotes the Wiener measure on $W$, with $P(w_{0} = 0) = 1$, and $E$ is the corresponding expectation operator.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

$Y_{1}^{\prime},\ldots ,Y_{n}^{\prime}$ denote the standard horizontal vector fields on $N^{\prime}$ (for the precise definition see [18, Sect. 2]).

For $t &gt; 0$, consider the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d u ^ {\prime t} = \sum_ {1} ^ {n} Y _ {i} ^ {\prime} \left(u ^ {\prime t}\right) \sqrt {t} d w ^ {i} \tag {4.1} \\
u ^ {\prime t} (0) = u _ {0} ^ {\prime}.
\end{array}
$$

where in (4.1), $dw$ is the Stratonovitch differential of $w$.

Set

$$
u _ {s} ^ {t} = \sigma u _ {s} ^ {\prime t}, \quad x _ {s} ^ {t} = \varrho u _ {s} ^ {t}. \tag {4.2}
$$

$x_{s / t}^{t}$ is exactly the usual Brownian motion in $G_{y}$ starting at $x_0$.

$u_{s}^{\prime t}$ (resp.
$u_{s}^{t}$) is the horizontal lift of $x_{s}^{t}$ in $O^{\prime}$ (resp.
$O$) for the connection $V$.

$\tau_{s}^{0,t}$ denotes the parallel transportation operator for the connection $V$ from vector fibers over $x_0$ to vector fibers over $x_s^t$.
Set

$$
\tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} = \left[ \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} \right] ^ {- 1}. \tag {4.3}
$$

If $O_s'$ (resp.
$O_x$) is considered as a set of unitary operators (resp.
orthogonal operators) from $S$ (resp.
$R^n$) into $F_x$ (resp.
$T_xG$), then on $F_{x_0}$ (resp.
on $T_{x_0}M$) we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} = u _ {s} ^ {\prime t} \left[ u _ {0} ^ {\prime} \right] ^ {- 1} \tag {4.4} \\
\left(\text {resp.} \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} = u _ {s} ^ {t} \left[ u _ {0} \right] ^ {- 1}\right).
\end{array}
$$

$e_1, \ldots, e_n$ is a fixed oriented orthonormal base of $T_{x_0}G$.

$\tau_0^{s,t}(S(dx_s^t))$ denotes the linear mapping of $T_{x_0}M$ into itself $\tau_0^{s,t}S(dx_s^t)\tau_s^{0,t}$.
Note that $\tau_s^{0,t}$ is the identity on $T^H M$ identified with $T_yB$.

Also if $X, Y \in T_{x_s}M$, $\tau_0^{s,t}L_{x_s}(X,Y)$ is the linear mapping from $\xi_{x_0}$ into itself $\tau_0^{s,t}L_{x_s}(X,Y)\tau_s^{0,t}$.

We first give a probabilistic construction of the semi-group $e^{-\frac{\pi I^{L,t}}{2}}$.

Definition 4.1. $U_{s}^{t}$ denotes the process of linear mappings from $(F_{\pm}\otimes \xi)_{x_0}$ into $(F_{\pm}\otimes \xi)_{x_0}\otimes \varLambda (B)$ defined by the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d U _ {s} ^ {t} = U _ {s} ^ {t} \left[ \left(\frac {1}{2 t} \langle \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} (S (d x _ {s} ^ {t})) e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha} \rangle \sqrt {t} e _ {i} d y ^ {\alpha} + \frac {1}{4 t} \langle S (d x _ {s} ^ {t}) f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta} \rangle d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta}\right) \otimes 1 \right. \\
\quad - \left(\frac {t}{4} e _ {i} e _ {j} \otimes \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} L _ {x _ {s} ^ {t}} \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {i}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {j}\right) + \frac {1}{4} d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta} \otimes \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} L _ {x _ {s} ^ {t}} \left(f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta}\right) \right. \\
\left. - \frac {\sqrt {t}}{2} e _ {i} d y ^ {\alpha} \otimes \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} L _ {x _ {s} ^ {t}} \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha}\right)\right) d s \Bigg ] \tag {4.5}
\end{array}
$$

$$
U _ {0} ^ {t} = I.
$$

In (4.5), $I$ is the identity mapping on $(F_{\pm}\otimes \xi)_{x_0}$.

Of course $dx_{s}^{t}$ is here the Stratonovitch differential of $x_{s}^{t}$.

Standard results on stochastic differential equations show that for any $p \geq 1$

$$
E \left[ \sup  _ {0 \leq s \leq 1} | U _ {s} ^ {t} | ^ {p} \right]
$$

J.
M.
Bismut

is uniformly bounded.

We now have the following extension of [18, Theorem 2.5] and [20a, Theorem 2.2].

Theorem 4.2. For any $C^\infty$ section $h$ of $F_{\pm} \otimes \xi$

$$
\left(e ^ {- \frac {I ^ {L , t}}{2}} h\right) \left(x _ {0}\right) = E \left[ \exp \left\{- \frac {t \int_ {0} ^ {1} K \left(x _ {s} ^ {t}\right)}{8} d s \right\} U _ {1} ^ {t} \tau_ {0} ^ {1, t} h \left(x _ {1} ^ {t}\right) \right]. \tag {4.6}
$$

Proof.
The proof uses the special form of $I^{L,t}$ and an elementary stochastic calculus very similar to [18, Theorem 2.5].
It is left to the reader.
$\square$

We now disintegrate (4.6) with respect to $x_1^t$.

Namely let $p_t^\chi(x, x')$ denotes the heat equation kernel associated with $e^{\frac{t \Delta}{2}}$ (where $\Delta$ is the Laplace-Beltrami operator of $G_y$).

For $x' \in G_y$, $E_{x_0, x'}^t$ denotes the expectation operator for $x_s^t$ conditional on $x_1^t = x'$.
For the precise definition of $E_{x_0, x'}^t$ (which is well-defined for every $x' \in G_y$), see [16, Sect. 2].

Corollary.
For any $x' \in G_y$, $t &gt; 0$, the following identity holds

$$
P _ {1} ^ {L, t, y} \left(x _ {0}, x ^ {\prime}\right) = p _ {t} ^ {y} \left(x _ {0}, x ^ {\prime}\right) E _ {x _ {0}, x ^ {\prime}} ^ {t} \left[ \exp \left\{- t \int_ {0} ^ {1} \frac {K \left(x _ {s} ^ {t}\right)}{8} d s \right\} U _ {1} ^ {t} \tau_ {0} ^ {1, t} \right]. \tag {4.7}
$$

Proof.
Equality holds in (4.7) for a.e.
$x'$ by disintegrating (4.6) with respect to $x_1^t$ (whose law is exactly $p_t^\chi(x_0, x') dx'$).
Equality everywhere follows from the continuity of both sides of (4.7) in $x'$ (the continuity of the r.h.s.
in $x'$ can be proved as in [16, Theorem 2.14]).
$\square$

b) An a priori estimate

Note that equation (4.5) contains terms which become singular as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$.
This was also the case in [20a], but in [20a], we could control the singularity using the fact that it appeared in a term of the form $\exp \left(\frac{iB^t}{t}\right)$ (with $B^t$ real).

Here, this is not the case, so we must be more careful when studying the asymptotics of (4.7) as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$.

Let $P_{x_0, x_0}^t$ be the law of $x_s^t$ conditional on $x_1^t = x_0$ (i.e. $P_{x_0, x_0}^t$ corresponds to $E_{x_0, x_0}^t$).

Let $A^t$ be a measurable set in $W$, and assume that as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, for any $k \in N$

$$
P _ {x _ {0}, x _ {0}} ^ {t} \left(A ^ {t}\right) = o \left(t ^ {k}\right) \tag {4.8}
$$

where $o(t^k)$ is uniform in $x_0 \in M$.

We now have the following estimate.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

**Proposition 4.3.** As $t \downarrow 0$, for any $k \in N$

$$
p _ {t} ^ {y} \left(x _ {0}, x _ {0}\right) E _ {x _ {0}, x _ {0}} ^ {t} \left[ \exp \left\{- t \int_ {0} ^ {1} \frac {K \left(x _ {s} ^ {t}\right)}{8} d s \right\} \left| U _ {1} ^ {t} \tau_ {0} ^ {1, t} \right| 1 _ {A ^ {t}} \right] = o \left(t ^ {k}\right) \tag {4.9}
$$

and $o(t^k)$ is uniform in $x_0 \in M$.

**Proof.** We will limit ourselves to proving (4.9) when $\xi$ is the trivial bundle, so that $L = 0$.
The extension of the proof to the general case is easy and left to the reader.

Since $dy^{\alpha}$ or $dy^{\alpha}dy^{\beta}$ appear in the matrix expression which drives the equation of $U_{!}^{t}$, $U_{!}^{t}$ can be solved by a series which stops when the $m$ indices $dy^{1},\ldots ,dy^{m}$ have been saturated.
$U_{1}^{t}$ can then be written as

$$
\begin{array}{l} U _ {1} ^ {t} = I + \frac {1}{2 t} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \langle S (u _ {s} ^ {t} \sqrt {t} d w _ {s}), \dots \rangle e _ {i} d y ^ {a} + \dots + \frac {1}{4 t ^ {2}} \\ \cdot \int_ {0 \leq s \leq s ^ {\prime} \leq 1} \left\langle S \left(u _ {s} ^ {t} \sqrt {t} d w _ {s}\right), \dots \right\rangle \left\langle S \left(u _ {s ^ {\prime}} ^ {t} \sqrt {t} d w _ {s ^ {\prime}}\right), \dots \right\rangle e _ {i} d y ^ {a} e _ {j} d y ^ {\beta} + \dots \tag {4.10} \\ \end{array}
$$

i.e. $U_{s}^{t}$ is a finite sum of iterated stochastic integrals with respect to $\sqrt{t} dw$.

Let $V$ be any of the iterated stochastic integrals of the type

$$
\int_ {0 \leq s _ {1} \leq \dots \leq s _ {k} \leq 1} \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {1}} \rangle \dots \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {2}} \rangle \dots \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {k}} \rangle
$$

appearing in (4.10).

Using Cauchy-Schwarz's inequality we have

$$
E _ {x _ {0}, x _ {0}} ^ {t} [ | V | 1 _ {A ^ {t}} ] \leq \left[ E _ {x _ {0}, x _ {0}} ^ {t} | V | ^ {2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \left[ P _ {x _ {0}, x _ {0}} ^ {t} \left(A ^ {t}\right) \right] ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {4.11}
$$

Also a result of Molchanov (see Elie [11, Theorem 1.1]) shows that

$$
p _ {t} ^ {y} \left(x _ {0}, x _ {0}\right) \leq \frac {C}{t ^ {n / 2}} \tag {4.12}
$$

where $C$ is uniform on $M$.
Note that the uniformity on $M$ follows from the smoothness of the metric of $M$ restricted to the various $G_{y}$ (which are diffeomorphic to the compact manifold $X$).

We now estimate $E_{x_0,x_0}^t |V|^2$.
Clearly $V$ can be expressed as

$$
V = \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {k} \int_ {0 \leq s _ {1} \dots \leq s _ {j} \leq \frac {1}{2} \leq s _ {j + 1} \dots} \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {1}} \rangle \dots \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {k}} \rangle . \tag {4.13}
$$

Each of the terms appearing in the sum (4.13) is the product of two terms $H_{1}$, $H_{2}$ where

$$
\begin{array}{l} H _ {1} = \int_ {0 \leq s _ {1} \dots \leq s _ {j} \leq 1 / 2} \dots \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {1}} \rangle \dots \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {j}} \rangle \\ H _ {2} = \int_ {\frac {1}{2} \leq s _ {j + 1} \leq \dots \leq s _ {k} \leq 1} \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {j + 1}} \rangle \dots \sqrt {t} d w _ {s _ {k}} \rangle . \tag {4.14} \\ \end{array}
$$

So to estimate $E_{x_0,x_0}^t |V|^2$, using Cauchy-Schwarz's inequality, we only need to estimate $E_{x_0,x_0}^t |H_1|^4$ and $E_{x_0,x_0}^t |H_2|^4$.
Using the invariance of $P_{x_0,x_0}^t$ under the time change $s\to 1 - s$, we can limit ourselves to estimate $E_{x_0,x_0}^t |H_1|^4$.

132
J.
M.
Bismut

By [16, Definition 2.4], since $H_{1}$ only depends on $w_{s}$ for $0 \leq s \leq \frac{1}{2}$, we have

$$
E_{x_0, x_0}^t |H_1|^4 = \frac{1}{p_t(x_0, x_0)} E\left[|H_1|^4 p_{t/2}(x_{1/2}^t, x_0)\right]. \tag{4.15}
$$

Using Azencott’s estimate [11, VIII, Proposition 4.4], we know that for $N$ large enough (independent of $y$), for any $x' \in G_y$

$$
p_{t/2}^y(x', x_0) \leq \frac{C}{t^N}. \tag{4.16}
$$

Finally since $H_1$ is an iterated Stratonovitch integral, by expressing it by means of iterated Itô integrals whose coefficients are bounded (they only depend on $S$ and its derivatives), we find easily that $E|H_1|^4$ can be bounded uniformly.
In estimating (4.9), $p_t(x_0, x_0)$ finally appears with exponent $1/2$, and so we may bound $[p_t(x_0, x_0)]^{1/2}$ using (4.12).

So using (4.8) and (4.10)-(4.16) we immediately see that (4.9) holds.
$\square$

**Remark 1.** The key fact in the proof is that finitely many integrals appear in (4.10) and that their singularities are killed by $A^t$.

**c) An asymptotic expression for $P_1^{L, t, y}(x_0, x_0)$**

We now use the notations of [18, Sects. 2 and 3.b], and of [20a, Sect. 2c], which are taken from our initial work [16].

In particular

- $P_1$ is the probability law on $W$ of the Brownian bridge $w^1$ in $R^n$, with $w_0^1 = w_1^1 = 0$.
- $H_2$ is the set of $R^n$ valued constant functions on $[0, 1]$.
- equation (4.1) is now solved by setting

$$
\sqrt[t]{t} dw = \sqrt[t]{t} dw^1 + v^2 ds.
$$

For $t$ small enough (depending on $w^1$), $v^2 \in H_2$ is determined by the fact that $x_1^t = x_0$.
Of course $v^2$ depends explicitly on $(\sqrt[t]{t} dw^1, x_0)$.

- $H(\sqrt[t]{t} dw^1, x_0)$ is a bounded random variable defined in [18, (3.8)] such that as $t \downarrow 0$

$$
H(\sqrt[t]{t} dw^1, x_0) \to 1 \quad P_1 \text{ a.s.}
$$

Also if $H(\sqrt[t]{t} dw^1, x_0) \neq 0$, $v^2$ is well defined.

Of course equation (4.5) for $U_1^t$ is now solved replacing $\sqrt[t]{t} dw$ by $\sqrt[t]{t} dw^1 + v^2 ds$.

We now have

**Theorem 4.4.** As $t \downarrow 0$, for any $k \in \mathbb{N}$

$$
P_1^{L, y, t}(x_0, x_0) = \frac{1}{(\sqrt[4]{2\pi t})^n} \int_W U_1^t \tau_0^{1, t} H(\sqrt[t]{t} dw^1, x_0) dP_1(w^1) + o(t^k) \tag{4.17}
$$

and $o(t^k)$ is uniform for $x_0 \in M$.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Proof.
Note that by [16, Theorem 4.11 and (4.69)], using the notations of [16], as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$

$$
P_{x_0, x_0}^t \left[ G(\sqrt{t} \, dw^1) \, g(|q(\sqrt{t} \, dw)|) \div 1 \right] = o(t^k)
$$

where $o(t^k)$ is uniform in $x_0 \in M$.

Using now the Corollary of Theorem 4.16 in [16] and Proposition 4.3, the Theorem follows.
$\square$

## d) A probabilistic representation of $U_1^t$

To simplify the computations on the asymptotics of (4.17), we now introduce an extra Brownian motion, the way we did in [18, Sect. 3] and [20a].

Namely $(\gamma_{i,s}^j)_{i &lt; j}$ and $(\gamma_i^\alpha)$ denote a family of independent Brownian motions in $R$ starting at 0, which we also assume to be independent of $w^1$.
They are of course independent of $x_s^t$ etc. ...
Also, set $\gamma_{\alpha,s}^\beta = 0$.
$\gamma_s$ denotes the $(n + m, n + m)$ antisymmetric matrix whose coefficients are the $\gamma_{i,s}^j, \gamma_{i,s}^\alpha, \gamma_{\alpha,s}^\beta$.
$P'$ denotes the probability law of $\gamma$ on the probability space $W' = \mathcal{C}(R^+; R^{(n + m)^2})$ and $E^{P'}$ is the corresponding expectation operator.

**Definition 4.5.** $V_*^{1,t}, V_*^{2,t}$ denote the solutions of the stochastic differential equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
d V_s^{1,t} &amp;= V_s^{1,t} \left[ \frac{1}{2t} \left\langle \tau_s^{0,t}(S(dx_s^t)) e_i, f_\alpha \right\rangle \sqrt{t} \, e_i \, dy^\alpha - \frac{t}{2} \sum_{i &lt; j} e_i e_j \, \delta \gamma_i^j - \frac{\sqrt{t}}{2} e_i \, dy^\alpha \, \delta \gamma_i^\alpha \right] \\
V_0^{1,t} &amp;= I_{F_{x_0}} \\
d V_s^{2,t} &amp;= V_s^{2,t} \left[ \sum_{i &lt; j} \tau_0^{s,t} L(\tau_s^{0,t} e_i, \tau_s^{0,t} e_j) \, \delta \gamma_i^j - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{\alpha &lt; \beta} dy^\alpha \, dy^\beta \tau_0^{s,t} L(f_\alpha, f_\beta) \, ds \right. \\
&amp;\quad \left. + \tau_0^{s,t} L(\tau_s^{0,t} e_i, f_\alpha) \, \delta \gamma_i^\alpha \right] \\
V_0^{2,t} &amp;= I_{\xi_{x_0}}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{4.18}
$$

Here $V_s^{1,t}$ takes its values in $c(TG) \widehat{\otimes} \Lambda(B)$ and $V_s^{2,t}$ in $\operatorname{End}(\xi_{x_0}) \otimes \Lambda(B)$.
Note that in what follows, $V_s^{1,t} \otimes V_s^{2,t}$ denotes the tensor product of $V_s^{1,t}$ and $V_s^{2,t}$, where the corresponding elements in $\Lambda(B)$ are multiplied using the standard multiplication rules in $\Lambda(B)$.

We now claim.

**Proposition 4.6.** The following identity holds

$$
U_s^t = E^{P'} \left[ \exp \left\{ \frac{1}{4t} \int_0^s \left\langle S(dx_h^t) f_\alpha, f_\beta \right\rangle \, dy^\alpha \, dy^\beta \right\} V_s^{1,t} \otimes V_s^{2,t} \right]. \tag{4.19}
$$

**Proof.** By Theorem 1.3, $dy^\alpha \, dy^\beta$ commutes with the other expressions in $\mathcal{E}$.
(4.19) readily follows from Itô's formula for the process

$$
