\exp \left\{ \frac{1}{4t} \int_0^s \left\langle S(dx_h^t) f_\alpha, f_\beta \right\rangle \, dy^\alpha \, dy^\beta \right\} V_s^{1,t} \otimes V_s^{2,t}
$$

as used in [18, Proposition 3.7].
$\square$

134
J.
M.
Bismut

e) Integration of the equation of $V_{s}^{1,t}$

For technical reasons, we replace the Itô differentials in $V_{s}^{1,t}$ by Stratonovitch differentials.

Definition 4.7. $V_{s}^{\prime 1,t}$ is the solution of the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d V _ {s} ^ {\prime 1, t} = V _ {s} ^ {\prime 1, t} \left[ \frac {1}{2 t} \left\langle \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} \left(S \left(d x _ {s} ^ {t}\right)\right) e _ {i}, f _ {a} \right\rangle \sqrt {t} e _ {i} d y ^ {a} - \frac {t}{2} \sum_ {i &lt; j} e _ {i} e _ {j} d \gamma_ {i} ^ {j} - \frac {\sqrt {t}}{2} e _ {i} d y ^ {a} d \gamma_ {i} ^ {a} \right] \\
V _ {0} ^ {\prime 1, t} = I _ {F _ {\infty_ {0}}}. \tag {4.20}
\end{array}
$$

Proposition 4.8. The following relation holds

$$
V _ {s} ^ {1, t} = \exp \frac {n (n - 1) s t ^ {2}}{1 6} V _ {s} ^ {\prime 1, t}. \tag {4.21}
$$

Proof.
First note that

$$
(e _ {i} d y ^ {a}) ^ {2} = 0.
$$

If we express equation (4.18) as a Stratonovitch equation, the only correction will then be on $e_i e_j \delta \gamma_i^j$.
The proof of (4.21) is then identical to the proof of [18, (3.34)].
$\square$

We now explicitly integrate equation (4.20).
In what follows, $\operatorname{Spin}(n)$ is considered as acting on $F_{x_0}$, and $SO(n)$ acts on $TG$, by using the identification of $T_{x_0}G_y$ with $R^n$ by the base $e_1, \ldots, e_n$.
Also $\gamma$ is considered as acting on $TM$ by using the base $e_1 \ldots e_n$, $f_1 \ldots f_m$.

Definition 4.9. $A_{s}^{t}$ is the process valued in $\operatorname{Spin}(n)$ defined by the equation

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d A _ {s} ^ {t} = A _ {s} ^ {t} \left(- \frac {t}{2} \sum_ {i &lt; j} e _ {i} e _ {j} d \gamma_ {i} ^ {j}\right) \tag {4.22} \\
A _ {0} ^ {t} = I.
\end{array}
$$

$B_{s}^{t}$ is the process valued in $SO(n)$

$$
B _ {s} ^ {t} = \sigma \left(A _ {s} ^ {t}\right). \tag {4.23}
$$

Note that $A_{s}^{t}$ is a scaled Brownian motion in $\operatorname{Spin}(n)$.
In what follows the exponential exp is taken in the algebra $\mathcal{E}$.

We now have the key result.

Theorem 4.10. $V_{s}^{\prime 1,t}$ is exactly given by

$$
\begin{array}{l}
V _ {s} ^ {\prime 1, t} = \exp \left\{\int_ {0} ^ {s} \left\langle \left(\frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {h , t} (S (d x _ {h} ^ {t}))}{2 t} - \frac {d \gamma_ {h}}{2}\right) \left(B _ {h} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} e _ {i}, f _ {a} \right\rangle \sqrt {t} e _ {i} d y ^ {a} \right. \\
+ \frac {1}{4} \int_ {0 \leq h \leq h ^ {\prime} \leq s} \left\langle P _ {G} \left[ \frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {h , t} (S (d x _ {h} ^ {t}))}{\sqrt {t}} - \sqrt {t} d \gamma_ {h} \right] f _ {a}, \right. \\
\left. \cdot P _ {G} \left[ \frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {h ^ {\prime} , t} \left(S \left(d x _ {h ^ {\prime}} ^ {t}\right)\right)}{\sqrt {t}} - \sqrt {t} d \gamma_ {h ^ {\prime}} \right] f _ {\beta} \right\rangle d y ^ {a} d y ^ {\beta} \right\} A _ {s} ^ {t}. \tag {4.24}
\end{array}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Proof.
Set

$$
V _ {s} ^ {\prime 1, t} = D _ {s} A _ {s} ^ {t}. \tag {4.25}
$$

Clearly $D_{s}$ is the solution of the equation

$$
\begin{array}{l} d D _ {s} = D _ {s} A _ {s} ^ {t} \left\langle \left(\tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} \frac {\left(S \left(d x _ {s} ^ {t}\right)\right)}{2 t} - \frac {d \gamma_ {s}}{2}\right) e _ {i}, f _ {s} \right\rangle \sqrt {t} e _ {i} d y ^ {s} \left(A _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} \tag {4.26} \\ D _ {0} = I. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now $A_{s}^{t}$ is an even element of $c(T_{x_0}G)$, so that $A_{s}^{t}$ commutes with $dy^{s}$ in $\mathcal{E}$.
We then see that

$$
A _ {s} ^ {t} e _ {i} d y ^ {s} \left(A _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} = A _ {s} ^ {t} e _ {i} \left(A _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} d y ^ {s}. \tag {4.27}
$$

The definition of the Spin representation shows that in $c(TG)$

$$
A _ {s} ^ {t} e _ {i} \left(A _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} = B _ {s} ^ {t} e _ {i}. \tag {4.28}
$$

Since $B_s^t \in SO(n)$, (4.26) also writes

$$
\begin{array}{l} d D _ {s} = D _ {s} \left\langle \left(\frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {s , t} \left(S \left(d x _ {s} ^ {t}\right)\right)}{2 t} - \frac {d \gamma_ {s}}{2}\right) \left(B _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} e _ {i}, f _ {s} \right\rangle \sqrt {t} e _ {i} d y ^ {s} \tag {4.29} \\ D _ {0} = I. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now (4.29) can be written as

$$
\begin{array}{l} d D _ {s} = D _ {s} d F _ {s} \tag {4.30} \\ D _ {0} = I \\ \end{array}
$$

where $F_{s}$ is a process taking its value in the Heisenberg algebra $\mathcal{F}_{x_0}$ defined in Theorem 1.3, and $dF_{s}$ is the Stratonovitch differential of $F$.

An elementary result on Heisenberg algebras shows that the solution of (4.30) is exactly

$$
D _ {s} = \exp \left\{F _ {s} + \frac {1}{2} \int_ {0} ^ {s} \left[ F _ {h ^ {\prime}}, d F _ {h ^ {\prime}} \right] \right\}. \tag {4.31}
$$

Also note that using the commutation relations (1.11), and the antisymmetry of $S, \gamma$, we have for $h \leq h'$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left[ d F _ {h}, d F _ {h ^ {\prime}} \right] = \frac {1}{2} \left\langle \left(B _ {h} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} e _ {i}, \left(\frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {h , t} \left(S \left(d x _ {h} ^ {t}\right)\right)}{t} - d \gamma_ {h}\right) f _ {s} \right\rangle \\ \cdot \left\langle \left(B _ {h ^ {\prime}} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} e _ {i}, \left(\frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {h ^ {\prime} , t} \left(S \left(d x _ {h ^ {\prime}} ^ {t}\right)\right)}{t} - d \gamma_ {h ^ {\prime}}\right) f _ {\beta} \right\rangle t d y ^ {s} d y ^ {\beta}. \tag {4.32} \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $B_s^t \in SO(n)$, $B_s^t e_1, \ldots, B_s^t e_n$ is an orthonormal base of $T_{x_0}G_y$.
Using (4.31), (4.32) we find that (4.24) holds.
$\square$

136
J.
M.
Bismut

Remark 2. Formula (4.24) will play an essential role in the sequel.
Note again that the exponential in (4.24) is a _finite_ power series.

f) The asymptotics of the supertrace

Let us first recall a useful fact on Clifford algebras from Atiyah and Bott [5].
First note that $\tau$ has been defined in (1.13).

Proceeding as in Atiyah and Bott [5, p. 484], we then find if $\alpha \in c(R^n)$, if $|\alpha|$ is the length of $\alpha$ in the graded algebra $c(R^n)$, if $|\alpha| &lt; n$

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_s \alpha = 0. \tag{4.33}
$$

Also since $e_1 \ldots e_n = (-i)^t \tau$, since $S$ has dimension $2^l$,

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_s \left[ e_1 \ldots e_n \right] = (-2i)^l \tag{4.34}
$$

(4.33), (4.34) entirely determines $\operatorname{Tr}_s$ on $c(R^n)$.

In what follows, $dx^1, \ldots, dx^n$ denotes the base of $T_{x_0}^* G_y$ dual to $e_1, \ldots, e_n$.
$dx^1, \ldots, dx^n, dy^1, \ldots, dy^m$ will satisfy the usual anticommutation rules as elements of the exterior algebra $\Lambda(M)$.

Since $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ is an oriented base of $T_{x_0} G_y$, $dx^1 \ldots dx^n$ is the Riemannian orientation form of $T_{x_0} G_y$.

Also since when changing the orientation of $G_y$, $\operatorname{Tr}_s P_1^{L,t,y}(x,x)$ is changed into $-\operatorname{Tr}_s P_1^{L,t,y}(x,x)$, it is clear that if $\mathcal{L}(t,x)$ is defined by

$$
\mathcal{L}(t,x) = \operatorname{Tr}_s \left[ P_1^{L,t,y}(x,x) \right] dx^1 \ldots dx^n. \tag{4.35}
$$

$\mathcal{L}(t,x)$ is a standard differential form in $\Lambda(M)$.
Also recall that integration along the oriented fiber $G_y$ of any differential form over $M$ is well-defined.
In particular

$$
\int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s \left[ P_1^{L,t,y}(x,x) \right] dx = \int_{G_y} \mathcal{L}(t,x). \tag{4.36}
$$

Any differential form $\omega$ on $\Lambda(M)$ can be expressed in terms of the $dx^{i_1} \ldots dx^{i_p} dy^{j_1} \ldots dy^{j_q}$.
In particular, in the decomposition of $\omega$ according to this base of $\Lambda(M)$, we can select the term of maximal degree $n$ in the $x$ variables, i.e. the term where $dx^1 \ldots dx^n$ appears.
So we will write

$$
\omega = \eta dx^1 \ldots dx^n + \ldots \tag{4.37}
$$

where $\ldots$ is of degree $\leq n - 1$ in the $x$ variables, and $\eta$ only contains terms like $dy^{j_1} \ldots dy^{j_q}$.

If $\omega' \in \Lambda(M)$ is such that

$$
\omega' = \eta' dx^1 \ldots dx^n + \ldots \tag{4.38}
$$

we will write $\omega \triangleq \omega'$ if $\eta = \eta'$.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Using Theorem 4.4, Propositions 4.6, 4.8, and Theorem 4.10 we find that for any $k \in N$, as $t \downarrow 0$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Tr}_{s}\left[P_{1}^{L,t,y}(x_{0},x_{0})\right] &amp;= \int_{W\times W^{\prime}}\frac{\exp\frac{(n(n-1)t^{2})}{16}}{(\sqrt{2\pi t})^{n}}\exp\left\{\frac{1}{4t}\int_{0}^{t}\langle S(dx_{s}^{t})f_{\alpha},f_{\beta}\rangle\,dy^{\alpha}\,dy^{\beta}\right\} \\
&amp;\cdot\operatorname{Tr}_{s}\left[\exp\left\{\int_{0}^{1}\left\langle\left(\frac{\tau_{0}^{s,t}(S(dx_{s}^{t}))}{2t}-\frac{d\gamma_{s}}{2}\right)(B_{s}^{t})^{-1}e_{i},f_{\alpha}\right\rangle\sqrt{t}\,e_{i}\,dy^{\alpha}\right.\right. \\
&amp;\left.+\frac{1}{4}\int_{0\leq h\leq h^{\prime}\leq 1}\left\langle P_{G}\left[\frac{\tau_{0}^{h,t}(S(dx_{h}^{t}))}{\sqrt{t}}-\sqrt{t}\,d\gamma_{h}\right]f_{\alpha},\,P_{G}\left[\frac{\tau_{0}^{h^{\prime},t}(S(dx_{h^{\prime}}^{t}))}{\sqrt{t}}-\sqrt{t}\,d\gamma_{h^{\prime}}\right]f_{\beta}\right\rangle\right. \\
&amp;\left.\cdot\,dy^{\alpha}\,dy^{\beta}\right\} A_{1}^{t}\tau_{0}^{1,t}\left]\operatorname{Tr}\left[V_{1}^{2,t}\tau_{0}^{1,t}\right]\,H(\sqrt{t}\,dw^{1},x_{0})\,dP_{1}(w^{1})\,dP^{\prime}(\gamma)+o(t^{k}) \tag{4.39}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\operatorname{Tr}[V_1^{2,t}\tau_0^{1,t}]$ is the trace of $V_{1}^{2,t}\tau_{0}^{1,t}$ (considered as an element of $\operatorname{End}\xi_{x_0}\otimes \Lambda_y(B)$) and so is an element of $\Lambda_y(B)$.

**Definition 4.11.** $V_{s}^{2}$ denotes the solution of the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
dV_{s}^{2} &amp;= V_{s}^{2}\left[\sum_{i&lt;j}L_{x_{0}}(e_{i},e_{j})\delta\gamma_{i}^{j}-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{\alpha&lt;\beta}L_{x_{0}}(f_{\alpha},f_{\beta})\,dy^{\alpha}\,dy^{\beta}\,ds+L_{x_{0}}(e_{i},f_{\alpha})\delta\gamma_{i}^{\alpha}\right] \\
V_{0}^{2} &amp;= I_{\xi_{x_{0}}}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{4.40}
$$

$w_{s}^{\prime 1}$ is the Brownian bridge in $T_{x_0}G$ given by

$$
w_{s}^{\prime 1} = u_{0}w_{s}^{1}. \tag{4.41}
$$

Using the expression (4.39), we now prove the following key result.

**Theorem 4.12.** As $t \downarrow 0$, the differential form on $M\mathcal{L}(t,x_0)$ has a limit $\mathcal{L}(x_0)$, which is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(x_{0}) &amp;\stackrel{n}{=}(-i)^{l}\int_{W\times W^{\prime}}\exp\left\{-\frac{1}{8\pi}\int_{0}^{1}\langle R_{x_{0}}(dw_{s}^{\prime 1},w_{s}^{\prime 1})e_{i},e_{j}\rangle\,dx^{i}\,dx^{j}\right. \\
&amp;\quad+\frac{1}{4}\int_{0}^{1}\langle V_{w_{s}^{\prime}}S_{x_{0}}(dw_{s}^{\prime 1})f_{\alpha},f_{\beta}\rangle\,dy^{\alpha}\,dy^{\beta} \\
&amp;\quad+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{0}^{1}\left\langle\left(\frac{V_{w_{s}^{\prime}}S_{x_{0}}(dw_{s}^{\prime 1})}{2}-\frac{d\gamma_{s}}{2}\right)e_{i},f_{\alpha}\right\rangle\,dx^{i}\,dy^{\alpha} \\
&amp;\quad+\frac{1}{4}\int_{0}^{1}\langle P_{G}\left[S_{x_{0}}(w_{s}^{\prime 1})f_{\alpha}\right],P_{G}\left[S_{x_{0}}(dw_{s}^{\prime 1})f_{\beta}\right]\rangle\,dy^{\alpha}\,dy^{\beta} \\
&amp;\quad-\frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{i&lt;j}\gamma_{i,1}^{j}\,dx^{i}\,dx^{j}\Big{\}}\operatorname{Tr}\left[V_{1}^{2}\right]\,dP_{1}(w^{1})\,dP^{\prime}(\gamma). \tag{4.42}
\end{aligned}
$$

138
J.
M.
Bismut

Proof.
The same argument as in the proof of [20a, Theorem 2.9] shows that as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, since $w_0^1 = w_1^1 = 0$

$$
\frac {1}{t} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle S \left(d x _ {s} ^ {t}\right) f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta} \right\rangle\rightarrow \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle V _ {w _ {s} ^ {\prime}} S \left(d w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}\right) f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta} \right\rangle \tag {4.43}
$$

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle \frac {\tau_ {0} ^ {n , t} (S (d x _ {s} ^ {t}))}{t} e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha} \right\rangle\rightarrow \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle V _ {w _ {s} ^ {\prime}} S (d w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}) e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha} \right\rangle .
$$

Also observe that since

$$
d x _ {h} ^ {t} = u _ {h} ^ {t} (\sqrt {t} d w ^ {1} + v ^ {2} d s) \tag {4.44}
$$

the second term in the exponential in the r.h.s.
of (4.39) is

$$
\frac {1}{4} \int_ {0 \lesssim h \lesssim h ^ {\prime} \lesssim 1} \left\langle P _ {G} \left[ \tau_ {0} ^ {h, t} \left(S \left(u _ {h} ^ {t} \left(d w _ {h} ^ {1} + \frac {v ^ {2} d s}{\sqrt {t}}\right)\right)\right) - \sqrt {t} d \gamma_ {h} \right] f _ {\alpha}, \right. \tag {4.45}
$$

$$
\left. P _ {G} \left[ \tau_ {0} ^ {h ^ {\prime}, t} \left(S \left(u _ {h ^ {\prime}} ^ {t} \left(d w _ {h ^ {\prime}} ^ {1} + \frac {v ^ {2} d s}{\sqrt {t}}\right)\right)\right) - \sqrt {t} d \gamma_ {h ^ {\prime}} \right] f _ {\beta} \right\rangle d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta}.
$$

Now using [18, (3.24), (3.26)] or [16, (4.177), (4.178)], we know that for $t = 0$,

$$
v ^ {2} = \frac {\partial v ^ {2}}{\partial \sqrt {t}} = 0. \tag {4.46}
$$

It then follows that as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, (4.45) converges to

$$
\frac {1}{4} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle P _ {G} \left[ S _ {x _ {0}} \left(w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}\right) f _ {\alpha} \right], P _ {G} \left[ S _ {x _ {0}} \left(d w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}\right) f _ {\beta} \right] \right\rangle d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta}. \tag {4.47}
$$

Also recall that by (1.4), the set of antisymmetric matrices is identified with the set $\mathcal{A}$ of element of $c(R^n)$ whose length is exactly 2. Now by [18, (3.37)] we know that for $t$ small enough, if $\tau_0^{1,t}$ is calculated in $\operatorname{Spin}(n)$

$$
\tau_ {0} ^ {1, t} = \exp \left\{- \frac {t}{8} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle R _ {x _ {0}} \left(d w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}, w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}\right) e _ {i}, e _ {j} \right\rangle e _ {i} e _ {j} + o (t) \right\} \tag {4.48}
$$

where $o(z)$ is taken in $\mathcal{A}$.

Similarly, it is clear that

$$
A _ {1} ^ {t} = \exp \left[ - \frac {t}{2} \sum_ {i &lt; j} \gamma_ {i, 1} ^ {j} e _ {i} e _ {j} + o (t) \right] \tag {4.49}
$$

and so

$$
\begin{array}{l} A _ {1} ^ {t} \tau_ {0} ^ {1, t} = \exp \left\{- \frac {1}{8} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left\langle R _ {x _ {0}} \left(d w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}, w _ {s} ^ {\prime 1}\right) e _ {i}, e _ {j} \right\rangle (\sqrt {t} e _ {i}) (\sqrt {t} e _ {j}) \right. \\ \left. - \frac {1}{2} \sum_ {i &lt; j} \gamma_ {i, 1} ^ {j} (\sqrt {t} e _ {i}) (\sqrt {t} e _ {j}) + o (t) \right\} \tag {4.50} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $o(t)$ is still calculated in $\mathcal{A}$.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Also it is trivial to see that as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$

$$
V _ {1} ^ {2, t} \tau_ {0} ^ {1, t} \rightarrow V _ {1} ^ {2} \quad \text{in} \quad \operatorname{End} \xi_ {x _ {0}} \otimes \Lambda_ {y} (B).
$$

Note that in the factors where Clifford multiplication operators $e_i$ appear, they all have the weight $\sqrt[t]{t}$.

By formulas (4.33), (4.34), the only monomial in $c(R^n)$ which gives a non-zero contribution to $\mathrm{Tr}_s$ is $e_1 \ldots e_n$.
This monomial carries the minimal weight $t^{n/2}$ only if it has been obtained by products (in $c(R^n)$) in which each $e_j$ appears exactly once.
The weight $t^{n/2}$ exactly compensates $\frac{1}{t^{n/2}}$ in the integrand in the r.h.s.
of (4.39).
Also products of the form $e_1 \ldots e_n$ with weight $t^n$, $t^{3n/2}$ etc. ...
give a 0 contribution since they kill $\frac{1}{t^{n/2}}$.

Then we find that for $t \downarrow 0$, we can replace the factor $\sqrt[t]{t} e_i$ by $dx^i$ inclusively in (4.50), and also (4.43), (4.45) by their limit.

So we have proved that the integrand in (4.39) has a limit, which is the coefficient of $dx^1 \ldots dx^n$ in the integrand of the r.h.s.
of (4.42).

It remains to prove that the integral also converges.
Now recall that the exponentials in (4.39) and (4.42) are in fact finite power series.
Once we have observed the important fact that (4.43), (4.45) have limits, we can use Taylor's formulas on each of these expressions.
Since we deal with quantities which are polynomial functions of terms like (4.13), there is no difficulty to proceed exactly as in [16, Theorems 4.18 and 4.21] to prove that the integral does in fact converge.
$\square$

## g) The Index Theorem of Atiyah-Singer for families

We now go back to the usual definition of the Chern character, i.e. replace formula (2.37) by

$$
c h V = \operatorname{Tr} \exp - \left(\frac {C}{2 i \pi}\right). \tag {4.51}
$$

Let then $\mathcal{L}'(x)$ be the differential form in $\Lambda(M)$ which is obtained from $\mathcal{L}(x)$ by replacing each monomial $dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta}$ by $-\frac{i}{\pi} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta}$.

In what follows, we identify any antisymmetric matrix $A$ on $T_{x_0}M$ with the 2 form

$$
\langle X, Y \rangle \rightarrow \langle X, A Y \rangle
$$

so that $A$ is identified with

$$
- \frac {1}{2} \left\langle A e _ {i}, e _ {j} \right\rangle d x ^ {i} d x ^ {j} - \frac {1}{2} \left\langle A f _ {\alpha}, f _ {\beta} \right\rangle d y ^ {\alpha} d y ^ {\beta} - \left\langle A e _ {i}, f _ {\alpha} \right\rangle d x ^ {i} d y ^ {\alpha}. \tag {4.52}
$$

Also we write $\exp^{\wedge}$ instead of $\exp$ to indicate that the exponential is taken in $\Lambda(M)$.
$\overline{V}$.² denotes the solution of (4.40) where $dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta}$ is replaced by $\frac{-i}{\pi} dy^{\alpha} dy^{\beta}$.

We now give an expression for $\mathcal{L}'(x_0)$.

140
J.
M.
Bismut

Theorem 4.13. $\mathcal{L}'(x_0)$ is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}'(x_0) &amp;\stackrel{\pi}{=} \int_{\overline{W}} \exp^{\wedge} \left\{ \frac{i}{4\pi} \left( \int_{0}^{1} (R(w_s'^1, dw_s'^1) + 2V_{w_s'} S(dw_s'^1)) \right. \right. \\
&amp;\quad \left. - \int_{0}^{1} \left\langle P_G[S_{x_0}(w_s'^1) f_\pi], P_G S_{x_0}(dw_s'^1) f_\beta \right\rangle dy^\alpha dy^\beta \right\} dP_1(w^1) \\
&amp;\quad \left. \wedge \int_{\overline{W'}} \exp^{\wedge} \left\{ - \frac{i\gamma_1}{2\pi} \right\} \operatorname{Tr} \bar{V}_1^2 dP'(\gamma). \tag{4.53}
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof.
Observe that by Theorem 1.9

$$
R(e_i, e_j) f_\pi = 0.
$$

Also $TG$, $T^H M$ are stable under $R$.
It follows that

$$
\int_{0}^{1} R(w_s'^1, dw_s'^1) = - \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \left\langle R(w_s'^1, dw_s'^1) e_i, e_j \right\rangle dx^i dx^j.
$$

By Theorem 1.9, $\langle S(e_i)e_j, e_k \rangle = 0$.
It follows that $V_{w_s'} S(dw_s'^1)$ does not contain terms of the form $dx^i dx^j$.
Using the commutativity of the algebra of even forms in $\Lambda_{x_0}(M)$ and Fubini's theorem, we obtain (4.53).

Recall that $R^G(\ldots)$ is the curvature tensor of the bundle $TG$ in $M$.
$R^G(\ldots)$ is the restriction of $R(\ldots)$ to $TG$.

To calculate (4.53), we first give a remarkable formula.

Theorem 4.14. For any $Y, Z \in T_{x_0}M$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \left\langle Y, \left( R_{x_0}(w_s'^1, dw_s'^1) + 2V_{w_s'^1} S_{x_0}(dw_s'^1) \right) Z \right\rangle \\
\quad - 2 \int_{0}^{1} \left\langle P_G[S_{x_0}(w_s'^1) Y], P_G[S_{x_0}(dw_s'^1) Z] \right\rangle = - \int_{0}^{1} \left\langle R_{x_0}^G(Y, Z) w_s'^1, dw_s'^1 \right\rangle. \tag{4.54}
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof.
By Theorem 1.9, $T(dw_s'^1, w_s'^1) = 0$, so that

$$
\hat{D} S(w_s'^1, dw_s'^1) = V_{w_s'^1} S(dw_s'^1) - V_{dw_s'^1} S(w_s'^1). \tag{4.55}
$$

Since $w_0'^1 = w_1'^1 = 0$, integrating by parts, we find that

$$
2 \int_{0}^{1} V_{w_s'^1} S(dw_s'^1) = \int_{0}^{1} \hat{D} S(w_s'^1, dw_s'^1). \tag{4.56}
$$

Also

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \left\langle P_G[S(w_s'^1) Y], P_G[S(dw_s'^1) Z] \right\rangle &amp;= \int_{0}^{1} \left\langle S(w_s'^1) Y, S(dw_s'^1) Z \right\rangle \\
&amp;\quad - \int_{0}^{1} \left\langle P_H S(w_s'^1) Y, P_H S(dw_s'^1) Z \right\rangle. \tag{4.57}
\end{aligned}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

$T$ takes values in $TG$, and moreover $S(Y)w_{s}^{\prime 1}, S(Z)dw_{s}^{\prime 1} \in T^{H}M$.
Using the fact if $U, V \in TM$

$$
S (U) V - S (V) U + T (U, V) = 0. \tag{4.58}
$$

(4.57) is also equal to

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \langle S \left(w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right) Y, S \left(d w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right) Z \rangle - \int_{0}^{1} \langle S (Y) w_{s}^{\prime 1}, S (Z) d w_{s}^{\prime 1} \rangle . \tag{4.59}
$$

Still integrating by parts and using the antisymmetry of $S$, we find

$$
\begin{aligned}
- 2 \int_{0}^{1} \langle P_{G} [ S (w_{s}^{\prime 1}) Y ], P_{G} [ S (d w_{s}^{\prime 1}) Z ] \rangle \\
= \int_{0}^{1} \langle Y, [ S (w_{s}^{\prime 1}), S (d w_{s}^{\prime 1}) ] Z \rangle + \int_{0}^{1} \langle [ S (Y), S (Z) ] w_{s}^{\prime 1}, d w_{s}^{\prime 1} \rangle . \tag{4.60}
\end{aligned}
$$

The l.h.s.
of (4.54) is then exactly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \langle Y, \left(R \left(w_{s}^{\prime 1}, d w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right) + \hat{D} S \left(w_{s}^{\prime 1}, d w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right) + \left[ S \left(w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right), S \left(d w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right) \right]\right) Z \rangle \\
+ \int_{0}^{1} \langle [ S (Y), S (Z) ] w_{s}^{\prime 1}, d w_{s}^{\prime 1} \rangle . \tag{4.61}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now recall that if $R^L$ is the Levi-Civita curvature tensor of $M$, then

$$
R^{L} = R + \hat{D} S + [ S, S ]. \tag{4.62}
$$

(4.61) is equal to

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \langle Y, R^{L} \left(w_{s}^{\prime 1}, d w_{s}^{\prime 1}\right) Z \rangle + \int_{0}^{1} \langle [ S (Y), S (Z) ] w_{s}^{\prime 1}, d w_{s}^{\prime 1} \rangle . \tag{4.63}
$$

The well-known symmetries of $R^L$ show that (4.63) is also equal to

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \langle \left(R^{L} (Y, Z) - [ S (Y), S (Z) ]\right) d w_{s}^{\prime 1}, w_{s}^{\prime 1} \rangle . \tag{4.64}
$$

Since $S$ and $\hat{D}S$ send $TG$ into $T^{H}M$, using (4.62) again, we find that (4.64) is equal to

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \langle R (Y, Z) d w_{s}^{\prime 1}, w_{s}^{\prime 1} \rangle .
$$

The Theorem is proved.
$\square$

We are now ready to evaluate $\mathcal{L}'(x_0)$.

**Definition 4.15.** $\hat{A}$ denotes the ad $O(n)$ invariant polynomial on $\mathcal{A}$ such that if $C$ is an antisymmetric matrix with diagonal entries $\left[ \begin{array}{cc}0 &amp; x_{i}\\ -x_{i} &amp; 0 \end{array} \right]$, then

$$
\hat{A}(C) = \prod_{1}^{l} \frac{x_{i}}{2} / \left. sh\left(\frac{x_{i}}{2}\right) \right}. \tag{4.65}
$$

We will use the Weil homomorphisms for $TG$ and $\xi$ on $M$ (see Spivak [35]).

142
J.
M.
Bismut

Theorem 4.16. We have the following equalities of differential forms over $M$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{\bar{W}} \exp^{\wedge} \left\{ - \frac{i}{4\pi} \int_{0}^{1} \langle R^{G}(.,.) w'^{1}, d w'^{1} \rangle \right\} d P_{1}(w^{1}) = \hat{A} \left( \frac{R^{G}}{2\pi} \right) \tag{4.66} \\
\int_{\bar{W}'} \exp^{\wedge} \left\{ - \frac{i \gamma_{1}}{2\pi} \right\} \operatorname{Tr} \bar{V}_{1}^{2} d P'(\gamma) = \operatorname{Tr} \exp \left[ \frac{-L}{2i\pi} \right].
\end{array}
$$

Proof.
The first line of (4.66) is proved in [18, Theorem 3.17].
The second line is an obvious extension of the proof of [18, Theorem 3.18].
$\square$

In what follows, $\int_{G}$ again denotes integration along the fiber of differential forms.

By using Theorems 4.13, 4.14, 4.16, we finally obtain the Index Theorem of Atiyah-Singer for families.

Theorem 4.17. A representative in cohomology of $ch(\operatorname{Ker}D_{+} - \operatorname{Ker}D_{-})$ is the $C^\infty$ closed form over $B$

$$
\int_{G} \hat{A} \left( \frac{R^{G}}{2\pi} \right) \wedge c h(\xi). \tag{4.67}
$$

Remark 3. By combining the methods of this paper with what is done in [20a], we could also obtain an equivariant version of Theorem 4.17.

V.
The index theorem for families: A second heat equation proof

In this section, we briefly present a second heat equation proof of Theorem 4.17. This proof is still based on Theorem 2.6, but is not obtained by an explicit computation of a local index formula.

It consists in noting that if $D'$ is the Dirac operator acting on the whole bundle of spinors over $TM$, the index of $D_{+}'$ (which is an integer) can be computed in two ways:

a) By the classical Atiyah-Singer Index Theorem, whose heat equation proof [6, 12, 18, 26, 27] consists in expanding the metric of $M$ by a factor $\frac{1}{t}$.

b) By another method, which we develop here, which consists in expanding only the metric of $B$ by the factor $\frac{1}{t}$.
In this new computation of the index, the expression found in Theorem 2.6 appears explicitly as a sort of infinite dimensional characteristic class.

By identifying the two formulas for the index, and using an elementary result in $K$-theory and also Poincaré duality, we are then able to prove again Theorem 4.17.

This new proof has close formal similarities with the proof of Theorem 3.5.

The proof is only briefly sketched.
Since a priori estimates are here again essential, we briefly describe a probabilistic method based on the partial Malliavin calculus developed in Bismut and Michel [22] to obtain them.
It permits us to

Families index theorem and the heat equation

separate what happens in the horizontal and vertical directions of the fibration.
We hope that the indications given here are explicit enough to permit a more detailed analysis.

In a), the main assumptions and notations are given.
In b), an exotic formula for $\operatorname{Ind} D_{+}^{\prime}$ is briefly proved.
In c), a comparison with the classical formula for $\operatorname{Ind} D_{+}^{\prime}$ permits us to prove again the Index Theorem for families.

## a) Assumptions and notations

In this section, we do all the assumptions of Sects.
1 and 2.

Also note that we need only to establish the Index Theorem when $B$ is even-dimensional.
If $B$ is odd dimensional, we may as well replace $B$ by $B \times S_1$ .

So we assume that $B$ is even-dimensional, oriented and also has a spin structure.
These last two assumptions are only needed to simplify the computations.

Let $F_{+}^{\prime}, F_{-}^{\prime}$ be the bundles of positive and negative spinors over $B$ .

Of course, $F_{+}^{\prime}, F_{-}^{\prime}$ can be lifted to $M$ by $\pi$ .
We will still note $F_{+}^{\prime}, F_{-}^{\prime}$ the lifted bundles over $M$ .

Then $M$ is a spin manifold.
As shown in Atiyah et al.
[7], $c(TM)$ is the graded tensor product $c(TG)\bar{\otimes} c(TB)$ .
The bundles of positive and negative spinors $C_+$ , $C_-$ over $M$ are given by

$$
C _ {+} = \left(F _ {+} \otimes F _ {+} ^ {\prime}\right) \oplus \left(F _ {-} \otimes F _ {-} ^ {\prime}\right) \tag {5.1}
$$

$$
C _ {-} = \left(F _ {+} \otimes F _ {-} ^ {\prime}\right) \oplus \left(F _ {-} \otimes F _ {+} ^ {\prime}\right).
$$

The bundles $C_{+}\otimes \xi$ , $C_{-}\otimes \xi$ over $M$ are endowed with the connection $V$ .

Also $e_1 \ldots e_n$ , $f_1 \ldots f_m$ act on $C_{\pm} \otimes \xi$ by Clifford multiplication and interchange $C_{+} \otimes \xi$ and $C_{-} \otimes \xi$ .
If $(g \otimes g') \otimes h \in C_{\pm} \otimes \xi$ , then

$$
e _ {i} (g \otimes g ^ {\prime} \otimes h) = \left(e _ {i} g\right) \otimes g ^ {\prime} \otimes h
$$

$$
f _ {x} (g \otimes g ^ {\prime} \otimes h) = (- 1) ^ {\deg g} g \otimes f _ {x} g ^ {\prime} \otimes h.
$$

Definition 5.1. For $t &gt; 0$ , $D^{\prime t}$ denotes the operator acting on $C_{\pm} \otimes \xi$

$$
D ^ {\prime t} = e _ {i} V _ {e _ {i}} + \sqrt {t} f _ {x} V _ {f _ {x}}.
$$

We will write $D^{\prime}$ instead of $D^{\prime 1}$

Recall that $K$ is the scalar curvature of the fibers $G$ .
Also let $K^B$ be the scalar curvature of $B$ .

Recall that $R^G$ is the curvature tensor of $F$ .
If $Y, Z \in TM$ , $R^G(Y, Z)$ acts on $F_{\pm}$ .
It acts as $R^G(Y, Z) \otimes 1 \otimes 1$ on $C \otimes \xi$ .
In what follows, we will systematically omit the tensor product sign $\otimes$ .

We will otherwise use the same notations as in Sects.
1-4.

We first have the elementary.

144
J.
M.
Bismut

**Proposition 5.2.** The following formula holds

$$
\begin{aligned}
(D^{\prime t})^2 &amp;= - (V_{e_i})^2 - t (V_{f_\alpha})^2 + \frac{K}{4} + \frac{t K^B}{4} + \frac{1}{2} e_i e_j L(e_i, e_j) - \frac{1}{2} t f_\alpha f_\beta V_{T(f_\alpha, f_\beta)} \\
&amp;\quad - \sqrt{t} f_\alpha e_i V_{T(f_\alpha, e_i)} + \frac{1}{2} t f_\alpha f_\beta \left[ R^G(f_\alpha, f_\beta) + L(f_\alpha, f_\beta) \right] \\
&amp;\quad + \sqrt{t} f_\alpha e_i \left[ R^G(f_\alpha, e_i) + L(f_\alpha, e_i) \right].
\end{aligned}
\tag{5.2}
$$

**Proof.** Note that $TG$ and $T^H M$ are stable under $R$.
Since $R(e_i, e_j)$ restricted to $G$ is the Levi-Civita curvature of $TG$, by Lichnerowicz’s formula [30; 18, Theorem 1.9]

$$
\frac{1}{8} \langle R(e_i, e_j) e_k, e_l \rangle e_i e_j e_k e_l = \frac{K}{4}.
\tag{5.3}
$$

Similarly

$$
\frac{1}{8} \langle R^G(f_\alpha, f_\beta) f_\gamma, f_\delta \rangle f_\alpha f_\beta f_\gamma f_\delta = \frac{K^B}{4}.
$$

Also by Theorem 1.9, $R(e_i, e_j) f_\alpha = 0$, $R(e_i, f_\alpha) f_\beta = 0$.

(5.2) follows from an easy computation left to the reader.
$\square$

$D^t_+$, $D^t_-$ denotes the restriction of $D^t$ to the sections of $C_+ \otimes \xi$, $C_- \otimes \xi$.

Clearly the index of $D^t_+$ is independent of $t$ and equal to the index of $D^t_+$.

b) A formula for the index of $D^t_+$

We here use the notations in Theorem 2.6. (2.38) is then a $C^\infty$ differential form over $B$.

Also using Definition 4.15, we can define the $C^\infty$ form over $B$ $\hat{A}\left(\frac{R^B}{2\pi}\right)$.

We now prove the following result

**Theorem 5.3.** The index of $D^t_+$ is given by the formula

$$
\operatorname{Ind} D^t_+ = \left( -\frac{i}{\pi} \right)^{m/2} \int_B \hat{A} \left( \frac{i R^B}{2} \right) \wedge \int_{G_y} \operatorname{Tr}_s \left[ P_1^y(x, x) \right] dx.
\tag{5.4}
$$

**Proof.** We only give a brief sketch of the proof.
The idea of the proof is to use the method of the partial Malliavin calculus developed in Bismut and Michel [22, Sects. 1c) and 3].

First note that the Riemannian volume element $dv$ of $M$ can be written $dv(x) = dy dx^y$ (where $dx^y$ is the volume element in $G_y$).

Let $Q_t(x, x')$ be the $C^\infty$ kernel in $M$ of the operator $e^{-\frac{(D' t)^2}{2}}$.
For $x \in M$, $Q_t(x, x)$ is a linear mapping from $(C_\pm \otimes \xi)_x$ into itself.

Let $\operatorname{Tr}_+[Q_t(x,x)]$, $\operatorname{Tr}_-[Q_t(x,x)]$ be the trace of $Q_t(x,x)$ acting on $(C_+ \otimes \xi)_x$, $(C_- \otimes \xi)_x$.

Families index theorem and the heat equation

We claim that

$$
\operatorname {I n d} D _ {+} ^ {\prime} = \int_ {M} \left(\operatorname {T r} _ {+} - \operatorname {T r} _ {-}\right) \left(Q _ {t} (x, x)\right) d v (x) \tag {5.5}
$$

or equivalently

$$
\operatorname {I n d} D _ {+} ^ {\prime} = \int_ {B} d y \int_ {G _ {y}} \left(\operatorname {T r} _ {+} - \operatorname {T r} _ {-}\right) \left(Q _ {t} (x, x)\right) d x ^ {y}. \tag {5.6}
$$

(5.5) does not directly follow from Atiyah et al.
[6] since $D^{\prime t}$ is not self-adjoint.
However if $D^{c,t}$ is taken as in (3.11), (5.5) holds with respect to $D^{c,t}$ by [6, Theorem EIII].
Then $A^t = D^{\prime t} - D^{c,t}$ is of order 0. Replacing $D^{c,t}$ by $D^{c,t} + sA^t$, and proceeding as in Proposition 2.10 (or even using Proposition 2.10), we find that (5.5) holds.

We now briefly explain how to study the asymptotics of (5.6) as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$.

The idea is of course to separate what happens in the horizontal directions $T^H M$, and in the vertical directions $TG$.

$w = (w^{1},\ldots ,w^{n})$ and $w^{\prime} = (w^{\prime 1},\dots ,w^{\prime m})$ are two independent Brownian motions taking their values in $R^n$ and $R^m$.
$P$ and $P^{\prime}$ denotes the probability laws of $w$ and $w^{\prime}$.
We assume that $P(w_{0} = 0) = P^{\prime}(w_{0}^{\prime} = 0) = 1$.

$Z$ denotes the bundle of oriented orthonormal frames in $TB$, $Z'$ the corresponding $\operatorname{Spin}(m)$ bundle.
$Z_1', \ldots, Z_m'$ denotes the standard horizontal vector fields in $Z'$ for the Levi-Civita connection of $Z'$.
Let $\sigma'$ be the projection $Z' \to Z$, $\varrho'$ the projection $Z \to B$.

For $y_0 \in B$, $v_0' \in Z_{y_0'}'$ consider the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l} d v ^ {\prime t} = Z _ {t} ^ {\prime} \left(v ^ {\prime t}\right) \sqrt {t} d w ^ {\prime t} \tag {5.7} \\ v ^ {\prime t} (0) = v _ {0} ^ {\prime}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Set

$$
\begin{array}{l} v _ {s} ^ {t} = \sigma^ {\prime} v _ {s} ^ {\prime t} \tag {5.8} \\ y _ {s} ^ {t} = \varrho^ {\prime} v _ {s} ^ {t}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Then $y_{s/t}'$ is a standard Brownian motion in $B$ starting at $y_0$.

We now lift horizontally the curve $s \to y_s^t$ in the fiber bundle $M_{\overline{x}} B$.

Namely let $U$ be an open set containing $y_0$ such that $\pi^{-1}(U) \simeq U \times X$.
We also assume that $U$ is diffeomorphic to an open ball in $R^n$.

Let $X_{a}(x,y)$ be the vector field on $X$ which lifts horizontally $\frac{\partial}{\partial y^{a}}$.
For $x_0 \in X$, we can solve the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l} d x = X _ {a} \left(x, y _ {s} ^ {t}\right) \langle d y ^ {a}, v _ {s} ^ {t} \big / \big / t d w ^ {\prime} \rangle \tag {5.9} \\ x (0) = x _ {0}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Of course (5.9) only makes sense up to the first time when $y_{s}^{t}$ exits from $U$.
For one given $x_{0}, (y_{s}^{t}, x_{s})$ is the horizontal lift in $U \times X$ of the curve $s \to y_{s}^{t}$.
Also by coupling equations (5.7) and (5.9) and using the results of [13, Chaps. 1 and 4], (5.9) defines a stochastic flow of diffeomorphisms $\psi_{s}(\sqrt[t]{t} dw',.)$ on $X$ with

$$
x _ {s} = \psi_ {s} (\bigvee t d w ^ {\prime}, x _ {0}).
$$

$\psi_{s}(\bigvee t dw', x_{0})$ is jointly continuous in $(s, x_{0})$, $C^\infty$ in $x_{0}$ with derivatives in $x_{0}$ which are jointly continuous in $(s, x_{0})$.
By iterating the procedure, i.e. by considering the

J.
M.
Bismut

successive exit times of $y_{s}^{t}$ from open sets in $B$, and piecing together the various constructions, we can define $\psi_{s}(\sqrt[t]{t} dw,.)$ which is the globally defined horizontal lift of $y_{s}^{t}$.
For each $s$, $\psi_{s}(\sqrt[t]{t} dw,.)$ is a diffeomorphism from $G_{y_0}$ into $G_{y_s^t}$ and depends continuously on $s \in R^{+}$ (as a family of diffeomorphisms).

Also this horizontal lift can be extended to the vector bundles $C_{\pm} \otimes \xi$.
Of course, the actions of $\psi_s(\sqrt[t]{t} dw,.)$ is linear on the considered vector bundles.
If $x_0 \in G_{y_0}$, $x_s = \psi_s(\sqrt[t]{t} dw, x_0)$, we write the equation for $x_s$ in the form

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d x _ {s} = \left(d y _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {H}, \tag {5.10} \\
x (0) = x _ {0}.
\end{array}
$$

The infinitesimal generator associated with $\psi_s(\sqrt[t]{t},.)$ is obviously $\frac{t}{2} (V_{f_\pi})^2$.

We now briefly show how to modify the construction so as to obtain the generator $\frac{1}{2} \left[ (V_{e_i})^2 + t(V_{f_\pi})^2 \right]$.

Namely, for $x_0 \in G_y$, $u_0' \in O_{x_0'}$, consider the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d u _ {s} ^ {\prime t} = \left(d y _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {H} \left(u _ {s} ^ {\prime t}\right) + Y _ {i} ^ {\prime} \left(u _ {s} ^ {\prime t}\right). d w ^ {i} \tag {5.11} \\
u ^ {\prime} (0) = u _ {0} ^ {\prime}.
\end{array}
$$

Set

$$
u _ {s} ^ {t} = \sigma u _ {s} ^ {\prime t}, \quad x _ {s} ^ {t} = \varrho u _ {s} ^ {t}.
$$

Then if $\tau_s^{0,t}$ is the parallel transport operator from vector fibers over $x_0$ to vector fibers over $x_s^t$, and if $\tau_0^{s,t} = [\tau_s^{0,t}]^{-1}$, by [13, IX, Theorem 1.1] if $h$ is a smooth section of $C_{\pm} \otimes \xi$.

$$
\tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} h \left(x _ {s} ^ {t}\right) - h \left(x _ {0}\right) - \frac {1}{2} \frac {1}{2} \tau_ {0} ^ {b, t} \left[ \left(\left(V _ {e _ {i}}\right) ^ {2} + t \left(V _ {f _ {\pi}}\right) ^ {2}\right) h \left(x _ {b} ^ {t}\right) \right] d b \tag {5.12}
$$

is a martingale.
It follows easily that

$$
\left(e ^ {+ \frac {s}{2} \left(\left(V _ {e _ {i}}\right) ^ {2} + t \left(V _ {f _ {\pi}}\right) ^ {2}\right)} h\right) \left(x _ {0}\right) = E \left[ \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} h \left(x _ {s} ^ {t}\right) \right].
$$

To construct the semi-group associated with $e^{-\frac{(D'')^2}{2}}$, we must proceed as in Definition 4.1. Namely consider the stochastic differential equation

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d U _ {s} ^ {\prime t} = U _ {s} ^ {\prime t} \left\{\frac {1}{4} \sqrt {t} f _ {\alpha} \sqrt {t} f _ {\beta} \langle \left(u _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} T _ {x _ {s} ^ {t}} \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\alpha}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\beta}\right), \delta w \rangle \right. \\
+ \frac {\sqrt {t} f _ {\alpha} e _ {i}}{2} \left\langle \left(u _ {s} ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} T _ {x _ {s} ^ {t}} \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\alpha}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {i}\right), \delta w \right\rangle \\
- \left[ \frac {1}{4} e _ {i} e _ {j} \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} L \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {i}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {j}\right) \right. \\
+ \frac {1}{4} \sqrt {t} f _ {\alpha} \sqrt {t} f _ {\beta} \left(\tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} R ^ {G} \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\alpha}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\beta}\right) + \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} L \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\alpha}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\beta}\right)\right) \\
\left. + \frac {1}{2} \sqrt {t} f _ {\alpha} e _ {i} \left(\tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} R ^ {G} \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\alpha}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {i}\right) + \tau_ {0} ^ {s, t} L \left(\tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} f _ {\alpha}, \tau_ {s} ^ {0, t} e _ {i}\right)\right) \right] d s \Bigg \} \tag {5.13} \\
\end{array}
$$

$$
U _ {0} ^ {\prime t} = I.
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Of course $T$ takes its values in $TG$, so that the scalar products $\langle (u_s^t)^{-1} T, \ldots, \delta w \rangle$ are taken in $R^n$.

Then as in [18, Theorem 2.5], it can be proved that

$$
e^{- \frac{s(D't)^2}{2}} h(x_0) = E \left[ \exp \left[ -\int_0^s \left( \frac{K(x_v^t)}{8} + \frac{t K^B(x_v^t)}{8} \right) dv \right] U_s^a \tau_0^{s,t} h(x_s^t) \right]. \tag{5.14}
$$

Also note that

$$
e^{- \frac{(D't)^2}{2}} h(x_0) = \int_M Q_t(x_0, x') h(x') dv(x'). \tag{5.15}
$$

As $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, $y_s^t$ tends to concentrate on $y_0$, while the diffusion $x_s^t$ is free in the vertical directions of $G$.

Let us briefly analyse this phenomenon probabilistically.
Let $h$ be a $C^\infty$ section of $F_\pm \otimes \xi$ over $G_y$.

If $y_s^t$ is a path such that $y_1^t = y_0$, $\tau_0^{1,t} h(x_1^t)$ is in $(F_\pm \otimes \xi)_{x_0}$.
$U_1$ acts on $(F_\pm \otimes F_\pm^t \otimes \xi)_{x_0}$.
It follows that if $\tau_0^{1,t}$ denotes the restriction of $\tau_0^{1,t}$ to $F_\pm^{t}, y_0$, then

$$
\operatorname{Tr} \left[ i^{m/2} f_1 \dots f_m U_1^a \left[ \tau_0^{1,t} h(x_1^t) \otimes \tau_0^{1,t} \right] \right] \tag{5.16}
$$

is an element of $(F_\pm \otimes \xi)_{x_0}$.

Let $q_t(y, y')$ be the heat kernel associated with the Laplace-Beltrami operator on $B$, $E_{y_0, y_0}^t$ the expectation operator for $q_s^t$ conditional on $y_1^t = y_0$ (see [16, Chap. 2]).
In what follows, equations (5.11), (5.13) are solved with $y'$ having the law $E_{y_0, y_0}^t$.

We claim that if $dy^1 \ldots dy^m$ is the oriented volume element in $T_{y_0}B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{t \downarrow 0} q_t(y_0, y_0) E_{y_0, y_0}^t \left[ \int_W \operatorname{Tr} \left[ i^{\frac{m}{2}} f_1 \dots f_m U_1^a (\tau_0^{1,t} h(x_1^t) \otimes \tau_0^{1,t}) \right] \right. \\
\left. \cdot \exp \left\{ -\int_0^1 \left[ \frac{K(x_s^t)}{8} + \frac{t K^B(x_s^t)}{8} \right] ds \right\} dP(w) \right] dy^1 \ldots dy^m \\
\frac{m}{2} \left( -\frac{i}{\pi} \right)^{m/2} \tilde{A} \left( \frac{i R_B}{2} \right) \wedge \int_{G_{y_0}} P_1^{y_0}(x, x') h(x') dx'. \tag{5.17}
\end{aligned}
$$

The proof of (5.17) is not difficult in the sense that no conditioning in the vertical variables is needed.
It uses explicitly equation (5.13), our results in [16, Chap. 4], and the same reasoning as in the proof of Theorem 4.12 to change the Clifford variables $f_a$ into the Grassmann variables $dy^a$.
We also refer to [18, Sect. 3].

Now (5.14) and (5.15) show that for each $t &gt; 0$ the l.h.s.
of (5.17) coincides with

$$
\int_{G_{y_0}} \operatorname{Tr} \left[ i^{\frac{m}{2}} f_1 \dots f_m Q_t(x_0, x') h(x') \right] dx' dy^1 \ldots dy^m. \tag{5.18}
$$

(5.17) and (5.18) show that as $t \downarrow \downarrow 0$, the kernel in (5.18) converges weakly to the kernel in the r.h.s.
of (5.17).
The problem is to obtain a pointwise convergence.

J.
M.
Bismut

To do this, we can use the partial Malliavin calculus in Bismut and Michel [22, Sects. 1, 3].
Namely, by still using the asymptotic representation of the Brownian loop $y_{s}^{t}$ of [16, Sect. 4] and the partial Malliavin calculus on the Brownian motion $w$ as in [22] – which changes neither $y_{s}^{t}$ neither $\tau_0^{\prime 1,t}$ – we can obtain uniform bounds as $t\downarrow \downarrow 0$ of the derivatives of the kernels (5.18) in $x'$, in particular deduce from (5.18) the needed pointwise convergence, and prove that

$$
\lim_{t \downarrow \downarrow 0} \left[ \operatorname{Tr} \frac{i^{m}}{2} f_{1} \dots f_{m} Q_{t} \left(x_{0}, x_{0}\right) \right] dy^{1} \dots dy^{m} \stackrel{m}{=} \left(- \frac{i}{\pi}\right)^{m/2} \hat{A} \left(\frac{i R^{B}}{2}\right) \wedge P_{1}^{y} \left(x_{0}, x_{0}\right) \tag{5.19}
$$

and that the convergence takes place boundedly.
From (5.19), we deduce

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{t \downarrow \downarrow 0} \int_{M} \operatorname{Tr} \left[ i^{\frac{m + n}{2}} e_{1} \dots e_{n} f_{1} \dots f_{m} Q_{t} (x, x) \right] dv(x) \\
= \left(- \frac{i}{\pi}\right)^{m/2} \int_{B} \hat{A} \left(\frac{i R^{B}}{2}\right) \wedge \int_{G_{y}} \operatorname{Tr}_{s} \left[ P_{1}^{y} (x, x) \right] dx. \tag{5.20}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using (1.13), we know that the l.h.s.
of (5.20) is exactly

$$
\lim_{t \downarrow \downarrow 0} \int_{M} \left(\operatorname{Tr}_{+} - \operatorname{Tr}_{-}\right) \left(Q_{t} (x, x)\right) dv(x) \tag{5.21}
$$

which by (5.5) coincides with $\operatorname{Ind} D_{+}^{\prime}$.

The sketched proof is finished.

Remark 1. Instead of using the Dirac operator $D^{\prime t}$, we could have as well used the Dirac operator $D^{c,t}$ defined in (3.11).
Instead of (2.38), (3.5) would have appeared in (5.4).

Remark 2. It is plausible that the uniform bounds which are needed to prove Theorem 5.3 could also be obtained by the method of Berline and Vergne [12] or Getzler [27].

c) The index theorem for families

We now prove Theorem 4.17 using Theorems 2.6 and 5.3.

In fact the Atiyah-Singer Index Theorem (see [18, Theorem 3.19]) shows that

$$
\operatorname{Ind} D_{+}^{\prime} = \left(- \frac{i}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{n + m}{2}} \int_{M} \hat{A} \left(\frac{i R}{2}\right) \wedge \overline{c h} (\xi). \tag{5.22}
$$

In what follows, equality will be taken in the sense of cohomology classes.
Obviously

$$
\hat{A} \left(\frac{i R}{2}\right) = \hat{A} \left(\frac{i R^{B}}{2}\right) \hat{A} \left(\frac{i R^{G}}{2}\right). \tag{5.23}
$$

Families index theorem and the heat equation

Using (5.22), (5.23), we find that

$$
\operatorname {I n d} D _ {+} ^ {\prime} = \left(- \frac {i}{\pi}\right) ^ {\frac {n + m}{2}} \int_ {B} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \int_ {G} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {G}}{2}\right) \wedge \bar {c h} \xi . \tag {5.24}
$$

Theorem 5.3 then shows that

$$
\int_ {B} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \int_ {G} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {y}} (x, x) \right] d x = \left(- \frac {i}{\pi}\right) ^ {n / 2} \int_ {B} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \int_ {G} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {G}}{2}\right) \bar {c h} (\xi) \tag {5.25}
$$

Now let $\xi'$ be any vector bundle over $B$ .
In (5.25) we can replace $\xi$ by $\xi \otimes \pi^{*}(\xi')$ .
It is trivial to verify that

$$
\int_ {G} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {y}} (x, x) \right] d x \tag {5.26}
$$

is changed into

$$
\left[ \int_ {G} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {y}} (x, x) \right] d x \right] \bar {c h} \xi^ {\prime}. \tag {5.27}
$$

Also

$$
\bar {c h} (\xi \otimes \pi^ {*} (\xi^ {\prime})) = \bar {c h} \xi \pi^ {*} \bar {c h} \xi^ {\prime}. \tag {5.28}
$$

So we get that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \int_ {B} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \int_ {G} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {y}} (x, x) \right] d x \bar {c h} \xi^ {\prime} \\ = \left(- \frac {i}{\pi}\right) ^ {n / 2} \int_ {B} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \left[ \int_ {G} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {G}}{2}\right) \bar {c h} \xi \right] \bar {c h} \xi^ {\prime}. \tag {5.29} \\ \end{array}
$$

Now recall the classical result that

$$
K (B) \otimes_ {Z} R = H ^ {* \text {e v e n}} (B; R). \tag {5.30}
$$

Using Poincaré duality, we find from (5.29) and (5.30)

$$
\hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \int_ {G} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {y}} (x, x) \right] d x = \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {B}}{2}\right) \wedge \left(- \frac {i}{\pi}\right) ^ {n / 2} \int_ {G} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {G}}{2}\right) \bar {c h} \xi . \tag {5.31}
$$

Since $\hat{A}\left(\frac{iR^B}{2}\right)$ is stable, we get from (5.31)

$$
\int_ {G} \operatorname {T r} _ {s} \left[ P _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {y}} (x, x) \right] d x = \left(- \frac {i}{\pi}\right) ^ {n / 2} \int_ {G} \hat {A} \left(\frac {i R ^ {G}}{2}\right) \bar {c h} \xi . \tag {5.32}
$$

Using Theorem 2.6 and (5.32), we find that Theorem 4.17 has been proved again.
