# The elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

Jean Ludwig, Günther Rosenbaum, and Jürgen Samuel

Fakultät für Mathematik, Universität Bielefeld, D-4800 Bielefeld 1, Federal Republic of Germany

# Introduction

Let $G$ be a locally compact topological group which is C.C.R.
i.e. for every irreducible unitary representation $\pi$ of the $C^*$-algebra $C^*(G)$ of $G$, $\pi(C^*(G))$ is the algebra of compact operators on the Hilbert-space $\mathcal{H}_{\pi}$ of $\pi$.
The two-sided ideal $FR(G)$ of the elements $\xi$ in $C^*(G)$ for which $\pi(\xi)$ is an operator of finite rank for every $\pi \in \hat{G}$ is then dense in $C^*(G)$ [2].
In 1960 Fell observed that for a semi-simple injective group $G$ the smaller ideal $BR(G)$ ($\subset FR(G)$) of the elements $\eta \in C^*(G)$ with bounded rank, (i.e. $\sup_{\pi \in \hat{G}} \operatorname{rank} \pi(\eta) &lt; \infty$) is dense in $C^*(G)$.
$BR(G)$ contains even a dense subalgebra consisting of $C^\infty$-functions with compact support (see [4], Sect.
12).
For a nilpotent Lie group the situation is more complicated.
It is easy to see that for the Heisenberg groups $H_n$ $BR(H_n)$ is not dense in $C^*(H_n)$.

In fact, if $Z$ denotes the center of the Lie algebra $\mathcal{A}_n$ of $H_n$, then for any $0 \neq \eta \in z^*$, for any Schwartz-function $f$ we have:

$$
\operatorname{tr} \pi_{\eta}(f) = \frac{1}{|\eta|} \left( f \circ \exp|_Z \right)^\wedge (\eta).
$$

It follows from this formula that the ideal $BT(H_n)$ of the elements of bounded trace is contained in the intersection of the kernels of the one-dimensional representations of $C^*(H_n)$.
Furthermore, for any $0 \neq \eta \in Z^*$, it is easy to construct a Schwartz-function $g_\eta$ with $\pi_\eta(g_\eta) \neq 0$ which is contained in $BT(H_n)$.

Hence the hull $h(BT(H_n))$ of $BT(H_n)$ in $(H_n)^\wedge$ is the closed subset of the one-dimensional representations of $H_n$.
In this paper we show that for a nilpotent Lie group $G$ with Lie algebra $\mathcal{G}$ the hull of $BT(G)$ is always the set of the representations $\pi$ whose Kirillov-orbit $O(\pi)$ in $\mathcal{G}^*$ is not of maximal dimension.
Furthermore for any $O_\pi$ of maximal dimension we construct a Schwartz-function $f_\pi$ in $BR(G)$ with $\pi(f_\pi) \neq 0$.
The paper is organized in the following way.

In Sect.
2 we introduce the variable nilpotent Lie groups $(G, M)$ their Lie

168
J.
Ludwig et al.

algebras and their $C^*$-algebras $C^*(G, M)$.
We use the variable nilpotent groups instead of the ordinary ones because this enables us to give a much shorter proof of Theorem 5.3. We do not need any longer to contemplate the free nilpotent groups as Brown was forced to do in his proof of the Kirillov-conjecture (see [1]).

Using recent results of Hulanicki [7] we show that the functional calculus introduced by Dixmier [3] makes it possible to produce for every closed subset $A \subset C^*(G, M)^\wedge$ a dense subset consisting of Schwartz-functions of the Petersen ideal $j(A)$ in $C^*(G, M)$.
We conclude this section with the definition of the ideals $BT_d(G, M)$ ($d \in 2\mathbb{N}$) of the elements with bounded trace on $(G, M)_d^\wedge$, the subspace of all orbits of dimension $d$.

In Sect.
3 we show that the hull $h_d$ of $BT_d$ contains the intersection of $(G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge}$ with the closure of $(G, M)_d^{\wedge}$ (Theorem 3.4).

Section 4 is the most delicate step in the proof of 5.3. We study for a Schwartz-class function $f$ the expression

$$
|f|_{d} = \sup_{\pi \in \hat{G}_d} |\operatorname{tr} \pi(f)|.
$$

We show that in the case where the support of the Fourier-transform $(f \circ \exp)^\wedge$ of $f$ is compact and disjoint from $g_{\prec d}^*$, $|f|_d$ is always finite.

Unfortunately $(\ast)$ cannot be applied directly to prove 5.3. For there we must decide wether the number

$$
\sup_{\pi \in \hat{G}_d} \operatorname{tr} \pi(f^* * f) \tag{**}
$$

is finite or not.
But we do not know much about $\operatorname{supp}(f^* * f)^\wedge$.
In the last section we prove the main Proposition 5.2 by an induction argument using essentially 2.8 and the results of Sect.
4. Theorem 5.3 is then an immediate application of Sect.
3 and of 5.2.

Let us give now some general definitions.
A positive element $x$ of a $C^*$-algebra $A$ is called an element with bounded trace on a subset $C$ of $\hat{A}$ if the function

$$
\pi \rightarrow \operatorname{tr} \pi(x) \quad \text{is bounded on } C.
$$

An element $x$ of $A$ is called an element with bounded rank on $C$, if the function

$$
\pi \rightarrow \operatorname{rank} \pi(x) \quad \text{is bounded on } C.
$$

The set of all positive elements of $A$ with bounded trace on $C$ is denoted by $BT_C(A^+)$, the twosided ideal spanned by $BT_C(A^+)$ is denoted by $BT_C(A)$.
The set of elements of $A$ with bounded rank on $C$ is denoted by $BR_C(A)$.
One can show that $BR_C(A)$ is dense in $BT_C(A)$, (see [11]).
If $BT_{\hat{A}}(A)$ is dense in $A$ then $A$ is called a $C^*$-algebra with bounded trace.
As $BR_C(A) \subset BT_C(A)$ are twosided ideals in $A$, the hull $h_{C^*} = h(BT_C(A)) = h(BR_C(A)) = \{\pi \in \hat{A} \mid \pi(BT_C(A) = 0)\}$ contains a lot of information on $BR_C(A)$ and $BT_C(A)$.

For instance the Pedersen ideal $j(h_{C})$, i.e. the minimal twosided ideal of $A$

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

with hull $C$ is automatically contained in $BR_C(A) \subset BT_C(A)$.
Thus if the hull is known it is possible to construct by a purely algebraic method a dense subset $BT_C(A)$ (see Sect.
2).

Let us finish the introduction by indicating that Sect.
3 is due to the second and third author and the other sections to the first one.

## 2. Variable nilpotent Lie groups, functional calculus, the ideals $BT_k$ and $I_k$

In 2.1 we say what a variable nilpotent Lie group $(G, M)$ is, we define its $C^*$-algebra $C^*(G, M)$ and its Schwartz-space $\mathcal{S}(G, M)$.
Using results of Hulanicki we show that the minimal ideals in the $C^*$-algebra of a variable nilpotent Lie group are generated by Schwartz-functions (Theorem 2.8) and finally we give the definition of the ideals $BT_d$ of bounded trace on the $d$-dimensional part of the dual of $(G, M)$.

### 2.1. Definition

Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a real vector space of dimension $n$.
Let $M$ be a separable locally compact topological Hausdorff space.
We say that $(\mathcal{G}, M)$ is a variable nilpotent Lie algebra (of dimension $n$) if the following properties hold:

1. for every $m \in M$ we have a bilinear map:

$$
[ , ]_m \colon \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{G}
$$

such that $\mathcal{G}_m \colon = (\mathcal{G}, [, ]_m)$ is a nilpotent Lie algebra.

2. The map: $M \times \mathcal{G} \times \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{G}$

$$
(m, x, y) \to [x, y]_m
$$

is continuous.

### 2.2

Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a variable nilpotent Lie algebra.
On the locally compact space $G: = \mathcal{G}$ we can define a group multiplication $\underline{\mathbf{\Pi}}_m$ by using the Campbell-Baker-Hausdorff formula, i.e.

$$
x_m \cdot y := C \cdot B \cdot H(x, y) \quad \text{in} \quad \mathcal{G}_m = x + y + \frac{1}{2}[x, y]_m + \dots; \quad x, y \in \mathcal{G}; m \in M.
$$

Thus $G_m \colon = (G, \underline{\mathbf{\Pi}}_m)$ is a simply connected, connected nilpotent Lie group and $(G \cdot M)$ is a variable nilpotent Lie group in the terminology of [10].

Especially the map: $M \times G \times G \to G$

$$
(m, x, y) \to x_m \cdot y
$$

is continuous and the inverse $(x)^{-\frac{1}{m}}$ of $x$ in $G_m$ is just the element $-x$.

We fix a Lebesgue measure $dx$ on $\mathcal{G}$.
This measure $dx$ is then for every $m \in M$ a Haar-measure of $G_m$.

On the space $C_c(M \times G)$ of the continuous complex-valued functions with compact support we define the convolution product $\underline{\mathbf{\Pi}}_m^*$

$$
f \cdot g(m, x) := \int_{G_m} f(m, y) g(m, (y)^{-\frac{1}{m}} \cdot x) dy \quad x \in G; m \in M, f, g \in C_c(G, M),
$$

J.
Ludwig et al.

the involution \*:

$$
f ^ {*} (m, x) := f (m, (x) ^ {- \frac {1}{m}}) = \overline {{f (m , - x)}} \quad x \in G; m \in M; f \in C _ {c} (M \times G)
$$

the norm $||_{1}$

$$
\begin{array}{l} | f | _ {1} := \sup  _ {m \in M} | f (m) | _ {1} \\ := \sup  _ {m \in M} \int_ {G} | f (m, x) | d x. \\ \end{array}
$$

The involution _ is isometric and the normed algebra $C_c(M \times G) = (C_c(M \times G), _, \*, ||\_1)$ has bounded approximate units (see [10]).

We denote by $C^*(G, M)$ the enveloping $C^*$ -algebra of $C_c(M \times G)$ .
Denote for $m \in M$ the canonical homomorphism $p_m$ of $C_c(M \times G)$ onto $C_c(\{m\} \times G) \cong C_c(G_m)$ :

$$
p _ {m} (f) (x) := f (m, x); \quad x \in G, f \in C _ {c} (M \times G).
$$

If $\pi$ is an irreducible unitary representation of $G_{m}$ then $\rho \coloneqq \pi \circ p_{m}$

$$
\rho (f) = \pi \left(p _ {m} (f)\right) \quad f \in C _ {c} (M \times G)
$$

is an irreducible unitary representation of $C_c(M \times G)$ , hence $\rho$ defines an element in the dual space

$$
(G, M) ^ {\wedge} \quad \text {of} \ C ^ {*} (G, M).
$$

It is not difficult to see that

$$
C ^ {*} (G, M) ^ {\wedge} = \bigcup_ {m \in M} \hat {G} _ {m}
$$

i.e. for every $\rho \in C^{*}(G,M)^{\wedge}$ there exists $m\in M$ and $\pi \in \hat{G}_m$ so that

$$
\rho = \pi \circ p _ {m} \quad (\text {see [10]}).
$$

Let us also introduce the $L^1$ -algebra of $(G,M)$ .

$L^1 (G,M)$ is the completion under the $||_{1}$ -norm of $C_c(G,M)$ .

Evidently $L^1 (G,M)$ is a subalgebra of $C^* (G,M)$

## 2.3. The topology of $C^* (G,M)$

Let $(G,M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G},M)$ .

The groups $G_{m}$ act on $\mathcal{G}^{*}$ via the coadjoint representation $\mathrm{Ad}_m^*$ .
We define the following equivalence relation on $M \times \mathcal{G}^{*}$

$$
(m, l) \simeq (m ^ {\prime}, l ^ {\prime})
$$

if $m = m'$ and if $l' = \mathrm{Ad}_m^*(x)l$ for some $x \in G_m$ .
Denote by $M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$ the corresponding space of the equivalence classes.
We provide $M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$ with

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

the quotient topology.
Let $p$ be the canonical projection of $M \times \mathcal{G}^*$ onto $M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$.
A subset $\tilde{U}$ in $M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$ is then open if and only if $U^1 = p^{-1}(\tilde{U})$ is open in $M \times \mathcal{G}$ and $G$-invariant i.e. if for every continuous map

$$
\varphi \colon M \to G
$$

the subset $U^{\varphi} = \{(m, \mathrm{Ad}^{*}(\varphi(m))l) \mid (m,l) \in U\}$ is contained in $U$.
If $V$ is any open subset in $M \times \mathcal{G}^{*}$ then

$$
\tilde {V} = \left\{\left(m, \operatorname {A d} ^ {*} (\varphi (m)) l \mid \varphi \colon M \to G \text { continuous}, (m, l) \in V \right\} \right.
$$

is open in $M \times \mathcal{G}^*$ and $G$-invariant, hence $\tilde{V}$ defines an open subset in $M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$.

In [10] it has been shown that the Kirillov map $\tilde{K}$ which assigns to every $\rho = \pi \circ p_m$ in $C(G, M)^\wedge$ its orbit $\tilde{K}(\rho)^1 = (m, K(\pi)) \in M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$ is a homeomorphism.

## 2.4. Functional calculus

Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group.
We fix an euclidean norm $\| \cdot \|$ on $\mathcal{G}$.
Let $U$ be the symmetric neighbourhood $U^1 = \{x \in \mathcal{G} \mid \| x \| \leq 1\}$ of $0$ in $\mathcal{G}$.
For $m \in M$ we define the subadditive function

$$
\mathcal {T} _ {m} (x) \colon = \min  \left\{k \mid x \in U ^ {k (\cdot)} = U _ {\underset {k - \text {t i m e s}} {\text {m}}} ^ {\star} U \right\}.
$$

We write

$$
w _ {m} (x) \colon = 1 + \mathcal {T} _ {m} (x); \quad x \in G.
$$

Then for every $\alpha \geq 0$ we have:

$$
w _ {m} ^ {\alpha} (x) \geq 1; \quad w _ {m} ^ {\alpha} (x) = w _ {m} ^ {\alpha} (- x); \quad w _ {m} ^ {\alpha} \left(x _ {m} ^ {\prime} y\right) \leq w _ {m} ^ {\alpha} (x) \cdot w _ {m} ^ {\alpha} (y).
$$

Let

$$
L _ {m, \alpha^ {1}} = \left\{f \in L ^ {1} (G) \mid | f | _ {m, \alpha^ {1}} = \int | f (x) | w _ {m} (x) d x &lt;   \infty \right\}.
$$

$L_{m,\alpha}$ is a Banach-algebra and a subalgebra of $L^1 (G_m)$.
It is easy to see that

$$
w _ {m} (x) \leq 2 \left(1 + \| x \|\right); \quad \text {for every} \ m \in M; x \in G \tag {2.4.1}
$$

and that for every compact subset $K \subset M$ for every compact neighbourhood $U$ of 0 in $\mathcal{G}$ the Haar-measure

$$
\left| U ^ {k \left(\cdot\right)} \right| \quad \text {of} \quad U ^ {k \left(\cdot\right)}
$$

satisfies,

$$
\left| U ^ {k (\cdot)} \right| \leq C (1 + k) ^ {N} \tag {2.4.2}
$$

for every $m \in K, k \in \mathbb{N}$; for some $C &gt; 0, N &gt; 0$.

2.5. Let $B$ be a Banach-\*-algebra.
We say that a function $\varphi$ operates on an element $f$ in $B$ if the Gelfand transform $\hat{f}$ of $f$ with respect to the smallest

172
J.
Ludwig et al.

Banach-subalgebra $\mathcal{A}$ containing $f$ is real and there exists $g$ in $\mathcal{A}$ such that $\varphi \circ \hat{f} = \hat{g}$.
We write

$$
g = \varphi \{f \}.
$$

For $f \in B$ let

$$
e(f) = \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \frac{(if)^j}{j!}.
$$

Suppose that $\| e(nf) \|_B = O(|n|^N)$ as $|n| \to \infty$.
Then every $\varphi \in C_c^k(\mathbb{R})$ with $k &gt; N + 1$ and $\varphi(0) = 0$ operates on $f$ and

$$
\varphi \{f\} = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\varphi}(\lambda) e(\lambda f) \, d\lambda.
$$

**Theorem** (Hulanicki, Theorem 2.1 in [7]).
Suppose $G$ is of polynomial growth, let $U$ and $N$ be defined as in (2.4.2).
Suppose $f = f^* \in L_\alpha \cap L^2(G)$ with $\alpha &gt; \beta + \frac{N}{2} + 1$, then

$$
\| e(i\lambda f) \|_\alpha = O(|\lambda|)^{3(\beta + \frac{N}{2}) + 4} \quad \text{as } |n| \to \infty.
$$

**Corollary** [7].
Suppose $f^* = f \in L_\alpha \cap L^2(G)$, $\alpha &gt; \beta + \frac{N}{2} + 1$, $\varphi \in C_c^k(\mathbb{R})$ with $k &gt; 3$ ($\beta + \frac{N}{2} + 2$) and $\varphi(0) = 0$, then $\varphi$ operates on $f$ in $L_\beta$ and there exists a measure $\mu$ on $G$ with $\int w^\beta(x) \, d|\mu|(x) &lt; \infty$ such that

$$
\varphi \{f\} = f * \mu.
$$

2.6. Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group and $(\mathcal{G}, M)$ its Lie algebra.
Let $b_1, \ldots, b_n$ be a basis of $\mathcal{G}$.
For a multi-index $(i_1, \ldots, i_n) = I$, for $m \in M$ we write

$$
b_m^I = b_{1m}^{i_1} \cdots m^{i_n} b_n^{i_n} \in U(\mathcal{G}_m)
$$

where $U(\mathcal{G}_m)$ denotes the enveloping algebra of $\mathcal{G}_m$.
Let $|I| = i_1 + \ldots + i_n$.

We say that a function $f$ on $M \times G$ is in the Schwartz-space $\mathcal{S}(G, M)$ if $f$ satisfies the following conditions.

1. $f$ is continuous, $p_m(f) = 0$ outside a compact subset of $M$ and $f \in L^1(G, M)$.

2. for every $m \in M$, $p_m(f)$ is $C^\infty$.

3. $$
   \| f \|_{k, \alpha^*} = \sup_{m \in M} \sum_{|I| \leq k} |X^I * p_m(f)|_{m, \alpha} &lt; \infty
   $$

for every $\alpha &gt; 0$; for every $k \in \mathbb{N}$.

It is clear that for $m$ fixed $f \in \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ implies that $p_m(f)$ is in the ordinary Schwartz-space $\mathcal{S}(G_m)$.

The following theorem is an immediate consequence of the proof of Theorem 2.5, of the corollary above and of the estimations (2.4.1), (2.4.2).

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

**Theorem.** Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group.
Let $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ with $\varphi(0) = 0$.
For $f = f^* \in \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ we have $e(i\lambda f) \in \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ for every $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\varphi\{f\} \in \mathcal{S}(G, M)$.

**Remark.** It is clear that $\mathcal{S}(G, M)$ is dense in $C^*(G, M)$ as $\mathcal{S}(G, M) \cap C_c(M \times G)$ is dense in $C_c(M \times G)$ for the $|\cdot|_1$ norm.

## 2.7. Minimal ideals

Let $A$ be an algebra over $\mathbb{C}$.
By $\operatorname{Prim} A$ we mean the space of the primitive ideals of $A$.
We provide $\operatorname{Prim} A$ with the hull-kernel topology: $S \subset \operatorname{Prim} A$ is closed if there exists a subset $F$ in $A$ so that

$$
S = \{I \in \operatorname{Prim} A \mid I \supset F\}.
$$

The hull of a subset $H \subset A$ is the closed subset

$$
h(H) \colon = \{I \in \operatorname{Prim} A \mid I \supset H\}
$$

of $\operatorname{Prim} A$.

**Proposition.** Let $A$ be an algebra.
Let $S$ be a closed subset of $\operatorname{Prim} A$.
Suppose that there exists $a, b \in A$ such that $b \cdot a = a$ and such that $h(\{b\}) \supset S$.
Then every two-sided ideal $J$ of $A$ with $h(J) \subset S$ contains $a$.

**Proof.** Suppose the contrary i.e. we assume that there exists a two-sided ideal $J_0$ of $I$ with $h(J_0) \subset S$ and $a \notin J_0$.
Let $M = J_0 + A(1 - b)$.

$M$ is a modular left ideal in $A$.

If $A = M$ then $b \in M$, hence there exists $c \in A$ and $i \in J_0$ such that

$$
b = i + c - c \cdot b.
$$

But then:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
a = b \cdot a = i \cdot a + c \cdot a - c \cdot b \cdot a \\
= i \cdot a + c \cdot a - c \cdot a = i \cdot a \in J_0,
\end{array}
$$

a contradiction.

So $M$ is a proper modular left ideal in $A$ and hence there exists a maximal modular proper left ideal $\tilde{M}$ of $A$ containing $M$.
Let $\pi$ denote the left regular representation on $A / \tilde{M}$.
$\pi$ is algebraically irreducible and $I = \ker \pi$ is a primitive ideal.
As $J_0 \cdot A \subset M \subset \tilde{M}$, $J_0 \subset I$.

Thus

$$
I \in h(J_0) \subset S \subset h(\{b\}).
$$

That means

$$
b \in I = \{x \in A \mid x \cdot A \subset \tilde{M}\}.
$$

Whence

$$
\begin{array}{l}
b^2 = b \cdot b \in \tilde{M} \quad \text{and so as } b \text{ is a modular right unit of } \tilde{M} \\
b = (b - b \cdot b) + b \cdot b \in \tilde{M} + \tilde{M} = \tilde{M},
\end{array}
$$

whence $\tilde{M} = A$ a contradiction.
Q.e.d.

174
J.
Ludwig et al.

2.8. We return now to the variable nilpotent Lie group $(G, M)$.
Let $S$ be a closed subset of $(G, M)^\wedge \simeq \operatorname{Prim}(C^*(G, M))$.
For $\pi \notin S$ there exists $\xi = \xi^* \in C^*(G, M)$ with

$$
\pi(\xi) \neq 0, \quad \pi'(\xi) = 0 \quad \forall \pi' \in S.
$$

As $\mathcal{S}(G,M)$ is dense in $C^* (G,M)$ we can pick $f = f^{*}\in \mathcal{S}(G,M)$ with

$$
\| \pi (f) \|_{\mathrm{op}} = 1; \quad \sup_{\pi^{\prime}\in S}\| \pi^{\prime}(f)\|_{\mathrm{op}}\leq \frac{1}{2}.
$$

Choose $\varphi = \bar{\varphi} \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ such that $\varphi \equiv 0$ on a neighbourhood of $[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}]$ and such that $\varphi\{\pi(f)\} \neq 0$.
Choose also $\Psi = \bar{\Psi} \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ with $\Psi \equiv 0$ on a neighbourhood of $[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}]$ and such that $\Psi \cdot \varphi = \varphi$.

Then for $b := \Psi\{f\}$ and $a := \varphi\{f\} \in \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ (by 2.6) we have

$$
b * a = a, \quad h(\{b\}) \supset S \quad \text{and} \quad \pi(a) \neq 0.
$$

It is now easy to prove the following theorem (see also [9]):

**Theorem.** Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group.
Let $S$ be a closed subset of $(G, M)^\wedge$.
The minimal two-sided ideal $j(S)$ of $C^*(G, M)$ with $h(j(S)) = S$ is generated (as an ideal) by Schwartz-functions.
Furthermore for $\pi \notin S$, for any open neighbourhood $U$ of $\pi$ there exists $f$ in $j(S) \cap \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ such that $\pi(f) \neq 0$ and such that $\operatorname{supp} \hat{f} \subset U$ i.e. $\pi'(f) = 0 \ \forall \pi' \notin U$.

2.9. Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.

Let us denote for $d \in \mathbb{N}$ the subset

$$
M \times \mathcal{G}_{\leq d}^* \quad (\text{resp. } M \times \mathcal{G}_{&gt;d}^*)
$$

of $M \times \mathcal{G}^*$ consisting of all the elements

$$
(m, l) \quad \text{of} \quad M \times \mathcal{G}^*
$$

such that

$$
\dim \operatorname{Ad}^*(G_m) \cdot l \leq d \quad (\text{resp. } \dim \operatorname{Ad}^*(G_m) \cdot l &gt; d).
$$

It is easy to see that $M \times \mathcal{G}_{\leq d}^*$ is a closed $G$-invariant subset of $M \times \mathcal{G}^*$ and that $M \times \mathcal{G}_{&gt;d}^*$ is open and $G$-invariant.
Hence $M \times \mathcal{G}_{\leq d}^*$ (resp.
$M \times \mathcal{G}_{&gt;d}^*$) defines a closed (resp.
open) subset $(G, M)_{\leq d}^*$ (resp.
$(G, M)_{&gt;d}^*$) of $(G, M)^\wedge$.

Let

$$
\begin{aligned}
(G, M)_d^{\wedge} &amp;= (G, M)_{\leq d}^{\wedge} \cap (G, M)_{&gt;d-2}^{\wedge} \\
&amp;= \left\{ \left( \pi \circ p_m \right) \mid \pi \in G_m^{\wedge}, \dim K(\pi) = d \right\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

We define now our ideals $BT_d$ and $BR_d$.

$$
BT_d = BT_d(G, M) = \left\{ \xi \in C^*(G, M) \mid \sup_{\rho \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}} \operatorname{tr}|\rho(\xi)| &lt; \infty \right\},
$$

$$
BR_d = BR(G, M) = \left\{ \xi \in C^*(G, M) \mid \sup_{\rho \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}} \operatorname{rank} \rho(\xi) &lt; \infty \right\}.
$$

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

$BR_d \subset BT_d$ are two-sided ideals in $C^*(G, M)$.
Obviously $h(BR_d) = h(BT_d)$ ($\subset (G, M)^\wedge$).
We know from Theorem 2.8 that $BR_d \cap \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ (resp.
$BT_d \cap \mathcal{S}(G, M)$) is dense in $BR_d$ (resp.
in $BT_d$).
It is our aim to determine the hull $h_d$ of $BT_d$.

2.10. For the proof of Theorem 5.3 we need also the abelian algebra $C_c(M \times \mathcal{G})$ where we look at $\mathcal{G}$ as an (abelian) vector group.

The unitary dual of $C^*(\mathcal{G}, M)$ is of course $M \times \mathcal{G}^*$.
We define the $C^*$-integral $i_{(m,l)}$ on $C^*(\mathcal{G}, M)$ ($m \in M, l \in \mathcal{G}^*$) in the following way.

For $f \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$ we write

$$
i_{(m, l)}(f) := \operatorname{tr} \pi(l) (p_m(f)): \tag{2.10.1}
$$

where $\pi(l) \in \hat{G}_m$ is the element of $\hat{G}_m$ corresponding to $l$.
Let us give a more concrete description of $i_{(m,l)}$.
We choose a Jordan-Hölder basis $\mathcal{V}$ of $\mathcal{G}_m^*$.
Let $\mathcal{B} = \{b_1, \ldots, b_n\} \subset \mathcal{G}$ be the dual basis of $\mathcal{V}$.
If $I$ denotes the index set of $l$ (for the basis $\mathcal{B}$) then

$$
i_{(m, l)}(f) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{\frac{3}{2} \dim 0}} \frac{1}{Q_I(0)^{\frac{1}{2}}} \int_{\mathbb{R}^{\dim 0}} \hat{f}(P(z, 0)) \, dz
$$

where $Q_I(0) = \det(\langle l, [b_i, b_j]_m \rangle_{i,j \in I})$; $0 = G_m \cdot l$ and where $P = P^\nu$ is the canonical polynomial map associated with $\nu$ and 0 (see 4.1).

Let us denote by $I_*$ the ideal in $C^*(\mathcal{G}, M)$ defined by

$$
I_{d^1} = \left\{ \xi \in C^*(\mathcal{G}, M) \mid \sup_{(m, l) \in M \times \mathcal{G}_h^*} i_{(m, l)} p_m(\xi)_*^* p_m(\xi) &lt; \infty \right\}.
$$

## 3. A subset of $h(BT_d)$

In this section we show that $h_{d^1} = h(BT_d)$ contains the intersection of $(G, M)_{\leq d-2}^{\wedge}$ with the closure of $(G, M)_d^{\wedge}$, i.e.

$$
h_d \supset (G, M)_{\leq d-2}^{\wedge} \cap \overline{(G, M)_d^{\wedge}}, \quad d \in 2\mathbb{N}.
$$

3.1. Let $G$ be a nilpotent Lie group and $\mathcal{G}$ its Lie algebra.
Let $O$ be a $G$-orbit in $\mathcal{G}^*$, $l \in O$ and $\pi \in \hat{G}$ the corresponding irreducible representation.
We choose now a polarization $\mathcal{A}$ of $l$, a Haar-measure $dh$ on $H = \exp \mathcal{A}$, a $G$-invariant measure $d \dot{x}$ on $G/H$ such that the Haar-measure $dx$ on $G$ is given by

$$
dx = dh \, d\dot{x}.
$$

Then for every $f \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})$

$$
\operatorname{tr} \pi(f \circ \log) = \int_{G/H} f_\pi(x, x) \, d\dot{x}
$$

where:

$$
f_\pi(x, y) = \int_H f \circ \log(x \cdot h \cdot y^{-1}) \, e^{-i \langle l, \log h \rangle} \, dh; \quad x, y \in G.
$$

Let us mention that $f_\pi'' \in "S(G/H \times G/H)$ (see [6]).

176
J.
Ludwig et al.

3.2. Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
Let $d \in 2\mathbb{N}$.

Let $\rho_{k} = \pi_{k} \circ p_{m_{k}}$ be a sequence in $(G, M)_{d}^{\wedge}$ which converges to $\rho_{\infty} = \pi_{\infty} \circ p_{m_{\infty}} \in (G, M)_{d'}^{\wedge}$.
Then necessarily $d' \leq d$.
Let us fix an euclidean norm on $\mathcal{G}$.
We choose $l_{\infty} \in O_{\infty} = K(\pi_{\infty})$.
There exists for every $k$, $l_{k} \in O_{k} = (\pi_{k})$ so that

$$
l_{\infty} = \lim_{k} l_{k} \quad (\text{by 2.3}).
$$

We select for $l_{k}$ a polarization $\mathcal{A}_{k}$ in $\mathcal{G}_{m_k}$ and then we choose for every $k$ a Malcev basis $\mathcal{B}^k = \{b_1^k,\dots ,b_n^k\}$ of $\mathcal{G}_{m_k}$ through $\mathcal{A}_k$ i.e. $\{b_{n + 1 - r}^k,\ldots ,b_n^k\}$ is a basis of $\mathcal{A}_k$.
Applying the Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization process we can assume that $\mathcal{B}^k$ is orthonormal for every $k$ and (passing to a subsequence) that

$$
\lim_{k} b_{j}^{k} = b_{j}^{\infty}; \quad j = 1, \dots , n
$$

exists.
Hence $\mathcal{B}^{\infty} = \{b_1^{\infty},\ldots ,b_n^{\infty}\}$ is a Malcev basis of $\mathcal{G}_{m_{\infty}}$, which passes through the subalgebra $\mathcal{A}_{\infty} = \sum_{j = 1}^{r}\mathbb{R}b_{n + 1 - j}$ of $\mathcal{G}_{m_{\infty}}$.
$\mathcal{A}_{\infty}$ is evidently subordinated to $l_{\infty}$.

For $v \in \mathbb{N}U(\infty)$ we define Haar-measures $d_v h$ on $H_v = \exp \mathcal{A}_v$ and $G$-invariant measures $d_v \dot{x}$ on $G_v / H_v$ by the formulas:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{H_{v}} \mathrm{g}(h) d_{v} h: = \int_{\mathbb{R}^{r}} \mathrm{g}\left(\exp t_{1} b_{n + 1 - r}^{v} \dots \exp t_{r} b_{n}^{v}\right) dt_{1} \dots dt_{r}; \quad \mathrm{g} \in C_{c}(H_{v}), \\
\int_{G_{v} / H_{v}} \varphi(\dot{x}) d_{v} \dot{x}: = \int_{\mathbb{R}^{n - r}} \mathrm{g}\left(\exp t_{1} b_{1}^{v} \dots \exp t_{n - r} b_{n - r}^{v}\right) dt_{1} \dots dt_{n - r}; \quad \varphi \in C_{c}(G_{v} / H).
\end{array}
$$

The following proposition can be proved by standard techniques.

**Proposition.** Let $\{x_{k}\}, \{y_{k}\}$ be sequences in $G$ converging to $x_{\infty}$ resp.
to $y_{\infty}$.

For every $f \in L_1(G, M) * C_c(G, M)$

$$
\tilde{\mathrm{g}}_{k}(x_{k}, y_{k}): = \int_{H_{h}} f\left(x_{k \stackrel{\star}{m_{h}}} h_{k \stackrel{\star}{m_{h}}} y_{k}^{-1}\right) e^{-i\langle l_{h}, \log h_{h}\rangle} d_{k} h_{k}
$$

converges to

$$
\tilde{\mathrm{g}}_{\infty}(x_{\infty}, y_{\infty}): = \int_{H_{\infty}} f\left(x_{\infty \stackrel{\star}{m_{\infty}}} h_{\infty \stackrel{\star}{m_{\infty}}} y_{\infty}^{-1}\right) e^{-i\langle l_{\infty}, \log h_{\infty}\rangle} d_{\infty} h_{\infty}.
$$

3.3. The next lemma is well known to the specialists.
The proof is left to the reader.

**Lemma.** Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a nilpotent Lie algebra, $G = \exp \mathcal{G}$ its Lie group and let $l \in \mathcal{G}^*$.
Furthermore let $\mathcal{A} \subset \mathcal{G}$ be a subalgebra with $\langle l, [\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{A}] \rangle = 0$ and let $H = \exp \mathcal{A}$.

Let $dh$ and $d\mu$ be the Haar-measures on $H$ resp.
on $G/H$.
If $\mathcal{A}$ is not a polarization of $l$ then for any $f \in L^{1}(G)$:

$$
\operatorname{tr} \pi(l, \mathcal{A})(f^{*} * f) = \int_{G / H} (f^{*} * f)_{\pi(l, \mathcal{A})}(x, x) d\mu(\dot{x}) &lt; \infty \Leftrightarrow f \in \ker \pi(l, \mathcal{A})
$$

where $\pi(l, \mathcal{A}) = \mathrm{ind}_H^G \chi_l$ and where $\chi_l$ is the unitary character of $H$ defined by $d\chi_l = l|_{\mathcal{A}}$.

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

3.4. Theorem.
Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
Let $d \in 2\mathbb{N}$.
Then

$$
h(BR_d) = h(BT_d) \supset (G, M)_{\leq d-2} \cap (\overline{G, M})_d^{\wedge}.
$$

Proof.
Let $\rho = \pi \circ p_m \in (G, M)_{\leq d-2} \cap (\overline{G, M})_d^{\wedge}$.

There exists a sequence $\rho_k$ in $(G, M)_k^{\wedge}$ which converges to $\rho = 1\rho_{\infty}$.
We may assume that we are in the situation of Proposition 3.2. For $f \in \mathcal{S}(G, M) * C_c(G, M)$ it follows from 3.2 and 3.1 that

$$
\operatorname{tr} \pi(p_m(f^* * f)) \leq C &lt; \infty
$$

whenever $\operatorname{tr} \rho_k(f^* * f) \leq C$ for all $k$.

Hence if we take $f$ in the dense subspace

$$
\mathcal{S}(G, M) * C_c(G, M) \cap BT_k
$$

of $BT_k$, the fact that $\dim O(\pi) \leq d - 2$ and 3.3 imply that

$$
p_m(f) \in \ker \pi(l, \mathcal{L}_\infty)
$$

where $l$ is an element in $K(\pi)$ and $\mathcal{L}_\infty$ the limit subalgebra of Proposition 3.2 subordinated to $l$.
But it is well-known that $\ker \pi(l, \mathcal{L}_\infty) \subset \ker \pi$ (see [8]).
Hence $\rho \in h(BT_k)$.
Q.e.d.

3.5. Let us now return to the notations of 2.10. For $f \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$, for $l \in \mathcal{G}^*$ and for a polarization $\mathcal{L} \subset \mathcal{G}_m$ of $l$ we can form the function

$$
f_{l, \mathcal{L}}(m, x) = \int_{\mathcal{L}} f(m, \operatorname{expad} x(h)) e^{-i\langle l, h \rangle} dh.
$$

Then

$$
i_{(m, l)}(f) = \int_{\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{L}} f_{l, \mathcal{L}}(m, x) d\dot{x}
$$

if $\mathcal{L}$ is a polarization (for an appropriate measure $d\dot{x}$ on $\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{L}$).

It is easy to prove results similar to those in 3.2 for $f_{l, \mathcal{L}}$ and we obtain the following

Proposition.
Let $(\mathcal{G}, M)$ be a variable Lie algebra.
For every $f \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$ the function

$$
(m, l) \rightarrow i_{(m, l)}(f^* * f) \geq 0
$$

on $M \times \mathcal{G}^*$ is lower-semi-continuous.

## 4. A Jacobian

In this section we compute the Jacobian of a certain polynomial mapping and we use this result to estimate $\sup_{(m,l) \in (M \times \mathcal{G}^*)_d} i_{m,l}(f^* * f)$ for special elements of $\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$.

This is the most important step in the proof of 5.2 (and 5.3).

178
J.
Ludwig et al.

4.1. We fix here some notations used in Pukanszky's parametrization of unipotent orbits (see [14]).

Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a Lie algebra of nilpotent endomorphisms on a finite dimensional real vectorspace $V$, let $G = \exp \mathcal{G}$ be the corresponding unipotent group.
Let $\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{G}_0 \supset \ldots \supset \mathcal{G}_r = (0)$ be a Jordan-Hölder sequence of $\mathcal{G}$ and $B = \{b_1, \ldots, b_r\} \subset 1 \leq i \leq r$, a Jordan-Hölder basis of $\mathcal{G}$ with $b_i \ni \mathcal{G}_{i-1} \setminus \mathcal{G}_i$.
We fix also $V = V_0 \supset \ldots \supset V_n = (0)$ a Jordan-Hölder sequence of $V$ relative to $\mathcal{G}$ and $\mathcal{V} = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\}$ with $v_i \in V_{i-1} \setminus V_i$ a Jordan-Hölder basis.
Let $W$ be the dual space of $V$ and $\mathcal{W} = \{w_1, \ldots, w_n\}$ be the dual basis of $\mathcal{V}$ in $W$.
For $v \in V$ we define the set $\tilde{g}_j(v) = \{x \in \mathcal{G} \mid x \cdot v \in V_j\}$ for $0 \leq j \leq n$.
We get an index set $I(v)$ of $v$ in the following way.

$$
I(v) = \{i_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; i_d\} \subset \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

with

$$
i \in I(v) \Leftrightarrow \tilde{g}_{i-1}(v) \not\cong\tilde{g}_i(v).
$$

For $v \in V$ we consider the radical $\mathcal{G}(v) = \{x \in \mathcal{G} \mid x \cdot v = 0\}$ and we define

$$
\mathcal{G}_j(v) := \mathcal{G}_j + \mathcal{G}(v), \quad 0 \leq j \leq r.
$$

This gives us a second index set $J(v) = \{j_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; j_d\} \subset \{1, \ldots, r\}$

$$
j \subset J(v) \Leftrightarrow \mathcal{G}_{j+1}(v) \subsetneq \mathcal{G}_j(v).
$$

Thus for every $v \in V$ we get a pair of indices $(I(v), J(v))$.
The set $E = \{(I(v), J(v)) \mid v \in V\}$ is obviously finite.
Let for $(I, J) \in E$

$$
V_{I,J} = \{v \in V \mid I(v) = I, J(v) = J\}.
$$

The set $V_{I,J}$ is $G$-invariant and for $v \in V_{I,J}$

$$
\dim G \cdot v = \# I = \# J.
$$

For a fixed pair $(I, J) \in E$ let us set

$$
d(v) = d = \# I = \# J.
$$

There exists a system of $n$ functions $\{P_j^{\mathcal{V}} \mid 1 \leq j \leq n\}$ on $\mathbb{R}^d \times V_{I,J}$ with the following properties:

a) for any fixed $v \in V_{I,J}$, $P_j^{\mathcal{V}}(x,v)$ is a polynomial in $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_d)$ and the coefficients are rational functionals on $V_{I,J}$, $(j = 1, \ldots, n)$,

b) $P_{i_k}^{\mathcal{V}}(x,v) = x_k$ for any $i_k \in I$, $v \in V_{I,J}$,

c) if $1 \leq i \leq n$ and $i_k \leq i &lt; i_{k+1}$ then $P_i^{\mathcal{V}}(x, v)$ depends on $x_1, \ldots, x_k$ only,

d) for any $v \in V_{I,J}$ we have

$$
G \cdot v = \left\{P^{\mathcal{V}}(z, v) := \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j^{\mathcal{V}}(v, z) v_j \mid z \in \mathbb{R}^d \right\}.
$$

For any $v \in V$ the functions $\{P_i^{\mathcal{V}}(x,v) \mid 1 \leq i \leq n\}$ are uniquely determined by the condition that they are polynomials in $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ satisfying the conditions b), c) and d).

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

Let us introduce also the polynomial $Q_{I,J}$ on $V((I,J)\in E)$.

$$
Q_{I,J}(v):=\det\left(\langle w_{i}, b_{j} \cdot v \rangle\right)_{i\in I,j\in J}.
$$

$Q_{I,J}$ is homogeneous and $G$-invariant on $V_{I,J}$.
Furthermore for any $(I,J)\in E$ there exists a certain subset $E_{I,J}\subset E$ so that

$$
V_{I,J} = \{v \in V \mid Q_{I',J'}(v) = 0, (I',J') \in E_{I,J}; Q_{I,J}(v) \neq 0\}.
$$

See [14] for details and proofs.

## 4.2. Let us use the notations of 4.1.

Let $(I,J)\in E$ and let $d = \# I = \# J$.
Choose now any subset $C = (c_{1},\ldots ,c_{k})$ of $d$ elements in $\{1,\dots ,n\}$.

This allows us to define for $v \in V_{I,J}$ a polynomial map

$$
P_{C}^{\mathcal{V}}(v) \colon \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}
$$

by setting

$$
P_{C}^{\mathcal{V}}(v)(z):= (P_{c_{1}}^{\mathcal{V}}(z, v), \dots, P_{c_{d}}^{\mathcal{V}}(z, v)); \quad z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}
$$

(of course if $C = I$ then $P_{C}^{\mathcal{V}}(v) = P_{I}^{\mathcal{V}}(v) = \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R}^{d}}$).

## Lemma.

$(\star)$ $\operatorname{Jac}(P_C^{\mathcal{V}}(v)(z)) = \det \left(\frac{\partial}{\partial z_i} P_J^{\mathcal{V}}(v)(z)\right)_{i=1,\dots,d}$

$$
= \left[ \det \left(\langle w_{i}, b_{j} \cdot P^{\mathcal{V}}(z, v) \rangle\right)_{i\in C_j} \right] \left[ \det \left(\langle w_{i}, b_{j} v \rangle\right)_{i\in I_j} \right]^{-1}.
$$

**Proof.** The proof proceeds by induction on $\dim g^+ \dim V$.
If $\dim V + \dim g = 0$ or 1, then everything is clear.
As $\mathcal{V}$ is a Jordan-Hölder basis, the vector $w_1$ of $V^* = W$ is annihilated by the adjoint action of $g$ on $V$ and $g \cdot w_2 \subset \mathbb{R}w_1$.
Especially $W_1 = \mathbb{R}w_1 + \mathbb{R}w_2$ is a $g$-invariant subspace of $W$.
Let us suppose first that $\langle w_2, g \cdot v \rangle = (0)$.
Then $0 \neq \{v\}^\perp \cap W_1$ is $g$-invariant and contains a 1-dimensional $g$-invariant subspace $W_0$.
$V_0 \coloneqq W_0^\perp \subset V$ is also $g$-invariant and $G \cdot v \subset V_0$.

If the number 1 or the number 2 is an element of $C$, then as $P_{i}^{\mathcal{V}}(z,v) = \mathrm{const}$ and as

$$
\langle w_{i}, b_{j} \cdot P^{\mathcal{V}}(z, v) \rangle = - \langle b_{j} \cdot w_{i}, P^{\mathcal{V}}(z, v) \rangle \subset \langle g \cdot W_{1}, G \cdot v \rangle = (0)
$$

for $j = 1, \dots, n; i = 1, 2$ the left hand-side and the right-hand-side of $(\star)$ is zero.

Hence we may suppose that 1 and 2 are not contained in $C$.
We choose a vector $\tilde{v}_1 \neq 0$ in $(\mathbb{R}v_1 + \mathbb{R}v_2) \cap V_0$.
Let $\vec{\mathcal{V}} = \{\tilde{v}_1, \dots, \tilde{v}_{n-1}\}$ where $\tilde{v}_2 = v_3, \dots, \tilde{v}_{n-1} = v_n$.
$\vec{\mathcal{V}}$ is a Jordan-Hölder basis of the $g$-module $V_0$.
If we put $\tilde{w}_{j-1} = w_j \bmod W_0, j = 3, \dots, n$, and if we choose $\tilde{w}_1$ in $W_1$ with $\langle \tilde{w}_1, \tilde{v}_1 \rangle = 1$, then $\vec{\mathcal{W}} = \{\tilde{w}_1, \dots, \tilde{w}_{n-1}\}$ is the dual basis of $V_0$ in $V_0^* \cong V^*/W_0$.

Put $\tilde{I} = \{i - 1 \mid i \in I\}$, $\tilde{C} = \{c - 1 \mid c \in C\}$, $\tilde{J} = J$.

(4.2.1) Then $(\tilde{I},\tilde{J})$ is the index set of $v$ (relative to $(\tilde{V},\tilde{\mathcal{B}})$).
Furthermore

$$
P_{\tilde{C}}^{\vec{\mathcal{V}}}(v)(z) = P_{C}^{\mathcal{V}}(v)(z), \quad z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}. \tag{4.2.2}
$$

180
J.
Ludwig et al.

The induction hypothesis and (4.2.1), (4.2.2) imply $(\ast)$.
We must study now the case $\langle w_2, \varphi \cdot v \rangle \neq (0)$.
Then $i_1 = 2$ and there exists $0 \neq \varphi$ in $\varphi^*$ such that

$$
x \cdot w_2 = \varphi(x) w_1; \quad x \in \varphi.
$$

Let $j_0$ be the smallest number with $\varphi(bj_0) \neq 0$.
Then $j_0 \in J$.
Let us first assume that $j_0 = 1$; $\varphi_1 = \sum_{i=2}^{r} \mathbb{R}b_i$ is an ideal in $\varphi$ of codimension one and $\varphi_1 = \ker \varphi$.

Let $G_1 = \exp \varphi_1 \subset G$.
For any $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, the $G$-orbit $G \cdot v$ of $v$ contains at least one element $v_\lambda = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i b_i$, whose second component $x_2$ equals $\lambda$.
The $G_1$-orbit of $v_\lambda$ is the subset

$$
\begin{array}{l}
G_1 \cdot v_\lambda = \left\{ P^\psi(z, v) \mid z \in \mathbb{R}^d, z_1 = \lambda \right\} \\
= \left\{ P^\psi((\lambda, \bar{z})), v_\lambda \right\} \mid \bar{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d-1}.
\end{array}
$$

Hence for the canonical polynomials $\{P_j^{\psi}(\cdot, v_\lambda)\}_{j=1}^n$ which describe the $G_1$-orbit of $v_\lambda$, we have

$$
P_j^\psi((\lambda, \bar{z}), v) = P_j^{\psi}(\bar{z}, v_\lambda), \quad \bar{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d-1}. \tag{4.2.3}
$$

Define the map $Q\colon \mathbb{R}^d \to G \cdot v \subset V$ by:

$$
Q(z_1, \bar{z}) = \exp(\alpha^{-1} z_1 b_1) \cdot P^\psi((0, \bar{z}), v); \quad \bar{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d-1}, \; z_1 \in \mathbb{R},
$$

where $\alpha = \langle w_2, b_1 \cdot v \rangle$.

Let us write:

$$
Q(z_1, \bar{z}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_j(z_1, \bar{z}) v_j.
$$

At the jumping points $i_s$ of $v$ we have

$$
r_s \dot{=} Q_{i_s}(z_1, \dots, z_k) = z_s + Q_{i_s}'(z_1, \dots, z_{s-1})
$$

where $Q_{i_s}'$ is a polynomial which depends on $z_1, \ldots, z_{s-1}$ only.
Let us express $z$ in terms of $r = (r_1, \ldots, r_d)$.
We get

$$
z_i = r_i + R_i'(r_1, \dots, r_{i-1}) = R_i(r_1, \dots, r_{i-1})
$$

where $R_j'$ is a polynomial which depends on $r_1, \ldots, r_{i-1}$ only.
Furthermore $z_1 = r_1$ (because of the choice of $\alpha$).

If we set $R = (R_2, \ldots, R_k)$ then

$$
Q(z_1, R(z_1, \bar{z})) = P^\psi((z_1, \bar{z}), v); \quad (z_1, \bar{z}) \in \mathbb{R}^d. \tag{4.2.4}
$$

We also have:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial z_1} Q(z_1, \bar{z}) = \alpha^{-1} b_1 \cdot Q(z_1, \bar{z}).
$$

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

181

(4.2.5) Hence

$$
\frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {1}} Q _ {j} (z _ {1}, \bar {z}) = \alpha^ {- 1} \langle w _ {j}, b _ {1} \cdot Q (z _ {1}, \bar {z}) \rangle ; \quad j = 1, \dots , n.
$$

Thus:

$\operatorname{Jac} P_C^\nu(v)(z)$ (by 4.2.3, 4.2.4, 4.2.5)

$$
\begin{array}{l} = \det  \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {1}} Q _ {c _ {1}} (z _ {1}, R (z _ {1}, \bar {z})) \dots \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {d}} Q _ {c _ {1}} (z _ {1}, R (z _ {1}, \bar {z})) \\ \vdots &amp; \vdots \\ \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {1}} Q _ {c _ {d}} (z _ {1}, R (z _ {1}, \bar {z})) \dots \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {d}} Q _ {c _ {d}} (z _ {1}, R (z _ {1}, \bar {z})) \end{array} \right] \\ = \frac {1}{\alpha} \det  \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} \langle w _ {c _ {1}}, b _ {1} P ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) \rangle &amp; \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {2}} P _ {c _ {1}} ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) &amp; \dots \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {d}} P _ {c _ {1}} ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) \\ \vdots &amp; \vdots &amp; \vdots \\ \langle w _ {c _ {d}}, b _ {1} P ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) \rangle &amp; \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {2}} P _ {c _ {d}} ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) &amp; \dots \frac {\partial}{\partial z _ {d}} P _ {c _ {d}} ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) \end{array} \right] \\ = \frac {1}{\langle w _ {2} , b _ {1} \cdot v \rangle} \cdot \left\{\sum_ {s = 1} ^ {d} (- 1) ^ {s + 1} \langle w _ {c _ {s}}, b _ {1} P ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v) \rangle \cdot \operatorname {J a c} P _ {C _ {s}} ^ {\nu} (\bar {z}, v _ {z _ {1}}) \right\} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\widetilde{C}_s = (c_1, \ldots, c_{s-1}, c_{s+1}, \ldots, c_d)$ (by the induction hypotheses applied to $\mathcal{G}_1$ and $V$)

$$
\begin{array}{l} = \frac {1}{\langle w _ {2} , b _ {1} \cdot v \rangle} \cdot \left\{\sum_ {s = 1} ^ {d} (- 1) ^ {s} \langle w _ {c _ {s}}, b _ {1} \cdot P ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v _ {c _ {s}}) \rangle \right. \\ \cdot \left[ \det  \left(\langle w _ {i}, b _ {j} P ^ {\nu} ((z _ {1}, \bar {z}), v)\right) \right\rangle_ {\substack {i \in C _ {s} \\ j \in (j _ {2}, \dots , j _ {d} ]}} \\ \cdot \left[ \det  \left(\langle w _ {i}, b _ {j} v _ {z _ {1}} \rangle\right) _ {\substack {i \in (i _ {2}, \ldots , i _ {d} ] \\ j \in (j _ {2}, \ldots , j _ {d} ]}} \right] ^ {- 1} \Bigg \}. \\ \end{array}
$$

(4.3.3) $= \left[\det \left(\langle w_{i},b_{j}P^{\nu}((z_{1},\bar{z}),v)\rangle\right)_{\substack{i\in I\\ j\in J}}\right]\cdot Q_{I,J}(v)^{-1}.$

We return now to the general case where $j_0$, the smallest number with $\varphi(b_{j_0}) \neq 0$ is different from 1.

Let

$$
b _ {j} ^ {\prime} = b _ {j - 1} - \varphi \left(b _ {j - 1}\right) \varphi \left(b _ {j _ {0}}\right) ^ {- 1} b _ {j _ {0}}; \quad j = j _ {0} - 1, \dots , 2,
$$

$$
b _ {j} ^ {\prime} = b _ {j}; \quad j = r, \dots , j _ {0} + 1,
$$

$$
b _ {1} ^ {\prime} = b _ {j _ {0}}.
$$

Then $\mathcal{B}_1' = \{b_2', \ldots, b_r'\}$ is a Jordan-Hölder basis of $\mathcal{G}_1 = \ker \varphi$ and $\mathcal{B}' = \{b_1', \ldots, b_r'\}$ is a Jordan-Hölder basis of $\mathcal{G}$.
The index set for $v$ relative to $(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{B}')$ is the set $(I, J')$ where $J' = \{j_1', \ldots, j_d'\}$ with $j_1' = 1$, $j_i' = j_{i-1}$, $i = 2, \ldots, k_0$ ($j_{k_0} = j_0$) and $j_i' = j_i$; $i = k_0 + 1, \ldots, d$.

(4.3.3) applied to $(I, J')$ and an easy computation then imply $(\ast)$ Q.e.d.

182
J.
Ludwig et al.

4.4. Let now $V$ be the dual space $\mathcal{G}^*$ of a nilpotent Lie algebra $\mathcal{G}$.
Let $\mathcal{V} = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\}$ be a Jordan-Hölder basis of $\mathcal{G}^*, \mathcal{B}' = \{b_j'\}_{j=1}^n$ the dual basis of $\mathcal{V}$ in $\mathcal{G}$.

Then $\{b_j\}_{j=1}^n$ (where $b_j = b_{n=j}'$) is a Jordan-Hölder basis of $\mathcal{G}$ and for every $l \in \mathcal{G}^*$ the index sets $I(l)$ and $(J(l))$ are equal.

**Proposition.** Let $l \in \mathcal{G}^*$ and $I(l) = J(l) = I$ its index set relative to $\mathcal{V}$ and $\mathcal{B}$.

For any subset $C = \{c_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; c_d\}$ of $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ ($d = \dim G \cdot l$) we have

$| \text{Jac } P_C^\mathcal{V}(l)(z) | = Q_I(l)^{-\frac{1}{2}} |\det(\langle P^\mathcal{V}(z, l), [b_i, b_j] \rangle)_{i, j \in C}|^\frac{1}{2}.$

**Proof.** As $\{b_{j_1}, \ldots, b_{j_d}\}$ is linearly independent modulo the radical $\mathcal{G}(p)$ of $p = P^\mathcal{V}(z, l) \in G \cdot l$ ($j_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; j_d = I$) we may write

$$
b_{c_t} = \sum_{s=1}^d \alpha_{is}(z) b_{i_s} \quad \text{modulo } \mathcal{G}(p).
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det \left( \langle P^\mathcal{V}(z, l), [b_i, b_j] \rangle_{i, j \in C} \right) &amp;= \left[ \det(\alpha_{is}(z))_{1 \leq i, s \leq d} \right]^2 Q_I(l) \tag{4.4.1}
\end{aligned}
$$

and so

$$
\begin{aligned}
| \text{Jac } P_C^\mathcal{V}(l)(z) | &amp;= Q_I(l)^{-1} |\det(\langle b_j P^\mathcal{V}(z, l), b_i \rangle_{i \in C} | \quad \text{(by 4.3)} \\
&amp;= Q_I(l)^{-1} |\det(\alpha_{is}(z)_{1 \leq i, s \leq d}) | Q_I(l) \\
&amp;= Q_I(l)^{-\frac{1}{2}} |\det(\langle P^\mathcal{V}(z, l), [b_i, b_j] \rangle_{i, j \in C}) | \quad \text{(by 4.4.1)}. \quad \text{Q.e.d.}
\end{aligned}
$$

4.5. An estimation of $\sup \{i_{(m,l)}(f^* * f) \mid (m,l) \in M \times \mathcal{G}_d^*\}$

Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
Let $d \in 2\mathbb{N}$.

(4.5.1) Denote by $j_d$ the ideal in $\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$ consisting of all the elements $f \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$ such that the function $(m, l) \to p_m(f)^\wedge$ has compact support such that this support is disjoint from $M \times \mathcal{G}_{&lt;d}^*$.

We will show that $i_d(f) := \sup \{i_{m,l}(f^* * f) \mid (m,l) \in M \times \mathcal{G}_d^*\} &lt; \infty$ for $f \in j(d)$.

We begin with a general observation.

Let $P = (p_1, \ldots, p_d)$ be a polynomial map from $\mathbb{R}^d$ into $\mathbb{R}^d$.
Denote by $\operatorname{Reg}(P)$ the open subset

$$
\operatorname{Reg}(P) := \{z \in \mathbb{R}^k \mid \operatorname{Jac} P(z) \neq 0\} = \{z \in \mathbb{R}^k \mid P \text{ is regular in } z\}
$$

for every $z \in \operatorname{Reg}(P)$ there exists an open neighbourhood $U$ of $z$ such that $P|_U \to P(U) = :V$ is a diffeomorphism.
Hence for $u \in \mathbb{R}^d$ the subset

$$
I_P(u) := P^{-1}(\{u\}) \cap \operatorname{Reg}(P) \quad \text{is discrete}
$$

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

and every element $v \in I_P(u)$ corresponds to a zero dimensional irreducible component of the algebraic variety

$$
\mathcal {V} = \{v \in \mathbb {R} ^ {k} \mid P _ {i} (v) - u _ {i} = 0, i = 1, \dots , d \}.
$$

It follows then from [5] that there exists a number $N$ depending only on the total degree of $P$ and on $d$ such that the sets $I_P(u)$ $(u\in \mathbb{R}^k)$ contain at most $N$ elements, i.e. $\# I_P(u)\leq N$.

Let us consider the polynomial maps $P_C^\nu(v)$ of 4.2. The total degree of $P_C^\nu(v)$ depends ultimately on $\dim \mathcal{G}$, hence the numbers

$$
N _ {d, c, n ^ {1}} = \max  \left\{\# \left(I _ {P _ {C} ^ {\nu} (v)} (u)\right) \mid v \in \mathcal {G} _ {d} ^ {*}, u \in \mathbb {R} _ {1} ^ {d} \# C = d, \dim \mathcal {G} \leq N \right.
$$

and

$$
N ^ {1} = N _ {d, n ^ {1}} = \sup  _ {c} N _ {d, c, n}
$$

exist.

**4.6. Proposition.** Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
Let $d \in 2\mathbb{N}$.
For every $f \in j_d$ (see (4.5.1)) we have

$$
i _ {d} (f) = \sup  \left\{i _ {m, l} \left(f ^ {*} * f\right) \mid (m, l) \in M \times \mathcal {G} _ {d} ^ {*} \right\} &lt;   \infty .
$$

_Proof._ Look first at a single nilpotent Lie algebra $\mathcal{G}$ with Lie group $G$.
Let $\mathcal{V}$ be a Jordan-Hölder basis of $\mathcal{G}^*$, $\mathcal{B}$ its dual basis as in 4.4. Let $C \subset \{1, \ldots, n\}$ be a subset of $d$ elements.

Let $h \in C_c(\mathcal{G}^*)$ with $\operatorname{supp} h \subset \{Q_C^\nu &gt; 0\}$.

We shall now estimate

$$
\sup  _ {l \in \mathcal {G} _ {d} ^ {*}} Q _ {I (l)} ^ {\nu} (l) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \int_ {\mathbb {R} ^ {d}} | h (P ^ {\nu} (z, l)) | d z.
$$

Let $0$ be a $G$-orbit in $\mathcal{G}_d^*$.
We may assume that $0 \cap \{Q_C^\nu &gt; 0\} \neq \emptyset$.
Let $I$ be the index of $0$.

As $h = 0$ on $\{Q_C = 0\}$ there exists a compact subset $K\subset \mathbb{R}^d$ such that $P^{\nu}(K,l)\subset \{Q_C^\nu &gt;0\}$ and such that for some $l\in 0$

$$
Q _ {I} (l) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \int_ {\mathbb {R} ^ {d}} | h (P ^ {\nu} (z, l)) | d z \leq Q _ {I} (l) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \int_ {K} | h (P ^ {\nu} (z, l)) | d z + \varepsilon .
$$

Let $\mathbb{R}^k\supset L^1 = P_C^\nu (l)(K)$.
As $P_C^\nu (l)$ is regular on $K$ (see 4.2) there exists for every $x\in L$ a connected neighbourhood $U(x)$ of $x$ in $\mathbb{R}^k$ which is contained in $P_C^\nu (l)\{\operatorname {Reg}(P_C^\nu (l))\}$ and which has the following property:

If $\{a_1, \ldots, a_r\} = I_{P_C^\nu(l)}(x)$, then for $i = 1, \ldots, r$ there exist neighbourhoods $V_i(x)$ of $a_i$ such that $V_i(x) \cap V_j(x) = \emptyset$ if $i \neq j$ and such that $P_C^\nu(l) / V_i(x)$ is a diffeomorphism onto $U(x)$.
Let us recall that $r \leq N_{d,n}$.

As $K$ is compact there exists a finite number of such neighbourhoods $V_{1}(x_{1}),\ldots ,V_{s}(x_{s})$ whose union covers $K$.

Let $W_{1} = V_{1}(x_{1})\cap K$; $W_{j} = \{V_{j}(x_{j})\backslash (U_{j^{\prime} &lt; j}V_{j^{\prime}}(x_{j^{\prime}}))\} \cap K$ and let

$$
Y _ {j} = P _ {C} ^ {\nu} (l) \left(W _ {j}\right); \quad j = 1, \dots , s. \text{ Let } \varphi_ {j ^ {1}} = P _ {C} ^ {\nu} (l) | _ {W _ {j}}.
$$

$K$ is then the disjoint union of the $W_{j}$'s.
Thus:

184
J.
Ludwig et al.

$$
\begin{array}{l}
Q_{I}(l)^{- \frac{1}{2}} \int_{\mathbb{R}^{d}} |h(P^{\gamma}(z, l))| \, dz \leq \varepsilon + Q_{I}(l)^{- \frac{1}{2}} \int_{K} |h(P^{\gamma}(z, l))| \, dz \\
= \varepsilon + \sum_{j = 1}^{s} Q_{I}(l)^{- \frac{1}{2}} \int_{W_{j}} |h(P^{\gamma}(z, l))| \, dz \\
= \varepsilon + \sum_{j = 1}^{s} Q_{I}(l)^{- \frac{1}{2}} \int_{Y_{j}} |h(P^{\gamma}(\varphi_{j}^{-1}(u), l))| \, Q_{C}(P^{\gamma}(\varphi_{j}^{-1}(u), l))^{-\frac{1}{2}} \, Q_{I}(l)^{\frac{1}{2}} \, du \\
\text{(by 4.4)} \\
= \varepsilon + \sum_{j = 1}^{s} \int_{Y_{j}} |Q_{C}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot h(P^{\gamma}(\varphi_{j}^{-1}(u), l))| \, du \\
\leq \varepsilon + \sum_{j = 1}^{s} \int_{Y_{j}} (Q_{C}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot h)_{C}^{\sim}(u) \, du \\
\end{array}
$$

(where for a function $H$ on $\mathcal{G}^*$,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
H_{C}(u)^{\sim}: = \sup_{\xi \in \mathbb{R}^{n - d}} |H(\sum_{i \notin C} u_{i} v_{i} + \sum_{j \in C} \xi_{j} v_{j})|; \, u \in \mathbb{R}^{d})) \tag{4.6.1} \\
\leq \varepsilon + N \int_{\mathbb{R}^{h}} (Q_{C}^{-\frac{1}{2}} h)_{C}^{\sim}(u) \, du \\
\end{array}
$$

(as no $u \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ can be simultaneously in more than $N_{d,n}$ different $Y_{j}$'s)

$$
= \varepsilon + N |(Q_{C}^{-\frac{1}{2}} h)_{C}^{\sim}|_{1}.
$$

(4.6.2) Hence for any $h \in C_c(\mathcal{G}^*)$ with $\operatorname{supp} h$ compact and

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\operatorname{supp} h \subset \{Q_{C}^{\gamma} &gt; 0\} \\
\sup_{l \in \mathcal{G}_{d}^{*}} Q_{I(l)}^{\gamma}(l)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \int |h(P^{\gamma}(z, l))| \, dz \leq N_{d,n} |(Q_{C}^{-\frac{1}{2}} h)_{C}^{\sim}|_{1} &lt; \infty \, (l \in \mathcal{G}_{d}^{*}).
\end{array}
$$

Let us now return to the variable nilpotent Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
The proposition will be proved if we show that the following assertion is true.

(4.6.3) Let $f \in j_d$ and let $\{(m_k, l_k)\}_k$ be a sequence in $M \times \mathcal{G}_d^*$ converging to $(m_{\infty}, l_{\infty})$.
Then

$$
\sup_{k} i_{m_{k}, l_{k}} (f^* * f) &lt; \infty.
$$

Let $g = f^* * f$.
Define $h \in C_c(M \times \mathcal{G}^*)$ by

$$
h(m, l): = p_{m}(g)^{\wedge}(l).
$$

We fix on euclidean norm on $\mathcal{G}^*$ and we choose for every $k$ a Jordan-Hölder basis $\mathcal{V}_k$ in $(\mathcal{G}_{m_k})^*$ which is orthonormal.
Passing to a subsequence we can assume that $\mathcal{V}_k$ converges to a Jordan-Hölder basis $\mathcal{V}_{\infty}$ of $(\mathcal{G}_{m_{\infty}})^*$.

Let $M' := \{m_k\}_k \cup \{m_{\infty}\} \subset M$.
$M'$ is compact.

Let $E_{d,\infty} := E_d(\mathcal{G}_{m_\infty}^*)$ be the set of all the indices belonging to elements in $(\mathcal{G}_{m_\infty}^*)_d$.

The functions $q_{C}(C \in E_{d,\infty})$

$$
q_{C}(m_{k}, l): = Q_{C}^{\gamma_{k}}(l) = \det\left(\langle l, [b_{i}^{\gamma_{k}}, b_{j}^{\gamma_{k}}] \rangle_{i, j \in C}\right)
$$

are continuous on $M' \times \mathcal{G}^*$.

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

Let $U_{C^1} = \{q_C &gt; 0\} \subset M' \times \mathcal{G}^*$; $C \in E_{d_c \infty}$.
As $U_1 = \bigcup_{C \in E_{d_c \infty}} U_C$ contains $\operatorname{supp} h \cap \{m_\infty\} \times \mathcal{G}^*$.
$U$ contains also $\operatorname{supp} h \cap \{m_k\} \times \mathcal{G}^*$ for (almost) all $k$.
Hence $\operatorname{supp} h \subset \bigcup_{C \in E_{d_c \infty}} U_C$ and there exists for every $C \in E_{d_c \infty}$ $h_C \in C_c(M' \times \mathcal{G}^*)$ with $\operatorname{supp} h_C \subset \{q_c &gt; 0\}$ so that

$$
h|_{M' \times \mathcal{G}^*} = \sum_{C \in E_{d \infty}} h_C.
$$

But then (4.6.2) implies that

$$
\sup_{(m, l) \in M' \times \mathcal{G}_d^*} Q_{I(l)}^{Y_m}(l)^{-1} \int_{\mathbb{R}^d} |h_C(P^{Y_m}(z, l))| \, dz &lt; \infty \quad \text{for every } C
$$

and so

$$
\sup_{(m_k, l_k)} i_{(m_k, l_k)}(f^* * f) &lt; \infty. \quad \text{Q.e.d.}
$$

## 5. The proof of the main theorem

We prove first a proposition needed in 5.2 where we show that the hull of $BT_d$ is contained in $(G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge}$.
It follows then easily that the hull of $BT_d(G, M)$ is the set $(G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge} \cap \overline{((G, M)_d^{\wedge})}$ (Theorem 5.3).

### 5.1. Proposition

Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}M)$.
Suppose that

$$
h(BT_d(G, M)) = (G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge} \cap \overline{((G, M)_d^{\wedge})}.
$$

Let $\rho = \pi \circ p_m \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}$.
For every $g \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}, M)$ with $p_m(g)^{\wedge}(K(\pi)) \neq 0$, there exists $f \in BT_d(G, M) \cap \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ with $\rho(f) \neq 0$ such that for $h = g^* * g \in \mathcal{S}(g, M)$

$$
\operatorname{tr} \rho'(f^* * f) \leq i_{(m', l')} (h) \quad \text{for every} \quad \rho' = \pi' \circ p_{m'} \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}, l' \in K(\pi').
$$

**Proof.** The function $(G, M)^{\wedge} \ni \rho' \to i_{\rho'}(h)$ is lower-semi-continuous (see 3.5).

As $\alpha_1 = i_{\rho}(h) &gt; 0$, there exists for $0 &lt; \alpha' &lt; \alpha$ a $G$-invariant neighbourhood $\tilde{U}$ in $M \times \mathcal{G}^*/\mathrm{Ad}^*$ so that

$$
i_{(m', l')} (h) \geq \alpha'; \quad (m', l') \in \tilde{U}.
$$

$\tilde{U}$ corresponds to a neighbourhood $\tilde{U}$ of $\rho$ in $(G, M)^{\wedge}$.
As $h(BT_d(G)) = (G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge} \cap \overline{((G, M)_d^{\wedge})}$ there exists by Theorem 2.8 $f \in \mathcal{S}(G, M) \cap BT_d(G, M)$ with $\rho(f) \neq 0$.

We can assume, as $f \in BT_d$, that

$$
\sup \left\{ \operatorname{tr} \rho'(f^* * f) \mid \rho' \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge} \right\} \leq \alpha'
$$

and that

$$
\rho'(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \rho' \notin \tilde{U}.
$$

Hence:

$$
\operatorname{tr} \rho'(f^* * f) \leq \alpha' \leq i_{(m', l')} (h); \quad \rho' = \pi' \circ p_{m'} \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}, l' \in K(\pi'). \quad \text{Q.e.d.}
$$

186
J.
Ludwig et al.

5.2. Proposition.
Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
Let $d \in 2\mathbb{N}$.
For every $\rho \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}$ there exists $f \in \mathcal{S}(G, M) \cap BR_d$ with $\rho(f) \neq 0$ i.e. $h(BR_d) \subset (G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge}$.

Proof.
Induction on $\dim G$.

For $\dim G = 1$ or for $d = 0$ everything is clear.
Assume now that $n:=\dim G&gt;1$ and that $d \geq 2$.
Let $\rho=(\pi \circ p_m) \in (G, M)_d^{\wedge}$.
Let us show that the following assertion $(\ast)$ is true.

$(\ast)$ Let $f, f_1 \in \mathcal{S}(G, M)$ with $f^* = f, f_1^* = f_1$, $f * f_1 = f_1 * f = f, f_1 \in \ker(G, M)_{\prec d}^{\wedge}$.
For every sequence $\{\rho_k = \pi_k \circ p_{m_k}\}_{k \in \mathbb{N}}$ converging to $\pi_\infty \circ p_{m_\infty} := (\pi \circ p_m)$ we have

$$
\sup_k \operatorname{tr} \rho_k (f^* * f) &lt; \infty.
$$

If we have proved $(\ast)$ the proposition follows.
Indeed by 2.8 there exists $f$ as in $(\ast)$ for which also $\rho(f) \neq 0$.

$(\bullet)$ then implies that for a certain neighbourhood $\tilde{U}$ of $\rho$

$$
\sup_{\rho \in \tilde{U} \cap (G, M)_d^{\wedge}} \rho(f^* * f) &lt; \infty.
$$

Hence using again 2.8 and the fact that $BR_d$ is a two-sided ideal we find $f' \in \mathcal{S}(G, M) \cap BR_d$ with

$$
\rho(f') \neq 0.
$$

Proof of $(\ast)$: we fix an euclidean norm on $\mathcal{G}$, we choose for every $k$ an ideal $\mathcal{A}_k$ of codimension 1 such that the orbit $O_k := K(\pi_k)$ of $\pi$ is saturated by $\mathcal{A}_k$ (i.e. $l_k + \mathcal{A}_k^{\perp} \subset O_k$ for every $l_k \in O_k$) and finally we choose a Jordan-Hölder basis $\mathcal{B}_k$ of $\mathcal{G}_{m_k}$ passing through $\mathcal{A}_k$.
We may assume that $\{\mathcal{B}_k\}$ converges to a Jordan-Hölder basis $\mathcal{B}_\infty$ of $\mathcal{G}_{m_\infty}$ and thus $\{\mathcal{A}_k\}_k$ converges to the ideal $\mathcal{A}_\infty$ of dimension $n-1$ in $\mathcal{G}_{m_\infty}$.

Let $M':= \mathbb{N} \cup \{\infty\}$ be the one point compactification of $\mathbb{N}$.
We define a new variable nilpotent Lie group $(G', M')$ of dimension $n$ in the following way.
We set

$$
\mathcal{G}' := \mathbb{R}^n.
$$

For $v \in M'$, $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n = \mathcal{G}' \times \mathcal{G}'$ let

$$
[x, y]_v := (a_1^v(x, y), \dots, a_n^v(x, y))
$$

where

$$
a_j^v: \mathcal{G}' \times \mathcal{G}' \to \mathbb{R} \quad j = 1, \dots, n
$$

is defined by:

$$
\left[ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i b_i^v, \sum_{j=1}^n y_j b_j^v \right]_{m_v} =: \sum_{s=1}^n a_s^v(x, y) b_s^v
$$

and where $\mathcal{B}_v = : \{b_1^v, \dots, b_n^v\}$.

We remark that the subspace $\mathcal{A} := (0) \times \mathbb{R}^{n-1}$ of $\mathcal{G}'$ is an ideal in $\mathcal{G}_v'$ for every $v$ and so we get a variable nilpotent Lie group $(H, M')$ of dimension $n-1$

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

whose Lie algebra is $(\mathcal{A}, M')$.
Let us denote by $(G', M')_{d, \mathcal{A}}^{\wedge}$ the subset of all the representations $\tau' = \pi' \circ p_{m'}$ of $(G', M')_{d}^{\wedge}$ whose orbit $K(\pi')$ is saturated by $\mathcal{A}$ and by $BR_{d, \mathcal{A}}$ the two sided ideal of all the $\xi$'s in $C^*(G', M')$ with

$$
\sup \left\{\operatorname{tr} \tau (\xi * \xi) \mid \tau \in (G', M')_{d, \mathcal{A}}^{\wedge} \right\} &lt; \infty.
$$

We show now that

$$
(*) \quad h(BR_{d, \mathcal{A}}) \subset (G', M')_{&lt; d}.
$$

In order to do that we produce for $\tau \in (G', M')_{d, \mathcal{A}}^{\wedge}$ an $\tilde{f} \in \mathcal{S}(G', M')$ such that $\tilde{f} \in BT_{d, \mathcal{A}}$ and $\tau(\tilde{f}) \neq 0$.

Every element $\tau'$ in $(G', M')_{d, \mathcal{A}}$ is induced from an element

$$
\sigma' = \omega' \circ p_{v'} \in (H, M')_{d-2}^{\wedge}
$$

i.e.

$$
\tau' = \chi' \circ p_{v'} = \operatorname{ind}_{H_{v'}}^{G_{v'}} \omega' \circ p_{v'}.
$$

We apply now the induction hypothesis to $(H, M')$.

(5.2.1) Choose $g \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}', M') \cap j_d$ (see (4.5.1)) such that $(p_{v'}(g))^{\wedge} \geq 0$ for all $v'$ and such that $p_v(g)^{\wedge}(K(\chi)) \neq 0$ (where $\tau = \chi \circ p_v$).

Let $b \in \mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})$ with $\hat{b} \geq 0$ and $\int_{\mathbb{R}} \hat{b} &gt; 0$.

(5.2.2) Choose $\beta \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{A}, M')$ with $p_v(\beta)^{\wedge}(K(\omega)) \neq 0$ ($\chi = \operatorname{ind} \omega$) such that

$$
p_{v'}(\beta)^{\wedge} \geq 0
$$

and such that

$$
p_{v'}(\tilde{g})^{\wedge} \leq p_{v'}(g)^{\wedge}
$$

pointwise (for every $v'$ ) where

$$
\tilde{g} = b \otimes \beta \in \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}', M').
$$

Changing $g$ if necessary, we may assume that $\int \hat{b} = 1$.

(5.2.3) By Proposition 5.1 there exists $\tilde{f}_1 \in \mathcal{S}(H, M')$ such that $\tilde{f}_1 \geq 0$ (in the $C^*$-sense), such that $\omega(p_v(\tilde{f}_1)) \neq 0$ and such that

$$
\operatorname{tr} \sigma'(\tilde{f}_1) \leq i_{\sigma'}(\beta^* * \beta) \quad \text{for every} \quad \sigma' \in (H, M')_{d-2}^{\wedge}.
$$

Choose finally $\alpha \in \mathcal{S}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{M})$ with

$$
\tau(\tilde{f} * \alpha) \neq 0 \quad \text{(this is possible by (5.2.3))}.
$$

(5.2.4) Let $\tilde{f}_1^1 = \tilde{f}_1 * \alpha * \alpha^* * \tilde{f}_1 = \tilde{f}_1 * \tilde{\alpha}$.
Then $\tilde{\alpha} = \alpha * \alpha^* * \tilde{f}_1 \in \mathcal{S}(G', M')$ hence

$$
c = |\tilde{\alpha}_{[(M' \times \mathcal{A})]}|_1 &lt; \infty. \tag{5.2.5}
$$

Of course $\tau(\tilde{f}) \neq 0$.
We show that

$$
\tilde{f} \in BT_{d, \mathcal{A}}. \tag{5.2.6}
$$

188
J.
Ludwig et al.

Let $\tau' = \chi' \circ p_{\nu'} = \mathrm{ind}_{H_{\nu'}}^{G_{\nu'}} \omega' \circ p_{\nu'} \in (G', M')_{d, \ell}^{\wedge}$.
We have the following formulas (see [13])

$$
\operatorname{tr} \tau'(\tilde{f}) = \int_{\mathbb{R}} \operatorname{tr} \omega'^t \left( p_{\nu'} (\tilde{f}/M' \times \ell) \right) dt \tag{5.2.7}
$$

where $\omega'^t = \omega' \circ \exp(t \operatorname{ad} b_1^\nu)$; $t \in \mathbb{R}$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{(v', l')} (\tilde{g}) &amp;= \int_{\mathbb{R}} i_{(v', l_i)} (\tilde{g}/M' \times \ell)) dt \tag{5.2.8} \\
&amp;= \int_{\mathbb{R}} i_{(v', l_i)} (\beta) dt \quad \text{(as } \tilde{g} = b \otimes \beta \text{ and } b(0) = \int \tilde{b} = 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $l' \in K(\chi')$ and $l_i' \in K(\omega'^t)$.

Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{tr} \tau'(\tilde{f}) &amp;= \int_{\mathbb{R}} \operatorname{tr} \omega'^t \left( p_{\nu'} (\tilde{f}/M' \times \ell) \right) dt \quad &amp; \text{by (5.2.5)} \\
&amp;\leq c \int_{\mathbb{R}} \operatorname{tr} \omega'^t \left( p_{\nu'} (\tilde{f}_1) \right) dt \quad &amp; \text{by (5.2.3), (5.2.4)} \\
&amp;\leq c \int_{\mathbb{R}} i_{(v', l_i)} (\beta^* * \beta) dt \quad &amp; \text{by (5.2.3)} \\
&amp;\leq c |\beta|_1 \int_{\mathbb{R}} i_{(v', l_i')} (\beta) dt \quad &amp; \text{by (5.2.2)} \\
&amp;= c |\beta|_1 i_{(v', l')} (\tilde{g}) \quad &amp; \text{by (5.2.7)} \\
&amp;\leq c |\beta|_1 i_{(v', l')} (g) \quad &amp; \text{by (5.2.2)} \\
&amp;\leq c |\beta_1| C \quad \text{(independent of } \tau' \in (G', M')_{d, \ell}^{\wedge} \text{)} \text{ by 4.6.}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves (\*\*).

Take now $f, g$ as in (\*).
Define $\tilde{f}, \tilde{f}_1 \in \mathcal{S}(G', M')$ by setting

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}(v, (x_1, \dots, x_n)) &amp;:= f(v, \sum x_i b_i^v), \quad (v, (x_1, \dots, x_n)) \in M' \times \mathcal{S}^* \\
\tilde{f}_1(v, (x_1, \dots, x_n)) &amp;:= g(v, \sum x_i b_i^v).
\end{aligned}
$$

Evidently

(5.2.9) $\tilde{f}_1 * \tilde{f} = \tilde{f} * \tilde{f}_1 = \tilde{f}$ as the same is true for $f$ and $f_1$.
For every $k \in \mathbb{N}$ the formula

$$
\tilde{\pi}_k(x_1, \dots, x_n) := \pi_k \left( \exp\left( \sum x_i b_i^k \right) \right)
$$

defines an element $\tilde{\pi}_k$ in $(G_k')^{\wedge}$ and $\tilde{\rho}_k \coloneqq \tilde{\pi}_k \circ p_k \in (G', M')_{d, \ell}^{\wedge}$.
Let $\tilde{\rho}' = \tilde{\pi}' \circ p_v \in (G', M')_{&lt; d}^{\wedge}$.
Then $\pi'$ defined by

$$
\pi' \left( \exp\left( \sum x_i b_i^v \right) \right) := \tilde{\pi} \left( (x_1, \dots, x_n) \right)
$$

is an element in $(\tilde{G}_{m_v})_{&lt; d}$ and so

$$
0 = \pi' \left( p_v(f_1) \right) = \tilde{\pi}' \left( p_v(\tilde{f}_1) \right).
$$

Elements of bounded trace in the $C^*$-algebra of a nilpotent Lie group

Hence

$$
\tilde{f}_1 \in \ker \left((G', M')_{&lt;d}\right).
$$

Thus by 2.8 $\tilde{f} \in j((G', M')_{&lt;d}')$.

But we know from $(**)$ that

$$
j((G', M')_{&lt;d}) \subset j(h(BT_{d,\tilde{\kappa}}) \subset BT_{d,\tilde{\kappa}}).
$$

Thus $\tilde{f} \in BT_{d,\tilde{\kappa}}$ and so $\sup \{\operatorname{tr} \tilde{\rho}_k(\tilde{f}^* * \tilde{f}) \mid k \in \mathbb{N}\} &lt; \infty$ which implies $(*)$ as

$$
\operatorname{tr} \tilde{\rho}_k(\tilde{f}^* * \tilde{f}) = \operatorname{tr} \rho_k(f^* * f); \quad k \in \mathbb{N}. \quad \text{Q.e.d.}
$$

Theorem 5.3 is now an immediate consequence of 5.2 and 3.4.

**5.3. Theorem.** Let $(G, M)$ be a variable nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $(\mathcal{G}, M)$.
For any $d \in 2\mathbb{N}$

$$
h(BT_d(G, M)) = \overline{(G, M)_d^{\wedge}} \cap (G, M)_{&lt;d}^{\wedge}
$$

and

$$
h(BT_{\leq d}(G, M)) = \bigcup_{d' \leq d} \overline{(G, M)_{d'}^{\wedge}} \cap (G, M)_{&lt;d'}^{\wedge}.
$$

Especially if $G$ is a simply connected nilpotent Lie group the hull of the elements of bounded trace is the set

$$
h(BT(G)) = \hat{G}_{&lt;d_{\max}}.
$$

(Here $d_{\max} = \max \{d' \mid d' = \dim G \cdot l; l \in \mathcal{G}^*\}$.)

**5.4. Final remark.** Let $G$ be a connected, simply connected nilpotent Lie group with Lie algebra $\mathcal{G}$.
Let $B$ be the kernel of $\hat{G}_{&lt;d_{\max}}$ in $C^*(G)$, i.e.

$$
B = \{\cap \ker \rho \mid \rho \in \hat{G}_{&lt;d_{\max}}\}.
$$

$B$ is (by 5.3) a $C^*$-algebra of bounded trace.
This fact has some influence on the behaviour of converging sequences in $\hat{B} \cong \hat{G}_{d_{\max}}$.
Let $\mathcal{C}' = \{\rho_k'\}_k \subset \hat{G}_{d_{\max}}$ be a sequence in $\hat{G}_{d_{\max}}$.
Denote by $L(\mathcal{C}')$ the elements in $\hat{G}_{d_{\max}}$ which are limit points of $\mathcal{C}'$ and by $A(\mathcal{C}')$ the elements in $\hat{G}_{d_{\max}}$ which are accumulation points of $\mathcal{C}'$.
Now (see [11] and [12]) we can extract from $\mathcal{C}'$ a subsequence $\mathcal{C} = \{\rho_k\}_k$ of $\mathcal{C}'$ which has the following property:

1. If $L(\mathcal{C}') = \emptyset$ then $A(\mathcal{C}) = \emptyset (= L(\mathcal{C}))$.

2. If $L(\mathcal{C}') \neq \emptyset$ then $L(\mathcal{C}) = A(\mathcal{C})$ is a finite set in $\hat{G}_{d_{\max}}$, and for every $\tau \in L(\mathcal{C})$, there exists $m_\tau \in \mathbb{N}$ such that for any $\xi$ in the Pedersen ideal $j = j(\emptyset)$ of $B$ (i.e. $\xi \in j(\hat{G}_{&lt;d_{\max}})$) we have

$$
\lim_k \operatorname{tr} \rho_k(\xi) = \sum_{\tau \in L(\mathcal{C})} m_\tau \operatorname{tr} \tau(\xi).
$$

The numbers $m_\tau$ can be interpreted as the rank of $\rho_k(\xi_\tau)$ for certain elements $\xi_\tau \in j(\hat{G}_{&lt;d_{\max}})$.

But there does also exist a description of the $m_\tau$'s based on the behaviour of the orbits $O_k$ attached to the $\rho_k$'s.
We will come back to this question in a subsequent paper.

190
J.
Ludwig et al.
