# On the local theta-correspondence\*

Stephen S.
Kudla

Mathematics Department, University of Maryland, College Park, MD 20742, USA

# Introduction

Let $W, \langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ be a non-degenerate symplectic vector space over a non-archimedian local field $\mathcal{A}$ of characteristic 0, and let $\tilde{S}p(W)$ be the non-trivial 2-fold central extension of the symplectic group $Sp(W)$ .
Fix a non-trivial additive character $\psi$ of $\mathcal{A}$ and let $(\omega, S)$ be the corresponding (smooth) oscillator representation of $\tilde{S}p(W)$ .
If $(G, G')$ is a reductive dual pair in $Sp(W)$ , [7], and if $\tilde{G}$ and $\tilde{G}'$ are the inverse images of $G$ and $G'$ in $\tilde{S}p(W)$ , then $(\omega, S)$ may be viewed as a representation of $\tilde{G} \times \tilde{G}'$ .
The local theta correspondence is defined as follows: if $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}\tilde{G}$ is an irreducible smooth representation of $\tilde{G}$ , let

$$
\Theta (\pi ; \tilde {G} ^ {\prime}) = \left\{\pi^ {\prime} \in \operatorname {I r r} \tilde {G} ^ {\prime} | \operatorname {H o m} _ {\tilde {G} \times \tilde {G} ^ {\prime}} (\omega , \pi \otimes \pi^ {\prime}) \neq 0 \right\},
$$

and define $\Theta(\pi'; \tilde{G})$ analogously.
The representations $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are said to correspond if $\pi' \in \Theta(\pi; \tilde{G}')$ or, equivalently, $\pi \in \Theta(\pi'; \tilde{G})$ .
A fundamental property of this local correspondence is embodied in the local Howe duality conjecture (HDC) which asserts that, for all $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} \tilde{G}$ and $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} \tilde{G}'$

$$
\left| \Theta \left(\pi ; \tilde {G} ^ {\prime}\right) \right| \leq 1 \quad \text {and} \quad \left| \Theta \left(\pi^ {\prime}, \tilde {G}\right) \right| \leq 1, \tag {HDC}
$$

so that the local theta correspondence is a bijection on its domain of definition.
Recent progress toward this conjecture has been made by Howe [10] and Rallis [13] but it remains open in the general case.

In the present paper we will prove that, at least for a reductive dual pair of type $(O, Sp)$ , the local theta correspondence is compatible with induction.
More precisely, recall that if $G$ is the group of $\mathcal{A}$ -rational points of a connected reductive algebraic group over $\mathcal{A}$ , then Bernstein and Zelevinsky show that to each $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G$ there is a Levi subgroup $M$ and a (super)cuspidal $\rho \in \mathrm{Irr} M$ , such that $\pi$ is a constituent of the induced representation $I(M, \rho) = \operatorname{ind}_P^G \rho$ , where $P = MN$ is a parabolic subgroup with Levi factor $M$ .
Moreover the data $[\pi]$

- Sloan Fellow, partially supported by NSF Grant #MCS-8201660

S.S.
Kudla

$=[M,\rho ]$ is unique up to associativity.
The result of [3] extends, with minor modification, when $G$ or $G^{\prime}$ is not (algebraically) connected, to the groups $\tilde{G}$ and $\tilde{G}^{\prime}$.
The local theta correspondence should then be natural in the sense that there is a bijection between certain subsets of the sets of equivalence classes $\{[M,\rho ]\}$ for $\tilde{G}$ and $\{[M^{\prime},\rho^{\prime}]\}$ for $\tilde{G}^{\prime}$ i.e.

$$
\theta ([ M, \rho ], \tilde {G} ^ {\prime}) = [ M ^ {\prime}, \rho^ {\prime} ]
$$

and

$$
\theta ([ M ^ {\prime}, \rho^ {\prime} ], \tilde {G}) = [ M, \rho ]
$$

such that

$$
\pi^ {\prime} \in \Theta (\pi , G ^ {\prime}) \Rightarrow [ \pi^ {\prime} ] = \theta ([ \pi ]). \tag {I}
$$

If $\pi$ is cuspidal, so that $[\pi] = [G, \pi]$, property (I) is a consequence of the local Howe duality conjecture; but, in general, the two properties are somewhat complementary.
Of course, one would like to have an explicit recipe for the bijection $\theta: [M, \rho] \to [M', \rho']$.

For the dual pair $(O(V), Sp(W))$ in $Sp(\mathbf{W})$, with $\mathbf{W} = V \otimes W$, we prove a precise form of (I)-Theorem 2.5. The proof involves essentially two facts.
First, if we consider the family of dual pairs $(O(V_m), Sp(W_n))$ where $V_m$ runs over a fixed Witt class and $\dim W_n = 2n$, then for each cuspidal $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}\tilde{O}(V_n)$ there is a unique minimal $n = n(\pi)$ such that $\Theta(\pi, \tilde{S}p(W_n))$ is non-empty, and

$$
\Theta (\pi , \tilde {S} p (W _ {n})) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \phi &amp; \text {if} n &lt; n (\pi) \\ \{\theta (\pi) \} \text {with} \theta (\pi) \in \operatorname {I r r} \tilde {S} p (W _ {n}) \text {cuspidal} &amp; \text {if} n = n (\pi) \\ \{\pi_ {j} ^ {\prime} \}, \pi_ {j} ^ {\prime} \text {induced} \forall j, &amp; \text {if} n &gt; n (\pi). \end{array} \right.
$$

Similarly for each cuspidal $\pi^{\prime}\in \mathrm{Irr}\tilde{S} p(W_{n})$ there exists an $m(\pi)$ such that

$$
\Theta (\pi^ {\prime}, \tilde {O} (V _ {m})) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \phi &amp; \text {if} m &lt; m (\pi) \\ \{\theta (\pi^ {\prime}) \} \text {with} \theta (\pi^ {\prime}) \in \operatorname {I r r} \tilde {O} (V _ {m}) \text {cuspidal} &amp; \text {if} m = m (\pi) \\ \{\pi_ {j} \}, \pi_ {j} \text {induced} \forall j, &amp; \text {if} m &gt; m (\pi). \end{array} \right.
$$

Actually, if we work literally with $\tilde{O}(V_m)$, we must take a slight technical modification - see Theorem 2.1 and Remark 2.4. There is a global analogue of this result due to Rallis [13] and Howe-Piatetski-Shapiro [9] which yields a non-trivial decomposition of the space of cusp forms and whose proof involves computation of Fourier coefficients.
The proof in the local case involves for example, restrictions of certain induced representations to subgroups of the form $Sp(W_1) \times Sp(W_2)$ where $W = W_1 + W_2$, and a study of the orbit structure of such a subgroup on a flag manifold (Prop.
3.4). This technique originates in [6] and is essential in [13, 14].
We also make use of the invariant distribution theorem (Theorem II.1.1) of [13].

The second fact involved in the proof of (I) arises from a computation of the Jacquet modules of $(\omega, S)$ with respect to the maximal parabolics of $\tilde{O}(V_m)$ and $\tilde{S}p(W_n)$.
Here the key fact is that these Jacquet modules have an invariant filtration whose successive quotients are induced from oscillator representations for similar reductive dual pairs; this allows us to proceed by induction.
A computation of this sort also occurs in [13], cf.
also [1].

On the local theta-correspondence

This paper amounts to an attempt to apply the outlook of [3] to the theta correspondence and was inspired by the wonderful lectures of Bernstein at Harvard during the spring of 1983. I would like to thank him for his encouragement and for a number of incisive comments.
I would also like to thank J.
Adams, R.
Howe, S.
Rallis and G.
Zetler for a number of useful discussions.
In particular the methods of this paper are substantially those of [12] and [13].
Finally I would like to thank the department of mathematics of Harvard University for providing a stimulating and congenial environment during the academic year 1982-1983 and the Sloan Foundation for making that environment available.

Notation.
Throughout this paper $\ell$ will be a non-archimedian local field of characteristic 0. Fix a non-trivial additive character $\psi: \ell \to \mathbf{C}^1$.
Here $\mathbf{C}^1 = \{z \in \mathbf{C} \mid |z| = 1\}$.

For $x \in \ell$,

$$
|x| = q^{-\operatorname{ord}x}
$$

where $q$ is the order of the residue class field and $\operatorname{ord}$ is the valuation on $\ell$.

We will follow the conventions of [2, 3].
In particular, if $H \subset G$ are $\ell$-groups and $(\rho, E)$ is a smooth representation of $H$ on a complex vector space $E$, then $\operatorname{ind}_H^G \rho$ will denote the representation of $G$ by right translations on the space of functions:

$$
\{f: G \to E \mid 1) \mid f(hg) = \delta(h)^\frac{1}{2} \rho(h) f(g)
$$

$$
2) \text{ is locally constant of compact support modulo } H\text{}
$$

where $\delta = \Delta_G / \Delta_H$.
Also $\operatorname{Ind}_H^G \rho$ (resp.
$\sharp$-ind$_H^G \rho$) will denote the analogous representation in which the condition on the support of $f$ in 2) (resp.
the factor $\delta^\sharp$) is omitted.
If $(\rho, E)$ is any smooth representation, $(\tilde{\rho}, \tilde{E})$ will denote its contragradient.
All representations will be smooth representations unless otherwise indicated.
For any $\ell$-space $X$, $S(X)$ denotes the space of locally constant functions of compact support and $C^\infty(X)$ denotes the space of locally constant functions.

If $G$ is an $\ell$-group, we let $A(G)$ be the category of all smooth representations of $G$ and $\operatorname{Irr}(G)$ the set of equivalence classes of irreducible smooth representations.

Finally we will need the following invariants from the theory of quadratic forms: If $\eta$ is a non-trivial additive character of $\ell$, consider the character of the second degree, in the terminology of [17] defined by

$$
x \mapsto \eta(x^2). \tag{0.1}
$$

Then $\gamma(\eta)$ is the Weil invariant of (0.1), and, in the notation of [15], for $a \in \ell^\times$,

$$
\gamma(a, \eta) = \gamma(a\eta) \gamma(\eta)^{-1} \tag{0.2}
$$

where $a\eta(x) = \eta(ax)$.
For values and properties of $\gamma(a,\eta)$ see [15].

If $V, (\cdot, \cdot)$ is a non-degenerate inner product space over $\ell$, then $d(V) \in \ell^\times / (\ell^\times)^2$ is the determinant of $V$ and $h_\ell(V)$ is the Hasse invariant of $V$ [16].

Also $(\cdot, \cdot)_\ell$ is the Hilbert symbol for the field $\ell$.

Finally, if $H$ is $GL_n(\ell)$ (resp.
a central extension of $GL_n(\ell)$), then $|\cdot|$ denotes the character $h \to |\det h|$ for the normalized absolute value of $\ell$.

S.S.
Kudla

# § 1. Preliminaries

1.0. Let $(\, , )_{\ell}$ be the Hilbert symbol of the field $\ell$, and define a 2-fold covering $\tilde{G}L(n) = GL(n) \times \mu_2$ of $GL(n)$ with multiplication:

$$
(g, \varepsilon) (g', \varepsilon') = (g g', \varepsilon \varepsilon' (\det g, \det g')_{\ell}).
$$

For convenience we will frequently write $H_{n} = GL(n)$ and $H_{n}^{\prime} = \tilde{G} L(n)$.
If $P$ is any parabolic subgroup of $H_{n}$ with Levi decomposition $P = MN$, and $M \simeq H_{n_1} \times \ldots \times H_{n_k}$, then the inverse image $P^{\prime}$ of $P$ in $H_{n}^{\prime}$ has a decomposition $P^{\prime} = M^{\prime} \cdot N$ where $M^{\prime}$ is the inverse image of $M$ and $N$ identified with the subgroup $\{(n, 1) | n \in N\} \in H_{n}^{\prime}$.
Note that there is a surjective homomorphism

$$
H_{n_1}^{\prime} \times \dots \times H_{n_k}^{\prime} \rightarrow M^{\prime}
$$

$$
((g_1, \varepsilon_1), \dots, (g_k, \varepsilon_k)) \mapsto ((g_1, g_2, \dots, g_k), \varepsilon_1 \dots \varepsilon_k \alpha)
$$

with

$$
\alpha = \prod_{i &lt; j} (\det g_i, \det g_j)_{\ell}.
$$

Finally, if $\psi$ is the fixed additive character, let $\chi$ be the character of $H_{n}^{\prime}$ defined by:

$$
\chi(g, \varepsilon) = \varepsilon \gamma (\det g, \frac{1}{2} \psi)^{-1} \tag{1.0.1}
$$

where $\gamma(a, \eta)$ is the Weil invariant given by (0.2).

1.1. Fix an anisotropic inner product space $V_0, (\, , )_0$ over $\ell$, and for each $m \in \mathbf{Z}_{\geq 0}$, let $V_m$ be the orthogonal direct sum of $V_0$ with $m$ hyperbolic planes.
Let $\ell = \dim V_m$.
We assume that $V_m$ comes equipped with a fixed Witt decomposition

$$
V_m = V_m^{\prime} + V_m^0 + V_m^{\prime\prime},
$$

where $V_m^0 = V_0$, and with bases $\{v_1, \ldots, v_m\}$ for $V_m^{\prime}$ and $\{v_1^{\prime}, \ldots, v_m^{\prime}\}$ for $V_m^{\prime\prime}$ satisfying $(v_i, v_j) = (v_i^{\prime}, v_j^{\prime}) = 0$ and $(v_i, v_j^{\prime}) = \delta_{ij}$.
The orthogonal group $O(V_m)$ then has a fixed maximal $\ell$-split torus.
Of course, if $V_0 = \{0\}$, then $O(V_m)$ is split for all $m$ and is the trivial group for $m = 0$.

Let $\nu \colon O(V_m) \to \mu_2$ be the determinant character and define a 2-fold covering $G_m = \tilde{O}(V_m) = O(V_m) \times \mu_2$ with $(g, \varepsilon)(g', \varepsilon') = (g g', \varepsilon \varepsilon'(\nu(g), \nu(g'))_{\ell})$.
Of course $G_m$ is just the inverse image of $O(V_m)$ in $\tilde{G}L(V_m)$, and is a split extension if the residue characteristic of $\ell$ is not 2. Note that the group $G_m$ has an automorphism

$$
G_m \xrightarrow{\sim} G_m \tag{1.1.1}
$$

$$
(g, \varepsilon) \mapsto (g, \varepsilon \nu(g))
$$

covering the identity map of $O(V_m)$.

We denote by $\chi$ the restriction to $G_m$ of the character of $\widehat{GL(V_m)}$ defined by (1.0.1).
It will also be convenient to consider the characters

$$
zeta_{a, b}(h, \varepsilon) = ((-1)^{b(a - b) + b(b - 1)/2}, \det h)_{\ell} \tag{1.1.2}
$$

On the local theta-correspondence

and

$$
\kappa (a, b) = \zeta_ {a, a - b} \chi^ {a - b}. \tag {1.1.3}
$$

It is then easy to check the following.

Lemma 1.1.1. (i) $\kappa(a, b)$ depends only on $a$ and $b \mod 2$.

(ii) $\kappa (a,b) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}1 &amp; a\equiv b(2)\\ \chi &amp; a\equiv 1(2),b\equiv 0(2)\\ \chi^{-1} &amp; a\equiv 0(2),b\equiv 1(2). \end{array} \right.$

(iii) $\kappa (n,a)^{-1}\kappa (n,b) = \kappa (a,b).$

1.2. For $j$ with $1 \leq j \leq m$, let $V_{j}^{\prime} = \operatorname{span}\{v_{1},\ldots ,v_{j}\}$, $V_{j}^{\prime \prime} = \operatorname{span}\{v_1',\dots,v_j'\}$, and

$$
V _ {j} ^ {0} = \operatorname {s p a n} \left\{v _ {j + 1}, \dots , v _ {m}, v _ {j + 1} ^ {\prime}, \dots , v _ {m} ^ {\prime} \right\} + V _ {m} ^ {0},
$$

so that we obtain a Witt decomposition

$$
V _ {m} = V _ {j} ^ {\prime} + V _ {j} ^ {0} + V _ {j} ^ {\prime \prime}
$$

with $V_{j}^{0} \simeq V_{m - j}$.
If $P(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$ is the subgroup of $O(V_{m})$ which stabilizes $V_{j}^{\prime \prime}$, then there is Levi decomposition $P(V_{j}^{\prime \prime}) = M(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})N(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$ where

$$
M \left(V _ {j} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \simeq G L \left(V _ {j} ^ {\prime}\right) \times O \left(V _ {j} ^ {0}\right),
$$

and we call $P(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$ a maximal parabolic subgroup of $O(V_{m})$ - see Remark 1.2.1 below for a discussion of this choice.
Let $P_{j}$ (resp.
$M_{j}$) be the inverse image of $P(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$ (resp.
$M(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$) in $G_{m}$ and let $N_{j}$ be the image of $N(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$ under the natural splitting $m \to (n,1)$.
Then $P_{j} = M_{j}N_{j}$ and

$$
M _ {j} \simeq H _ {j} \times G _ {m - j}, \tag {1.2.0}
$$

where we note that the $GL(V_{j}) \simeq H_{j}$ factor of $M(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$ lies in $SO(V_{m})$ and hence may be identified with a subgroup of $G_{m}$.
Every maximal parabolic subgroup of $O(V_{m})$ is conjugate to one of the $P(V_{j}^{\prime \prime})$'s.
By abuse of language (e.g. if the covering $G_{m} \to O(V_{m})$ is non-trivial), we will call the groups $P_{j}$ a standard set of maximal parabolic subgroups.

A standard set of parabolic subgroups of $O(V_{m})$ is then indexed by sequences of positive integers $\mathbf{s} = (s_1, \ldots, s_k)$ with $|\mathbf{s}| = \sum_{j} s_j \leq m$.
The parabolic subgroup $P(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ is the stabilizer of the isotropic flag

$$
V _ {s _ {1}} ^ {\prime \prime} \subset V _ {s _ {1} + s _ {2}} ^ {\prime \prime} \subset \dots \subset V _ {| \mathbf {s} |} ^ {\prime \prime}, \tag {1.2.1}
$$

and $P(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ has Levi decomposition $P(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime}) = M(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})N(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ where

$$
M \left(V _ {\mathbf {s}} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \simeq H _ {s _ {1}} \times \dots \times H _ {s _ {k}} \times O \left(V _ {m - | \mathbf {s} |}\right).
$$

Let $P_{\mathbf{s}}$ (resp.
$M_{\mathbf{s}}$) denote the inverse image of $P(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ (resp.
$M(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$) in $G_{m}$ and let $N_{\mathbf{s}}$ be the image of $N(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ under the natural splitting.
Then, as before, $P_{\mathbf{s}} = M_{\mathbf{s}}N_{\mathbf{s}}$ with $M_{\mathbf{s}} \simeq H_{s_1} \times \ldots \times H_{s_k} \times G_{m - |\mathbf{s}|}$.
Formally we let $P_0 = G_m$, and we

S.S.
Kudla

note that

$$
P _ {\mathbf {s}} = P _ {s _ {1}} \cap P _ {s _ {1} + s _ {2}} \cap \dots \cap P _ {| \mathbf {s} |}. \tag {1.2.2}
$$

In particular, $P_{|\mathbf{s}|}$ (resp.
$P_{s_1}$ ) is the unique maximal parabolic subgroup $P_j$ with $j$ maximal (resp.
minimal) which contains $P_{\mathbf{s}}$ .
Note that $P_{\mathbf{s}}$ and $P_{\mathbf{s}'}$ are associate if and only if $|\mathbf{s}| = |\mathbf{s}'|$ and the sets $\mathbf{s}$ and $\mathbf{s}'$ differ by a permutation.

Remark 1.2.1. When $\dim V_0 = 0$ , so that $V_m$ is split, a technical remark is in order.
Let

$$
V _ {m} ^ {\prime -} = \operatorname {s p a n} \left\{v _ {1}, \dots , v _ {m - 1}, v _ {m} ^ {\prime} \right\}
$$

and

$$
V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime -} = \operatorname {s p a n} \left\{v _ {1} ^ {\prime}, \dots , v _ {m - 1} ^ {\prime}, v _ {m} \right\}.
$$

For each $j$ , let

$$
P ^ {0} \left(V _ {j} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) = P \left(V _ {j} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \cap S O \left(V _ {m}\right)
$$

and let

$$
P ^ {0} \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime -}\right) = P \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime -}\right) \cap S O \left(V _ {m}\right).
$$

Then the conjugacy classes of maximal parabolic subgroups of $SO(V_m)$ are represented by $P^0 (V_j^{\prime \prime})$ , for $1\leq j\leq m - 2$ , $P^0 (V_m^{\prime \prime})$ and $P^0 (V_m^{\prime \prime -})$ .
Moreover:

$$
P ^ {0} \left(V _ {m - 1} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) = P ^ {0} \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \cap P ^ {0} \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime -}\right).
$$

is not maximal.
On the other hand, we have

$$
P ^ {0} \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) = P \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime}\right)
$$

and

$$
P ^ {0} \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime -}\right) = P \left(V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime -}\right),
$$

and these two subgroups are conjugate in $O(V_m)$ .
On the other hand the parabolic subgroup $P(V_{m-1}')$ is no longer contained in $P(V_m'')$ or $P(V_m''')$ and is now maximal! Note that the "extra" parabolic $P(V_{m-1}')$ has Levi factor

$$
M \left(V _ {m - 1} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) = H _ {m - 1} \times O \left(V _ {1}\right).
$$

Similarly, when $|\mathbf{s}| = m$ , we may define flags $V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime -}$ by replacing $V_{m}^{\prime \prime}$ with $V_{m}^{\prime \prime -}$ in (1.2.1), and the above remarks carry over verbatim to the parabolic subgroups $P^{0}(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ , $P^{0}(V_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime -})$ etc. For example

$$
P ^ {0} \left(V _ {\mathbf {s}} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \cap P ^ {0} \left(V _ {\mathbf {s}} ^ {\prime \prime -}\right) = P ^ {0} \left(V _ {\mathbf {t}} ^ {\prime \prime}\right)
$$

where $\mathbf{t} = (s_1, \ldots, s_k - 1)$ .

1.3. For each $n \in \mathbf{Z}_{\geq 0}$ , let $W_{n}, \langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ be a non-degenerate symplectic vector space of dimension $2n$ over $\mathcal{A}$ .
We assume that $W_{n}$ comes equipped with a fixed complete polarization

$$
W _ {n} = W _ {n} ^ {\prime} + W _ {n} ^ {\prime \prime} \tag {1.3.1}
$$

and bases $\{e_1, \ldots, e_n\}$ for $W_n'$ and $\{e_1', \ldots, e_n'\}$ for $W_n''$ such that $\langle e_i, e_j' \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ , i.e. $\{e_1, \ldots, e_n'\}$ is a fixed symplectic basis for $W_n$ , $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ .
The symplectic group $Sp(W_n)$ then has a fixed maximal $\mathcal{A}$ -split torus, and is the trivial group if $n = 0$ .

On the local theta-correspondence

Let $G_{n}^{\prime} = \tilde{S} p(W_{n})$ be the unique non-trivial 2-fold central extension of $Sp(W_{n})$ (or the group $\mu_{2}$ if $n = 0$).
Then the polarization (1.3.1) gives rise to an isomorphism

$$
G _ {n} ^ {\prime} = \tilde {S} p \left(W _ {n}\right) \xrightarrow {\sim} S p \left(W _ {n}\right) \times \mu_ {2} \tag {1.3.2}
$$

with $(g,\varepsilon)(g^{\prime},\varepsilon^{\prime}) = (gg^{\prime},\varepsilon \varepsilon^{\prime}c(g,g^{\prime}))$ where the cocycle $c(g,g^{\prime})$ is given explicitly by Rao [15].

1.4. For $j$ with $1 \leq j \leq n$, let $W_{j}^{\prime} = \operatorname{span}\{e_{1}, \ldots, e_{j}\}$, $W_{j}^{\prime \prime} = \operatorname{span}\{e_{1}^{\prime}, \ldots, e_{j}^{\prime}\}$, and $W_{j}^{0} = (W_{j}^{\prime} + W_{j}^{\prime \prime})^{\perp}$, so that there is a decomposition $W_{n} = W_{j}^{\prime} + W_{j}^{0} + W_{j}^{\prime \prime}$ with $W_{j}^{0} \simeq W_{n - j}$.
For any sequence $\mathbf{s} = (s_{1}, \ldots, s_{k})$ of positive integers with $|\mathbf{s}| \leq n$, let $P(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ be the stabilizer of the isotropic flag

$$
W _ {s _ {1}} ^ {\prime \prime} \subset W _ {s _ {1} + s _ {2}} ^ {\prime \prime} \subset \dots \subset W _ {| \mathbf {s} |} ^ {\prime \prime}.
$$

and let $P_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$ be the inverse image of $P(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ in $G_{n}^{\prime}$.
Then $P(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ has a Levi decomposition $P(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime}) = M(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})N(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ with

$$
M \left(W _ {\mathbf {s}} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \simeq H _ {s _ {1}} \times \dots \times H _ {s _ {h}} \times S p \left(W _ {n - | \mathbf {s} |}\right).
$$

Let $M_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$ be the inverse image of $M(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ in $G_{n}^{\prime}$ and let $N_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$ be the image of $N(W_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime \prime})$ under the natural section given by (1.3.2).
Then $M_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$ normalizes $N_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$ and $P_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime} \simeq M_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime} \ltimes N_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$.
Moreover there is a natural homomorphism

$$
H _ {s _ {1}} ^ {\prime} \times \dots \times H _ {s _ {h}} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {n - | \mathbf {s} |} ^ {\prime} \rightarrow M _ {\mathbf {s}} ^ {\prime} \tag {1.4.1}
$$

given by:

$$
\left(\left(h _ {1}, \varepsilon_ {1}\right), \dots , \left(h _ {k}, \varepsilon_ {k}\right), (g, \varepsilon)\right) \mapsto \left(\left(h _ {1}, \dots , h _ {k}, g\right), \varepsilon_ {1} \dots \varepsilon_ {k} \varepsilon \alpha\right)
$$

where

$$
\alpha = \prod_ {i &lt;   j} (\det  h _ {i}, \det  h _ {j}) _ {\ell} \cdot \left(\prod_ {j = 1} ^ {k} (\det  h _ {j}, x (g)) _ {\ell}\right)
$$

where $x(g)$ is as in [15, Lemma 5.1].

Again the $P_{\mathbf{s}}^{\prime}$ give a standard set of parabolic subgroups of $G_{n}^{\prime}$ and satisfy the analogue of (1.2.2).
Again we formally let $P_0^{\prime} = G_{n}^{\prime}$.

1.5. If $G$ is one of the groups above and if $P = MN$ is any parabolic subgroup of $G$, we have induction and localization functors as usual [2, 3] $R \colon A(G) \to A(M)$ and $I \colon A(M) \to A(G)$ which we assume to be normalized so that $I(M, \tilde{\rho}) = \widetilde{I(M, \rho)}$ and $\operatorname{Hom}_G(\pi, I(M, \rho)) = \operatorname{Hom}_M(R(M, \pi), \rho)$.
We also have the functor $\widetilde{R} \colon A(G) \to A(M)$ given by

$$
\widetilde {R} (M, \pi) = \widetilde {R (M , \tilde {\pi})}
$$

and which satisfies

$$
\operatorname {H o m} _ {G} (I (M, \rho), \pi) = \operatorname {H o m} _ {M} (\rho , \widetilde {R} (M, \pi)).
$$

A representation $\pi \in A(G)$ will be called cuspidal if the representations $R(M, \pi)$ are zero for all proper Levi subgroups; and $\operatorname{Irr}_{\varepsilon} G$ will denote the set of equivalence classes of cuspidal irreducible representations.
If $P$ is indexed by a set $\mathbf{s}$ as in 1.2 and 1.4, we will write $I_{\mathbf{s}}$ and $R_{\mathbf{s}}$ for $I(M, \cdot)$ and $R(M, \cdot)$ etc.

S.S.
Kudla

It is easily checked that Theorem 2.9 of [3] extends immediately to the group $G_{n}^{\prime}$.
In particular, if $\pi^{\prime} \in \mathrm{Irr} G_{n}^{\prime}$, then there exists a set $\mathbf{s} = (s_{1}, \ldots, s_{k})$ with $s_{i} \in \mathbf{Z}_{&gt;0}$ and $|\mathbf{s}| \leq n$ and representations $\sigma_{i}^{\prime} \in \mathrm{Irr}_{c} H_{i}^{\prime}$ and $\rho^{\prime} \in \mathrm{Irr}_{c} G_{n - |\mathbf{s}|}^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\pi^{\prime} \in J H \left(I_{\mathbf{s}} \left(\sigma_{1}^{\prime} \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_{k}^{\prime} \otimes \rho^{\prime}\right)\right).
$$

The data $(\mathbf{s},\pmb {\sigma},\rho)$ is unique up to associativity in $G_{n}^{\prime}$ and we write

$$
[ \pi^{\prime} ] = [ \sigma_{1}^{\prime}, \dots , \sigma_{r}^{\prime}, \rho ],
$$

the index $\mathbf{s}$ being understood.

The situation for $G_{m}$ is a little more delicate due to the (algebraic) disconnectedness of $O(V_{m})$, (except in the trivial case).
Nonetheless it is easy to prove the following result by restriction to $SO(V_{m})$ and its inverse image in $G_{m}$:

**Proposition 1.5.1.** Suppose $M$ and $N$ are standard Levi subgroups of $G_{m}$ and suppose that $\rho \in \mathrm{Irr}_{c}M$ and $\eta \in \mathrm{Irr}_{c}N$.
Let $\nu$ be the lift to $G_{m}$ of the determinant character of $O(V_{m})$.

(i) If

$$
J H (I (M, \rho)) \cap J H (I (N, \eta)) \neq \emptyset
$$

then

$$
(M, \rho) \sim (N, \eta) \quad \text{or} \quad (N, v \eta)
$$

(ii) If $(M,\rho)\sim (N,\eta)$, then

$$
J H^{0} (I (M, \rho)) = J H^{0} (I (N, \eta)) \quad \text{if } \rho \simeq v \rho
$$

and

$$
J H^{0} (I (M, \rho) \oplus I (M, v \rho)) = J H^{0} (I (N, \eta) \oplus I (N, v \eta)) \quad \text{if } \rho \nleq v \rho.
$$

Here $(M,\rho)\sim (N,\eta)$ means associativity in $G_{m}$ and $JH^{0}$ etc. are as in [3].
$\square$

Therefore, if $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_{m}$, there exist $\mathbf{s} = (s_{1},\ldots ,s_{r})$ with $s_i\in \mathbf{Z}_{&gt;0}$, $|\mathbf{s}|\leq m$, $\pmb{\sigma} = (\sigma_{1},\dots,\sigma_{r})$, with $\sigma_{i}\in \mathrm{Irr}_{c}H_{i}$, and $\rho \in \mathrm{Irr}_cG_{m - |\mathbf{s}|}$ such that

$$
\pi \in J H \left(I_{\mathbf{s}} \left(\sigma_{1} \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_{r} \otimes \rho\right)\right) \tag{1.5.1}
$$

and this data is unique up to associativity in $G_{m}$ and possible multiplication by $\nu$.

Note that if $\dim V_{m}$ is odd, then $JH(I(M,\rho))$ and $JH(I(M,v\rho))$ are disjoint and so no multiplication by $\nu$ is involved.

**Remark 1.5.1.** In the case $\dim V_0 = 0$ the group $G_{1}$ has no cuspidal representations and, as a result, the Levi subgroups $M_{\mathbf{s}}$ with $|\mathbf{s}| = m - 1$ have no cuspidal representations.
Thus such $M_{\mathbf{s}}$ will never appear as the first member of a pair $(M,\rho)$ with $\rho$ cuspidal, i.e. the 'extra' parabolics for $G_{m}$ - see Remark 1.2.1 - are not involved in the classification.
In any case we write

$$
[\pi] = \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \left[ \sigma_{1}, \dots , \sigma_{r}, \rho \right] \\ \left[ \sigma_{1}, \dots , \sigma_{r}, \rho \right] + \left[ \sigma_{1}, \dots , \sigma_{r}, v \rho \right] \end{array} \right. \tag{1.5.2}
$$

if (1.5.1) holds (resp.
if (1.5.1) holds for both $\rho$ and $v\rho$ with $\rho \not\simeq v\rho$.)

On the local theta-correspondence

Remark 1.5.2. We will call a representation $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$ ambiguous if $[\pi]$ is given by the second case in (1.5.2).

For any $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$ there exists $\mathbf{s}, \pmb{\sigma}$ and $\rho$ such that

$$
\pi \hookrightarrow I_{\mathbf{s}}(\sigma_1 \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_r \otimes \rho).
$$

If $\pi$ is ambiguous, it may also happen that, for some permutation $\mathbf{s}'$ of $\mathbf{s}$ and corresponding permutation $\pmb{\sigma}'$ of $\pmb{\sigma}$, we have

$$
\pi \hookrightarrow I_{\mathbf{s}'}(\sigma_1' \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_r' \otimes v \rho)
$$

as well.
We will call such $\pi$ strongly ambiguous and will write

$$
[\pi]_+ = [\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_r, \rho] + [\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_r, v \rho].
$$

Similarly, if $\pi$ is ambiguous but not strongly ambiguous, or if $\pi$ is not ambiguous, we write

$$
[\pi]_+ = [\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_r, \rho].
$$

1.6. The center of the group $G_m$ contains the group $\{(1, \varepsilon) | \varepsilon \in \mu_2\} = \langle \varepsilon \rangle$, and for any $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$, we define $a = a(\pi) \in \mathbf{Z}/2\mathbf{Z}$ by

$$
\pi(1, \varepsilon) = \varepsilon^a
$$

so that $\mathrm{Irr} G_m = \mathrm{Irr}^0 G_m \bigsqcup \mathrm{Irr}^1 G_m$ with $\mathrm{Irr}^0 G_m \simeq \mathrm{Irr} O(V_m)$.
Moreover there is a bijection between $\mathrm{Irr}^0 G_m$ and $\mathrm{Irr}^1 G_m$ given by multiplication by the character $\chi$ defined by (1.0.1):

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Irr}^0 G_m \to \mathrm{Irr}^1 G_m \\
\pi \mapsto \chi \cdot \pi.
\end{array}
$$

For later convenience we let

$$
\pi[0] = \begin{cases}
\pi &amp; \text{if } a(\pi) = 0 \\
\chi^{-1} \pi &amp; \text{if } a(\pi) = 1
\end{cases}
$$

and let

$$
\pi[1] = \chi \pi[0].
$$

Of course the representation theory of $G_m$ is thus reduced to that of $O(V_m)$, but it is the group $G_m$ which arises most naturally from the dual pair construction, and we want to avoid making ad hoc adjustments in that formalism!

## §2.

The theta correspondence

For $m, n \in \mathbf{Z}_{\geq 0}$ let

$$
\mathbf{W}_{m,n} = V_m \otimes W_n,
$$

and let $\ll, \gg = (, ) \otimes \langle , \rangle$, so that there is, as usual a natural homomorphism $\alpha: O(V_m) \times Sp(W_n) \to Sp(\mathbf{W}_{m,n})$.
The space $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W}_{m,n}$ inherits the polarization

$$
\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W}' + \mathbf{W}''.
$$

S.S.
Kudla

where $\mathbf{W}' = V_m \otimes W_n'$ and $\mathbf{W}'' = V_m \otimes W_n''$ and hence there is an isomorphism

$$
\tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W}) \xrightarrow{\sim} Sp(\mathbf{W}) \times \mu_2.
$$

There is a lift $\tilde{\alpha}$ of $\alpha$ to

$$
\tilde{\alpha} \colon G_m \times G_n' \to \tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W})
$$

which is not quite unique due to the automorphism (1.1.1) of $G_m$, but we fix a choice given by $\tilde{\alpha}((h, \varepsilon)) = (h \otimes 1_{W_n}, \varepsilon^n)$ for $(h, \varepsilon) \in G_m = O(V_m) \times \mu_2$.
Note that the diagram

$$
\begin{array}{c}
G_n' \quad \xrightarrow{\tilde{\alpha}} \tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W}) \\
\Big{\uparrow} \\
\tilde{G}L(W_n') \xrightarrow{\tilde{\alpha}'} \tilde{G}L(\mathbf{W}')
\end{array}
$$

commutes where

$$
\tilde{\alpha}'(g, \varepsilon) = (1_V \otimes g, \varepsilon^{\ell}((-1)^{\frac{1}{2}\ell(\ell-1)} d(V_m), x(g))_d). \tag{2.1}
$$

Let $(\omega, S)$ be the smooth oscillator representation of $\tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W})$ determined by the character $\psi$ and define

$$
\omega_{m,n} = \tilde{\alpha}^*(\omega),
$$

so that $(\omega_{m,n}, S)$ is a smooth representation of $G_m \times G_n'$.
Note that if $V_m$ or $W_n$ is the zero space then so is $\mathbf{W}$, and $(\omega, S)$ reduces to the non-trivial character of $\tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W}) \cong \mu_2$.
As a result,

$$
\omega_{m,0} \simeq \mathbf{1}_{G_m} \otimes \omega^{\ell} \tag{2.2}
$$

where $\mathbf{1}_{G_m}$ is the trivial representation of $G_m$ and $\omega$ is the non-trivial character of $G_0'$, while, if $V_0 = 0$,

$$
\omega_{0,n} \simeq \mu^n \otimes \mathbf{1}_{G_n'} \tag{2.3}
$$

where $\mathbf{1}_{G_n'}$ is the trivial representation of $G_n'$ and $\mu$ is the non-trivial character of $G_0 \simeq \mu_2$.

As in the introduction, if $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$, let

$$
\Theta_n(\pi) = \left\{ \pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_n' \mid \mathrm{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n'} (\omega_{m,n}, \pi \otimes \pi') \neq 0 \right\}
$$

and if $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_n'$, let

$$
\Theta_m(\pi') = \left\{ \pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m \mid \mathrm{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n} (\omega_{m,n}, \pi \otimes \pi') \neq 0 \right\}.
$$

Note that $\omega_{m,n}|_{\langle \varepsilon \rangle} = \varepsilon^n$ where $\langle \varepsilon \rangle$ is as in §1.6, and so $\Theta_n(\pi) = \emptyset$ for $n$ and $a(\pi)$ of opposite parity.
Thus it turns out to be more natural to study the sets $\Theta_n(\pi[n])$ where $\pi[n]$ is defined in §1.6.

Theorem 2.1. (i) For $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_m$, let

$$
n(\pi) = \min \{ n \mid \Theta_n(\pi[n]) \neq \emptyset \}.
$$

On the local theta-correspondence

Then if $n(\pi) &lt; \infty$,

$$
\Theta_{n}(\pi [n]) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \emptyset &amp; \text{if } n &lt; n(\pi) \\ \{\theta(\pi)\} &amp; \text{if } n = n(\pi) \\ \{\pi_{j}^{\prime}\} &amp; \text{if } n &gt; n(\pi) \end{array} \right.
$$

where $\theta(\pi) \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_{n(\pi)}'$, and the $\pi_j' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_n'$ are not cuspidal.
In particular, $\theta(\pi)$ is the unique cuspidal representation in the set $\prod_{n=0}^{\infty} \Theta_n(\pi[n])$.

(ii) For $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_n'$, let

$$
m(\pi') = \min \{m \mid \Theta_{m}(\pi') \neq \emptyset\}.
$$

Then, if $m(\pi') &lt; \infty$,

$$
\Theta_{m}(\pi') = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \emptyset &amp; \text{if } m &lt; m(\pi) \\ \{\theta(\pi')\} &amp; \text{if } m = m(\pi) \\ \{\pi_{j}\} &amp; \text{if } m &gt; m(\pi) \end{array} \right.
$$

where $\theta(\pi') \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_{m(\pi')}$ and the $\pi_j \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$ are not cuspidal.
In particular, $\theta(\pi')$ is the unique cuspidal representation in the set $\prod_{m=0}^{\infty} \Theta_m(\pi')$.

Corollary 2.2. If $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_m$ and $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_n$, then

$$
\theta(\theta(\pi)) = \pi [n(\pi)]
$$

and

$$
\theta(\theta(\pi')) = \pi'.
$$

Remark 2.3. We could, as is traditional, have renormalized the representation $\omega_{m,n}$ by setting

$$
\omega_{m,n}^{\mathrm{B}} = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \omega_{m,n} &amp; \text{for } n \text{ even} \\ \chi^{-1} \omega_{m,n} &amp; \text{for } n \text{ odd}. \end{array} \right.
$$

where $\chi$ is the character of $G_{m} \subset \widehat{GL(V_{m})}$ given in Sect.
1.0. Since $\tilde{\alpha}$ already factors through $O(V_{m})$ for $n$ even, $\omega_{m,n}^{\mathrm{B}}$ is a smooth representation of $O(V_{m}) \times G_{n}'$.
In fact, in the Schrödinger model associated to the polarization $(\mathbf{W}', \mathbf{W}'')$ of $\mathbf{W}$, $S \simeq S(\mathbf{W}') \simeq S(V^n)$ and, for $h \in O(V_{m})$ and $\varphi \in S$

$$
\omega_{m,n}^{\mathrm{B}}(h) \varphi(x) = \varphi(h^{-1} x).
$$

For $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}^0 G_m \cong \mathrm{Irr} O(V_m)$,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Theta_{n}(\pi [n]) = \left\{ \pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_{n}' \mid \mathrm{Hom}_{G_{m} \times G_{n}'}(\omega_{m,n}, \pi [n] \otimes \pi') \neq \emptyset \right\} \\
= \left\{ \pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_{n}' \mid \mathrm{Hom}_{O(V_{m}) \times G_{n}'}(\omega_{m,n}^{\mathrm{B}}, \pi \otimes \pi') \neq 0 \right\}.
\end{array}
$$

Thus this renormalization removes the 'twists' from Theorem 2.1, and elsewhere, but, as remarked in Sect.
1.6, we wanted to avoid this convenient but ad hoc adjustment.

In the Schrödinger model just described the action of $\widehat{GL(W_n')} \subset G_n'$ is given by

$$
\omega_{m,n}(g, \varepsilon) \phi(x) = \chi_{V}(g, \varepsilon) | \det g |^{\frac{1}{2} \ell} \phi(x g) \tag{2.4}
$$

S.S.
Kudla

where $\ell = \dim V_{m}$ and

$$
\chi_{V}(g, \varepsilon) = \varepsilon^{\ell} \gamma (\det g, \frac{1}{2} \psi)^{-\ell} (d(V_{m}), \det g)_{\ell}. \tag{2.5}
$$

Here $\gamma(t, \frac{1}{2}\psi)$ is the Weil invariant given by (0.2).
We will frequently write simply $\chi_V$ for $\chi_{V_m}$ since this character is, in fact, independent of $m$.

If $\sigma \in \mathrm{Irr}_c H_j$, define $\theta(\sigma) \in \mathrm{Irr}_c H_j$ by

$$
\theta(\sigma)(g) = \tilde{\sigma}(^t g^{-1}). \tag{2.6}
$$

We may now state our main result:

**Theorem 2.5.** (i) If $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$ is strongly ambiguous, as defined in Remark 1.5.2, then $\Theta_n(\pi[n]) = \emptyset$ for all $n$.

(ii) If $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$ is not strongly ambiguous, let

$$
[\pi]_+ = [\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_r, \rho]
$$

with $\sigma_i \in \mathrm{Irr}_c H_{s_i}$, $\rho \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_{m - |\mathbf{s}|}$ and suppose that $\pi' \in \Theta_n(\pi[n])$.
Then, if $n \geq n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}|$,

$$
[\pi'] = [\chi_V \theta(\sigma_1), \dots, \chi_V \theta(\sigma_r), \chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n}, \chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n + 1}, \dots, \chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - |\mathbf{s}| - n(\rho) - 1}, \theta(\rho)].
$$

If $n &lt; n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}|$, then there exists a sequence $i_1, \ldots, i_t$ with $t = n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}| - n$, such that

$$
\sigma_{i_1} = | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - 1}, \dots, \sigma_{i_t} = | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - t}
$$

and then

$$
[\pi'] = [\chi_V \theta(\sigma_1), \dots, \widehat{\chi_V \theta(\sigma_{i_1})}, \dots, \widehat{\chi_V \theta(\sigma_{i_t})}, \dots, \chi_V \theta(\sigma_r), \theta(\rho)].
$$

This expression for $[\pi']$ is independent of the choice of sequence.

Applying the symmetry of the correspondence and noting that

$$
\pi \in \Theta_n(\pi') \Rightarrow \pi = \pi[n]
$$

we obtain:

**Corollary 2.6.** For $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_n'$, let

$$
[\pi'] = [\sigma_1', \dots, \sigma_r', \rho']
$$

with $\sigma_i' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c H_{s_i}'$ and $\rho' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_{n - |\mathbf{s}|}'$, and suppose that $\pi \in \Theta_m(\pi')$.
Then if $m \geq m(\rho') + |\mathbf{s}|$,

$$
[\pi]_+ = [\theta((\chi_V)^{-1} \sigma_1'), \dots, \theta((\chi_V)^{-1} \sigma_r'), | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - 1}, \dots, | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - m + m(\rho') + |\mathbf{s}|}, \theta(\rho')[n]].
$$

If $m &lt; m(\rho') + |\mathbf{s}|$, there exists a sequence $i_1, \ldots, i_t$ with $t = m(\rho') + |\mathbf{s}| - m$ such that

$$
\sigma_{i_1}' = \chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n}, \dots, \sigma_{i_t}' = \chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n + t - 1}
$$

and then

$$
[\pi]_+ = [\theta((\chi_V)^{-1} \sigma_1'), \dots, \widehat{\theta((\chi_V)^{-1} \sigma_{i_1}')}, \dots, \widehat{\theta((\chi_V)^{-1} \sigma_{i_t}')}, \dots, \theta((\chi_V)^{-1} \sigma_r'), \theta(\rho')[n]].
$$

Again this expression for $[\pi]_+$ is independent of the choice of sequence.

Actually the proof of Theorem 2.5 yields a little more information.

On the local theta-correspondence

Corollary 2.7. (i) If $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_m$ with

$$
[\pi]_+ = [\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_r, \rho]
$$

and if $n &lt; n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}|$, then $\Theta_n(\pi) = \emptyset$ unless $\pi$ occurs as a subrepresentation of $I_{\mathbf{s}}(\sigma_1 \otimes \ldots \otimes \sigma_r \otimes \rho)$ for some ordering of the $\sigma_i$'s such that there is a sequence $i_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; i_t$ with

$$
\sigma_{i_1} = ||\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - 1, \sigma_{i_2} = ||\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - 2, \ldots, \sigma_{i_t} = ||\frac{1}{2}\ell - n - t
$$

and $t = n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}| - n$.

(ii) If $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr} G_n'$ with

$$
[\pi'] = [\sigma_1', \dots, \sigma_r', \rho']
$$

and if $m &lt; m(\rho') + |\mathbf{s}|$, then $\Theta_m(\pi') = \emptyset$ unless $\pi'$ occurs as a subrepresentation of $I_{\mathbf{s}}'(\sigma_1' \otimes \ldots \otimes \sigma_r' \otimes \rho')$ for some ordering of the $\sigma_i$'s such that there is a sequence $i_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; i_t$ with

$$
\sigma_{i_1}' = \chi_V ||\frac{1}{2}\ell - n, \ldots, \sigma_{i_t}' = \chi_V ||\frac{1}{2}\ell - n + t - 1,
$$

with $t = m(\rho') + |\mathbf{s}| - m$.

The proofs of these theorems will be given in the following sections.
In particular, the proof of Theorem 2.5 is based on a computation of the Jacquet modules (Theorem 2.8) of $\omega_{m,n}$ with respect to the maximal parabolic subgroups of $G_m$ and $G_n'$.
The result illustrates the inductive properties of the oscillator representation.

Let $(\sigma_k, S(H_k))$ denote the representation of $H_k \times H_k$ on $S(H_k)$ given by:

$$
\sigma_k(h_1, h_2) \varphi(x) = \varphi'(h_1 \times h_2).
$$

Note that if $\sigma \in \mathrm{Irr}_c H_k$, then

$$
\mathrm{Hom}_{H_k \times H_k}(\sigma_k, \sigma \otimes \sigma') \neq 0
$$

if and only if $\sigma' = \theta(\sigma)$ where, as above, $\theta(\sigma)(h) = \tilde{\sigma}(h^{-1})$.

Theorem 2.8. For $1 \leq j \leq m$, (resp.
$1 \leq j \leq n$), let $\tau_j = R_j(\omega_{m,n})$ (resp.
$\tau_j' = R_j'(\omega_{m,n})$) where $R_j$ (resp.
$R_j'$) is the localization functor as described in Sect.
1.5.

(i) There is an $M_j \times G_n'$ invariant filtration $\tau_j = \tau_j^{(0)} \supset \ldots \supset \tau_j^{(r)} \supset \{0\}$ of $\tau_j$ with successive quotients $\tau_{jk} \simeq \tau_j^{(k)} / \tau_j^{(k+1)}$, with $0 \leq k \leq \min(n,j) = r$ such that

$$
\tau_{jk} \cong \operatorname{ind}_{Q_{jk} \times P_k'}^{M_j \times G_n'} \xi_{jk} \sigma_k \otimes \omega_{m-j,n-k}
$$

where the character $\xi_{jk}$ is given by:

$$
\xi_{jk} =
\begin{cases}
\chi_V ||\frac{1}{2}\ell - j + \frac{k-1}{2} &amp; \text{on } H_k' \\
||\frac{1}{2}\ell - j + \frac{k-1}{2} &amp; \text{on } H_k \\
||\frac{1}{2}\ell - \frac{1}{2}j - n + \frac{k-1}{2} &amp; \text{on } H_{j-k} \\
\kappa(n,n-k) &amp; \text{on } G_{m-j}
\end{cases}
$$

with $\kappa(\cdot, \cdot)$ given by (1.1.3).

S.S.
Kudla

(ii) There is a $G_{m} \times M_{j}^{\prime}$ invariant filtration of $\tau_{j}^{\prime}$:

$$
(\tau_{j}^{\prime})^{(0)} \supset \dots \supset (\tau_{j}^{\prime})^{(r^{\prime})} \supset \{0\}
$$

with successive quotients $\tau_{jk}^{\prime} = (\tau_j^{\prime})^{(k)} / (\tau_j^{\prime})^{(k + 1)}$ with $0\leq k\leq \min (m,j) = r^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\tau_{jk}^{\prime} \simeq \operatorname{ind}_{P_k \times Q_{jk}}^{G_{m} \times M_j'} \xi_{jk}^{\prime} \sigma_k \otimes \omega_{m - k, n - j}
$$

where

$$
\xi_{jk}^{\prime} = \begin{cases}
| |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - \frac{k + 1}{2}} &amp; \text{on } H_k \\
\kappa(n, n - j) &amp; \text{on } G_{m - k} \\
\chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - \frac{k + 1}{2}} &amp; \text{on } H_k' \\
\chi_V | |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n + \frac{1}{2}(j - k - 1)} &amp; \text{on } H_{j - k}'.
\end{cases}
$$

Here the identification of Sects.
1.2 and 1.3 are used on Levi factors.
Also $Q_{jk} \subset M_j$ is the parabolic subgroup:

$$
Q_{jk} \simeq \left\{ \left( \begin{array}{c|c} * &amp; * \\ \hline 0 &amp; * \\ &amp; j - k \end{array} \right) \right\} \times G_{m - j}
$$

and $Q_{jk}^{\prime} \subset M_j^{\prime}$ is the parabolic subgroup

$$
Q_{jk}^{\prime} \simeq \left\{ \left( \left( \begin{array}{c|c} * &amp; * \\ \hline 0 &amp; * \\ j - k &amp; k \end{array} \right), \varepsilon \right) \in H_j^1 \right\} \cdot G_{n - j}^{\prime}.
$$

Thus the Levi factor of $Q_{jk}$ (resp.
$Q_{jk}^{\prime}$) is isomorphic to $H_k \times H_{j-k} \times G_{m-j}$ (resp.
$H_{j-k}^{\prime} \cdot H_k^{\prime} \cdot G_{n-j}^{\prime}$).

## §3.

Proof of Theorem 2.1

An $L^2$ variant of the type of argument we give here appears in [13], which motivated our proof.
A finite field version appeared much earlier in [6].
Related global calculations appear in [4] and [5].

We begin by showing that for $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_m$ (resp.
$\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_n$) the set $\coprod_{n} \Theta_n(\pi[n])$ (resp.
$\coprod_{m} \Theta_m(\pi')$) contains at most one cuspidal element.
First note that if $G$ is any $\ell$-group and if $(\pi, E)$ is an irreducible square integrable representation, then there exists a $\mathbb{C}$-anti-linear isomorphism

$$
\xi \colon E \to \tilde{E} \tag{3.1}
$$

which intertwines $\pi$ and $\tilde{\pi}$.
Explicitly, if for $v \in E$ and $\tilde{v} \in \tilde{E}$ we let

$$
\phi_{v, \tilde{v}}(g) = \langle \pi(g^{-1}) v, \tilde{v} \rangle,
$$

then $\xi$ is defined by

On the local theta-correspondence

$$
\langle v', \xi(v) \rangle = \int_{G} \phi_{v', \tilde{v}}(g) \overline{\phi_{v, \tilde{v}}(g)} \, dg
$$

for some choice of $\tilde{v} \in \tilde{E}, \tilde{v} \neq 0$.

For the oscillator representation $(\omega, S)$ of $\tilde{S}p(W)$, realized in the Schrödinger model associated to a polarization $W'$, $W''$ of $W$, define, for $\varphi \in S$ and $x \in W'$,

$$
\bar{\omega}(g) \varphi(x) = \overline{(\omega(g) \bar{\varphi})(x)}. \tag{3.2}
$$

Note that $(\bar{\omega}, S)$ is just the oscillator representation associated to the character $\bar{\psi} = \psi^{-1}$.

Returning to a dual pair $\tilde{\alpha}_{V,W} \colon G_m \times G_n' \to \tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W})$ suppose that $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_m$ and $\pi' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_n'$ and that $\lambda \in \mathrm{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n'}(\omega_{m,n}, \pi \otimes \pi')$.
Let

$$
\bar{\lambda}(\varphi) = (\xi \otimes \xi')(\lambda(\bar{\varphi})) \tag{3.3}
$$

where $\xi$ and $\xi'$ are defined as above, for some fixed choices of $\tilde{v}, \tilde{v}'$.
Then $\bar{\lambda} \in \mathrm{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n'}(\bar{\omega}_{m,n}, \tilde{\pi} \otimes \tilde{\pi}')$ where

$$
\bar{\omega}_{m,n} = \tilde{\alpha}_{V,W}^*(\bar{\omega}). \tag{3.4}
$$

If we let $\bar{V}$ (resp.
$\bar{W}$) denote the space $V, -(, )$ (resp.
$W, -\langle , \rangle$), then it is easily checked that

$$
\tilde{\alpha}_{\bar{V},W} = \tilde{\alpha}_{V,\bar{W}} \tag{3.5}
$$

and that

$$
\tilde{\alpha}_{\bar{V},W}^*(\omega) = \tilde{\alpha}_{\bar{V},\bar{W}}^*(\omega) = \tilde{\alpha}_{\bar{V},W}^*(\bar{\omega}). \tag{3.6}
$$

Now suppose that $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_m$, and $\pi_i' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_{n_i}'$, $i = 1,2$, with

$$
\pi[n_i] \in \Theta_m(\pi_i'), \quad i = 1,2
$$

and fix a non-zero $\lambda_i \in \mathrm{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n'}(\omega_{m,n_i}, \pi[n_i] \otimes \pi_i')$.
For convenience we write $V = V_m$, $G = G_m$, $W = W_n$, $G' = G_n'$, $W_i = W_{n_i}$, and $G_i' = G_{n_i}'$, $i = 1,2$, where $W_n = W_{n_1} + \bar{W}_{n_2}$.
The isomorphism

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathbf{W} = V \otimes W \\
\simeq V \otimes W_1 + V \otimes \bar{W}_2 \tag{3.7}
\end{array}
$$

induces, in the terminology of [11], a see-saw dual pair in $Sp(\mathbf{W})$:

$$
\begin{array}{c}
O(V) \times O(V) \\
\Delta \Big| \\
O(V)
\end{array}
\quad
\begin{array}{c}
Sp(W) \\
\uparrow \\
Sp(W_1) \times Sp(W_2).
\end{array} \tag{3.8}
$$

Here we identify $Sp(\bar{W}_2) = Sp(W_2)$, and the horizontal sloping lines connect members of a dual pair.
Setting $W_i = V \otimes W_i$ we obtain a commutative diagram:

$$
\begin{array}{c}
G \times G_1' \times G \times G_2' \xrightarrow{\tilde{\beta}} \tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W}) \\
\tilde{\alpha}_{V,W_1} \times \tilde{\alpha}_{\bar{V},W_2} \Bigg\downarrow \\
\tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W}_1) \times \tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W}_2) \xrightarrow{\tilde{\lambda}} \tilde{S}p(\mathbf{W})
\end{array} \tag{3.9}
$$

S.S.
Kudla

which defines $\tilde{\beta}$, and since

$$
\tilde {j} ^ {*} (\omega) = \omega_ {1} \otimes \omega_ {2} \tag {3.10}
$$

we have

$$
\tilde {\beta} ^ {*} (\omega) = \omega_ {m, n _ {1}} \otimes \bar {\omega} _ {m, n _ {2}}. \tag {3.11}
$$

Let $\tilde{\Delta}\colon G\to G\times G$, be the diagonal map.
A routine calculation then shows:

**Lemma 3.1.** As homomorphisms from $G\times G_1^\prime \times G_2^\prime$ to $\hat{S} p(\mathbf{W})$:

$$
\tilde {\beta} \circ (\tilde {\Delta} \times 1) = (\delta \otimes 1) \left(\tilde {\alpha} _ {V, W} \circ (1 \times \tilde {i})\right)
$$

where, for $(h,\varepsilon)\in G$

$$
\delta (h, \varepsilon) = ((- 1) ^ {n _ {2} (n _ {1} + 1)}, \det h) _ {\ell}. \quad \square
$$

Now consider, for $\lambda_{i}$, $i = 1,2$, as above:

$$
\lambda_ {1} \otimes \bar {\lambda} _ {2} \in \operatorname {H o m} _ {G \times G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}} \left(\omega_ {m, n _ {1}} \otimes \bar {\omega} _ {m, n _ {2}}, \pi [ n _ {1} ] \otimes \pi_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \pi [ n _ {2} ] \otimes \tilde {\pi} _ {2} ^ {\prime}\right).
$$

Viewing this as a homomorphism for $\Delta(G) \times G_1' \times G_2' \simeq G \times G_1' \times G_2'$ and applying (3.11) and the lemma, we obtain a non-zero homomorphism:

$$
\lambda_ {1} \otimes \bar {\lambda} _ {2} \in \operatorname {H o m} _ {G \times G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}} ((1 \times \tilde {i}) ^ {*} \omega_ {m, n}, (\delta^ {- 1} \pi [ n _ {1} ] \otimes \pi [ n _ {2} ]) \otimes \pi_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \tilde {\pi} _ {2} ^ {\prime}).
$$

Recall that $\pi [n] = \kappa (n,a)\cdot \pi$, for $\kappa (n,a)$ defined by (1.1.3) and $a = a(\pi)$ defined in §1.6.
Also, applying Lemma 1.1.1, we have:

$$
\delta^ {- 1} \cdot \kappa \left(n _ {1}, a\right) \kappa \left(n _ {2}, a\right) ^ {- 1} = \kappa (n, 0) \tag {3.12}
$$

and so

$$
\lambda_ {1} \otimes \bar {\lambda} _ {2} \in \operatorname {H o m} _ {G \times G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}} ((1 \times \tilde {i}) ^ {*} \omega_ {m, n} ^ {\mathrm {g}}, (\pi \otimes \tilde {\pi}) \otimes \pi_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \tilde {\pi} _ {2} ^ {\prime})
$$

where $\omega_{m,n}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is as in Remark 2.4.

Finally, composing with the quotient $\pi \otimes \tilde{\pi} \to \mathbf{1}_G \to 0$, we obtain a non-zero element:

$$
\lambda \in \operatorname {H o m} _ {G \times G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}} ((1 \times \tilde {i}) ^ {*} \omega_ {m, n} ^ {\mathrm {g}}, \mathbf {1} _ {G} \otimes \pi_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \tilde {\pi} _ {2} ^ {\prime}). \tag {3.13}
$$

We may now apply the following invariant distribution theorem of Rallis [13, Thm. II, 1.1]:

**Theorem 3.2 (Rallis).** (i) Realize $(\omega_{m,n},S)$ in the Schrödinger model associated to the polarization

$$
\mathbf {W} _ {m, n} = V _ {m} \otimes W _ {n} ^ {\prime} + V _ {m} \otimes W _ {n} ^ {\prime \prime},
$$

i.e. $S = S(V_{m}\otimes W_{n}^{\prime \prime})$ and let $\omega_{m,n}^{\mathrm{g}}$ be as in Remark 2.4. Then

$$
(S ^ {*}) ^ {O (V _ {m})} = \operatorname {s p a n} \left\{\omega_ {m, n} \left(g ^ {\prime}\right) \delta_ {0} \mid g ^ {\prime} \in G _ {n} ^ {\prime} \right\}
$$

where $S^*$ is the space of linear functionals on $S$ and $\delta_0$ is the delta distribution at the origin.

(ii) Assume that $V_0 = \{0\}$ so that $V_m$ and $G_m$ are split and realize $(\omega_{m,n}, S)$ in the Schrödinger model associated to the polarization

$$
\mathbf {W} _ {m, n} = V _ {m} ^ {\prime} \otimes W _ {n} + V _ {m} ^ {\prime \prime} \otimes W _ {n},
$$

On the local theta-correspondence

i.e. $S = S(V_{m}^{\prime}\otimes W_{n})$.
Then, as a representation of $G_{n}^{\prime}$, $\omega_{m,n}$ factors through $Sp(W_{n})$ and

$$
(S ^ {*}) ^ {S p \left(W _ {n}\right)} = \operatorname {s p a n} \left\{\omega_ {m, n} (g) \delta_ {0} \mid g \in G _ {m} \right\}
$$

where $\delta_0$ is, again, the delta distribution at the origin.

Corollary 3.3 (Rallis).
With the notation of the preceding theorem:

(i) The space of $O(V_{m})$-coinvariants $S_{O(V_{m})}$ is isomorphic to a submodule of $I_n'(\chi_V \cdot ||\frac{1}{2}\ell)$ i.e.

$$
S _ {O (V _ {m})} \hookrightarrow I _ {n} ^ {\prime} \left(\chi_ {V} | | ^ {\frac {1}{2} \ell}\right).
$$

(ii) The space of $Sp(W_{n})$-coinvariants, $S_{Sp(W_{n})}$ is isomorphic to a submodule of $I_{m}(||\frac{1}{2}(\ell - m - 1) - n|)$, i.e.

Now

$$
\begin{array}{c} S _ {S p (W _ {n})} \hookrightarrow I _ {m} (| | ^ {\frac {1}{2} (\ell - m - 1) - n}). \\ \mathrm {H o m} _ {G \times G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}} ((1 \times \tilde {i}) ^ {*} \omega_ {m, n}, \mathbf {1} _ {G} \otimes \pi_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \pi_ {2} ^ {\prime}) \\ \Bigg | \Bigg | \\ \mathrm {H o m} _ {G \times G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}} (S _ {O (V _ {m})}, \pi_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \tilde {\pi} _ {2} ^ {\prime}) \end{array}
$$

so that $\lambda \neq 0$ implies that $\pi_1' \otimes \tilde{\pi}_2'$ is a $G_1' \times G_2'$ constituent of

$$
I _ {n} ^ {\prime} \left(\chi_ {V} \right| \left| \frac {1}{2} \ell\right) \left| _ {G _ {1} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {2} ^ {\prime}}. \tag {3.14}
$$

Since $\pi_1'$ and $\pi_2'$ are cuspidal, we need only consider the cuspidal part of (3.14), and this is easily done via the following result on the orbit structure of $P_n' \setminus G_n' / (G_{n_1}' \times G_{n_2}')$.

Proposition 3.4. (i) Suppose that $W_{n} = W_{n_{1}} + W_{n_{2}}$ with $n_1\geq n_2$.
Let $X^{\prime}$ be the space of maximal isotropic subspaces of $W_{n}$.
Then the $Sp(W_{n_1})\times Sp(W_{n_2})$ orbits in $X^{\prime}$ are parameterized by $t$ with, $0\leq t\leq n_{2} = \min (n_{1},n_{2})$ where

$$
\mathcal {C} _ {t} ^ {\prime} = \left\{U \in X ^ {\prime} \mid \dim (U \cap W _ {n _ {1}}) = t + n _ {1} - n _ {2}, \dim (U \cap \bar {W} _ {n _ {2}}) = t \right\}.
$$

(ii) Suppose that $V = V_{m_1} + \overline{V}_{m_2}$ with $m_1 \geq m_2$.
Let $X$ be the space of maximal isotropic subspaces of $V$.
Then the $O(V_{m_1}) \times O(V_{m_2})$ orbits in $X$ are parameterized by $t$ with $0 \leq t \leq m_2$ where

$$
\mathcal {C} _ {t} = \left\{U \in X \mid \dim U \cap V _ {m _ {1}} = m _ {1} - m _ {2} + t, \dim U \cap V _ {m _ {2}} = t \right\}.
$$

(iii) Moreover, the stabilizer in $Sp(W_{n_1}) \times Sp(W_{n_2})$ (resp.
$O(V_{m_1}) \times O(V_{m_2})$) of a point $x$ of $X'$ (resp.
$X$) is contained in a proper parabolic subgroup unless $n_1 = n_2$ (resp.
$m_1 = m_2$) and $x \in \mathcal{C}_0$.

(iv) If $n_1 = n_2$ then, in $X'$,

$$
\mathcal {C} _ {0} ^ {\prime} \simeq S p \left(W _ {n _ {1}}\right)
$$

and the action of $Sp(W_{n_1})\times Sp(W_{n_1})$ is given by

$$
\left(g _ {1}, g _ {2}\right): x \mapsto g _ {1} x g _ {2} ^ {- 1}.
$$

S.S.
Kudla

If $m_{1} = m_{2}$, then, in $X$,

$$
\mathcal{C}_{0} \simeq O(V_{m_{1}})
$$

and the action of $O(V_{m_1}) \times O(\bar{V}_{m_2}) \simeq O(V_{m_1}) \times O(V_{m_1})$ is given by

$$
(h_{1}, h_{2}) \colon x \mapsto h_{1} \times h_{2}^{-1}.
$$

Proof.
To lighten the notation we write $W = W_{n}$, $W_{i} = W_{n_{i}}$, $i = 1,2$, $G' = Sp(W)$ and $G_{i}' = Sp(W_{i})$.
If $U \in X'$, write

$$
U_{i} = U \cap W_{i} \tag{3.15}
$$

and let

$$
d_{i} = \dim U_{i}. \tag{3.16}
$$

Then, letting $\mathrm{pr}_i$ denote the projection of $W$ to $W_i$,

$$
W_{i} \supset U_{i}^{\perp} \supset \mathrm{pr}_{i}(U) \supset U_{i} \tag{3.17}
$$

and so, counting dimensions, we see that

$$
\mathrm{pr}_{i}(U) = U_{i}^{\perp} \tag{3.18}
$$

$$
\dim U_{1}^{\perp} / U_{1} = \dim U_{2}^{\perp} / U_{2},
$$

and

$$
d_{1} - d_{2} = n_{1} - n_{2}. \tag{3.19}
$$

Define an anti-isometry (for $W_{1}$ and $\bar{W}_{2}$ induced forms)

$$
\phi_{U} \colon U_{1}^{\perp} / U_{1} \xrightarrow{\sim} U_{2}^{\perp} / U_{2} \tag{3.20}
$$

by the condition, $\forall u \in U$

$$
\mathrm{pr}_{2}(u) + U_{2} = \phi_{U}(\mathrm{pr}_{1}(u) + U_{1}).
$$

Note that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
0 = \langle u, u' \rangle \\
= \langle u_{1}, u_{1}' \rangle + \langle u_{2}, u_{2}' \rangle \\
\end{array}
$$

if $u_{i} = \mathrm{pr}_{i}(u)$, $u_{i}' = \mathrm{pr}_{i}(u')$.
Clearly the integer $t = d_{2}$, $0 \leq t \leq n_{2}$ is an invariant of the $G_{1}' \times G_{2}'$ orbit of $U$, and, letting

$$
\mathcal{C}_{t}' = \{U \in X' \mid d_{2}(U) = t\} \tag{3.21}
$$

we have:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{t}' \simeq \left\{ (U_{1}, U_{2}, \phi) \mid U_{1} \in X_{d_{1}}'(W_{1}), U_{2} \in X_{d_{2}}'(W_{2}) \text{ and } \phi \in \operatorname{Isom}(U_{1}^{\perp} / U_{1}, U_{2}^{\perp} / U_{2}) \right\}. \tag{3.22}
$$

Here $X_{k}'(W)$ is the space of isotropic $k$-planes in $W$, etc. Note that here we use $W_{2}$ not $\bar{W}_{2}$.
The action of $G_{1}' \times G_{2}' \ni (g_{1}, g_{2})$ on the right side of (3.22) is given by

$$
(g_{1}, g_{2}) \colon (U_{1}, U_{2}, \phi) \mapsto (g_{1} U_{1}, g_{2} U_{2}, g_{2} \circ \phi \circ g_{1}^{-1}). \tag{3.23}
$$

Statements (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) are now clear in the symplectic case.
The proof in the orthogonal case is analogous and hence is omitted.
$\square$

On the local theta-correspondence

Thus the cuspidal representation $\pi_1' \otimes \tilde{\pi}_2'$ can only occur in (3.14) if $n_1 = n_2$, and in that case it must occur as a constituent of the representation $(\sigma_{n_1}', S(G_{n_1}')')$ where

$$
\sigma_{n_1}'(g_1, g_2) \varphi(x) = \varphi(g_1^{-1} \times g_2)
$$

for $x \in G_{n_1}'$ and $\varphi \in S(G_{n_1}')$.
This implies, as usual, that $\pi_2' \simeq \pi_1'$ and so, the uniqueness of $\theta(\pi)$ is proved.
The proof of uniqueness of $\theta(\pi')$ is completely analogous so we omit it.

To complete the proof of Theorem 2.1 we must show that every element of the set $\Theta_{n(\pi)}(\pi[n(\pi)])$ is cuspidal.
For convenience let $n = n(\pi)$, and suppose that $\pi' \in \Theta_n(\pi[n])$ is such that

$$
\pi' \hookrightarrow I_j'(\sigma' \otimes \rho')
$$

for some $j &gt; 0$.
Then, using Theorem 2.8, we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim \operatorname{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n'}(\omega_{m,n}, \pi[n] \otimes \pi') &amp;\leq \dim \operatorname{Hom}_{G_m \times G_n'}(\omega_{m,n}, \pi[n] \otimes I_j'(\sigma' \otimes \rho')) \\
&amp;= \dim \operatorname{Hom}_{G_m \times M_j'}(\tau_j', \pi[n] \otimes (\sigma' \otimes \rho')) \\
&amp;\leq \sum_{k=0}^{\min(m,j)} \dim \operatorname{Hom}_{G_m \times M_j'}(\tau_{jk}', \pi[n] \otimes (\sigma' \otimes \rho')).
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\pi[n]$ is cuspidal only the $k = 0$ term can contribute, and that term gives:

$$
\dim \operatorname{Hom}_{G_m \times H_j' \times G_{n-j}'}(\omega_{m,n-j}, (\xi_{j0}')^{-1} \pi[n] \otimes (\sigma' \otimes \rho')).
$$

This vanishes unless $\sigma' = \chi_V|$ and $\rho' \in \Theta_{n-j}(\pi[n-j])$.
Here we use the fact that (Lemma 1.1.1) $\kappa(n,n-j)^{-1} \cdot \kappa(n,a) = \kappa(n-j,a)$ so that

$$
\kappa(n,n-j)^{-1} \cdot \pi[n] = \pi[n-j]. \tag{3.24}
$$

But, by hypothesis, $\Theta_{n-j}(\pi[n-j]) = \emptyset$, and so no such $\pi'$ can occur.
This finishes the proof of Theorem 2.1 (i); the proof of (ii) is analogous and so will be omitted.

## §4.

Proof of Theorem 2.5

First suppose that $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_m$.
Then, via Theorem 2.1, we may assume that $n &gt; n(\pi)$ and that $\pi' \in \Theta_n(\pi[n])$ satisfies

$$
\pi' \hookrightarrow I_j'(\sigma' \otimes \rho')
$$

with $j &gt; 0$ maximal.
Here $\sigma' \in \mathrm{Irr} H_j'$ and, since $j$ is maximal, $\rho' \in \mathrm{Irr}_c G_{n-j}'$.
Now the argument of the last part of the proof of Theorem 2.1 implies that $\sigma' = \chi_V|$ and $\rho' \in \Theta_{n-j}(\pi[n-j])$.
But now, since $\rho'$ is cuspidal, Theorem 2.1 implies that $n-j = n(\pi)$ and $\rho' = \theta(\pi)$.
Thus

$$
\pi' \hookrightarrow I_{\mathbf{s}}'(\sigma_1' \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_j' \otimes \theta(\pi))
$$

where $\mathbf{s} = (1, \dots, 1)$, $|\mathbf{s}| = j$ and, for $1 \leq k \leq j$,

$$
\sigma_k' = \chi_V| \quad |^{\frac{1}{2}\ell - n + k - 1}.
$$

S.S.
Kudla

This proves part (ii) of Theorem 2.5 in the case when $ r = 0 $, since a cuspidal $ \pi $ is never ambiguous.

Next suppose that $ \pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G\_{m} $ with

$$
\pi \hookrightarrow I _ {n} \left(\sigma_ {1} \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_ {r} \otimes \rho\right) \tag {4.1}
$$

where $ \sigma*{i}\in \mathrm{Irr}*{c}H*{s*{i}} $ and $ \rho \in \mathrm{Irr}_cG_{m - |n|} $.
Then $ \pi \hookrightarrow I*j(\sigma_1\otimes \rho_1) $ where $ j = s*{1} $ and

$$
\rho_ {1} \hookrightarrow I _ {(s _ {2}, \dots , s _ {r})} (\sigma_ {2} \otimes \dots \otimes \sigma_ {r} \otimes \rho).
$$

Suppose that $ \pi^{\prime}\in \Theta\_{n}(\pi [n]) $ and consider:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {G _ {m} \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \left(\omega_ {m, n}, \pi [ n ] \otimes \pi^ {\prime}\right) \\ \leq \dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {G _ {m} \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \left(\omega_ {m, n}, I _ {j} \left(\sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ]\right) \otimes \pi^ {\prime}\right) \\ = \dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {M _ {j} \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \left(\tau_ {j}, \sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ] \otimes \pi^ {\prime}\right) \\ \lesssim \sum_ {k = 0} ^ {\min  (n, j)} \dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {M j \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \left(\tau_ {j k}, \sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ] \otimes \pi^ {\prime}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $ \sigma\_{1} $ is cuspidal, only the terms with $ k = j $, or $ j = 1 $ and $ k = 0 $ can contribute.
The first of these yields

$$
\begin{array}{l} \dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {M _ {j} \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \left(\operatorname {i n d} _ {M _ {j} \times P _ {j}} ^ {M _ {j} \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \xi_ {j j} \sigma_ {j} \otimes \omega_ {m - j, n - j}, \sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ] \otimes \pi^ {\prime}\right) \\ = \dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {M _ {j} \times M _ {j} ^ {\prime}} \left(\xi_ {j j} \sigma_ {j} \otimes \omega_ {m - j, n - j}, \sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ] \otimes \bar {R} _ {j} ^ {\prime} \left(\pi^ {\prime}\right)\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

This can be non-zero only if there exists a constituent

$$
\sigma_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \rho_ {1} ^ {\prime} \in J H (\bar {R} _ {j} ^ {\prime} (\pi^ {\prime}))
$$

such that

$$
\operatorname {H o m} _ {M _ {j} \times M _ {j} ^ {\prime}} \left(\xi_ {j j} \sigma_ {j} \otimes \omega_ {m - j, n - j}, \sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ] \otimes \sigma_ {1} ^ {\prime} \otimes \rho_ {1} ^ {\prime}\right) \neq 0.
$$

Since $ \sigma\_{1} $ is cuspidal we conclude that, with the notation of (2.7),

$$
\xi_ {j j} ^ {- 1} \sigma_ {1} ^ {\prime} = \theta \left(\xi_ {j j} ^ {- 1} \sigma_ {1}\right);
$$

and so, using the value of $ \xi\_{jj} $ given in Theorem 2.8,

$$
\sigma_ {1} ^ {\prime} = \chi_ {V} \theta \left(\sigma_ {1}\right). \tag {4.2}
$$

Since $ \xi*{jj}|*{G\_{m - j}} = \kappa (n,n - j) $, and recalling (3.24), we must also have

$$
\rho_ {1} ^ {\prime} \in \Theta_ {n - j} \left(\rho_ {1} [ n - j ]\right). \tag {4.3}
$$

Next consider the case $ k = 0 $, $ j = 1 $.
This term contributes:

$$
\dim \operatorname {H o m} _ {M _ {1} \times G _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \left(\xi_ {1 0} \omega_ {m - 1, n}, \sigma_ {1} \otimes \rho_ {1} [ n ] \otimes \pi^ {\prime}\right)
$$

and so can be non-zero only if

$$
\sigma_ {1} = | | ^ {\frac {1}{2} \ell - n - 1} \tag {4.4}
$$

and

$$
\pi^ {\prime} \in \Theta_ {n} \left(\rho_ {1} [ n ]\right). \tag {4.5}
$$

On the local theta-correspondence

An easy inductive argument on $m$ now shows that $[\pi']$ is as in (ii) of Theorem 2.5. Here note that if $m = 0$ every $\pi \in \mathrm{Irr} G_0$ is cuspidal.
For example, if $n \geq |\mathbf{s}| + n(\rho)$ the same inequality is true for $n - s_1$ and $|\mathbf{s}| - s_1 + n(\rho)$, or for $n$ and $|\mathbf{s}| - 1 + n(\rho)$.
Therefore, the first case in the above argument and the inductive hypothesis applied to $\rho_1'$ and $\rho_1[n - j]$ would yield

$$
[\pi'] = [\chi_V \theta(\sigma_1), \dots, \chi_V \theta(\sigma_r), \chi_V |^{j \ell - n}, \dots, \chi_V |^{j \ell - |\mathbf{s}| - n(\rho) - 1}, \theta(\rho)]
$$

while the second case and the inductive hypothesis applied to $\pi'$ and $\rho_1[n]$ would yield

$$
[\pi'] = [\chi_V \theta(\sigma_2), \dots, \chi_V \theta(\sigma_r), \chi_V |^{j \ell - 1 - n}, \chi_V |^{j \ell - n}, \chi_V |^{j \ell - |\mathbf{s}| - n(\rho) - 1}, \theta(\rho)],
$$

provided $\sigma_1 = |^{j\ell - n - 1}$.
But then

$$
\chi_V \theta(\sigma_1) = \chi_V |^{j\ell - n - 1},
$$

so that the two expressions agree (up to permutation, i.e. associativity).
The general argument proceeds in the same way, taking account of the various cases, so we omit it.

Note that we have used precisely the inclusion (4.1) and the fact that $\pi'$ was an arbitrary element of $\Theta_n(\pi[n])$.
Thus if $\pi$ were strongly ambiguous, as defined in Remark 1.5.2, we would obtain two non-associate expressions for $[\pi']$ since by Theorem 2.1 (ii), $\theta(\rho)$ and $\theta(v\rho)$ are unequal for $\rho \neq v\rho$.
No such $\pi'$ can exist and so statement (i) of Theorem 2.5 is proved.

Finally, observe that if $n &lt; n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}|$, and if we iterate the inductive step above, then the 'second case' must occur at least $t = n(\rho) + |\mathbf{s}| - n$ times.
This can only happen if $\pi \hookrightarrow I_{\mathbf{s}}(\sigma_1 \otimes \ldots \otimes \sigma_r \otimes \rho)$ where some subsequence $\sigma_{i_1}, \ldots, \sigma_{i_t}$ of the $\sigma_j$'s is as in Corollary 2.7 (i), and so that part of Corollary 2.7 is proved.

Finally we note that Theorem 2.5 could have been proved in a symmetric way beginning with $\pi'$, and this symmetric proof yields Corollary 2.7 (ii).

§5.
Proof of Theorem 2.8

In this section we will compute the Jacquet modules $R_j(\omega_{m,n})$ and $R_j'(\omega_{m,n})$ with respect to maximal parabolics of $G_m$ and $G_n'$.
Such a computation is also contained in the proofs of [13], but since the precise statement (Theorem 2.8) which we need is not so easy to find in [13], and for the sake of completeness, we will give a detailed exposition.
Of course, since the computations in the two cases are essentially the same, we will only compute $R_j'(\omega_{m,n})$.

For convenience, since $m$ and $n$ are fixed, we will write $V = V_m$, $W = W_n$, and $\omega = \omega_{m,n}$.

Fix $j$ with $1 \leq j \leq n$ and let $P_j' = M_j' N_j'$ and

$$
W = W_j' + W_j^0 + W_j'''
$$

be as in Sect.
1.3. Then, associated to the decomposition

$$
\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W}' + \mathbf{W}^0 + \mathbf{W}''
$$

S.S.
Kudla

with $\mathbf{W}' = V \otimes W_j'$, $\mathbf{W}^0 = V \otimes W_j^0$ and $\mathbf{W}'' = V \otimes W_j''$, we have a mixed model [8] of $(\omega, S)$ with

$$
S \simeq S (V ^ {j}) \otimes S ^ {0}.
$$

Here $(\omega_0,S^0)$ is a model of the representation $\omega_{m,n - j}$.
Let $n^{\prime}(c,d)\in N_j^{\prime}$, where

$$
n ^ {\prime} (c, d) = \left( \begin{array}{c c c} 1 &amp; {} ^ {t} c &amp; d - \frac {1}{2} \langle c, c \rangle \\ &amp; 1 &amp; c \\ &amp; &amp; 1 \end{array} \right)
$$

with $d = {}^t d \in M_r(\mathcal{E})$, $c = (c_1, \ldots, c_r)$, $c_j \in W^0$.
We view $c$ as an element of $\operatorname{Hom}(W^0, W'')$ via

$$
c \colon w ^ {0} \rightarrow \langle w ^ {0}, c \rangle = (\langle w ^ {0}, c _ {1} \rangle , \dots , \langle w ^ {0}, c _ {r} \rangle) \in \mathcal {E} ^ {r}.
$$

Then we have

$$
\omega \left(n ^ {\prime} (c, d)\right) \varphi (x) = \psi \left(\frac {1}{2} \operatorname {t r} (d (x, x))\right) \rho_ {0} \left(x ^ {t} c\right) \varphi (x) \tag {5.0}
$$

where $x \in V^j$, $\varphi(x) \in S^0$ and $\rho_0$ is the action of the Heisenberg group $H(\mathbf{W}^0)$ in $S^0$.

Let $N_{j0}^{\prime} = \{n^{\prime}(0,d)|d = {}^{t}d\in M_{j}(\mathcal{E})\}$.
Then a standard argument yields:

**Lemma 5.1.** There is an isomorphism of $G_{m} \times P_{j}^{\prime}$ modules

$$
S _ {N _ {j 0} ^ {\prime}} \xrightarrow {\sim} S (X _ {0}) \otimes S ^ {0}
$$

where

$$
X _ {0} = \{x \in V ^ {j} | (x, x) = 0 \}
$$

and such that the natural homomorphism $S \to S_{N_{j0}}$ is given by restriction to $X_0 \subset V^j$.
Let $\tilde{T} = S(X_0) \otimes S^0$.
The space $X_0$ has a decomposition

$$
X _ {0} = \prod_ {k = 0} ^ {\min  (j, m)} X _ {0 k} \tag {5.1}
$$

where $X_{0k} = \{x\in X_0|\dim \operatorname {span}x = k\}$.
Here and elsewhere

$$
\operatorname {s p a n} x = \operatorname {s p a n} \left\{x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {j} \right\}
$$

if $x = (x_{1},\ldots ,x_{j})\in V^{j}$.
This decomposition induces a $G_{m}\times P_{j}^{\prime}$ invariant filtration

$$
\tilde {T} = \tilde {T} ^ {(0)} \supset \tilde {T} ^ {(1)} \supset \dots \supset \tilde {T} ^ {(r)} \supset \{0 \} \tag {5.2}
$$

of $\tilde{T}$, where $r = \min (j,m)$, whose successive quotients $\tilde{T}_k = \tilde{T}^{(k)} / \tilde{T}^{(k + 1)}$ have the form

$$
\tilde {T} _ {k} \simeq S \left(X _ {0 k}\right) \otimes S ^ {0}. \tag {5.3}
$$

The representation of $G_{m} \times P_{j}^{\prime}$ on $\tilde{T}_{k}$ may be identified with an induced representation as follows:

For simplicity let $G = G_{m} \times H_{j}^{\prime} \times G_{n - j}^{\prime}$ and $N = N_{j}^{\prime}$.
Also let $X = X_{0k}$ and fix $x_0 \in X$ given by

$$
x _ {0} = (0, \dots , 0, v _ {1} ^ {\prime}, \dots , v _ {k} ^ {\prime}) \tag {5.4}
$$

On the local theta-correspondence

where $v_1', \ldots, v_m'$ are as in Sect.
1.1. Let $H$ be the stabilizer of $x_0$ in $G$ where we view $G$ as acting on $X$ via its projection to $G_m \times H_j'$.
Define a representation $\tau$ of $H \rtimes N$ in $S^0$ by:

$$
\tau(h) = \xi(h) \xi'(h) \omega_0(h) \tag{5.5}
$$

where $\xi$ and $\xi'$ are characters given by:

$$
\xi(h, \varepsilon) = \kappa(n, n - j)(h, \varepsilon) \tag{5.6}
$$

for $(h, \varepsilon) \in G_m$ and,

$$
\xi'(g, \varepsilon) = \chi(g, \varepsilon)^\ell | \det g |^{\ell/2} (d(V_m), \det g)_\ell \tag{5.7}
$$

for $(g, \varepsilon) \in H_j'$, and let

$$
\tau(n) = \rho_0(x_0^t c) \tag{5.8}
$$

if $n = n'(c, d) \in N$.

Then we have:

**Lemma 5.2.** The representation $\omega$ of $G \rtimes N$ on $\tilde{T}_k$ is given by

$$
\omega \cong \mathfrak{h} - \operatorname{ind}_{H \rtimes N}^{G \rtimes N} \tau.
$$

**Proof.** Consider first the restriction of $\omega$ to $G$.
We have

$$
\left. \omega \right|_G \simeq \mu \otimes \xi \xi' \omega_0
$$

where $(\mu, S(X))$ is the natural action of $G$ on $S(X)$ induced by its action on $X$.
Thus there is a natural isomorphism:

$$
\left. \omega \right|_G \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathfrak{h} - \operatorname{ind}_H^G(\tau|_H) \tag{5.9}
$$

$$
\varphi \rightarrow f
$$

given by

$$
f(g) = (\xi \xi' \omega_0)(g) \varphi(g^{-1} x_0).
$$

On the other hand, there is a natural isomorphism

$$
\mathfrak{h} - \operatorname{ind}_H^G(\tau|_H) \xrightarrow{\sim} (\mathfrak{h} - \operatorname{ind}_{H \rtimes N}^{G \rtimes N} \tau)|_G \tag{5.10}
$$

$$
f \longrightarrow F
$$

given by

$$
F(g, n) = \tau(1, g(n)) f(g).
$$

Combining (5.9) and (5.10) we see that it is sufficient to check that the map

$$
\varphi \rightarrow F
$$

given by

$$
F(g, n) = \tau(1, g(n)) (\xi \xi' \omega_0)(g) \varphi(g^{-1} x_0) \tag{5.11}
$$

intertwines the $N$-actions, and this follows by a direct calculation.
$\square$

To compute $\omega_N$ we apply the following:

S.S.
Kudla

Lemma 5.3. There is a natural isomorphism

$$
(\mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H \bowtie N} ^ {G \bowtie N} \tau) _ {N} \xrightarrow {\sim} \mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H} ^ {G} (\tau_ {N})
$$

induced by restriction to $G$ , i.e. by the inverse of (5.11).
In particular the natural homomorphism

$$
\mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H \bowtie N} ^ {G \bowtie N} \tau \rightarrow (\mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H \bowtie N} ^ {G \bowtie N} \tau) _ {N}
$$

is given by

$$
F \mapsto \iota (f)
$$

where

$$
f (g) = F (g, 1),
$$

and where

$$
\iota \colon \mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H} ^ {G} (\tau | _ {H}) \rightarrow \mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H} ^ {G} \tau_ {N}
$$

is the homomorphism corresponding to

$$
\iota \colon \tau | _ {H} \to \tau_ {N}.
$$

Proof.
It is clear that

$$
(\mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H \bowtie N} ^ {G \bowtie N} \tau) [ N ] \subset \mathfrak {H} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H} ^ {G} (\tau [ N ])
$$

i.e. consider $S^0 [N]$ -valued functions.
It then remains to check that these two spaces coincide.
We omit the details.

To compute $\tau_{N}$ we consider the decomposition

$$
V _ {m} = V _ {k} ^ {\prime} + V _ {k} ^ {0} + V _ {k} ^ {\prime \prime}
$$

as in Sect.
1.2, and we realize the representation $(\omega_0,S^0)$ in the mixed model associated to the corresponding decomposition

$$
\mathbf {W} ^ {0} = \mathbf {W} ^ {0 \prime} + \mathbf {W} ^ {0 0} + \mathbf {W} ^ {0 \prime \prime}
$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{0'} = V_k' \otimes W_j^0$ , $\mathbf{W}^{00} = V_k^0 \otimes W_j^0$ , and $\mathbf{W}^{0''} = V_k'' \otimes W_j^0$ .
Then

$$
S ^ {0} \simeq S \left(\left(W _ {j} ^ {0}\right) ^ {k}\right) \otimes S ^ {0 0} \tag {5.12}
$$

where $(\omega_{00}, S^{00})$ is a model for $\omega_{m - k, n - j}$ .
Since for any $n = n'(c, d) \in N$ ,

$$
x _ {0} ^ {t} c \in V _ {k} ^ {\prime \prime} \otimes W _ {j} ^ {0} = \mathbf {W} ^ {0 \prime \prime}
$$

we have:

$$
\rho_ {0} \left(x _ {0} ^ {t} c\right) \varphi (z) = \psi \left(\operatorname {t r} \langle z, x _ {0} ^ {t} c \rangle\right) \varphi (z) \tag {5.13}
$$

for $z \in (W_j^0)^k$ and $\varphi \in S^0$ .
It is then easy to check:

Lemma 5.4. There is a natural isomorphism

$$
S _ {N} ^ {0} \xrightarrow {\sim} S ^ {0 0}
$$

such that the quotient

$$
S ^ {0} \to S _ {N} ^ {0}
$$

is given by

$$
\varphi \mapsto \varphi (0)
$$

where $0\in (W_j^0)^k$

On the local theta-correspondence

Now observe that

$$
H \subset P _ {k} \times \left(H _ {j} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {n - j} ^ {\prime}\right) \vDash N
$$

where $P_{k}$ is the maximal parabolic as in Sect.
1.2, and that the representation $\tau_{N}$ of $H$ on $S_N^0\simeq S^{00}$ extends to a representation $\tilde{\tau}$ of this larger group as follows:

$$
\tilde {\tau} \mid_ {H _ {k}} = \xi_ {0} \tag {5.14}
$$

where

$$
\xi_ {0} = | | ^ {j - n}; \tag {5.15}
$$

$$
\tilde {\tau} \mid_ {G _ {m - k}} = \xi \omega_ {0 0} \tag {5.16}
$$

where $\xi$ is given by (5.6);

$$
\tilde {\tau} \mid_ {H _ {j} ^ {\prime}} = \xi^ {\prime} \tag {5.17}
$$

where $\xi^{\prime}$ is given by (5.7);

$$
\tilde {\tau} \mid_ {G _ {n - j} ^ {\prime}} = \omega_ {0 0} \tag {5.18}
$$

and $\tilde{\tau}$ is trivial on $N_{k} \times N_{j}^{\prime}$.
In particular we have proved that

$$
\left(\tilde {T} _ {k}\right) _ {N _ {j} ^ {\prime}} \xrightarrow {\sim} \mathbb {1} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H} ^ {G _ {m} \times H _ {j} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {n - j} ^ {\prime}} \left(\tilde {\tau} \mid_ {H}\right). \tag {5.19}
$$

Now we want to identify $(\tilde{T}_k)_{N_j'}$ with a representation induced from a parabolic subgroup.
Let

$$
R _ {j k} ^ {\prime} = \left\{\left(\left(\frac {*}{0} \Bigg | _ {k} ^ {*}\right), \varepsilon\right) \right\} \subset H _ {j} ^ {\prime}
$$

and define homomorphisms $\operatorname{pr}: R_{jk}^{\prime} \to H_{k}$ by

$$
\operatorname {p r} \left(\left(\frac {*}{0} \Bigg | _ {k} ^ {*}\right), \varepsilon\right) = a
$$

and $\operatorname{pr}: P_k \to H_k$ via (1.2.0).
Also define an inclusion $i: H_k \to P_k$ via (1.2.0).
Then, recalling that (1.2.0) was defined via $H_k \simeq GL(V_k') \hookrightarrow M_k$, whereas the components $v_1', \ldots, v_k'$ of $x_{0k}$ lie in $V_k''$, we have:

$$
H = \left\{\left(h, g, g ^ {\prime}\right) \in P _ {k} \times R _ {j k} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {n - j} ^ {\prime} \mid \operatorname {p r} (h) = ^ {t} \operatorname {p r} (g) ^ {- 1} \right\}. \tag {5.20}
$$

Then, since the set

$$
\left\{\left(i \left(^ {t} x\right), 1, 1\right) \in P _ {k} \times R _ {j k} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {n - j} ^ {\prime} \mid x \in H _ {k} \right\} \tag {5.21}
$$

gives a set of representatives for $H \setminus P_k \times R_{jk}' \times G_{n-j}'$, the representation

$$
\mu_ {j k} = \mathbb {1} - \operatorname {i n d} _ {H} ^ {P _ {k} \times R _ {j k} ^ {\prime} \times G _ {n - j} ^ {\prime}} \xi_ {0} \xi \omega_ {0 0} \otimes \xi^ {\prime} \otimes \omega_ {0 0}
$$

may be realized, by restriction to the set (5.21), on the space

$$
S (H _ {k}) \otimes S ^ {0 0}.
$$

Moreover, in that realization, the action of $P_{k} \times R_{jk}^{\prime} \times G_{n - j}^{\prime}$ is given by:

S.S.
Kudla

Lemma 5.4. Let $x \in H_k$ and $\varphi \in S(H_k) \otimes S^{00}$.

(i) If $(h,g)\in H_k\times H_k'\subset P_k\times R_{jk}'$, then

$$
\mu_{jk}(h, g) \varphi(x) = \chi_V(g) |\det g|^{\frac{1}{2} \ell + n - j} \varphi(^t h x g).
$$

where

$$
\chi_V(g) = \chi(g)'\ell(d(V), \det g)_\ell.
$$

(ii) If $g \in H_{j-k}' \subset R_{jk}'$, then

$$
\mu_{jk}(g) \varphi(x) = \chi_V(g) |\det g|^{\ell/2} \varphi(x).
$$

(iii) If $(h,g)\in G_{m - k}\times G_{n - j}^{\prime}$, then

$$
\mu_{jk}(h, g) \varphi(x) = \kappa(n, n - j)(h) \omega_{00}(h) \omega_{00}(g) \varphi(x).
$$

(iv) The unipotent radicals of $P_k$ and $R_{jk}'$ act trivially.

Proof.
Since the factor $G_{m-k} \times H_{j-k}' \times G_{n-j}'$ of $P_k \times R_{jk}' \times G_{n-j}'$ lies in $H$, and centralizes the set (5.21), and since $\xi_0$ is trivial on this factor, statements (ii) and (iii) follow from (5.7), (5.17), (5.6) and (5.16).
Statement (iv) is proved analogously.
Finally, to prove (i) suppress the inclusion $i: H_k \to P_k$ and consider

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mu_{jk}(h, g) \varphi(x) = f\left((^t x, 1, 1)(h, g, 1)\right) \\
= f\left((^t x h, g, 1)\right) \\
= f\left((^t \operatorname{pr}(g)^{-1}, g, 1)(^t \operatorname{pr}(g)^t x h, 1, 1)\right) \\
= |\det g|^{n - j + \ell/2} \chi_V(g) \varphi(^t h x g). \quad \square
\end{array}
$$

Finally we want to replace ‘g-ind’ with ‘ind’ so that we obtain Theorem 2.8(ii) with

$$
\xi_{jk}' = \begin{cases}
\delta_k^{-\frac{1}{2}} &amp; \text{on } H_k \\
\kappa(n, n - j) &amp; \text{on } G_{m-k} \\
\chi_V | \quad |^{\frac{1}{2} \ell + n - j} (\delta_{jk}' \cdot \delta_j')^{-\frac{1}{2}} &amp; \text{on } H_k' \\
\chi_V | \quad |^{\frac{1}{2} \ell} (\delta_{jk}' \delta_j')^{-\frac{1}{2}} &amp; \text{on } H_{j-k}'.
\end{cases}
$$

where $\delta_j'$ (resp.
$\delta_{jk}'$) is the module of $P_j'$ (resp.
$R_{jk}'$) and $\delta_k$ is that of $P_k$.
Since

$$
\delta_{jk}' = \begin{cases}
| \quad |^{k - j} &amp; \text{on } H_k' \\
| \quad |^k &amp; \text{on } H_{j-k}',
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\delta_j' = | \quad |^{2n - j + 1} \quad \text{on } H_j',
$$

and

$$
\delta_k = | \quad |^{-(\ell - k - 1)} \quad \text{on } H_k,
$$

we obtain the claimed values for $\xi_{jk}'$.
