# Changing rotation intervals of endomorphisms of the circle

R.
Bamon$^{1}$, I.
Malta$^{2}$\*, and M.J.
Pacifico$^{3}$

$^{1}$ Departamento de Matemática, Universidad de Chile, Casilla 653, Santiago, Chile
$^{2}$ Departamento de Matemática PUC/RJ, Rua Marquês de São Vicente, 225, 22.453 - Rio de Janeiro, Brasil
$^{3}$ Instituto de Matemática, Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro, Caixa Postal 68530, 21.910 - Rio de Janeiro, Brasil

# Introduction

Let $\operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$ be the set of endomorphisms of the circle $S^1$, of degree one and class $C^r$, $r \geq 0$, endowed with the $C^r$ topology.
Given $\tilde{f}$ in $\operatorname{End}_1^0(S^1)$, let $f \colon \mathbb{R} \simeq$ be a lift of $\tilde{f}$ to $\mathbb{R}$, that is $\tilde{f} \circ \pi = \pi \circ f$ where $\pi \colon \mathbb{R} \to S^1 = \mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$ is the natural projection.
Since $\tilde{f}$ is of degree one, one has $f(x + 1) = f(x) + 1$ for all real $x$.

In [4], Newhouse, Palis and Takens introduced the concept of rotation set for endomorphisms of degree one, which generalizes the notion of the Poincaré rotation number of a homeomorphism homotopic to the identity.
This rotation set was defined by

$$
\rho(\tilde{f}) = \rho(f) = \text{closure} \left\{ \limsup_{n \to \infty} \frac{f^n(x) - x}{n} \,\middle|\, x \in \mathbb{R} \right\}.
$$

Clearly $\rho(\tilde{f})$ is defined up to translation by integers.
It was proved that $\rho(\tilde{f})$ is a compact interval and $\tilde{f}$ has periodic points if and only if $\rho(\tilde{f})$ intersects the set of rational numbers.
They also proved that the rotation set (or rotation interval) is a topological invariant and its end points vary continuously with $\tilde{f}$.
Later Ito [2] showed that it is not necessary to take the closure in the definition of rotation sets.

The concept of rotation intervals has raised a few interesting questions, for instance, it is natural to ask how the rotation interval varies in generic one-parameter families of degree one endomorphisms.
Clearly this question gives rise to the problem of describing the set, $\Sigma^r$, of endomorphisms for which the rotation interval remains unchanged by $C^r$ small perturbations.
The aim of this paper is to prove that $\Sigma^r$ is an open dense set in $\operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$ for $r \geq 1$, and to characterize the endomorphisms in $\Sigma^r$.
We also characterize the endomorphisms in the boundary of a connected component of $\Sigma^r$.

In order to state our results we need some definitions.

R.
Bamon et al.

Let $\operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ be the set of $C^r$ degree one endomorphisms of $\mathbb{R}$, that is, $f\colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is of class $C^r$ and satisfies $f(x + 1) = f(x) + 1$ for all real $x$.
We consider in $\operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ the $C^r$ topology.
Clearly any $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ defines an endomorphism $\tilde{f} \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$ and each $\tilde{f} \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$ defines, up to translation by integers, a unique $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$.
If $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ we denote by $\rho(f) = [\rho_1(f), \rho_2(f)]$ the rotation interval of $f$.

Definition.
i) Let $\tilde{f} \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$.
We say that $\rho(\tilde{f})$ is $C^r$-persistent if there exists a neighborhood $\mathcal{V}$ of $\tilde{f}$ in $\operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$ such that $\rho(\tilde{g}) = \rho(\tilde{f}) \bmod \mathbb{Z}$ for all $\tilde{g} \in \mathcal{V}$.

ii) Let $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$.
We say that $\rho_i(f), i = 1$ or $i = 2$ is $C^r$-persistent if there exists a neighborhood $\mathcal{V}$ of $f$ in $\operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ such that $\rho_i(g) = \rho_i(f)$ for all $g \in \mathcal{V}$.
If $\rho_1(f)$ and $\rho_2(f)$ are $C^r$-persistent we say that $\rho(f)$ is $C^r$-persistent.

Clearly $\rho(\tilde{f})$ is $C^r$-persistent if and only if $\rho(f)$ is $C^r$-persistent for some lift (and hence for all) $f$ of $\tilde{f}$.

Theorem A.
Let $\Sigma^r$ be the set of endomorphisms $\tilde{f} \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$, $r \geq 1$, such that $\rho(\tilde{f})$ is $C^r$-persistent.
Then

(i) $\Sigma^r$ is open and dense in $\operatorname{End}_1^r(S^1)$.

(ii) The end points of $\rho(\tilde{f})$, for $\tilde{f} \in \Sigma^r$, are rational numbers.

Remark.
In fact it will be shown that if $\rho(\tilde{f})$ is $C^r$-persistent, $r \geq 1$, then it is $C^1$-persistent.

Theorem A follows immediately from Theorem B below, where we give necessary and sufficient conditions on $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ for $\rho_1(f)$ or $\rho_2(f)$ to be $C^r$-persistent.

For $f\operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ and $p / q \in Q$, we define

$$
A _ {p, q} (f) = \{x \in \mathbb {R} \mid f ^ {q} (x) &gt; x + p \}
$$

$$
B _ {p, q} (f) = \{x \in \mathbb {R} \mid f ^ {q} (x) &lt; x + p \}.
$$

Where no confusion is likely we will omit the letter $f$ in the above sets.
The boundary of a set $S$ is denoted by $\partial S$.

Theorem B.
Let $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$, $r \geq 1$.
Then

(i) $\rho_1(f) = p / q$ if and only if there is an $a \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f^q(a) = a + p$ and $f^q(x) \geq a + p$ for all $x \geq a$.

(ii) $\rho_2(f) = p / q$ if and only if there is a $b \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f^q(b) = b + p$ and $f^q(x) \leq b + p$ for all $x \leq b$.

(iii) $\rho_1(f) = p / q$ is $C^r$-persistent if and only if $A_{p,q}$ and $B_{p,q}$ are non empty sets and there exists $a \in \partial A_{p,q}$ such that $f^q(x) &gt; a + p$ for all $x &gt; a$.

(iv) $\rho_2(f) = p / q$ is $C^r$-persistent if and only if $A_{p,q}$ and $B_{p,q}$ are non empty sets and there exists $b \in \partial B_{p,q}$ such that $f^q(x) &lt; b + p$ for all $x &lt; b$.

(v) Given $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ and $i = 1$ or $i = 2$, there exists $g \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$, $C^r$-close to $f$, such that

$$
\rho_ {i} (g) \in \mathbb {Q} \text{ is } C ^ {r} \text{-persistent. Moreover, if}
$$

$$
\rho_ {i} (f) \in \mathbb {Q} \text{ then we can obtain such a } g \text{ with}
$$

$$
\rho_ {i} (g) = \rho_ {i} (f).
$$

Changing rotation intervals of endomorphisms of the circle

Remarks.

1. As a consequence of (i) and (ii) of Theorem B we have that if $p / q$ is an interior point of $\rho(\bar{f})$ then there exist a repellor $z_0$ of period $q$ which has a homoclinic point $w_0$ .
   That is, $f^q(w_0) = z_0$ and there exist sequences $w_n \to z_0$ , $k_n \to \infty$ such that $f^{kn}(w_n) = w_0$ .
   This result implies, in particular, that $\bar{f}$ has positive topological entropy as has already been proved in [1].

2) From Theorem B it follows that an endomorphism in the boundary of a connected component of $\Sigma$ , generically presents either a unique saddle-node or a unique hyperbolic repellor having a unique homoclinic point of tangency.

For example, when $\rho_1(f) = 0$ and $\rho_2(f) &gt; 0$ the generic behavior is

![img-41.jpeg](img-41.jpeg)
Fig.

1. a Saddle-node; b Saddle-node; c Homoclinic point of tangency

We prove Theorem B in Sect.
3. In Sect.
2 we give some preliminaries results which will be used to prove Theorem B.

We are grateful to IMPA for a very kind hospitality.

# 2. Preliminaries

In this section we prove some technical lemmas which will be used to prove Theorem B.
The first one ((i) and (ii) of Theorem B) gives necessary and sufficient conditions for an end point of the rotation interval of $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ to be a rational number $p/q$ .
These conditions are given in terms of properties of $f^q$ .
The second one translates these properties, in the case we will need to deal with, in terms of properties of $f$ .
This lemma, together with the next one, allows us to perform perturbations of $f$ keeping control of $f^q$ .
Finally, the last lemma states that if an end point of the rotation interval of $f$ is irrational then it is not persistent by small perturbations of $f$ .

Let us first recall some simple properties of the rotation interval of $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ .

(i) $p / q\in \rho (f)$ if and only if there is an $x\in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f^{q}(x) = x + p$
(ii) $\rho (f^n) = n\rho (f),n\in \mathbb{N};$ in particular $\rho_{i}(f^{n}) = n\rho_{i}(f),i = 1,2;$
(iii) if $f \leq g$ then $\rho_i(f) \leq \rho_i(g), i = 1, 2$ .

The proof of these properties can be found in [3] and [4].

Now we observe that the $C^r$ -persistence of $\rho_i(f)$ is invariant by translation by integers.
That is, if $\rho_i(f)$ is $C^r$ -persistent and $g = f + k$ , $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ , then $\rho_i(g)$

R.
Bamon et al.

$= \rho_{i}(f) + k$ is $C^r$-persistent.
So, in all results about $C^r$-persistence of $\rho_{1}(f)$, we assume $\rho_{1}(f) \geq 0$.
The proofs of $C^r$-persistence of $\rho_{2}(f)$ are omitted.

**Lemma 1.** Let $f\operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$, $r \geq 1$.
Then:

(i) $\rho_{1}(f) = p / q$ if and only if there is an $a \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f^{q}(a) = a + p$ and $f^{q}(x) \geq a + p$ for all $x \geq a$.

(iii) $\rho_{2}(f) = p / q$ if and only if there is a $b \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f^{q}(b) = b + p$ and $f^{q}(x) \leq b + p$ for all $x \leq b$.

**Proof.** It is enough to prove the case when $p = 0$ and $q = 1$.
We will prove only (i).
The other statement follows similarly.
Suppose that for each fixed point $a$ there is an $y &gt; a$ such that $f(y) &lt; a$.
Then, for each $x \in \mathbb{R}$, the set $\{y &gt; x; f(x) = x\}$ is non empty.
We will prove that this implies $\rho_1(f) &lt; 0$.
For this, fix $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and define $\{y_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ inductively by

$$
\begin{array}{l}
y_{1} = \sup \left\{y &gt; x; f(y) = x \right\} \\
y_{n + 1} = \sup \left\{y &gt; y_{n}; f(y) = y_{n} \right\}, \quad n \geq 1.
\end{array}
$$

It is easy to see that $y_{n} \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$ and $f^{n}(y_{n + 1}) = y_{1}$.
So, for $n$ big enough we have $f^{n}(y_{n + 1}) = y_{1} &lt; y_{n + 1} - 1$ which implies $\rho_{1}(f^{n}) \leq -1$ and so $\rho_{1}(f) \leq -1 / n$.

Note that this result implies that if $p / q$ is an interior point of $\rho(f)$ then for all $y \in \mathbb{R}$ there are $x_1 &lt; y &lt; x_2$ such that $f^q(x_1) &gt; y + p &gt; f^q(x_2)$.
With this fact and applying the same idea of the proof of Lemma 1 we can prove the existence of a repeller of period $q$ which has a homoclinic point as stated in Remark 1.

**Lemma 2.** Let $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$, $r \geq 1$, with $\rho_1(f) = p / q &gt; 0$.
Let $a \in \mathbb{R}$ be such that

$$
f^{q}(a) = a + p.
$$

Then

$1^{\circ}$.) the following conditions are equivalents:

(i) if $x &gt; a$ then $f^{q}(x) &gt; a + p$

(ii) for each $j \in \{0, 1, \dots, q - 1\}$ if $x &gt; f^{j}(a)$ then $f(x) &gt; f^{j + 1}(a)$.

$2^{\circ}$.) if $(f^{q})'(a) \neq 0$, the following conditions are equivalents:

(i) there is an $x &gt; a$ such that $f^{q}(x) &lt; a + p$

(ii) there is a $j \in \{0, 1, \dots, q - 1\}$ and there is an $x &gt; f^{j}(a)$ such that $f(x) &lt; f^{j + 1}(a)$.

**Proof (1°).** We prove that (ii) implies (i) using the hypothesis successively for $j = 0, 1, \ldots, q - 1$.

To prove the converse we first note that (i) implies that if $x &gt; a$ then $f(x) &gt; f(a)$.
In particular, since $f$ is of degree one it also implies that if $x &gt; a + p$ then $f(x) &gt; f(a) + p$.
These two statements imply that if $x &gt; f(a)$ then there is $y &gt; a$ such that $f(y) = x$.
By (i) we have $f^q(y) &gt; a + p$, that is, $f^{q - 1}(x) &gt; a + p$ and so, $f^q(x) &gt; f(a) + p$.
Now we have statement (i) for $f(a)$ which implies that if $x &gt; f(a)$ then $f(x) &gt; f^2(a)$.

Repeating the above argument successively for $f^i(a)$, $i = 1, \dots, q - 1$, we conclude the proof.

$(2^{\circ})$ We will only prove that (ii) implies (i).
The converse is trivial.

Changing rotation intervals of endomorphisms of the circle

For each $0 \leq j \leq q - 1$ define $a_{j} = f^{j}(a)$.
Let $j \in \{0, \dots, q - 1\}$ be such that there is an $x &gt; a_{j}$ with $f(x) &lt; a_{j + 1}$.

Since $f &gt; \mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{R}}$, for each $\delta &gt; 0$ there are $y_{+}, y_{-} \in (a_{j}, a_{j + 1})$ such that $f(y_{+}) \in (a_{j + 1}, a_{j + 1} + \delta)$ and $f(y_{-}) \in (a_{j + 1} - \delta, a_{j + 1})$.

But $(f^q)'(a) \neq 0$ implies that $f^{q - j - 1}$ is either increasing or decreasing in a neighborhood of $a_{j + 1}$.
Then, if $\delta$ is small enough either $f^{q - j - 1}(y) &lt; a + p$ for all $y \in (a_{j + 1} - \delta, a_{j + 1})$ or $f^{q - j - 1}(y) &lt; a + p$ for all $y \in (a_{j + 1}, a_{j + 1} + \delta)$.

Anyway, there is $y \in (a_j, a_{j+1})$ such that $f^{q-j}(y) &lt; a + p$.

Since $f^j &gt; \mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $y &gt; a_j = f^j(a)$, there is $x &gt; a$ such that $f^j(x) = y$.
So $f^{q - j}(y) = f^q(x) &lt; a + p$.

Corollary 3. Let $a \in \mathbb{R}$ be such that $f^q(a) = a + p$ and $(f^q)'(a) \neq 0$.
If there is $0 \leq j &lt; q$ such that $f'(f^j(a)) &lt; 0$ then there exists $x &gt; a$ such that $f^q(x) &lt; a + p$.

Given $a \in \mathbb{R}$, let $I_a$ be the interval $I_a = [a, a + 1]$.
If $U \subset I_a$, let $U^* = \{y \in \mathbb{R}; y = x + n, n \in \mathbb{Z}, x \in U\}$.

Lemma 4. Let $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$, $r \geq 1$, $a \in \mathbb{R}$ and $U \subset I_a$ be an open interval.
Suppose that $f'(f^j(x)) &gt; 0$ for all $x \in \overline{U}$ and $0 \leq j &lt; q$.
Then given a neighborhood $\mathcal{V}$ of $f$, there are $g_1, g_2 \in \mathcal{V}$ such that $g_1^q \geq f^q$, $g_2^q \leq f^q$ and $g_i^q(x) \neq f^q(x)$ if and only if $x \in \bigcup_{j=0}^{q-1} f^{-j}(U^*)$, $i = 1, 2$.

Proof.
For each $0 \leq j &lt; q$, let $V_{j}$ be an open interval such that $f^{j}(\bar{U}) \subset V_{j}$ and $f^{\prime}(y) &gt; 0$ for all $y \in V_{j}$.
Let $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathcal{V}$ be a neighborhood of $f$ such that if $g \in \mathcal{W}$ then $g^{j}(\bar{U}) \subset V_{j}$ for $0 &lt; j &lt; q$.
Given $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ let $h_{\varepsilon} \in \operatorname{End}_0^r(\mathbb{R})$ be such that $\| h_{\varepsilon} \| &lt; \varepsilon$, $h_{\varepsilon} \geq 0$ and $h_{\varepsilon}(x) &gt; 0$ if and only if $x \in U^{*}$.
Now, take $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ small enough in such a way $g_{1} = f + h_{\varepsilon} \in \mathcal{W}$ and $g_{2} = f - h_{\varepsilon} \in \mathcal{W}$.
Thus $g_{1}$ and $g_{2}$ satisfy the required properties.

Corollary 5. Let $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$, $p/q \geq 0$ and suppose $f^q(x) \geq x + p$ (resp.
$f^q(x) \leq x + p$) for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
Then given a neighborhood $\mathcal{V}$ of $f$ there exist $g \in \mathcal{V}$ such that $\rho_1(g) &gt; p/q$ (resp.
$\rho_2(g) &lt; p/q$).

Proof.
If $f^q(x) = x + p$ for every $x \in \mathbb{R}$ then $g = f + \varepsilon$ has the desired property.
Otherwise, let $a \in [0,1]$ be such that $f^q(a) &gt; a + p$ and let $P = \{x \in I_a; f^q(x) = x + p\}$.
If $P = \emptyset$ then $f^q(x) &gt; x + p$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and so $\rho_1(f) &gt; p/q$.

Suppose then that $P \neq \emptyset$.
Clearly $(f^q)'(x) = 1$ for all $x \in P$.
From Corollary 3 we have $f'(f^j(x)) &gt; 0$ for $0 &lt; j &lt; q$ and $x \in P$.
Let $A = \{x \in \mathbb{R}; f'(f^j(x)) &gt; 0, 0 \leq j &lt; q\}$ and $(\alpha, \beta)$ be a connected component of $A$.
Then $(f^q)'(x) = (f^q)'(x) = 0$.
On the other hand, if $P \cap (\alpha, \beta) \neq \emptyset$ there is an $x \in (\alpha, \beta)$ such that $(f^q)'(x) = 1$.
These facts imply that $P$ intersects only a finite number of connected components of $A$, say, $P \subset \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (\alpha_i, \beta_i) \subset A$.
Clearly we can take $\bar{\alpha}_i, \bar{\beta}_i \in (a, a+1)$ such that $[\bar{\alpha}_i, \bar{\beta}_i] \subset (\alpha_i, \beta_i)$ for $1 \leq i \leq n$ with $P \subset \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} (\bar{\alpha}_i, \bar{\beta}_i)$.
The result is thus obtained by using Lemma 4 for the sets $U_i = (\bar{\alpha}_i, \bar{\beta}_i)$, $1 \leq i \leq n$.

Lemma 6. Let $f \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ be such that $\rho_1(f) = \alpha$, $\alpha \in \mathbb{R} \setminus Q$.
Then given a neighborhood $\mathcal{V}$ of $f$ in $\operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ there exists $g \in \mathcal{V}$ such that $\rho_1(g) = p / q &lt; \alpha$.

R.
Bamon et al.

Proof.
We will prove that for every $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ the endomorphism $f_{\varepsilon}(x) = f(x) - \varepsilon$ satisfies $\rho_1(f_{\varepsilon}) &lt; \rho_1(f)$ .
Since $\rho_1$ depends continuously on $f$ this result proves the lemma.
We can assume $\alpha \in (0,1)$ .
Let $p_n$ be the sequence of integers defined by $p_n &lt; n\alpha &lt; p_n + 1$ , $n \geq 1$ .
Since the set $\{n\alpha - p_n\}$ is dense in $[0,1]$ we have that given $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ , there exists $n_0 \geq 1$ such that $n_0\alpha &lt; p_{n_0} + \varepsilon$ .
On the other hand, a simple induction proves that for $n \geq 1$ , given $x \in \mathbb{R}$ , there is $y \geq x$ such that $f_{\varepsilon}^n(y) = f^n(x) - \varepsilon$ .
Thus, applying this result for $n = n_0$ and $x = x_0$ we get $y_0 \geq x_0$ such that $f_{\varepsilon}^{n_0}(y_0) = f^{n_0}(x_0) - \varepsilon &lt; x_0 + p_{n_0} \leq y_0 + p_{n_0}$ and so $\rho_1(f_{\varepsilon}) \leq \frac{p_{n_0}}{n_0} &lt; \alpha$ .

# 3. Proof of Theorem B

We will give the proof for the statements concerning $\rho_{1}(f)$ .
The proofs for $\rho_{2}(f)$ can be made adapting, in an obvious way, the lemmas and arguments used for $\rho_{1}(f)$ .
Clearly we can assume that $p / q \geq 0$ .

(iii) To prove the sufficiency we can assume $p = 0$ and $q = 1$ .
Suppose then that $B_{0,1} \neq \emptyset$ and let $a \in \partial A_{0,1}$ be such that $f(x) &gt; a$ for every $x &gt; a$ .

First we observe that, since $f$ is of class $C^1$ , if $a$ is not an isolated fixed point then $f'(a) = 1$ .
This implies that if $f(a) = a$ and $f(x) &gt; a$ if $x &gt; a$ , then for each fixed point $b$ of $f$ close enough to $a$ we have that $f(x) &gt; b$ if $x &gt; b$ .
Thus we conclude that there exists $\tilde{a} \in \partial A_{0,1}$ such that $\tilde{a}$ is the left end point of a connected component $(\tilde{a}, \tilde{b})$ of $A_{0,1}$ and satisfies $f(x) &gt; \tilde{a}$ if $x &gt; \tilde{a}$ .

Let $y_{1} \in (\tilde{a}, \tilde{b})$ and $M_{0} = \min_{x \geq y_{1}} f(x)$ .
Then $M_{0} &gt; \tilde{a}$ and so we can take $y_{0} \in (\tilde{a}, y_{1}]$ such that $f(y_{0}) &lt; M_{0}$ .
Let $M_{1} = \min_{y_{0} \leq x \leq y_{1}} f(x) - x$ .
Since $B_{0,1} \neq \emptyset$ , there exists $x_{0} &lt; \tilde{a}$ such that $f(x_{0}) &lt; x_{0}$ .
Let $\delta = \min \{x_{0} - f(x_{0}), M_{1}, M_{0} - f(y_{0})\}$ .
So if $\| f - g \|_{0} &lt; \delta$ we have $g(x_{0}) &lt; x_{0}$ and $g(x) &gt; x$ for $x \in [y_{0}, y_{1}]$ .
In particular there exists $a_{g} \in (x_{0}, y_{0})$ such that $a_{g}$ is the left end point of a connected component of $A_{0,1}(g)$ .
It is easy to verify that $g(x) &gt; a_{g}$ for $x &gt; a_{g}$ .
By Lemma 1 $\rho_{1}(g) = 0$ and so $\rho_{1}(f)$ is $C'$ -persistent.

We will prove the converse by contradiction.
The fact that $B_{p/q} \neq \emptyset$ and $A_{p/q} \neq \emptyset$ follows from Corollary 5. Now assume, by contradiction, that if $a \in \partial A_{p,q}$ then there exists $x &gt; a$ such that $f^q(x) = a + p$ .
We will prove that this assumption implies that arbitrarily close to $f$ there is $g \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ with $\rho_1(g) &lt; p/q$ .

![img-42.jpeg](img-42.jpeg)
Fig.
2

Changing rotation intervals of endomorphisms of the circle

![img-43.jpeg](img-43.jpeg)
Fig.
3

We can assume, without loss of generality that $x_0 = 0 \in A_{p,q}$ .
Let $P(f) = \{x \in [0,1]; f^q(x) = x + p \text{ and } y &gt; x \text{ implies } f^q(y) &gt; x + p\}$ .
With the same arguments used to prove Corollary 5 we prove that arbitrarily close to $f$ there exists $g_1 \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ such that $g_1^q \leq f^q$ , $f^q(x) = x + p$ for every $x$ such that $g_1^q(x) = x + p$ and $g_1^q(x) &lt; x + p$ for all $x \in P(f)$ .
Thus for such a $g_1$ we have that $P(g_1) = \emptyset$ , that is, if $g_1^q(x) = x + p$ then there is an $y &gt; x$ such that $g_1^q(y) \leq x + p$ .
Let $Q(g_1) = \{x \in [0,1]; g_1^q(x) = x + p \text{ and } y &gt; x \text{ implies } g_1^q(y) \geq x + p\}$ .
From Lemma 1 (i) and the fact that $g_1^q \leq f^q$ we know that if $Q(g_1) = \emptyset$ then $\rho_1(g_1) &lt; p/q$ and we are done.
Otherwise $Q(g_1)$ is finite.
In fact if $x_0$ is an accumulation point of $Q(g_1)$ then $(g_1^q)'(x_0) = 1$ and so $g_1^q$ is strictly increasing in a neighborhood $V = (x_0 - \delta, x_0 + \delta)$ of $x_0$ .
Let $x_1, x_2 \in V \cap Q(g_1)$ with $x_1 &lt; x_2$ .
Since $x_2 \in Q(g_1)$ , $y &gt; x_2$ implies $g_1^q(y) \geq x_2 + p &gt; x_1 + p$ and if $x_2 \geq y &gt; x_1$ then $y \in V$ and so $g_1^q(y) &gt; g_1^q(x_1) = x_1 + p$ .
But this result implies $x_1 \in P(g_1)$ , which is a contradiction.
Thus we conclude that to finish the proof it is enough to prove that arbitrarily close to $g_1$ there exists $g_2 \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ such that $P(g_2) = \emptyset$ and $Q(g_2)$ has, at least, one point less than $Q(g_1)$ .
To prove this let $a \in Q(g_1)$ .
From Lemma 2 we have that $g_1(y) \geq g^{j+1}(a)$ for all $y &gt; g_1^j(a)$ and $0 \leq j \leq q-1$ , and that there exist $0 \leq j_0 \leq q-1$ and $y_0 &gt; g_1^{j_0}(a)$ such that $g_1(y_0) = g^{j_0+1}(a)$ .
Thus $g_1^q(y_0) = a + p &lt; y_0 + p$ .
(Notice that if $p/q = 0$ we can assume $q = 1$ and $p = 0$ and so $j_0 = 0$ and $y_0 &gt; a$ .
If $p/q &gt; 0$ then $g_1(x) &gt; x$ for every $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and so $y_0 &gt; g_1^{j_0}(a) &gt; a$ .
This fact implies that there are $\varepsilon &gt; 0, \delta &gt; 0$ such that if $y \in (y_0 - \delta, y_0 + \delta) = U$ , then $g_1^q(y) &lt; y + p - \varepsilon$ .
Now we perform a small modification on $g_1$ in such a way to obtain $g_2 \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ satisfying:

(1) $g_{2}(x)\leq g_{1}(x)$
(2) $g_{2}(x) &lt; g_{1}(x)$ if and only if $x \in U^{*} = \{x \in \mathbb{R}; x = y + k, y \in U, k \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ and
(3) $g_2^q (y) &lt;   y + p$ if $y\in U^{*}$

Since $g_1^q(y) &lt; y + p$ for every $y \in U^*$ we conclude that if $g_1^q(x) = x + p$ then $g_i^j(x) \notin U^*$ for $0 \leq j \leq q$ and so from (2) we have $g_2^j(x) = g_1^j(x)$ for $0 \leq j \leq q$ .
In particular, using (3), we conclude that $g_2^q(x) = x + p$ if and only if $g_1^q(x) = x + p$ .
Now from Lemma 2 and (1) above we have that $P(g_2) = \emptyset$ and $Q(g_2) \subset Q(g_1)$ .
On the other hand, $y_0 &gt; g_1^{j_0}(a) = g_2^{j_0}(a)$ and $g_2(y_0) &lt; g_1(y_0) = g_1^{j_0 + 1}(a) = g_2^{j_0 + 1}(a)$ .
So $a \notin Q(g_2)$ by Lemma 2. This finish the proof of (iii).

R.
Bamon et al.

(v) First suppose that $\rho_{1}(f) = p / q\in \mathbb{Q}$.

From (iii) it is enough to prove that $C^r$ arbitrarily close to $f$ there exists $g \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ such that $\rho_1(g) = p / q$ and $g^q(x) &gt; a + p$ if $x &gt; a$ for some $a \in \partial A_{p,q}(g)$.

Let $a \in [0,1]$ be given by Lemma 1. That is $x &gt; a$ implies $f^q(x) \geq a + p = f^q(a)$.
If $(f^q)'(a) = 0$ then $B_{p,q} \neq \emptyset$ and $(\tilde{a}, a)$ is a connected component of $A_{p,q}$ for some $\tilde{a} &lt; a$.
Clearly $x &gt; \tilde{a}$ implies $f^q(x) &gt; \tilde{a} + p$ and so $\rho_1(f)$ is $C^r$-persistent.
Assume then $(f^q)'(a) \neq 0$.
From Corollary 3 we have $f'(f^j(a)) &gt; 0$ for $0 \leq j &lt; q$.
If $B_{p,q} \neq \emptyset$, using Lemma 4, we can obtain $g \in \operatorname{End}_1^r(\mathbb{R})$ such that $g$ is close to $f$, $B_{p,q}(g) \neq \emptyset$, $g^q \geq f^q$ and $g^q(a) &gt; a + p$.
Let $\tilde{a}$ be the left end point of the component of $\partial A_{p,q}(g)$ which contains $a$.
Clearly $x &gt; \tilde{a}$ implies $g^q(x) &gt; \tilde{a} + p$.
If $B_{p,q} \neq \emptyset$ we first use Lemma 4 to obtain $B_{p,q}(g) \neq \emptyset$.

Finally if $\rho_1(f) \in \mathbb{R} - \mathbb{Q}$ we use Lemma 6 to obtain $g$, $C^r$ close to $f$, with $\rho_1(g) = \frac{P}{q} \in \mathbb{Q}$.
This finishes the proof of Theorem B.
