This formula is not too interesting since it agrees with the formula (4) obtained by straight number-theoretical means (indeed, $\zeta_{\mathbb{Q}(V - 3)}(s) / \zeta(s) = 1 - 1/2^s + 1/4^s$ —..., which at $s = 2$ reduces to the series defining $\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} \Pi\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ ).
However, if we take $M$ instead to be the complement of one of the links is 5(b) or 5(c), then Thurston [6, pp. 6.38, 6.40] shows $\mathrm{Vol}(M) = 6 \mathrm{Vol}(\mathfrak{H}_3 / SL_2(R))$ , where now $R$ is the ring of integers of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-7})$ .
On the other hand, for the manifold of 5(b) he gives a decomposition into two pieces of the form

![img-51.jpeg](img-51.jpeg)
Fig.
6

$$
\alpha = \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {1}{2 \sqrt {2}}\right),
$$

$$
\beta = \pi - 2 \alpha ,
$$

and applying the volume formula on p.
7.16 of [6] we find that each of these pieces has volume

$$
2 A (\sqrt {7}) + A (\sqrt {7} + \sqrt {1 2}) + A (\sqrt {7} - \sqrt {1 2}).
$$

Comparing these two formulas (and using the formula for $\mathrm{Vol}(\mathfrak{H}_3 / SL_2(R))$ ), we obtain Eq.
(6) of the introduction.
This time, as we remarked at that point, the result is quite different from the formula (5) obtained number-theoretically; as a numerical check, we have the values

$$
A (\sqrt {7}) \cong 0. 9 6 2 6 7 3 0 1 4 6 1 7 \quad A \left(\cot \frac {\pi}{7}\right) \cong 1. 0 0 4 6 5 3 1 5 0 5 4 0
$$

$$
A (\sqrt {7} + \sqrt {1 2}) \cong 0. 6 9 0 1 4 8 2 9 9 9 5 8 \quad A \left(\cot \frac {2 \pi}{7}\right) \cong 0. 8 2 6 4 9 9 0 3 3 4 7 2
$$

$$
A (\sqrt {7} - \sqrt {1 2}) \cong - 0. 8 3 7 6 6 4 4 7 3 5 5 8 \quad A \left(\cot \frac {4 \pi}{7}\right) \cong - 0. 3 0 7 2 9 8 0 2 2 0 5 3
$$

so that both (5) and (6) give the value $\zeta_{\mathbb{Q}(V - 7)}(2)\cong 1.89484144897$ to twelve places.
(We explain in Appendix 1 how to calculate $A(x)$ numerically.) We can also compute $\zeta_{\mathbb{Q}(V - 7)}(2)$ directly by the method explained in Appendix 2 and check that this is the right value.

Hyperbolic manifolds and special values of Dedekind zeta-functions

Finally, we consider the field $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3 + 2\sqrt{5}})$ of degree 4 with $r = 2$ , $s = 1$ , $|D| = 275$ (this is the smallest discriminant for this $r$ and $s$ ).
Taking an appropriate $\Gamma$ here gives a quotient $\mathfrak{H}_3 / \Gamma$ which can be triangulated by a single tetrahedron $\varDelta$ with angles as shown in Fig.
7, while the arithmetic description of $\Gamma$ leads to

![img-52.jpeg](img-52.jpeg)
Fig.
7

$$
\operatorname {V o l} \left(\mathfrak {H} _ {3} / \Gamma\right) = \frac {2 7 5 ^ {3 / 2}}{2 ^ {7} \pi^ {6}} \zeta_ {K} (2).
$$

This example, due to Thurston, is discussed in Borel [1], p.
30. The group $\Gamma$ has torsion, so $\mathfrak{H}_3 / \Gamma$ is only an "orbifold" rather than a smooth hyperbolic manifold; it is of special interest because it has the smallest known volume of any orientable hyperbolic orbifold, arithmetic or otherwise.
We can compute this volume either number-theoretically or geometrically.
The number-theoretical method is described in Appendix 2. The geometrical method is the one used in the proof of Theorem 1. If we choose $P_0$ , $P_1$ as in Fig.7 and extend $P_0P_1$ to $\infty$ as in Fig.
2, then because of the many right angles in $\Delta$ we can subdivide $\Delta$ into four simplices $\mathfrak{S}_{\pi,\gamma}$ of the sort shown in Fig.
3 rather than the usual twelve.
Their angles can be computed in a straightforward way, and we find

$$
\Delta = \mathfrak {S} _ {\frac {\pi}{3}, \theta} - \mathfrak {S} _ {\beta , \theta - \frac {\pi}{3}} - \mathfrak {S} _ {\frac {\pi}{6}, \frac {\pi}{5}} + \mathfrak {S} _ {\frac {\pi}{6} + \beta , \frac {\pi}{5}}
$$

with

$$
\theta = \operatorname {a r c} \cot \left(\frac {\sqrt {- 3 + 2 \sqrt {5}}}{3}\right), \quad \beta = \operatorname {a r c} \cot \left(\frac {3 + \sqrt {5}}{2} \sqrt {3} + \frac {7 + 3 \sqrt {5}}{2 \sqrt {2 \sqrt {5} - 3}}\right).
$$

Now Eq.
(8) gives a formula for $\operatorname{Vol}(\Delta)$ as a sum of 12 values of $A(x)$ at (complicated!) algebraic arguments.
Computing these values by the method given in Appendix 1, we find $\operatorname{Vol}(\Delta) \cong 0.039050286$ , in agreement with the number-theoretical calculation.

We have discussed this last example in some detail because it shows how complicated the formula promised by Theorem 1 can be, even when the geometry of the hyperbolic manifold is very simple (in this case triangulated by a single, and very special, hyperbolic tetrahedron).
In general, it is very hard to

D.
Zagier

find examples of arithmetic hyperbolic manifolds for which one has both a good arithmetic and geometric description.
Thus it is clear that getting actual formulas for $\zeta_K(2)$ by this method is usually impractical, so that, unless an arithmetical proof giving an explicit formula of the form (2) is found, Theorem 1 must be considered as of mostly theoretical interest.

## Appendices to § 3

### 1. Computation of $A(x)$

By (10), calculating $A(x)$ is equivalent to calculating the Lobachevsky function $\mathrm{JI}(\theta)$.
Neither the sum nor the integral in (9) are very convenient for numerical work, but there is a very rapidly convergent method.
By periodicity, we can assume $|\theta| &lt; \frac{\pi}{2}$.
Then $\mathrm{JI}(\theta)$ is given by

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{\pi} \mathrm{JI}(\pi t) = t (2N + 1 - \log |2 \sin \pi t|) - \sum_{n=1}^{N} n \log \frac{n + t}{n - t} \\
- \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left(\zeta(2k) - \sum_{n=1}^{N} \frac{1}{n^{2k}}\right) \frac{t^{2k+1}}{k + \frac{1}{2}} \\
\end{array}
$$

for any $N \geq 0$.
This formula, which is easily proved by differentiation, is a special case of the results of [2].
The series converges for $|t| \leq N + 1$ and therefore converges very rapidly for $|t| \leq \frac{1}{2}$ and quite modest $N$.
Taking $N = 4$ and breaking off the series at $k = 4$, for example, we get

$$
\frac{1}{\pi} \mathrm{JI}(\pi t) = t (9 - \log |2 \sin \pi t|) - \sum_{n=1}^{4} \left(c_n t^{2n+1} + n \log \frac{n + t}{n - t}\right) + \varepsilon
$$

with

$$
\begin{array}{l}
c_1 = 0.147548637158, \quad c_2 = 0.00142852188, \\
c_3 = 0.00002919407, \quad c_4 = 0.00000076258 \\
\end{array}
$$

and $|\varepsilon| &lt; 1.2 \times 10^{-11}$ for $|t| \leq \frac{1}{2}$.

### 2. Epstein zeta functions

Let $Q(x, y) = ax^2 + bxy + cy^2$ be a positive definite binary quadratic form with integer coefficients and for $n \in \mathbb{N}$ let

$$
r(Q, n) = \# \left\{ (x, y) \in \mathbb{Z}^2 / \left\{ \pm 1 \right\} \mid Q(x, y) = n \right\}
$$

denote one-half the number of representations of $n$ by $Q$.
The series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} r(Q, n) n^{-s}$ is called an Epstein zeta-function, and the zeta-functions of imaginary quadratic fields are finite sums of such series.
For instance, since

Hyperbolic manifolds and special values of Dedekind zeta-functions

$\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-7})$ has class number 1, the norms of ideals are just the values of the norm form $x^{2} + xy + 2y^{2}$ , so

$$
\zeta_ {\mathbb {Q} (V - \overline {{7}})} (s) = \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \frac {r (x ^ {2} + x y + 2 y ^ {2} , n)}{n ^ {s}}.
$$

The Epstein zeta-functions have a well-known Fourier expansion, which at $s = 2$ becomes

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \frac {r \left(a x ^ {2} + b x y + c y ^ {2} , n\right)}{n ^ {2}} \\ = \frac {\pi^ {4}}{9 0 a ^ {2}} + 4 \pi \zeta (3) \frac {a}{\delta^ {3}} + \frac {8 \pi}{\delta^ {2}} \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \left(\pi n + \frac {a}{\delta}\right) \sigma_ {- 3} (n) e ^ {- \frac {\pi n \delta}{a}} \cos \frac {\pi n b}{a} \tag {12} \\ \end{array}
$$

with $\delta = \sqrt{4ac - b^2}$ , $\sigma_{-3}(n) = \sum_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d\gtrless 1}}d^{-3}$ , $\zeta (3) = \sum_{d\gtrless 1}d^{-3} = 1.202056903\ldots$ .
The series converges exponentially, and four terms of (12) suffice to compute $\zeta_{\mathbb{Q}(V - \overline{7})}(2)$ to twelve places.

Epstein zeta-functions can also be used to compute zeta-functions for number fields other than quadratic fields.
For example, the field $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3 + 2\sqrt{5}})$ is related to the genus field of the imaginary quadratic field $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-55})$ , and using this relationship one can prove the formula

$$
\zeta_ {K} (s) = \zeta_ {\mathbb {Q} (V \bar {5})} (s) \times \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \frac {r \left(x ^ {2} + x y + 1 4 y ^ {2} , n\right) - r \left(4 x ^ {2} + 3 x y + 4 y ^ {2} , n\right)}{n ^ {s}}; \tag {13}
$$

since $\zeta_{\mathbb{Q}(V\overline{5})}(2) = \frac{2\pi^4}{75\sqrt{5}}$ , this permits us to calculate $\zeta_K(2)$ easily using Eq.
(12) (in fact, very easily, since $e^{-\pi V\overline{5}\overline{5}} &lt; 10^{-10}$ , so the series in (12) is negligible for $x^{2} + xy + 14y^{2}$ and extremely rapidly convergent for $4x^{2} + 3xy + 4y^{2}$ ).
We find

$$
\zeta_ {K} (2) \cong \frac {2 \pi^ {4}}{7 5 \sqrt {5}} (1. 1 1 9 3 5 6 4 0 0 9 - 0. 2 1 2 2 6 4 7 7 2 4) \cong 1. 0 5 3 7 4 2 2 1 7
$$

and hence $\mathrm{Vol}(\mathfrak{H}_3 / \Gamma) \cong 0.0390502856$ for the group $\Gamma$ discussed at the end of §3.

## §4.

Proof of Theorem 2

We begin by proving the special case (4), even though this is well-known (see e.g., Milnor [6], p.
7.19), since it illustrates the general case.
Let $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{D})$ be an imaginary quadratic field with discriminant $D &lt; 0$ and $\chi(n) = \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)$ the associated character.
Then $\zeta_K(s)$ factors as $\zeta(s) L(s, \chi)$ , where $L(s, \chi) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \chi(n) n^{-s}$ ,

D.
Zagier

so $\zeta_K(2) = \frac{\pi^2}{6} L(2,\chi)$.
The function $\chi(n)$ is odd and periodic with period $|D|$, so it has a Fourier sine expansion, well known to be

$$
\chi(n) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{|D|}} \sum_{0 &lt; k &lt; |D|} \chi(k) \sin \frac{2\pi k n}{|D|}.
$$

Hence, by (9) and (10),

$$
L(2,\chi) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{|D|}} \sum_{0 &lt; k &lt; |D|} \chi(k) \operatorname{JI}\left(\frac{\pi k}{|D|}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{|D|}} \sum_{0 &lt; k &lt; |D|} \chi(k) A\left(\cot \frac{\pi k}{|D|}\right).
$$

Now let $K$ be an arbitrary abelian field.
Then $\zeta_K(s)$ is the product of $[K:\mathbb{Q}]$ $L$-series $L(s,\chi)$, where the $\chi$ are primitive Dirichlet characters whose conductors $f$ divide the conductor $N$ of $K$.
If $\chi$ is an even character, then $\chi(n)$ has a Fourier expansion

$$
\chi(n) = \frac{1}{G_{\chi}} \sum_{k=1}^{f} \chi(k) \cos \frac{2\pi k n}{f}
$$

where $G_{\chi}$ (defined by setting $n = 1$ in this formula) is a certain algebraic integer, the Gauss sum attached to $\chi$.
Therefore

$$
L(2m,\chi) = \frac{\pi^{2m}}{G_{\chi}} \sum_{k=1}^{f} \chi(k) b_{m,k,f} \quad (\chi \text{ even})
$$

where $b_{m,k,f} = \pi^{-2m} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2m}} \cos \frac{2\pi k n}{f}$, which is known to be a rational number $\left(b_{m,k,f} = \frac{2^{2m-1}}{(2m)!} B_{2m}\left(\frac{k}{f}\right)\right)$, where $B_r$ denotes the $r$-th Bernoulli polynomial.
If $\chi$ is an odd character, then instead

$$
\chi(n) = \frac{i}{G_{\chi}} \sum_{k=1}^{f-1} \chi(k) \sin \frac{2\pi k n}{f},
$$

(where again $G_{\chi}$ is defined by setting $n = 1$).
But

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{(2m-1)!}{2^{2m-1}} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{\sin 2n\theta}{n^{2m}} &amp;= 2 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sin 2n\theta \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2nt} t^{2m-1} dt \\
&amp;= 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \operatorname{Im}\left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{2in\theta - 2nt}\right) t^{2m-1} dt \\
&amp;= \sin 2\theta \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{t^{2m-1} dt}{\cosh 2t - \cos 2\theta} \\
&amp;= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{t^{2m-1} dt}{\cosh^{2}t \tan\theta + \sinh^{2}t \cot\theta},
\end{aligned}
$$

Hyperbolic manifolds and special values of Dedekind zeta-functions

and comparing this with the definition of $A_{m}(x)$ (Eq.
(3)) we find

$$
A _ {m} (\cot \theta) = \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sin 2 n \theta}{n ^ {2 m}}
$$

(which in view of (9) and (10) proves that $A_{1} = A$) and

$$
L (2 m, \chi) = \frac {i}{G _ {\chi}} \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {f - 1} \chi (k) A _ {m} \left(\cot \frac {\pi k}{f}\right) \quad (\chi \text{ odd}).
$$

Since $K$ is abelian, it is either totally real ($r = [K:\mathbb{Q}]$, $s = 0$) or totally imaginary ($r = 0$, $s = \frac{1}{2} [K:\mathbb{Q}]$).
In the first case all of the $\chi$ are even, so

$$
\zeta_ {K} (2 m) = \frac {\pi^ {2 m [ K : \mathbb {Q} ]}}{\prod_ {\chi} G _ {\chi}} \prod_ {\chi} \left(\sum_ {k = 1} ^ {f _ {\chi}} \chi (k) b _ {m, k, f _ {\chi}}\right),
$$

and this has the form $\frac{\pi^{2mr}}{\sqrt{|D|}} \times (\text{rational number})$ because $\prod_{\chi} G_{\chi} = \sqrt{D}$, $D &gt; 0$, and the set of $\chi$ is closed under the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(\bar{\mathbb{Q}} / \mathbb{Q})$.
(We could also have appealed to the Klingen-Siegel theorem.) In the second case half of the $\chi$ are even and half are odd, so

$$
\zeta_ {K} (2 m) = \frac {\pi^ {2 m s} i ^ {s}}{\prod_ {\chi} G _ {\chi}} \prod_ {\chi \text{ even}} \left(\sum_ {k = 1} ^ {f _ {\chi}} \chi (k) b _ {m, k, f _ {\chi}}\right) \prod_ {\chi \text{ odd}} \left(\sum_ {k = 1} ^ {f _ {\chi} - 1} \chi (k) A _ {m} \left(\cot \frac {\pi k}{f _ {\chi}}\right)\right).
$$

The factor in front equals $\pi^{2ms} / \sqrt{|D|}$ because $\prod G_{\chi} = \sqrt{D}$ and $(-1)^{s}D &gt; 0$; the second factor is rational for the same reason as before, and for the same reason the third factor is a rational (in fact, integral) linear combination of products of $s$ values of $A_{m}(x)$ at arguments $x = \cot \frac{\pi n}{N}$.
This completes the proof.

## § 5. Partial zeta-functions and decomposition of the volume

The zeta-function $\zeta_K(s)$ splits up naturally into $h$ summands $\zeta_K(\mathcal{A}, s)$, where $h$ is the class number of $K$ and for each ideal class $\mathcal{A}$ the partial zeta-function $\zeta_K(\mathcal{A}, s)$ is defined as $\sum_{\alpha \in \mathcal{A}} N(\alpha)^{-s}$.
From a number-theoretical point of view, these partial zeta-functions are just as good as Dedekind zeta-functions, so it is natural to make

**Conjecture 2.** Conjecture 1 remains true with $\zeta_K(2m)$ replaced by $\zeta_K(\mathcal{A}, 2m)$ for any ideal class $\mathcal{A}$ of $K$.

This conjecture can be verified in some cases.
For instance, if $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{D})$ is an imaginary quadratic field with class number 2, then the theory of genera gives

$$
\zeta_ {K} (\mathcal {A} _ {0}, s) + \zeta_ {K} (\mathcal {A} _ {1}, s) = \zeta_ {K} (s) = \zeta (s) L (s, \chi_ {D}),
$$

$$
\zeta_ {K} (\mathcal {A} _ {0}, s) - \zeta_ {K} (\mathcal {A} _ {1}, s) = L (s, \chi_ {D _ {1}}) L (s, \chi_ {D _ {2}}),
$$

D.
Zagier

where $\mathcal{A}_0$ and $\mathcal{A}_1$ denote the trivial and non-trivial ideal classes and $D_1 &gt; 0 &gt; D_2$ are fundamental discriminants with $D_1 \cdot D_2 = D$; the proof of Theorem 2 shows that for $s = 2m$ the right-hand side of both expressions is $\pi^{2m}|D|^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ times a rational linear combination of numbers $A_m\left(\cot \frac{\pi n}{|D|}\right)$.
Similar formulas hold for any imaginary quadratic field with one class per genus.
A less trivial example is provided by the field $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-55})$, whose class group is cyclic of order 4; here we can verify Conjecture 2 for $m = 1$ using Eq.
(13).

In the proof of Theorem 1, we obtained $\zeta_K(2)$ as (essentially) the volume of $\mathfrak{H}_3^s / \Gamma$, where $\Gamma$ is a torsion-free group without parabolic elements contained in a totally definite quaternion algebra over $K$.
However, the proof works even in the presence of elliptic or parabolic elements (provided we include cusps and elliptic fixed points as vertices of tetrahedra) and for quaternion algebras not ramified at the real places of $K$, except that then we have to take quotients of $\mathfrak{H}_2^t \times \mathfrak{H}_3^s$ ($0 \leq t \leq r$) and these may be non-compact.
In particular, we can take $\Gamma = SL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ acting on $\mathfrak{H}_2^t \times \mathfrak{H}_3^s$ (Hilbert modular group), in which case the quotient $X$ has $h$ cusps, but still has finite volume given as a simple multiple of $\zeta_K(2)$ (cf.
[1], 7.4(1)). The fact that $X$ has exactly the same number of cusps as the number of summands $\zeta_K(\mathcal{A}, 2)$ into which $\zeta_K(2)$ naturally decomposes suggests a possible geometric interpretation of Conjecture 2 for $m = 1$: it may be possible to break up $X$ into $h$ pieces, each containing one cusp, in such a way that the volumes of the individual pieces are proportional to the $\zeta_K(\mathcal{A}, 2)$; then if the pieces can be triangulated by simplices with algebraic coordinates, Conjecture 2 follows.
There are in fact various natural decompositions of $X$ into $h$ neighborhoods of cusps, but I have not been able to find any which gives the right volumes.

## §6.

Sharpening of Theorem 1 for imaginary quadratic fields

In Theorem 1, the arguments of $A(x)$ could in general be chosen to be of the form $\sqrt{-1}$ times a number quadratic over $K_{1}$, where $K_{1}$ was an arbitrarily chosen totally imaginary quadratic extension of $K$.
Looking at the proof, we see that $K_{1}$ had to be introduced only in order to split the quaternion algebra used to define the group $\Gamma$.
Hence if $K$ is imaginary quadratic and we take $\Gamma$ to be $SL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ or a torsion-free subgroup of finite index as in §5, we do not need $K_{1}$, so the same proof yields a formula of the form

$$
\zeta_K(2) = \frac{\pi^2}{\sqrt{|D|}} \sum_v c_v A(x_v)
$$

with $c_v \in \mathbb{Q}$ and $x_v \sqrt{-1}$ of degree at most 2 over $K$.
But in fact we can do better, namely we can find a representation of $\zeta_K(2)$ of this type with $x_v \sqrt{-1} \in K$ and hence (since $x_v$ is real) $x_v \in \mathbb{Q} \cdot \sqrt{|D|}$.
More precisely, we have:

Hyperbolic manifolds and special values of Dedekind zeta-functions

Theorem 3. Let $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-d})$ be an imaginary quadratic field of discriminant $-d$ .
Then

$$
\zeta_ {K} (2) = \frac {\pi^ {2}}{6 d \sqrt {d}} \sum_ {v} n _ {v} A \left(\frac {\lambda_ {v}}{\sqrt {d}}\right) \tag {14}
$$

for some finite collection of numbers $n_{\nu}\in \mathbb{Z},\lambda_{\nu}\in \mathbb{Q}$ with $\prod_{\nu}(\lambda_{\nu} + \sqrt{-d})^{n_{\nu}}\in \mathbb{Q}$

Proof.
We first prove (14) with "6" possibly replaced by a larger denominator and under the assumption that $M = \mathfrak{H}_3 / \Gamma$ admits an ideal triangulation for some torsion-free subgroup $\Gamma$ of $PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ of finite index. Here "ideal triangulation" means a triangulation of $M \cup \{\mathrm{cusps}\}$ into geodesic tetrahedra all of whose vertices lie at the cusps, i.e., there is a fundamental domain for the action of $\Gamma$ on $\mathfrak{H}_3$ which is a union of hyperbolic tetrahedra, disjoint except along their boundaries, with all vertices in $K \cup \{\infty\} \subset \mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\} = \partial \mathfrak{H}_3$ .
An ideal tetrahedron is described by three angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ with sum $\pi$ which are the angles of the cross-section of the tetrahedron seen from any vertex (if one vertex is chosen at infinity, then the tetrahedron seen from above will look like a Euclidean triangle with angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ ).
The volume of such a tetrahedron is $\mathrm{JI}(\alpha) + \mathrm{JI}(\beta) + \mathrm{JI}(\gamma) = \frac{1}{2} [A(\cot \alpha) + A(\cot \beta) + A(\cot \gamma)]$ ([6], Theorem 7.2.1). Since the angles of a triangle with vertices in $K \cup \{\infty\}$ have cotangents in $\mathbb{Q} \cdot \sqrt{d}$ (by translating and rotating the triangle we can put its vertices at 0, 1 and $x + y\sqrt{-d}$ with $x, y \in \mathbb{Q}$ , and then the cotangent of the angle at 0 is $x/y\sqrt{d}$ ), we can write this volume as $\frac{1}{2} [A(\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{d}}) + A(\frac{\mu}{\sqrt{d}}) + A(\frac{v}{\sqrt{d}})]$ with $\lambda, \mu, v \in \mathbb{Q}$ , $(\lambda + \sqrt{-d})(\mu + \sqrt{-d})(v + \sqrt{-d}) \in \mathbb{Q}$ .
Since the volume of $\mathfrak{H}_3 / PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ equals $\frac{d^{3/2}}{4\pi^2}\zeta_K(2)$ [6, Theorem 7.4.1], this proves the theorem under the assumption stated and with 6 replaced by $\frac{1}{2} [PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K):\Gamma]$ .

Now in fact any hyperbolic 3-manifold with at least one cusp admits an ideal triangulation.
This has been stated by Thurston and others and can be proved using the dual complex of a Ford domain subdivision, but we could not find a suitable reference.
However, in [7] Thurston shows that any such manifold $M$ admits a generalized ideal triangulation which is allowed to "fold back" on itself, so that some of the ideal tetrahedra are counted with multiplicity $-1$ instead of $+1$ (more precisely, given any geodesic triangulation of $\mathfrak{H}_3 / \Gamma$ we can find a $\Gamma$ -equivariant map from $\mathfrak{H}_3 \cup \{\mathrm{cusps}\}$ to itself, equivariantly homotopic to the identity, such that the images of the original simplices are ideal tetrahedra, possibly degenerate).
This is sufficient for our purposes since it implies that $\operatorname{Vol}(M)$ is an integral linear combination of volumes of ideal tetrahedra with vertices at the cusps, and hence in our situation of expressions $\frac{1}{2} \left[ A \left( \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + A \left( \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + A \left( \frac{v}{\sqrt{d}} \right) \right]$ with $\lambda, \mu, v$ and $(\lambda + \sqrt{-d})(\mu + \sqrt{-d})(v + \sqrt{-d})$ rational.
The fact that some of the tetrahedra are counted with multiplicity $-1$ means that some of the coefficients $n_v$ in (14) may be negative, but we do not

D.
Zagier

care about this and anyway could achieve $n_{\nu} &gt; 0$ (by changing the sign of $\lambda_{\nu}$) or even $n_{\nu} = 1$ (by repeating some $\lambda_{\nu}$).
Moreover, Thurston's construction has the property that it can be carried out equivariantly with respect to the (finite) group of isometries of $M$, at least if we allow ideal polyhedra rather than just ideal tetrahedra in the decomposition.
Apply this to $M = \mathfrak{H}_3 / \Gamma$ where $\Gamma$ is a torsion-free normal subgroup of $PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ of finite index (e.g. the full congruence subgroup $\Gamma(n)$ for some $n &gt; 3$).
Then the polyhedra of the subdivision of $M$ are permuted by the finite group $G = PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K) / \Gamma$ and we have

$$
\operatorname{Vol}(\mathfrak{H}_3 / PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)) = \frac{1}{|G|} \sum_{\sigma} \pm \operatorname{Vol}(\sigma) = \sum_{[\sigma]} \pm \operatorname{Vol}(\sigma) / N_{\sigma},
$$

where $\sigma$ ranges over all polyhedra of the subdivision in the first sum and over all $G$-orbits of such in the second, and where $N_{\sigma}$ denotes the order of the stabilizer of $\sigma$ in $G$ or equivalently (since an element stabilizing a polyhedron has finite order and $\Gamma$ is torsion-free) of the stabilizer in $PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ of a representative of $\sigma$ in $\mathfrak{H}_3$.
But the order of a torsion element of $PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ is at most 3 (this follows easily by looking at its trace), so this stabilizer is a finite group of $PSL_2(\mathbb{C})$ whose elements all have order 1, 2 or 3 and hence (by the classification of finite subgroups of $PSL_2(\mathbb{C})$) is isomorphic to a subgroup of the alternating group $A_4$ of order 12. It follows that $12 \cdot \operatorname{Vol}(\mathfrak{H}_3 / PSL_2(\mathcal{O}_K))$ is an integral linear combination of volumes of ideal polyhedra and hence (triangulating these polyhedra arbitrarily) of ideal tetrahedra.
This proves Theorem 3 with the denominator stated.

As an example of the theorem, we can subdivide the polyhedra in Fig.
6 into three tetrahedra, and working out their dihedral angles we obtain the formula

$$
\zeta_{\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-7})}(2) = \frac{2\pi^2}{21\sqrt{7}} \left(3A \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right) + 3A \left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{7}}\right) + A \left(\frac{5}{\sqrt{7}}\right)\right)
$$

with $\prod_{\nu} (\lambda_{\nu} + \sqrt{-d})^{n_{\nu}} = (1 + \sqrt{-7})^{1/2} (3 + \sqrt{-7})^{1/2} (5 + \sqrt{-7})^4 = 2^{5/2}$.
In this example the factor $1/6$ in (14) can be replaced by $2/3$, and this may well be true in general.
Other questions one can ask about Theorem 3 are whether it extends to partial zeta-functions as discussed in §5, whether there is a formula of the same type for $\zeta_K(4)$, $\zeta_K(6)$, etc. (with $A$ replaced by $A_m$ as in Conjecture 1), and whether Theorem 1 can be sharpened in general to have arguments $x_{\nu_j j} \in \mathbb{R} \cap \sqrt{-1} \cdot \sigma_j(K)$ for arbitrary number fields $K$.
Probably the answers to all of these questions will have to wait until there is a proof of Theorems 1 and 3 by the methods of analytic number theory, since it does not seem possible to push the geometric approach much further.

# The Eisenstein descent on $J_0(N)$

Benedict H.
Gross and Jonathan Lubin

Department of Mathematics, Brown University, Providence, RI 02912, USA

The Mordell-Weil group of the Jacobian $J_0(N)$ has several distinguished points, which arise from Heegner divisors on the modular curve $X_0(N)$.
Birch conjectures that these points have infinite order in a given simple factor if and only if the rank of that factor over the Hecke algebra is equal to 1. In [6] we used the theory of modular units to give simple criteria which guarantee that these points are not torsion in the Eisenstein factor.
It is then natural to attempt to bound the rank of this factor, using a first Eisenstein descent.

In the case where $N$ is prime, sharp upper bounds for the rank were obtained by Mazur [10, 11].
His descent relies crucially on the fact that for prime level the kernel of the Eisenstein ideal is a relatively simple finite flat groupscheme.
In fact, it is the extension of a multiplicative groupscheme by the étale group generated by the cuspidal divisors.
The purpose of this paper is to investigate the quite different situation that arises when the level $N$ is equal to $p^2$ and $p$ is an Eisenstein prime characteristic.
The essential new feature in this case is that the relevant finite flat groupscheme is of local-local type, hence the determination of its Oort-Tate invariants becomes a more difficult problem.
We solve this using some results of Lubin and Serre on canonical subgroups of formal groups of dimension 1 and height 2. We then show that the detailed groupscheme structure allows one to extract sharp rank bounds from the first descent.
The surprising fact which emerges is that the criteria which allow one to complete the first Eisenstein descent are exactly those which allow one to show the Heegner points have infinite order!

The plan of the paper is as follows.
In §1 we reconsider Mazur's results in prime level and establish some descent terminology.
In §2 we treat the descent when $N = p^2$, and in §3 we obtain sharp rank bounds over abelian fields.
Here is a simple example, which illustrates some of our results.
The first prime $p$ where $X_0(p^2)$ has positive genus is $p = 7$; in this case the genus is 1, so $J_0(49) = E$ is an elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$.
Let $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$ be a quadratic field of discriminant $d$ prime to 7, and let $\chi$ be the corresponding quadratic character of $(\mathbb{Z}/d\mathbb{Z})^*$.
If $\chi$ is even and $B_2(\chi) \neq 0$ (mod 7) then the Mordell-Weil group $E(K)$ over the real quadratic field $K$ has rank 0; if $\chi$ is odd and $B_5(\chi) \neq 0$ (mod 7)

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

then the Mordell-Weil group $E(K)$ over the imaginary quadratic field $K$ has rank 1. Actually, in the second case, the rank is 1 under the much weaker hypothesis that the circular unit $\prod_{a=1}^{7|d|} (1 - \zeta^a)^{z \omega(a)}$ is not a $7^{\text{th}}$ power in $K(\mu_7)^*$, when $\zeta$ is a $(7d^{\text{th}})$ root of unity and $\omega$ is the Teichmuller character (mod 7).
In both cases, we also show that the Tate-Shafarevitch group of $E$ over $K$ contains no elements of order 7.

# Chapter 1

1. Let $N$ be a prime.
   The cusps $[0]$ and $[\infty]$ on $X = X_0(N)$ are both rational over $\mathbb{Q}$ and the class of the divisor $d = [0] - [\infty]$ has order $n = \mathrm{num}\left(\frac{N - 1}{12}\right)$ in $J(\mathbb{Q})$.
   Let $D$ denote the cuspidal group generated by $d$.

Let $p$ be an odd prime which divides $n$, and let $C$ be the Sylow $p$-subgroup of $D$.
Then $C \simeq \mathbb{Z} / p^f$, where $f = \operatorname{ord}_p(n)$.
The subgroup $C$ is stable under the action of the Hecke correspondences $T_l$ for $l \neq N$ and the canonical involution $w_N$.
Let $T \subset \operatorname{End}(J)$ denote the commutative sub-algebra, generated by these correspondences over $\mathbb{Z}$, and let $I \subset T$ be the ideal which annihilates $C$.
Then $I$ is generated over $\mathbb{Z}$ by the elements $\eta_l = T_l - 1 - l$ for $l \neq N$, $w_N + 1$, and $p^f$; we have $T / I \simeq \mathbb{Z} / p^f$.

Let $\mathfrak{p} = (p, I)$ be the maximal ideal of $T$ which contains $I$, and define the completion $T_{\mathfrak{p}} = \varprojlim_{n} T / \mathfrak{p}^{n}$.
Mazur has shown that $T_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is a Gorenstein ring [10, p. 125], which is generated over $\mathbb{Z}_p$ by the single element $\eta_l \in T$.
Here $l$ is any odd prime which is not a $p^{\text{th}}$ power (mod $N$) and is not $\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.
The ideal $IT_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is principal, generated by $\eta_l$.
When $f = 1$ this implies that $T_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is a discrete valuation ring, which is totally ramified over $\mathbb{Z}_p$.

2. Let $\gamma_{\mathfrak{p}}$ denote the kernel of the map $T \to T_{\mathfrak{p}}$, and let $\gamma_{\mathfrak{p}}J$ denote the subabelian variety of $J$ generated by the images of $\alpha J$ for all $\alpha \in \gamma$.
   Following Mazur [10], we let $A$ denote the Eisenstein quotient $J / \gamma_{\mathfrak{p}}J$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ which is determined by $\mathfrak{p}$.
   The irreducible factors of $A$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$) correspond to the components of $\operatorname{Spec}(T)$ which contain the point $\mathfrak{p}$.

3. We recall some facts about the geometry of $A$, which are due to Deligne and Mazur [10, Appendix].
   First, $A$ has good reduction at all places $l \neq N$ of $\mathbb{Q}$.
   At $N$ it has split multiplicative reduction; let $A^0$ denote the connected component of the special fibre $\tilde{A}$ of the Néron model over $\mathbb{Z}_N$.
   Then $A^0$ is a $\mathbb{Z}/N$-split torus, and the projections $J \to A \to \tilde{A}$ induce isomorphisms:

$$
C \xrightarrow{\sim} A(\mathbb{Q})_{\mathrm{tor}} \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p \xrightarrow{\sim} \tilde{A}/A^0 \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p.
$$

The $p$-Sylow subgroup $C'$ of the Shimura subgroup $\Sigma$ of $J(\mathbb{Q}(\mu_N))$ also projects injectively into $A(\mathbb{Q}(\mu_N))_{\mathrm{tor}} \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p$.
We have the direct sum decomposition:

$$
A_I = C \oplus C' \simeq \mathbb{Z}/p^f \oplus \mu_{p^f} \tag{3.1}
$$

as group schemes over $\mathbb{Z}$.

The Eisenstein descent on
J_0(N)

4. Let $B = A / C$ be the quotient of $A$ by the image of the $p$ -cuspidal group.
   Then $B$ has good reduction at all places $l \neq N$ and split multiplicative reduction at $N$ .
   In this case, however, the $p$ -Sylow subgroup for $\tilde{B} / B^0$ is trivial.
   Here are some examples:

| N=11 | T=Z                   | A=J of dim 1  | y2+y=x3-x2-10x-20         | Δ=-115 |
| ---- | --------------------- | ------------- | ------------------------- | ------ |
| p=5  | Tp=Z5                 | B=J/C         | y2+y=x3-x2-7,820x-263,580 | Δ=-11  |
| N=19 | T=Z                   | A=J of dim 1  | y2+y=x3+x2-9x-15          | Δ=-193 |
| p=3  | Tp=Z3                 | B=J/C         | y2+y=x3+x2-769x-8,470     | Δ=-19  |
| N=37 | T={ (a,b)∈Z2: a≡b(2)} | A=J- of dim 1 | y2+y=x3+x2-23x-50         | Δ=373  |
| p=3  | Tp=Z3                 | B=J-/C        | y2+y=x3+x2-1,873x-31,833  | Δ=37   |
| N=23 | T=Z[1+√5/2]           | A=J of dim 2  | A/A0≈Z/11                 |        |
| p=11 | Tp=Z11                | B=J/C         | B/B0≈(1)                  |        |
| N=29 | T=Z[√2]               | A=J of dim 2  | A/A0≈Z/7                  |        |
| p=7  | Tp=Z7                 | B=J/C         | B/B0≈(1)                  |        |
| N=31 | T=Z[1+√5/2]           | A=J of dim 2  | A/A0≈Z/5                  |        |
| p=5  | Tp=Z5[√5]             | B=J/C         | B/B0≈(1)                  |        |

There are no cases beyond $N = 37$ where $\dim A = 1$ [10]

5. To set up the first $I$ -descent, let us fix an endomorphism $\eta \in T$ of $A$ which generates the locally principal ideal $IT_{\mathfrak{p}}$ and is otherwise prime to $p$ .
   By this we mean: $T \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p / \eta (T \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p) \simeq T / I \simeq \mathbb{Z} / p^f$ .
   Since $\eta$ annihilates the cuspidal group $C$ , we have a commutative triangle of isogenies

(5.1)

![img-53.jpeg](img-53.jpeg)

where $C = \ker \phi$ and $\phi (\ker \eta) = \ker \pi$ .
We have isomorphisms of group schemes over $\mathbb{Z}$ :

(5.2)

$$
\left\{ \begin{array}{l} \ker \phi \simeq \mathbb {Z} / p ^ {f} \\ \ker \pi \simeq \mu_ {p ^ {f}} \times G \end{array} \right.
$$

where $G$ is finite of order prime to $p$ .

6. Over the local field $K = \mathbb{Q}_N$ the abelian varieties $A$ and $B$ have split multiplicative reduction, so admit Tate parametrizations:

(6.1)

$$
0 \to L \xrightarrow [ q ]{} \operatorname {H o m} (M, K ^ {*}) \to A (K) \to 0
$$

$$
0 \to L ^ {\prime} \xrightarrow [ q ]{} \operatorname {H o m} \left(M ^ {\prime}, K ^ {*}\right) \to B (K) \to 0
$$

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

where $L, L', M$ and $M'$ are free abelian groups of rank $= \dim(A) = \dim(B)$.
The isogeny $\phi: A \to B$ induces group homomorphisms.

$$
\phi_1: L \to L'
$$

$$
\phi_2: M' \to M \tag{6.2}
$$

which are both injective so that the diagram

$$
\begin{array}{c}
0 \xrightarrow{} L \xrightarrow{} q \xrightarrow{} \operatorname{Hom}(M, K^*) \xrightarrow{} A(K) \xrightarrow{} 0 \\
\Biggl \downarrow_{\phi_1} \quad \Biggl \downarrow_{\operatorname{Hom}K^* \circ \phi_2} \quad \Biggl \downarrow_{\phi} \\
0 \xrightarrow{} L' \xrightarrow{} q' \xrightarrow{} \operatorname{Hom}(M', K^*) \xrightarrow{} B(K) \xrightarrow{} 0
\end{array}
\tag{6.3}
$$

commutes.
We claim that $\operatorname{coker} \phi_1 \simeq \mathbb{Z}/p^f$ and $\operatorname{coker} \phi_2 \simeq (0)$.
To show this, consider the diagram derived from the above using the valuation homomorphism $v: K^* \to \mathbb{Z}$:

$$
\begin{array}{c}
0 \xrightarrow{} L \xrightarrow{} \operatorname{Hom}(M, \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{} \tilde{A}/A^0 \xrightarrow{} 0 \\
\Biggl \downarrow_{\phi_1} \quad \Biggl \downarrow_{\operatorname{Hom} \circ \phi_2} \quad \Biggl \downarrow_{\phi} \\
0 \xrightarrow{} L' \xrightarrow{} \operatorname{Hom}(M' \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{} \tilde{B}/B^0
\end{array}
$$

Since $\tilde{B}/B^0 = \tilde{A}/A^0 \cdot C$, the map $\phi$ is surjective and $\ker \phi = C \simeq \mathbb{Z}/p^f$.
Since $\phi_1$ and $\operatorname{Hom} \circ \phi_2$ are injective, we must have $C \subseteq \operatorname{coker} \phi_1$.
But the order of $\operatorname{coker} \phi_1 \oplus \operatorname{coker} \operatorname{Hom} \circ \phi_2$ is equal to $p^f = \deg \phi$.
Hence $C = \operatorname{coker} \phi_1$ and $\operatorname{coker} \operatorname{Hom} \circ \phi_2 = (0)$.
This establishes our claim.

Using the snake lemma on (6.3) and the fact that $\operatorname{Hom}_{K^*} \circ \phi_2$ is an isomorphism, we find

$$
B(K)/\phi A(K) = (0). \tag{6.4}
$$

Hence

$$
A(K)/\pi B(K) = A(K)/\eta A(K) = \mathbb{Z}/p^f \oplus \mu_{p^f} \oplus G' \tag{6.5}
$$

where $G'$ has order prime to $p$.
The results in (6.4–6.5) clearly also hold whenever $K$ is a finite extension of $\mathbb{Q}_N$.

7. Let $F$ be a number field, with ring of integers $R$ and Galois group $G = \operatorname{Gal}(\bar{F}/F)$.
   For each place $v$, let $G_v$ be a decomposition subgroup in $G$ and $I_v$ an inertia subgroup of $G_v$.
   Let $R' = \mathbb{Z}[1/N]$; this is a Dedekind domain whose class group $\operatorname{Pic}(R')$ is finite and whose unit group $U'$ is finitely generated.
   Our aim in this section is to calculate the Selmer groups of the isogenies $\phi, \pi$, and $\eta$ over $F$ in terms of these invariants of $R'$.

The exact sequences of $G_v$-modules

$$
\begin{array}{c}
0 \xrightarrow{} \mathbb{Z}/p^f \xrightarrow{} A(\bar{F}_v) \xrightarrow{\phi} B(\bar{F}_v) \xrightarrow{} 0 \\
0 \xrightarrow{} \mu_{p^f} \times G(\bar{F}) \xrightarrow{} B(\bar{F}_v) \xrightarrow{\pi} A(\bar{F}_v) \xrightarrow{} 0
\end{array} \tag{7.1}
$$

The Eisenstein descent on $J_0(N)$
307

give coboundary maps:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\lambda_v: B(F_v)/\phi\,A(F_v) \hookrightarrow H^1(G_v, \mathbf{Z}/p^f) \tag{7.2} \\
\mu_v: A(F_v)/\pi\,B(F_v) \to H^1(G_v, \mu_{p^f})
\end{array}
$$

where the second is obtained by projecting the usual coboundary from $H^1(G_v, \mu_{p^f} \times G)$ to the first factor.
The map

$$
\mu_v: A(F_v)/\pi\,B(F_v) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p \hookrightarrow H^1(G_v, \mu_{p^f})
$$

is an injection, as $G$ is prime to $p$.

**Proposition 7.3.**

1. $H^1(G_v, \mathbf{Z}/p^f) = \mathrm{Hom}(G_v, \mathbf{Z}/p^f)$.
   The image of $\lambda_v$ is trivial if $v$ is archimedean or $v$ divides $N$.
   If $v$ is non-archimedean and prime to $N$, the image of $\lambda_v$ is $\mathrm{Hom}(G_v / I_v, \mathbf{Z}/p^f)$.

2. $H^1(G_v, \mu_{p^f}) \simeq F_v^* / F_v^{*p^f}$.
   The image of $\mu_v$ is trivial if $v$ is archimedean.
   If $v$ divides $N$, the image of $\mu_v$ is equal to $F_v^* / F_v^{*p^f} = \mathbf{Z} / p^f \oplus \mu_{p^f}$.
   If $v$ is a non-archimedean place which is prime to $N$, the image of $\lambda_v$ is equal to the cosets $\alpha (\bmod F^{*p^f})$ where $v(\alpha) \equiv 0 \pmod{p^f}$.

**Proof.** The case of archimedean $v$ is trivial, as $p$ is odd.
The case of $v$ dividing $N$ follows from (6.4–6.5).
When $v$ is non-archimedean and prime to $N$, the varieties $A$ and $B$ have good reduction (mod $v$), and the image consists of “unramified” cocycles [4].

We define the subgroups $\operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F) \subset H^1(G, \mathbf{Z}/p^f)$ and $\operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p \subseteq H^1(G, \mu_{p^f})$ to be those global classes whose local restrictions are in the image of $\lambda_v$ (respectively $\mu_v$) for all places $v$.

**Corollary 7.4.**

1. $\operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F) = \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Pic} R', \mathbf{Z}/p^f)$

2. $\operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p = H^1(R', \mu_{p^f})$ and Kummer theory gives an exact sequence:

$$
0 \to U'/U'^p \to H^1(R', \mu_{p^f}) \to \operatorname{Pic}(R')_{p^f} \to 0.
$$

**Proof.** By (7.3), $\operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F)$ consists of the unramified characters $\chi: G \to \mathbf{Z}/p^f$ which are trivial on $G_v$ for $v/N$.
Class-field theory identifies this subgroup of $\operatorname{Hom}(G, \mathbf{Z}/p^f)$ with $\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Pic} R', \mathbf{Z}/p^f)$.

By (7.3), $\operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p$ consists of the cocycles $\alpha \in F^* / F^{*p^f}$ such that $v(\alpha) \equiv 0 \pmod{p^f}$ for all $v$ not dividing $N$.
Hence $(\alpha) = \alpha^{p^f}$ where $\alpha$ is an ideal of $R'$.
This gives an exact sequence:

$$
\begin{array}{c}
0 \to U'/U'^p^f \to \operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p \to \operatorname{Pic}(R')_{p^f} \to 0 \\
\alpha \mapsto \operatorname{class}(\alpha).
\end{array}
$$

If we define $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F) = \operatorname{Sel}_{\eta}(A) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p$, then we have an exact sequence:

$$
0 \to \operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F) \to \operatorname{Sel}_I(F) \to \operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbf{Z}_p \tag{7.5}
$$

whose end terms are calculated by (7.4).

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

# Chapter 2

8. We now assume $N = p^2$, where $p$ is an odd prime.
   The curve $X = X_0(N)$ then has $p + 1$ cusps over $\mathbb{C}$; the two cusps $[0]$ and $[\infty]$ are rational over $\mathbb{Q}$ and the class of the divisor $d = [0] - [\infty]$ has order $n = \mathrm{num}\left(\frac{N - 1}{24}\right)$ in $J(\mathbb{Q})$.
   The $p - 1$ cusps $[a/p]$ for $1 \leq a \leq p - 1$ are all rational over $K = \mathbb{Q}(\mu_p)$.
   Let $D$ be the subgroup of $J(K)$ generated by the classes of divisors of degree 0 supported on the cusps of $X$.
   By the Manin-Drinfeld theorem, $D$ is a finite group.
   We let $C$ denote its $p$-Sylow subgroup.

The $\mathbb{Z}_p$-module $C$ admits an action of $G = \operatorname{Gal}(K / \mathbb{Q}) \simeq (\mathbb{Z} / p)^*$.
For each integer $k$ with $0 \leq k \leq p - 2$, we let $C_k$ denote the subgroup on which $G$ acts as via the character $\omega^k$, where $\omega$ gives the action of $G$ on $\mu_p$.
By an analysis of certain modular units [6] and the associated weight 2 Eisenstein series, Stevens has proven the following [22]:

# Proposition 8.1.

$$
C_k \simeq \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} 0 &amp; \text{if } k = 0, \frac{p - 1}{2}, \frac{p + 1}{2} \\ \mathbb{Z}_p / B_2(\omega^{-2k}) \mathbb{Z}_p &amp; \text{if } 0 &lt; k &lt; \frac{p - 1}{2} \\ \mathbb{Z}_p / pB_2(\omega^{-2k}) \mathbb{Z}_p &amp; \text{if } \frac{p + 1}{2} &lt; k &lt; p - 1. \end{array} \right.
$$

We will be interested in the case when $C_k$ is non-trivial but $B_2(\omega^{-2k}) \neq 0 \pmod{p}$.
Hence we shall assume that $p \geq 7$ and that $k$ is a fixed integer in the range $\frac{p + 1}{2} &lt; k &lt; p - 1$.
We set $k' = \frac{3p - 1}{2} - k$, so $k'$ also lies in this range.
Finally, we shall henceforth assume that:

$$
B_2(\omega^{-2k}) B_2(\omega^{-2k'}) \neq 0 \pmod{p}. \tag{8.2}
$$

In this situation, we find:

$$
\left\{ \begin{array}{l} C_k \simeq C_{k'} \simeq \mathbb{Z} / p \\ C_{p - k} \simeq C_{p - k'} \simeq 0. \end{array} \right. \tag{8.3}
$$

We remark that the assumption on Bernoulli numbers looks more natural if one passes to the odd character $\chi = \omega^{2k - p}$.
Then (8.2) is equivalent to assuming:

$$
B_1(\chi) B_1(\chi^{-1}) \neq 0 \pmod{p}. \tag{8.4}
$$

Via results of Ribet [14], this is in turn equivalent to assuming that the $\chi$ and $\chi^{-1}$ eigencomponents of the $p$-class group of $K$ are trivial.

One case where the hypothesis (8.4) is met is when $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$ and $k = k' = \frac{3p - 1}{4}$.
Then $\chi = \chi^{-1} = \omega^{\frac{p - 1}{2}}$ is the odd quadratic character associated to the field $L = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p})$.
Since the class number $h$ of $L$ is known to be less than $p$,

The Eisenstein descent on
J_0(N)

and $h = -B_{1}(\chi) = -B_{1}(\chi^{-1})$ , our hypothesis is certainly satisfied.
This is the case treated in [2] and [4].

9. We now imitate the argument in Chap.
   1. Let $T$ denote the commutative sub-algebra of $\operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{Q}}(J)$ generated by the Hecke correspondences $T_{l}$ for $l \neq p$ and the involution $w_{p} = w_{N}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ .
      Let $\mathfrak{p}$ and $\mathfrak{p}'$ denote the maximal ideals of $T$ which annihilate the subgroups $C_{k}$ and $C_{k'}$ respectively.
      Then $\mathfrak{p}$ is generated by the elements $T_{l} - l^{k} - l^{p - k}$ for $l \neq p$ , $w_{p} - (-1)^{k}$ , and $p$ .
      Let $T_{\mathfrak{p}}$ and $T_{\mathfrak{p}'}$ be the completions of $T$ with respect to these ideals, and let $A$ and $A'$ denote the corresponding Eisenstein quotients of $J$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ .

Let $L$ be the unique quadratic field contained in $K = \mathbb{Q}(\mu_p)$ , and let $R_p$ denote the endomorphism of $J$ over $L$ which is defined by Shimura in [21].
This induces the Atkin-Lehner operator $R_p^*$ on the space of cusp forms of weight 2 [1], and satisfies $R_p^3 = \left(\frac{-1}{p}\right) \cdot R_p \cdot p$ .
Since $R_p T_l = \left(\frac{l}{p}\right) T_l R_p$ , we have $R_p \mathfrak{p} = \mathfrak{p}' R_p$ .
The endomorphism $R_p$ induces an isogeny $f \colon A \to A'$ of degree $p^{\dim(A)}$ over $L$ , which makes the diagram commutative:

(9.1)

![img-54.jpeg](img-54.jpeg)

In particular, $A$ is isogenous to the twist $B$ of $A'$ by the quadratic extension $L / \mathbb{Q}$ .
Here are some examples.

$p = 7$

$k = 5,k^{\prime} = 5$

$A = J$ of dim 1

$B = A / C_{k}$

$p = 11$ (see [8])

$k = 7,k^{\prime} = 9$

$A$ of dim 1

$B = A / C_{k}$

$k = 8,k^{\prime} = 8$

$A$ of dim 1

$B = A / C_{k}$

$k = 9,k^{\prime} = 7$

$A$ of dim 1

$B = A / C_{k}$

$p = 13$

$k = 8,k^{\prime} = 11$

$A = J^{+}$ of dim 3

$k = 9,k^{\prime} = 10$

$A = J^{-} / J_{1}(13)$ of dim 3

$y^{2} + xy = x^{3} - x^{2} - 2x - 7$

$y^{2} + xy = x^{3} + x^{2} - 3,632x + 82,757$

$y^{2} + y = x^{3} - x^{2} - 7x + 10$

$y^{2} + y = x^{3} - x^{2} - 887x - 10,143$

$y^{2} + y = x^{3} - x^{2} - 30x - 76$

$y^{2} + xy + y = x^{3} + x^{2} - 305x + 7,888$

$k = 10,k^{\prime} = 9$

$A = J^{+}$

$k = 11,k^{\prime} = 8$

$A = J^{-} / J_{1}(13)$

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

The only cases beyond $p = 11$ when $\dim A = \dim B = 1$ are for:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
p = 19, 43, 67, 163 \\
k = k' = \frac{3p - 1}{4} = 14, 32, 50, 122 \\
A = A(p) \quad \Delta = -p^3 \\
B = A(p)^* \quad \Delta = -p^9 \quad \text{in the notation of [4].}
\end{array}
$$

10. The completions $T_{\mathfrak{p}}$ and $T_{\mathfrak{p}'}$ are both local rings of dimension 1 which contain $\mathbb{Z}_p$ and have residue fields $\mathbb{Z}/p$.
    We shall now assume:

$$
T_{\mathfrak{p}} \simeq T_{\mathfrak{p}'} \simeq \mathbb{Z}_p. \tag{10.1}
$$

In particular, $\mathfrak{p}$ and $\mathfrak{p}'$ are both regular points of $\operatorname{Spec}(T)$ and the Eisenstein quotients $A$ and $A'$ are both simple over $\mathbb{Q}$.

Although (10.1) holds for all the examples we have computed, we do not see how to prove it in general.
Perhaps the ideas of Ribet and Papier [16] could be used to show that $\mathfrak{p}$ and $\mathfrak{p}'$ are regular points of $\operatorname{Spec}(T)$, so $T_{\mathfrak{p}}$ and $T_{\mathfrak{p}'}$ are discrete valuation rings which are totally ramified over $\mathbb{Z}_p$.
Indeed, the hypothesis (8.2) on Bernoulli numbers implies that the maximal abelian $p$-extensions $K_{\chi}$ and $K_{\chi^{-1}}$ of $K = \mathbb{Q}(\mu_p)$ in which only the prime dividing $p$ is ramified and $\operatorname{Gal}(K / \mathbb{Q})$ acts via the characters $\chi = \omega^{2k - p}$ and $\chi^{-1}$ are both cyclic over $K$, with $\operatorname{Gal}(K_{\chi} / K) \simeq \operatorname{Gal}(K_{\chi^{-1}} / K) \simeq \mathbb{Z}_p$.
Let $l$ be a rational prime with $l \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ such that $\operatorname{Frob}(l)$ generates $\operatorname{Gal}(K_{\chi^{-1}} / K)$ and $\operatorname{Gal}(K_{\chi} / K)$; such primes exist by Nakayama's lemma and the Chebotarev density theorem (they have density $\geq \frac{p - 1}{p^2}$).
It seems reasonable to conjecture that $\eta_l = T_l - 1 - l$ is a uniformizing parameter in $T_{\mathfrak{p}}$ and $T_{\mathfrak{p}'}$.

11. Under the assumption (10.1) the quotients $A$ and $A'$ are simple over $\mathbb{Q}$, of dimension $g$.
    The action of $T$ on their tangent spaces gives two packets $\{f^{\sigma}\}$ and $\{f'^{\sigma}\}$ of conjugate eigenforms of weight 2, each containing $g$ elements.
    Let $f = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n q^n$ and put $T_k = \mathbb{Z}[\ldots a_n \ldots]$, $E = \mathbb{Q}(\ldots a_n \ldots)$.
    Then $T_k$ is an order in the totally real field $E$ of degree $g$, and $\mathfrak{p}$ may be identified with a regular prime ideal of $T_k$ with residual degree and ramification index equal to 1. For all primes $l$, we have the congruence:

$$
a_l \equiv l^k + l^{p - k} \pmod{p}. \tag{11.1}
$$

The form $f' = \sum_{n \geq 1} b_n q^n$ may be chosen with $b_n = \binom{n}{p} a_n$.
Its coefficients generate $T_{k'} = T_k$, and satisfy the congruence:

$$
b_l \equiv l^{k'} + l^{p - k'} \pmod{p}. \tag{11.2}
$$

In the examples of §9, we have $T_k = \mathbb{Z}$ for all $k$ when $p = 7, 11$.
When $p = 13$, we have $T_k = \mathbb{Z}[\alpha]$ for all $k$, where $\alpha$ satisfies the cubic equation $\alpha^3 + 2\alpha^2 - \alpha - 1$.

The Eisenstein descent on $J_0(N)$
311

Then $T_k$ is the maximal order in the field $E = \mathbb{Q}(\mu_7)^+$.
The ideal $\mathfrak{p}$ is generated by $(13, \alpha - 2)$ when $k = 8, 11$ and by $(13, \alpha - 5)$ when $k = 9, 10$.

12. Let $\pi = \bigotimes_{v} \pi_v$ be the cuspidal representation of $PGL_2(\mathbb{A}_{\mathbb{Q}})$ associated to the modular form $f$.

**Proposition 12.1** [15].
$\pi_p$ is a super-cuspidal representation of $PGL_2(\mathbb{Q}_p)$.

**Proof.** If $\pi_p$ is special, or in the principal series of representations, there is a Dirichlet character $\varepsilon$ of conductor $p$ such that $f \otimes \varepsilon = g$ is a weight two new form on $\Gamma_1(p)$ with character $\varepsilon^2$.
Since the coefficients of $f$ are real, we also have $f \otimes \varepsilon^{-1} = \overline{g}$.

Let $w$ be the Atkin-Lehner involution on the space of weight 2 cuspforms on $\Gamma_1(p)$.
Then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
g \| w = \lambda \bar {g} \\
\bar {g} \| w = \lambda^ {- 1} g \\
\end{array}
$$

for some constant $\lambda \in \overline{\mathbb{Q}}^*$.
Let $\mathfrak{p}$ denote a prime of $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ extending our distinguished prime of $E$.
By replacing $g$ by $\bar{g}$ if necessary, we may assume that both $g$ and $g \| w$ have $\mathfrak{p}$-integral Fourier coefficients.
As Serre has noted [18], these conditions insure the existence of a new form $h = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} c_n q^n$ of weight $t \leq p + 1$ on $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$ whose Fourier expansion is congruent to that of $g$ (mod $\mathfrak{p}$).
Hence

$$
c _ {l} \equiv \varepsilon (l) \left(l ^ {k} + l ^ {p - k}\right) (\mathrm {m o d} \mathfrak {p})
$$

for $l \neq p$.
Since $\varepsilon(l) \equiv l^n \pmod{\mathfrak{p}}$ for some $n$, this gives the congruence

$$
\left\{ \begin{array}{l}
c _ {l} \equiv l ^ {a} + l ^ {b} (\mathrm {m o d} \mathfrak {p}) \\
a - b \equiv (2 k - 1) (\mathrm {m o d} p - 1). \end{array} \right. \tag {12.2}
$$

But then Lemma 8 of [23] shows that one of $(a, b)$ is congruent to $0 \pmod{p - 1}$, the other is congruent to $t - 1 \pmod{p - 1}$, and $p$ divides the numerator of the Bernoulli number $B_t$.
This contradicts our hypothesis (8.2).

Let $l \neq p$ be a prime and let $V = \varprojlim_{n} A_{l^n} \otimes \mathbb{Q}$.
This vector space has rank 2 over the algebra $E \otimes \mathbb{Q}_l = \pi E_\lambda$, and we obtain a decomposition $V = \oplus V_\lambda$ into vector spaces of dimension 2 over the local fields $E_\lambda$.
This decomposition is stable under the action of $G_{\mathbb{Q}} = \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}} / \mathbb{Q})$ and the resulting homomorphisms

$$
r _ {\lambda} \colon G _ {\mathbb {Q}} \to \operatorname {A u t} (V _ {\lambda}) \simeq G L _ {2} (E _ {\lambda})
$$

form a compatible system of $\lambda$-adic representations with coefficients in $E$.
If $q \neq l, p$ is a prime, the characteristic polynomial of $\operatorname{Frob}(q)$ on $V_{\lambda}$ is $x^{2} - a_{q}x + q = 0$.
By (12.1) and a theorem of Deligne, the representation $r_{\lambda}$ is absolutely irreducible when restricted to a decomposition group $G_{\mathbb{Q}_p} = \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_p / \mathbb{Q}_p)$ at $p$.
Since $p$ is odd, it is monomial, hence of the form $r_{\lambda} = \operatorname{Ind} \chi$ where $\chi$ is the character of the Galois group $G_K$ of a quadratic extension $K$ of $\mathbb{Q}_p$.

Using local class field theory, we may identify $\chi$ with a character $\chi \colon K^{*} \to \overline{E}_{\lambda}^{*}$.
Since $r_{\lambda}$ is irreducible on $G_{\mathbb{Q}_p}$, $\chi(\alpha) \neq \chi(\alpha^\sigma)$ for some $\alpha \in K^*$, where $\sigma$

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

denotes the non-trivial automorphism of $K / \mathbb{Q}_p$.
Since $\operatorname{det}(r_{\lambda})$ is unramified at $p$, $\chi \circ \mathbb{N}_{K / \mathbb{Q}_p}$ is trivial when restricted to the unit group $U$ of $K^*$.
Let $f_{\chi}$ denote the conductor of $\chi$; since $(p^2) = \mathrm{disc}(K / \mathbb{Q}_p) \cdot \mathbb{N}_{K / \mathbb{Q}_p} f_{\chi}$ we have only two possibilities:

$$
\left\{ \begin{array}{l l} d _ {K} = (1), &amp; f _ {\chi} = (p) \\ d _ {K} = (p), &amp; f _ {\chi} = \left(\pi_ {K}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \left(\pi_ {K} ^ {2}\right) = (p) \text{ in } K. \end{array} \right. \tag {12.3}
$$

In particular, $\chi$ is tamely ramified, so trivial when restricted to the subgroup $(1 + \pi_K O_K)$ of $U$.
Let $n$ be the order of $\chi$ on $U$.

**Proposition 12.4.**

1. If $d_K = (1)$ then $n &gt; 2$ and $n \mid (p + 1)$
2. If $d_K = (p)$ then $n = 2$ and $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$

**Proof.**

1. In this case $U / (1 + pO_K) \simeq \mathbb{F}_{p^2}^*$ has order $p^2 - 1$; the subgroup of norms from $K / \mathbb{Q}_p$ give the subgroup $\mathbb{F}_p^*$ of order $p - 1$.
   Since $\chi$ is a character of the quotient, its order $n$ divides $(p + 1)$.
   Since $K^* = p^{\mathbf{Z}} \times \mathbb{F}_{p^2}^* \times (1 + pO_K)$, we must have $\chi(\zeta) \neq \chi(\zeta^\sigma)$ for some $\zeta \in \mathbb{F}_{p^2}^*$.
   Since $\chi(\zeta \zeta^\sigma) = 1$, we have $\chi(\zeta)^{-1} = \chi(\zeta^\sigma)$.
   Hence $\chi^2 \neq 1$.
2. In this case $U / (1 + \pi_K O_K) \simeq \mathbb{F}_p^*$ has order $p - 1$; the subgroup of norms is $\mathbb{F}_p^{*2}$ of index 2. Since $\chi \neq 1$ we have $n = 2$.
   Let $\pi_K$ be a uniformizer with $\pi_K^2 \in \mathbb{Q}_p^*$.
   Since $K^* = \pi_K^{\mathbf{Z}} \times \mathbb{F}_p^* \times (1 + \pi_K O_K)$, we must have $\chi(\pi^\sigma) = \chi(\pi)$.
   Hence $\chi(-1) = -1$, and $-1 \notin \mathbb{F}_p^{*2}$.
   Thus $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$.

Let $M$ be the cyclic extension of $K$ corresponding to $\chi$, and $e$ the ramification index of $M / \mathbb{Q}_p$ when $d_K = (1)$ we have $e = n$; when $d_K = (p)$ we have $e = 2n = 4$.
Hence:

**Corollary 12.5.**
$e$ is greater than 2 and divides $(p + 1)$.

13. With the above information on $r_{\lambda}$, we now turn to an analysis of the group scheme $C_k$.
    A short calculation shows that $C_k$ is in the kernel of the isogeny $f \colon A \to A'$ defined in (9.1).
    By (10.1) we have $A_{\mathfrak{p}^n} \simeq A_{\mathfrak{p}^n}' \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/p^n)^2$ as groups; hence the limits:

$$
\left\{ \begin{array}{l} F = \varinjlim_{n} A _ {\mathrm {p} ^ {n}} \\ F ^ {\prime} = \varinjlim_{n} A _ {\mathrm {p} ^ {n}} ^ {\prime} \end{array} \right. \tag {13.1}
$$

are $p$-divisible groups of height 2; the Weil pairing on $A_{p^n}$ and $A_{p^n}'$ shows that both $F$ and $F'$ are self-dual over $\mathbb{Q}$.
They are related by the isogeny $f \colon F \to F'$ over $L = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{\pm p})$.

**Proposition 13.2.**
The sequence $0 \to C_k \to F \xrightarrow[f]{} F' \to 0$ is exact.

**Proof.** Since $C_k \subset F_p$ is in the kernel of $f$, it suffices to show the kernel has order $p$.
To do this, we note that the kernel of $f \colon A \to A'$ is a maximal isotropic subspace of $A_p$.
But the Weil pairing is non-degenerate on $A_p \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/p)^2$, so $\ker f \cap A_p = C_k$.

Over the field $M$ of §12, the abelian varieties $A$ and $A'$ acquire good reduction and the $p$-divisible groups $F$ and $F'$ extend to groups over the ring of integers $R$.
Since they are self-dual of height 2, we have $\dim F = \dim F' = 1$.

The Eisenstein descent on $J_0(N)$
313

**Proposition 13.3.** $F$ and $F'$ are formal groups of height 2 and dimension 1 over $R$.

_Proof._ Let $\pi$ be a uniformizing parameter of $R$, and $F_0$ the reduced $p$-divisible group over $R / \pi$.
It suffices to show that the characteristic polynomial $f(T) = T^2 - aT + q$ of the Frobenius endomorphism of $F_0$ satisfies $f(T) \equiv T^2 \pmod{p}$.

When restricted to $\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_p / M)$, $r_k$ is unramified and is the sum of the characters $\chi \circ \mathbb{N}_{M / K} \oplus \chi^\sigma \circ \mathbb{N}_{M / K}$.
Hence $a = (1 + \zeta) \chi(\mathbb{N}_{M / K} \pi)$, where $\alpha = \chi^\sigma(N \pi) / \chi(N \pi)$ is a root of unity.
Since $q = \zeta \circ \chi(N_{M / K} \pi)^2$ is a power of $p$, $a \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$ also.

The fact that $A$ acquires good reduction over $M$ has an amusing consequence.
By an argument of Serre-Tate ([20], top of p.
497), the inertia subgroup of $\operatorname{Gal}(M / \mathbb{Q}_p)$ acts as automorphisms of $\tilde{A} \pmod{\pi}$.
These automorphisms admit 2-dimensional $\lambda$-adic representations with coefficients in $E$; hence $E(\mu_e)$ is a quadratic extension of $E$.

**Corollary 13.4.** The totally real field $E$ contains $\mathbb{Q}(\mu_e)^+$.

When $p = 13$ we have $e = 7$ or 14, which accounts for the example at the end of §11.
For $p = 7$, we have $e = 4$.
For $p = 11$ and $k = 7,8,9$ we have $e = 3,4,6$ respectively.

14. Since $F$ and $F'$ have height 2 over $R$, the endomorphisms $[p]_F$ and $[p]_{F'}$ have the form:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
[p]_F(x) = p x + \dots + a x^p + \dots + u x^{p^2} + \dots \\
[p]_{F'}(x) = p x + \dots + a' x^p + \dots + u' x^{p^2} + \dots
\end{array}
$$

where $a \equiv a' \equiv 0 \pmod{\pi}$ and $u$ and $u'$ are units of $R$.
Recall that $e = v(p)$ is the ramification index of $M$; let $f = v(a)$ and $f' = v(a')$.

**Proposition 14.1.** We have the inequalities $0 &lt; f / e, f' / e &lt; \frac{p}{p + 1}$.

_Proof._ Consider the representation of the inertia subgroup $I$ of $\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{M}/M)$ on the $p$-torsion $V = F_p = A_p$.
The subgroup $P$ of wild inertia acts trivially on the semi-simplification $V^{ss}$ of $V$, and the resulting representation of $I_t = I/P$ decomposes over $\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p$ as the sum of two characters.

If $f / e &gt; \frac{p}{p + 1}$, the characters which occur are the $e^{\text{th}}$ powers of the two fundamental characters $\theta_{p^2 - 1}$, $\theta_{p^2 - 1}^p$ of level 2 for $I_t$ [19, p. 272].
If $e \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p + 1}$ this action is irreducible on $V^{ss}$, which contradicts the existence of the subgroup $C_k \subseteq V$.
If $e \equiv 0 \pmod{p + 1}$, then by (12.5) we must have $e = p + 1$, and $I_t$ acts via the scalar character $\theta_{p^2 - 1}^{p + 1} = \theta_{p - 1}$.
But $I$ acts on $C$ by the character $\omega^k = \theta_{p - 1}^{ek} = \theta_{p - 1}^{2k}$, so $2k \equiv 1 \pmod{p - 1}$.
This contradicts the hypothesis $\frac{p + 1}{2} &lt; k &lt; p - 1$.

A similar argument, using the fact that $I$ acts by $\omega^{k'}$ on $C_{k'}$, shows $f' / e &lt; \frac{p}{p + 1}$.

The inequalities of (14.1) are equivalent to the assertion that the Newton polygons of $[p]_F$ and $[p]_{F'}$ have breaks at the points $(p, f)$ and $(p, f')$:

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

![img-55.jpeg](img-55.jpeg)
Fig.
1

In particular, both $F$ and $F'$ have canonical subgroups in the sense of Lubin [9].

**Proposition 14.2.** $C_k$ is the canonical subgroup of $F$ and $C_{k'}$ is the canonical subgroup of $F'$.
We have:

$$
f / e = \frac {2 (p - k)}{p + 1} \quad f ^ {\prime} / e = \frac {2 (p - k ^ {\prime})}{p + 1}.
$$

**Proof.** The two characters of $I_{t}$ on $V^{ss}$ are $\theta_{p - 1}^{e - f}$ and $\theta_{p - 1}^{f}$, where the first gives the action on the canonical subgroup of $F$ [19, p. 272].
One of these must give the action on $C_k$, where $I_{t}$ acts via $\omega^k = \theta_{p - 1}^{ek}$.
Hence $ek\equiv e - f$ (mod $p - 1$) or $ek\equiv f$ (mod $p - 1$), the first case occurring when $C_k$ is canonical.

Write $de = p + 1$, so $dek = (p + 1)k \equiv 2k \equiv 2 - df$ or $df(\bmod p - 1)$.
Hence $df$ is congruent to either $2 - 2k$ or $2k \bmod (p - 1)$.
But $df &lt; \frac{pde}{p + 1} = p$ by (14.1) and $\frac{p + 1}{2} &lt; k &lt; p - 1$.
Hence we must have $df = 2(p - k)$ or $df = 2k - (p - 1)$.
Dividing by $de = p + 1$ we have

$$
\frac {f}{e} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \frac {2 (p - k)}{p + 1} \\ \frac {2 k - (p - 1)}{p + 1} \end{array} \right. \tag {14.3}
$$

with the first case occurring when $C_k$ is the canonical subgroup.
A similar argument gives two possibilities for $f'/e$.

Whichever identity holds in (14.3), we have the inequalities:

$$
\frac {1}{p + 1} &lt;   \frac {f ^ {\prime}}{e}, \quad \frac {f}{e} &lt;   \frac {p}{p + 1}. \tag {14.4}
$$

By (13.2) we have $F' = F / C_k$.
If $C_k$ were not canonical, then $f' = f / p$ [9], which contradicts (14.4).
Hence $\frac{f}{e} = \frac{2(p - k)}{p + 1}$ as claimed.

As a check on (14.2), we note that $f + f' = e$, which is consistent with Lubin's theory [9].
We also have the following corollary.

The Eisenstein descent on $J_0(N)$
315

Corollary 14.5. The representation of the wild inertia $P$ on $V$ is nontrivial.
In particular, $V$ is not a semi-simple $I$-module.

Proof.
The roots of $[p]_F$ generate an extension of $M$ with ramification divisible by the denominator of $f / (p^2 - p)$.
But $f &lt; \frac{pe}{p + 1} \leq p$, so $f \neq 0 \pmod{p}$.
Hence there is wild ramification.

15. We now have enough information on the group schemes $C_k$ and $C_{k'}$ to perform the calculations for the first Eisenstein descent.
    Recall that $B$ is the twist of $A'$ by $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{\pm p})$, the diagram (9.1) gives us a commutative triangle of isogenies over $\mathbb{Q}$:

(15.1)

![img-56.jpeg](img-56.jpeg)

Here $\eta$ is an element of $T$ which generates the locally principal ideal $T$ and is otherwise prime to $p$, $\phi$ is induced from $f$ and has kernel $C_k$, and $\ker \pi = \phi (\ker \eta)$ is the product of a cyclic group of order $p$ and a group of order prime to $p$.
The Galois group of $\mathbb{Q}$ acts on the $p$-Sylow subgroup $C'$ of $\ker \pi$ via the character $\omega^{\frac{p - 1}{2}} \cdot \omega^{k'} = \omega^{p - k}$ and the sequence $0 \to C_k \to A \to C' \to 0$ of Galois modules in non-split, even when restricted to the inertia group at $p$ (14.5).

As in §7, we obtain an exact sequence of Selmer groups over the number field $F$.

(15.2) $0 \to \operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F) \to \operatorname{Sel}_{\eta}(F) \to \operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F).$

Tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}_p$, this gives us an estimate of $\operatorname{Sel}_p(F) = \operatorname{Sel}_\eta(F) \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p$ in terms of $\operatorname{Sel}_\phi(F)$ and $\operatorname{Sel}_\pi(F) \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p$, which we shall now determine.

Assume $F$ is unramified at $p$, and put $K = F(\mu_p)$.
By Kummer theory, we have identifications:

(15.3) $\left\{ \begin{array}{l} \operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F) \subseteq H^{1}(G, \mu_{p}^{\otimes k}) = (K^{*} / K^{*p})^{\omega^{p - k}} \\ \operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbb{Z}_{p} \subseteq H^{1}(G, \mu_{p}^{\otimes p - k}) = (K^{*} / K^{*p})^{\omega^{k}}. \end{array} \right.$

In fact, the Selmer groups are subgroups of $H^{1}(R, \mu_{p}^{\otimes k})$ and $H^{1}(R, \mu_{p}^{\otimes p - k})$ respectively, where $R$ is the ring of integers of $F$.

Proposition 15.4. 1) $\alpha \in (K^{*} / K^{*p})^{\omega^{p - k}}$ is an element of $\operatorname{Sel}_{\phi}(F)$ if and only if $(\alpha) = \alpha^{p}$ and $\alpha_{v} \in (1 + \pi_{v}^{\frac{2(p - k)p}{p + 1}} O_{v}) K_{v}^{*p}$ for all places $v$ of $K$ dividing $p$.

2. $\alpha \in (K^{*} / K^{*p})^{\omega^{k}}$ is an element of $\operatorname{Sel}_{\pi}(F) \otimes \mathbb{Z}_p$ if and only if $(\alpha) = \alpha^p$ and $\alpha_v \in (1 + \pi_v^{\frac{2(p - k')p}{p + 1}} O_v) K_v^{*p}$ for all places $v$ of $K$ dividing $p$.

Proof.

1. We may extend scalars to a tamely ramified extension of $K$ where $C_k$ is a group scheme of order $p$ over the full ring of integers.
   Hence, if $\alpha$ is in the Selmer group, $v(\alpha) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$ for all finite places $v$.
   At places $v$ dividing $p$, $C_k$

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

is isomorphic to a group scheme $G_{a,b}$ of Oort-Tate type [17], with $ab = p$ and $v(b) = \frac{2(p - k)}{p + 1} v(p)$.
This follows from (14.2), which gives the slopes in the Newton polygon.
The subgroup of the 1-units in the Selmer group then follows from the cohomological calculations of Roberts [12, 17].

2. This is similar, but now $\ker \pi \simeq G_{a', b'}$ with $a' b' = p$ and

$$
v(b') = \frac{2(p - k')}{p + 1} v(p).
$$

## Chapter 3

16. Let $p$ be an odd prime, and let $F$ denote an abelian extension of $\mathbb{Q}$ of degree prime to $p$.
    Let $R$ denote the ring of integers of $F$, $U = R^*$ the unit group, and $D$ the $p$-primary component of the class group.

Let $\chi$ be a character of $\operatorname{Gal}(F / \mathbb{Q})$ of conductor $m$, which takes values in an unramified extension $W$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p$.
We say $\chi$ is even or odd if it is trivial or nontrivial on complex conjugation.
If $M$ is a $\mathbb{Z}_p$-module on which $\operatorname{Gal}(F / \mathbb{Q})$ acts, we let $M^{\chi}$ denote the sub-module of $M \otimes W$ where the Galois groups act via the character $\chi$.

If $\chi \neq 1$ is even, we define the circular unit:

$$
E(\chi) = \sum_{a=1}^{m} \chi^{-1}(a) \otimes (1 - \zeta^{-a}) \tag{16.1}
$$

in $U^{\chi}$, where $\zeta$ is a primitive $m^{\text{th}}$ roof of unity.

If $\chi \neq \omega^{-1}$ is odd, we define the Bernoulli number:

$$
B_1(\chi) = \sum_{a=1}^{m} \chi(a) \otimes (a/m) \tag{16.2}
$$

in $W$.
Suppose that $N$ is a prime with $\chi(N) = 1$, and let $\mathfrak{q}$ denote a prime factor of $(N)$ in the ring $O = \mathbb{Z}[\zeta]$.
Define the Gauss sum:

$$
g(a/m) = - \sum_{(O/\mathfrak{q})^*} \binom{x}{\mathfrak{q}}^{-\frac{\mathbb{N}\mathfrak{q} - 1}{m}} \zeta_N^{Trx}
$$

as in [7], where $\binom{x}{\mathfrak{q}}$ is the $m^{\text{th}}$ power residue symbol and $\zeta_N$ is a primitive $N^{\text{th}}$ root of unity.
The Jacobi sum:

$$
J(\chi) = \sum_{a=1}^{m} \chi^{-1}(a) \otimes g(a/m) \tag{16.3}
$$

then lies in $(U')^z$, where $U'$ is the unit group of the Dedekind domain $R' = R[1/N]$.
We let $D'$ denote the class group of $R'$.

The following basic result is due to Ribet [4], Greenberg [3], Mazur, and Wiles [13]:

**Proposition 16.4.** 1) Assume $\chi \neq \omega$ is odd.
Then $D^{\chi} = 0$ if and only if $B_1(\chi^{-1}) \neq 0$ (mod $pW$).

The Eisenstein descent on $J_0(N)$
317

2. Assume $\chi \neq \omega$ is odd and $\chi(N) = 1$.
   Then $(D')^z = 0$ if and only if $J(\chi) \neq 0$ (mod $U'^p$).

3. Assume $\chi \neq 1$ is even.
   Then $D^z = 0$ if and only if $E(\chi) \neq 0$ (mod $U^p$).

17) We return to the descent on $X_0(N)$, where $N$ is prime.
    Let $p$ be an odd prime factor of $\text{num}\left(\frac{N - 1}{12}\right) = n$, and let $f = \operatorname{ord}_p(n)$.
    Combining (16.4) with the results in §7, we obtain:

**Proposition 17.1.**

1. Assume $\chi \neq \omega^{\pm 1}$ is odd and $(1 - \chi(N))B_1(\chi)B_1(\chi^{-1}) \neq 0$ (mod $pW$).
   Then $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^z = 0$.

2. Assume $\chi \neq \omega^{\pm 1}$ is odd, $\chi(N) = 1$, and $J(\chi)J(\chi^{-1}) \neq 0$ (mod $U'^p$).
   Then $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^z \hookrightarrow (U'/U'^{p^f})^z = W/p^f$.

3. Assume $\chi \neq 1$ is even, $\chi(N) \neq 1$, and $E(\chi)E(\chi^{-1}) \neq 0$ (mod $U^p$).
   Then $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^z \hookrightarrow (U/U^{p^f})^z = W/p^f$.

We now use the exact sequence:

$$
0 \rightarrow A(F)/IA(F)^z \xrightarrow[a]{} \operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^z \rightarrow III(A/F)^z \rightarrow 0 \tag{17.2}
$$

to obtain the following corollary, which is due to Mazur [11].

**Corollary 17.3.** Assume that $\chi \neq \omega^{\pm 1}$ is odd, and $(1 - \chi(N))B_1(\chi)B_1(\chi^{-1}) \neq 0$ (mod $pW$).
Then $A(F)^z = 0$ and $III(A/F)^z = 0$.

In cases 2) and 3), we suspect $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^z$ is always non-trivial, but that there are points of infinite order in $A(F)^z$ only when $\chi^2 = 1$.
In this direction, we have:

**Corollary 17.4.** Assume $\chi^2 = 1$ and either:

$$
\chi \neq \omega^{\pm 1} \text{ is odd, } \chi(N) = 1, \quad \text{and} \quad J(\chi) \neq 0 \ (\mathrm{mod} \ U'^p)
$$

$$
\chi \neq 1 \quad \text{is even, } \chi(N) \neq 1, \quad \text{and} \quad E(\chi) \neq 0 \ (\mathrm{mod} \ U^p)
$$

Then $A(F)^z$ is infinite and $III(A/F)^z = 0$.
If $f = 1$, we have $\text{rank } A(F)^z = \dim A$.

**Proof.** First assume $\chi$ is odd.
The theory of Heegner points on $X_0(N)$ [6, 11] allows us to construct a class in $A(F)/IA(F)$ which maps to $J(\chi)$ in the Selmer group.
Since $J(\chi) \neq 0$, this shows the map $\alpha$ in (17.2) is surjective.
The class must come from a point of infinite order in $A(F)^z$, as Mazur has shown $A_I \simeq \mathbb{Z}/p^f \oplus \mu_{p^f}$ as group schemes over $\mathbb{Q}$ [10].

Next assume $\chi$ is even.
Choose an auxiliary imaginary quadratic field $F'$ with character $\chi'$; we insist that $\chi'(N) = 1$ and that $B_1(\chi \chi') \neq 0$ (mod $p$).
The theory of Heegner points then gives a class in $A(FF')/IA(FF')^z$ which maps to $B_1(\chi \chi') E(\chi)$ in the Selmer group.
Since $E(\chi) \neq 0$, this shows the map $\alpha$ in (17.2) is surjective.

When $f = 1$, the rank of $A(F)^z$ is a multiple of $\dim A$, as $\operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{Q}}(A) \otimes \mathbb{Q}$ is a field of degree $= \dim A$.
But $A(F)/IA(F)^z$ is a $T_p/I \simeq \mathbb{Z}/p$ module of rank 1, so this multiple is equal to 1.

18. We now return to the descent on $X_0(p^2)$, always under the assumptions of Chap.
    2. Thus $k$ is integer in the range $\frac{p + 1}{2} &lt; k &lt; p - 1$ and $k' = \frac{3p - 1}{2} - k$, the

B.H.
Gross and J.
Lubin

Bernoulli numbers $B_{2}(\omega^{-2k})$ and $B_{2}(\omega^{-2k^{\prime}})$ are units, and the completions $T_{\nu}$ and $T_{\nu^{\prime}}$ are assumed isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_p$.
We shall assume that the conductor of $\chi$ is prime to $p$, and study the $\chi$ eigenspace of the Selmer group using the results of § 15.

**Proposition 18.1.** 1) Assume $\chi \omega^k$ is odd, and $B_1(\chi \omega^{-k}) B_1(\chi^{-1} \omega^{-k}) \neq 0 \pmod{pW}$.
Then $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^\chi = 0$.

2. Assume $\chi \omega^k$ is even, and $E(\chi \omega^k) E(\chi^{-1} \omega^k) \neq 0 \pmod{U^p}$.
   Then $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^\chi \hookrightarrow (U / U^p)^{\chi \omega^k} \simeq W / p$.

_Proof._ Let $K = F(\mu_p)$ and let $V \subseteq K^* / K^{*p}$ be the subgroup of all $\alpha$ with $(\alpha) = \alpha^p$.
Then $V$ is finite and sits in the exact sequence

$$
\begin{array}{l}
0 \to U / U ^ {p} \to V \to D _ {p} \to 0 \\
\alpha \mapsto \operatorname {c l a s s} (\alpha)
\end{array}
$$

where $U$ and $D$ denote the unit group and class group of $K$.
By the hypotheses and (16.4): $D_p^{\chi \omega^k} = D_p^{\chi^{-1}\omega^k} = 0$.
Hence we find (15.4):

$$
\operatorname {S e l} _ {\pi} (F) ^ {\chi} = \left\{\alpha \in \left(U / U ^ {p}\right) ^ {\chi \omega^ {k}}: \alpha \equiv 1 \bmod \pi_ {v} ^ {\frac {2 (p - k ^ {\prime}) p}{p + 1}} \text{ all } v | p \right\}.
$$

This is clearly trivial if $\chi \omega^k$ is odd; if $\chi \omega^k$ is even, it has rank 1 as $k &gt; \frac{2(p - k')p}{p + 1}$.

When $\chi \omega^k$ is odd, the mirror principle on the class group shows that $D_p^{\chi \omega^{p - k}} = D_p^{\chi^{-1}\omega^{p - k}} = 0$ also.
Hence:

$$
\operatorname {S e l} _ {\phi} (F) ^ {\chi} = \left\{a \in \left(U / U ^ {p}\right) ^ {\chi \omega^ {p - k}}: \alpha \equiv 1 \bmod \pi_ {v} ^ {\frac {2 (p - k) p}{p + 1}} \text{ all } v | p \right\}
$$

by (15.4).
When $\chi \omega^k$ is even, the unit eigenspace is trivial and the class group $D_p^{\chi \omega^{p - k}}$ has rank at most 1, by the mirror principal.
But we claim the Selmer group of $\phi$ is trivial in both cases, using the inequality $p - k &lt; \frac{2(p - k)p}{p + 1}$.
For a non-trivial $\alpha$ would satisfy $\alpha \equiv 1 \bmod \pi_v^{p + 1}$ and thus yield an unramified extension $K(\sqrt[p]{\alpha})$ in the $\chi^{-1} \omega^k$ eigenspace.
This cannot exist by our assumptions on the class group and class field theory.

**Corollary 18.2.** Assume $\chi \omega^k$ is odd and $B_1(\chi \omega^{-k}) B_1(\chi^{-1} \omega^{-k}) \neq 0 \pmod{pW}$.
Then $A(F)^\chi = 0$ and $\text{III}(A / F)^\chi = 0$.

_Proof._ This combines the determination of $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^\chi$ with the exact sequence (17.2).

In the even case, we suspect that $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^\chi$ is non-trivial, with the points coming from $A(F)$ precisely when $\chi^2 = 1$.
Using Heegner points as in the proof of (17.4), we can show the following:

**Corollary 18.3.** Assume $\chi^2 = 1$, $\chi \omega^k$ is even, and $E(\chi \omega^k) \neq 0 \pmod{U^p}$.
Suppose there exists an imaginary quadratic character $\chi'$ satisfying:

$$
\chi^ {\prime} (p) = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad B _ {1} \left(\chi \chi^ {\prime} \omega^ {- k}\right) \neq 0 (\mathrm {m o d} p). \tag {18.4}
$$

Then $\operatorname{rank} A(F)^{\chi} = \dim A$ and $\operatorname{III}(A / F)^{\chi} = 0$.

The Eisenstein descent on
J_0(N)

The hypothesis (18.4) is certainly satisfied in any given case of $k$ and $p$ , but we do not see how to prove it in general.
As a further open question, we feel it would be interesting to compute the Cassels-Tate pairing on the canonical generators of $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^\chi$ and $\operatorname{Sel}_I(F)^{\chi^{-1}}$ , when these spaces are distinct and of rank 1.

Stevens for their helpful suggestions.
