# A Castelnuovo bound for smooth surfaces

Henry C.
Pinkham\*

Department of Mathematics, Columbia University, New York, NY 10027, USA

To my father, Francis H.
Pinkham

# I.

Introduction

Let $X$ be a smooth variety of dimension $n$ and degree $d$ in $\mathbb{P}^r$.
It is well known that for large enough $k$, hypersurfaces of degree $k$ cut out a complete linear system on $X$, in other words the homomorphism

$$
H^{0}(\mathbb{P}^{r},\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}}(k))\to H^{0}(X,\mathcal{O}_{X}(k))
$$

is surjective.
Some authors say that $X$ is $k$-normal if this holds.
It is of interest to find an explicit bound $k_{0} = k_{0}(n,d,r)$ such that for all smooth, nondegenerate, varieties of dimension $n$ and degree $d$ in $\mathbb{P}^r$ this is true for all $k\geq k_0$.
This was done for curves by Castelnuovo [Ca] in 1893: He showed that for smooth, nondegenerate curves in $\mathbb{P}^3$ the best possible $k_{0}$ is $d - 2$.
An easy projection argument shows that this bound also works for curves in $\mathbb{P}^r$, $r\geq 3$, but it need not be the best possible: in fact in 1983 Gruson, Lazarsfeld and Peskine [GLP] showed (again for curves, this time only required to be reduced and irreducible) that the best possible $k_{0}$ is $d + 1 - r$.
They also classified the extremal examples.
As they point out, their work suggests the following hope:

$$
k_{0}(n,d,r) = d + n - r. \tag{*}
$$

If true this is the best possible, at least for surfaces, as one sees using [GLP], by constructing scrolls whose general hyperplane section in $\mathbb{P}^{r - 1}$ has a $d + 3 - r$ secant line.

In 1984 Mumford [BM] showed that

$$
k_{0}(n,d,r) \leq (n + 1)(d - 2) + 1.
$$

His method of proof is closely related to Castelnuovo's.

Here we will prove the following result.

- Partially supported by N.S.F.
  grant MC5-8005802 at Columbia University, USA

H.C.
Pinkham

Theorem.
Let $X$ be a nondegenerate, smooth surface of degree $d$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ (resp.
$\mathbb{P}^4$).
Then for any $k \geq d - 2$ (resp.
$d - 1$), hypersurfaces of degree $k$ cut out a complete linear system on $X$.
If $X$ is also regular, that is, $H^{1}(X,\mathcal{O}_{X}) = 0$, and in $\mathbb{P}^4$, then the result holds for $k = d - 2$.

As in the curve case a projection argument shows that the same bound $d - 2$ holds for surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^r$, $r \geq 5$: just project the surface generically to $\mathbb{P}^5$ and use the fact that the image is smooth.
For $r &gt; 5$ this bound has no hope of being sharp; unfortunately even for $r = 4$ and $5\mathrm{I}$ do not know if the theorem is the best possible\*.
Note that we are still one unit above the bound $(\ast)$.
It is not clear what happens for regular surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^5$, although there is some evidence, presented in §V, Remark 3, that for rational scrolls in $\mathbb{P}^5$ the bound is $d - 3$.

The method of proof once again is Castelnuovo's, moved up one dimension.
Although the proof given below has only been worked out for surfaces at present, it is likely that it will generalize to threefolds and yield the bound $d - 2$ for 3-dimensional smooth nondegenerate varieties in $\mathbb{P}^7$.
The proof is so geometric and so classical in the 2-dimensional case that it seems worthwhile to give it separately.
As it uses the Kodaira vanishing theorem and the explicit description of the singularities arising from generic projection it is only valid in characteristic 0. For that reason we will assume the base field is $\mathbb{C}$.

## II.

Castelnuovo's original method

In this Sect.
I will explain the part of Castelnuovo's original proof that generalizes to all dimensions.

As before, let $X$ be a smooth, nondegenerate variety of dimension $n$ and degree $d$ in $\mathbb{P}^r$.
If $r = n + 1$ then $X$ is a hypersurface, so that hypersurfaces of any degree cut out complete linear systems on $X$.
Thus there is nothing to prove.
So assume $r \geq n + 2$.
Let $L$ be a generic linear space of dimension $r - n - 2$ in $\mathbb{P}^r$, and let $p$ denote the projection of $\mathbb{P}^r$ from $L$ to $\mathbb{P}^{n + 1}$.
A dimension count shows that $L$ does not intersect $X$ so that $p$ restricts to a morphism $X \to \mathbb{P}^{n + 1}$.
Let $Y$ denote the image of $X$; it is easy to see that the restriction $f \colon X \to Y$ is birational and finite.
We can now apply the theory of conductors and adjoints.

By the theory of conductors there are effective Weil divisors $C$ and $D$ on $X$ and $Y$ respectively such that $\mathcal{I}(D)$, the ideal sheaf of $D$ on $Y$ is the conductor of $f\colon X\to Y$ and $\mathcal{I}(C)$ is its pullback to $Y$.
Since $X$ is smooth $C$ is of course Cartier.
For information on conductors see [Za2], §12 or [Kl], V.A.
Locally the conductor is the largest ideal of $\mathcal{O}_Y$ that is also an ideal of $\mathcal{O}_X$, for example if $f\colon X\to Y$ is the resolution of an ordinary double point $P$ on a curve $Y$, then the conductor is just the maximal ideal sheaf of the point $P$.
Since this is precisely the situation that obtains in codimension 1 on $D$ (simply reduce to the case of curves, where the result is well-known) both $D$ and $C$ are reduced.

Here are the three facts from the classical theory of adjoints that Castelnuovo uses in dimension 1. Here $K_{X}$ stands for the canonical divisor of $X$ and $H$ for the hyperplane section.

- See the note added in proof at the end of the paper

A Castelnuovo bound for smooth surfaces

Facts.

1. $K_{X}\equiv (d - n - 2)H - C$

2) the hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^{n + 1}$ containing $D$ cut out on $X$ the complete linear system $|kH - C| = |K_X + (k - d + n + 2)H|$ , in other words:

a) $f_{*}\mathcal{O}(K_{X}) = \mathcal{I}(D)\mathcal{O}_{Y}((d - n - 2)H),$ call this $\mathcal{I}$ for short, so that $H^0 (X,K_X)$ $= H^{0}(Y,\mathcal{I})$
b) $H^0 (\mathbb{P}^{n + 1},\mathcal{I}(D)\mathcal{O}(k))$ surjects to $H^0 (Y,\mathcal{I}(k - d + n + 2))$ .
$\mathcal{I}(D) =$ ideal sheaf of $D$ in $\mathbb{P}^{n + 1}$ 3) For $k \geq d - n - 1$ and $k \geq d - 3$ if $n = 1$ the hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^{n + 1}$ cut out a complete linear system on $D$ , in other words the map $H^0(\mathbb{P}^{n + 1}, \mathcal{O}(k)) \to H^0(D, \mathcal{O}_D(kH))$ is surjective.

A modern reference for the first two facts, which were used classically both for curves and surfaces, is [Za1], Appendix to Chap.
III.
As far as I know the third fact was used, or perhaps known, only for curves, in the form: "the double points of a plane curve of degree $d$ impose independent conditions on hypersurfaces of degree $\geq d - 3$ ".
It is an immediate consequence of the Kodaira vanishing theorem.
Consider the exact sequence:

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {I} (D) \mathcal {O} _ {Y} (k H) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {Y} (k H) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {D} (k H) \rightarrow 0.
$$

By 2a) the left hand term is $f_{*}\mathcal{O}(K_{X} + (k - d + n + 2)H)$ .
Since $f$ is finite, $H^{i}(Y,f_{*}\mathcal{O}(K_{X} + (k - d + n + 2)H) = H^{i}(X,K_{X} + (k - d + n + 2)H)$ and, by the Kodaira vanishing theorem, for $i &gt; 0$ this is 0 as soon as $k\geq d - n - 1$ .
When $X$ and $Y$ are curves and $k = d - 3$ both $H^{1}(X,K_{X})$ and $H^{1}(Y,(d - 3)H)$ are one dimensional by duality, so the result still holds.
In higher dimensions however, $H^{1}(X,K_{X})$ is exactly the obstruction to surjectivity for $k = d - n - 2$ , since $H^{1}(Y,(d - n - 2)H)$ always vanishes.

Now take $k \geq d - n - 1$ and consider the exact sequence on $X$ :

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {X} (k H - C) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {X} (k H) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {C} (k H) \rightarrow 0.
$$

As above $H^{1}(X, kH - C)$ vanishes, so that we have an exact sequence on global sections.
Thus

Proposition.
For $k \geq d - n - 1$ , to show that hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^r$ cut out a complete linear system on $X$ it is necessary and sufficient to show that they cut out a complete linear system on $C$ .

Indeed, by fact $2H^{0}(X,kH - C)$ is cut out by hypersurfaces coming from $\mathbb{P}^{n + 1}$ .

The proposition reduces the dimension of the variety to be considered by 1, but unfortunately replaces the smooth variety $X$ by a variety $C$ which need not be, in general.

The last step in Castelnuovo's proof is to use the fact that the morphism $C \to D$ is generically a double cover, and that we already know that $H^0(D, kH)$ is cut out by hypersurfaces in $\mathbb{P}^{n+1}$ .
This step does not generalize readily to higher dimensions.
Here is how it goes in dimension 1.

$X$ is now a smooth curve, which we may take in $\mathbb{P}^3$ , and $Y$ its generic projection on $\mathbb{P}^2$ .
If $g$ is the genus of $X$ , then $Y$ has $\delta = (d - 1)(d - 2) / 2 - g$ ordinary double points $r_i$ .
The inverse image of each $r_i$ consists of two points $p_i$

H.C.
Pinkham

and $q_{i}$.
Thus $C$ is the divisor $\Sigma p_{i} + q_{i}$ on $X$, and $D$ is the (Weil) divisor $\Sigma r_{i}$.
By the proposition we must show that for $k \geq d - 2$ hypersurfaces of degree $k$ cut out the complete linear system $H^{0}(C, kH) = H^{0}(C, \mathcal{C}_{C})$.
Since $C$ is just a collection of reduced points, $H^{0}(C, \mathcal{C}_{C})$ is simply the vector space of complex valued functions on the points $p_{i}$ and $q_{i}$, so that it will be enough to find for each point in $C$, say $p_{1}$, a hypersurface vanishing at all the points of $C$ except $p_{1}$.
By fact 3 we know that we can find a hypersurface in $\mathbb{P}^2$ of degree $d - 3$ vanishing at all the points of $D$ except $r_{1}$.
Lift it to a hypersurface in $\mathbb{P}^3$ by coning from the center of projection.
Add to this hypersurface any hypersurface of degree $k - d + 3 &gt; 0$ vanishing at $q_{1}$ but not $p_{1}$.
This hypersurface vanishes at all the points of $C$ except $p_{1}$, so we are done.

The rest of this paper consists in generalizing this argument to one higher dimension.

## III.

Double covers and the case of no triple points

In this section we present some standard facts about double covers, and then make some remarks about the case where the projection $X \to Y$ has no triple points.
These remarks, which give some idea about what to expect in the higher dimensional case, will not be used in the rest of the paper.

As always $X$ denotes a smooth nondegenerate variety of dimension $n$ and degree $d$ in $\mathbb{P}^r$ and $Y$ its generic projection on $\mathbb{P}^{n+1}$, $C$ and $D$ the divisors on $X$ and $Y$ respectively corresponding to the conductor ideal of $f\colon X\to Y$.
We assume that $n$ is at least 2, since the case of curves has already been settled, and that $f\colon X\to Y$ has no triple points, so that both $C$ and $D$ are smooth, and the restriction $g$ of $f$ to $C$ is a double cover.
In practice this rarely, but occasionally happens (for example when $X$ lies on a quadric); however this case presents a key idea without the technical difficulties that arise when multiple points of higher order are present, which is why I present it here.
Even in this simple case we will only be able to conclude in the case of surfaces and threefolds.

Since $g \colon C \to D$ is a double cover of smooth varieties, there is a (not necessarily effective) divisor $L$ with $2L$ linearly equivalent to an effective divisor $B$ on $D$, called the branch divisor, such that $\mathcal{C}_C = \mathcal{C}_D \oplus \mathcal{C}_D(-L)$.
This is the decomposition into invariant and anti-invariant parts under the involution of sheet interchange.
The multiplication of $\mathcal{C}_D(-L)$ with itself is sent to $\mathcal{C}_D$ with the help of the divisor $B$.
In local coordinates the construction can be explained as follows: let $\{U_i\}$ be an affine open cover of $D$, and let $\{f_i\}$ be a set of local equations for the divisor $B$.
Consider the geometric line bundle $F \to D$ associated to the divisor $L$, and let $z_i$ be the fiber coordinate of $F$ on the open $U_i$.
Then $C$ is the subvariety of $F$ given locally by $z_i^2 - f_i = 0$; this patches since $z_i^2$ and $f_i$ have the same transition functions by construction.
The involution is given by $z_i \to -z_i$.
Letting $z$ stand for $\{z_i\}$ the global decomposition can be written $\mathcal{C}_C = \mathcal{C}_D \oplus z\mathcal{C}_D(-L)$.
For more details see [Pe].

The symbol $H$ will be used for the hyperplane section both of $C$ and of $D$.
Therefore we have $\mathcal{C}_C(H) = \mathcal{C}_D(H) \oplus \mathcal{C}_D(H - L)$.
For any $k$ let $V_k$ be the sub-

A Castelnuovo bound for smooth surfaces

system of $H^0 (C,kH)$ cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$.
Under the involution of sheet interchange $V_{k}$ decomposes as $V_{k}^{+}\oplus V_{k}^{-}$, where $V_{k}^{+}\subset H^{0}(D,kH)$ and $V_{k}^{-}\subset H^{0}(D,kH - L)$.
By fact 3) of the previous section the first inclusion is an equality for $k\geq d - n - 1$; it remains to determine when the second inclusion is an equality.
The key point is the following obvious

**Proposition.** $V_{1}^{-}$ has no base points on $D$.

Indeed if $r$ is a base point of $V_{1}^{-}$ on $D$ then the two points above $r$ are mapped to the same point by $V_{1}$ if $r$ is not in the branch locus, and the unique point above $r$ is mapped to a singular point if not: just examine the local representation of the double cover given above.
But that is absurd since $V_{1}$ is very ample.

We are now in a position to apply the generalized lemma of Castelnuovo ([Mu], Theorem 2, p.
41): $V_{k}^{-}$ is generated by $H^{0}(D,(k - 1)H)\oplus V_{1}^{-}$ if

$$
H ^ {i} (D, (k - i - 1) H + i L) = 0 \quad \text{for} \ i &gt; 0.
$$

(Mumford assumes in the statement of the lemma that $V_{1}^{-}$ is ample, but does not use this in the proof.
We are also using Mumford's "useful remark" p.
45, since $V_{1}^{-}$ is not necessarily the complete linear system.)

We want to apply the Kodaira vanishing theorem; to do so we must compute the canonical divisor of $D$.
We have $K_{C} = g*(K_{D} + L)$, and by adjunction from $X$, $K_{C} = (d - n - 2)H$.
Thus $K_{D} = (d - n - 2)H - L$.
(Recall that $H$ denotes the hyperplane sections of both $C$ and $D$, so that $g*H = H$.) Written in terms of $K_{D}$ the cohomology groups that must vanish are

$$
H ^ {i} (D, K _ {D} + (k - i - d + n + 1) H + (i + 1) L) \quad \text{for} \ i &gt; 0. \tag {**}
$$

$D$ has dimension $n - 1$, and for $i = n - 1$ and $k\geq d - 2$ this cohomology group always vanishes.
Indeed, $D$ is irreducible by fact 4 of the next section, since the Veronese surface cannot be the plane section of a smooth variety, so just use Serre duality plus the fact that $2L$ is linearly equivalent to an effective, nontrivial ([FL], 5.2) divisor.
Now assume that $(i + 1)L$ is base point free for $i &gt; 0$.
Then for $k\geq d - 2$ the cohomology groups for $i &lt; n - 1$ vanish by the Kodaira vanishing theorem.

Thus with the very restrictive hypothesis given at the beginning of this section, and the unproven assertion given in the previous paragraph, we see that by the Castelnuovo method we may hope to prove that $H^0 (X,kH)$ is cut out by hypersurfaces for $k\geq d - 2$.
Furthermore, to improve this estimate a lower bound on the "size" of $L$ would be needed, but in fact examples suggest that such a lower bound does not exist (see §5, Remark 3).

For surfaces we have already seen how the desired cohomology group vanishes for $k \geq d - 2$.
Finally when $X$ is a threefold one can again make these cohomology groups (\*\*) vanish for $k \geq d - 2$.
We have already disposed of the case $i = 2$.
For $i = 1$ and $k \geq d - 2$ use the exact sequence of adjunction:

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {D} (K _ {D} + m H) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {D} (K _ {D} + m H + B) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {B} (K _ {B} + m H) \rightarrow 0
$$

H.C.
Pinkham

for $m = k - d + 3$.
By [PR], 2.4, $B$ is a smooth curve.
By the Kodaira vanishing theorem, $H^1$ of the sheaves on the left and on the right vanish for $m &gt; 0$, so $H^1$ of the one in the middle does too, which is what we needed to prove.

## IV.

Generic projection for surfaces

We recall without proof the standard facts concerning generic projections of surfaces.
Proofs are given in [Mo], Appendix I, pp.
72-115 and in [GH], pp.
611-618.

1. Any smooth surface in $\mathbb{P}^r$, $r &gt; 5$, can be linearly projected to a smooth surface in $\mathbb{P}^5$.
2. The projection of a smooth surface in $\mathbb{P}^5$ from a generic point to $\mathbb{P}^4$ has as only singularities a finite number $n$ of "improper nodes": two branches of the surface meet at a point, with their tangent planes also just intersecting in that point.
3. Let $f \colon X \to Y$ be a generic projection of a smooth surface $X$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$.
   Then the singular locus of $Y$ is a curve $D$ with only singularities a number $t$ of ordinary triple points with transverse tangent directions.
   The inverse image of $D$ on $X$ is a curve $C$ with only singularities $3t$ nodes (i.e., ordinary double points), 3 nodes above each triple point of $D$.
   The restriction $g \colon C \to D$ lifts to a double cover $g^{\sim} \colon C^{\sim} \to D^{\sim}$ of the normalizations of $C$ and $D$ which is branched at $2b$ points which are smooth points of $D$.
   The curve $D$ is a curve of ordinary double points for $Y$, except a) at the triple points which are ordinary triple points for $Y$, and b) at the branch points of $g \colon C \to D$, which are pinch points (local equation $x^{2} - yz^{2} = 0$) for $Y$.
   $C$ is called the chord curve, and $D$ of course the double curve.
   With their reduced structure they are the divisors associated to the conductor ideal of $f \colon X \to Y$ described in §II.
4. Except in the case of generic projections of the Veronese surface, the curves $C$ and $D$ are irreducible.
5. The morphism $g \colon C \to D$ is always ramified, in other words $b &gt; 0$.

Remarks.
The first three facts are proved both in [GH] and in [Mo], the last two only in [Mo]: see Theorem 3, parts 3 and 4, p.
60. Surprisingly enough, fact 4 was only noticed, by Franchetta [Fr], in 1941. Franchetta's proof is reproduced in Enriques' book on surfaces [En] in the first chapter, pp.
8-13.
The proof in [Mo], due to Mumford, deduces the result as a simple corollary, using Bertini's theorem, of the fact that the Veronese surface is the only surface in $\mathbb{P}^5$ whose secant variety is 4 dimensional.
This was proved by Severi [Se] in 1901! In the same paper Severi also shows that the only smooth surface in $\mathbb{P}^4$ that is not linearly normal is the projection of the Veronese.
Zak [Z] and Lazarsfeld [LV] have recently generalized this result.
Fact 5 can also be deduced from the Fulton-Hansen connectedness theorem: [FL] example after Corollary 5.2, p.
46.

We will need some more precise information concerning the triple points of $D$.
Let $u$ be such a triple point, and $s_1, s_2, s_3$ the three nodes of $C$ above $u$.
Let

A Castelnuovo bound for smooth surfaces

$r_1, r_2, r_3$ be the three points above $u$ on the normalization $D^{\sim}$ of $D$ , and $p_i$ and $q_i$ the two points above $r_i$ on the normalization $C^{\sim}$ of $C$ .

6. The points $s_1, s_2, s_3$ span a plane in $\mathbb{P}^5$ .
7. Indices can be chosen so that the points on $C^{\sim}$ above $s_i$ are $p_i$ and $q_{i+1}$ (the indices are taken mod 3).

Fact 6 follows for example from the argument in [GH] given at the bottom of p.
612 (" $J$ is at most three-dimensional") and fact 7 is a well known classical fact (see [SR] IX, 2.12, for example) which can be established either by examining directly the normalization of $Y$ or, more pedantically, by blowing up $D$ in $\mathbb{P}^3$ and using the fact that this blow up is known to desingularize $Y$ : [Wi] Theorem 2.7. The blow up of a triple point is described, for example, in [Pi], Sect.
4, Example 4.

Remark.
A similar description of the singular loci for the generic projection of a smooth threefold can be given: see [PR], 2.4.

# V.

The end of the proof

We can now finish the proof using the results of Sect.
II and Sect.
IV.
We mainly treat the case of a surface $X$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ , relegating the case of $\mathbb{P}^4$ to a few remarks at the end of the section.
By fact 4 of Sect.
IV we may assume that the curves $C$ and $D$ are irreducible, since the Veronese surface is projectively normal and therefore satisfies the theorem.
First we prove a proposition generalizing that of Sect.
III.

We use the notation of Sect.
IV, fact 3. Since $g^{\sim} \colon C^{\sim} \to D^{\sim}$ is a double cover, there is a divisor $L$ on $D^{\sim}$ such that $\mathcal{C}_{C^{\sim}} = \mathcal{C}_{D^{\sim}} \oplus \mathcal{C}_{D^{\sim}}(-L)$ .
Since $2L$ is linearly equivalent to the branch divisor $B$ of $g^{\sim}$ , which has degree $2b$ , we see that $L$ has degree $b$ , which by fact 5 of Sect.
IV is positive.
As in Sect.
III we use $z = \{z_i\}$ for the fiber coordinate of the geometric line bundle $F$ over $D^{\sim}$ associated to the divisor $L$ .
We use $H$ to denote the hyperplane section of either $C$ or $D$ , or its pullback to either $C^{\sim}$ or $D^{\sim}$ .
We have a diagram:

![img-57.jpeg](img-57.jpeg)

where the morphisms of $C^{\sim}$ and $D^{\sim}$ to projective space are given by sublinear systems of $H^{0}(C^{\sim},H)$ and $H^{0}(D^{\sim},H)$ respectively.
Choose homogeneous coordinates $x_0,\ldots ,x_5$ on $\mathbb{P}^5$ so that the last four $x_{i}$ are the coordinates of the $\mathbb{P}^3$ to which we are projecting.
Viewing the $x_{i}$ as global sections of $H^{0}(C^{\sim},H)$ $= H^{0}(D^{\sim},H)\oplus zH^{0}(D^{\sim},H - L)$ , we can write:

$$
x _ {i} = g _ {i} + z f _ {i}, \quad g _ {i} \in H ^ {0} (D ^ {\sim}, H), f _ {i} \in H ^ {0} (D ^ {\sim}, H - L). \tag {1}
$$

By construction $f_{i} = 0$ for $2 \leq i \leq 5$ , so we get a well defined pencil $V^{-} = \{f_{0}, f_{1}\}$ in $H^{0}(D^{\sim}, H - L)$ .
Note also that for $2 \leq i \leq 5$ , $g_{i} \in H^{0}(D, H)$ .

H.C.
Pinkham

# Proposition 1. The pencil $V^{-}$ is base point free.

Proof.
Suppose $V^{-}$ has a base point $r \in D^{\sim}$.
If $r$ is not a branch point of $g^{\sim}$, this means that the 2 points $p$ and $q$ above $r$ on $C^{\sim}$ are mapped to the same point in $\mathbb{P}^5$.
But we know that this is not the case, even when $r$ maps to a triple point of $D$, by fact 7 of Sect.
IV.
If $r$ is a ramification point of $g^{\sim}$, this would mean that the image of $r$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ is a cuspidal singularity of $C$.
This is impossible by fact 3 of Sect.
IV.

Remark 1. Instead of projecting all the way to $\mathbb{P}^3$, just project to the $\mathbb{P}^4$ with coordinates, say, $x_1, \ldots, x_5$.
Then by the same argument the improper nodes of $X$ (see fact 2, §IV) occur precisely at the zeroes of $f_0$.
Thus there are degree $(H - L)$ of them.
This is a well-known classical formula for the number of improper nodes: see [SR], p.
197 and [Kl], Eq.
(V, 35).
Thus if we have a smooth surface in $\mathbb{P}^4$, then $H \equiv L$.
Using the Cayley-Zeuthen formulas ([SR], IX.3, or a modern presentation [P], Sect.
3) one obtains the formula in [H], Appendix A, Example 4.1.3, satisfied for all smooth surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^4$.

Next we need to study the sublinear system $V^{+}$ of $H^0 (D^{\sim},H)$ given by $\{g_i\}$, $0\leq i\leq 5$.
Recall the standard exact sequence:

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {D} \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {D ^ {\sim}} \rightarrow \bigoplus (\mathbb {C} ^ {2}) _ {u} \rightarrow 0 \tag {2}
$$

where the direct sum on the right is over all the triple points $u$ of $D$.
The right hand map, on the summand corresponding to $u$, is:

$$
\pi_ {u} \colon g \rightarrow \left(g \left(r _ {1}\right) - g \left(r _ {2}\right), g \left(r _ {2}\right) - g \left(r _ {3}\right)\right) \tag {3}
$$

where, as before $r_1, r_2, r_3$ are the points on $D^{\sim}$ above $u$.

Tensoring the exact sequence (2) by $\mathcal{O}_D(kH)$, and taking the long exact cohomology sequence, one gets a map, which I will also call $\pi_u$, from $H^0(D^\sim, kH)$ to $\mathbb{C}^2$.

Proposition 2. The linear system $V^{+} \subset H^{0}(D^{\sim}, H)$ is mapped surjectively by $\pi_{u}$ onto $\mathbb{C}^{2}$.
In fact, since $g_{2}, \ldots, g_{5}$ map to 0, $\pi_{u}$ induces an isomorphism from $\{g_{0}, g_{1}\}$ to $\mathbb{C}^{2}$.

Proof.
This will follow from fact 6 of the preceding section: the $s_i$ span a plane.
First we prove a lemma.

Lemma 1. Let $x = g + zf$ be a section of $H^0 (C,kH)$, with $g\in H^0 (D^\sim ,kH)$ and $f\in H^{0}(D^{\sim},kH - L)$.
For any triple point $u$ of $D$, if $\pi_u(g) = 0$ then $f$ vanishes at all three points $r_i$ on $D^{\sim}$ above $u$.

By adding to $x$ a suitable linear combination of the $x_{j}$, $2 \leq j \leq 5$, we may assume that $g(r_{i}) = 0$, $i = 1,2,3$.
Using the notation of §IV, fact 7, we get by hypothesis:

$$
x \left(s _ {i}\right) = z \left(p _ {i}\right) f \left(r _ {i}\right) = z \left(q _ {i + 1}\right) f \left(r _ {i + 1}\right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3.
$$

Now use the fact that $z(p_i) \neq 0$ and $z(p_i) = -z(q_i)$.
From the equalities above one gets $x(s_1) = -x(s_2) = +x(s_3) = -x(s_1)$, etc., which forces $x(s_i) = 0$ but this means $f(r_i) = 0$, $i = 1, 2, 3$, thus proving the lemma.

A Castelnuovo bound for smooth surfaces

The proposition now follows readily.
If $\pi_u\colon \{g_0,g_1\} \to \mathbb{C}^2$ is not surjective, there is an element, which after a change of coordinates we may take to be $g_{1}$, that maps to 0. We may then apply the lemma, which implies that $x_{1}$ takes the same value at $s_1,s_2$ and $s_3$.
Now the same is true for $x_{2},\ldots ,x_{5}$, since the $s_i$ all map to $u$.
Thus the $s_i$ lie on a line, which contradicts fact 6 of Sect.
IV.
So $\pi_u$ is in fact surjective.

Next we must compute the canonical bundle of $C^{\sim}$ and $D^{\sim}$.
Let $T$ denote the inverse image on $C^{\sim}$ of the double points of $C$, and also (following the same convention as for $H$) the inverse image on $D^{\sim}$ of the double points of $D$.
Thus $g^{\sim *}(T) = T$.

**Proposition 3.** $K_{C^{\sim}} = (d - 4)H - T$ and $K_{D^{\sim}} = (d - 4)H - T - L$.

**Proof.** This is standard.
The dualizing sheaf $\omega_{C}$ of $C$ is obtained by adjunction from $K_{X} = (d - 4)H - C$, and is $\omega_{C} = (d - 4)H$.
Since $C^{\sim}$ is the normalization of $C$, and $C$ has only nodes, the first equality follows the well known local computation of differentials on a nodal curve.
The second equality follows from the Riemann-Hurwitz relation applied to the double cover $g^{\sim} \colon C^{\sim} \to D^{\sim} \colon K_{C^{\sim}} = g^{\sim *}(K_{D^{\sim}} + L)$.

**Remark 2.** Similarly one can compute the dualizing sheaf of $D$ ([Ro], Theorem 3.1): $\omega_{D} = (f_{*}\mathcal{O}_{X} / \mathcal{O}_{Y})((d - 4)H) = (g_{*}\mathcal{O}_{C} / \mathcal{O}_{D})((d - 4)H)$.
This isomorphism, which in fact has a natural generalization in all dimensions (for which see [Ro]), can be used to give a more intrinsic description of $\mathcal{O}_C$

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {D} \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {C} \rightarrow \omega_ {D} (4 - d) \rightarrow 0 \tag {4}
$$

(as usual we suppress $g_*$ when there can be no confusion).
See Remark 5 at the end of this section for a proof of the main theorem using this description.

We can now proceed with the proof.
Recall that by the proposition of §II we have reduced the theorem of the introduction to showing that $H^0(C, kH)$ is cut out by hypersurfaces for $k \geq d - 2$.
First note the trivial

**Lemma 2.** For $k \geq d - 3$, $H^0(C, kH)$ has codimension 3t in $H^0(C^{\sim}, kH)$, where $t$ is the number of triple points of $D$.

Indeed we have an exact sequence

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {C} (k H) \rightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {C ^ {\sim}} (k H) \rightarrow \bigoplus \mathbb {C} \rightarrow 0 \tag {5}
$$

where the direct sum on the right is taken over all the nodes of $C$.
By Proposition 3, when $k \geq d - 3$, $H^{1}(C,kH) = 0$, yielding the desired conclusion.

Our strategy will therefore be to exhibit, for every $k \geq d - 2$, a subspace of codimension $3t$ in $H^0(C^\sim, kH)$ cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$.
This will be established in two steps.
First some notation.
Recall that $H^0(C^\sim, kH) = H^0(D^\sim, kH) \oplus zH^0(D^\sim, kH - L)$.
Let $p^+$ and $p^-$ denote the projections onto the first and second terms of the direct sum, respectively.
Then:

**Proposition 4.** For $k \geq d - 2$, the anti-invariant subspace $zH^0(D^\sim, kH - T - L) \subset H^0(C, kH)$ is cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$, and has codimension 3t in $H^0(D^\sim, kH - L)$.

H.C.
Pinkham

Proposition 5. For $k \geq d - 2$, there is a subspace $V_{k}^{+}$ of $H^0 (C,kH)$, cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$, that projects onto $H^0 (D^{\sim},kH)$ via $p^{+}$.

Since the subspaces constructed in the two propositions are independent, we see there exists a subspace of $H^0 (C,kH)$, cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$, that has codimension $3t$ in $H^0 (C^\sim ,kH)$.
By the lemma it must be all of $H^0 (C,kH)$.
This finishes the proof for surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^5$.

It remains to prove the propositions.

## Proof of Proposition 4

Step 1. By fact 3 of §II, $H^0 (D^\sim ,kH - T)\subset H^0 (D,kH)$ is cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^3$ for $k\geq d - 3$.

Step 2. By Proposition 1, $V^{-} \subset H^{0}(D^{\sim}, H - L)$ is a base point free pencil.
Applying Castelnuovo's lemma (sometimes called the base point free pencil trick: see [Mu], Theorem 2, p.
41) we see that $H^{0}(D^{\sim}, (k - 1)H - T) \otimes V^{-}$ surjects onto $H(D^{\sim}, kH - T - L)$ if $H^{1}(D^{\sim}, (k - 2)H - T + L) = 0$.
Since $K_{D^{-}} = (d - 4)H - T - L$ by Proposition 3, and degree $(L) &gt; 0$ by §IV, fact 5, this is the case when $k \geq d - 2$.

Step 3. By the first step $H^0 (D^\sim ,(k - 1)H - T)\otimes \{x_0,x_1\}$ is cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$ for $k\geq d - 2$.
Now $H^0 (D^\sim ,(k - 1)H - T)\otimes \{g_0,g_1\} \subset H^0 (D^\sim ,kH - T)$ is clearly contained in $H^0 (D,kH)$, which itself is cut out by hypersurfaces when $k\geq d - 2$ by §II, fact 3. Thus $H^0 (D^\sim ,(k - 1)H - T)\otimes V^{-}$ is cut out by hypersurfaces for $k\geq d - 2$, and by step 2 this is all of $H^0 (D^\sim ,kH - T - L)$.
This proves the first assertion of the proposition.
The second assertion follows from the fact that $T$ has degree $3t$, and $\mathcal{C}_{D^*}(kH - T - L)$ is nonspecial for $k\geq d - 3$ by Proposition 3.

## Proof of Proposition 5

Step 1. Let $u$ be any triple point of $D$.
Then for $k \geq d - 4$ there is a section of $H^0(D, kH)$ vanishing at all the triple points of $D$ except $u$.
Indeed, we obviously have $H^0(D^\sim, kH - T) \subset H^0(D, kH) \subset H^0(D^\sim, kH)$, since $H^0(D^\sim, kH - T)$ consists of the sections of $H^0(D^\sim, kH)$ vanishing at the inverse images of the triple points, while $H^0(D, kH)$ those agreeing at the inverse images of each triple point.
As we have already noted in the proof of Proposition 4, $H^0(D^\sim, kH - T)$ has codimension $3t$ in $H^0(D^\sim, kH)$ for $k \geq d - 3$, while $H^0(D, kH)$ has codimension at most (in fact exactly) $2t$ by the exact sequence (2).
Thus "the triple points of $D$ impose independent conditions on hypersurfaces of degree $k$ for $k \geq d - 3$".

Step 2. We must show that for any $k \geq d - 2$ and for any $g \in H^0(D^\sim, kH)$ there is an $h \in H^0(C, kH)$ such that $p^+(h) = g$.
If $g \in H^0(D, kH)$ this follows from §II, fact 3.

Step 3. By the exact sequence (2) tensored by $\mathcal{C}(kH)$, it is enough to prove the result for $g$ such that $\pi_u(g) = 0$ for all except one of the triple points.
Take for $h$

A Castelnuovo bound for smooth surfaces

the section of $H^0(D, (k-1)H)$ constructed in step 1 multiplied by an element of $H^0(C, H)$ which projects to the appropriate element of $V^+$, which exists by Proposition 2.

The proof of the propositions, and therefore that of the main theorem for $X$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$, is now complete.

Remark 3. If the surface $X$ is regular, then the estimate in §II, fact 3 can be improved to $k \geq d - 4$.
An inspection of the proof above shows that if one knows that $H^0(D^-, H - 2L) = 0$ (this is needed in step 2 of the proof of Proposition 4) then the hypersurfaces of degree $k$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ cut out a complete linear system on $X$ for $k \geq d - 3$.
A necessary condition for this to hold is that $\deg(H - 2L) \leq g - 1$, where $g$ is the genus of $D^-$.
For rational scrolls one in fact has $\deg(H - 2L) = g - 1$, as one sees from the Cayley-Zeuthen formulas for scrolls: [SR], IX.4 and [Kl], III.B, so that one would expect them to be $d - 3$ normal.
However there are numerous examples of regular surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^5$ where $H^0(D^-, H - 2L) \neq 0$, for which the method fails.

A few words must now be said about smooth surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^4$.
The proof is almost identical to the proof for surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^5$: the only places that changes have to be made are Proposition 2 and step 3 of Proposition 5. The pencil $\{g_0, g_1\}$ is replaced by a single element $g$, and Proposition 2 is replaced by the assertion that neither coordinate of $\pi_u(g) \in \mathbb{C}^2$ is equal to 0. Then in step 3 of Proposition 5 one must multiply $h$ by a hyperplane in $\mathbb{P}^4$ containing one of the $s_i$ (which in this case of course are aligned).
On the other hand combining Remarks 1 and 2 one gets:

Corollary.
Let $X$ be a smooth, nondegenerate surface of degree $d$ in $\mathbb{P}^4$.
Assume $X$ is regular, that is $H^1(X, \mathcal{C}_X) = 0$.
Then the hypersurfaces of degree $d - 2$ cut out a complete linear system on $X$.

Remark 4. As Lazarsfeld has pointed out to me, Rao (see [R], 1.10) has constructed, for any finite graded module $M$ over $\mathbb{C}[X_0,\ldots,X_4]$, a smooth surface $X$ in $\mathbb{P}^4$ such that $M$ is up to grading $\oplus H^1(\mathbb{P}^4, \mathcal{I}_{X/\mathbb{P}}(n))$.
Thus there are many smooth surfaces in $\mathbb{P}^4$ that are not projectively normal.

Remark 5. We can use Remark 2 to phrase the proof of the main theorem more intrinsically, perhaps.
The changes are mainly cosmetic.
Using exact sequence (4) plus fact 3 of §II, we see that the point is to show that $H^0(D, \omega_D(k - d + 4))$ is cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$.
Now we have an exact sequence

$$
0 \rightarrow \omega_{D^{-}} \rightarrow \omega_{D} \rightarrow \oplus (\mathbb{C}^2)_u \rightarrow 0 \tag{6}
$$

which is essentially the dual of exact sequence (2).
Tensor this exact sequence by $(k - d + 4)H$ and take the associated cohomology sequence.
Proposition 4 says that $H^0(D^-, \omega_{D^-}(k - d + 4))$ is cut out by hypersurfaces of degree $k$, so we are now reduced to showing that the cokernel $\oplus (\mathbb{C}^2)_u$ is.
It is awkward to do this directly because $\omega_D$ is not locally free, so one proves instead a dual statement: this is Proposition 5.

Acknowledgements.

Moishezon and N.
Shepherd-Barron for bibliographic help, R.
Lazarsfeld for Remark 4 of §5, and U.
Persson for an informative late night discussion.

# Characterization of Jacobian varieties in terms of soliton equations\*

Takahiro Shiota

Department of Mathematics, Brandeis University, Waltham, MA 02254, USA

## Table of contents

3. The Novikov conjecture 356

## Introduction and the result

For $\Omega$ in the Siegel upper half space $\mathfrak{H}_g$, let $X = \mathbb{C}^g / (\mathbb{Z}^g + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^g)$ be the corresponding principally polarized abelian variety, and let

$$
\mathfrak{A}(z) = \mathfrak{A}(z, \Omega) = \sum_{m \in \mathbb{Z}^g} \exp(2\pi i^t m z + \pi i^t m \Omega m)
$$

be Riemann's theta function for $X$.
Our main result is the following

**Theorem.** The following two conditions (A) and (B) for $X$ are equivalent:

(A) i) There exist vectors $a_1, a_2, a_3 \in \mathbb{C}^g$, $a_1 \neq 0$, and a quadratic form $Q(t) = \sum_{i,j=1}^{3} Q_{ij} t_i t_j$, $Q_{ij} \in \mathbb{C}$, such that for any $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$

$$
\tau(t) = \tau(t, \zeta) = \exp(Q(t)) \, \mathfrak{A}(t_1 a_1 + t_2 a_2 + t_3 a_3 + \zeta) \tag{1}
$$

- Research supported by the Educational project for Japanese mathematical scientists

T.
Shiota

gives a solution to the Kadomtsev-Petviashvili (KP) equation in Hirota's form (see the Remark below for the notation):

$$
\left(D _ {1} ^ {4} + 3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \tau \cdot \tau = 0; \tag {2}
$$

ii) The theta divisor $\Theta$ of $X$ is irreducible.

(B) $X$ is isomorphic to the Jacobian variety of a complete smooth curve of genus $\mathfrak{g}$ over $\mathbb{C}$ .

That (B) implies (A) is a well-known result of Krichever [15].
The converse, that (A) implies (B), was conjectured by Novikov as an answer to the Schottky problem: characterization of the Jacobian locus in the moduli space of the principally polarized abelian varieties.
We refer the reader to [21] for the Schottky problem and [10] for its recent development.
In [10] a previous version of the present work is mentioned.
Partial answers to Novikov's conjecture have been obtained by various authors, e.g., [1, 6, 19, 28].
The arguments of [1] and [28] are based on Gunning's work [12], in which the Jacobians are characterized by Fay's trisecant formula [7, (45)], [22, Chap. 3b] for the theta function of a Jacobian.
As is well known, Fay's formula yields the KP equation as a certain degenerate limit [7, Cor. 2.13].

Mulase [19], inspired by Lax's observation on finite dimensional solutions to the KdV hierarchy [16, Theorem 6.13], developed a new approach to the problem based on the Burchnall-Chaundy-Krichever theory [2, 15, 20].
The fruitfulness of this approach was then pointed out by the author [27] who showed that one can easily refine the result of [19] to obtain a comparatively small number of Hirota's bilinear equations which characterize Jacobian varieties.

In this paper, we proceed further in this direction and prove Novikov's conjecture by analysis of soliton equations.
We first introduce the KP hierarchy.
This is an infinite set of differential equations which contains the KP equation (2), and which is defined by a set of Lax equations for a pseudodifferential-operator-valued function $L = L(t)$ , or by a set of Hirota's bilinear equations for a scalar-valued function $\tau = \tau(t)$ , called the $\tau$ -function.
These two formulations are related to each other by introducing the wave function.
Then we prove the theorem in the following slightly stronger form:

Theorem'.
The following conditions (A\*) and (C) are both equivalent to the above condition (B):

(A\*) i) Same as (A i) above;
ii) For the theta divisor $\Theta$ of $X$ and the vector $a_1$ as in (A i), the set $\Sigma = \bigcap_{c \in \mathbb{C}} (\Theta + c a_1)$ has codimension $\geq 2$ in $X$ .
(C) There exist a $\mathbf{g} \times \infty$ -matrix $\bar{A} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots)$ , $a_j \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathbf{g}}$ , of rank $\mathbf{g}$ and a quadratic form $Q(t) = \sum_{i,j=1}^{\infty} Q_{ij} t_i t_j$ , $Q_{ij} = Q_{ji} \in \mathbb{C}$ , such that for any $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathbf{g}}$ ,

$$
\bar {\tau} (t) = \bar {\tau} (t, \zeta) = \exp (Q (t)) \vartheta (\bar {A} t + \zeta) \tag {3}
$$

is a $\tau$ -function for the KP hierarchy.

Jacobian varieties

Moreover, for a solution $\tau$ as in (A\*), the $\tau$-function $\overline{\tau}$ in (C) can be taken to be an extension of $\tau$, i.e., $\tau(t_1, t_2, t_3; \zeta) = \overline{\tau}(t_1, t_2, t_3, 0, 0, \ldots; \zeta)$.

Note that $(\mathrm{A}^{*}\mathrm{ii})$ is weaker than (A ii).
By this theorem and [26, Prop. 8.6] or [8, Thm. 1.2], the locus $\Sigma$ is empty if $(\mathrm{A}^{*})$ is satisfied.
Hence assuming (A i), $\Sigma \neq \emptyset$ implies $\operatorname{codim}_X\Sigma = 1$, which happens if and only if $X$ is the product $X_{1} \times X_{2}$ as a polarized abelian variety, so that

$$
\partial (z) = \partial_ {1} \left(z _ {1}\right) \partial_ {2} \left(z _ {2}\right), \quad z = \left(z _ {1}, z _ {2}\right),
$$

and such that we have

$$
\Sigma = X _ {1} \times \Theta_ {2} \cup \Xi_ {1} \times X _ {2}
$$

for some $\Xi_1 \subset X_1$ of codimension $\geq 2$.
In this case, Lemma 7(a) in Sect.
3 applies to show that the vectors $a_j$ in (A i) are written in the form

$$
a _ {1} = \left(b _ {1}, 0\right), \quad a _ {2} = \left(b _ {2}, 0\right), \quad a _ {3} = \left(b _ {3}, v\right).
$$

Thus $X_{1}$ satisfies the condition (A\*) and $X_{2}$ is arbitrary, since in general a function $\tau$ solves the KP equation (2) if and only if $f(t_{3})\tau$ does for some (hence any) $f \neq 0$.
Then by our theorem $X_{1}$ is a Jacobian and $\Xi_{1}$ is empty.

The proof of the theorem is divided into two parts.
After a brief introduction to the KP hierarchy in Sect.
1, we prove in Sect.
2 the equivalence of (B) and (C) using the ideas of [19] and [27].
That (B) implies (C) is still due to Krichever [15], on which the whole argument of Sect.
2 is based.
That (C) implies (B) follows from Gunning's work [12] (see Appendix 1), but our approach is completely different and gives a new insight on the problem.
We then prove in Sect.
3 the equivalence of $(\mathrm{A}^{*})$ and (C) by extending the solution (1) to the KP equation to a solution $(\tau$-function) of the KP hierarchy of the form (3).
The method is summarized at the beginning of Sect.
3. Our argument is mostly the same as the author's Harvard thesis, in which the Novikov conjecture was proved assuming $\Sigma = \emptyset$.
To deal with the possible presence of $\Sigma$, we add Sect.
3.-1 to observe some invariance properties of $\Sigma$ by using the KP equation, so that by making some changes in Sect.
3.1, we can handle the locus $\Sigma$ geometrically.

Our argument shows that the Burchnall-Chaundy-Krichever theory and the general theory of the KP hierarchy give a very natural framework to study the Schottky problem.
This insight is an important contribution of Mulase's work, although at the time [19] and [27] were written Mulase and the author did not know the full power of this approach.

Remark.
Hirota's bilinear equations.
For a polynomial $P(Y_{1},\ldots ,Y_{n}) \in \mathbb{C}[Y_{1},\ldots ,Y_{n}]$, we define $P(D) = P(D_{1},\dots,D_{n})\colon (f,g)\mapsto P(D)f\cdot g$ by

$$
P (D) f \cdot g = P \left(\partial / \partial y _ {1}, \dots , \partial / \partial y _ {n}\right) \left(f (t + y) g (t - y)\right) | _ {y = 0},
$$

where $t = (t_1, \dots, t_n)$ and $f$ and $g$ are functions.
We call $P(D)$ Hirota's bilinear differential operator, and $P(D)f \cdot g = 0$, $P(D)f \cdot g = h$ etc., Hirota's bilinear differential equations.
See [14] for basic properties, useful formulas and applications of Hirota's operators.
We have

T.
Shiota

$$
\left(\frac {\partial}{\partial t _ {1}}\right) ^ {2} \frac {\left(D _ {1} ^ {4} + 3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \tau \cdot \tau}{2 \tau^ {2}} = \left(u _ {1 1 1} + 1 2 u u _ {1} - 4 u _ {3}\right) _ {1} + 3 u _ {2 2},
$$

where we set $u = (\partial/\partial t_1)^2 \log \tau$ and denote the partial differentiation $\partial/\partial t_j$ by the subscript $j$ on the right hand side.
Hence (2) implies the KP equation in the usual form

$$
\left(u _ {1 1 1} + 1 2 u u _ {1} - 4 u _ {3}\right) _ {1} + 3 u _ {2 2} = 0, \tag {2'}
$$

and in the case where $\tau$ is given by the formula (1) for some $\Omega \in \mathfrak{H}_{\mathfrak{g}}$ , $a_{j}$ and $Q$ , (2') also implies (2) by changing $Q$ if necessary.

Note that if $f = f(t)$ is given by the formula

$$
f (t) = \exp (Q (t)) \vartheta \left(t _ {1} a _ {1} + \dots + t _ {n} a _ {n} + \zeta\right),
$$

where $\vartheta(z) = \vartheta(z, \Omega)$ is Riemann's theta function, $a_j \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ , $Q(t)$ a quadratic form and $\zeta$ is a parameter in $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ , then by the familiar identity

$$
\vartheta (z + w, \Omega) \vartheta (z - w, \Omega) = \sum_ {\delta \in (\mathbb {Z} / 2 \mathbb {Z}) ^ {\mathrm {g}}} \vartheta \left[ \begin{array}{c} \delta / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 z, 2 \Omega) \vartheta \left[ \begin{array}{c} \delta / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 w, 2 \Omega),
$$

which can be found in [22, Chap. 2] among other places, the condition

$$
P (D) f \cdot f = 0 \quad \text {for all} \zeta \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}}
$$

is equivalent to the following $2^{\mathrm{g}}$ linear relations for zero-values of second order theta functions and their derivatives with respect to the $z$ -variables:

$$
P (\partial / \partial y _ {1}, \dots , \partial / \partial y _ {n}) \left(\exp (2 Q (y)) \vartheta \left[ \begin{array}{c} \delta / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 y _ {1} a _ {1} + \dots + 2 y _ {n} a _ {n}, 2 \Omega)\right) \Bigg | _ {y = 0} = 0
$$

for all $\delta \in (\mathbb{Z} / 2\mathbb{Z})^{\mathrm{g}}$

Proof.
Substitute the formula

$$
\begin{array}{l} f (t + y) f (t - y) \\ = \exp (2 Q (t) + 2 Q (y)) \sum_ {\delta \in (\mathbb {Z} / 2 \mathbb {Z}) ^ {\mathrm {g}}} \vartheta \left[ \begin{array}{c} \delta / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 \sum t _ {j} a _ {j} + \zeta , 2 \Omega) \vartheta \left[ \begin{array}{c} \delta / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] (2 \sum y _ {j} a _ {j}, 2 \Omega) \\ \end{array}
$$

into the definition of $P(D)f \cdot f$ and note the fact that the formula so obtained gives the Fourier expansion of $P(D)f \cdot f$ with respect to $\zeta$ .
In particular, Condition (A i) of the theorem is equivalent to:

(Ai') There exist $a_1, a_2, a_3 \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ and $c_1, c_2 \in \mathbb{C}$ such that

$$
\left(\frac {\partial^ {4}}{\partial y _ {1} ^ {4}} + 3 \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial y _ {2} ^ {2}} - 4 \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial y _ {1} \partial y _ {3}} + c _ {1} \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial y _ {1} ^ {2}} + c _ {2}\right) \vartheta \left[ \begin{array}{c} \delta / 2 \\ 0 \end{array} \right] \left(2 \sum_ {1} ^ {3} y _ {j} a _ {j}, 2 \Omega\right) \Bigg | _ {y = 0} = 0
$$

for all $\delta \in (\mathbb{Z} / 2\mathbb{Z})^{\mathrm{g}}$

Here $c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$ are related to $Q(t)$ in (A i) as follows: if

$$
Q (t) = \sum_ {i, j = 1} ^ {3} Q _ {i j} t _ {i} t _ {j}, \quad Q _ {i j} = Q _ {j i},
$$

Jacobian varieties

then

$$
c _ {1} = 2 4 Q _ {1 1}, \quad c _ {2} = 4 8 Q _ {1 1} ^ {2} + 1 2 Q _ {2 2} - 1 6 Q _ {1 3}.
$$

$Q$ may be chosen in such a way that $Q_{1j} = Q_{j1} = 0$ for all $j$ (see the remark in Appendix 0), hence we may assume $c_1 = 0$ in (A i').

# 1. Preliminaries

We recall some basic facts on the KP hierarchy.
Our formulation and argument are mainly along [3, Sect. 1], but most of the basic ideas come from the classical works of Gardner et al., Lax, and other authors in the 60's and early 70's.
See also [17].

# 1.0. Formal pseudodifferential operators

A (formal) pseudodifferential operator in a single variable $x$ is a formal Laurent series in $(d / dx)^{-1}$ with $\mathbb{C}[[x]]$-coefficients,

$$
P = \sum_ {j = - \infty} ^ {m} a _ {j} (x) (d / d x) ^ {j}, \quad m \in \mathbb {Z}.
$$

The set of all pseudodifferential operators, $\mathbb{C}[[x]]((d / dx)^{-1}))$, is denoted by $\Psi$.
If $P$ is as above and $a_{m}\neq 0$, then $m = \operatorname{ord}P$ is the order of $P$.
The product of two elements of $\Psi$ is defined by the following generalization of the Leibnitz rule:

$$
(\sum_{j}a_{j}(x)(d / dx)^{j})(\sum_{n}b_{n}(x)(d / dx)^{n}) = \sum_{\substack{j,n\\ k\geq 0}}\binom {j}{k}a_{j}(x)(d^{k}b / dx^{k})(x)(d / dx)^{j - k + n}.
$$

Under this product $\Psi$ is a non-commutative associative $\mathbb{C}$-algebra and is a Lie algebra by commutation of operators.
For $m\in \mathbb{Z}$, let $\Psi (m) = \{P\in \Psi ;\operatorname {ord}P\leq m\}$.
Then $\Psi^{-} = \Psi (-1)$ is a (Lie) subalgebra of $\Psi$ with $1 + \Psi^{-}$ its formal Lie group.
We denote by $D$ the subalgebra $\mathbb{C}[[x]][d / dx]$ of $\Psi$ of ordinary differential operators.
We have $\Psi = D\oplus \Psi^{-}$ as a $\mathbb{C}$-vector space, and for $P\in \Psi$, we denote the corresponding decomposition by $P = P_{+} + P_{-}$.
The group $1 + \Psi^{-}$ acts on $\Psi$ by conjugation: $P\mapsto SPS^{-1}$, $S\in 1 + \Psi^{-}$, so that if $P = (d / dx)^{m} + (\text{lower order terms})$ with $m\neq 0$, then the orbit of $P$ under this action is

$$
P + \Psi (m - 2),
$$

and the stability subgroup of $P$ is

$$
(1 + \Psi^ {-}) \cap Z _ {P} = 1 + \mathbb {C} [ [ P ^ {- 1 / m} ] ] P ^ {- 1 / m}.
$$

Here $Z_{P}$ denotes $\ker (\operatorname{ad} P: \Psi \to \Psi) = \mathbb{C}((P^{-1 / m}))$, the commutator subring of $P$.
For a subset $R$ of $\Psi$, we denote $R(m) = R \cap \Psi(m)$.

T.
Shiota

Introducing a formal scalar parameter $k$, we define the action of $\Psi$ on the space of formal oscillating functions,

$$
\{f(x, k) e^{kx} ; f(x, k) \in \mathbb{C}[[x]]((k^{-1}))\},
$$

by the following two properties:

i) $(\sum a_{j}(x)(d / dx)^{j})e^{kx} = \sum a_{j}(x)k^{j}e^{kx},$
ii) $P(Qe^{kx}) = (PQ)e^{kx}$ for $P, Q \in \Psi$.

Thus $k$ is a formal spectral parameter and this action is just a formal analogue of the oscillatory integral.
If $Qe^{kx} = f(x,k)e^{kx}$, $Q \in \Psi$, then we denote $Q = f(x,(d / dx))$.

The above argument immediately generalizes to the case where operators and functions depend on parameters $t = (t_1, t_2, \ldots)$ and/or $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$.
Here $t_j$ may be formal parameters, i.e., operators etc. are holomorphic or meromorphic in some (may be none) or all of the $t_j$'s in $\mathbb{C}$ and are formal power series in the other $t_j$'s.
We assume that only finitely many $t_j$'s are in $\mathbb{C} - 0$; so that when all the $t_j$'s are in $\mathbb{C}$, we assume $t \in \mathbb{C}^\infty = \lim_{\mathrm{def}} \mathbb{C}^n$.
The $t_j$'s will be called the time variables or time parameters.

Operators etc. depending on parameters are in $\Psi \hat{\otimes}$ (suitable space of functions) etc., but in general we will simply denote them by $P = P(t) \in \Psi$ etc. to show the dependence on parameters.
The $t$-dependence of oscillating functions is mainly of the form $f(t, x, k) e^{kx + \xi}$, where $\xi = \xi(t, k) = \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} t_j k^j$.
$P \in \Psi$ acts on those functions as $P(fe^{kx + \xi}) = (P(fe^{kx})) e^{\xi}$.

## 1.1. The KP hierarchy

Let us consider the affine subspace $d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ of $\Psi(1)$.
The KP hierarchy is a set of deformation equations for $L = L(t) \in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ (e.g., $L(t) \in d / dx + \Psi^{-} \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t]]$) with the deformation parameter $t = (t_1, t_2, \ldots)$, defined by the following equations of Lax type:

$$
(\partial / \partial t_n) L = [B_n, L], \quad n = 1, 2, \dots, \tag{4}
$$

where $B_n = (L^n)_+$.
Introducing $B_n^c = -(L^n)_-$ so that $L^n = B_n - B_n^c$, we see that the right hand side of (4) equals $[B_n^c, L] \in \Psi^{-} \cong T_L(d / dx + \Psi^{-})$, hence (4) defines an infinite number of vector fields on $d / dx + \Psi^{-}$.

**Lemma [3].** The KP hierarchy (4) is equivalent to the following set of Zakharov-Shabat equations,

$$
(\partial / \partial t_n) B_m - (\partial / \partial t_m) B_n = [B_n, B_m], \quad n, m = 1, 2, \dots. \tag{5}
$$

Also, (4) implies the following set of "complementary" Zakharov-Shabat equations,

$$
(\partial / \partial t_n) B_m^c - (\partial / \partial t_m) B_n^c = [B_n^c, B_m^c], \quad n, m = 1, 2, \dots. \tag{5'}
$$

**Corollary (Commuting flow property).** The vector fields on $d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ defined by the KP hierarchy (4) commute with each other.

Jacobian varieties

Proof of lemma.
(4) implies $(\partial/\partial t_n)(L^m) = [B_n, L^m]$ since $\partial/\partial t_n$ and $[B_n, \cdot]$ are derivations, so that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\{\partial B _ {m} / \partial t _ {n} - \partial B _ {n} / \partial t _ {m} - [ B _ {n}, B _ {m} ] \} - \{\partial B _ {m} ^ {c} / \partial t _ {n} - \partial B _ {n} ^ {c} / \partial t _ {m} - [ B _ {n} ^ {c}, B _ {m} ^ {c} ] \} \\
= \left[ B _ {n}, L ^ {m} \right] - \left[ B _ {m}, L ^ {n} \right] - \left[ B _ {n}, B _ {m} \right] + \left[ B _ {n} ^ {c}, B _ {m} ^ {c} \right] = 0.
\end{array}
$$

Since $D \cap \Psi^{-} = \{0\}$, both terms on the left hand side are zero, showing (5) and (5').
Conversely, (5) implies $\partial L^{m} / \partial t_{n} - [B_{n},L^{m}] \in \Psi(n - 2)$, so that $\partial L / \partial t_{n} - [B_{n},L] \in \Psi(n - m - 1)$ as

$$
\partial L ^ {m} / \partial t _ {n} - [ B _ {n}, L ^ {m} ] = m (\partial L / \partial t _ {n} - [ B _ {n}, L ]) L ^ {m - 1} + \dots .
$$

Letting $m\to \infty$, we get (4).
QED.

Remarks.
i) The commuting flow property implies the existence of formal solutions to the initial value problem (4) with any prescribed initial data $L(0) = L_0 \in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$.
See Lemma 10 in Sect.
3 (note that the solvability of (17) implies the solvability of (4), as will be seen in Sect.
1.2). Uniqueness holds since we consider only meromorphic or formal solutions to (4).

ii) If $L$ and $L'$ are solutions to (4) such that $L(0)$ and $L'(0)$ commute, then there exist $c_j \in \mathbb{C}, j = 2, 3, \ldots$, such that

$$
L ^ {\prime} (t) = \left(L + \sum_ {j = 2} ^ {\infty} c _ {j} L ^ {- j + 1}\right) (N t), \tag {*}
$$

where $N = (N_{ij})_{i,j=1,2,\ldots}$ is an upper triangular infinite matrix defined by

$$
\left(X + \sum_ {n = 2} ^ {\infty} c _ {n} X ^ {- n + 1}\right) ^ {j} \equiv \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {\infty} N _ {i j} X ^ {i} \bmod \mathbb {C} [ [ X ^ {- 1} ] ], \quad j = 1, 2, \dots ,
$$

so that $N_{ij} \in \mathbb{Z}[c_2, c_3, \ldots]$ and $N_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$ for $i \geq j - 1$.
Conversely, for any solution $L$ to (4) and for any $c_j$'s in $\mathbb{C}$, these formulas give another solution $L'$ to (4).
We call such $L$ and $L'$ equivalent.
The $c_j$ and $N$ are uniquely determined by $L(0)$ and $L'(0)$, so we denote

$$
N = N (L (0), L ^ {\prime} (0)) \quad \text {or} \quad N (L, L ^ {\prime}).
$$

iii) The first equation in (4) reads $\partial L / \partial t_1 = [d / dx, L]$, thus the $\partial / \partial t_1$-flow acts as translation in $x$.
So in what follows a function of $x$ and $t$ has the form $f(x + t_1, t_2, \ldots)$.
For such $f$ we often omit " $x$ " and simply denote $f(t)$.
This should be considered as a power-series-valued function $t \mapsto f(x + t_1, t_2, \ldots)$.
Similarly, a formal oscillating function $f(x + t_1, t_2, \ldots, k) e^{kx + \xi(t, k)}$ will be denoted by $f(t, k) e^{\xi(t, k)}$.
For example, $\sum a_j(t)(d / dx)^j$ means $\sum a_j(x + t_1, t_2, \ldots)(d / dx)^j$, so that

$$
[ d / d x, \sum a _ {j} (t) (d / d x) ^ {j} ] = \sum (\partial a _ {j} (t) / \partial t _ {1}) (d / d x) ^ {j}.
$$

Proof of ii).
If $L(0)$ and $L'(0)$ commute with each other, then we have

$$
L ^ {\prime} (0) = L (0) + \sum_ {j = 2} ^ {\infty} c _ {j} L (0) ^ {- j + 1}
$$

T.
Shiota

for some $c_{j} \in \mathbb{C}$, and this relation determines the $c_{j}$ uniquely.
Thus $(\ast)$ holds at $t = 0$, so that we have only to show that the right hand side of $(\ast)$ solves the KP hierarchy if $L$ does.
So we define $L'$ by $(\ast)$, and $B_{n}$ and $B_{n}'$ by

$$
B _ {n} (t) = \left(L (t) ^ {n}\right) _ {+}, \quad B _ {n} ^ {\prime} (t) = \left(L ^ {\prime} (t) ^ {n}\right) _ {+}.
$$

Then we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\partial L ^ {\prime} / \partial t _ {n} = \sum_ {i} N _ {i n} ((\partial / \partial t _ {i}) (L + \sum_ {j} c _ {j} L ^ {- j + 1})) (N t) \\
= [ \sum_ {i} N _ {i n} B _ {i}, L + \sum_ {j} c _ {j} L ^ {- j + 1} ] (N t) \\
= \left[ B _ {n} ^ {\prime}, L ^ {\prime} \right] (t) \\
\end{array}
$$

by the definition of $N$.
QED.

## 1.2. Wave function and $\tau$-function

Since $d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ is the orbit of $d / dx$ under the action of $1 + \Psi^{-}$ by conjugation, it is natural to look for a lifting of the KP hierarchy to a set of commuting vector fields on $1 + \Psi^{-}$ invariant under right multiplication by

$$
G = 1 + \mathbb {C} [ (d / d x) ^ {- 1} ] (d / d x) ^ {- 1},
$$

the subgroup of constant coefficient operators in $1 + \Psi^{-}$.
The lifting can be constructed by introducing the wave function and regarding the KP hierarchy as the compatibility condition for linear equations for the wave function.

A formal oscillating function

$$
w (t, k) = \left(\sum_ {j = 0} ^ {\infty} w _ {j} (t) k ^ {- j}\right) e ^ {\xi (t, k)},
$$

with $\xi(t,k) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} t_j k^j$ and $w_0(t) = 1$ (a normalization condition), is called the wave function for $L = L(t) \in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ if it satisfies the following set of linear equations:

$$
L w = k w
$$

$$
\partial w / \partial t _ {n} = B _ {n} w, \quad n = 1, 2, \dots , \tag {6}
$$

where $B_{n} = (L^{n})_{+}$

**Claim.** (6) has a solution $w$ if and only if $L$ satisfies the KP hierarchy (4), in which case the solution is unique up to multiplication by $1 + \mathbb{C}[[k^{-1}]]k^{-1}$.

Setting $w(t,k) = S(t)e^{\xi (t,k)}$, $S(t)\in 1 + \Psi^{-}$, so that

$$
S (t) = \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {\infty} w _ {j} (t) (d / d x) ^ {- j},
$$

(6) is reduced to the following set of linear equations for $S = S(t)$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l}
L S = S (d / d x) \\
\left(\partial / \partial t _ {n}\right) S = B _ {n} S - S (d / d x) ^ {n} = B _ {n} ^ {c} S, \tag {6'}
\end{array}
$$

Jacobian varieties

where $B_{n}^{c} = -(L^{n})_{-}$.
$S = S(t) \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$ is called the wave operator associated to $L$ if it satisfies (6').

Claim'.
(6') has a solution $S$ if and only if $L$ satisfies (4), in which case the solution is unique up to right multiplication by $G$.

Proof.
Given $L$ and $B_{n}^{c}$, (6') is reduced to infinitely many ordinary differential equations, one for each $w_{j}$ and each independent variable $x$ or $t_{n}$, of the following simple form

$$
\partial w _ {j} / \partial x, \partial w _ {j} / \partial t _ {n} = (\text {linear combination of} (d / d x) ^ {m} w _ {k}, k + m &lt; j),
$$

whose coefficients are scalar multiples of coefficients of $L$ and $B_{n}^{c}$.
These equations can be solved recursively under the compatibility conditions (4) and (5'), and the solution is unique if the values of $w_{j}$ at $x = 0$, $t = 0$ are chosen.
On the other hand, every equation in (6') satisfies the following invariance property: Given $L$ and $B_{n}^{c}$, the set of solutions $S$ to the equation is invariant under right multiplication by $G$.
Thus the space of solutions consists of a single $G$-orbit, since the $G$-action followed by the "evaluation at the origin"

$$
S = \sum w _ {j} (d / d x) ^ {- j} \mapsto \sum \left(w _ {j} | _ {(x = 0, t = 0)}\right) (d / d x) ^ {- j} \in 1 + \mathbb {C} [ (d / d x) ^ {- 1} ] ] (d / d x) ^ {- 1}
$$

is surjective.
Finally, (6') implies $(\partial L / \partial t_n - [B_n^c,L])S = 0$, proving (4).
QED.

Corollary.
The KP hierarchy is equivalent to the following set of equations for $S = S(t) \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$,

$$
\partial S / \partial t _ {n} = - (S (d / d x) ^ {n} S ^ {- 1}) _ {-} S, \quad n = 1, 2, \dots .
$$

This defines a set of $G$-invariant vector fields on $1 + \Psi^{-}$ commuting with one another.

Let $w = Se^{\xi}$ be a wave function for the KP hierarchy, i.e., a solution to (6) for some $L$, with corresponding wave operator $S = S(t) \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$.
Then it is shown in [3] that there exists a scalar-valued function $\tau = \tau(t) \in \mathbb{C}$, called the $\tau$-function associated to $L$, such that

$$
\begin{array}{l} w (t, k) = \frac {\tau \left(t _ {1} - 1 / k , t _ {2} - 1 / 2 k ^ {2} , t _ {3} - 1 / 3 k ^ {3} , \dots\right)}{\tau (t)} e ^ {\xi (t, k)} \\ = \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \left(\frac {p _ {n} (- \bar {\partial}) \tau}{\tau}\right) (t) k ^ {- n} e ^ {\xi (t, k)}, \tag {7} \\ \end{array}
$$

or equivalently

$$
\begin{array}{l} S (t) = \tau (t) ^ {- 1} \tau \left(t _ {1} - (d / d x) ^ {- 1}, t _ {2} - 2 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 2}, t _ {3} - 3 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 3}, \dots\right) \\ = \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \left(\frac {p _ {n} (- \bar {\partial}) \tau}{\tau}\right) (t) \left(\frac {d}{d x}\right) ^ {- n}, \tag {7'} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $p_n(X) \in \mathbb{Q}[X_1, \ldots, X_n]$ are Schur functions, defined by

$$
\exp \left(\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} X _ {j} q ^ {j}\right) = \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} p _ {n} (X) q ^ {n},
$$

T.
Shiota

and $\tilde{\partial} = (\partial/\partial t_1, 2^{-1}\partial/\partial t_2, 3^{-1}\partial/\partial t_3, \ldots)$.
In (7) and (7'), the second line follows from the first line by the Taylor expansion.
Since

$$
p_n(-\tilde{\partial}) = -n^{-1}\partial/\partial t_n + P(\partial/\partial t_1, \dots, \partial/\partial t_{n-1})
$$

for some polynomial $P$, $\tau$ is uniquely determined by $S$ or $w$ up to a constant, hence by $L$ up to the multiplication action of

$$
G_1 \underset{\text{def}}{=} \left\{ \exp \left( c_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} c_j t_j \right); c_0, c_j \in \mathbb{C} \right\}.
$$

Indeed, the map $\tau \bmod \mathbb{C}^* \mapsto S$ by (7') is bijective and intertwines the action of $G$ on the space of wave operators with the action of $G_1 / \mathbb{C}^*$ on the space of $\tau$-functions modulo $\mathbb{C}^*$:

$$
\begin{array}{c}
S \quad \xmapsto \exp(-\sum c_j (d/dx)^{-1}/j) \\
\uparrow \quad \tau \bmod \mathbb{C}^* \xmapsto \exp(\sum c_j t_j) \\
\end{array}
\quad
\begin{array}{c}
S' \\
\uparrow \\
\downarrow \\
\tau' \bmod \mathbb{C}^*.
\end{array}
$$

The $\tau$-functions satisfy, and are characterized by, infinitely many Hirota bilinear equations, which are given by the following generating function expansion [3, formula (1.7.1)]:

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} p_j(-2y) p_{j+1}(\tilde{D}) \exp \left( \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} y_i D_i \right) \tau \cdot \tau = 0, \tag{8}
$$

where $\tilde{D} = (D_1, 2^{-1}D_2, 3^{-1}D_3, \ldots)$ with $D_j$'s Hirota's symbols, so that for each multiindex $\alpha$, the coefficient of $y^\alpha$ in (8) gives Hirota's equation for a $\tau$-function.
(8) is also quoted in [1] with a different normalization and is used to show that the set of equations they obtained from Fay's trisecant formula is a part of the KP hierarchy.
It is very likely that choosing suitable subsets of equations in (8) shows various geometric properties of the theta divisor of a Jacobian (see, e.g., [9]).

Hirota's bilinear operator intertwines the diagonal action of $G_1$ on the space of pairs of functions and the action of $G_1^2 = \{\rho^2; \rho \in G_1\} = G_1$ on the space of functions:

$$
P(D) \rho f \cdot \rho g = \rho^2 P(D) f \cdot g, \quad \rho \in G_1
$$

(see [14, p. 47]), so that in particular, for any $P(D)$ the space of solutions to $P(D)f \cdot f = 0$ is $G_1$-invariant.

Let us go back to the wave operator.
Besides (7'), we have another nice formula which relates $\tau$ and the formal transpose $S^{-1}$ of $S^{-1}$ [3, (1.6.3)]:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
S^{-1} = \tau(t)^{-1} \tau(t_1 + (-d/dx)^{-1}, t_2 + 2^{-1} (-d/dx)^{-2}, \dots) \\
= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{p_n(\tilde{\partial}) \tau}{\tau} \right)(t) \left( -\frac{d}{dx} \right)^{-n},
\end{array}
$$

which implies

$$
S(t)^{-1} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{d}{dx} \right)^{-n} \left( \frac{p_n(\tilde{\partial}) \tau}{\tau} \right)(t).
$$

Jacobian varieties

(Note that this is not a consequence of algebraic manipulation on (7).) Thus for $n = 1,2,\ldots$

$$
L^n = S(d/dx)^n S^{-1} = \sum_{j,m=0}^{\infty} \frac{p_j(-\tilde{\partial})\tau}{\tau} \left(\frac{d}{dx}\right)^{n-j-m} \frac{p_m(\tilde{\partial})\tau}{\tau}.
$$

Dividing the sum into two parts according as $j + m \leq n$ or $&gt;n$, we get formulas for $B_n$ and $B_n^c$ in terms of $\tau$.
In particular,

$$
B_n^c = - \sum_{j+m=n+1} \frac{(p_j(-\tilde{\partial})\tau)(p_m(\tilde{\partial})\tau)}{\tau^2} \left(\frac{d}{dx}\right)^{-1} + \dots = - \frac{p_{n+1}(\tilde{D})\tau \cdot \tau}{\tau^2} \left(\frac{d}{dx}\right)^{-1} + \dots.
$$

Here the last equality follows from an identity for generating functions [14, p. 46(V)]:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\exp \left(\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} y^j j^{-1} D_j\right) \tau \cdot \tau = \tau(t_1 + y, t_2 + y^2/2, \dots) \tau(t_1 - y, t_2 - y^2/2, \dots) \\
= \left(\exp \left(\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} y^j j^{-1} (\partial/\partial t_j)\right) \tau\right) \left(\exp \left(- \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} y^j j^{-1} (\partial/\partial t_j)\right) \tau\right).
\end{array}
$$

Thus by computing the coefficients of $(d/dx)^{-1}$ in (6'), we get a sequence of Hirota's equations for a $\tau$-function:

$$
((1/2) D_1 D_n - p_{n+1}(\tilde{D})) \tau \cdot \tau = 0, \quad n = 2, 3, \dots, \tag{9}
$$

which also follows from the coefficient of $y_n$ in (8).
Since this is of the form $(c_n D_1 D_n - P(D_1, \ldots, D_{n-1})) \tau \cdot \tau = 0$ with $c_n = 1/2 - 1/n$ (here we used the property that $P(D)f \cdot f = 0$ for any odd $P$ and any $f$), this gives a nontrivial Hirota equation if $n \geq 3$, and coincides with the KP equation (2) if $n = 3$.
In Sect.
3, Hirota's equations of this form will be used to prove a regularizing property of the KP equation.

## 2. Finite dimensional solutions

In this section, we give a characterization of Jacobian varieties in terms of the KP hierarchy, based on the Burchnall-Chaundy-Krichever theory [2, 15, 20].

## 2.0. Commutative subring of $D$ and its deformation

As was observed by Lax [16, Thm. 6.13] and McKean and van Moerbeke [18, Sect. 5], a finite dimensional solution to a hierarchy of Lax equations satisfies stationary Lax equations, which play an important role in the soliton theory [18, 23].
To describe this property, we associate to each $L \in d/dx + \Psi^{-}$ some additional data and observe how they are deformed by the KP hierarchy.

For $L \in d/dx + \Psi^{-}$, we define $\Phi: T_0 \mathbb{C}^\infty \to D$ by

$$
\Phi\left(\sum_{n \geq 1} c_n \partial/\partial t_n\right) = \sum_{n \geq 1} c_n B_n, \quad B_n = (L^n)_+,
$$

T.
Shiota

and we set $dL = -\mathrm{ad}(L)\circ \Phi \colon T_0\mathbb{C}^\infty \to \Psi^{-}$, i.e.,

$$
d L \left(\sum c _ {n} \partial / \partial t _ {n}\right) = \left[ \sum c _ {n} B _ {n}, L \right].
$$

When $L$ depends on the time variables $t$ and satisfies the KP hierarchy, $dL(t)$ coincides with the tangent map to $L\colon t\mapsto L(t)$ at $t$, under the identification $T_{t}\mathbb{C}^{\infty}\cong \mathbb{C}^{\infty}\cong T_{0}\mathbb{C}^{\infty}$.
We also set

$$
K _ {L} = \ker d L,
$$

$$
R_{L} = \mathbb{C}\oplus \varPhi(K_{L}) = \left\{P = c_{0} + \sum_{\substack{n\geq 1\\ \text{finete}}}c_{n}B_{n}\in \mathbb{C}\oplus \bigoplus_{n = 1}^{\infty}\mathbb{C}B_{n};[P,L] = 0\right\},
$$

so that we have the following commutative diagram with exact rows:

![img-58.jpeg](img-58.jpeg)

Lemma 0. Let $L$ be an element of $d / dx + \Psi^{-}$.

a) Let $Z_{L} = \ker (\operatorname{ad} L: \Psi \to \Psi)$ be the commutator subring of $L$.
Then

$$
R _ {L} = Z _ {L} \cap D = \{P \in D; [ P, L ] = 0 \}.
$$

b) $R_{L}$ is a maximal commutative subring of $D$ if $R_{L} \neq \mathbb{C}$.
Conversely, any maximal commutative subring $R$ of $D$ such that there exists $P \in R$ of the form

$$
P = (d / d x) ^ {n} + c (d / d x) ^ {n - 1} + (\text{lower order terms}), \quad n \geq 1, c \in \mathbb {C};
$$

can be written as $R_{L}$ for some $L\in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$

Proof.
Let $Z_L' = Z_L \cap D = \ker(\operatorname{ad} L) \cap D$, and let $S \in 1 + \Psi^-$ be such that $S^{-1}LS = d / dx$.
Setting $\bar{R} = S^{-1}Z_L'S$, we have

$$
\bar {R} = \ker (\operatorname {a d} (d / d x)) \cap S ^ {- 1} D S = \mathbb {C} ((d / d x) ^ {- 1})) \cap S ^ {- 1} D S.
$$

Thus $\bar{R}$ is a commutative ring, and is a maximal commutative subring of $S^{-1}DS$ provided $\bar{R}\neq \mathbb{C}$, for if $Q\in \Psi$ commutes with $P\in \mathbb{C}((d / dx)^{-1})) - \mathbb{C}$, then $Q\in \mathbb{C}((d / dx)^{-1}))$.
Hence $Z_L' = \mathbb{C}((L^{-1}))\cap D$ is maximal commutative in $D$ if $Z_L'\neq \mathbb{C}$.
We have

$$
R _ {L} = Z _ {L} ^ {\prime} \cap \bigoplus_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \mathbb {C} B _ {n} \subset Z _ {L} ^ {\prime} = \mathbb {C} ((L ^ {- 1})) \cap D \subset \bigoplus_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \mathbb {C} B _ {n} \quad (B _ {0} = 1),
$$

which shows $Z_L' = R_L$.
For the last assertion of the lemma, let $R$ and $P \in R$ be as stated, so there exists $S \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$ such that

$$
S ^ {- 1} P S = (d / d x) ^ {n} + c (d / d x) ^ {n - 1}.
$$

Then $S(d / dx)S^{-1} = L\in (d / dx) + \Psi^{-}$ satisfies $R_{L} = Z_{L}^{\prime} = R$.
QED.

Jacobian varieties

Remark 0. Since $R_{L} \subset \bigoplus \mathbb{C}B_{n}$, we have $\dim R_L(n) / R_L(n - 1) \leq 1$ for any $n$.
In particular, $\operatorname{tr.deg}_{\mathbb{C}}R_{L} = 1$ unless $R_{L} = \mathbb{C}$.
This is true for any commutative subring of $D$ which contains $P \in D$ of the form

$$
a (x) (d / d x) ^ {m} + (\text{lower order terms}), \quad m \neq 0, a (0) \neq 0.
$$

Next, let $L = L(t)$ be a solution to the KP hierarchy.
We consider $K_{L(t)}$ and $R_{L(t)}$ as functions of $t$.
By the uniqueness of the solution to the initial value problem and the commuting flow property, $K_{L(t)}$ is independent of $t$, so we denote it by $K_L$.
If $L$ is a globally meromorphic solution, then $L$ factors through $\mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_L$ as $L\colon \mathbb{C}^{\infty}\to \mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_L^{-1}\to d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ with $d\bar{L}$ injective.
If $L$ and $L'$ are equivalent solutions, then

$$
R _ {L ^ {\prime} (t)} = R _ {L (N t)}, \quad K _ {L} = N K _ {L ^ {\prime}} \quad \text{for} N = N (L, L ^ {\prime})
$$

(see Remark (ii), Sect.
1.1). Denote $R_{t} = R_{L(t)}$.
We have:

Lemma 1. a) (Mulase [19], Manin [17, Remark 2.7 of Chap. III]).
If $S = S(t)$ is the wave operator associated to $L$, then $\bar{R}_t = S(t)^{-1} R_t S(t) \subset \mathbb{C}((d / dx)^{-1}))$ is independent of $t$, and so it will be denoted by $\bar{R}$.

b) If $R_{t}$ cannot be generated by only one element as a $\mathbb{C}$-algebra, then $R_{t} = R_{s}$ implies $L(t) = L(s)$.

Proof.
a) That $\bar{R}_t \subset \mathbb{C}((d / dx)^{-1}))$ for each $t$ is obvious.
Suppose $\sum c_n(d / dx)^n \in \bar{R}_{t_0}$, where $c_n \in \mathbb{C}$, $c_n = 0$ for $n \gg 0$.
Then $\sum c_n L(t_0)^n \in D$,

$$
\left(\partial / \partial t _ {m}\right) \sum c _ {n} L ^ {n} | _ {t = t _ {0}} = \left[ B _ {m} \left(t _ {0}\right), \sum c _ {n} L \left(t _ {0}\right) ^ {n} \right] \in [ D, D ] \subset D,
$$

etc. In general, we have $(\partial/\partial t)^{\alpha}\sum c_{n}L^{n}|_{t = t_{0}}\in D$ for any multiindex $\alpha$, by using (4) and (5) repeatedly.
Thus $\sum c_nL(t)^n\in D$, hence $\sum c_n(d / dx)^n\in \bar{R}_t$ and $\bar{R}_t\supset \bar{R}_{t_0}$.
Since $t_0$ is arbitrary, we have a).
(Note that here we only used $S^{-1}LS = d / dx$.
Thus $\bar{R}$ is determined by $L$ and is independent of the choice of $S$.
The wave equations, the second line of $(6')$, were not used here.)

b) Let $S(s), S(t) \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$ be such that

$$
S (s) ^ {- 1} L (s) S (s) = d / d x = S (t) ^ {- 1} L (t) S (t).
$$

Suppose $R_{t} = R_{s}$ and $L(t) \neq L(s)$.
Then $S(s)^{-1}S(t)$ does not commute with $d / dx$, so that we have a nontrivial automorphism $\sigma$ of the ring $\bar{R}$,

$$
P \mapsto P ^ {\sigma} = S (s) ^ {- 1} S (t) P (S (s) ^ {- 1} S (t)) ^ {- 1},
$$

which preserves the top order term of each $P \in \bar{R}$.
Note that $\sigma$ is well defined by $S(t) \bmod G$ and $S(s) \bmod G$, hence by $L(t)$ and $L(s)$.
Let $(f_1, \ldots, f_k)$ be a generating set of $\bar{R}$ such that

i) $0 &lt; \operatorname{ord}f_{1} &lt; \ldots &lt; \operatorname{ord}f_{k},$
ii) For each $j$, $\operatorname{ord} f_j \notin \mathbb{N} \cdot \operatorname{ord} f_1 + \ldots + \mathbb{N} \cdot \operatorname{ord} f_{j-1}$,
iii) $\mathbb{N} \cdot \operatorname{ord} f_1 + \ldots + \mathbb{N} \cdot \operatorname{ord} f_k = \{\operatorname{ord} P; P \in \bar{R} - 0\}$,

where $\mathbb{N} = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$.
By assumption, we have $k &gt; 1$.
Since $\sigma \neq$ identity, there exists $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, $1 \leq n \leq k$, such that $f_{n}^{\sigma} \neq f_{n}$ and $f_{j}^{\sigma} = f_{j}$ for $j &lt; n$.

T.
Shiota

Suppose first that $n = 1$.
Then $f_1^\sigma = f_1 + c$, $f_2^\sigma = f_2 + a(f_1)$ for some $c \in \mathbb{C} - 0$, $a(X) \in \mathbb{C}[X]$ with $(\operatorname{ord}f_1)(\deg a) &lt; \operatorname{ord}f_2$.
This follows from i)-iii) and the fact that $\operatorname{ord}(P^\sigma - P) &lt; \operatorname{ord}P$.
Set $\operatorname{ord}f_j = m_j$ ($j = 1, 2$).
Since $\operatorname{tr.deg.}_{\mathbb{C}}\bar{R} = 1$ by Remark 0, there exists $F(X, Y) \in \mathbb{C}[X, Y]$ such that $F \neq 0$ and $F(f_1, f_2) = 0$.
Let $F_0$ be such a polynomial of the lowest weight $\operatorname{wt}(F_0)$ with respect to $\operatorname{wt}(X^i Y^j) = m_1 i + m_2 j$.
Let

$$
G (X, Y) = F _ {0} (X + c, Y + a (X)) - F _ {0} (X, Y).
$$

Then $G(f_{1},f_{2}) = 0$ and $\mathrm{wt}(G) &lt; \mathrm{wt}(F_0)$, so that $G$ must be zero.
However, since the top weight terms $\bar{F}_0$ of $F_0$ is not of the form $cY^r$ for $F_0(f_1,f_2) = 0$, we have $\partial \bar{F}_0 / \partial X \neq 0$.
This together with $c \neq 0$ and $m_1 / m_2$ imply $\mathrm{wt}(c\partial \bar{F}_0 / \partial X) \neq \mathrm{wt}(a(X)\partial \bar{F}_0 / \partial Y)$, and hence $G = c\partial \bar{F}_0 / \partial X + a(X)\partial \bar{F}_0 / \partial Y + \ldots \neq 0$, a contradiction.

Next, suppose that $n &gt; 1$.
Taking the polynomial relation of the form $F(f_{1},\ldots ,f_{n - 1},f_{n}) = 0$ with the lowest positive degree in $f_{n}$, we get a contradiction in a similar way.
Thus $R_{t} = R_{s}$ must imply $L(t) = L(s)$.
QED.

## 2.1. Finite dimensional solutions

**Lemma/Definition 2.** An operator $L \in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ is called finite dimensional (resp.
g-dimensional) if it satisfies (one of) the following equivalent conditions:

i) $dL$ is of finite rank (resp.
of rank $g$),
ii) $\mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_L$ is finite dimensional (resp.
g-dimensional),
iii) $\dim_{\mathbb{C}} R_L(n) / R_L(n - 1) = 1$ for $n \geqslant 0$ (resp.
there exist $v_1, \ldots, v_g \in \mathbb{N}$, $v_1 &lt; \ldots &lt; v_g$, such that for $n \in \mathbb{N}$,

$$
\dim_ {\mathbb {C}} R _ {L} (n) / R _ {L} (n - 1) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} 0 &amp; \text{if } n = v _ {j} \text{ for some } j, \\ 1 &amp; \text{otherwise}, \end{array} \right.
$$

and such that the $v_{j}$'s satisfy $j \leq v_{j} &lt; 2j$,

iv) $\dim_{\mathbb{C}} R_L(2N + 1) \geqslant N + 2$ for some $N$ (resp.
$\dim_{\mathbb{C}} R_L(2g + 1) = g + 2$),
iv') $\dim_{\mathbb{C}}(K_L \cap \mathbb{C}^{2N + 1}) \geqslant N + 1$ for some $N$ (resp.
$\dim_{\mathbb{C}}(K_L \cap \mathbb{C}^{2g + 1}) = g + 1$).

**Proof of the equivalence.** Define the condition iii') to be the condition iii) except that the last assertion, $j \leq v_{j} &lt; 2j$, is dropped.
Then i) $\Leftrightarrow$ ii) $\Leftrightarrow$ iii') and iv) $\Leftrightarrow$ iv') by (10), and clearly iii) $\Rightarrow$ iv).
We show iv) $\Rightarrow$ iii') $\Rightarrow$ iii) as follows.
Since the subset

$$
\operatorname {ord} R _ {L} = \left\{n \in \mathbb {N}; \dim_ {\mathbb {C}} R _ {L} (n) / R _ {L} (n - 1) = 1 \right\}
$$

of $\mathbb{N}$ is closed under addition, we have:

$$
\dim R _ {L} (n - 1) \geqslant n / 2 + 1 \Rightarrow \operatorname {ord} R _ {L} \ni n. \tag {*}
$$

(Note that $\operatorname{ord} R_L(n - 1) \ni j, n - j$ for some $j$ if $\dim R_L(n - 1) \geqslant n / 2 + 1$.) The condition $\dim R_L(n - 1) \geqslant n / 2 + 1$ is sharp because $\operatorname{ord} R_L$ contains 0. Repeated use of $(\ast)$ for $n \geqslant 2N + 2$ (or $n \geqslant 2g + 2$) shows iv) $\Rightarrow$ iii').
Also, iii) follows from iii') by means of $(\ast)$:

$$
\operatorname {ord} R _ {L} \not\exists v _ {j} \Rightarrow v _ {j} / 2 + 1 &gt; \dim R _ {L} (v _ {j} - 1) = v _ {j} - (j - 1) \geqslant 1. \quad \mathrm {QED}.
$$

Jacobian varieties

A solution $L = L(t)$ to the KP hierarchy is called finite dimensional (resp.
g-dimensional) if for some (hence any) $t$, the operator $L(t)$ is finite dimensional (resp.
g-dimensional).
The condition is independent of $t$.
It is known that a finite dimensional solution is meromorphic in $\mathbb{C}^{\infty}$ and its $\tau$-function is entire [26].
Condition iv) in Lemma/Definition 2 shows that we only need the first $2\mathrm{g} + 1$ Lax equations to characterize the g-dimensional operators.
So we have:

Lemma 3. Denote $t = (t_{1}, t_{2}, \ldots)$, $t' = (t_{1}, \ldots, t_{2g + 1})$.
If $L' = L'(t')$ is a g-dimensional solution to the first $2g + 1$ equations in (4), i.e., if

$$
\partial L ^ {\prime} / \partial t _ {n} = \left[ B _ {n}, L ^ {\prime} \right], \quad B _ {n} = \left(L ^ {\prime n}\right) _ {+}, \quad \text{for } n = 1, \dots , 2 g + 1,
$$

with

$$
\operatorname {r a n k} \left(d L ^ {\cdot}: \mathbb {C} ^ {2 g + 1} \rightarrow \Psi^ {-}\right) = g,
$$

then $L'$ is automatically extended to a g-dimensional solution $L$ to the KP hierarchy (4) as follows: there is a linear map $\pi \colon \mathbb{C}^{\infty} \to \mathbb{C}^{2g + 1}$ such that $L(t) = L'(\pi t)$ solves (4), with the initial condition $L(t',0) = L'(t')$.

Proof.
By assumption, $K_{L'}$ satisfies Condition iv'), so that by ii) $\mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_{L'} \cong \mathbb{C}^{2g + 1} / (K_{L'} \cap \mathbb{C}^{2g + 1}) \cong \mathbb{C}^g$.
Taking any $\pi$ such that

![img-59.jpeg](img-59.jpeg)

commutes, we have the desired extension $L(t) = L'(\pi t)$.
QED.

We do not need this lemma to prove the main theorem.
However, as was shown in the author's notes [27], one can proceed this way and obtain a comparatively small number of Hirota's bilinear equations for a $\tau$-function, which characterize the $g$-dimensional solutions without any global or geometric assumption on the solutions.
Note also that the situation is particularly simple in the KdV case, i.e., $K_{L} \ni \partial / \partial t_{2}$, where only $g + 1$ equations characterize $g$-dimensional solutions (see, e.g., [16, 18]).

Suppose $L$ is a g-dimensional solution.
Then the meromorphic map $L$ factors through $\mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_{L} \cong \mathbb{C}^{g}$ as $L \colon \mathbb{C}^{\infty} \to \mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_{L} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{J}} d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ with $d\tilde{L}$ injective.
Thus if we define

$$
\Gamma = \left\{s \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\infty} / K _ {L}; \tilde {L} (s) = \tilde {L} (0) \right\} = \left\{s \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\infty} / K _ {L}; \tilde {L} (t + s) = \tilde {L} (t) \forall t \right\},
$$

then $\Gamma$ is a discrete subgroup of $\mathbb{C}^{\infty} / K_L$.
Hence

$$
T _ {L} = \left(\mathbb {C} ^ {\infty} / K _ {L}\right) / \Gamma \tag {11}
$$

is a complex abelian Lie group which effectively meromorphically parametrizes $L$ and is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by this property.
We call $L$ compact if $T_{L}$ is compact, i.e., if $\Gamma$ is of maximal rank.
Note that compactness just means that the effective parameter space is compact.
Since $L$ is meromorphic it blows up somewhere unless $g = 0$, so that the image of the map $L$ is not

T.
Shiota

compact.
Compact solutions are usually called quasiperiodic, meaning that their $\tau$-functions are quasiperiodic as we will see in Theorem 6 below.

Remark 1. If we have a $\tau$-function of the form (3) for a principally polarized abelian variety $X$, then the corresponding solution $L$ is $g$-dimensional and compact.
Indeed, we observe that $T_L \cong X$ as follows: By (3) and the quasiperiodicity of the theta function, if $\bar{A} s \in \mathbb{Z}^g + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^g$, then $\tau(t + s) = \rho_{s,\zeta} \tau(t)$ for some $\rho_{s,\zeta} \in G_1$ (see Sect.
1.2). Thus for the corresponding $L$, we have $L(t + s) = L(t)$.
This shows that there is a projection from $X$ onto $T_L$, since $\operatorname{rank} \bar{A} = g$.
Then this is a bijection since $\vartheta$ represents the principal polarization of $X$.

## 2.2. Burchnall-Chaundy-Krichever theory

The following is the fundamental fact in the study of finite dimensional solutions:
