Theorem (Burchnall and Chaundy [2], Krichever [15]).
There exists a natural bijection between the following two sets of data:

Data A.
a) $C$ a complete reduced and irreducible curve over $\mathbb{C}$,

b) $p \in C$ a smooth point, and $[z] \in (\mathfrak{m}_p - \mathfrak{m}_p^2) / \mathfrak{m}_p^3$, an equivalence class of local coordinates at $p$,

c) $\mathcal{F}$ a torsion free rank 1 sheaf on $C$ such that

$$
H ^ {0} (C, \mathcal {F}) = 0, \quad H ^ {1} (C, \mathcal {F}) = 0.
$$

Data B.
A commutative subring $R \subset D$ with $\mathbb{C} \subset R$, such that for any $n \gg 0$ there exists $P \in R$ of the form

$$
P = (d / d x) ^ {n} + c (d / d x) ^ {n - 1} + (\text{lower order terms}), \quad c \in \mathbb {C}.
$$

The formulation of this theorem is due to Mumford [20] except that the conditions on $z$ and $R$ are slightly changed: If we take an equivalence class of $z$ modulo $\mathfrak{m}_p^2$ instead of $\mathfrak{m}_p^3$, then in Data $B$ we should identify $R$ with its conjugates by multiplication by an exponential function, $e^{-cx}Re^{cx}$.
On the other hand, if we take $z\in \hat{\mathfrak{m}}_p - \hat{\mathfrak{m}}_p^2$ in Data $A$, then in Data $B$ we should take a pair $(R,L)$, where $R$ is as above and $L\in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ commutes with $R$.
We recall the construction of the map $(C,p,\mathcal{F})\mapsto R$ and the proofs of the following Lemmas 4 and 5 in Appendix 0. For $R\mapsto (C,p,\mathcal{F})$, the basic idea is the following: $\operatorname{Spec} R$ is 1-dimensional by Remark 0, and is of the form $C - p$ with $p\in C$ a smooth point, since $\mathcal{C}_p = \{PQ^{-1}\in \Psi ;P,Q\in R,\operatorname {ord}P\leqq \operatorname {ord}Q\}$ is a discrete valuation ring.
Each point $q$ of $C - p$ corresponds to a $\mathbb{C}$-algebra homomorphism $\lambda \colon R\to \mathbb{C}$.
Then the associated common eigenspace of $R$,

$$
\{f \in \mathbb {C} [ [ x ] ]; P f = \lambda (P) f \text{ for all } P \in R \},
$$

is isomorphic to $\mathrm{Hom}_R(\mathcal{F}_q / \mathfrak{m}_q\mathcal{F}_q,\mathbb{C})$.

If $L = L(t)$ is a $g$-dimensional solution to the KP hierarchy, then $R_{L(t)}$ is maximal commutative by Lemma 0b), satisfies the condition of Data $B$ by

Jacobian varieties

Lemma/Definition 2, and determines $L(t)$ completely since Lemma 1 b) applies unless $g = 0$, the trivial case.
Moreover, $\operatorname{Spec} R_t$ is independent of $t$ by Lemma 1 a).
Thus denoting the corresponding triple of Data $A$ by $(C_t, p_t, \mathcal{F}_t)$, we observe:

i) $C = C_t$, $p = p_t$ are independent of $t$, and $C$ is of arithmetic genus $g$;
ii) $\mathcal{F}_t$ determines $L(t)$ completely.

Note also that the triples $(C, p, \mathcal{F}_t)$ and $(C', p', \mathcal{F}_t')$ for equivalent solutions $L$ and $L'$ are related by $C' = C$, $p' = p$ and $\mathcal{F}_t' = \mathcal{F}_{Nt}$ with $N = N(L, L')$ as in Remark (ii), Sect.
1.1, since $R_{L'(t)} = R_{L(Nt)}$.
We further observe:

iii) $\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{F}_t,\mathcal{F}_t)\cong \mathcal{C}_C$; since we have the following

Remark 2 (Mumford, Wilson).
If $C$ is any complete reduced irreducible curve, $\mathcal{F}$ any torsion free rank 1 sheaf on $C$, then

$$
\mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F}) \cong \mathcal {C} _ {C} \Leftrightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{l} \operatorname {P i c} ^ {0} (C) \to \overline {{\operatorname {P i c} (C)}} \text { is locally one to one} \\ \mathcal {L} \mapsto \mathcal {L} \otimes \mathcal {F} \end{array} \right]
$$

$\Leftrightarrow$ this map is globally one to one,

and in the Burchnall-Chaundy-Krichevar set-up, these hold if and only if $R$ is a maximal commutative subring of $D$.
Here $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}(C)}$ is the space of torsion free rank 1 sheaves of fixed Euler-Poincaré characteristic (= that of $\mathcal{F}$).

Proof.
The map $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C) \to \overline{\operatorname{Pic}(C)}$ has the differential at 0 given by

$$
H ^ {1} (C, \mathcal {C} _ {C}) \rightarrow \operatorname {E x t} ^ {1} (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F}). \tag {*}
$$

We have an exact sequence

$$
0 \to \mathcal {C} _ {C} \to \mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F}) \to \mathcal {K} \to 0
$$

with $\mathcal{K}$ supported on the singularities of $C$, which gives an exact cohomology sequence

$$
0 \to \mathbb {C} \stackrel {\cong} {\to} \mathbb {C} \to H ^ {0} (\mathcal {K}) \to H ^ {1} (\mathcal {C} _ {C}) \to H ^ {1} (\mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {F} \cdot \mathcal {F})) \to 0.
$$

By using a spectral sequence [11, §4.2], we have another exact sequence

$$
0 \to H ^ {1} (\mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F})) \hookrightarrow \operatorname {E x t} ^ {1} (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F}) \to H ^ {0} (\mathcal {E} \times \ell^ {1} (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F})).
$$

Thus $(\ast)$ is injective if and only if $\mathcal{C}_C \cong \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{F})$, in which case we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {L} \otimes \mathcal {F} \cong \mathcal {F} \Rightarrow \mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {L}, \mathcal {C}) \cong \mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {L}, \mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F})) \cong \mathcal {H} o m (\mathcal {L} \otimes \mathcal {F}, \mathcal {F}) \cong \mathcal {C} \\ \Rightarrow \mathcal {L} \cong \mathcal {C}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The last assertion of the remark is shown in the following Lemma 4. QED.

In the Burchnall-Chaundy-Krichever set-up, we can consider the deformation of general $R$ in Data $B$ (not necessarily maximal commutative) or general $\mathcal{F}$ in Data $A$ by the KP hierarchy: Given $R$, take any $\operatorname{L} \in d / dx + \Psi^{-}$ which commutes with $R$, and then solve the KP hierarchy with the initial data

T.
Shiota

$L$.
If $L(t)$ is the solution to this problem with $S(t)$ the corresponding wave operator, then define:

$$
R _ {t} = S (t) \bar {R} S (t) ^ {- 1} \quad \text {with} \quad \bar {R} = S (0) ^ {- 1} R S (0) \subset \mathbb {C} (((d / d x) ^ {- 1})).
$$

This is well defined up to the equivalence $\{R_t\} \sim \{R_{N_t}\}$ corresponding to the equivalence of $L$.
Then $R_{t} \subset R_{L(t)} \subset D$, $R_{t}$ determines $R_{L(t)}$ as its commutator subring in $D$, $R_{t}$ is as in Data $B$ since so is $R$, and the corresponding triple in Data $A$ is of the form $(C, p, \mathcal{F}_t)$.
Note that if $R$ is maximal commutative, then $R_{t} = R_{L(t)}$.
To describe $\mathcal{F}_t$, we observe:

**Lemma 4** [15, 20].
Let $C, p$ (and $[z]$) be as in Data A.
Then there exists a surjective homomorphism

$$
\phi \colon \mathbb {C} ^ {\infty} \to \operatorname {P i c} ^ {0} (C), \quad t \mapsto \phi_ {t},
$$

such that for any $\mathcal{F}$ as in Data $A$,

$$
\mathcal {F} _ {t} = \mathcal {F} \otimes \phi_ {t}
$$

gives the deformation of $\mathcal{F}$ with respect to the KP hierarchy in the above sense.
Moreover, $\phi$ is given explicitly as follows: Let $z \in \mathfrak{m}_p - \mathfrak{m}_p^2$ belong to the equivalence class $[z]$ chosen in Data A, and let $U$ be a small complex neighborhood of $p$ in $C$ such that $z$ is a local coordinate on $U$.
Then for $t \in \mathbb{C}^\infty$, we define

$$
\phi_ {t} = \mathcal {O} _ {C} \quad \text {on} \ U \quad \text {and on} \quad C - p,
$$

glued to itself on $U - p$ by the transition function $\exp \xi (t,1 / z)$

where $\xi (t,1 / z) = \sum_{j = 1}^{\infty}t_jz^{-j}$

This lemma, and also its corollary and Lemma 5 below, are proved in Appendix 0.

Thus $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$ acts transitively on $(\mathcal{F}_t; t \in \mathbb{C}^\infty)$ by tensor product, and the action is locally faithful if and only if $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$ and $\{\mathcal{F}_t; t \in \mathbb{C}^\infty\}$ have the same dimension, i.e., if and only if the arithmetic genus of $C$ is the dimension of $L$.
By Lemma/Definition 2, this is also equivalent to the maximal commutativity of the ring $R_t$ corresponding to $(C, p, \mathcal{F}_t)$.
This proves the last assertion of Remark 2.

A finite dimensional solution is meromorphic on $\mathbb{C}^{\infty}$ and its $\tau$-function is entire.
If $(C, p, \mathcal{F}_t)$ corresponds to the solution $L(t)$ as above, then

(the locus of poles of $L$) = (the locus of zeros of $\tau$)

$$
= \left\{t \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\infty}; h ^ {0} (C, \mathcal {F} _ {t}) = h ^ {1} (C, \mathcal {F} _ {t}) \neq 0 \right\}
$$

by [26].
Noting this, we go back to the case where $R_{t}$ is maximal commutative (so that the $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$-action of $\{\mathcal{F}_t\}$ is faithful by Remark 2).
Noting that $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$ for a reduced irreducible curve $C$ is compact if and only if $C$ is smooth (see e.g., [4, §2]), we observe:

**Corollary to Lemma 4.** For a finite dimensional solution $L = L(t)$ corresponding to $(C, p, \mathcal{F}_t)$ with $\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{F}_t, \mathcal{F}_t) \cong \mathcal{O}$, we have an isomorphism

$$
T _ {L} \cong \operatorname {P i c} ^ {0} (C)
$$

Jacobian varieties

as complex Lie groups, where $T_{L}$ is the effective parameter space for $L$ defined by (11).
In particular, $C$ is smooth if and only if $L$ is compact, in which case $\mathcal{F}_t$ is invertible and we have an isomorphism

$$
T_{L} \cong \operatorname{Pic}^{g-1}(C) \cong \operatorname{Jac}(C)
$$

as polarized abelian varieties.
Here the polarization of $T_{L}$ is defined by the divisor of the $\tau$-function, which is well-defined by the uniqueness of the $\tau$-function up to multiplication by an exponential function.

Hence $T_{L}$ for a compact $L$ has a natural principal polarization.
Even more explicitly, we have:

**Lemma 5** (Krichever [15]).
If $C$ is a complete smooth curve over $\mathbb{C}$ and $p \in C$, then there exists a $g \times \infty$-matrix $\bar{A} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots)$, $a_j \in \mathbb{C}^g$, with $\text{rank } \bar{A} = g$ and a quadratic form $Q(t) = \sum Q_{ij} t_i t_j$, such that for any $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$

$$
\tau(t) = e^{Q(t)} \vartheta(\bar{A} t + \zeta) \tag{3}
$$

gives a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy associated with a solution belonging to the pair $(C, p)$, and any solution belonging to $(C, p)$ is given, up to equivalence, by (3) for some $\zeta$.
Hence $\vartheta$ is Riemann's theta function for $C$.
The formula for equivalent $\tau$ is also given by (3) but with some change in $\bar{A}$ and $Q$.

Combining these, we get our first main result, which was pointed out to the author by Mulase and appeared in [19, 27] with the same basic idea for its proof.

**Theorem 6.** For a principally polarized abelian variety $X = \mathbb{C}^g / (\mathbb{Z}^g + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^g)$, $\Omega \in \mathfrak{H}_g$, the following conditions (B), (C') and (C) are equivalent:

(B) $X \cong \operatorname{Jac}(C)$ for a complete smooth curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$,

(C') $X \cong T_L$ for a compact solution $L = L(t)$ to the KP hierarchy,

(C) There exist $\bar{A} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots) \in \mathbb{C}^{g \times \infty}$ with $\text{rank } \bar{A} = g$ and a quadratic form $Q(t)$ such that the function $\tau(t)$ defined in (3) with $\vartheta(z) = \vartheta(z, \Omega)$, is a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy for some (hence any) $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$.

**Proof.** $(\mathrm{C}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}')$ by Remark 1 of Sect.
2.1 and the well-definedness of the divisor of the $\tau$-function; $(\mathrm{C}') \Rightarrow (\mathrm{B})$ by the Corollary to Lemma 4; $(\mathrm{B}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C})$ by Lemma 5. QED.

## Appendix 0

### Baker-Akhiezer function

We recall from [20] and [15] the construction of the map $(C, p, \mathcal{F}) \mapsto R$ and proofs of Lemmas 4 and 5.

Let $(C, p, \mathcal{F})$ be as in Data $A$, and let $z \in \mathfrak{m}_p - \mathfrak{m}_p^2$ belong to the equivalence class modulo $\mathfrak{m}_p^3$ chosen in Data $A$.
Take a small complex neighborhood $U$ of $p$ on which $z$ is still a local coordinate.
Define a sheaf $\mathcal{F}^*$ on $C \times \mathbb{C}$ to be

T.
Shiota

$\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{C}}$ on $U \times \mathbb{C}$ and on $(C - p) \times \mathbb{C}$, but is glued to itself on $(U - p) \times \mathbb{C}$ by the transition function $e^{x/z}$.
Here $x$ is the standard coordinate function on $\mathbb{C}$.

$d/dx$ acts on sections of $\mathcal{F}^*$ on $(C - p) \times \mathbb{C}$, which gives

$$
d/dx \colon \mathcal{F}^*(np) \to \mathcal{F}^*((n + 1)p).
$$

The completion $\hat{\mathcal{C}}_p$ of the local ring $\mathcal{C}_p$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{C}[[z]]$.
Thus by choosing an identification $\mathcal{F}(p) \otimes \hat{\mathcal{C}}_p \cong \hat{\mathcal{C}}_p$, we can think of a section $s$ of $\mathcal{F}^*(p)$ as $s(z,x) \in \mathbb{C}[[z,x]]$ on $U \times \mathbb{C}$ and as $s(z,x)e^{x/z} \in \mathbb{C}[[z,x]]e^{x/z}$ on $(C - p) \times \mathbb{C}$.
$d/dx$ acts on $s$ just as on the formal oscillating function $s(1/k,x)e^{kx}$.
Thus we can generalize this action to the action of $\Psi$ in a natural way, so that if $P \in \Psi(m)$, then

$$
P \colon \mathcal{F}^*(np) \otimes (\hat{\mathcal{C}}_p \otimes \mathbb{C}[[x]]) \to \mathcal{F}^*((n + m)p) \otimes (\hat{\mathcal{C}}_p \otimes \mathbb{C}[[x]]).
$$

Now, since $H^i(C, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ ($i = 0,1$), we have $H^i(C \times V, \mathcal{F}^*) = 0$ ($i = 0,1$) for a small neighborhood $V$ of 0 in $\mathbb{C}$, so that

$$
H^0(C \times V, \mathcal{F}^*(p)) \xrightarrow{\cong} H^0(C \times V, \mathcal{F}^*(p)/\mathcal{F}^*) \cong H^0(V, \mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{C}}). \tag{#}
$$

Let $s$ be the inverse image of the constant $1 \in H^0(V, \mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{C}})$ under (#), i.e., the section of $\mathcal{F}^*(p)$ on $C \times V$ such that $s = 1 + O(z)$ at $\{p\} \times V$, or equivalently (up to constant) that $(d/dx)s = (z^{-1} + O(z))s$, $s \neq 0$.
This is independent of the choice of $z$ in the fixed equivalence class modulo $m_p^3$.
$(d/dx)^j s$ for $j = 0, \dots, n$ span $H^0(C \times V, \mathcal{F}^*((n + 1)p))$.
Thus for each $f \in \Gamma(C - p, \mathcal{C}_C)$, we have $fs = Ps$ for a unique $P \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{C}})[d/dx] \subset D = \mathbb{C}[[x]][d/dx]$, and we have an inclusion $\Gamma(C - p, \mathcal{C}_C) \hookrightarrow D$ by sending $f$ to $P$.
If we take $S \in 1 + \Psi^-$ such that $s = Se^{kx}$, this can also be written as

$$
P = Sf((d/dx)^{-1})S^{-1} = f(L^{-1}),
$$

where $L = S(d/dx)S^{-1} \in d/dx + \Psi^{-}$.
Defining $R$ to be the image of this inclusion, we observe that this is the desired subring of $D$.

To show Lemma 4, we replace $x$ by time parameters $t \in \mathbb{C}^\infty$ and do the same construction: Define a sheaf $\mathcal{F}^*$ on $C \times \mathbb{C}^\infty$ to be $\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{C}^\infty}$ on $U \times \mathbb{C}^\infty$ and on $(C - p) \times \mathbb{C}^\infty$, but glued to itself on $(U - p) \times \mathbb{C}^\infty$ by the transition function $\exp \xi(t, 1/z)$, where $\xi(t, 1/z) = \sum_{j} t_j z^{-j}$.
Taking a small neighborhood $W$ of 0 in $\mathbb{C}^\infty$, we have a section $s$ of $\mathcal{F}^*(p)$ on $C \times W$ such that $s = 1 + O(z)$ at $\{p\} \in W$.
Consider $s$ also as a section of $\pi_* \mathcal{F}^*(p)$ on $W$, where $\pi \colon C \times \mathbb{C}^\infty \to \mathbb{C}^\infty$.
By the uniqueness of $s$ by (#) under the normalization condition at $p$, we can extend $s$ automatically to a globally holomorphic section of $\pi_* \mathcal{F}^*(p)$ on $\mathbb{C}^\infty - \{t \in \mathbb{C}^\infty; h^i(C, \mathcal{F}_t) \neq 0\}$.
(Actually $s$ is meromorphic on $\mathbb{C}^\infty$ by [26, Proposition 5.1 or 8.6].) Introduce a formal variable $x$ by replacing $t_1$ by $t_1 + x$.
Then $d/dx$, $\partial/\partial t_n$ ($n = 1, 2, \ldots$) act on sections of $\mathcal{F}^*$ on $(C - p) \times \mathbb{C}^\infty$, which give

$$
d/dx \colon \mathcal{F}^* \to \mathcal{F}^*(p), \quad \partial/\partial t_n \colon \mathcal{F}^* \to \mathcal{F}^*(np).
$$

Hence by the same argument as above, for each (general) $t$ we have the maps

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Gamma(C - p, \mathcal{C}_C) \xrightarrow{\cong} R_t \subset D \\
f \mapsto P_f = P_f(t)
\end{array}
\quad \text{defined by} \quad
fs = P_f s,
$$

Jacobian varieties

and

$$
T _ {i} \mathbb {C} ^ {\infty} \rightarrow D
$$

$$
\partial / \partial t _ {n} \mapsto B _ {n} = B _ {n} (t)
$$

$$
\text {by} \quad (\partial / \partial t _ {n}) s = B _ {n} s.
$$

Let $L = S(d / dx)S^{-1}$ with $S = S(t)\in 1 + \Psi^{-}$ defined by $s = Se^{\xi (t,k)}$ .
Since

$$
(\partial / \partial t _ {n}) s = (\partial / \partial t _ {n}) (e ^ {\xi} (1 + O (z))) = (z ^ {- n} + O (z)) s,
$$

we have $B_{n} \equiv L^{n}$ modulo $\Psi^{-}$ , so that $B_{n} = (L^{n})_{+}$ .
Thus $L$ is a solution to the KP hierarchy with $s$ and $S$ the associated wave function and wave operator, respectively.
Moreover, we have $P_{f} = Sf((d / dx)^{-1})S^{-1}$ , so that

$$
R _ {t} = S (t) S (0) ^ {- 1} R _ {0} S (0) S (t) ^ {- 1}
$$

and $R_{t}$ commutes with $L(t)$ .
This shows that $R_{t}$ is the deformation by the KP hierarchy, and hence so is $\mathcal{F}_t$ .
This proves Lemma 4.

To show Lemma 5, assume that $C$ is smooth.
In this case, the above wave function $s$ is called the Baker-Akhiezer function.
The identification $\mathcal{F}(p) \otimes \tilde{C}_p \cong \tilde{C}_p$ is usually chosen so that $\sigma \otimes 1$ corresponds to $s_0^{-1} \sigma$ , where $0 \neq s_0 \in H^0(C, \mathcal{F}(p))$ .
Under this convention, we have the following formula for $s$ on $(C - p) \times \mathbb{C}^\infty$ as a meromorphic function:

$$
s = s (t, q, \zeta) = \exp \left(\sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} t _ {n} \int_ {0} ^ {q} \eta_ {n}\right) \frac {\partial (\bar {A} t + \zeta - w (q)) \partial (\zeta)}{\partial (\zeta - w (q)) \partial (\bar {A} t + \zeta)}, \tag {*}
$$

where i) $\partial (z) = \partial (z,\Omega)$ is Riemann's theta function for $C$

ii) $(t,q,\zeta)\in \mathbb{C}^{\infty}\times (C - p)\times \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}},\partial (\zeta)\neq 0;$
iii) $w\colon C\to \operatorname {Jac}(C)$ is the Abel map defined by

$$
w (q) = \left(\int_ {p} ^ {q} \omega_ {1}, \dots , \int_ {p} ^ {q} \omega_ {\mathrm {g}}\right) \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}} / (\mathbb {Z} ^ {\mathrm {g}} + \Omega \mathbb {Z} ^ {\mathrm {g}}) \cong \operatorname {J a c} (C)
$$

with $(\omega_{1},\ldots ,\omega_{g})$ suitably normalized basis of holomorphic 1-forms on $C$

iv) $\bar{A} = (a_{ij})$ is a $g\times \infty$ -matrix with entries $a_{ij}\in \mathbb{C}$ defined by

$$
\omega_ {i} = \left(a _ {i 1} + a _ {i 2} z + a _ {i 3} z ^ {2} + \dots\right) d z \quad \text {at} \quad p, \quad i = 1, \dots , g,
$$

with $\omega_{i}$ as above, so that in particular we have

a) rank $\bar{A} = g$ , and
b) for a general $p \in C$ , the image of the line $\mathbb{C}a_1$ in $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / (\mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}} + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}})$ is dense, where $a_1 = '(a_{11},\ldots ,a_{g1})$ is the first column of $\bar{A}$ ;

and finally

v) $\eta_{n}$ is the normalized meromorphic 1-form on $C$ with unique pole of order $n + 1$ at $p$ of the form $d(z^{-n}) + O(1)$ , and the integral of $\eta_{n}$ is so defined that

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {q} \eta_ {n} = z ^ {- n} + O (z) \quad \text {at} \quad q = p.
$$

T.
Shiota

The formula $(\ast)$ gives the wave function belonging to $(C,p)$, since for the general $t \in \mathbb{C}^{\infty}$, the right hand side of $(\ast)$ is meromorphic on $C - p$, having $g$ poles independent of $t$, and is $e^{\xi(t,1/z)}(1 + O(z))$ near $p$.
See [15] for details.

Define the quadratic form $Q(t) = \sum Q_{ij}t_it_j$ with coefficients $Q_{ij} \in \mathbb{C}$ by the Laurent expansion

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {q} \eta_ {i} = z ^ {- i} - 2 \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} Q _ {i j} z ^ {j} / j \quad (\text{at } q = p).
$$

By an identity of Riemann, we have $Q_{ij} = Q_{ji}$.
Let

$$
\tau (t, \zeta) = \exp (Q (t)) \vartheta (A t + \zeta). \tag {**}
$$

Then by $(\ast)$ we have

$$
s = e ^ {\xi (t, 1 / z)} \cdot \frac {\tau \left(t _ {1} - z , t _ {2} - z ^ {2} / 2 , t _ {3} - z ^ {3} / 3 , \dots , \zeta\right)}{\tau (t , \zeta)}
$$

up to multiplication by a function of $z$.
Thus $\tau$ is a $\tau$-function belonging to $(C, p)$.
This shows Lemma 5, since the set of solutions belonging to $(C, p)$ consists of a single orbit of the $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$-action, which corresponds to the $\mathbb{C}^g$-action to $\tau$ in the $\zeta$-variable,

$$
\tau (t, \zeta) \mapsto \tau (t, \zeta + \sigma), \quad \sigma \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}};
$$

and in the case $\vartheta (\zeta) = 0$, excluded from $(\ast)$, Formula $(\ast \ast)$ still gives a $\tau$-function which vanishes at $t = 0$.

Remark.
One can choose the local coordinate $z$ at $p$ in such a way that

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {q} \eta_ {1} = z ^ {- 1}
$$

holds.
In this case, by the definition and symmetry of $Q_{ij}$, we have $Q_{1j} = Q_{j1} = 0$ for all $j$, so that $Q(t)$ is independent of $t_1$.

## Appendix 1

Fay's trisecant formula and the KP hierarchy

In Sato's theory of soliton equations [24, 25], the KP hierarchy is naturally identified with dynamical systems on an infinite dimensional Grassmann manifold, and the bilinear identity for a $\tau$-function (8) is interpreted as Plücker's relations [24, Thm. 1], or infinitely many addition formulas [24, Thm. 3].
The simplest addition formula is:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\alpha_ {1} - \alpha_ {0}\right) \left(\alpha_ {3} - \alpha_ {2}\right) \tau \left(t + \left[ \alpha_ {0} \right] + \left[ \alpha_ {1} \right]\right) \tau \left(t + \left[ \alpha_ {2} \right] + \left[ \alpha_ {3} \right]\right) \\ - \left(\alpha_ {2} - \alpha_ {0}\right) \left(\alpha_ {3} - \alpha_ {1}\right) \tau \left(t + \left[ \alpha_ {0} \right] + \left[ \alpha_ {2} \right]\right) \tau \left(t + \left[ \alpha_ {1} \right] + \left[ \alpha_ {3} \right]\right) \\ + \left(\alpha_ {3} - \alpha_ {0}\right) \left(\alpha_ {2} - \alpha_ {1}\right) \tau \left(t + \left[ \alpha_ {0} \right] + \left[ \alpha_ {3} \right]\right) \tau \left(t + \left[ \alpha_ {1} \right] + \left[ \alpha_ {2} \right]\right) = 0, \tag {*} \\ \end{array}
$$

Jacobian varieties

where $\alpha_{j}$ are formal scalar parameters and $[\alpha] = (\alpha, \alpha^{2}/2, \alpha^{3}/3, \ldots)$.
We sketch the proof of $(\ast)$.
In (8), setting

$$
y = (1/2) \left(\left[ \alpha_{0} \right] - \left[ \alpha_{1} \right] - \left[ \alpha_{2} \right] - \left[ \alpha_{3} \right]\right)
$$

and rewriting (8) as a “contour integral” (see [3, §§ 1.5–1.7]), we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
0 = \oint \exp \left(- 2 \sum y_{n} k^{n}\right) \tau \left(t - y - [1/k]\right) \tau \left(t + y + [1/k]\right) dk / 2 \pi i \\
= \oint \frac{(1 - k \alpha_{0})}{(1 - k \alpha_{1}) (1 - k \alpha_{2}) (1 - k \alpha_{3})} \tau (t - y - [1/k]) \tau (t + y + [1/k]) dk / 2 \pi i \\
= \text{“sum of residues at } k = \alpha_{1}^{-1}, \alpha_{2}^{-1}, \alpha_{3}^{-1} \text{”} \\
= \frac{1}{(\alpha_{1} - \alpha_{2}) (\alpha_{2} - \alpha_{3}) (\alpha_{3} - \alpha_{1})} \left[ \begin{array}{l} \text{the left hand side of } (*) \text{ with } t \text{ replaced by} \\ t - ([\alpha_{0}] + [\alpha_{1}] + [\alpha_{2}] + [\alpha_{3}]) / 2 \end{array} \right].
\end{array}
$$

When $\tau$ is defined by (3) for the theta function of a Jacobian and $\bar{A}$ and $Q$ are chosen as in Appendix 0, $(\ast)$ becomes (the formal power series version of) Fay’s trisecant formula: Replacing $\zeta + \bar{A}(t + [\alpha_1] + [\alpha_3])$ with $\zeta$, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\partial \left(\zeta + \int_{\alpha_{3}}^{\alpha_{0}} \omega\right) \partial \left(\zeta + \int_{\alpha_{1}}^{\alpha_{2}} \omega\right) E(\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{0}) E(\alpha_{3}, \alpha_{2}) + \partial \left(\zeta + \int_{\alpha_{1}}^{\alpha_{0}} \omega\right) \partial \left(\zeta + \int_{\alpha_{3}}^{\alpha_{2}} \omega\right) \\
\cdot E(\alpha_{3}, \alpha_{0}) E(\alpha_{2}, \alpha_{1}) = \partial \left(\zeta + \int_{\alpha_{1} + \alpha_{3}}^{\alpha_{0} + \alpha_{2}} \omega\right) \partial (\zeta) E(\alpha_{2}, \alpha_{0}) E(\alpha_{3}, \alpha_{1}),
\end{array}
$$

where $E(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a certain “prime form” [7, 22], and the $\alpha_{j}$ are identified with points on the curve “near” $p$, via the local coordinate $z$.
Therefore, Fay’s trisecant formula follows from, and plays an important role in, the theory of the KP hierarchy.
For example, in our theorem, that (C) implies (B) can be proved by using Gunning’s work [12], which is based on Fay’s formula.
In general, every addition formula for a $\tau$-function obtained by Sato [24, Thm. 3] gives an identity for the theta function of a Jacobian [9, (17)], as is observed by Fay [9, Prop. 1.1].
Those identities were found independently by Fay [7, (43)].

In $(\ast)$, replacing $t$ with $t - ([\alpha_0] + [\alpha_1] + [\alpha_2] + [\alpha_3]) / 2$ and expanding the left hand side into a power series in the $\alpha$ variables, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{i} \left\{ \chi_{I_{0} I_{1}} (\tilde{D}/2) \chi_{I_{2} I_{3}} (-\tilde{D}/2) - \chi_{I_{0} I_{2}} (\tilde{D}/2) \chi_{I_{1} I_{3}} (-\tilde{D}/2) \right. \\
\left. + \chi_{I_{0} I_{3}} (\tilde{D}/2) \chi_{I_{1} I_{2}} (-\tilde{D}/2) \right\} \tau \cdot \tau \alpha_{0}^{I_{0}} \alpha_{1}^{I_{1}} \alpha_{2}^{I_{2}} \alpha_{3}^{I_{3}} = 0,
\end{array}
$$

where $\chi_{ij}$ are Schur functions, defined by

$$
(\beta - \alpha) \exp \left( \sum_{j = 1}^{\infty} (\alpha^{j} + \beta^{j}) t_{j} \right) = \sum_{i, j = 0}^{\infty} \alpha^{i} \beta^{j} \chi_{ij} (t),
$$

and $\tilde{D} = (D_1, D_2/2, D_3/3, \ldots)$ denotes Hirota’s symbols.
Hence $(\ast)$ is equivalent to the family of Hirota’s bilinear equations

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left\{ \chi_{I_{0} I_{1}} (\tilde{D}/2) \chi_{I_{2} I_{3}} (-\tilde{D}/2) - \chi_{I_{0} I_{2}} (\tilde{D}/2) \chi_{I_{1} I_{3}} (-\tilde{D}/2) \right. \\
\left. + \chi_{I_{0} I_{3}} (\tilde{D}/2) \chi_{I_{1} I_{2}} (-\tilde{D}/2) \right\} \tau \cdot \tau = 0, \tag{**}
\end{array}
$$

T.
Shiota

where $l_0, l_1, l_2, l_3 = 0, 1, \ldots$.
In particular, if we set

$$
l _ {0} = l, \quad l _ {1} = 0, \quad l _ {2} = 1, \quad l _ {3} = 2
$$

in $(**)$, or equivalently set $\alpha_{1} = \alpha_{2} = \alpha_{3} = 0$ in $(*)$ after divided by $(\alpha_{1} - \alpha_{2})$ $(\alpha_{2} - \alpha_{3})$ $(\alpha_{3} - \alpha_{1})$, then we obtain the equations used by Arbarello and DeConcini [1, p. 137]:

$$
\left\{p _ {l} (\tilde {D} / 2) D _ {1} - p _ {l - 1} (\tilde {D} / 2) \left(D _ {1} ^ {2} / 2 + D _ {2} / 2\right) \right\} \tau \cdot \tau = 0.
$$

## 3. The Novikov conjecture

In this section, we prove $(\mathrm{A}^{*})\Rightarrow (\mathrm{C})$ by extending the solution

$$
\tau (t, \zeta) = \exp (Q (t)) \vartheta \left(t _ {1} a _ {1} + t _ {2} a _ {2} + t _ {3} a _ {3} + \zeta\right), \quad t = \left(t _ {1}, t _ {2}, t _ {3}\right), \tag {1}
$$

of the KP equation to a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy of the form

$$
\bar {\tau} (t, \zeta) = \exp (Q (t)) \vartheta (\bar {A} t + \zeta), \quad t = \left(t _ {1}, t _ {2}, \dots\right). \tag {3}
$$

Here and in what follows, we simply say "solution (resp.
$\tau$-function, etc.)" instead of saying "family of solutions (resp.
$\tau$-functions, etc.) parametrized by $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$" if no confusion can arise.

Let $\Gamma = \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}} + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}}$, $\Omega \in \mathfrak{H}_{\mathrm{g}}$.
We denote by $\rho(v, \cdot)$ the automorphy factor for Riemann's theta function for $X = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / \Gamma$, so that

$$
\rho (m, z) = 1, \quad \rho (\Omega m, z) = \exp (- \pi i ^ {\mathrm {t}} m \Omega m - 2 \pi i ^ {\mathrm {t}} m z) \quad \text{for} \ m \in \mathbb {Z} ^ {\mathrm {g}},
$$

$$
\vartheta (z + \gamma) = \rho (\gamma , z) \vartheta (z) \quad \text{for} \ \gamma \in \Gamma, \quad z \in \mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}},
$$

and these determine the function $\vartheta$ uniquely up to a constant [21, 22].
We also denote by

$$
G _ {0} = \left\{\exp \left(c _ {0} + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {3} c _ {j} t _ {j}\right); c _ {0}, c _ {j} \in \mathbb {C} \right\}
$$

and

$$
G _ {1} = \left\{\exp \left(c _ {0} + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} c _ {j} t _ {j}\right); c _ {0}, c _ {j} \in \mathbb {C} \right\}
$$

the groups acting on the space of solutions to (2) and the space of $\tau$-functions for the KP hierarchy, respectively.

First assume the condition (Ai) for $X$.
Take $A = (a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3})$ as in (Ai).
The solution (1) to the KP equation satisfies the following:

i) (Regularity).
$\tau(t, \zeta)$ is holomorphic in $(t, \zeta) \in \mathbb{C}^3 \times \mathbb{C}^g$ and $\partial \tau / \partial t_1 \neq 0$ at the general point of the zero locus of $\tau$ which is not contained in $\{(t, \zeta); \zeta + At \in \Sigma\}$, where $\Sigma$ is as in $(\mathrm{A}^* \mathrm{ii})$.

ii) (Quasiperiodicity).
There exists $c(\gamma, \zeta) \in \mathbb{C}^*$ depending on $(\gamma, \zeta) \in \Gamma \times \mathbb{C}^g$ and a homomorphism $\rho' \colon \Gamma \to G_0$ such that

$$
\tau (t, \zeta + \gamma) = c (\gamma , \zeta) \rho^ {\prime} (\gamma) (t) \tau (t, \zeta)
$$

Jacobian varieties

holds for any $\gamma \in \Gamma$, $(t, \zeta) \in \mathbb{C}^3 \times \mathbb{C}^g$.
Moreover, in our case we may take $c = \rho$, the automorphy factor for Riemann's theta function as above.

iii) (Translation equivariance).
There exists a "twisted" homomorphism $\rho_0\colon \mathbb{C}^3\to G_0$, i.e., $\rho_0(s + r)(t) = \rho_0(s)(r + t)\rho_0(r)(t)$, such that

$$
\tau (t + s, \zeta) = \rho_ {0} (s) (t) \tau (t, \zeta + A s)
$$

holds for any $s \in \mathbb{C}^3$, $(t, \zeta) \in \mathbb{C}^3 \times \mathbb{C}^g$.

Indeed, for $\tau$ as in (1) we have

$$
\rho^ {\prime} (\gamma) (t) = \rho (\gamma , A t), \quad \rho_ {0} (s) (t) = \exp (Q (t + s) - Q (t)).
$$

The basic idea of proving $(\mathbf{A}^{*})\Rightarrow (\mathbf{C})$ is to use (i)-(iii) to nicely extend $\tau$ to a $\tau$-function $\overline{\tau}$ for the KP hierarchy, so that $\overline{\tau}$ has the form (3).
Functions of this form are characterized up to some elementary factor by the properties (i)-(iii) analogous to (i)-(iii), i.e., those for which $\mathbb{C}^3$, $A$ and $G_{0}$ in the statement are all replaced by $\mathbb{C}^{\infty}$, $\overline{A} = (a_{1},a_{2},\ldots)$ and $G_{1}$, respectively.
The argument is divided into four steps.

The first step (Sect.
3. - 1) is to observe invariance properties of the locus $\Sigma$.
This enables us to handle $\Sigma$ geometrically when we construct the extension of the solution $\tau$ in the third step.
The argument is based on the formal power series calculus for the KP equation.

In the second step (Sect.
3.0), we observe that the regularity of a $\tau$-function $\overline{\tau}$ follows from the regularity of its restriction $\tau$ to the space of first three time variables, $\{(t_1, t_2, t_3, 0, 0, \ldots)\}$, provided that $\tau$ has at most simple zeros and $\overline{\tau}$ has at most branch points.
This shows an important role of the KP equation in our proof of the theorem: It maintains the regularity of the solution to the KP hierarchy constructed in the next step.

The third step (Sect.
3.1) is the main technical part of the proof: construction of the extension.
Instead of constructing $\overline{\tau}$ directly from $\tau$, we first construct an appropriate wave operator, and observe that the corresponding $\tau$-function $\overline{\tau}$ extends $\tau$ and satisfies the conditions (i) and (ii) above, and some condition which is apparently weaker than (iii).
Each step of the construction procedure is the combination of a local argument and a global one.
The local argument provides some data locally in an appropriate blowing up of the $\zeta$-space, whose existence is guaranteed by the first step.
We also use the result of the second step to maintain the regularity.
Then the global argument glues the local data together to obtain global data.
We use cohomological tools to do this because of the non-uniqueness of local data.

In the last step (Sect.
3.2), observing that for any $t$ the automorphy factor for $\overline{\tau}(t, \cdot)$ represents the principal polarization of $X$, we determine the explicit formula for $\overline{\tau}$, which is a slight modification of (3), and prove (iii).
Thus the corresponding solution to the KP equation is finite dimensional with dimension $= \operatorname{rank}(\overline{A})$.
Then the Burchnell-Chaundy-Krichever theory applies to show that this common number is $g$.
$\overline{\tau}$ will be first constructed as a formal power series in $t$ and holomorphic in $\zeta$.
It is then automatically extended globally in $t \in \mathbb{C}^{\infty}$ by (iii).

T.
Shiota

# 3. - 1. Anomalous solutions to the KP equation

The purpose of this section is to prove Lemma 7 stated below.
Fixing the projection $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}\to X = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / \Gamma$, $\Gamma = \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}} + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}}$, we identify a subset (resp.
complex analytic subspace) of $X$ with the corresponding $\Gamma$-invariant subset (resp.
complex analytic subspace) of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ if no confusion can arise.
We also identify a vector $v\in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ with the corresponding translation invariant vector field on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$.

Definition.
Let $v \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$, and let $S$ be a complex analytic subspace of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ which is invariant under the flow associated to $v$.
$S$ may not be reduced.
Let $\pi \colon \mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\pi} \to \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ be the blowing up along $S$.
A meromorphic function $f$ on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is called $(v, S)$-microrregular if $f \circ \pi$ is a meromorphic function on $\mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ whose restriction to any line parallel to $v$ is well-defined meromorphic and is not identically $\infty$.

Lemma 7. Assume the condition (A i) of the Theorem.
Let $\tau$ and $(a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3})$ be as in (A i), and let $\Sigma$ be as in $(\mathbf{A}^{*}\mathrm{ii})$, i.e.,

$$
\Sigma = \bigcap_{c \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}} (\Theta + c a_{1}).
$$

Then there exists a $\Gamma$-invariant complex analytic subspace $\Sigma_{1}$ of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ whose underlying topological space is $\Sigma$, such that

(a) $\Sigma_{1}$ is invariant under the flows associated to $a_1$ and $a_2$;
(b) if $\Sigma$ has codimension $\geq 2$ in $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ (as in $(\mathbf{A}^{*}\mathbf{ii})$) or if $\Sigma$ is invariant under the $a_3$-flow, then $\Sigma_{1}$ is invariant under the $a_3$-flow; and
(c) if $\Sigma_{1}$ is invariant under the $a_3$-flow, then

$$
(\partial / \partial t_{j}) \log \tau (t, \zeta), \quad j = 1, 2, 3,
$$

are $(a_{1},\Sigma_{1})$-microrregular in the $\zeta$-variables for any $t$.

Moreover, we may take $\Sigma_{1}$ in such a way that

(d) the blowing up $\mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ along $\Sigma_{1}$ is non-singular.

Proof.
The last assertion (d) follows from Hironaka's resolution of singularities [13].
Indeed, if $\Sigma_{1}$ satisfies the assertions (a)-(c) of the lemma and if the blowing up $\mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ along $\Sigma_{1}$ is singular, then one can find a blowing up $\mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{x}}$ of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ with appropriate invariance properties such that $\mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is non-singular.
Replacing $\Sigma_{1}$ by the center of the blowing up $\mathring{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}\to \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$, we have the assertions (a)-(d).

Therefore, we have only to find $\Sigma_{1}$ which satisfies the assertions (a)-(c) of the lemma.
We define $\Sigma_{1}$ to be a complex analytic subspace of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ which corresponds to the sheaf of ideals $\mathcal{I}$ generated by the ideal sheaves $\mathcal{I}_{\Theta + ca_1}$ of all the translates $\Theta + ca_1$, $c \in \mathbb{C}$, of $\Theta$ in the $a_1$-direction.
In this case, (c) follows from the definition of $\Sigma_{1}$ and the assertion (a), while (a) and (b) are naturally reduced to some analytic properties of the KP equation.
So we first observe those properties and prove (a)-(c), assuming the basic facts stated in two lemmas.
After that we prove the lemmas by formal power series calculus for the KP equation.

Jacobian varieties

A $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy cannot vanish identically on a line parallel to the $t_1$-axis [26, Prop. 8.6], [25, Sects. 9, 12], but a (non-zero) solution to the KP equation may vanish on such a line.
We call such a solution anomalous.
A solution is called normal if it is not anomalous.
These notions are also localized in an obvious way.
Anomalous solutions are obviously not extendible to $\tau$-functions for the whole KP hierarchy, so that it is natural to ask how much they are different from the normal solutions.
Let us start with a simple example.
Since

$$
(D_1^4 + 3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)fg \cdot fg = f^2(D_1^4 + 3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)g \cdot g
$$

holds for any $f = f(t_3)$ and $g = g(t_1, t_2, t_3)$, $fg$ solves the KP equation if $g$ does (and vice versa, unless $f \equiv 0$).
So if $f$ vanishes somewhere, then such a solution is anomalous.
The following lemma shows that this is the general form of an anomalous solution if the parameter dependence is not considered.

**Lemma A.** Let $\tau(t)$, $t = (t_1, t_2, t_3)$, be a formal power series solution to the KP equation $(D_1^4 + 3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)\tau \cdot \tau = 0$, such that

$$
\tau \not\equiv 0, \quad \tau(t_1, 0, 0) \equiv 0.
$$

Then there exist a normal solution $\overline{\tau}(t) \in \mathbb{C}[[t_1, t_2, t_3]]$ to the KP equation, i.e., $\overline{\tau}(t_1, 0, 0) \not\equiv 0$, and an integer $m \geq 1$, such that

$$
\tau(t) = t_3^m \overline{\tau}(t).
$$

The next step is to generalize this observation to the case where the solution $\tau$ depends on parameters.
This is not quite trivial.
Indeed, a deformation of an anomalous solution may not be anomalous.
The simplest example is:

$$
\tau(t, \lambda) = \lambda^2 t_1 + 2\lambda t_2 + 3t_3, \quad \lambda \in \mathbb{C},
$$

which is anomalous at $\lambda = 0$ and normal elsewhere, and whose obvious extension to a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy

$$
\overline{\tau}(t, \lambda) = \lambda^2 t_1 + 2\lambda t_2 + 3t_3 + 4\lambda^{-1}t_4 + \dots + n\lambda^{3-n}t_n + \dots
$$

becomes singular at $\lambda = 0$.
Less trivial examples can be constructed algebraically.
Thus in general the anomaly of a solution to the KP equation is not stable under a deformation.
On the other hand, for the KdV equation, i.e., when $\tau(t, \lambda)$ is independent of $t_2$, the anomaly of a solution is stable.
So we generalize Lemma A by adding some conditions on the $t_2$-dependence of $\tau$ as follows:

**Lemma B.** Let $\tau = \tau(t, \zeta, \lambda)$, $t = (t_1, t_2, t_3)$, be a family of solutions to the KP equation with parameters $\zeta$ and $\lambda$.
Assume the following conditions:

i) $\tau$ is holomorphic in $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^h$, $h \geq 0$, and is a formal power series in $t$ and $\lambda$ (a scalar parameter),

ii) $\tau$ is quasiperiodic and translation equivariant in the $(t_2, \zeta)$-variables with multiplicative factors independent of $\lambda$.
More specifically, there exist a lattice $\Gamma$

T.
Shiota

in $\mathbb{C}^h$, a vector $a_2 \in \mathbb{C}^h$ and multiplicative factors $\rho$ depending on $(\gamma, \zeta) \in \Gamma \times \mathbb{C}^h$ and $\sigma$ on $s \in \mathbb{C}$:

$$
\rho(\gamma, \zeta, \cdot), \quad \sigma(s, \cdot) \in G_0 = \begin{bmatrix} \text{group of functions} \\ \exp\left(c_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{3} c_j t_j\right); \, c_0, c_j \in \mathbb{C} \end{bmatrix},
$$

such that $\Gamma$, $a_2$, $\rho$, $\sigma$ are independent of $\lambda$, $\mathbb{C}a_2 \bmod \Gamma$ is dense in $\mathbb{C}^h / \Gamma$ and for any $\gamma \in \Gamma$ and $\overline{s} = (0, s, 0)$ we have

$$
\tau(t, \zeta + \gamma, \lambda) = \rho(\gamma, \zeta, t) \, \tau(t, \zeta, \lambda),
$$

$$
\tau(t + \overline{s}, \zeta, \lambda) = \sigma(s, t) \, \tau(t, \zeta + s a_2, \lambda).
$$

Then if $\tau(\cdot, \zeta_0, 0) \neq 0$ and $\tau((\cdot, 0, 0), \zeta_0, 0) \equiv 0$ for some $\zeta_0 \in \mathbb{C}^h$, then there exist $f(t_3, \lambda) \in \mathbb{C}[[t_3, \lambda]]$ and a solution $\overline{\tau} = \overline{\tau}(t, \zeta, \lambda)$ to the KP equation which satisfies the Conditions (i) and (ii) above, such that

$$
f(t_3, 0) = t_3^m \quad \text{for some } m \geq 1,
$$

$$
\overline{\tau}(\cdot, \zeta, 0) \quad \text{is normal} \quad (\text{at } t = 0) \quad \text{for any } \zeta,
$$

and

$$
\tau(t, \zeta, \lambda) = f(t_3, \lambda) \, \overline{\tau}(t, \zeta, \lambda).
$$

Thus if, moreover, $\tau$ is holomorphic in $t_3$ and $\lambda$ near $t_3 = 0$, $\lambda = 0$, then the anomaly of $\tau$ at $t_3 = 0$, $\lambda = 0$ implies that for any small enough $\lambda$, $\tau$ is anomalous at some $t_3$.
Note also that $h = 0$ corresponds to the KdV case.

Now we prove (a)-(c), assuming Lemmas A and B.
The assertion (a) follows from Lemma A.
Next, let

$$
V = \mathbb{C}a_1 + \mathbb{C}a_2 + \mathbb{C}a_3, \quad \Sigma' = \bigcap \{\Theta + v; v \in V\}.
$$

Define $\Sigma_1'$ to be the complex analytic subspace of $\mathbb{C}^g$ (or of $X$) whose underlying topological space is $\Sigma'$ and which corresponds to the sheaf of ideals $\mathcal{I}'$ generated by the ideal sheaves of all the translates $\Theta + v$ of $\Theta$ by $v \in V$.
Let $\pi: \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g \to \mathbb{C}^g$ be the blowing up with center $\Sigma_1'$.
Denoting the closure of $\Gamma + V$ in $\mathbb{C}^g$ by $\overline{\Gamma + V}$, we see that $\Sigma_1'$ is $\overline{\Gamma + V}$-invariant.
Hence $\overline{\Gamma + V}$ acts on $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$, $\pi$ is $\overline{\Gamma + V}$-equivariant, and

$$
\tilde{\tau}(t, \tilde{\zeta}) = \tau(t, \pi(\tilde{\zeta}))
$$

decomes a quasiperiodic, translation-equivariant solution to the KP equation, after extending these notions in an obvious way.
Let $\tilde{V}$ be the connected component of $\overline{\Gamma + V}$ containing the origin.
This acts on $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$.
Let $W$ be another vector subspace of $\mathbb{C}^g$ satisfying $\mathbb{C}^g = \tilde{V} \oplus W$.
By this decomposition we denote $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$ by $(\zeta', \zeta'')$ and $\tilde{\zeta} \in \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$ by $(\zeta', \tilde{\zeta}'')$, with $\zeta' \in \tilde{V}$, $\zeta'' \in W$ and $\tilde{\zeta}'' \in \tilde{W} = \pi^{-1}(W)$.
Then $\tilde{\tau}(t, \tilde{\zeta}) = \tilde{\tau}(t, \zeta', \tilde{\zeta}'')$ can be thought of as a family of $(\Gamma \cap \tilde{V})$-quasiperiodic, translation-equivariant solutions on $\mathbb{C}^3 \times \tilde{V}$ parametrized by $\tilde{\zeta}'' \in \tilde{W}$.
Note that $\tilde{\tau}(\cdot, \cdot, \tilde{\zeta}'') \equiv 0$ if $\tilde{\zeta}'' \in \pi^{-1}(\Sigma' \cap W)$.
But we can modify the solution $\tilde{\tau}$ near such $\tilde{\zeta}''$ and obtain a non-vanishing family of solutions which only differs from $\tilde{\tau}$ by multiplication by a function of $\tilde{\zeta}''$ as follows.
Since $\pi$ is the blowing up of $\mathcal{I}'$,

Jacobian varieties

the inverse image ideal sheaf $\hat{\mathcal{I}}' = \pi^{-1}\mathcal{I}' \cdot \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C}^g}$ is an invertible sheaf.
Noting that $\hat{\mathcal{I}}'$ is also $\bar{V}$-invariant, for each $\bar{\zeta}_0'' \in \bar{W}$ we can find a $\bar{V}$-invariant regular function $f = f(\bar{\zeta}'')$ generating $\hat{\mathcal{I}}'$ near $\bar{V} \times \{\bar{\zeta}_0''\}$.
Defining $\tilde{\tau}' = \tilde{\tau}'(t, \zeta', \bar{\zeta}'')$ by

$$
\tilde{\tau}'(t, \zeta', \bar{\zeta}'') = f(\bar{\zeta}'')^{-1} \tilde{\tau}(t, \zeta', \bar{\zeta}''),
$$

we obtain a family of solutions to the KP equation defined near $\bar{\zeta}'' = \bar{\zeta}_0''$ which is quasiperiodic and translation equivariant in the $(t, \zeta')$-variables and which satisfies $\tilde{\tau}'(\cdot, \cdot, \bar{\zeta}') \neq 0$ for any $\bar{\zeta}'$, so by density

$$
\tilde{\tau}'(\cdot, \zeta', \bar{\zeta}'') \neq 0 \quad \text{for any } (\zeta', \bar{\zeta}'').
$$

Using this, we call $\tilde{\tau}$ anomalous at $\bar{\zeta} = (\zeta', \bar{\zeta}'')$ if $\tilde{\tau}'(\cdot, \zeta', \bar{\zeta}'')$ is anomalous (at some $t$, not necessarily $t_3 = 0$).
The definition is independent of the choice of $\tilde{\tau}'$.

**Claim.** The set $\{\bar{\zeta} \in \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g; \tilde{\tau} \text{ is anomalous at } \bar{\zeta}\}$ is either empty or $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$.

**Proof.** It suffices to show that this set is open and closed.
The openness follows from Lemma B: Let $Z = \mathbb{C}a_2$, and let $\bar{Z}$ be the connected component of the closure of $\Gamma + Z$ containing the origin.
We have $\bar{Z} \subset \bar{V} \subset \mathbb{C}^g$.
Changing the coordinates if necessary, we assume $\bar{Z} = \mathbb{C}^h \times \{(0)\}$.
Let $B = \{(0)\} \times \mathbb{C}^{g - h} \subset \mathbb{C}^g$, so that $B$ is perpendicular to $\bar{Z}$ in $\mathbb{C}^g$ with respect to the standard bilinear inner product $v \cdot w = \sum v_j w_j$, and $\mathbb{C}^g = \bar{Z} \oplus B$.
Corresponding to this decomposition, we denote $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$ by $(\lambda', \lambda'')$ and $\bar{\zeta} \in \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$ by $(\lambda', \lambda'')$ with $\lambda' \in \bar{Z}$, $\lambda'' \in B$, $\lambda'' \in \tilde{B} = \pi^{-1}(B)$.
Then the modified family of solutions $\tilde{\tau}'$ introduced above is $(\Gamma \cap \bar{Z})$-quasiperiodic and translation equivariant in $(t_2, \lambda')$ with multiplicative factors independent of $\lambda''$.
The last assertion follows from the orthogonality of $\bar{Z}$ and $B$, and our definition of Riemann's theta function.
Noting that $\mathbb{C}a_2 \bmod (\Gamma \cap \bar{Z})$ is dense in $\bar{Z} / (\Gamma \cap \bar{Z})$, we apply Lemma B to observe that if $\tilde{\tau}'$ is anomalous at some $(\lambda_0', \lambda_0'')$, then it is anomalous at $(\lambda', \lambda'')$ for any $\lambda'$, and $\lambda''$ near $\lambda_0''$.
This proves the openness.

Next, the closedness follows from the compactness of $\bar{V} / (\Gamma \cap \bar{V})$, i.e., if $\tilde{\tau}'$ is normal at some $\bar{\zeta} \in \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$ and all $t \in \mathbb{C}^3$, then by compactness it is uniformly normal and so it is normal at nearby $\bar{\zeta}$ and all $t$.
This proves the claim.

Let $\Sigma$ and $\Sigma_1$ be as before.
Noting that $\Sigma_1 \neq \Sigma_1'$ if and only if $\tilde{\tau}$ is anomalous at some $\bar{\zeta}$, we observe the equivalence of the following conditions (i)-(iv) for $\tau (\neq 0)$:

(i) $\Sigma_1' \neq \Sigma_1$;
(ii) $\tilde{\tau}$ is anomalous at some (hence any) $\bar{\zeta} \in \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$;
(iii) $\Sigma + \mathbb{C}a_3 = \mathbb{C}^g$;
(iv) $\Sigma' \neq \Sigma$.

(Clearly $(\mathrm{iv}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{i}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{ii}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{iii})$).
Since $\Sigma' + \mathbb{C}a_3 = \Sigma'$ cannot be the whole space $\mathbb{C}^g$, we have $(\mathrm{iii}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{iv})$.) In particular, $\Sigma + \mathbb{C}a_3 \neq \mathbb{C}^g$ or $\Sigma' = \Sigma$ implies $\Sigma_1' = \Sigma_1$.
This proves (b).
To prove (c), assume that $\Sigma_1$ is $a_3$-invariant.
Then it is $V$-invariant, so that $\Sigma_1 = \Sigma_1'$ and $\tilde{\tau}$ is normal.
Taking $\tilde{\tau}'$ as above, we observe that the restriction of $(\partial/\partial t_j)\log \tilde{\tau}'$ to any line parallel to $a_1$ are well-defined meromorphic.
Since $(\partial/\partial t_j)\log \tilde{\tau}' = (\partial/\partial t_j)\log \tilde{\tau}$, we have (c).
This proves Lemma 7 modulo Lemmas A and B.

T.
Shiota

Proof of Lemma A.
Define the weighted degree $\mathrm{wt}(t^{\alpha})$ of a monomial $t^\alpha = t_1^{\alpha_1}t_2^{\alpha_2}t_3^{\alpha_3}$ by $\mathrm{wt}(t^{\alpha}) = \alpha_{2} + 2\alpha_{3}$.
For $\tau = \tau (t)$ as in Lemma A, we write

$$
\tau (t) = \sum_ {i = j} ^ {\infty} \tau_ {i} (t), \quad \tau_ {j} (t) \neq 0, \tag {a}
$$

with $\tau_{i}$ weighted homogeneous power series

$$
\sum_ {n = 0} ^ {[ i / 2 ]} p _ {i, n} \left(t _ {1}\right) t _ {2} ^ {i - 2 n} t _ {3} ^ {n}, \quad p _ {i, n} \left(t _ {1}\right) \in \mathbb {C} [ [ t _ {1} ] ],
$$

of weighted degree $i = j, j + 1, \ldots$.
We have $j \neq 0$ by assumption.
We prove that $j$ is even, $j = 2m$, and

$$
p _ {i, n} \left(t _ {1}\right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } n &lt; m \quad \text { and all } i. \tag {b}
$$

Substituting (a) into the KP equation, we obtain a set of weighted homogeneous equations

$$
D _ {1} ^ {4} \left(\sum_ {p + q = r} \tau_ {p} \cdot \tau_ {q}\right) + \left(3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \left(\sum_ {p + q = r + 2} \tau_ {p} \cdot \tau_ {q}\right) = 0 \tag {c}
$$

for $r\geq 2j - 2$.
In particular, we have

$$
\left(3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \tau_ {j} \cdot \tau_ {j} = 0. \tag {d}
$$

I.
We first observe that $j$ is even, $= 2m$, and $p_{2m,m} \neq 0$.
Let $m, k$ be such that $j = 2m + k$ and

$$
\tau_ {j} (t) = p _ {j, m} \left(t _ {1}\right) t _ {1} ^ {k} t _ {3} ^ {m} + \dots , \quad p _ {j, m} \neq 0.
$$

Here and in what follows, we denote by “...” lower order terms in $t_3$.
Substituting this formula into (d), we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \left(3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \left(p _ {j, m} \left(t _ {1}\right) t _ {2} ^ {k} t _ {3} ^ {m} + \dots\right) \cdot \left(p _ {j, m} \left(t _ {1}\right) t _ {2} ^ {k} t _ {3} ^ {m} + \dots\right) \\ = - 6 k p _ {j, m} \left(t _ {1}\right) ^ {2} t _ {2} ^ {2 k - 2} t _ {3} ^ {2 m} + \dots . \\ \end{array}
$$

Hence $k = 0$ and $j = 2m$ as claimed.

II.
We probe (b) by induction on $i(\geq 2m)$.
First, if (b) was not true for $i = 2m$, then there would exist $n &lt; m$ such that

$$
\tau_ {2 m} (t) = p _ {2 m, m} \left(t _ {1}\right) t _ {3} ^ {m} + p _ {2 m, n} \left(t _ {1}\right) t _ {2} ^ {2 (m - n)} t _ {3} ^ {n} + \dots , \quad p _ {2 m, n} \neq 0.
$$

Substituting this into (d), we would have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \left(3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \left(p _ {2 m, m} t _ {3} ^ {m} \cdot p _ {2 m, m} t _ {3} ^ {m} + 2 p _ {2 m, m} t _ {3} ^ {m} \cdot p _ {2 m, n} t _ {2} ^ {2 (m - n)} t _ {3} ^ {n} + \dots\right) \\ = \left(3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \left(2 p _ {2 m, m} t _ {3} ^ {m} \cdot p _ {2 m, n} t _ {2} ^ {2 (m - n)} t _ {3} ^ {n} + \dots\right) \\ = 6 D _ {2} ^ {2} p _ {2 m, m} t _ {3} ^ {m} \cdot p _ {2 m, n} t _ {2} ^ {2 (m - n)} t _ {3} ^ {n} + \dots \\ = 1 2 (m - n) (2 (m - n) - 1) p _ {2 m, m} p _ {2 m, n} t _ {2} ^ {2 (m - n - 1)} t _ {3} ^ {m + n} + \dots , \\ \end{array}
$$

Jacobian varieties

a contradiction.
Here we used the identity

(e) $(3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)(t_3^m f(t) \cdot t_3^m g(t)) = t_3^{2m}(3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)f(t) \cdot g(t),$

in which both sides vanish if $f$ and $g$ are independent of $t_2, t_3$.
Thus we have (b) for $i = 2m$, and

$$
\tau_{2m}(t) = p_{2m,m}(t_1)t_3^m.
$$

Next, for $k \geq 2m$ we assume (b) for $i = 2m, 2m + 1, \ldots, k$ and prove it for $i = k + 1$.
By (c) we have

(f) $2(3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)\tau_{2m} \cdot \tau_{k+1}$

$$
= -D_1^4\left(\sum_{p + q = 2m + k - 1} \tau_p \cdot \tau_q\right) - (3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)\left(\sum_{\substack{p + q = 2m + k + 1 \\ 2m + 1 \leq p, q \leq k}} \tau_p \cdot \tau_q\right).
$$

By inductive hypothesis, $\tau_{s}(t)$ is divisible by $t_3^m$ if $2m \leq s \leq k$, so by (e), the last term on the right hand side of (f) is divisible by $t_3^{2m}$.
Since the same is true for the first term, it is also true for the left hand side of (f).
But

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)\tau_{2m} \cdot \tau_{k+1} \\
= \sum_n (3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3)p_{2m,m}t_3^m \cdot p_{k+1,n}t_2^{k+1-2n}t_3^n \\
= \sum_n \left\{3(k + 1 - 2n)(k - 2n)p_{2m,m}p_{k+1,n} - 4(m - n - 1)\right. \\
\left. \cdot \left(D_1 p_{2m,m} \cdot p_{k+1,n+1}\right) \right\} t_2^{k-2n-1} t_3^{m+n},
\end{aligned}
$$

so that the coefficient of $t_2^{k-2n-1} t_3^{m+n}$ in this last formula must vanish for $n &lt; m$.
Hence

$$
p_{k+1,m-1} = 0,
$$

and

$$
p_{k+1,n+1} = 0 \Rightarrow p_{k+1,n} = 0 \quad \text{for } n &lt; m.
$$

Thus $p_{k+1,n} = 0$ for $n &lt; m$.
This proves (b) for $i = k + 1$.
QED.

Proof of Lemma B.
For $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^h$, let

$$
m(\zeta) = \sup \{m \in \mathbb{Z}; t_3^m \text{ divides } \tau(t, \zeta, 0) \text{ in } \mathbb{C}[[t_1, t_2, t_3]]\},
$$

so that $m(\zeta) = 0$ if $\tau(\cdot, \zeta, 0)$ is a normal solution; $m(\zeta) = \infty$ if $\tau(\cdot, \zeta, 0) \equiv 0$; and by Lemma A, $1 \leq m(\zeta) &lt; \infty$ if $\tau(\cdot, \zeta, 0)$ is anomalous.
By the translation equivariance of $\tau$, $m(\zeta)$ is constant on each translate of the line $\mathbb{C}a_2$.
Then by the quasiperiodicity of $\tau$ and the density of $\mathbb{C}a_2 \bmod \Gamma$ in $\mathbb{C}^h / \Gamma$, we observe that $m(\zeta)$ is independent of $\zeta$, so we denote it by $m$.
By the assumption that $\tau(\cdot, \zeta, 0)$ is anomalous for some $\zeta$, we have $1 \leq m &lt; \infty$.
Hence

$$
\tau(t, \zeta, 0) = t_3^m \bar{\tau}_0(t, \zeta)
$$

with $\bar{\tau}_0(\cdot, \zeta)$ normal for any $\zeta$.
Next, starting with this $\bar{\tau}_0$ and $f_0 = t_3^m$, we define $f_j = f_j(t_3)$ and $\bar{\tau}_j = \bar{\tau}_j(t, \zeta)$ inductively for $j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, such that

T.
Shiota

$$
f(t_3, \lambda) = \sum_j f_j(t_3) \lambda^j \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta, \lambda) = \sum_j \bar{\tau}_j(t, \zeta) \lambda^j
$$

give the desired decomposition $\tau = f\bar{\tau}$ of $\tau$.
For $l \geq 1$, suppose that $f_j$ and $\bar{\tau}_j$ are already chosen for $j \leq l-1$ in such a way that

(a) $f_j$ only depend on $t_3$,
(b) $\bar{\tau}_j$ satisfy the same quasiperiodicity and translation equivariance conditions as $\tau$ does, and
(c) $\tau \equiv \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} f_j \lambda^j\right) \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) \bmod \lambda^l$.

Note that (b) follows from (a), (c) and the assumption that the multiplicative factors for the quasiperiodicity and translation equivariance of $\tau$ are independent of $\lambda$.
Define $\tau' = \tau'(t, \zeta)$ by

$$
\tau \equiv \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} f_j \lambda^j\right) \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) + \tau' \lambda^l \bmod \lambda^{l+1}. \tag{d}
$$

Let $\varphi = \tau' / \bar{\tau}_0$.
Since $\bar{\tau}_0|_{t_3=0} \neq 0$ by normality, $\varphi$ is a formal power series in $t_3$ with coefficients depending on $(t_1, t_2, \zeta)$:

$$
\varphi = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} c_j(t_1, t_2, \zeta) t_3^j.
$$

We define $f_l$ and $\bar{\tau}_l$ by

$$
f_l = \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} c_j t_3^j, \quad \bar{\tau}_l = \sum_{j=m}^{\infty} \bar{\tau}_0 c_j t_3^{j-m}.
$$

We have $\tau' = f_l \bar{\tau}_0 + t_3^m \bar{\tau}_l$, so that

$$
\tau \equiv \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l} f_j \lambda^j\right) \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) \bmod \lambda^{l+1}.
$$

To observe that this is the desired decomposition modulo $\lambda^{l+1}$, we have only to observe the following

**Claim.** $c_0, \ldots, c_{m-1}$ are constant in all the variables $(t_1, t_2, \zeta)$.

**Proof.** By definition, $\tau'$ satisfies the same quasiperiodicity and translation-equivariance as $\tau$, and as $\bar{\tau}_0$.
Thus $\varphi$, and all the $c_j$ are $\Gamma$-periodic and satisfy the conditions

$$
\varphi(t, \zeta) = \varphi(t_1, 0, t_3; \zeta + t_2 a_2),
$$

(e) $c_j(t_1, t_2, \zeta) = c_j(t_1, 0, \zeta + t_2 a_2).$

Now we use the KP equation.
Denote the Hirota operator $D_1^4 + 3D_2^2 - 4D_1D_3$ by $\mathbf{P}$.
Substituting (d) into the KP equation $\mathbf{P} \tau \cdot \tau = 0$ and using the identity $\mathbf{P} f g \cdot f g = f^2 \mathbf{P} g \cdot g$ for $f = f(t_3)$ and $g = g(t_1, t_2, t_3)$, we obtain

$$
(f_0^2 + \ldots) \mathbf{P} \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) \cdot \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) + 2 \lambda^l \mathbf{P} \tau' \cdot f_0 \bar{\tau}_0 \equiv 0 \bmod \lambda^{l+1}.
$$

Jacobian varieties

Here we used $l \geq 1$.
Since $f_0^2 \neq 0$, this implies that

$$
\mathbf{P} \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) \cdot \left(\sum_{j=0}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \lambda^j\right) \equiv 0 \bmod \lambda^l,
$$

and that

$$
\mathbf{P} \tau' \cdot f_0 \bar{\tau}_0 = -\frac{1}{2} f_0^2 \mathbf{P} \left(\sum_{j=1}^{l-1} \bar{\tau}_j \cdot \bar{\tau}_{l-j}\right) \equiv 0 \bmod f_0^2.
$$

By using $\tau' = \sum c_j t_3^j \bar{\tau}_0$ and $f_0 = t_3^m$, we rewrite this equation as

$$
\sum \mathbf{P} c_j t_3^j \bar{\tau}_0 \cdot t_3^m \bar{\tau}_0 = \sum (\mathbf{P} c_j \bar{\tau}_0 \cdot \bar{\tau}_0) t_3^{j+m} - 4(j-m) \sum \frac{\partial c_j}{\partial t_1} \bar{\tau}_0^2 t_3^{j+m-1} \equiv 0 \bmod t_3^{2m}.
$$

Hence

$$
\frac{\partial c_0}{\partial t_1} \bar{\tau}_0^2 = 0, \tag{f}
$$

$$
\mathbf{P} c_{j-1} \bar{\tau}_0 \cdot \bar{\tau}_0 + 4(m-j) \frac{\partial c_j}{\partial t_1} \bar{\tau}_0^2 = 0, \quad 1 \leq j \leq m.
$$

Now we use these equations to show that $c_j = \text{const.}$, $0 \leq j \leq m-1$.
Suppose $j = 0$ or $c_{j-1} = \text{const.}$.
By using (f) and the fact that $\bar{\tau}_0^2 \neq 0$, $m-j \neq 0$ and $\mathbf{P} \bar{\tau}_0 \cdot \bar{\tau}_0 = 0$, we have

$$
\partial c_j / \partial t_1 \equiv 0.
$$

This implies that $c_j$ is holomorphic because the locus of poles of $c_j$ is contained in the locus of zeros of $\bar{\tau}_0|_{t_3=0}$, and the latter does not contain any line parallel to $\partial/\partial t_1$ because of the normality of $\bar{\tau}_0$.
But $c_j$ is also $\Gamma$-periodic and satisfies (e), so that $c_j = \text{const.}$.
This proves the claim by induction on $j$.
QED.

## 3.0. Regularity of $\tau$-functions

We observe the regularity property of a $\tau$-function at a good point of its zero locus.
This will maintain the regularity of $\bar{\tau}$ and the single-valuedness of approximate wave operators to be constructed in the next step.
The argument is simple, but this is the place where we use the main condition (A i) of the Theorem in the most essential way in its proof.

For a small $\varepsilon &gt; 0$, let $U = \{t_1 \in \mathbb{C} : |t_1| &lt; \varepsilon\}$, $V = U - \{0\}$ and $W =$ (the universal covering space of $V$), with natural projection $\pi: W \to V$ and inclusion $\iota: V \to U$.
A function $f \in \mathcal{O}(W) = \Gamma(W, \mathcal{O}_W)$ is called single-valued holomorphic (SVH for short) on $U$ if there exists a holomorphic function $g$ on $U$ such that $f = \pi^* \iota^* g$, in which case we identify $f$ and $g$.
The definition is generalized to the case where $f$ and $g$ depend on other parameters formally or holomorphically.
Defining the vector field $\partial/\partial t_1$ on $W$ to be the lifting of $\partial/\partial t_1$ on $V$, we consider $W \times_{\mathbb{C}} \operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3, \ldots]]$ as a time parameter space for the KP hierarchy.
In what follows, $\otimes$ will mean completed tensor product, e.g.,

$$
\mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3, \ldots]] = \varprojlim \mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3, \ldots]] / (t_1^n, \ldots, t_{n-1}^n, t_n, t_{n+1}, \ldots).
$$

T.
Shiota

Lemma 8. Let $\bar{\tau} \in \mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3, \ldots]]$ be a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy.
Suppose that the restriction $\tau$ of $\bar{\tau}$ to the $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$-space is SVH on $U$ and satisfies $\tau(0) = 0$, $(\partial \tau / \partial t_1)(0) \neq 0$.
Then $\bar{\tau}$ itself is SVH on $U$.

Proof.
For $k \geq 4$, $l \geq 1$, let $I_k^l$ be the ideal of $\mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3, \ldots]]$ generated by $t_k^l$ and $t_{k+m}$, $m = 1, 2, \ldots$, so that $\bigcap_i I_k^l = I_{k+1}^1$, $\bigcap_k I_k^l = 0$.
By assumption, $\bar{\tau} \bmod I_4^1$ is SVH.
Suppose that $\bar{\tau} \bmod I_k^l$ is SVH for some $k \geq 4$, $l \geq 1$.
We prove the lemma by induction on $(k, l)$.
Setting

$$
\bar {\tau} \equiv \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {l} \tau_ {j} \left(t _ {1}, \dots , t _ {k - 1}\right) t _ {k} ^ {j} \bmod I _ {k} ^ {l + 1}, \tag {12}
$$

we show that $\tau_{i}$ is SVH on $U$.
By assumption, $\tau_0,\ldots ,\tau_{l - 1}$ are SVH on $U$, and $\tau_0(0) = 0$, $(\partial \tau_0 / \partial t_1)(0)\neq 0$.
By (9), $\bar{\tau}$ satisfies Hirota's bilinear differential equation of the form

$$
\left(D _ {1} D _ {k} - P \left(D _ {1}, \dots , D _ {k - 1}\right)\right) \bar {\tau} \cdot \bar {\tau} = 0, \tag {13}
$$

where $P(X) = P(X_{1},\ldots ,X_{k - 1})\in \mathbb{Q}[X_{1},\ldots ,X_{k - 1}]$.
Substituting (12) into (13) and computing the coefficient of $t_k^{l - 1}$, we get

$$
\sum_ {i + j = l - 1} \left\{2 (i + 1) \left(\frac {\partial \tau_ {i + 1}}{\partial t _ {1}} \tau_ {j} - \tau_ {i + 1} \frac {\partial \tau_ {j}}{\partial t _ {1}}\right) - P (D) \tau_ {i} \cdot \tau_ {j} \right\} = 0,
$$

so that

$$
\left(\frac {\partial}{\partial t _ {1}}\right) \left(\tau_ {i} / \tau_ {0}\right) = F / \tau_ {0} ^ {2}, \tag {14}
$$

where

$$
F = (1 / 2l)\left\{\sum_{i + j = l - 1}P(D)\tau_{i}\cdot \tau_{i} - \sum_{\substack{i + j = l - 1\\ i &lt;   l - 1}}2(i + 1)\left(\frac{\partial\tau_{i + 1}}{\partial t_{1}}\tau_{j} - \tau_{i + 1}\frac{\partial\tau_{j}}{\partial t_{1}}\right)\right\} .
$$

By assumption, $F$ is SVH on $U$, and $\tau_0$ can be written as

$$
\tau_ {0} (t) = \left(t _ {1} - f \left(t _ {2}, \dots , t _ {k - 1}\right)\right) h (t), \quad \text{for } t _ {1} \quad \text{near } 0,
$$

where $h$ is nonvanishing holomorphic, $f \in \mathbb{C}[[t_2, \ldots, t_{k-1}]]$, $f(0) = 0$.
Then by (14)

$$
\tau_ {i} / \tau_ {0} = \int \frac {F h ^ {- 2} d t _ {1}}{\left(t _ {1} - f\right) ^ {2}} = \frac {G}{t _ {1} - f} + H \log \left(t _ {1} - f\right) + (\mathrm {S V H}),
$$

with $G$ and $H$ independent of $t_1$.
Thus

$$
\tau_ {l} = \tilde {H} \tau_ {0} + (\mathrm {S V H}), \tag {15}
$$

where $\tilde{H} = H\log (t_1 - f)$

On the other hand, $\bar{\tau}$ also satisfies the KP equation (2) since it is a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy.
Substituting (12) into (2) and computing the coefficient of $t_k^l$, we have

$$
\sum_ {i + j = l} \left(D _ {1} ^ {4} + 3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \tau_ {i} \cdot \tau_ {j} = 0
$$

Jacobian varieties

(here we used that $k \geq 4$), so that by (15)

$$
\left(D _ {1} ^ {4} + 3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \tau_ {0} \cdot \hat {H} \tau_ {0} = (\mathrm {S V H})
$$

near $t_1 = 0$.
Expanding the left hand side of this equation, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\left(D _ {1} ^ {4} + 3 D _ {2} ^ {2} - 4 D _ {1} D _ {3}\right) \tau_ {0} \cdot \tau_ {0}\right) \hat {H} + 6 \left(D _ {1} ^ {2} \tau_ {0} \cdot \tau_ {0}\right) \frac {\partial^ {2} \hat {H}}{\partial t _ {1} ^ {2}} \\ + \tau_ {0} ^ {2} \left(\frac {\partial^ {4}}{\partial t _ {1} ^ {4}} + 3 \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial t _ {2} ^ {2}} - 4 \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial t _ {1} \partial t _ {3}}\right) \hat {H} = (\mathrm {S V H}). \\ \end{array}
$$

The first term vanishes since $\tau_0$ equals $\overline{\tau}(t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}, 0, 0, \ldots)$, which satisfies the KP equation (2) as $k - 1 \geq 3$.
But the second and third terms may still give poles:

$$
\begin{array}{l} - 6 H \left(\left(t _ {1} - f\right) ^ {- 2} D _ {1} ^ {2} \tau_ {0} \cdot \tau_ {0} + \left(t _ {1} - f\right) ^ {- 4} \tau_ {0} ^ {2}\right) + (\mathrm {S V H}) \\ = 6 H h ^ {2} \left(t _ {1} - f\right) ^ {- 2} + O \left(\left(t _ {1} - f\right) ^ {- 1}\right) \\ = (\mathrm {S V H}). \\ \end{array}
$$

Hence $H$ must be zero, so that $\tau_{l}$ is SVH by (15).
By induction on $l$, we see that $\overline{\tau} \bmod \bigcap_{l} I_{k}^{l}$ is SVH.
Since $\bigcap_{l} I_{k}^{l} = I_{k+1}^{1}$, the lemma is proved by induction on $k$.
QED.

Corollary.
Let $U, V$ and $W$ be as above.
Let

$$
\bar {S} = \bar {S} (t) \in 1 + \Psi^ {-} \otimes (\mathcal {C} (W) \otimes \mathbb {C} [ [ t _ {2}, t _ {3}, \dots ] ])
$$

be a wave operator for the KP hierarchy for which there exists an SVH function $f = f(t_{1},t_{2},t_{3})$ on $U$ with $f(0) = 0$, $(\partial f / \partial t_1)(0)\neq 0$, such that the restriction $S$ of $\bar{S}$ to the $(t_1,t_2,t_3)$-space satisfies

$$
S \equiv f (t) ^ {- 1} f \left(t _ {1} - (d / d x) ^ {- 1}, t _ {2} - 2 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 2}, t _ {3} - 3 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 3}\right)
$$

modulo $\Psi(-4)$.
Then the $\tau$-function $\overline{\tau}$ for $\bar{S}$ is SVH on $U$ and its restriction to the $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$-space coincides with $f$ up to a constant.
In particular, $\bar{S}$ is single-valued in $V$ with a pole at 0.

Proof.
Let $\overline{\tau} \in \mathcal{C}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3, \ldots]]$ be a $\tau$-function for $\bar{S}$.
(A $\tau$-function is locally defined at any point of $W$, so it is extended globally in $W$ by the uniqueness up to a constant.) By assumption, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \bar {S} (t) = \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {\infty} \left(p _ {j} (- \hat {\partial}) \bar {\tau}\right) \bar {\tau} ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- j} \\ \equiv \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {3} \left(p _ {j} (- \hat {\partial}) f\right) f ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- j} \\ \end{array}
$$

modulo $\Psi(-4)$ when $t = (t_1, t_2, t_3, 0, 0, \ldots)$.
Since $p_j(-\hat{\partial}) = -j^{-1} \partial / \partial t_j + \ldots$, this implies that $\overline{\tau}(t_1, t_2, t_3, 0, 0, \ldots) = f$ up to a constant.
Hence Lemma 8 applies to show that $\overline{\tau}$ is single-valued holomorphic on $U$.
QED.

T.
Shiota

# 3.1. Construction of the wave operator

For $n = 1,2,\ldots$, we define the maps (connections)

$$
X _ {n} \colon 1 + \Psi^ {-} \otimes \mathbb {C} [ [ t _ {1}, t _ {2}, \dots ] ] \rightarrow \Psi^ {-} \otimes \mathbb {C} [ [ t _ {1}, t _ {2}, \dots ] ]
$$

by

$$
X _ {n} S (t) = \left(\partial S / \partial t _ {n} + \left(S (d / d x) ^ {n} S ^ {- 1}\right) _ {-} S\right) (t),
$$

so that the KP hierarchy is expressed as $X_{n}S = 0$, $n = 1,2,\ldots$ (see Sect.
1.2). Employing our old convention that $S(x,t) = S(t_{1} + x,t_{2},t_{3},\ldots)$, we may assume that the first equation $X_{1}S = 0$ is trivial.

Let $G = 1 + \mathbb{C}[[(d / dx)^{-1}]](d / dx)^{-1}$, and let $\Gamma = \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}} + \Omega \mathbb{Z}^{\mathrm{g}}$ as before.
Let $S$ be a $\Psi$-valued meromorphic map on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$.
$S$ is called quasiperiodic if there exists a homomorphism $P_0\colon \Gamma \to G$ such that

$$
S (\zeta + \gamma) = S (\zeta) P _ {0} (\gamma)
$$

for the general $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ and any $\gamma \in \Gamma$.
The definition also applies to the case where $S$ depends on time parameters $t$, say, but in which case we require that $P_0$ be independent of $t$.

Let $t = (t_{1}, t_{2}, t_{3})$, $s = (s_{1}, s_{2}, s_{3})$.
Let $S$ be a $\Psi \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t]]$-valued meromorphic map on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$.
$S$ is called translation equivariant (in $(t_{1}, t_{2}, t_{3})$) if there exist $A \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g} \times 3}$ and

$$
P _ {1} (s) \in 1 + \mathbb {C} [ [ (d / d x) ^ {- 1} ] ] (d / d x) ^ {- 1} \otimes \mathbb {C} [ [ s ] ]
$$

such that for the general $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ we have

$$
S (t + s, \zeta) = S (t, \zeta + A s) P _ {1} (s).
$$

The definition is generalized to the case where $S$ depends on $(t_4, t_5, \ldots)$ while $s$ remains a three vector ("partial" translation equivariance), as well as the case where both $t$ and $s$ are infinite vectors ("full" translation equivariance).
If $S$ is translation equivariant in $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$, then $S$ is automatically extended to a meromorphic map on $\mathbb{C}_t^3 \times \mathbb{C}_{\zeta}^g$, and $P_1$ becomes a homomorphism from $\mathbb{C}^3$ into $G$.

We assume the condition $(\mathbf{A}^{*})$ for the principally polarized abelian variety $X = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / \Gamma$, and fix $A = (a_{1},a_{2},a_{3})$ throughout the argument.
For $\tau$ as in (A i), let

$$
S _ {0} (t, \zeta) = \tau (t, \zeta) ^ {- 1} \tau \left(t _ {1} - (d / d x) ^ {- 1}, t _ {2} - 2 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 2}, t _ {3} - 3 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 3}, \zeta\right). \tag {16}
$$

Then by the properties i)-iii) for $\tau$ stated at the beginning of Sect.
3, $S_0$ becomes globally meromorphic, quasiperiodic and translation equivariant.
By the KP equation (2) it is shown by computation that $S_0$ satisfies the following approximate wave equations,

$$
X _ {2} S _ {0} \equiv 0 \bmod \Psi (- 3), \quad X _ {3} S _ {0} \equiv 0 \bmod \Psi (- 2).
$$

Lemma 9. Suppose that (A\*) holds.
Then for $S_0$ as above, there exists $S = S(t_1, t_2, t_3, \zeta) \in 1 + \Psi^-$ such that $S$ is globally meromorphic in $t$ and $\zeta$, quasiperiodic and translation equivariant and satisfies

Jacobian varieties

$$
X _ {2} S = 0, \quad X _ {3} S = 0,
$$

$$
S \equiv S _ {0} \bmod \Psi (- 4).
$$

Lemma 10. Given $S' \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$, the following initial value problem,

$$
X _ {n} \bar {S} = 0, \quad n = 1, 2, \dots ,
$$

$$
\bar {S} (0) = S ^ {\prime}, \tag {17}
$$

has a unique solution $\bar{S} = \bar{S} (t)\in 1 + \Psi^{-}\otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_1,t_2,\ldots ]]$.

Lemma 9 is the fundamental lemma for our extension procedure, and will be proved after the proofs of Lemma 10 and the corollary below.

Applying Lemma 10 to $S' = S(0, \zeta)$, where $S$ is as in Lemma 9, we have:

Corollary to Lemmas 9, 10. Suppose that (A\*) holds.
Then for $S_0$ as above, there exists $\bar{S} = \bar{S}(t, \zeta) \in 1 + \Psi^{-}$ such that $\bar{S}$ is globally meromorphic, quasiperiodic and partially translation equivariant in $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$, and satisfies

$$
X _ {n} \bar {S} = 0, \quad n = 1, 2, \dots ,
$$

$$
\bar {S} (t _ {1}, t _ {2}, t _ {3}, 0, 0, \dots , \zeta) \equiv S _ {0} \bmod \Psi (- 4).
$$

Proof of Lemma 10. We solve the following infinitely many initial value problems inductively.
Here $t = (t_1, \ldots, t_n)$, $t' = (t_1, \ldots, t_{n-1})$.

$$
\left. \begin{array}{l} \bar {S} _ {n} = \bar {S} _ {n} (t) \in 1 + \Psi^ {-} \otimes \mathbb {C} [   [ t ]   ] \\ X _ {n} \bar {S} _ {n} = 0, \quad \bar {S} _ {n} (t ^ {\prime}, 0) = \bar {S} _ {n - 1} (t ^ {\prime}) \end{array} \right\} \quad \text {for} \ n = 1, 2, \dots ,
$$

$$
\bar {S} _ {0} = S ^ {\prime}.
$$

Setting $\bar{S}_n = \bar{S}_{n - 1}(t') + \sum_{j = 1}^{\infty}s_{n,j}(t')t_n^j$ and substituting this into $X_{n}\bar{S}_{n} = 0$, we get a recursion formula for $s_{n,j}$: Since

$$
\bar {S} _ {n} ^ {- 1} = \sum_ {m = 0} ^ {\infty} \left(- (\bar {S} _ {n - 1} - 1) - \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} s _ {n, j} t _ {n} ^ {j}\right) ^ {m}
$$

by the Neumann series, $\bar{S}_n^{-1}$ is of the form

$$
\bar {S} _ {n - 1} ^ {- 1} + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} s _ {n, j} ^ {\prime} (t _ {1}, \dots , t _ {n - 1}) t _ {n} ^ {j}
$$

and the coefficients of $s_{n,j}^{\prime} \in \Psi^{-}$ are differential polynomials of coefficients of $\bar{S}_{n-1}$ and $s_{n,i}$ for $i \leq j$.
Hence by the equation $X_{n}\bar{S}_{n} = 0$, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} j s _ {n, j} t _ {n} ^ {j - 1} \\ = - \left(\left(\bar {S} _ {n - 1} + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} s _ {n, j} t _ {n} ^ {j}\right) \left(\frac {d}{d x}\right) ^ {n} \left(\bar {S} _ {n - 1} ^ {- 1} + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} s _ {n, j} ^ {\prime} t _ {n} ^ {j}\right)\right) _ {-} \left(\bar {S} _ {n - 1} + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} s _ {n, j} t _ {n} ^ {j}\right), \\ \end{array}
$$

T.
Shiota

which gives the formula

$$
s_{n,j} = \begin{pmatrix}
\text{an operator whose coefficients are differential} \\
\text{polynomials of coefficients of } \bar{S}_{n-1} \text{ and } s_{n,i}, \; i &lt; j
\end{pmatrix}
$$

for each $j$.
Thus $\bar{S}_n$ exists and is unique.
Define $\bar{S} = \lim_{n\to \infty}\bar{S}_n$.
To show that this solves the initial value problem (17), we have only to observe

$$
X_m \bar{S}_n = 0 \quad \text{for } m &lt; n \tag{18}
$$

(so that $X_m \bar{S} = \lim_{n} X_m \bar{S}_n = 0$).
We prove this by induction on $n$.
Suppose this is true for $n - 1$, so that

$$
X_m \bar{S}_n \equiv 0 \bmod t_n \Psi^{-}.
$$

For $l \geq 1$, suppose $X_m \bar{S}_n \equiv 0 \bmod t_n^l \Psi^{-}$.
Setting $L = \bar{S}_n(d/dx) \bar{S}_n^{-1}$, $B_n^c = -(L^n)_-$ and $B_m^c = -(L^m)_-$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\partial/\partial t_n)(X_m \bar{S}_n) &amp;= (\partial/\partial t_n)(\partial \bar{S}_n / \partial t_m - B_m^c \bar{S}_n) \\
&amp;= (\partial/\partial t_m)(\partial \bar{S}_n / \partial t_n) - (\partial/\partial t_n)(B_m^c \bar{S}_n) \\
&amp;= (\partial/\partial t_m)(B_n^c \bar{S}_n) - (\partial/\partial t_n)(B_m^c \bar{S}_n) \\
&amp;= (\partial L^n / \partial t_m)_- \bar{S}_n - (\partial L^m / \partial t_n)_- \bar{S}_n + B_n^c \partial \bar{S}_n / \partial t_m - B_m^c \partial \bar{S}_n / \partial t_n \\
&amp;\equiv ([B_m^c, L^n] - [B_n^c, L^m] + [B_n^c, B_m^c])_- \bar{S}_n \bmod t_n^l \Psi^{-} \\
&amp;= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Here we used $\partial L / \partial t_m - [B_m^c, L] = [(X_m \bar{S}_n) \bar{S}_n^{-1}, L] \equiv 0 \bmod t_n^l \Psi^{-}$.
This shows that $X_m \bar{S}_n \equiv 0 \bmod t_n^{l+1} \Psi^{-}$.
Hence $X_m \bar{S}_n = 0$ by induction on $l$.
Then by induction on $n$, we obtain (18).
Moreover, $\bar{S}$ is unique since its restrictions $\bar{S}_n$ to the $(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$-space are all unique, as is shown above inductively.
QED.

Proof of corollary.
Applying Lemma 10 with the initial data $S(0,\zeta)$, we get a solution $\bar{S} = \bar{S}(t,\zeta)$ to the equations $X_n \bar{S} = 0$.
By the uniqueness of the initial value problems for $X_2$ and $X_3$, the restriction of $\bar{S}$ to the $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$-space coincides with $S$.
By the inductive construction of $\bar{S}$ in the proof of Lemma 10, $\bar{S}$ depends on $\zeta$ meromorphically since so does $S$.
And by the $G$-equivariance of $X_n$, the relations

$$
S(t, \zeta + \gamma) = S(t, \zeta) P_0(\gamma) \quad \text{(quasiperiodicity)},
$$

$$
S(t + s, \zeta) = S(t, \zeta + A s) P_1(s) \quad \text{(translation equivariance)}
$$

for $S$ induce the same relations for $\bar{S}$ except that $t = (t_1, t_2, t_3)$ is replaced by $t = (t_1, t_2, \ldots)$, while the $s$-variables remain a three vector.
This proves the corollary.

Proof of Lemma 9. We denote $t = (t_1, t_2, t_3)$, $t' = (t_2, t_3)$, and define

$$
\Psi = \Psi \otimes M, \quad \Psi' = \mathbb{C}(((d/dx)^{-1})) \otimes N
$$

for some rings $M$ and $N$ of functions of the form $f(t, \zeta)$ and $g(t', \zeta)$, respectively.

Jacobian varieties

We assume that $N = \{f \in M; \partial f / \partial t_1 = 0\}$.
$M$ and $N$ will be specified in each context either implicitly or explicitly.
We also define $\Psi(n) = \Psi(n) \otimes M$, $\Psi'(n) = \Psi(n) \cap \Psi'$ and $\Psi^{-} = \Psi(-1)$.

We prove the lemma by "successive approximation": Starting from $S_0$, we construct a sequence $\{S_n\}$ of operators in $1 + \Psi^{-}$, such that

$$
S_1 \equiv S_0 \bmod \Psi(-4), \tag{19}
$$

$$
S_n \equiv S_{n-1} \bmod \Psi(-3-n) + \Psi'(-2-n), \quad n \geq 2, \tag{19}
$$

$$
X_2 S_n \equiv 0 \bmod \Psi(-3-n), \quad X_3 S_n \equiv 0 \bmod \Psi(-2-n), \tag{20}
$$

and that the $S_n$ are globally meromorphic, quasiperiodic and translation equivariant, i.e.,

$$
S_n(t, \zeta + \gamma) = S_n(t, \zeta) P_{0n}(\gamma), \quad \gamma \in \Gamma, \tag{21}
$$

$$
S_n(t + s, \zeta) = S_n(t, \zeta + As) P_{1n}(s),
$$

for some $P_{0n} \colon \Gamma \to G$ and $P_{1n} \colon \mathbb{C}^3 \to G$.
Note that $S_0$ satisfies $(20)_0$ and $(21)_0$.

If $\{S_n\}$ is such a sequence, then $S = \lim_{n} S_n$ exists by (19) and satisfies the conditions for $S$ in Lemma 9. Indeed, the quasiperiodicity and translation equivariance hold with $P_j = \lim_{n} P_{jn}$, where the limits exist, since (19) and (21) imply

$$
P_{jn}(\cdot) \equiv P_{j,n-1}(\cdot) \bmod \Psi(-2-n).
$$

Step I.
Construction for fixed $\tilde{\zeta}$ and locally in $t$.
Since (20) can be considered independently of the $\zeta$-dependence of $S_n$, we divide our argument into two parts, and first solve (16), (19) and (20) for a single "nice" solution to the KP equation and near $t = 0$.
Later the argument will be applied to $\tilde{\tau}(\cdot, \tilde{\zeta})$ to construct $S_n$ locally and to prove its single-valuedness.
Let $\varepsilon$ be small and positive, and let $U = \{|t_1| &lt; \varepsilon\}$, $V = U - \{0\}$ and $W =$ (the universal covering space of $V$) as in Lemma 8. Let $\tau$ be a solution to the KP equation belonging to $\mathcal{O}(U) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]]$.
Suppose $\tau(t_1, 0, 0) \neq 0$ on $V$, and suppose either

i) $\tau(0) \neq 0$, or
ii) $\tau(0) = 0$, $(\partial \tau / \partial t_1)(0) \neq 0$.

Claim.
Let $S_0$ be as in (16).
Then there exists a sequence $\{S_n\}$ of operators in $1 + \Psi^{-}$ satisfying (19) and (20) such that in Case i), the $S_n$ are holomorphic in $U$, and in Case ii), the $S_n$ are meromorphic in $U$ with a pole only at 0.

We prove this for Case ii) by the following two steps a), b).
Case i) is shown by the same argument as in Step a).

a) We construct a sequence $\{S_n\}$ satisfying (19) and (20) such that the $S_n$ are holomorphic in $W$, i.e.,

$$
S_n \in 1 + \Psi^{-} \otimes (\mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]]).
$$

b) Then we observe that the $S_n$ so constructed are all single-valued in $V$ with a pole at $t_1 = 0$.

T.
Shiota

The assumption that $(\partial \tau / \partial t_1)(0) \neq 0$ will be used only in Step b).
Let us take $M = \mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]]$, $N = \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]]$ for $M, N$ in the definition of $\Psi, \Psi'$.

a).
Let $S_0$ be as in (16).
For $n \geq 1$, assume that $S_{n-1}$ is already defined and satisfies $(20)_{n-1}$.
We construct $S_n$ as follows.
Set

$$
L = S_{n-1}(d/dx)S_{n-1}^{-1}, \quad B_j = (L^j)_+, \quad j = 2, 3,
$$

$$
X_2S_{n-1} = a(t)(d/dx)^{-2-n} + \dots, \quad X_3S_{n-1} = b(t)(d/dx)^{-1-n} + \dots.
$$

We have

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{n-1}^{-1} \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2} - B_2, \frac{\partial}{\partial t_3} - B_3 \right] S_{n-1} \\
= \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2} - (d/dx)^2 + S_{n-1}^{-1} X_2 S_{n-1}, \frac{\partial}{\partial t_3} - (d/dx)^3 + S_{n-1}^{-1} X_3 S_{n-1} \right] \\
= \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2} - (d/dx)^2 + a(t)(d/dx)^{-2-n} + \dots, \right. \\
\left. \frac{\partial}{\partial t_3} - (d/dx)^3 + b(t)(d/dx)^{-1-n} + \dots \right] \\
= \left( \frac{\partial}{\partial t_1} \right) (3a(t) - 2b(t))(d/dx)^{-n} + \dots.
\end{aligned}
$$

But this expression must vanish, since the left hand side is a $(1 + \Psi^-)$-conjugation of a differential operator $\partial B_2 / \partial t_3 - \partial B_3 / \partial t_2 + [B_2, B_3]$, while the right hand side is of negative order.
Thus by setting $g = 3a(t) - 2b(t)$, we have $\partial g / \partial t_1 = 0$ and $g \in \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]]$.
Let

$$
G(t_2, t_3) = \int_0^{t_2} g(s, t_3) \, ds \in \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]],
$$

and define

$$
S' = S_{n-1} \exp\left( -3^{-1} G(d/dx)^{-2-n} \right). \tag{22}
$$

Since $\exp\left( -3^{-1} G(d/dx)^{-2-n} \right)$ commutes with $(d/dx)$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_j S' &amp;= \left( X_j S_{n-1} - 3^{-1} S_{n-1} \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial t_j} \right)(d/dx)^{-2-n} \right) \exp\left( -3^{-1} G(d/dx)^{-2-n} \right) \\
&amp;= \begin{cases}
(a - g/3)(d/dx)^{-2-n} + \dots = (2/3)b(d/dx)^{-2-n} + \dots &amp; \text{if } j = 2, \\
b(d/dx)^{-1-n} + \dots &amp; \text{if } j = 3.
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $F \in \mathcal{O}(W) \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_2, t_3]]$ be such that

$$
\partial F / \partial t_1 = (1/3)b,
$$

which exists (but is not unique) as $W$ is simply connected.
Define

$$
S_n = S_n(S', F) = \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} (1/m!) F^m S'(d/dx)^{(-3-n)m}. \tag{23}
$$

Since $S_n \equiv S' + F(d/dx)^{-3-n}$ modulo $\Psi(-4-n)$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_j S_n &amp;\equiv \frac{\partial S'}{\partial t_j} + \left( (S' + F(d/dx)^{-3-n})(d/dx)^j \right) (S'^{-1} - F(d/dx)^{-3-n})_+ S' \\
&amp;\equiv X_j S' - j \left( dF/dt_1 \right)(d/dx)^{j-4-n} \\
&amp;\equiv 0 \mod \Psi(j-5-n).
\end{aligned}
$$

Jacobian varieties

This shows (20).
