Observing by direct computation that $g = 3a - 2b$ vanishes when $n = 1$, we get (19).
It is clear from the construction that $S_{n}$ is holomorphic in $W$.
So we have $\underline{a}$.

b).
For $S_{n}$ defined in $\underline{a}$, let $S = \lim S_{n}$.
Then $S$ satisfies

$$
X_{j} S = 0, \quad j = 2, 3; \quad S \equiv S_{0} \bmod \Psi(-4)
$$

and is holomorphic on $W$.
Let us extend $S$ to $\bar{S} \in \Psi \otimes \mathbb{C}[[t_4, t_5, \ldots]]$ by the equations

$$
X_{j} \bar{S} = 0, \quad j = 4, 5, \dots.
$$

By Lemma 10 and its proof, $\bar{S}$ exists, is unique and is holomorphic on $W$.
We have $S \equiv S_0 \bmod \Psi(-4)$, while the restriction of $\bar{S}$ to the $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$-space coincides with $S$.
Thus the Corollary to Lemma 8 applies to show that $\bar{S}$ is single-valued on $V$ with a pole at 0, and so is $S$.
Now, this shows the single-valuedness of all the $S_n$ inductively as follows: First, $S_0$ is single-valued with a pole at 0 by definition.
Next, let $n \geq 1$ and suppose that $S_{n-1}$ is single-valued with a pole at the origin.
Since

$$
\begin{array}{l}
S_{n} = S_{n-1} \cdot \left(1 - 3^{-1} G(d/dx)^{-2-n} + F(d/dx)^{-3-n} + \dots\right), \\
S \equiv S_{n} \bmod \Psi(-4-n) + \Psi'(-3-n)
\end{array}
$$

and $S$ is single-valued, $F$ must be single-valued.
As a single-valued primitive function of a meromorphic function, $F$ has (at most) a pole at 0, and so does $S_{n}$, proving the assertion in b) by induction on $n$.

Step II.
Global construction.
To construct the sequence $\{S_n\}$ globally in $\zeta$, we translate the argument in Step I into the new context where everything is globally meromorphic, quasiperiodic and translation equivariant.
We use Lemma 7 to deal with the possible vanishing of $\tau$ in the bad direction.
Throughout this step, $\Sigma$ and $\Sigma_1$ are as in Lemma 7, so that they are invariant under the flows associated to $a_1, a_2, a_3$, and for the blowing up $\pi: \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g \to \mathbb{C}^g$ with center $\Sigma_1$, it is assumed that $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^g$ is non-singular.

A meromorphic function $f$ on $\mathbb{C}^g$ is said to be $a$-quasiperiodic ($a$ for additive) if there exists a homomorphism $\sigma_0: \Gamma \to \mathbb{C}$ such that $f(\zeta + \gamma) = f(\zeta) + \sigma_0(\gamma)$ holds for the general $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$ and any $\gamma \in \Gamma$.
The definition is generalized to the case where $f$ depends on time parameters $t$, in which case we require that $\sigma_0$ be independent of $t$.
A meromorphic function $f$ on $\mathbb{C}_t^3 \times \mathbb{C}_\zeta^g$ is $a$-translation equivariant if there exists a homomorphism $\sigma_1: \mathbb{C}^3 \to \mathbb{C}$ such that $f(t + s, \zeta) = f(t, \zeta + As) + \sigma_1(s)$ holds.
A function $f$ is periodic (resp.
purely translation equivariant) if it is $a$-quasiperiodic with $\sigma_0 = 0$ (resp.
$a$-translation equivariant with $\sigma_1 = 0$).

We will construct the sequence $\{S_n\}$ satisfying (19)-(21) and the following condition (24), which is satisfied when $n = 0$ by Lemma 7:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text{The coefficients of } S_n \text{ are } (a_1, \Sigma_1) \text{-microregular} \\
\text{as meromorphic functions of } \zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g.
\end{array}
\tag{24}
$$

T.
Shiota

To define $\{S_n\}$, suppose that $S_{n-1} = S_{n-1}(t, \zeta)$ is already defined, and satisfies (20)$_{n-1}$, (21)$_{n-1}$ and (24)$_{n-1}$.
Since $X_j$ commute with the $G$-action,

$$
X_2 S_{n-1} = a(t, \zeta)(d/dx)^{-2-n} + \dots, \quad X_3 S_{n-1} = b(t, \zeta)(d/dx)^{-1-n} + \dots
$$

are quasiperiodic and translation equivariant.
Since the top order terms of operators are invariant under the $G$-action, the functions $a$ and $b$, hence also $g = 3a - 2b$, are periodic and purely translation equivariant.
Moreover, by (A\*ii), (24) and $\partial g / \partial t_1 = 0$, $g$ is also holomorphic, hence constant.
Thus the operator $S'$, defined by (22), becomes

$$
S_{n-1} \exp(-3^{-1} g t_2 (d/dx)^{-2-n}),
$$

and so it is quasiperiodic and translation equivariant and satisfies (24).

Next, we choose $F$, a solution of

$$
\partial F / \partial t_1 = (1/3) b, \tag{25}
$$

in an appropriate way so that the operator $S_n$ in (23) is globally meromorphic and satisfies (21) and (24).

**Lemma 11.** Suppose that $S_{n-1}$ satisfies the conditions (20), (21), (24) and

$$
S_{n-1} \equiv S_0 \bmod \Psi(-4). \tag{26}
$$

Then there exists a $\mathbb{C}$-valued globally meromorphic function $F = F(t, \zeta)$, which is a-quasiperiodic, a-translation equivariant and $(a_1, \Sigma_1)$-microregular in the $\zeta$-variables and satisfies (25).

Let us assume the lemma for a moment and define $S_n$ by (23) using such $F$.
Then (24) holds by the microregularity of $F$; (19) and (20) by $\mathfrak{a}$ in Step I; and (21) by the following intertwining property of $S_n$.
Namely, the $G \times \mathbb{C}$-action on the pairs $(S', F)$ is transmuted to the $G$-action on $S_n(S', F)$: If $P \in G$ and $c \in \mathbb{C}$, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_n(S' P, F + c) &amp;= \sum_m (1/m!) (F + c)^m S' P(d/dx)^{(-3-n)m} \\
&amp;= \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \sum_{j=0}^{m} \left(1/(j!(m-j)!)\right) F^j S' P c^{m-j}(d/dx)^{(-3-n)m} \\
&amp;= S_n(S', F) P \cdot \exp(c(d/dx)^{-3-n}).
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus modulo Lemma 11, the inductive step of the proof of Lemma 9 is completed.

**Proof of Lemma 11.** By the pure translation equivariance of $b$, we can eliminate the $t$-variables and reduce the problem to the problem of finding an $a$-quasiperiodic solution $F = F(\zeta)$ to

$$
(\partial / \partial \zeta_1) F(\zeta) = f(\zeta) = (1/3) b(0, \zeta) \tag{27}
$$

which is $(a_1, \Sigma_1)$-microregular.
Here $\partial / \partial \zeta_1$ is the invariant vector field $a_1$ on $\mathbb{C}^g$ considered as a differential operator.
If $F(\zeta)$ solves this problem, then the

Jacobian varieties

original problem is solved by $F(\zeta + At) + c(\zeta) + d$ for any $c \in \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathbb{C})$ and $d \in \mathbb{C}$.

By (24), $f$ is $(a_{1},\Sigma_{1})$-microregular.
Let $\pi \colon \hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}\to \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ be the blowing up with center $\Sigma_{1}$, and let $\hat{\Theta}$ be the strict transform of $\Theta$ under $\pi$.
We assume that $\hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is non-singular.
We denote by $\tilde{f}$ (resp.
$\tilde{F}$) the meromorphic function on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ extending $\pi^{*}(f|_{\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} - \Theta})$ (resp.
$\pi^{*}(F|_{\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} - \Theta})$).
Note that the locus of poles of $\tilde{f}$ is contained in $\hat{\Theta}$.
By abuse of notation, we denote by $\partial/\partial\zeta_{1}$ the vector field on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ extending $(\pi^{-1})_{\mathfrak{g}}(\partial/\partial\zeta_{1}|_{\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} - \Sigma})$, which is well-defined by the $\partial/\partial\zeta_{1}$-invariance of $\Sigma_{1}$.
For $\zeta \in \hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ and $c\in \mathbb{C}$, we denote $\exp (c\partial /\partial \zeta_1)\zeta$ by $\zeta +ca_{1}$.

We first solve (27) locally in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$.
Let $\alpha \in \hat{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$, and let $H \ni \alpha$ be a hypersurface defined near $\alpha$ which is transversal to $a_1$.
Let $U'$ be a sufficiently small neighborhood of $\alpha$ in $H$ such that

$$
U = \left\{\zeta^ {\prime} + \zeta_ {1} a _ {1}; | \zeta_ {1} | &lt;   1, \zeta^ {\prime} \in U ^ {\prime} \right\}
$$

has the following property: There exists $c \in \mathbb{C}$, $|c| &lt; 1$, such that $\tilde{f}$ is holomorphic on $(H + ca_1) \cap U$.
Such $U'$ exists by the microregularity of $f$.
Then we define

$$
\tilde {F} (\zeta) = \int_ {c} ^ {\zeta_ {1}} \tilde {f} \left(\zeta^ {\prime} + s a _ {1}\right) d s,
$$

for $U \ni \zeta = \zeta' + \zeta_1 a_1$, $\zeta' \in U'$, $|\zeta_1| &lt; 1$.
We claim that this defines a meromorphic function on $U$ with poles only on $\tilde{\Theta}$.

The absence of extra singularities follows from the condition on $c$, that $\tilde{f}$ is holomorphic on $U' + ca_1$.
To observe the single-valuedness of $\tilde{F}$, we have only to observe it for the $\zeta_1$-variable at each $\zeta \in \tilde{\Theta}$.
Changing $U'$ to a smaller one and modifying $\tilde{\tau}$ as in the proof of Lemma 7 if necessary, we assume $\tilde{\tau}(\cdot, \zeta) \neq 0$ for any $\zeta \in U$.
Let $\zeta \in \tilde{\Theta} \cap U$, so that $\tilde{\tau}(0, \zeta) = 0$.
If $(\partial \tilde{\tau} / \partial \zeta_1)(0, \zeta) \neq 0$, then $(\partial \tilde{\tau} / \partial t_1)(0, \zeta) \neq 0$ and the result follows from Step I, since we have the single-valuedness of $\tilde{F}(\zeta + t_1 a_1 + t_2 a_2 + t_3 a_3)$ in $t_1$ near $t = 0$.
To deal with the "bad" points, we use the following argument due to Mumford:

Let $p \colon U \to U'$, $\zeta = \zeta' + \zeta_1 a_1 \mapsto \zeta'$, be the natural projection, and let

$$
\Theta^ {\prime} = \left\{\zeta \in \tilde {\Theta} \cap U; (\partial \tilde {\tau} / \partial \zeta_ {1}) (0, \zeta) = 0 \right\}.
$$

For $a' \in U'$, let $\gamma$ be a loop in $\mathbb{C}$ such that $a' + \gamma$ is a loop in $p^{-1}(a') \cap (U - \tilde{\Theta})$.
If $x' \in U'$ and is sufficiently close to $a'$, then $x' + \gamma$ is a loop in $p^{-1}(x') \cap (U - \tilde{\Theta})$, so that

$$
I = \oint_ {x ^ {\prime} + \gamma} \tilde {f} (\zeta) d \zeta_ {1}
$$

is continuous in $x'$ near $a'$.
By the above argument, this is zero if $x' \notin p(\Theta')$, but $p(\Theta')$ has codimension $\geq 1$ in $U'$ since $\Theta'$ has codimension $\geq 2$ in $U$.
Thus $I$ vanishes identically near $x' = a'$.
Since $a'$ is arbitrary, this shows that $\tilde{F}$ is single-valued, and is meromorphic on $U$.
This proves the existence of a local solution to (27).

To obtain the existence of a globally meromorphic, $a$-quasiperiodic solution to (27), we have only to "glue" the local solutions by using the following cohomological observation.

T.
Shiota

Lemma 12. a) Let $f$ be a meromorphic function on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$, and let $\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}$ be a translation invariant vector field on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$.
Let $\Sigma$ be a $\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}$-invariant complex analytic subspace of $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ and let $\pi: \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}} \to \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ be the blowing up along $\Sigma$.
Suppose that $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is non-singular, and that $f$ is $(\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}, \Sigma)$-microregular.
If for each $\zeta \in \tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ there exists a neighborhood of $\zeta$ in $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ in which

$$
\partial \tilde{F} / \partial \zeta_{1} = \tilde{f}
$$

has a meromorphic solution $\tilde{F}$ with poles only on $\tilde{\Theta}$, the locus of poles of $\tilde{f}$, then there exists a globally meromorphic solution $F$ to

$$
\partial F / \partial \zeta_{1} = f \tag{28}
$$

in $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ which is $(\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}, \Sigma)$-microregular.

b) Let $\Gamma$ be a lattice in $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ such that $X = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / \Gamma$ is an abelian variety.
If $f$ is as in a) and is periodic with respect to $\Gamma$, then there exists a global solution $F$ to (28) as in a) such that $F$ is a-quasiperiodic with respect to $\Gamma$.

Proof.
(The author learned the following proof from Lee, Yukie and Oda.) a) Let $Y = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ or $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$.
Let $\mathcal{O}_Y$ be the structure sheaf of $Y$ and $\mathcal{O}_Y' = \operatorname{Ker}(\partial/\partial \zeta_1: \mathcal{O}_Y \to \mathcal{O}_Y)$, $\mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}}$ the sheaf of germs of meromorphic functions on $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ with poles only on $\tilde{\Theta}$, and $\mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}}^0 = \operatorname{Im}(\partial/\partial \zeta_1: \mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}} \to \mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}})$.
Since $f$ is microregular, $\tilde{\Theta}$ does not contain a line parallel to $\partial/\partial \zeta_1$, so that

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}} \xrightarrow{\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}} \mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}}^0 \longrightarrow 0 \tag{29}
$$

is exact.
Hence we have an exact cohomology sequence

$$
H^{0}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}}) \xrightarrow{\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}} H^{0}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{M}_{\tilde{\Theta}}^0) \longrightarrow H^{1}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}). \tag{30}
$$

On the other hand, since every holomorphic function has a holomorphic primitive function with respect to $\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}$ locally and globally in $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$, the sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}} \xrightarrow{\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}} \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}} \longrightarrow 0
$$

is exact and the first arrow in the following exact sequence is surjective:

$$
H^{0}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) \xrightarrow{\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}} H^{0}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) \longrightarrow H^{1}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) \longrightarrow H^{1}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}). \tag{*}
$$

By a spectral sequence, we have the following exact sequence

$$
0 \rightarrow H^{1}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}}) \rightarrow H^{1}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) \rightarrow H^{0}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, R^{1} \pi_{\ast}(\mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}})). \tag{**}
$$

Since $\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is non-singular, we have $R^{i}\pi_{\ast}(\mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) = 0$ for $i &gt; 0$ by [13, Cor. 2 to the main Theorem II' (p. 153)].
Thus $H^{1}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}},\mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) = H^{1}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}},\mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) = 0$ by $(**)$ $H^{1}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}},\mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) = 0$ by $(*)$, and $\partial/\partial \zeta_{1}$ in (30) is surjective.
This proves (a).

b) We denote $\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}}$, $\mathcal{O}' = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}}'$.
For a global solution $F$ to (28) as in (a), define $\sigma(\gamma) = F(\cdot + \gamma) - F$ for $\gamma \in \Gamma$.
$\partial \sigma(\gamma) / \partial \zeta_1 = f - f = 0$ for $f$ is periodic, so that by (29), $\sigma(\gamma) \in H^{0}(\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}_{\tilde{\mathbb{C}}^{\mathrm{g}}}) = H^{0}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}') \subset H^{0}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O})$.
Hence the crossed homomor

Jacobian varieties

phism $\sigma \colon \gamma \mapsto \sigma(\gamma)$ defines a group cohomology class $\widetilde{\sigma} \in H_{\mathrm{gr}}^{1}(\Gamma, H^{0}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}))$.
We have an exact sequence

$$
0 \to H _ {\mathrm {g r}} ^ {1} (\Gamma , H ^ {0} (\mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}}, \mathcal {O})) \to H _ {\Gamma} ^ {1} (\mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}}, \mathcal {O}) \to H ^ {1} (\mathbb {C} ^ {\mathrm {g}}, \mathcal {O}) ^ {\Gamma} = 0
$$

by the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence for equivariant cohomology [11].
Here $H_I^1 (\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}},\mathcal{O})$ is the equivariant sheaf cohomology group, which in our case coincides with the ordinary cohomology $H^{1}(X,\mathcal{O}_{X})$ of the quotient space $X = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / \Gamma$ since the action of $\Gamma$ on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is fixed point free.
By the Hodge spectral sequence we have an exact sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow H ^ {0} (X, \Omega_ {X} ^ {1}) \longrightarrow H ^ {1} (X, \mathbb {C}) \longrightarrow H ^ {1} (X, \mathcal {O} _ {X}) \longrightarrow 0
$$

Hence there exists $\sigma' \in H_{\mathrm{gr}}^{1}(\Gamma, \mathbb{C}) = \mathrm{Hom}(\Gamma, \mathbb{C})$ which is mapped to $\widetilde{\sigma} \in H_{\mathrm{gr}}^{1}(\Gamma, H^{0}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}))$.
Then there exists $h \in H^{0}(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O})$ such that

$$
\sigma (\gamma) = \sigma^ {\prime} (\gamma) + h (\cdot + \gamma) - h.
$$

Differentiating both sides by $\partial/\partial \zeta_1$, we have $\partial h / \partial \zeta_1 \in H^0(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O})^\Gamma = \mathbb{C}$.
Thus $h(\zeta) = c\zeta_1 + h'$ for some $h' \in H^0(\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}, \mathcal{O}')$ and $c \in \mathbb{C}$.
Here $\zeta_1 = \zeta_1(\zeta)$ is a linear function on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$ with $(\partial/\partial \zeta_1)\zeta_1 = 1$.
Then $F - h'$ is the desired $a$-quasiperiodic solution to (28).
This shows b).

## 3.2. The $\tau$-function $\bar{\tau}$

Let $\bar{S} = \bar{S}(t, \zeta)$ be as in the corollary to Lemmas 9, 10, i.e., a wave operator for the KP hierarchy which is globally meromorphic in $\zeta$, quasiperiodic and translation equivariant in $(t_1, t_2, t_3)$ and satisfies $\bar{S}(t_1, t_2, t_3, 0, 0, \ldots, \zeta) \equiv S_0$ modulo $\Psi(-4)$.
Define $\bar{\tau} = \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta)$ to be the $\tau$-function corresponding to $\bar{S}$, i.e.,

$$
\bar {S} (t, \zeta) = \bar {\tau} (t, \zeta) ^ {- 1} \bar {\tau} (t _ {1} - (d / d x) ^ {- 1}, t _ {2} - 2 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 2}, \dots , \zeta),
$$

and such that

$$
\bar {\tau} (0, \zeta) = \tau (0, \zeta).
$$

This is well defined, satisfies the following regularity condition:

$$
\bar {\tau} \in \mathcal {O} \left(\mathbb {C} _ {t} ^ {3} \times \mathbb {C} _ {\zeta} ^ {\mathrm {g}}\right) \otimes \mathbb {C} \llbracket t _ {4}, t _ {5}, \dots \rrbracket , \tag {31}
$$

and is quasiperiodic and partially translation equivariant:

There exist a homomorphism $\rho_1\colon \Gamma \to G_1$ and a twisted homomorphism $\rho_{2}\colon$ $\mathbb{C}^3\to G_1$ with $\rho_1(\cdot)(0) = 1$, such that

$$
\bar {\tau} (t, \zeta + \gamma) = \rho_ {1} (\gamma) (t) \rho (\gamma , \zeta) \bar {\tau} (t, \zeta), \tag {32}
$$

T.
Shiota

$$
\bar{\tau}(t + s, \zeta) = \rho_2(s)(t) \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta + A s) \tag{33}
$$

for $s = (s_1, s_2, s_3) \in \mathbb{C}^3$, $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$, $\gamma \in \Gamma$, $t = (t_1, t_2, \ldots)$; where $\rho$ is the automorphy factor for Riemann's theta function.

Indeed, as a $\mathbb{C}[[t_4, t_5, \ldots]]$-valued function, $\bar{\tau}$ is holomorphic in $t_1$ at a general point of the zero set of $\tau$ by the corollary to Lemma 8 and holomorphic in $t_1, t_2, t_3, \zeta$ outside the zero set by the construction, so it is holomorphic everywhere.
This shows (31).
Next, since the $G$-action on the space of wave operators is transmuted to the $G_1 / \mathbb{C}^*$-action on the space of $\tau$-functions modulo $\mathbb{C}^*$, the quasiperiodicity and the partial translation equivariance of $\bar{S}$ imply the following:

$$
\bar{\tau}(t, \zeta + \gamma) = c_1(\zeta, \gamma) \rho_1(\gamma)(t) \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta),
$$

$$
\bar{\tau}(t + s, \zeta) = c_2(\zeta, s) \rho_2(s)(t) \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta + A s),
$$

where $t, s, \rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ are as above, while $c_1$ and $c_2$ are independent of $t$.
Setting $t = 0$ and using the definition (1) of $\tau$, we observe that $c_1(\zeta, \gamma) = \rho(\gamma, \zeta)$, $c_2$ is independent of $\zeta$ so that it can be absorbed into $\rho_2$ etc. This implies (32), (33).

**Lemma 13.** If $\bar{\tau} = \bar{\tau}(\cdot, \zeta)$ is a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy with a parameter $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$ and satisfies (31)-(33), then there exist a $g \times \infty$-matrix $\bar{A}$ and an infinite quadratic polynomial $Q(t, \zeta)$ such that

$$
\bar{\tau}(t, \zeta) = \exp(Q(t, \zeta)) \vartheta(\bar{A} t + \zeta). \tag{34}
$$

In particular, $\bar{\tau}$ is globally holomorphic in all the $(t, \zeta)$-variables, and "fully" translation equivariant:

There exists a twisted homomorphism $\rho_3\colon \mathbb{C}^\infty \to G_1$ such that

$$
\bar{\tau}(t + s, \zeta) = \rho_3(s)(t) \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta + \bar{A} s)
$$

for any $t$, $s \in \mathbb{C}^\infty$, $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$.

**Proof.** By our definition of Riemann's theta function, its automorphy factor $\rho(\gamma, \zeta)$ is given by

$$
\rho(m, \zeta) = 1, \quad \rho(\Omega m, \zeta) = \exp\left(-\pi i^t m \Omega m - 2\pi i^t m \zeta\right) \tag{35}
$$

for $m \in \mathbb{Z}^g$, and this $\rho$ determines $\vartheta$ uniquely up to a constant [21, 22].
For $\rho_1$ in (32), set

$$
\rho_1(m_1 + \Omega m_2)(t) = \exp\left(\sum_{i,j} c_{ij} m_{1i} t_j + \sum_{i,j} d_{ij} m_{2i} t_j\right)
$$

for $m_1, m_2 \in \mathbb{Z}^g$.
Then by (35), the automorphy factor $\rho'$ for the function

$$
\bar{\tau}'(t, \zeta) = \exp\left(-\sum_{i,j} c_{ij} \zeta_i t_j\right) \bar{\tau}(t, \zeta)
$$

becomes

$$
\rho'(\gamma, \zeta, t) = \rho(\gamma, \zeta + \bar{A} t),
$$

with

$$
\bar{A} = (\bar{a}_{ij}), \quad \bar{a}_{ij} = (2\pi i)^{-1} \left(\sum_k \Omega_{ik} c_{kj} - d_{ij}\right), \quad 1 \leq i \leq g, \quad 1 \leq j &lt; \infty.
$$

Jacobian varieties

Thus there exists $f(t) \in \mathbb{C}[[t_1, t_2, \ldots]]$ such that

$$
\bar{\tau}'(t, \zeta) = \exp(f(t)) \, \vartheta(\zeta + \bar{A}t),
$$

$$
\bar{\tau}(t, \zeta) = \exp(f(t) + B(t, \zeta)) \, \vartheta(\zeta + \bar{A}t), \tag{36}
$$

where $B(t, \zeta) = \sum c_{ij} \zeta_i t_j$.
Hence to prove (34), we have only to observe that $f$ is at most quadratic in $t$.
We denote

$$
f(t) = q(t) + f'(t)
$$

with $q(t)$ consisting of at most quadratic terms and with $f'(t)$ cubic or higher order terms.
We prove that $f' = 0$ as follows: First by (33), $f'$ is independent of $t_1, t_2, t_3$.
Indeed, substituting (36) into (33), we get

$$
\exp(f'(t) - f'(t + s)) = \rho_2(s)(t) \exp((q + B)(t + s) - (q + B)(t))
$$

for $t = (t_1, t_2, \ldots)$, $s = (s_1, s_2, s_3, 0, 0, \ldots)$.
Since the right hand side is of the form $\exp(\text{at most linear in } t)$, so is the left and side.
But this is possible only if $f'$ is independent of $t_1, t_2, t_3$.
Next, set

$$
\bar{\tau}_0 = \exp(q(t) + B(t, \zeta)) \, \vartheta(\zeta + \bar{A}t), \quad \bar{\tau}_1 = \exp(f'(t))
$$

and

$$
\bar{S}_j(t) = \bar{\tau}_j(t)^{-1} \, \bar{\tau}_j(t_1 - (d/dx)^{-1}, t_2 - 2^{-1}(d/dx)^{-2}, \ldots), \quad j = 0, 1.
$$

Then $\bar{S}_0$ is globally meromorphic in $t$ and $\zeta$ and is fully translation equivariant:

There exists a homomorphism $P \colon \mathbb{C}^\infty \to G$ such that

$$
\bar{S}_0(t + s, \zeta) = \bar{S}_0(t, \zeta + \bar{A}s) P(s) \tag{37}
$$

holds for any $t$, $s \in \mathbb{C}^\infty$, $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^g$.

$\bar{S}_1$ is independent of $t_1$ and $x$, so that the decomposition

$$
\bar{\tau} = \bar{\tau}_0 \bar{\tau}_1
$$

induces the decomposition of $\bar{S}$:

$$
\bar{S} = \bar{S}_0 \bar{S}_1.
$$

Substituting this into the equations $X_n \bar{S} = 0$ and using

$$
\bar{S}(d/dx) \bar{S}^{-1} = \bar{S}_0(d/dx) \bar{S}_0^{-1},
$$

we have

$$
(X_n \bar{S}_0) \bar{S}_1 + \bar{S}_0(\partial \bar{S}_1 / \partial t_n) = 0,
$$

$$
\bar{S}_0^{-1}(X_n \bar{S}_0) + (\partial \bar{S}_1 / \partial t_n) \bar{S}_1^{-1} = 0. \tag{38}
$$

Since $\bar{S}_1$ commutes with $G$ and is independent of $\zeta$, (37) and (38) imply

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\partial \bar{S}_1 / \partial t_n) \bar{S}_1^{-1}(t + s) &amp;= -\bar{S}_0^{-1}(X_n \bar{S}_0)(t + s, \zeta) \\
&amp;= -P(s)^{-1} (\bar{S}_0^{-1}(X_n \bar{S}_0))(t, \zeta + \bar{A}s) P(s) \\
&amp;= P(s)^{-1} \left( (\partial \bar{S}_1 / \partial t_n) \bar{S}_1^{-1} \right)(t) P(s) \\
&amp;= (\partial \bar{S}_1 / \partial t_n) \bar{S}_1^{-1}(t),
\end{aligned}
$$

T.
Shiota

for $t, s \in \mathbb{C}^{\infty}$, i.e., $(\partial \overline{S}_1 / \partial t_n) \overline{S}_1^{-1}$ is independent of $t$.
Since

$$
(\partial \bar {S} _ {1} / \partial t _ {n}) \bar {S} _ {1} ^ {- 1} (t) = (\partial / \partial t _ {n}) \left(f ^ {\prime} \left(t _ {1} - (d / d x) ^ {- 1}, t _ {2} - 2 ^ {- 1} (d / d x) ^ {- 2}, \dots\right) - f ^ {\prime} (t)\right)
$$

and $f'$ consists only of cubic and higher order terms, this implies that $f' = 0$ and $\overline{S} = \overline{S}_0$.
QED.

By (34), $\overline{\tau}$ corresponds to a finite dimensional solution $L = L(t, \zeta)$ to the KP hierarchy with dimension equal to $\mathrm{rank} \, \bar{A} \leq g$.
Indeed, (34) is a slight modification of (3): Denoting $Q(t, \zeta) = Q'(t) + Q''(t, \zeta)$ with $Q'$ a quadratic form in $t$ and $Q''$ consisting of at most linear terms in $t$, we observe that $\exp(-Q''(t, \zeta)) \overline{\tau}(t, \zeta) = \exp(Q'(t)) \vartheta(\zeta + \bar{A} t)$ is also a $\tau$-function corresponding to $L$ and having the form (3).
Moreover, in our construction of $\overline{\tau}$, it is obvious that the first three columns of $A$ are nothing but $a_1, a_2, a_3$ in the condition (A i) of the theorem.
Thus to complete the proof of $(\mathbf{A}^*) \Rightarrow (\mathbf{C})$, it suffices to observe the following:

**Lemma 14.** Let $X = \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} / \Gamma$ be a principally polarized abelian variety, and let $\Theta$ be the theta divisor of $X$.
Let $\bar{A} \neq 0$ be a $\mathrm{g} \times \infty$-matrix.
Suppose that $\Sigma'' = \bigcap \{\Theta + v; v \in \bar{A} \mathbb{C}^{\infty}\}$ has codimension $\geq 2$ in $X$.
If there exists a quadratic form $Q(t)$ such that for any $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$,

$$
\bar {\tau} (t, \zeta) = \exp (Q (t)) \vartheta (\bar {A} t + \zeta) \tag {3}
$$

is a $\tau$-function for the KP hierarchy, then $\bar{A}$ is of rank $g$.

**Proof.** Let $\tilde{Y}$ be the image of the linear map $\bar{A} \colon \mathbb{C}^{\infty} \to \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}}$, and let $Y = \tilde{Y} / (\tilde{Y} \cap \Gamma) \subset X$.
If $\bar{Y}$ is the closure of $Y$ in $X$, then either $\bar{Y} = Y$, or $\bar{Y} - Y$ is dense in $\bar{Y}$.
We first show that $Y$ is closed in $X$.
Let $\bar{S}$ be the wave operator corresponding to $\bar{\tau}$, let $L = \bar{S}(d/dx)\bar{S}^{-1}$, and let

$$
\bar {S} _ {\zeta} = \bar {S} (0, \zeta), \quad L _ {\zeta} = L (0, \zeta),
$$

$$
R _ {\zeta} = \{P \in D; [ P, L _ {\zeta} ] = 0 \}, \quad \bar {R} _ {\zeta} = \bar {S} _ {\zeta} ^ {- 1} R _ {\zeta} \bar {S} _ {\zeta}.
$$

By Lemma 1 a) of Sect.
2, $\bar{R}_{\zeta} \subset \mathbb{C}((d / dx)^{-1}))$ for each $\zeta \in \mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} - \Theta$.
Moreover,

i) The map $\zeta \mapsto \bar{R}_{\zeta}$ is holomorphic on $\mathbb{C}^{\mathrm{g}} - \Theta$;
ii) $\bar{R}_{\zeta}$ is periodic with period $\Gamma$;
iii) For any $\zeta_0, \bar{R}_{\zeta}$ is constant along $\tilde{Y} + \zeta_0$.

Indeed, i) follows from the definition of $\bar{R}_{\zeta}$ and the relation

$$
R _ {\zeta} = \left\{c _ {0} + \sum_ {j} c _ {j} \left(L ^ {j}\right) _ {+}; \left(c _ {j}\right) _ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} \in \ker \bar {A} \right\} \tag {39}
$$

(see Sect.
2.0). ii) follows from the quasiperiodicity of $\overline{\tau}$, while iii) from the translation equivariance of $\overline{\tau}$ and Lemma 1 a) of Sect.
2.

By i), iii) and the assumptions that $\Theta$ does not contain $Y + \zeta$ unless $\zeta \in \Sigma''$, and that $\mathrm{codim} \, \Sigma'' \geq 2$, we see that $\bar{R}_{\zeta}$ is entire.
Then by ii) it is constant in $\zeta$, so we denote it simply by $\bar{R}$.
(39) also shows that $R_{\zeta}$ is holomorphic in $\zeta$ away from $\Theta$ (with poles on $\Theta$).
Thus assigning the triple $(C_{\zeta}, p_{\zeta}, \mathcal{F}_{\zeta})$ to each $R_{\zeta}$ as in Burchnall, Chaundy and Krichever's theorem, we observe that $C_{\zeta} = C$ and $p_{\zeta}$

Jacobian varieties

$= p$ are constant in $\zeta \in X$, that the arithmetic genus of $C$ is rank $\bar{A}$, and that the map

$$
X \ni \zeta \mapsto \mathcal{F}_{\zeta} \in \overline{\operatorname{Pic}(C)}
$$

gives an embedding of $X$ into $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}(C)}$, the moduli space of torsion free rank 1 sheaves $\mathcal{F}$ on $C$ with $\chi(C, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
Now by the translation equivariance of $\bar{\tau}$, the image of $Y + \zeta$ under this map is the orbit of $\mathcal{F}_{\zeta}$ under the time evolution by the KP hierarchy, which is the action of $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$ by the tensor product.
Since $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}(C)}$ is an algebraic variety and this action is algebraic [5], $Y + \zeta$ must be locally closed, hence closed.

Next, the closedness of $Y$ implies the surjectivity of $\bar{A}$ as follows.
Since $Y$ is closed and is isomorphic to the effective parameter space $T_{L}$ of $L(\cdot, \zeta)$, the solution $L(\cdot, \zeta)$ is compact for any $\zeta \notin \Sigma''$.
By Lemma 5 and corollary to Lemma 4, this implies that $C$ is compact, and that for any $\zeta \in X - \Sigma''$, the restriction of $\Theta$ to $Y + \zeta$ gives the principal polarization on $Y + \zeta$, by which $Y + \zeta$ is isomorphic to $\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)$ up to translation.
Hence denoting by $\Theta_{Y}$ the theta divisor of $X$ restricted to $Y$ and considering this to be a subvariety of $X$ as well as a subvariety of $Y$, we observe that the theta divisor restricted to $Y + \zeta$ must have the form $\Theta_{Y} + f(\zeta)$ if $\zeta \notin \Sigma''$.
Since $\Sigma''$ has codimension $\geq 2$ in $X$, this defines the map $f: X \to X$.
Since $f$ is constant on each translate $Y + \zeta$ on $Y$, we have $f(X) \cong X / Y$.
Hence $X = Y \times f(X)$ with $\Theta = \Theta_{Y} \times f(X)$, which cannot represent an ample line bundle on $X$ unless $Y = X$.
This proves the surjectivity of $\bar{A}$.
QED.
