# On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

J.-P.
Labesse¹ and J.
Schwermer²

¹ Dépt.
de Mathématiques, Université de Dijon, B.P.
n° 138, F-21004 Dijon Cedex, France
² Mathematisches Institut der Universität, Wegelerstr.
10, D-5300 Bonn 1, Federal Republic of Germany

# § 0. Introduction

Let $\Gamma \subset G(E)$ be an arithmetic subgroup of a semi simple connected algebraic group $G$ defined over an algebraic number field $E$ , and let $V$ be a finite dimensional complex representation of the group $G_{\infty} = G(\mathbb{R} \otimes E)$ of real points of $G$ .
Then the cusp cohomology $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^{*}(\Gamma, V)$ is a subspace of the cohomology of $\Gamma$ with coefficients in $V$ defined in analytical terms [3]; using the identification of the cohomology $H^{*}(\Gamma, V)$ of $\Gamma$ with the relative Lie algebra cohomology it is given as

$$
H _ {\text {c u s p}} ^ {*} (\Gamma , V) = H ^ {*} \left(\mathfrak {g}, K _ {\infty}; L _ {0} ^ {2} \left(\Gamma \backslash G _ {\infty}\right) \otimes V\right) \tag {1}
$$

and, in particular, it isolates a finite set (depending on $V$ ) of representations $\pi$ of $G_{\infty}$ occurring in the cuspidal spectrum $L_0^2 (\Gamma \backslash G_{\infty})$ with finite multiplicities $m(\pi ,\Gamma)$ .
The study of the cusp cohomology of $\Gamma$ is therefore the study of special types of cuspidal automorphic forms.

For example, if $G = SL_2 / \mathbb{Q}$ , $\Gamma \subset SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$ and $V = V_k$ is of dimension $k$ , then (1) is the Eichler-Shimura isomorphism

$$
H _ {\text {c u s p}} ^ {1} (\Gamma , V _ {k}) \cong S _ {k + 1} ^ {+} (\Gamma) \oplus S _ {k + 1} ^ {-} (\Gamma)
$$

where $S_{k+1}^{\pm}(\Gamma)$ is the space of holomorphic or anti-holomorphic cusp forms with respect to $\Gamma$ of weight $k+1$ ; and the dimension of $S_{k+1}^{+}(\Gamma)$ is the multiplicity $m(D_k^{\pm}, \Gamma)$ of the holomorphic (antiholomorphic) discrete series representation $D_k^{\pm}$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ .
More generally, if $G_{\infty}$ has discrete series representations the Selberg trace formula allows one to get hold of cusp cohomology classes by computing Euler-Poincaré characteristics (cf.
§2).
But if $G_{\infty}$ has no discrete series (e.g. in case $G = SL_n$ with $n &gt; 2$ or $G_{\infty} = H(\mathbb{C})$ is a complex Lie group) the Euler-Poincaré characteristics of the representations occurring in (1) vanish and the ordinary trace formula does not seem to be of any direct help.

On the other hand, there are geometrical methods as additional tools.
For example, if $\Gamma \subset SL_2(\mathcal{O}_E)$ , $\mathcal{O}_E$ a ring of imaginary quadratic integers, or $\Gamma$

384
J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

$\subset SL_3(\mathbb{Z})$ the study of a suitable involution $\sigma$ on the associated arithmetic quotient $\Gamma \backslash X = \Gamma \backslash G_{\infty} / K_{\infty}$ and the computation of the Lefschetz number of the induced map on the cohomology of $\Gamma$ led in [18, 29] and [32, 33] to rather precise results about the existence of cusp cohomology classes for a given $\Gamma$.
Some of the classes exhibited in this way are related to cusp forms for subgroups of $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$ via lifting of automorphic forms by Langlands functoriality.

It is the purpose of this paper to show that, in turn, the principle of Langlands functoriality leads in some cases to a correspondence between cusp cohomology classes for different arithmetic groups.
In particular, one obtains non vanishing cusp cohomology classes which correspond to non discrete series representations.
The main result (cf.
6.2., 6.3.) concerning arithmetic subgroups of $SL_2$ and $SL_3$ can be formulated as follows; for simplicity we assume that we are dealing with trivial coefficients.

**Theorem.** Let $E / F$ be a finite extension of a totally real algebraic number field $F$ such that there is a tower

$$
E = F _ {l} &gt; F _ {l - 1} &gt; \dots &gt; F _ {1} &gt; F _ {0} = F
$$

of intermediate fields such that $F_{i+1} / F_i$ is either a cyclic extension of prime degree or a cubic non-normal extension.
Let $\mathcal{O}_E$ be the ring of integers of $E$; there exists a subgroup $\Gamma \subset SL_m(\mathcal{O}_E)$, $m = 2,3$ of finite index such that the cusp cohomology of $\Gamma$

$$
H _ {\mathrm {c u s p}} ^ {*} (\Gamma , \mathbb {C}) \neq 0
$$

with trivial coefficients does not vanish.

Under the same assumptions there is an analogue for the cusp cohomology with $V$ as coefficients; in this case the representation $V$ has to be of a specific type.
Non vanishing results are also obtained for arithmetic subgroups of inner forms of $SL_m / E$ in 6.5. New examples of arithmetic cocompact subgroups of $SL_2(\mathbb{C})$ with non vanishing first Betti number are given in 6.6. Our results may be extended to $S$-arithmetic groups (cf.
6.7.).

We briefly indicate the outline of the paper.
In §1 we give the adelic interpretation of the cusp cohomology for congruence subgroups $\Gamma$ of $G(E)$; it will be denoted in the inductive limit by $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^*(G, E; V)$.
Then by inserting so called pseudo coefficients in the Selberg trace formula we show in §2 that there exist always cuspidal cohomology classes for a sufficiently small subgroup $\Gamma$ of $SL_2(F)$, $F$ a totally real algebraic number field, such that the corresponding cuspidal automorphic representation $\pi$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ is special at some finite place (cf.
2.5.). Now we want to apply the principle of Langlands functoriality.
It yields maps

$$
\tilde {\Psi} \colon \mathcal {A} (G L _ {2}, F) \to \mathcal {A} (G L _ {m}, E), \quad m = 2, 3
$$

between sets of equivalence classes of irreducible automorphic representations of $GL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ and $GL_m(\mathbb{A} \otimes E)$ in the following known cases:

(1) $m = 2$, $E / F$ is a cyclic extension of prime degree or a cubic non normal extension and $\tilde{\Psi}$ is the base change map [28]; (2) $m = 3$, and $\tilde{\Psi}$ is the so called

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

adjoint lift [14].
These facts are recalled in §4.
Since we are dealing with $SL_{m}$ instead of $GL_{m}$ §3 contains some general considerations concerning the transfer of automorphic representations from $GL_{n}$ to $SL_{n}$; analogous ideas are already used in the case $n = 2$ in [23].
In particular, we obtain (4.5) a bijection between the set of irreducible cuspidal representations of $GL_{n}(\mathbb{A}\otimes F)$ modulo twisting by characters and $L$-packets of irreducible cuspidal representations of $SL_{n}(\mathbb{A}\otimes F)$.
This allows us to associate to a cuspidal representation $\pi$ of $SL_{2}(\mathbb{A}\otimes F)$ with $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}}$ special at some finite place $\mathfrak{p}$ of $F$ via $\tilde{\Psi}$ a cuspidal ($L$-packet of) representation $\Psi (\pi)$ of $SL_{m}(\mathbb{A}\otimes E)$ whose local component at any place above $\mathfrak{p}$ is again special.
If moreover $\pi$ corresponds to a cusp cohomology class in $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^{*}(SL_{2},F;V)$ where $V$ denotes a finite dimensional representation of $SL_{2}(\mathbb{R}\otimes F)$, then also $\Psi (\pi)$ represents a non-trivial cohomology class in $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^{*}(SL_{m},E;\Psi (V))$ where the finite dimensional representation $\Psi (V)$ of $SL_{m}(\mathbb{R}\otimes E)$ is attached to $V$ via the infinite component of $\Psi$.
This compatibility of liftings and $(\mathfrak{g},K)$-cohomology is dealt with (even for an arbitrary $m$) in §5 where one finds also a precise description of $\Psi (V)$.
Together with the existence result this gives by a step-by-step argument our main result.

Two remarks are in order: (1) The main results described above have to be viewed as a consequence of the fact that liftings (or Langlands functoriality) define correspondences between representations of Hecke algebras (c.f.
[24]) in cohomological spaces attached to different groups.

(2) By working with $PGL_{n}$ instead of $SL_{n}$ one can avoid introducing the notion of $L$-packet, but the adelization of the problem is then less natural since $PGL_{n}$ does not satisfy strong approximation, and then one runs into equivalent difficulties.
We have preferred to present the results in an approach which will turn out to be useful in other situations.

This paper grew out of the attempt of the second author to understand a claim made by the first one in a letter commenting on [29].
We are very grateful to the Max-Planck-Institut für Mathematik, Bonn for its hospitality and support during the final period of this work.
Conversations with G.
Henniart were helpful in many respects.
We also thank Miss Deutler for typing the manuscript.

§ 1. Cusp cohomology

1.1. Let $G$ be a connected semi simple algebraic group defined over an algebraic number field $F$; we fix a maximal compact subgroup $K_{\infty}$ in the Lie group $G_{\infty} := G(\mathbb{R} \otimes F)$.
Let $\mathbb{A}$ (resp.
$\mathbb{A}_f$) denote the ring of adeles (resp.
finite adeles) of $\mathbb{Q}$, and, for a given compact open subgroup $K$ of $G(\mathbb{A}_f \otimes F)$, we put $\Gamma = G(F) \cap K$.

Assume now that $G = SL_{n}$; since $G$ is quasi simple and simply connected the strong approximation theorem [21] implies that there is a natural bijection

$$
S _ {K} := G (F) \backslash G (\mathbb {A} \otimes F) / K _ {\infty} K = \Gamma \backslash G _ {\infty} / K _ {\infty}.
$$

Given a finite dimensional complex representation $V$ of $G_{\infty}$ one has a corresponding local system $\tilde{V}$ on $S_K$; since $G_{\infty} / K_{\infty} = X$ is contractible the cohomol-

J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

ogy of $\Gamma$ with coefficients in $V$ is isomorphic to the de Rham cohomology of $S_K$ with coefficients in $\tilde{V}$:

$$
H^*(\Gamma, V) = H^*(S_K, \tilde{V}).
$$

Denote by $\mathfrak{g}$ the Lie algebra of $G_\infty$; then the de Rham cohomology may be identified with the relative Lie algebra cohomology [4]

$$
H^*(S_K, \tilde{V}) = H^*(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty, C^\infty(\Gamma \setminus G_\infty) \otimes V).
$$

Since it will be convenient for us to work with arbitrarily small compact open subgroups $K$ we form the inductive limit

$$
H^*(G, F; V) := \varinjlim_K H^*(S_K, \tilde{V}).
$$

Using that $C^\infty(\Gamma \setminus G_\infty)$ is isomorphic to the space $C^\infty(G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F))^K$ of $K$-invariant smooth functions on $G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ as a $(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty)$-module one sees that

$$
H^*(G, F; V) = H^*(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty, C^\infty(G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)) \otimes V).
$$

This may also be seen as the inductive limit of the $H^*(\Gamma, V)$ where $\Gamma$ runs through the set of congruence subgroups of $G(F)$.

1.2. As usual we denote by $\rho_0$ the representation of $G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ acting by right translations on the space $L_0^2(G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F))$ of square integrable cuspidal functions on $G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$; it decomposes into a direct Hilbert sum of irreducible admissible representations $\pi$ with finite multiplicities $m_0(\pi)$, i.e.

$$
\rho_0 = \bigoplus m_0(\pi) \pi.
$$

Let $L_0^2(G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F))^\infty$ be the space of $C^\infty$-vectors (i.e. vectors which are $K$-finite for some $K$ and $C^\infty$ in the usual sense for the action of $G_\infty$).
Then the natural map in cohomology induced by inclusion

$$
j^*: H^*(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty, L_0^2(G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F))^\infty \otimes V) \to H^*(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty, C^\infty(G(F) \setminus G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)) \otimes V)
$$

is injective ([1], 5.5). By definition the cusp cohomology $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^*(G, F; V)$ with coefficients in $V$ is the image of $j^*$ and one has ([4] XIII)

$$
H^*_{\mathrm{cusp}}(G, F; V) = \bigoplus_{\pi = \pi_\infty \otimes \pi_f} m_0(\pi) H^*(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty, \pi_\infty \otimes V) \otimes \pi_f.
$$

By taking the $K$-invariants one defines in the same way for a fixed $K$ the cusp cohomology of $S_K$ and one obtains

$$
H^*_{\mathrm{cusp}}(S_K, \tilde{V}) = \bigoplus_{\pi = \pi_\infty \otimes \pi_f} m_0(\pi) H^*(\mathfrak{g}, K_\infty, \pi_\infty \otimes V) \otimes \pi_f^K
$$

where $\pi_f^K$ is the space of $K$-fixed vectors in $\pi_f$.

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

# § 2. Some results in harmonic analysis

2.1. Mainly to fix notations we have to recall some known facts on the representation theory of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$.
Let $k \geq 1$ be a natural integer, we shall denote by $V_k$ the irreducible complex representation of dimension $k$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$.
This representation occurs as a quotient of the (non-unitary) principal series representation $I_{k,\varepsilon_k}$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$, associated to the character $t \to |t|^k \varepsilon_k(\mathrm{sign} t)$ of $\mathbb{R}^\times$, where $\varepsilon_k(-1) = (-1)^{k-1}$ and the other constituents of $I_{k,\varepsilon_k}$ are two discrete series representations $D_k^+$ and $D_k^-$; more precisely one has the exact sequence

$$
0 \to D _ {k} ^ {+} \oplus D _ {k} ^ {-} \to I _ {k, \varepsilon_ {k}} \to V _ {k} \to 0
$$

(Observe that $D_k^+$ and $D_k^-$ correspond to holomorphic and antiholomorphic cusp forms of weight $k + 1$.)

Let $\pi$ be an admissible irreducible representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$.
Then one easily checks that

$$
H ^ {q} (\mathfrak {g}, K _ {\infty}, \pi \otimes V _ {k}) = 0 \quad \text{for all } q
$$

unless $\pi = D_k^{\pm}$ or $\pi = V_{k}$ where one has

$$
H ^ {q} (\mathfrak {g}, K _ {\infty}, D _ {k} ^ {\pm} \otimes V _ {k}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \mathbb {C} &amp; q = 1 \\ 0 &amp; \text{otherwise} \end{array} \right.
$$

$$
H ^ {q} (\mathfrak {g}, K _ {\infty}, V _ {k} \otimes V _ {k}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \mathbb {C} &amp; q = 0, 2 \\ 0 &amp; \text{otherwise}. \end{array} \right.
$$

Then using pseudo coefficients constructed in [8, II, 4.2.8] (or to be more up to date by referring to [7]) one obtains when $F$ is a totally real number field the

2.2. Lemma.
Let $V$ be a finite dimensional representation of $G_{\infty} = SL_2(\mathbb{R}\otimes F)$; then there exists a compactly supported $C^\infty$-function $\phi_V$ on $G_{\infty}$ such that for any admissible representation $\pi$ of $G_{\infty}$ the trace of $\pi (\phi_V)$ equals the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of $\pi \otimes V$ i.e. $\operatorname{tr}\pi (\phi_V) = \chi (\pi ,V)$ where

$$
\chi (\pi , V) \colon = \sum_ {q = 0} ^ {\dim G _ {\infty} / K _ {\infty}} (- 1) ^ {q} \dim H ^ {q} (\mathfrak {g}, K _ {\infty}; \pi \otimes V).
$$

For the convenience of the reader we sketch the argument.
One easily reduces the problem to the case where $G_{\infty} = SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ and $\pi$ and $V$ are irreducible.
Now let $s \to \varphi(is)$ be an entire function on the complex plane such that $\varphi$ is the Fourier transform of some $C^\infty$-compactly supported function on $\mathbb{R}$ and such that moreover $\varphi(s) = \varphi(-s)$ and $\varphi(in) \neq 0$ where $n = \dim V$.
Fix an integer $r$; by the Paley Wiener Theorem for $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ [9] there exists a function $g_r \in C_c^\infty(SL_2(\mathbb{R}))$ such that

$$
g _ {r} \left(k _ {1} x k _ {2}\right) = \chi_ {r} \left(k _ {1}\right) g _ {r} (x) \chi_ {r} \left(k _ {2}\right)
$$

J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

where $x \in SL_2(\mathbb{R})$, $k_i \in SO(2)$ and $\chi_r$ being the $r$-th power of the basic character of $SO(2)$, and such that moreover

$$
\operatorname{tr} I_{s, \varepsilon}(g_r) = \varphi(i s)
$$

if $\varepsilon(-1) = (-1)'$ and is zero otherwise.
Now let

$$
\phi_V = (g_{n-1} + g_{-(n-1)} - g_{n+1} - g_{-(n+1)}) \varphi(i n)^{-1}.
$$

We obtain by construction

$$
for \pi = D_k^{\pm} \quad \operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_V) = \begin{cases} -1 &amp; \text{if } k = n \\ 0 &amp; \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
for \pi = V_k \quad \operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_V) = \begin{cases} +2 &amp; \text{if } k = n \\ 0 &amp; \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

and for $\pi$ a principal series representation $\operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_V) = 0$.
By 2.1 this proves the claim.

Remark.
Functions of this type have been used in the study of Shimura varieties modeled on $GL_2$ (cf.
[24], [5, p. 402]).

2.3. Let $F_\mathfrak{p}$ be a local non-archimedean field, and denote by $\sigma_\mathfrak{p}$ the Steinberg (or special) representation of $G_\mathfrak{p} = SL_2(F_\mathfrak{p})$.
If $H$ is some open compact subgroup of $G_\mathfrak{p}$ we define $f_H$ to be the characteristic function of $H$ divided by its volume.
Let $\mathcal{C}_\mathfrak{p}$ be the ring of integers of $F_\mathfrak{p}$ and $\omega_\mathfrak{p}$ a uniformizing element in $\mathcal{C}_\mathfrak{p}$; then we introduce the open compact subgroups

$$
K_\mathfrak{p} = SL_2(\mathcal{C}_\mathfrak{p}), \quad K'_\mathfrak{p} = \begin{pmatrix} \omega_\mathfrak{p} &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; 1 \end{pmatrix} K_\mathfrak{p} \begin{pmatrix} \omega_\mathfrak{p}^{-1} &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; 1 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{and} \quad B_\mathfrak{p} = K_\mathfrak{p} \cap K'_\mathfrak{p}.
$$

The group $B_\mathfrak{p}$ is an Iwahori subgroup of $G_\mathfrak{p}$.
Then the function $\phi_\mathfrak{p}$ in $C_c^\infty(G_\mathfrak{p})$ defined by

$$
\phi_{\mathfrak{p}} := f_{K_\mathfrak{p}} + f_{K'_\mathfrak{p}} - f_{B_\mathfrak{p}}
$$

has properties analogous to the function $\phi_V$ in 2.2.

2.4. Lemma.
For any irreducible admissible representation $\pi$ of $G_\mathfrak{p}$ one has $\operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_\mathfrak{p}) = 0$ unless $\pi = \pi_0$ is the trivial representation then $\operatorname{tr} \pi_0(\phi_\mathfrak{p}) = 1$ or $\pi = \sigma_\mathfrak{p}$ is the Steinberg representation then $\operatorname{tr} \sigma_\mathfrak{p}(\phi_\mathfrak{p}) = -1$.

Remark.
By [4, XI, 3.9.] this can be reformulated as $\operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_\mathfrak{p}) = \chi_\mathfrak{p}(\pi)$ for any irreducible admissible unitary representation $\pi$ of $G_\mathfrak{p}$ where $\chi_\mathfrak{p}(\pi)$ denotes the Euler Poincaré characteristic of the continuous cohomology of $G_\mathfrak{p}$ with coefficients in $\pi$.

Proof.
Since $\phi_\mathfrak{p}$ is biinvariant under the Iwahori subgroup $B_\mathfrak{p}$ one observes that $\operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_\mathfrak{p})$ can only be non-zero in subquotients of unramified principal representations of $G_\mathfrak{p}$ [30].
One therefore has to check the following cases: irreducible unramified principal series, limits of unramified discrete series, the trivial

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

and the Steinberg representation.
We refer to [22, p. 171] for analogous arguments.
If $S$ is some finite set of finite places of $F$ we define $\phi_S = \bigotimes_{\mathfrak{p} \in S} \phi_{\mathfrak{p}}$.

2.5. Proposition.
Let $F$ be a totally real algebraic number field, fix a finite dimensional representation $V$ of $G_{\infty} = SL_2(\mathbb{R} \otimes F)$, and a finite set $S$ of finite places.
Then there exists an irreducible admissible representation $\pi$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ such that

(1) $m_0(\pi)\neq 0$ i.e. $\pi$ is a cuspidal automorphic representation for $SL_2$ over $F$
(2) $\chi (\pi_{\infty},V)\neq 0$ and $\pi_{\infty}$ is a discrete series representation
(3) $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}} = \sigma_{\mathfrak{p}}$ the Steinberg representation of $SL_2(F_{\mathfrak{p}})$ for all $\mathfrak{p}\in S$

We have a decomposition $SL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F) = G_{\infty} \times G_S \times G^S$ and we consider a function $\phi = \phi_V \otimes \phi_S \otimes \phi^S$ on $SL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ where $\phi_V$ and $\phi_S$ have been constructed in 2.2 and 2.4 and where $\phi^S$ is the characteristic function of a compact open subgroup $K^S$ (of the group of finite adeles outside $S$) divided by its volume.
We may (and will) assume that $S$ in non-empty.

We apply the Selberg trace formula for $SL_2$ over $F$ to $\phi$ (see [23] §5).
Since the functions $\phi_V$ and $\phi_S$ have trace zero in the principal series representation a classical argument [19, §16] shows that the terms (5.3), (5.4), (5.5) and (5.6) of [23] vanish.
The choice of $\phi_S$ shows that the "labile" contributions (5.10) also vanish since $\operatorname{tr} \pi(\phi_{\mathfrak{p}}) = 0$ for $\mathfrak{p} \in S$ if $\pi$ is a dihedral representation (see 4.1 for this notion).
Moreover, we may choose $K^S$ small enough so that the only "elliptic" conjugacy class which contributes non-trivially is the unit element.
Hence if $K^S$ is small enough the trace formula reduces to the following equality:

$$
2 ^ {| F \cap \mathbb {Q} |} m (1, V) + \sum_ {\pi \cong L _ {0} ^ {2}} m _ {0} (\pi) \operatorname {t r} \pi (\phi) = \operatorname {v o l} \left(S L _ {2} (F) \backslash S L _ {2} (\mathbb {A} \otimes F)\right) \phi (1) \tag {4}
$$

where $m(1,V)$ is the multiplicity of the trivial representation in $V$.

As a consequence of the Plancherel formula one sees that $\phi_V(1)\phi_S(1)\neq 0$; since $\phi^S (1) = 1 / \mathrm{vol}(K^S)$ the right hand side of (4) can therefore be made arbitrarily large for $K^S$ small enough.

Then we conclude that there exists some $\pi$ such that

$$
m _ {0} (\pi) \operatorname {t r} \pi (\phi) \neq 0.
$$

This implies $m_0(\pi) \neq 0$, $\operatorname{tr} \pi_{\infty}(\phi_V) = \chi(\pi_{\infty}, V) \neq 0$ and $\operatorname{tr} \pi_{\mathfrak{p}}(\phi_{\mathfrak{p}}) \neq 0$.
Since at all places $\mathfrak{q}$ of $F$, $\pi_{\mathfrak{q}}$ must be infinite dimensional, we see that $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}} = \sigma_{\mathfrak{p}}$ if $\mathfrak{p} \in S$ (and also $\pi_{\infty}$ is a discrete series representation); this proves our claim.

This kind of argument is already used in [28, p. 227-230], see also [6].

2.6. This result is obtained easily in the following classical setting.
Let $\Gamma_0(m)$ be the Hecke congruence subgroup of $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$ of level $m = \prod_{i}p_{i}$ with $p_i\neq p_j$ ($i\neq j$).
If $\varphi \in S_k(\Gamma_0(m))$ is a new form of weight $k + 1$ with respect to $\Gamma_0(m)$ and is an eigenfunction for the Hecke operators then the associated admissible representation $\pi (\varphi)$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{A})$ has among others the following properties [13, §5C]:

390
J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

(1) The infinite component $\pi(\varphi)_{\infty}$ is a discrete series representation $D_k^{\pm}$.

(2) At a finite place $p = p_i$ of $\mathbb{Q}$ the component $\pi(\varphi)_p$ is the Steinberg representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{Q}_p)$.

Assertion (2) follows from the fact that $\pi(\varphi)_p$ has conductor 1 in this case.
Note that (1) implies that $\pi(\varphi)$ contributes non trivially to the cusp cohomology $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^1(S_{K_m}, V_k)$ where $K_m \subset SL_2(\mathbb{A}_f)$ denotes the congruence subgroup corresponding to $\Gamma_0(m)$.

§ 3. The transfer from $GL_n$ to $SL_n$

The following considerations concerning the transfer of automorphic representations from $GL_n$ to $SL_n$ are an outgrowth of some elementary ideas already used in the case $n = 2$ in [23, p. 734–737 and p. 768–771].
The generalisation to the case of an arbitrary $n$ is straightforward.
In [15, 16] one can find a study of the local case but since we need the global case we work out the local and the global transfer at the same time and without using these papers.

The notion of $L$-packets introduced here is the ad-hoc definition already used in [23], and which is believed to fit into the general philosophy [27] and which agrees with all requirements one can check for the moment.

3.1. $L$-packets for $SL_n$ – Let $F$ be a local or a global field and let

$$
\begin{array}{l}
G = SL_n(F) \quad \text{or} \quad SL_n(\mathbb{A}_F) \\
\tilde{G} = GL_n(F) \quad \text{or} \quad GL_n(\mathbb{A}_F)
\end{array}
$$

respectively.
Given an admissible irreducible representation $\pi$ of $G$ and $g \in \tilde{G}$ one defines a new representation $\pi^g$ of $G$ by

$$
\pi^g(x) = \pi(g \times g^{-1}), \quad x \in G.
$$

Note that this is a formal notation which is meaningless for $(g, K_{\infty})$-modules.
When real places play a role, then this has to be understood as the compositum of $\pi$ with the outer automorphism $x \to y_{\infty} g \times g^{-1} y_{\infty}^{-1}$ where $y_{\infty} \in G_{\infty}$ with $y_{\infty} g K_{\infty} g^{-1} y_{\infty}^{-1} = K_{\infty}$ and $K_{\infty}$ is the given maximal compact subgroup of $G_{\infty}$.
For a given $g$ such an element $y_{\infty}$ always exists, since the maximal compact subgroups of $G_{\infty}$ are conjugated by inner automorphisms, and $y_{\infty}$ is well defined modulo $K_{\infty}$ on the left.
The collection of equivalence classes of such $\pi^g$ where $g$ runs through $\tilde{G}$ is called an $L$-packet of irreducible admissible representations of $SL_n$.
This notion yields a partition of the set of equivalence classes of admissible irreducible representations of $G$; the quotient set, namely the set of $L$-packets, will be denoted by $\mathcal{L}(SL_n, F)$.

We give two examples which will occur repeatedly:

(i) the discrete series representations $D_k^+$ and $D_k^-$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ for a fixed $k$ build up one $L$-packet.

(ii) the Steinberg representation of $SL_2$ over a local non archimedean field constitutes an $L$-packet with a single element.

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

For the group $\tilde{G}$ the notion of an $L$-packet is nothing else than an equivalence class of admissible irreducible representations.

Let $\mathcal{E}$ be the group of characters of the abelian quotient $G \backslash \tilde{G}$; we say that two admissible irreducible representations $\tilde{\pi}_1$ and $\tilde{\pi}_2$ of $\tilde{G}$ are $\mathcal{E}$-equivalent if there exists a $\chi \in \mathcal{E}$ such that $\tilde{\pi}_2 \cong \tilde{\pi}_1 \otimes \chi$.
The set of $\mathcal{E}$-equivalence classes of admissible irreducible representations of $\tilde{G}$ will be denoted by $\mathcal{E}(GL_n, F)$.

**3.2. Lemma.** Let $\tilde{\pi}$ be an admissible irreducible representation of $\tilde{G}$; then the restriction $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ to $G$ depends only on the $\mathcal{E}$-equivalence class of $\tilde{\pi}$, and $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ is a direct sum of admissible irreducible representations of $G$.

The first assertion is a simple observation.
For the proof of the second one we have to distinguish two cases:

(a) Suppose that $F$ is a local field, and let $Z$ be the center of $\tilde{G}$.
Since $GZ \backslash \tilde{G}$ is compact abelian the admissibility of $\tilde{\pi}|_{GZ}$ follows from [34], and $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ is also admissible since $Z$ acts by scalars.
This proves the claim in the local case.
Moreover, note that if $F$ is local and $\tilde{\pi}$ some unramified principal series representation then $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ has one and only one irreducible constituent which is spherical.

(b) In case $F$ is a global field we decompose $\tilde{\pi}$ into its local components.
Then the lemma follows from the local case and the last observation.

We assume again that $F$ is a local field; let $\tilde{\pi}$ be an admissible irreducible representation of $\tilde{G}$ and $\pi$ be some component of $\tilde{\pi}|_G$.
It follows that $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ is a sum (with multiplicity one at least when $\tilde{\pi}$ has a Whittaker model) of representations $\pi^g$, i.e. all components of $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ belong to the same $L$-packet and, conversely, all elements in the $L$-packet of $\pi$ occur in $\tilde{\pi}|_G$.

For a given admissible irreducible representation $\tilde{\pi}$ of $\tilde{G}$ the $L$-packet which contains the components of $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ will be denoted by $R_n(\tilde{\pi})$ or $R_{n,F}(\tilde{\pi})$.
By the first assertion of 3.2. this defines a map (by abuse of notation also denoted by $R_n$)

$$
R_n \colon \mathcal{E}(GL_n, F) \to \mathcal{L}(SL_n, F).
$$

**3.3. Lemma.** $R_n$ is a bijection.

We only have to prove it when $F$ is a local field, but then it is an immediate consequence of Frobenius reciprocity for admissible representations applied to the induction step from $GZ$ to $\tilde{G}$.
Here we use the fact that $\mathcal{E}$ the group of characters of $GZ \backslash \tilde{G}$ acts transitively by twistings on the irreducible constituents of the induced representation from $GZ$ to $\tilde{G}$ of any irreducible representation of $GZ$.

**Remark.** The notions of $L$-packets introduced here have obvious analogous for unitary representations.

**3.4. Cuspidal $L$-packets.** Let $F$ be a global field; an $L$-packet $\Pi \in \mathcal{L}(SL_n, F)$ is called cuspidal if some $\pi \in \Pi$ occurs non-trivially in the cuspidal spectrum $L_0^2(SL_n(F) \backslash SL_n(\mathbb{A} \otimes F))$.
The set of cuspidal $L$-packets for $SL_n$ will be denoted by $\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}_0(SL_n, F)$.
Let $\mathcal{E}\mathcal{A}_0(GL_n, F)$ be the set of equivalence classes of cuspidal

J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

irreducible representations of $GL_n$ over $F$ modulo twisting by characters of the determinant.
We point out that we consider all characters and not only Grössen-character.

3.5. Proposition.
The map $R_{n}$ induces a bijection between $\mathcal{EA}_0(GL_n,F)$ and $\mathcal{LA}_0(SL_n,F)$.

Let $Z$ be the center of $\tilde{G} = GL_n(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$, and put $Z_0 = Z \cap G$.
We consider a unitary Grössencharacter $\omega$ of $Z$; we denote the restriction of $\omega$ on $Z_0$ by $\omega_0$.
We may confine ourselves to consider representations of $\tilde{G}$ and $G$ that admit $\omega$ and $\omega_0$ as central characters respectively.
Let $\rho_{0,\omega_0}$ be the representation of $G = SL_n(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ acting by right translations on the space

$$
L _ {0} ^ {2} \left(S L _ {n} (F) \backslash S L _ {n} (\mathbb {A} \otimes F), \omega_ {0}\right)
$$

of cuspidal square integrable functions which transform under $Z_0$ by $\omega_0$.
We may extend $\rho_{0,\omega_0}$ to a representation $\rho_{0,\omega}^1$ of the group

$$
G ^ {1} = Z G L _ {n} (F) S L _ {n} (\mathbb {A} \otimes F)
$$

on the same representation space as follows: for a given function $f$ in $L_0^2 (SL_n(F)\backslash SL_n(A\otimes F),\omega_0)$ we put

$$
\left(\rho_ {0, \omega} ^ {1} (z \gamma x) f\right) (y) = \omega (z) f \left(\gamma^ {- 1} y \gamma x\right)
$$

where $z \in Z$, $\gamma \in GL_n(F)$ and $x \in SL_n(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$.
Now we consider the representation $\tilde{\rho}_{0,\omega}$ of $\tilde{G}$ acting by right translations on the space $L_0^2(GL_n(F) \backslash GL_n(\mathbb{A} \otimes F), \omega)$ of cuspidal square integrable (modulo $ZGL_n(F)$) functions which transform under $Z$ by $\omega$.
Using induction by stages one sees that

$$
\tilde {\rho} _ {0, \omega} = \operatorname {I n d} _ {G ^ {1}} ^ {\tilde {G}} (\rho_ {0, \omega} ^ {1}).
$$

This has the following consequences:

(i) The representation $\rho_{0,\omega}^{1}$ is a discrete sum of irreducible representations of $G^{1}$ (with multiplicity one).

(ii) Each $\tilde{\pi} \in \mathcal{A}_0(GL_n, F)$ with central character $\omega$ occurs (with multiplicity one) in $\mathrm{Ind}_{G^1}^{\tilde{G}}(\pi^1)$ for some constituent $\pi^1$ of $\rho_{0,\omega}^1$.

On the other hand since $\rho_{0,\omega}^{1}$ and $\rho_{0,\omega_0}$ are representations on the same space we see that a constituent $\pi^1$ of $\rho_{0,\omega}^{1}$ restricted to $G$ splits into a direct sum with finite multiplicities of irreducible admissible representations of $G$.
Let $\pi$ be some irreducible constituent of $\pi^1|_G$.
Since $\pi^1$ is an irreducible representation of $G^1$, all other components of $\pi^1|_G$ have to be equivalent to representations of the form $\pi^\gamma$ for some $\gamma \in GL_n(F)$; in particular they all belong to the same $L$-packet.
By Lemma 3.2 we see that $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ and $\pi^1|_G$ are (the Hilbert completions of) admissible representations of $G$, and hence we may use a Frobenius reciprocity argument to see that $\tilde{\pi}|_G$ and $\pi^1|_G$ have a common admissible component; it follows that $\pi$ belongs to the $L$-packet $R_n(\tilde{\pi})$.
Note that Frobenius reciprocity applies to this situation since we are dealing with admissible representations.
Our claim is now an immediate consequence of 3.3.

3.6. Let $\mathcal{EA}(GL_n,F)$ be the set of equivalence classes of irreducible automorphic representations of $GL_{n}$ over $F$ modulo twisting by characters of the

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

determinant, and let $\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_n,F)$ be the set of $L$-packets of irreducible automorphic representations of $SL_n$ over $F$.
By Proposition 3.5 and Proposition 2 in [26] one obtains the following.

**Proposition.** The map $R_{n}$ induces a bijection between $\mathcal{E}\mathcal{A}(GL_n,F)$ and $\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_n,F)$.

The extension of the above results to twisted forms of $SL_{n}$ and $GL_{n}$ is left to the reader.

## § 4. Langlands functoriality

**4.1. Dihedral representations.** Let $E$ be a (separable) quadratic extension of a local or a global field $F$.
Let $\theta$ be a character of the absolute Weil group $W_E$ of $E$; we refer to [35] for a definition of $W_E$.
By induction to $W_F$ one obtains a two-dimensional complex representation of the absolute Weil group $W_F$ of $F$.
Langlands correspondence associates to such a representation of $W_F$ an admissible irreducible representation of $GL_2(F)$ in case $F$ is a local field and an automorphic representation of $GL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ in case $F$ is a global field [19, p. 396]; this representation is called dihedral and usually denoted by $\pi(\theta)$.

Now suppose that $F$ is a global field and that $\mathfrak{p}$ is some place of $F$.
In case $\mathfrak{p}$ does not split in $E$ then $\pi(\theta)_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is a (local) dihedral representation of $GL_2(E \otimes F_{\mathfrak{p}})$.
In case $\mathfrak{p}$ splits in $E$ then $E \otimes F_{\mathfrak{p}} = F_{\mathfrak{q}_1} \otimes F_{\mathfrak{q}_2}$ and $\pi(\theta)_{\mathfrak{p}} = \pi(\theta_{\mathfrak{q}_1}, \theta_{\mathfrak{q}_2})$ is a principal series representation.
If $\theta$ is a unitary character then such representations are irreducible.
From these remarks and the classification of irreducible admissible representation of $GL_2$ [19] one obtains the following.

**4.2. Lemma.** Let $\pi = \pi(\theta)$ be a global dihedral representation of $GL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ where $\theta$ is a unitary character of $W_E$, $E/F$ a quadratic extension.
Then a local component $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}}$ of $\pi$ cannot be a special representation.

Global dihedral representations of $GL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ are characterized by the following result (cf.
[23], Prop.
6.5, p.
771).

**4.3. Proposition.** An admissible irreducible representation $\pi$ of $GL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ is dihedral if and only if there exists a non trivial Grössencharacter $\varepsilon$ of $F$ such that $\pi \otimes \varepsilon \cong \pi$.
In such a case the character $\varepsilon$ is the one attached by class field theory to a quadratic extension $E$ of $F$, and $\pi = \pi(\theta)$ for some character $\theta$ of the absolute Weil group $W_E$.

**4.4. Instances of Langlands functoriality.**

Let $F$ be an algebraic number field, denote by $\mathcal{A}(G,F)$ the set of equivalence classes of irreducible automorphic representations of $G(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ and denote by $\mathcal{A}_0(G,F)$ the subset of $\mathcal{A}(G,F)$ of equivalence classes of cuspidal representations.
Some instances of Langlands functoriality are known to hold; they yield maps

$$
\tilde{\Psi}_i \colon \mathcal{A}(GL_n, F) \to \mathcal{A}(GL_m, E)
$$

in the following three cases $i = 1, 2, 3$:

J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

(1) $E / F$ is a cyclic extension of prime degree, $n = m = 2$ and $\bar{\Psi}_1$ is the base change map defined in [28];
(2) $E / F$ is a cubic non normal extension, $n = m = 2$ and $\bar{\Psi}_2$ is the base change map defined in [20];
(3) $E = F$, $n = 2$, $m = 3$ and $\bar{\Psi}_3$ is the so called adjoint lifting defined in [14].

We need the following results.

## 4.5. Proposition.

The maps $\bar{\Psi}_i$, $i = 1,2,3$, have the following properties.

(a) If $\pi \in \mathcal{A}(GL_2,F)$ is a cuspidal non dihedral representation, then $\bar{\Psi}_i(\pi)$ is cuspidal.
(b) If $\mathfrak{p}$ is a finite place of $F$ and if the local component $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}}$ of $\pi$ in $\mathcal{A}(GL_2,F)$ is a special representation then $\bar{\psi}_i(\pi)_{\mathfrak{q}}$ is a special representation if $i = 1,2$ or the Steinberg representation if $i = 3$ for any finite place $\mathfrak{q}$ of $E$ above $\mathfrak{p}$.
(c) The maps $\bar{\Psi}_i$ are compatible with twisting by characters, i.e.

$$
\bar {\Psi} _ {i} (\pi \otimes \chi) = \bar {\Psi} _ {i} (\pi) \otimes \bar {\Psi} _ {i} (\chi).
$$

Property (a) follows from [28] Lemma 11.3., p.
215 in case (1), from [20, p. 569] in case (2) and from [14, Thm. 9.3] and proposition 4.3 above in case (3).

Property (b) follows from 11.40., p.
221 and Lemma 7.6., p.
102 of [28] in case (1), from [20, p. 568] in case (2) and from [14, p. 489] and 4.3 in case (3).

Property (c) is readily checked using the definition of the base change map in cases (1) and (2).
In the case $i = 3$ one simply has $\bar{\Psi}_i(\pi \otimes \chi) = \bar{\Psi}_i(\pi)$ and the adjoint lifting is trivial on characters.

We now will consider any composite $\bar{\Psi}$ of any number of the above maps $\bar{\Psi}_i$ (in case it is defined).
Property (c) in 4.5 implies that $\bar{\Psi}$ induces a map

$$
\bar {\Psi} \colon \mathcal {E A} (G L _ {n}, F) \rightarrow \mathcal {E A} (G L _ {m}, E).
$$

Using 3.6. one can define a map

$$
\Psi \colon \mathcal {L A} (S L _ {n}, F) \rightarrow \mathcal {L A} (S L _ {m}, E)
$$

in the following obvious way by putting $\Psi = R_{m,E} \circ \overline{\Psi} \circ R_{n,F}^{-1}$.
This transfer from $GL_n$ to $SL_n$ allows us to obtain as an immediate consequence of 4.5. and 4.2. the following result.

## 4.6. Proposition.

Let $F$ be an algebraic number field, and let $E / F$ be an extension such that there is a well defined associate map $\bar{\Psi}$ (and hence a map $\Psi$) given as a composite of some $\bar{\Psi}_i$.
If $\pi$ is an admissible irreducible representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{A} \otimes F)$ with the following properties

(1) $\pi$ is cuspidal
(2) For some finite place $\mathfrak{p}$ of $F$ the local component $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}}$ of $\pi$ is the Steinberg representation

then the image $\Psi(\Pi)$ in $\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_m, E)$ of the $L$-packet $\Pi$ of $\pi$ contains a representation which is cuspidal and whose local component at any place above $\mathfrak{p}$ is the Steinberg representation.

This is an immediate consequence of 4.5. and 4.2. for each $\bar{\Psi}_i$ and for $\Psi$.

By abuse of notation we write $\Psi (\pi)$ for $\Psi (\Pi)$ if $\Pi$ is the $L$-packet of $\pi$.

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

# § 5. Functoriality and $(\mathfrak{g}, K)$-cohomology

5.1. In the previous section we have considered maps $\Psi$ between $L$-packets of automorphic representations on different groups.
These maps are tensor products of local ones.
The aim of this section is to study the behaviour of these maps at archimedean places and specifically to check that they are compatible with $(\mathfrak{g}, K)$-cohomology (in a sense to be made precise in 5.4.). We consider only archimedean fields, and, as usual by saying representation of a reductive Lie group we understand admissible $(\mathfrak{g}, K)$-module.

It will be convenient for us to introduce the Langlands parametrization of the set of $L$-packets via classes of homomorphisms of Weil groups in $L$-groups.

Let $F$ be $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$; we recall that the connected component of the (complex) $L$-group of $SL_n$ over $F$ is $PGL_n(\mathbb{C})$ with trivial action of $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/F)$.
We also recall that the Weil group $W_F$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{C}^*$ if $F = \mathbb{C}$, and that $W_{\mathbb{R}}$ is a non-trivial extension

$$
W_{\mathbb{C}} \xrightarrow{c} W_{\mathbb{R}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}), \tag{1}
$$

which can be described as follows.
Let $W_{\mathbb{R}}$ be the subgroup of $GL_2(\mathbb{C})$ generated by the matrices

$$
c(z) = \begin{pmatrix} z &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; \overline{z} \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{with } z \in \mathbb{C}^* \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 0 &amp; -1 \\ 1 &amp; 0 \end{pmatrix};
$$

then $\sigma$ projects on the non-trivial element of $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})$ in (1).
Let $H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{F}, PGL_{n}(\mathbb{C}))$ be the set of conjugacy classes of admissible homomorphisms (i.e. continuous and semi simple) of $W_{F}$ into $PGL_{n}(\mathbb{C})$.

The Langlands parametrization in [25] (cf.
also [2]) provides us with bijective maps

$$
\Pi_{n, F} \colon H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{F}, PGL_{n}(\mathbb{C})) \to \mathcal{L}(SL_{n}, F).
$$

They can be used to describe maps

$$
\varphi_{\alpha} \colon \mathcal{L}(SL_{n}, F) \to \mathcal{L}(SL_{m}, E)
$$

which turn out to be the basic ingredients of the archimedean components of the maps $\Psi$ discussed in §4.
We use the following three cases

case $\alpha = 1$, $n = m = 2$, $F = \mathbb{R}$, $E = \mathbb{C}$.

The map $\varphi_1$ corresponds via $\Pi_*$ to the map

$$
H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{\mathbb{R}}, PGL_2(\mathbb{C})) \to H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{\mathbb{C}}, PGL_2(\mathbb{C}))
$$

given by the restriction from $W_{\mathbb{R}}$ to $W_{\mathbb{C}}$, and $\varphi_1$ is the base change map from $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ to $SL_2(\mathbb{C})$ [25, 28].

case $\alpha = 2$, $n = 2$, $m = 3$, $F = E = \mathbb{R}$.

The adjoint map $GL_2(\mathbb{C}) \to GL_3(\mathbb{C})$ induces a map from $PGL_2(\mathbb{C})$ to $PGL_3(\mathbb{C})$ and hence a map

$$
H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{\mathbb{R}}, PGL_2(\mathbb{C})) \to H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{\mathbb{R}}, PGL_3(\mathbb{C}));
$$

J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

it defines $\varphi_{2}$ via $\Pi_{\Phi}$, and $\varphi_{2}$ is the adjoint lifting from $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ to $SL_3(\mathbb{R})$ (cf.
3.1.3. [14] and [25]).

Case $\alpha = 3$, $n = 2$, $m = 3$, $F = E = \mathbb{C}$.

The map $\varphi_{3}$ is defined as $\varphi_{2}$ but $W_{\mathbb{C}}$ replaces $W_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $\varphi_{3}$ is the adjoint lifting from $SL_2(\mathbb{C})$ to $SL_3(\mathbb{C})$.

Observe that the symmetric square from $GL_2(\mathbb{C})$ to $GL_3(\mathbb{C})$ and the adjoint map induce the same map from $PGL_2(\mathbb{C})$ to $PGL_3(\mathbb{C})$.
We shall use the following fact

5.2. The maps induced by the maps $\Psi$ (in §4) at archimedean places can be expressed as a composite of some product of $\varphi_{2}$ with the trivial correspondences defined by diagonal maps

$$
\mathcal{L}(SL_n, F) \rightarrow \mathcal{L}(SL_n, F) \times \dots \times \mathcal{L}(SL_n, F).
$$

5.3. We are interested in a few representations of $SL_n(F)$ for which we shall describe the Langlands parameter i.e. the corresponding element in $H_{\mathrm{ad}}^{1}(W_{F}, PGL_{n}(\mathbb{C}))$.
Such an element can be represented by (classes of) maps from $W_{F}$ into $GL_{n}(\mathbb{C})$.

(a) The $L$-packet $\{D_k^+, D_k^-\}$, $k \geq 1$, of discrete series representations of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ is associated to the map $d_k \colon W_{\mathbb{R}} \to GL_2(\mathbb{C})$ defined by

$$
d_k(c(z)) = \begin{pmatrix} e^{ik\theta} &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; e^{-ik\theta} \end{pmatrix}
$$

if $r e^{i\theta}$ is a polar decomposition of $z$, and by

$$
d_k(\sigma) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 &amp; (-1)^k \\ 1 &amp; 0 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

The $(n-1)$-st symmetric power of $d_k$ corresponds via $\Pi_{n,\mathbb{R}}$ to an $L$-packet of representations of $SL_n(\mathbb{R})$, which we shall denote by $\pi_{k,\mathbb{R}}^{(n)}$.

An explicit description of $\pi_{k,\mathbb{R}}^{(n)}$ can be given as follows; later on we only need the case $n = 3$.
Take one of the standard maximal parabolic subgroups of $SL_3(\mathbb{R})$ with Langlands decomposition $P = {}^0\mathrm{MAN}$; one has ${}^0M \cong SL_2^{\pm}(\mathbb{R})$.
Then $\pi_{k,\mathbb{R}}^{(3)}$ is equivalent to the irreducible unitary principal series representation $\operatorname{Ind}(P, D_{2k}, 0)$ where $D_{2k}$ denotes the discrete series representation of $SL_2^{\pm}(\mathbb{R})$ whose restriction to $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ is $D_{2k}^{+} \oplus D_{2k}^{-}$.
For an arbitrary $n$, one has (denoting an $L$-packet and the direct sum of its constituents by the same letter)

$$
\pi_{k,\mathbb{R}}^{(n)} \cong \operatorname{Ind}(P, D_{(n-1)k} \otimes D_{(n-3)k} \otimes \dots, 0)
$$

where $P$ is a cuspidal parabolic subgroup such that ${}^0M$ is a subgroup of index two of a product of copies of $SL_2^{\pm}(\mathbb{R})$, and one copy of $(\pm 1)$ for $n$ odd, and one induces up a tensor product of discrete series representations of ${}^0M$ as indicated above.

By restricting the $(n-1)$-st symmetric power of $d_k$ to $W_{\mathbb{C}}$ one defines an element in $H_{\mathrm{ad}}^1(W_{\mathbb{C}}, PGL_n(\mathbb{C}))$ which corresponds via $\Pi_{n,\mathbb{C}}$ to a representation

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

$\pi_{k,\mathbb{C}}^{(n)}$ of $SL_n(\mathbb{C})$ .
If $P_0$ is the standard minimal parabolic subgroup of $SL_n(\mathbb{C})$ and $\mu_k^{(n)}$ the one-dimensional unitary representation obtained from $\mathrm{Sym}^{(n - 1)}d_kW_{\mathbb{C}}$ then $\pi_{k,\mathbb{C}}^{(n)}$ is equivalent to the representation of $SL_n(\mathbb{C})$ unitarily induced by $\mu_k^{(n)}$ from $P_0$ to $SL_n(\mathbb{C})$ .
Recall that for complex groups the $L$ -packets have only one constituent [25].

(b) The irreducible finite dimensional representation $V_{k}$ of dimension $k$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ is associated to the map $f_{k}\colon W_{\mathbb{R}}\to GL_{2}(\mathbb{C})$ defined by

$$
f _ {k} (c (z)) = \left( \begin{array}{c c} (\overline {{z}} z) ^ {k} &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; 1 \end{array} \right) \quad \text {for} \ z \in \mathbb {C} ^ {*} \operatorname {resp.} f _ {k} (\sigma) = \left( \begin{array}{c c} (- 1) ^ {k - 1} &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; 1 \end{array} \right).
$$

The symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^{(n - 1)}f_k$ defines a map from $W_{\mathbb{R}}$ into $PGL_n(\mathbb{C})$ , which corresponds via $\Pi_{n,\mathbb{R}}$ to the finite dimensional (rational) representation $V_{k}^{(n)}$ of $SL_{n}(\mathbb{R})$ with highest weight $(k - 1)\rho$ where $\rho$ denotes, as usual, the half sum of the positive roots.

The restriction of $\mathrm{Sym}^{(n - 1)}f_k$ to $W_{\mathbb{C}}$ corresponds via $\Pi_{n,\mathbb{C}}$ to the finite dimensional representation $W_{k}^{(n)}$ of $SL_n(\mathbb{C})$ which is the tensor product of the holomorphic and the antiholomorphic finite dimensional representations of $SL_n(\mathbb{C})$ with highest weight $(k - 1)\rho$ , i.e. one has

$$
W _ {k} ^ {(n)} = V _ {k} ^ {(n)} \otimes \bar {V} _ {k} ^ {(n)}.
$$

5.4. Proposition.
(1) If $\pi$ is some constituent of the $L$ -packet $\pi_{k,\mathbb{R}}^{(n)}$ of representations of $SL_n(\mathbb{R})$ , then there exists some $i &gt; 0$ such that

$$
H ^ {i} \left(\mathfrak {s l} _ {n} (\mathbb {R}), S O (n); \pi \otimes V _ {k} ^ {(n)}\right) \neq 0.
$$

(2) There exists some $i &gt; 0$ such that

$$
H ^ {i} \left(\mathfrak {s l} _ {n} (\mathbb {C}), S U (n); \pi_ {k, \mathbb {C}} ^ {(n)} \otimes W _ {k} ^ {(n)}\right) \neq 0.
$$

The representations $\pi_{k,\mathbb{R}}^{(n)}$ and $\pi_{k,\mathbb{C}}^{(n)}$ have been described as generalized principal series representations.
Using [4, III, 3.3] the proof of the proposition is an exercise left to the reader.
For the complex case we also refer to [11].

5.5. Corollary.
If $\varphi = \varphi_{1}$ , $\varphi_{2}$ or $\varphi_{3} \circ \varphi_{1}$ with $\varphi_{x}$ as in 5.2., then the representation $\varphi(D_{k}^{\pm}) \otimes \varphi(V_{k})$ has non-trivial $(\mathfrak{g}, K)$ -cohomology.

This is an immediate consequence of 5.4. and the definition of the maps $\varphi_{x}$ via the Langlands parameter.

§6.
The main result

6.1. We now consider a totally real algebraic number field $F$ , and a finite extension $E / F$ such that there is a well defined map $\Psi \colon \mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_2,F)\to \mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_m,E)$ , $m = 2,3$ (cf.
§4).
Let $V$ be an (irreducible) finite dimensional representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{R}\otimes F)$ , and let $\pi$ be a cuspidal irreducible representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{A}\otimes F)$ such that $\pi_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is the Steinberg representation for some finite place $\mathfrak{p}$ of $F$ and such that its infinite component $\pi_{\infty}$ has non trivial Euler-Poincaré

398
J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

characteristic $\chi(\pi_{\infty}, V)$ with respect to $V$.
Then we know by 2.1., 5.2. and 5.5. that any component of the cuspidal $L$-packet $\Psi(\pi)$ has non-trivial relative Lie algebra cohomology with respect to the finite dimensional representation $\Psi(V)$ of $SL_m(\mathbb{R} \otimes E)$.
Thus we get as a consequence of the existence result 2.5. the following:

**6.2. Theorem.** – Let $F$ be a totally real algebraic number field, and let $E/F$ be a finite extension such that there is a tower

$$
E = F_1 &gt; F_{1-1} &gt; \dots &gt; F_1 &gt; F_0 = F
$$

of intermediate fields such that $F_{i+1}/F_i$ is either a cyclic extension of prime degree or a cubic non-normal extension.
The map $\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_2, F) \to \mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}(SL_m, E)$ where $m = 2$ or 3 associated by 4.6. to this situation is denoted by $\Psi$.
Let $V$ be an irreducible finite dimensional representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{R} \otimes F)$.
Then the cusp cohomology with respect to the coefficient system $\Psi(V)$ does not vanish:

$$
H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^*(SL_m, E, \Psi(V)) \neq 0.
$$

**6.3. Corollary.** Let $E/F$ be a finite extension of a totally real algebraic number field $F$ such that $E/F$ satisfies the assumptions of 6.2. Consider the arithmetic group $SL_m(\mathcal{O}_E)$, $m = 2, 3$, where $\mathcal{O}_E$ denotes the ring of integers of $E$.
Then there exists a congruence subgroup $\Gamma \subset SL_m(\mathcal{O}_E)$ such that the cusp cohomology of $\Gamma$ with trivial coefficients

$$
H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^*(\Gamma, \mathbb{C}) \neq 0
$$

does not vanish.

**Remarks.** (1) This corollary is also true for a non trivial coefficient system of the form $\Psi(V)$ as in 6.2. One should note that in general the cusp cohomology $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^*(\Gamma, W)$ of an arithmetic subgroup $\Gamma$ of $SL_n(\mathcal{O}_E)$ vanishes unless the highest weight of the given finite dimensional representation $W$ of $SL_n(\mathbb{R} \otimes E)$ satisfies a rather strong condition given in [36, §5], automatically satisfied by our $\Psi(V)$.

(2) In the case $m = 2$ and $E = F$ is a totally real extension of $\mathbb{Q}$ of degree $d$ 6.3. is a classical result.
Moreover one has $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^i(\Gamma, \mathbb{C}) = 0$ if $i \neq d$, $\Gamma \subset SL_2(\mathcal{O}_F)$, by 2.1. (cf.
e.g. [18]).

If $m = 2$ and $E$ is an imaginary quadratic extension of $F = \mathbb{Q}$ then 6.3. is obtained by a geometric method in [17].
It can also be deduced from the analysis of an involution and the associated Lefschetz number on the cohomology worked out in [32]; see also [18, §3].

(3) In the case $m = 3$ and $E = F = \mathbb{Q}$ this result is in [29]; there one finds a lower bound for the dimension of $H_{\mathrm{cusp}}^*(\Gamma(m), \mathbb{C})$ where $\Gamma(m)$ is a congruence subgroup of level $m$ when $m$ is prime and congruent to 3 mod 8 and to $-1$ mod 3.

**6.4. The compact case.** Let $G$ be $SL_m$, $m = 2, 3$, over an algebraic number field $E$, we assume that $E$ satisfies the assumptions in 6.2. We consider an inner form $G'$ of $G$, and we denote by $S = S_G$ the (finite) set of places $v$ of $E$ where $G'$ is non-trivially twisted.
Since $m = 2$ or 3 this is equivalent to say that $G'(E_v)$ is compact.
Moreover, we assume that $S$ does not contain all archimedean places; observe that in the case $m = 3$, $S$ contains no archimedean place.

On liftings and cusp cohomology of arithmetic groups

Given a cuspidal $L$-packet $\Pi$ of irreducible representations of $G$ over $E$ such that $\Pi_v$ is square integrable for $v \in S$ we see by [19 §16] for $m = 2$ and [12] for $m = 3$ that there exists an automorphic $L$-packet $\Pi'$ of representations of $G'$ over $E$ such that $\Pi_v = \Pi_v'$ for $v \notin S$.
We shall write $\Pi = \theta(\Pi')$.

In addition, if we assume that $\Pi_{\infty} = \bigotimes_{v|\infty}\Pi_v$ has non-trivial relative Lie algebra cohomology with coefficients in $W = \bigotimes_{v|\infty}W_v$ then this holds true for $\Pi_{\infty}' = \bigotimes_{v|\infty}\Pi_v'$ with coefficients in $W' = \bigotimes W_v'$ where $W_v'$ is defined as follows: if $v \notin S$ take $W_v' = W_v$, if $v \in S$ then $W_v$ is a finite dimensional representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ and hence $W_v$ is the restriction to $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ of a holomorphic representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{C})$ whose restriction to $SU(2)$ defines $W_v'$.

Now let $E / F$ be an extension as in 6.2., using the notation there we consider $W = \Psi(V)$.
By proposition 2.5. and the argument in 6.2, there exists a cuspidal representation $\pi$ of $G$ over $E$ such that $H^{*}(\mathfrak{sl}_{m}(E \otimes \mathbb{R}), K_{\infty}; \pi \otimes W)$ does not vanish and such that $\pi_{v}$ is the Steinberg representation if $v \in S$ is a finite place of $E$.
Observe that $\pi_{v}$ is a discrete series for all archimedean places $v \in S$.
Such a representation $\pi$ belongs to an $L$-packet $\Pi$ of the form $\theta(\Pi')$, and hence for any constituent $\pi'$ of $\Pi'$ we have $H^{*}(\mathfrak{g}', K_{\infty}', \pi' \otimes W') \neq 0$.
We denote by $H^{*}(G', E, W')$ the inductive limit of $H^{*}(\Gamma', W')$ where $\Gamma' \subset G'(\mathcal{O}_E)$ runs through the congruence subgroups of $G'$ over $E$.
As in §1 one obtains under the assumption that $S = S_{G'}$ is non empty (cf.
[4])

$$
H ^ {*} (G ^ {\prime}, E, W ^ {\prime}) = \underset {\pi^ {\prime} = \pi_ {\infty} ^ {\prime} \otimes \pi_ {f} ^ {\prime}} {\oplus} m (\pi^ {\prime}) H ^ {*} (\mathfrak {g} ^ {\prime}, K ^ {\prime}, \pi_ {\infty} ^ {\prime} \otimes W ^ {\prime}) \otimes \pi_ {f} ^ {\prime}
$$

where $m(\pi')$ denotes the multiplicity with which $\pi'$ occurs in

$$
L ^ {2} \left(G ^ {\prime} (E) \backslash G ^ {\prime} (\mathbb {A} \otimes E)\right).
$$

Let $H_{\theta \text{-cusp}}^{*}(G', E, W')$ be the subspace spanned by automorphic representations $\pi'$ such that $\theta(\pi')$ is a cuspidal automorphic $L$-packet of $G$ over $E$.
In an analogous way we define $H_{\theta \text{-cusp}}^{*}(\Gamma', W')$ for a congruence subgroup $\Gamma' \subset G'(\mathcal{O}_E)$.
(Note that $\theta(\pi')$ is cuspidal if and only if $\pi'$ is infinite dimensional when $m = 2$ or 3 and $S$ is non empty).

6.5. Proposition.
Let $E / F$ be a finite extension of a totally real algebraic number field, which satisfies the assumptions in 6.2. Let $G'$ over $E$ be an inner form of $G = SL_{m} / E$, $m = 2$ or 3, twisted non trivially over a finite non empty set $S$ not containing all archimedean places.
Given a finite dimensional irreducible representation of $SL_2(\mathbb{R} \otimes F)$ let $W'$ be attached to $W = \Psi(V)$ (cf.
6.2.) as indicated above.
Then

$$
H _ {\theta - \operatorname {c u s p}} ^ {*} (G ^ {\prime}, E, W ^ {\prime}) \neq 0.
$$

6.6. Let $G = SL_2 / E$, and assume that all archimedean places of $E$ except one are real.
Let $G' / E$ be some inner form of $G$ such that $S_{G'} = S$ contains all real places of $E$.
A sufficiently small congruence subgroup $\Gamma'$ of $G'$ over $E$ defines then an arithmetic subgroup of $SL_2(\mathbb{C})$ again denoted by $\Gamma'$.
Then proposition 6.5. gives that

$$
H ^ {1} (\Gamma^ {\prime}, \mathbb {C}) \neq 0
$$

for small enough $\Gamma'$, in other words the first cohomology group of $\Gamma'$ with trivial coefficients does not vanish.

400
J.-P.
Labesse and J.
Schwermer

Via the exceptional Lie algebra isomorphism $\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb{C})\simeq \mathfrak{so}(3,1)$ one can produce arithmetic cocompact subgroups $\Gamma'$ of $SL_2(\mathbb{C})$ and for sufficiently small such subgroups Millson has proved the above non vanishing result [31].
The subgroups $\Gamma'$ considered above does not always fall in the class of arithmetic subgroups considered by Millson.

For example take $E / F$ imaginary quadratic and $S = S_G$ a set of places of $E$ not stable under the Galois action, then we produce subgroups $\Gamma'$ not covered by Millson's techniques (see [10]).
The case $E / F$ cubic non cyclic leads to other new examples.

6.7. Let $E$ as above, and consider a finite set $S_{1}$ of finite places of $E$.
The results in 6.5. can be easily extended to $S_{1}$-arithmetic subgroups of twisted forms of $SL_{n} / E$ ($n = 2$ or 3) using 2.4., 2.5., 6.2., 6.4. and [4] XIII, provided that the set $S_{2}$ of places of $E$ where the twisting is non trivial is non empty, and disjoint from $S_{1}$.
In the split case one would need a generalization of Theorem 5.5. in [1] to extend our results to $S_{1}$-arithmetic groups.
We will come back to these questions in another paper.

# On the Paley-Wiener theorem

Michael Cowling

School of Mathematics, University of New South Wales, PO Box 1, Kensington NSW 2033, Australia

In Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$, let $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)_r$ be the space of smooth functions supported in the closed ball of radius $r$, and $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^n)_r$ the space of their Fourier transforms, analytically continued, which are of exponential type $r$.

**Theorem.** If $m &lt; n$, the map $P \colon C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)_r \to C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^m)_r$, defined

$$
P f(x_1, \dots, x_m) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_1, \dots, x_m, x_{m+1}, \dots, x_n) \, dx_{m+1} \dots dx_n,
$$

is surjective.
Consequently $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^m)_r = \mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^n)_r|_{\mathbb{C}^m}$.

**Corollary.** If $W$ is a finite group of orthogonal transformations of $\mathbb{C}^n$ which leave $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mathbb{C}^m$ invariant, and if $\lambda_0$ in $\mathbb{C}^{n-m}$ is $W$-invariant, and $F$ in $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^m)_r$ is $W$-invariant, then there exists $W$-invariant $F'$ in $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^n)_r$ such that

$$
F'(\lambda \oplus \lambda_0) = F(\lambda) \quad \lambda \in \mathbb{C}^m. \tag{1}
$$

**Proof of Corollary.** It is sufficient for $F'$ in $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^n)_r$ to satisfy (1), for then averaging over $W$ produces a $W$-invariant function with the same properties.
Next, because $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^n)_r$ is invariant under complex translations $(C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)_r)$ is invariant under multiplication by exponentials; it suffices to find $G$ in $\mathrm{PW}(\mathbb{C}^n)_r$ such that $G|_{\mathbb{C}^m} = F$.
The theorem does this.
$\square$

Consequently, the Paley-Wiener theorem of L.
Clozel and P.
Delorme [1] can be sharpened to obtain a bijection between $C_r^\infty(G, K)_r$ and families of functions $F_i(\delta, \cdot)$ in Paley-Wiener spaces $\mathrm{PW}(\mathfrak{A}_i^*)_r$.
They lose control of supports in their Lemma 7; our corollary improves this.

**Proof of theorem.** We may suppose $r = 1$.
Take $h_j$ in $C^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ such that $h_j \geq 0$,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\operatorname{supp}(h_j) \subseteq \left[1 - \frac{3}{j+2}, \frac{1 - \frac{1}{j+2}}{j} \right] \quad j \in \mathbb{N}, \\
\| h_j^{(k)} \|_\infty \leq C_k j^{2k} \quad j \in \mathbb{N}, \, k \in \mathbb{N}, \\
\sum_{j \in \mathbb{N}} h_j(t) = 1 \quad -1/4 \leq t &lt; 1.
\end{array}
$$

M.
Cowling

Set $g_j(x) = h_j(|x|)$.
If $f \in C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^m)_1$, then

$$
|f^{(\alpha)}(x)| \leq C_{\alpha,i}(1 - |x|)^i \quad x \in R^m \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{N}^m \quad i \in \mathbb{N},
$$

and so for $\alpha$ in $\mathbb{N}^m$,

$$
\|(g_j f)^{(\alpha)}\|_\infty \leq C_{\alpha,i}' j^{-i} \quad j \in \mathbb{N} \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{N}^m \quad i \in \mathbb{N},
$$

and in $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^m)$,

$$
f = \sum_{j \in \mathbb{N}} g_j f.
$$

Now choose $k_j$ in $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^{n-m})_{1/j+2}$ of total integral 1, such that

$$
\|k_j^{(\alpha)}\|_\infty \leq C_\alpha (1 + j^{|\alpha|}) \quad j \in \mathbb{N} \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{N}^{n-m}.
$$

Then the function $f'$, given by the formula

$$
f'(x_1, \dots, x_m, x_{m+1}, \dots, x_n) = \sum_{j \in \mathbb{N}} (g_j f)(x_1, \dots, x_m) k_j(x_{m+1}, \dots, x_n),
$$

lies in $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)_1$, and $Pf' = f$.
The rest is obvious.
$\square$

# The ball in $\mathbb{C}^n$ is a closed complex submanifold of a polydisc

Erik Løw

Department of Mathematics, Oslo University, 0316 Oslo 3, Norway

By the Remmert-Bishop-Narasimhan imbedding theorem every $n$-dimensional Stein manifold $M$ can be imbedded as a closed complex submanifold of $\mathbb{C}^{2n+1}$ (see [1]).
Furthermore, there is a proper holomorphic map from $M$ to $\mathbb{C}^{n+1}$.
Similar results seem to be unknown when the target domain is bounded.
In fact, if $B_n$ is the unit ball in $\mathbb{C}^n$ and $\Delta_m$ the unit polydisc in $\mathbb{C}^m$, then for $m \geq 2$ there can not exist a proper holomorphic map from $\Delta_m$ to any $B_n$.
Furthermore, there do not exist proper holomorphic maps from $B_n$ to $\Delta_n$ when $n \geq 2$.
(Proper maps can never lower the dimension.
For these results, see 15.2. of [4]).
Therefore the question is if there can exist proper holomorphic maps from $B_n$ to some higher dimensional polydisc $\Delta_{m(n)}$.
In this paper we prove that this is indeed the case with $m(n) = 4M(n)$, where $M(n)$ is a geometric constant depending on the dimension.
We do not pursue the problem of trying to minimize $m(n)$.
Thus it is still an open problem if it can be done with $m(n) = n + 1$.

The construction is remarkably similar to the construction of inner functions found by Hakim and Sibony in [2] and the author in [3].
We use the distance $\delta(z, w) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} |z - w|$.
When $z, w \in S = \partial \mathbf{B}_n$, we get $\delta^2(z, w) = 1 - \operatorname{Re}\langle z, w \rangle$.
Furthermore, if $z \in S$, then $B(z, r) = \{w \in S; \delta(z, w) &lt; r\}$.
We shall be using the following obvious geometric fact:

(1) There exists an integer $M(n)$ such that for any $r &gt; 0$ there exist $M(n)$ families $\mathcal{F}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_{M(n)}$ of balls with radii $2r$, $\mathcal{F}_i = \{B(z_{i,j}, 2r); j = 1, \ldots, M_i\}$ such that the balls in each family are pairwise disjoint and $S = \bigcup_{i,j} B(z_{i,j}, r)$, i.e. the smaller balls of radius $r$ cover $S$.

Given some functions $f_{1}, \ldots, f_{k}$, let $F$ denote the $k$-tuple $F = (f_{1}, \ldots, f_{k})$ and $M_{F}(z) = \max |f_{i}(z)|$.
Let us now try to give an informal explanation of the main idea of the proof.
For each point $w \in S$ and large number $m$, $g(z) = \exp \left\{-m(1 - \langle z, w \rangle)\right\}$ is a function that peaks at $w$, i.e. $g(w) = 1$ and $|g(z)| = \exp \left\{-m\delta^{2}(z, w)\right\} \leq 1$ when $z \in S \cdot g$ is highly oscillatory.
We wish to build up the components of our proper map as sums of (constant multiples of) these functions.
This will be done inductively, always trying to push the modulus on $S$ of

E.
Løw

each component towards the value 1. If $f_{i}$ is one such component and $\mathcal{F}_{i}$ a family of balls as above, we may add to $f_{i}$ an appropriate linear combination $g_{i}$ of these peak functions (see the definition of $g_{i}$ in the proof of Lemma 1) to increase $|f_{i}|$ near the points $z_{i,j}$.
The problem is that the function $g_{i}$ is highly oscillatory and will just as often push the value down instead of up.
This problem we may solve by introducing $M(n)$ more components $f_{M(n) + 1}, \ldots, f_{2M}(n)$ and when we adjust $f_{M(n) + i}$, we use the same family $\mathcal{F}_{i}$, but introduce a phase difference of $\pi$ in the oscillation of the peak functions.
This means that if $|f_{i}(z)|$ is pushed down, then $|f_{M(n) + i}(z)|$ will be pushed up and vice versa.
Still, it may be that $|f_{i}(z)|$ is just pushed up and down when we continue this process.
So how can we control this? Clearly, we cannot expect the maximum $M_{F}$ to increase in each step, since it may be the highest value that we push down.
Neither can we expect the sum of the moduli of the components to increase.
What we can do, however, is to introduce a weight function $\phi$ of the moduli such that high values weigh more than low ones.
This can be done such that the sum of the weights of the components always increases after an adjustment.
$\phi(x) = -\ln(1 - x)$ turns out to be the right one.
When $M_{F}(z) &lt; 1$ we define the weight of $F$ by $W_{F}(z) = \sum_{i} \phi(|f_{i}(z)|)$.

Lemma 1. There exist constants $\varepsilon_0, C$ such that: If $a &lt; 1$, $\varepsilon \leq \varepsilon_0$, $\varepsilon \leq (1 - a)^6$ and $R &lt; 1$ and $F = (f_{1},\ldots ,f_{2M(n)})$ is continuous on $S$ with $U_{1} = \{z\in S; M_{F}(z) &gt; a\}$ then there exist entire functions $G = (g_{1},\dots,g_{2M(n)})$ such that

(a) $M_{F + G}(z)\leq \max \left\{M_F(z),1\right\} +2\varepsilon$
(b) $M_G(z)\leq \varepsilon$ when $|z|\leq R$
(c) $U_{2} = \{z\in S;M_{F + G}(z) &gt; a - 3\varepsilon \} \supset U_{1}$
(d) If $z \in S \setminus U_2$, then $W_{F + G}(z) \geq W_F(z) + C\varepsilon^{\frac{1}{3}}$.

Proof.
There exists $r_0 &gt; 0$ such that $|f_i(z) - f_i(z')| &lt; \varepsilon$ when $\delta(z, z') &lt; 4r_0$.
Let $r &lt; r_0$ and $\mathcal{F}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_{M(n)}$ the corresponding family of balls,

$$
\mathcal {F} _ {i} = \left\{B \left(z _ {i, j}, 2 r\right); j = 1, \dots , N _ {i} \right\}.
$$

Let $\mathcal{F}_{M(n) + i} = \mathcal{F}_i$ and $z_{M(n) + i,j} = z_{i,j}$ for $i = 1, \ldots, M(n)$, so we have $2M(n)$ families of balls.
For $z \in S$, let

$$
V _ {i, k} (z) = \left\{z _ {i, j}; k r \leq \delta \left(z, z _ {i j}\right) &lt;   (k + 1) r \right\}
$$

and $N_{i,k}(z) = \operatorname{card} V_{i,k}(z)$.
Since the balls in $\mathcal{F}_i$ are disjoint it follows that there is a constant $C_1 &gt; 0$ such that

$$
N _ {i, k} (z) \leq C _ {1} k ^ {2 n - 1}. \tag {2}
$$

Now define $\beta_{i,j}$ as follows: $\beta_{i,j} = 0$ if $|f_i(z_{i,j})| &gt; a - \varepsilon$.
Otherwise we define $\beta_{i,j}$ when $i \leq M(n)$ by the condition $|f_i(z_{i,j}) + \beta_{i,j}| = |f_i(z_{i,j})| + |\beta_{i,j}| = 1$ and when $i &gt; M(n)$ by $|f_i(z_{i,j}) - \beta_{i,j}| = |f_i(z_{i,j})| + |\beta_{i,j}| = 1$.
Now define

$$
g _ {i} (z) = \sum_ {j} \beta_ {i, j} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, j} \rangle\right) \right\}.
$$

The ball in $\mathbb{C}^n$ is a closed complex submanifold of a polydisc

Then, if $z$ is not in any of the balls in $\mathcal{F}_i$ with $\beta_{i,j} \neq 0$, then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left| g _ {i} (z) \right| \leq \sum_ {k \geq 2} \sum_ {z _ {i, j} \in V _ {i, k} (z)} \exp \left\{- m \delta^ {2} \left(z, z _ {i, j}\right) \right\} \leq \sum_ {k \geq 2} C _ {1} k ^ {2 n - 1} \exp \left(- m k ^ {2} r ^ {2}\right) \tag {3} \\
\leq C _ {2} \exp (- 4 m r ^ {2}) \\
\end{array}
$$

provided $mr^2$ is large enough.
At any point $z \in S$ the same estimate holds if we only sum over the balls not containing $z$.
The right-hand side of (3) will be $\varepsilon$ if we choose $m$ and $r$ such that

$$
m r ^ {2} = \frac {1}{4} \log \frac {C _ {2}}{\varepsilon}. \tag {4}
$$

Condition (4) can be satisfied with arbitrarily small $r$ and arbitrarily large $m$ and guarantees that the above estimate holds.
We now turn to the verification of conditions (a) to (d).

Suppose $z \in S$ and consider the function $f_{i} + g_{i}$.
If $z$ is not in any ball with $\beta_{i,j} \neq 0$, then $|f_{i}(z) + g_{i}(z)| \leq |f_{i}(z)| + \varepsilon$.
If $z$ is in some ball with $\beta_{i,j} \neq 0$, then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left| f _ {i} (z) + g _ {i} (z) \right| \leq \left| f _ {i} (z) - f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) \right| + \left| f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) + \beta_ {i, j} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, j} \rangle \right\} \right\} \\
+ \left| \sum_ {k \neq j} \beta_ {i, k} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, k} \rangle\right) \right\} \right| \leq \varepsilon + 1 + \varepsilon . \\
\end{array}
$$

Hence $|f_{i}(z) + g_{i}(z)| \leq \max \{|f_{i}(z)|, 1\} + 2\varepsilon$ which implies (a).

If $z \in U_1$, then there is some $k$ such that $|f_k(z)| &gt; a$ and since $z$ is not in any ball with $\beta_{k,j} \neq 0$, we have $|f_k(z) + g_k(z)| &gt; a - \varepsilon$ which proves (c).

Now let $z \in S \setminus U_2$, so $|f_i(z)| \leq a$ and $|f_i(z) + g_i(z)| \leq a - 3\varepsilon$ for all $i$.
We want to estimate $W_{G + F}(z)$.
First we show that we can replace $f_i(z) + g_i(z)$ by only the terms that count at $z$.
Consider a fixed family $\mathcal{F}_i$.
If $z$ is not in any of the balls $B(z_{i,j}, 2r)$ then $|g_i(z)| &lt; \varepsilon$ and

$$
\phi \left(\left| f _ {i} (z) + g _ {i} (z) \right|\right) - \phi \left(\left| f _ {i} (z) \right|\right) \geq - \frac {\varepsilon}{1 - a}
$$

since $\phi'(x) = \frac{1}{1 - x} \leq \frac{1}{1 - a}$ when $0 \leq x \leq a$.
If $z \in B(z_{i,j}, 2r)$ for some $j$, then $|f_i(z_{i,j})| \leq a - \varepsilon$ so $\beta_{i,j} \neq 0$ and

$$
\left| f _ {i} (z) + g _ {i} (z) - \left(f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) + \beta_ {i, j} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, j} \rangle \right\} \right\} \right\} \right| \leq 2 \varepsilon .
$$

Hence

$$
\left| f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) + \beta_ {i, j} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, j} \rangle\right) \right\} \right| \leq a - \varepsilon &lt; a
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\phi \left(\left| f _ {i} (z) + g _ {i} (z) \right|\right) - \phi \left(\left| f _ {i} (z) \right|\right) \geq \phi \left(\left| f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) + \beta_ {i, j} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, j} \rangle\right) \right\} \right|\right) \\
- \phi \left(\left| f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) \right|\right) - \frac {3 \varepsilon}{1 - a}. \\
\end{array}
$$

If $I(z) = \{i; z \in B(z_{i,j}, 2r) \text{ for some } j\}$ this gives (j has one value for each i):

$$
\begin{array}{l}
W _ {F + G} (z) - W _ {F} (z) \geq \sum_ {i \in I} \phi \left(\left| f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) \right. \right. \tag {5} \\
\left. + \beta_ {i, j} \exp \left\{- m \left(1 - \langle z, z _ {i, j} \rangle\right) \right\} \right|) - \phi \left(\left| f _ {i} \left(z _ {i, j}\right) \right|\right) - C _ {3} \frac {\varepsilon}{1 - a}. \\
\end{array}
$$

E.
Løw

Now write $\alpha_{i} = |f_{i}(z_{i,j})|$ and $w_{i} = \exp \left\{-m(1 - \langle z,z_{i,j}\rangle)\right\}$, so $|w_{i}|\leq 1$.
We also group together terms with index $i$ and $i + M(n)$ to get

(6) $W_{F + G}(z) - W_F(z)\geq \sum_{i\in I,i\leq M(n)}\{\phi (|\alpha_i + (1 - \alpha_i)w_i|) - \phi (\alpha_i)\}$

$$
+ \left\{\phi \left(\left| \alpha_ {i + M (n)} - \left(1 - \alpha_ {i + M (n)}\right) w _ {i} \right|\right) - \phi \left(\alpha_ {i + M (n)}\right) \right\} - C _ {3} \frac {\varepsilon}{1 - a}.
$$

We now want to show that for $0 \leq A$, $B \leq a$,

$$
h (w) = \left\{\phi \left(| A + (1 - A) w |\right) - \phi (A) \right\} + \left\{\phi \left(| B - (1 - B) w |\right) - \phi (B) \right\} \geq (1 - a) | w | ^ {2}.
$$

Let $w = t e^{i\theta}$, $A(t,\theta) = A + (1 - A)t e^{i\theta}$ and $B(t,\theta) = B - (1 - B)t e^{i\theta}$.

Let us first consider the case when $\theta = 0$.
Then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\phi (A + (1 - A) t) - \phi (A) = - \ln (1 - t) \\
\phi (| B - (1 - B) t |) - \phi (B) \geq \phi (B - (1 - B) t) - \phi (B) = - \ln (1 + t).
\end{array}
$$

Hence $h(w) \geq -\ln (1 - t^2) \geq t^2$.
The same reasoning also applies to $\theta = \Pi$, hence the estimate holds when $w$ is real.
When $w$ is not real we first project $A(t, \theta)$ and $B(t, \theta)$ down on the real axis and then we estimate how much more we have.
Hence, let $z = x + iy$, $|z| &lt; 1$.
Then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\phi (| z |) - \phi (x) = - \ln \left(\frac {1 - \sqrt {x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}}}{1 - | x |}\right) = - \ln \left(1 - \frac {\sqrt {x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}} - | x |}{1 - | x |}\right) \geq \frac {\sqrt {x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}} - | x |}{1 - | x |} \\
= \frac {y ^ {2}}{2 \sqrt {s} (1 - | x |)} \geq \frac {y ^ {2}}{2 (1 - | x |)} \geq \frac {y ^ {2}}{2 (1 - x)} \\
\end{array}
$$

for some $s$ with $x^{2} \leq s \leq x^{2} + y^{2} \leq 1$ by the mean value theorem.
Hence

$$
\begin{array}{l}
h (w) \geq t ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta + \frac {[ (1 - A) t \sin \theta ] ^ {2}}{2 (1 - [ A + (1 - A) t \cos \theta ])} + \frac {[ (1 - B) t \sin \theta ] ^ {2}}{2 (1 - [ B - (1 - B) t \cos \theta ])} \\
= t ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta + \frac {1}{2} t ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \left[ \frac {1 - A}{1 - t \cos \theta} + \frac {1 - B}{1 + t \cos \theta} \right] \\
\geq t ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta + \frac {(1 - a) t ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta}{1 - t ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta} \geq (1 - a) t ^ {2}.
\end{array}
$$

Hence all summands in the sum (6) are positive.
Furthermore, there is an index $i \in I$ such that $z \in B(z_{i,j}, r)$, so by (4)

$$
\left| w _ {i} \right| = \exp \left\{- m \delta^ {2} \left(z, z _ {i, j}\right) \right\} \geq \exp (- m r ^ {2}) = \left(\frac {\varepsilon}{C _ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

Hence (6) gives

$$
W _ {F + G} (z) - W _ {F} (z) \geq \frac {1}{\sqrt {C _ {2}}} (1 - a) \varepsilon^ {\frac {1}{2}} - C _ {3} \frac {\varepsilon}{1 - a} \geq C \varepsilon^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

The ball in $\mathbb{C}^n$ is a closed complex submanifold of a polydisc

since $(1 - a) &gt; \varepsilon^{\frac{1}{2}}$ which proves (d).
Finally, if $|z| \leq R$, then $\operatorname{Re}(1 - \langle z, w \rangle) \geq 1 - R$ for all $w \in S$ and since each $\mathcal{F}_i$ contains no more than $C_5 / r^{2n-1}$ balls we get, assuming $mr^2 \geq 1$,

$$
\left| g _ {i} (z) \right| \leq \frac {C _ {5}}{r ^ {2 n - 1}} \exp \left\{- m (1 - R) \right\} \leq C _ {5} m ^ {\frac {2 n - 1}{2}} \exp \left\{- m (1 - R) \right\}.
$$

Choosing $m$ large enough proves (b).

**Lemma 2.** Let $F = (f_{1}, \ldots, f_{2M(n)})$ be a continuous function on $S$ with $M_{F} \leq 1$ and let $\varepsilon &gt; 0$, $R &lt; 1$.
Then there exist entire functions $G = (g_{1}, \ldots, g_{2M(n)})$ such that $1 - \varepsilon \leq M_{F + G}(z) \leq 1 + \varepsilon$ for all $z \in S$ and $M_{G}(z) \leq \varepsilon$ when $|z| \leq R$.

**Proof.** Let $a = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon$ and choose $\varepsilon_{i} &gt; 0$ such that $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\varepsilon_{i} &lt; \frac{1}{6}\varepsilon$, $\varepsilon_{i} &lt; (1 - a)^{6}$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\varepsilon_{i}^{\frac{1}{2}} = \infty$.
Suppose we have found $2M(n)$-tuples of entire functions $G_{1},\ldots,G_{k}$ such that, if we let $H_{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}G_{i}$, then

(a) $M_{F + H_k}\leq 1 + 2\sum_{i = 1}^k\varepsilon_i &lt; 1 + \varepsilon$
(b) $M_{H_k}(z)\leq \sum_{i = 1}^k\varepsilon_i &lt;   \varepsilon$ when $|z|\leq R$
(c) $U_{j} = \left\{z\in S;M_{F + H_{j}}(z) &gt; a - 3\sum_{i = 1}^{j}\varepsilon_{i} &gt; 1 - \varepsilon \right\} \supset U_{j - 1}$ for $j = 1,\ldots ,k$
(d) If $z \in S \setminus U_k$, then $W_{F + H_k}(z) \geq W_F(z) + C \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i^{\frac{1}{2}}$.

This holds trivially for $k = 0$.
If we now apply Lemma 1 to the data $a - 3\sum_{i=1}^{k}\varepsilon_i$, $\varepsilon_{k+1}, R, F + H_k$, which we may, to produce $G_{k+1}$, then all the above properties follow trivially also for $k + 1$.

We claim that for $k$ large enough, $S = U_{k}$, because if $z \in S \setminus U_{k}$ and $H_{k} = (h_{k,1}, \ldots, h_{k,2M(n)})$ then $x_{i} = |f_{i}(z) + h_{k,i}(z)| \leq a = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon$ for all $i$, but

$$
\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {2 M (n)} - \ln \left(1 - x _ {i}\right) = W _ {F + H _ {k}} (z) \geq C \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {k} \varepsilon_ {i} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

which is impossible if $k$ is large enough.
Hence, with $G = H_{k}$, (a), (b), and (c) prove the lemma.

**Remark.** We shall apply Lemma 2 repeatedly with the hypothesis $M_F \leq a$ for some $a$, in which case the conclusions of the lemma hold with 1 replaced by $a$.
We shall refer to $F, a, \varepsilon, R$ as data for the lemma.

**Theorem.** There exist proper holomorphic maps from $B_{n}$ to $\Delta_{4M(n)}$.

**Proof.** Let $\{a_i\}$, $\{\varepsilon_i\}$ be sequences such that $a_i$ increases strictly to 1, $a_i + \varepsilon_i \leq a_{i+1}$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \varepsilon_i &lt; \frac{1}{2}$.
Let $F_0 = F_0' = (0, 0, \ldots, 0)$, $R_0 = \frac{1}{2}$, $R_0' = \frac{3}{4}$ and suppose we

E.
Løw

have inductively found $2M(n)$-tuples $F_{1},\ldots ,F_{k}$ and $F_1^{\prime},\ldots ,F_k^{\prime}$ of entire functions and radii $R_{1},\ldots ,R_{k}$ and $R_1^{\prime},\ldots ,R_k^{\prime}$ such that, if we let $H_{k} = \sum_{i = 0}^{k}F_{i}$ and $H_{k}^{\prime} = \sum_{i = 0}^{k}F_{i}^{\prime}$, then

(1) $a_{k} - \varepsilon_{k}\leq M_{H_{k}}(z),M_{H_{k}^{\prime}}(z)\leq a_{k} + \varepsilon_{k}\leq a_{k + 1}$ for all $z\in S$
(2) $M_{F_i}(z)\leq \varepsilon_i$ when $|z|\leq R_{i - 1}^{\prime}$ and $M_{F_i^{\prime}}(z)\leq \varepsilon_i$ when $|z|\leq R_{i}$ and $R_{i - 1}^{\prime} &lt; R_{i} &lt; R_{i}^{\prime}$ for $i = 0,\dots ,k$
(3) $M_{H_k}(z)\geq a_k - 2\varepsilon_k$ when $|z|\geq R_k$ $M_{H_k^{\prime}}(z)\geq a_k - 2\varepsilon_k$ when $|z|\geq R_k^{\prime}$

This holds trivially for $k = 0 (a_0 = 0)$.
We then apply Lemma 2 to the data $H_k$, $a_{k+1}$, $\varepsilon_{k+1}$, $R_k'$ to produce $F_{k+1}$.
Then properties (1) and (2) follow immediately for $H_{k+1}$.
By (1) and the uniform continuity of $H_{k+1}$, we may choose $R_{k+1} &gt; R_k'$ such that (3) holds.
Now apply Lemma 2 to the data $H_k'$, $a_{k+1}$, $\varepsilon_{k+1}$, $R_{k+1}$ to produce $F_{k+1}'$.
By the same argument all properties also hold for $H_{k+1}'$ and we may choose $R_{k+1}' &gt; R_{k+1}$.
We may assume $\lim R_k = 1$.
By (2)

$$
H: = \lim H _ {k} = \sum_ {i = 0} ^ {\infty} F _ {i} \quad \text {and} \quad H ^ {\prime}: = \lim H _ {k} ^ {\prime} = \sum_ {i = 0} ^ {\infty} F _ {i} ^ {\prime}
$$

exist and satisfy $M_H(z), M_{H'}(z) \leq \frac{1}{2}$ when $|z| \leq \frac{3}{4}$.
We claim that the $4M(n)$ tuple $(H, H')$ is proper.
By (1), $M_H, M_{H'} \leq 1$, hence $(H, H')$ maps $B_n$ into $\Delta_{4M(n)}$.
Now suppose $z \in B_n$ and $R_k \leq |z| &lt; R_{k+1}$ for some $k$.
If $|z| \leq R_k'$, then by (3) and (2)

$$
M _ {H} (z) \geq M _ {H _ {k}} (z) - \sum_ {i &gt; k} M _ {F _ {i}} (z) \geq a _ {k} - 2 \varepsilon_ {k} - \sum_ {i &gt; k} \varepsilon_ {i} \rightarrow 1 \quad \text {when} \quad k \rightarrow \infty .
$$

Similarly, if $|z| \geq R_k'$ then $M_{H'}(z) \to 1$ when $k \to \infty$.
It follows that $(H, H')$ is proper.
