# Locally finite approximation of Lie groups, I

Eric M.
Friedlander\* and Guido Mislin

$^{1}$ Department of Mathematics, Northwestern University, Evanston, Illinois 60201, USA
$^{2}$ Department of Mathematics, ETH-Zentrum, CH-8092 Zürich, Switzerland

For a compact, connected Lie Group $G$, we earlier demonstrated that the classifying space $BG$ was “cohomologically approximated” by the classifying spaces of (finite) groups of points of an associated algebraic group with values in finite fields [5].
In Theorem 1.3, we generalize this result to arbitrary Lie groups with only finitely many components.
Namely, we prove that the classifying space of such a group can be “cohomologically approximated” by the classifying space of a group expressible as a countable union of finite groups (i.e. a locally finite group).

The usefulness of such an approximation results from the opportunity it affords to extend known theorems for finite groups to arbitrary compact Lie groups.
With this philosophy, we present a family of applications of H.
Miller’s proof of the Sullivan Conjecture.
Namely, our approximation theorem in conjunction with H.
Miller’s theorem enables us to provide a reasonably explicit description of the homotopy groups of mapping complexes with source the classifying space of a Lie group with only finitely many components (see Theorem 3.3 below).
In [6], we prove further properties of our approximation which enable applications to the generalized cohomology of classifying spaces.

It is our pleasure to acknowledge the great benefit we have derived from many patient explanations of A.
Borel as well as useful conversations with P.
Gabriel and H.
Miller and constructive suggestions of the referee.
The first author gratefully acknowledges the warm hospitality of ETH Zurich during the development of this paper.

## 1. The approximation theorem

In Theorem 1.3, we demonstrate that any Lie group $G$ with finitely many components admits a “locally finite approximation away from $p$”.
In the special case in which $G$ is connected, this approximation theorem is a sharpened form of [5, Theorem 1.4].

- Partially supported by the N.S.F.

E.M.
Friedlander and G.
Mislin

As motivation, we shall recall the well-known approximation of the Lie group $S^1 \simeq \mathbb{R} / \mathbb{Z}$.
The inclusion $i \colon \mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z} \to S^1$ with $\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}$ given the discrete topology is a homomorphism of topological groups.
The induced map on classifying spaces $Bi \colon B\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z} \to BS^1$ satisfies a) $Bi^* \colon H^*(BS^1; \mathbb{Z} / n\mathbb{Z}) \to H^*(B\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}, \mathbb{Z} / n\mathbb{Z})$ is an isomorphism for any $n &gt; 0$; and b) $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{grps}}(\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}, F) = \{1\}$ for any finite group $F$.

As introduced below, the approximation $\Phi \colon B\mathfrak{g} \to BG$ differs in two important respects from the simple example $Bi \colon B\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z} \to BS^1$.
Namely, we do not require $\Phi$ to be induced by a homomorphism of groups and $\Phi$ will fail in general to be a suitable cohomological approximation at the designated prime $p$ since, for $G$ connected, our conditions on $\Phi$ imply that $\mathfrak{g}$ be $p$-acyclic.
Replacing $\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}$ by its subgroup $\mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}[1/p]$ of elements of order prime to $p$, we obtain an example

$$
\Phi \colon B \mathbb {Q} / \mathbb {Z} [ 1 / p ] \to B S ^ {1}
$$

of a locally finite approximation away from $p$.

1.1 Definition.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finitely many components and let $p$ be a prime number not dividing $|\pi_0(G)|$, the order of the group of connected components of $G$.
A map $\Phi \colon B\mathfrak{g} \to BG$ from the classifying space of a countable locally finite group $\mathfrak{g}$ to the classifying space of $G$ is said to be a locally finite approximation away from $p$ (occasionally abbreviated "l.f.
approx/p") if $\Phi$ satisfies the following three conditions:

a) For any finite $\pi_0(G)$-module $A$, whose order is not divisible by $p$, $\Phi$ induces an isomorphism

$$
\Phi^ {*}: H ^ {*} (B G, A) \xrightarrow {\sim} H ^ {*} (B \mathfrak {g}, A) = H ^ {*} (\mathfrak {g}, A).
$$

b) $\Phi$ induces a surjection

$$
\Phi_ {*}: \mathfrak {g} \cong \pi_ {1} (B \mathfrak {g}) \rightarrow \pi_ {1} (B G) = \pi_ {0} (G).
$$

c) Let $\pi = \ker (\Phi_{*}:\mathfrak{g}\to \pi_{0}(G))$.
Then $\tilde{H}^{*}(\pi ,\mathbb{Z} / p) = 0$ and $\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{grps}}(\pi ,F) = \{1\}$ for any finite group $F$.

1.2 Remark.
Observe that $\tilde{H}_{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Z}_{p^{\infty}}) = \varinjlim \tilde{H}_{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Z}/p^{n}) = 0$ if $\tilde{H}^{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Z}/p) = 0$, where $\mathbb{Z}_{p^{\infty}} = \varinjlim \mathbb{Z}/p^{n}$.
Because $\pi = \ker (\Phi_{*} : \mathfrak{g} \to \pi_{0}(G))$ is locally finite, the groups $\tilde{H}_{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Q})$ and $\tilde{H}^{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Q})$ are trivial and, since $\mathbb{Z}_{p^{\infty}} \simeq \mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$, (1.1 c) implies therefore that $\tilde{H}_{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Z}_{(p)}) = 0 = \tilde{H}^{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Z}_{(p)})$.
It follows that $\tilde{H}^{*}(\pi, \mathbb{Z}_{p^{\infty}}) = 0$, a property of $\pi$ which will be used later.
Moreover, because $\pi_{0}(G)$ is a finite group of order prime to $p$, we conclude that $\tilde{H}^{*}(\mathfrak{g}, B) = 0$ for any $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}[\pi_{0}(G)]$-module $B$.

We proceed to state and then to prove the existence of locally finite approximations away from $p$.

1.3 Theorem.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finitely many components and let $p$ be a prime number not dividing the order of $\pi_0(G)$.
Then there exists a locally finite approximation away from $p$ (l.f.
approx/p), $\Phi \colon B\mathfrak{g} \to BG$.

Locally finite approximation of Lie groups, I
427

In our proof of Theorem 1.3, we consider $G \to G / R$ with $R$ a complex torus and $G / R \to \bar{G}$ surjective with finite kernel.
The following two lemmas reduce the proof of Theorem 1.3 to a consideration of $\bar{G}$ which we analyze using techniques of [5].

1.4 Lemma.
Let $f \colon \bar{G} \to G$ be a surjective homomorphism of Lie groups with finitely many components and let $p$ be a prime not dividing $|\pi_0(\bar{G})|$.
Assume $\ker(f)$ has the homotopy type of the torus $(S^1)^n$.
If $\Phi \colon B_{\mathfrak{g}} \to BG$ is a l.f.
approx/p, then there is a map of fibration sequences

![img-61.jpeg](img-61.jpeg)

with each vertical map a l.f.
approx/p.

1.5 Lemma.
As in (1.4), let $f \colon \bar{G} \to G$ be a surjective homomorphism of Lie groups with finitely many components and let $p$ be a prime not dividing $|\pi_0(\bar{G})|$.
Assume that $\ker(f)$ is finite and let $P \subset \ker(f)$ be a $p$-Sylow subgroup.
If $\Phi \colon B_{\mathfrak{g}} \to BG$ is a l.f.
approx/p, then so is $\bar{\Phi} \colon B_{\mathfrak{g}} = \text{pull-back}(B_{\mathfrak{g}} \to BG \leftarrow B\bar{G}/P) \to B\bar{G}$.

Proof of Theorem 1.3. By a well-known theorem [8, Chap. XV], the quotient of $G$ by a maximal compact subgroup $K$ is contractible so that $BK \to BG$ is a homotopy equivalence.
The complex form $K(\mathbb{C})$ of $K$ has reductive connected component and $BK \to BK(\mathbb{C})$ is also a homotopy equivalence.
Thus, it suffices to assume $G = K(\mathbb{C})$, which we shall do for notational convenience.

Let $\bar{G}$ denote the image of $\operatorname{Ad} \colon G \to \operatorname{Aut}(G^0)$.
Then $\bar{G}$ is the group of complex points of a smooth integral group scheme $\bar{G}_{\mathbf{Z}}$ which fits in an extension

$$
1 \to G _ {\mathbf {Z}} ^ {\mathrm {a d}} \to \bar {G} _ {\mathbf {Z}} \to \bar {\tau} \to 1
$$

where $G_{\mathbf{Z}}^{\mathrm{ad}}$ is the Chevalley integral group scheme associated to the adjoint form of $G^{0}$ and $\bar{\tau}$ is the constant integral group scheme associated to the image of $\pi_0(G)$ in $\pi_0(\operatorname{Aut}(G^0))$ [14; Chap. XXIV - 1.3].
The identity component $R$ of the center of $G$ is a complex torus normal in $G$.
Moreover, $G / R \to \bar{G}$ is surjective with finite kernel.
Consequently, Lemmas (1.4) and (1.5) imply that it suffices to verify the existence of a l.f.
approx/p for $\bar{G}$.

Define $\bar{\mathfrak{g}} = \bar{G}_{\mathbf{Z}}(\bar{\mathbb{F}}_p)$ and define $\bar{\Phi} \colon B\bar{\mathfrak{g}} \to B\bar{G}$ as in the proof of [5; 1.4].
Namely, using etale homotopy theory we obtain a map $B\bar{\mathfrak{g}} \to \prod_{l+p} (\mathbb{Z}/l)_{\infty} B\bar{G}$, where $(\mathbb{Z}/l)_{\infty}(-)$ is the Bousfield-Kan $\mathbb{Z}/l$-completion functor, which induces an isomorphism in cohomology with any finite $(\mathbb{Z}[1/p])[\bar{\tau}]$-module as coefficients [4].
Then $\bar{\Phi}$ is the unique lift for the lifting problem

![img-62.jpeg](img-62.jpeg)

E.M.
Friedlander and G.
Mislin

whose existence and uniqueness is given by the vanishing of $\tilde{H}^* (B\tilde{\mathfrak{g}},\mathbb{Q})$ (since $\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}$ is locally finite) and $\tilde{H}^{*}(B\tilde{\mathfrak{g}},\mathbb{Z} / p)$ (by [13; remark on p. 582] applied to $\tilde{G}_{\mathbb{Z}}^{0}(\tilde{\mathbb{F}}_{p})$) used in conjunction with Sullivan's "arithmetic fibre square" technique [15].
So constructed, $\tilde{\varPhi}$ satisfies (1.1 a) and (1.1 b).
We have already observed that $\tilde{H}^{*}(B\tilde{\mathfrak{g}},\mathbb{Z} / p) = 0$; because each simple factor of $G_{\mathbb{Z}}^{\mathrm{ad}}$ has the property that its group of $\tilde{\mathbb{F}}_p$-rational points is a simple (discrete) group, and because $\tilde{\pi} = \ker (\tilde{\varPhi}\colon \tilde{\mathfrak{g}}\to \pi_0(\tilde{G}))$ equals $G_{\mathbb{Z}}^{\mathrm{ad}}(\tilde{\mathbb{F}}_p)$, $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{grps}}(\tilde{\pi},F) = \{1\}$ for any finite group $F$, as required by (1.1 c).

Proof of Lemma 1.4. The fibration $\xi \colon B\tilde{G}\to BG$ is classified by an element $\xi^{\prime}\in H^{3}(BG,\underline{Z}^{n})$ where $\underline{Z}^n$ is the $\pi_0(G)$-module given by the action of $\pi_0(G)$ on $\pi_1(\ker (f))$.
Let $\Phi^{*}\xi \colon X\to B\mathfrak{g}$ be the induced fibration classified by $\Phi^{*}\xi^{\prime}\in H^{3}(\mathfrak{g},\underline{Z}^{n})$.
Because $\underline{Z}^n$ is a trivial $\pi = \ker (\Phi_{*}\colon \mathfrak{g}\to \pi_{0}(G))$-module, (1.1 c) implies (cf.
Remark 1.2) that $\tilde{H}^{*}(\pi ,\underline{Z}^{n})\simeq \tilde{H}^{*}(\pi ,\underline{Z}[1 / p]^{n})$ so that $\tilde{H}^3 (\mathfrak{g},\underline{Z}^n)\simeq \tilde{H}^3 (\mathfrak{g},\underline{Z}[1 / p]^n)$ which is isomorphic to $H^{2}(\mathfrak{g},\underline{\mathbb{Q}} /\underline{\mathbb{Z}} [1 / p]^{n})$ because $\mathfrak{g}$ is locally finite.
We conclude the existence of a map of fibration sequences

![img-63.jpeg](img-63.jpeg)

in which $\lambda$ satisfies (1.1 a), (1.1 b), and (1.1 c).
Write $Y = B\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}$, $\mathfrak{g} = \varinjlim_{\pi}\mathfrak{g}_{\pi}$ with each $\mathfrak{g}_{\pi}$ finite, $\tilde{\mathfrak{g}} = \varinjlim_{\pi}\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}_{\pi}$ with $\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}_{\pi} \subset \tilde{\mathfrak{g}}$ the inverse image of $\mathfrak{g}_{\pi} \subset \mathfrak{g}$, and $A = \mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}[1/p]^n$.
Because $H^{2}(\mathfrak{g}_{\pi}, A) = \varinjlim_{m} H^{2}(\mathfrak{g}_{\pi}, mA)$ where $_{m}A = \ker(A \xrightarrow{m} A)$, we conclude that each $\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}_{\pi}$ and thus also $\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}$ are locally finite.
By construction, $X \to B\tilde{G}$ and therefore $\tilde{\Phi} \colon B\tilde{\mathfrak{g}} \to X \to B\tilde{G}$ satisfy (1.1 a) and (1.1 b).
Since $\tilde{\pi} = \ker(\tilde{\Phi}_{*} \colon \tilde{\mathfrak{g}} \to \pi_{0}(\tilde{G}) = \pi_{0}(G))$ is an extension of $A = \mathbb{Q} / \mathbb{Z}[1/p]^{n}$ by $\pi$, $\tilde{\Phi}$ also satisfies (1.1 c).

Proof of Lemma 1.5. Let $C$ denote $\ker(f) \cap \tilde{G}^0$.
Because $C$ is a finite normal subgroup of $\tilde{G}^0$, it is central in $\tilde{G}^0$.
Because $|P|$ and $|\pi_0(\tilde{G})|$ are relatively prime, $P$ must be the subgroup of $\ker f$ consisting of all $p$-torsion elements and therefore $P$ is normal in $\tilde{G}$.
By definition of $\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}$, we have a map of fibration sequences

![img-64.jpeg](img-64.jpeg)

and a central extension

$$
1 \rightarrow C / P \rightarrow \tilde {\pi} \rightarrow \pi \rightarrow 1 \tag {1.3.2}
$$

where $\tilde{\pi} = \ker (\tilde{\Phi}_{*}:\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}\to \pi_{0}(\tilde{G} /P) = \pi_{0}(\tilde{G}))$.
Using (1.3.1), we easily conclude that (1.1 a) for $\varPhi$ implies (1.1 a) for $\tilde{\varPhi}$.
Observe that the obstructions for existence

Locally finite approximation of Lie groups, I
429

and uniqueness for the lifting problem

![img-65.jpeg](img-65.jpeg)

lie in $\tilde{H}^{*}(\tilde{\mathfrak{g}},P) = H^{0}(\pi_{0}(\tilde{G}),\tilde{H}^{*}(\tilde{\pi},P))$.
By (1.1 c) for $\Phi$ and (1.3.2), $\tilde{H}^{*}(\tilde{\pi},\mathbb{Z}/p) = 0$ so that a unique lifting $\tilde{\Phi}\colon B\tilde{\mathfrak{g}}\to B\tilde{G}$ of $\tilde{\Phi}$ exists and automatically satisfies (1.1 a).
Because $\Phi$ satisfies (1.1 b), the fibre of $\Phi$ and thereby of $\tilde{\Phi}$ is connected, thus, $\tilde{\Phi}$ and thus $\tilde{\Phi}$ satisfy (1.1 b).
As for (1.1 c), we have already observed the vanishing of $\tilde{H}^{*}(\tilde{\pi},\mathbb{Z}/p)$.
Finally, consider any map $\theta\colon \tilde{\pi}\to F$ with $F$ finite.
By (1.1 c) for $\Phi$, image $(\theta)\subset F$ equals image $(C/P)$ so that we may assume $F$ is abelian of order prime to $p$.
Applying (1.1 a) for $\tilde{\Phi}$, we conclude that

$$
\operatorname {H o m} (\tilde {\pi}, F) = H ^ {1} (\tilde {\pi}, F) = H ^ {1} (\tilde {\mathfrak {g}}, \mathbb {Z} [ \pi_ {0} (\tilde {G}) ] \otimes F)
$$

is isomorphic to $H^{1}(B\tilde{G},\mathbb{Z}[\pi_{0}(\tilde{G})]\otimes F)\simeq H^{1}(B\tilde{G}^{0},F)$ which is 0 because $\tilde{G}^0$ is connected.

## 2. A direct construction

A well known theorem of Chevalley asserts that a connected reductive complex linear algebraic group $G$ is obtained by base change from a reductive group scheme $G_{\mathbb{Z}}$ (i.e. an affine smooth group scheme over $\operatorname{spec} \mathbb{Z}$ with connected, reductive geometric fibres c.f.
[14; p. 15]).
In Proposition 2.1, we extend this result to any linear algebraic group over the complex numbers, whose connected component is reductive.
Such a group $G$ is obtained by base change from a group scheme $G_A$ smooth over $\operatorname{spec} A$ for some ring $A$ of $S$-integers in a number field.
This enables us to provide in Theorem 2.2 a more explicit construction of a locally finite approximation away from $p$ whose locally finite group arises as the discrete group of $\mathbb{F}_p$-valued points of such a group scheme $G_A$.
One important consequence of this alternate construction is that the resulting locally finite approximation is induced by a chain of homomorphism and base change maps.
The disadvantage of this construction is that it only provides locally finite approximations away from $p$ for all sufficiently large primes $p$ and does not apply to small primes.

(2.1) Proposition.
Let $G_{\mathbb{C}}$ be a linear algebraic group over $\mathbb{C}$ with reductive connected component.
There exists a number field $L \subset \mathbb{C}$, a finite set of primes of $L$ with associated ring $A$ of $S$-integers in $L$, and a group scheme $G_A$ smooth over $A$, with reductive connected component $G_A^0$, such that $G_{\mathbb{C}} \cong G_A \times_{\operatorname{spec}(A)} \operatorname{spec}(\mathbb{C})$.

Proof.
We claim that there is a $\tilde{\mathbb{Q}}$-group $G_{\mathbb{Q}}$ such that

$$
G _ {\mathbb {Q}} \times_ {\operatorname {s p e c} (\mathbb {Q})} \operatorname {s p e c} (\mathbb {C}) \cong G _ {\mathbb {C}}.
$$

E.M.
Friedlander and G.
Mislin

From the classification of reductive groups we know that there is a group $G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0}$ with $G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0} \times_{\mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{Q})} \mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{C}) \cong G_{\mathbb{C}}^{0}$.
By adapting the classification of nonabelian extensions of abstract groups [9, Chap. IV.8] to the case of algebraic groups over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ and $\mathbb{C}$ (using Hochschild-cohomology as described in [2]), one proceeds as follows.
From the short exact sequence of group schemes $1 \to G_{\mathbb{C}}^{0} \to G_{\mathbb{C}} \to \pi_{\mathbb{C}} \to 1$, $\pi_{\mathbb{C}}$ the constant group scheme on the abstract group $\pi = G_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbb{C}) / G_{\mathbb{C}}^{0}(\mathbb{C})$, we obtain an abstract kernel (in the sense of Mac Lane)

$$
\left(\pi_ {\mathbb {C}}, G _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {0}, \psi_ {\mathbb {C}}: \pi_ {\mathbb {C}} \rightarrow \left(\operatorname {A u t} _ {\mathrm {g r}} G _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {0} / \operatorname {I n} _ {\mathrm {g r}} G _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {0}\right) = E _ {\mathbb {C}}\right).
$$

Note that $E_{\mathbb{C}} \cong E_{\mathbb{Q}} \times_{\mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{Q})} \mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{C})$ where $E_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is the constant group scheme $\mathrm{Aut}_{\mathrm{gr}} G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0} / \mathrm{In}_{\mathrm{gr}} G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0}$.
In follows that $\psi_{\mathbb{C}} = \psi_{\mathbb{Q}} \times_{\mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{Q})} \mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{C})$ for a unique $\psi_{\mathbb{Q}}: \pi_{\mathbb{Q}} \to E_{\mathbb{Q}}$, and we obtain an "abstract kernel" $(\pi_{\mathbb{Q}}, G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0}, \psi_{\mathbb{Q}})$, where $\pi_{\mathbb{Q}} = G_{\mathbb{Q}} / G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0}$.
We denote the associated obstruction 3-cocycle by $c_{\mathbb{Q}}: \pi_{\mathbb{Q}} \times \pi_{\mathbb{Q}} \to C_{\mathbb{Q}}$, $C_{\mathbb{Q}}$ the center of $G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0}$.
Observe that $[c_{\mathbb{Q}}] \in H^{3}(\pi_{\mathbb{Q}}, C_{\mathbb{Q}})$ is 0, since by extension of scalars one has an isomorphism $\sigma: H^{*}(\pi_{\mathbb{Q}}, C_{\mathbb{Q}}) \cong H^{*}(\pi_{\mathbb{C}}, C_{\mathbb{C}})$, $C_{\mathbb{C}} \subset G_{\mathbb{C}}^{0}$ the center, and therefore $\sigma[c_{\mathbb{Q}}] = 0$, since the corresponding abstract kernel is realized by $G_{\mathbb{C}}$.
As a consequence, the $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$-extensions of $G_{\mathbb{Q}}^{0}$ with quotient $\pi_{\mathbb{Q}}$ and action $\psi_{\mathbb{Q}}$ are classified by $H^{2}(\pi_{\mathbb{Q}}, C_{\mathbb{Q}})$ which is a finite group, isomorphic to $H^{2}(\pi_{\mathbb{C}}, C_{\mathbb{C}})$ under $\sigma$, and we conclude that there is a unique $G_{\mathbb{Q}}$ with $G_{\mathbb{Q}} \times_{\mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{Q})} \mathrm{spec}(\mathbb{C}) = G_{\mathbb{C}}$.
The affine algebraic group $G_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is obviously of the form $G_{A} \times_{\mathrm{spec}(A)} \mathrm{spec}(\bar{\mathbb{Q}})$, where $A$ is the ring of $S$-integers in a number field $L, S$ a finite set of primes in $L$, and we may assume $G_{A}$ to be smooth over $A$ with reductive connected component by choosing $S$ large enough.

We now present our alternate construction of a locally finite approximation $\Phi \colon B\mathfrak{g} \to BG$ away from $p$.
This construction is used systematically in [6] and is there denoted by $BT_{G} \to BG$.

Let $G_{A}$ be a group scheme over $\operatorname{spec} A$.
Let $\operatorname{spec} B \to \operatorname{spec} A$ be a base change morphism.
Denote by $G_{A}(B)$ the group of $B$-points of $G_{A}$ and, as in [5, §2], write $G_{A}(B)_{B}$ for the constant group scheme over $\operatorname{spec} B$ with group of components $G_{A}(B)$.
The natural $B$-morphism $\rho \colon G_{A}(B)_{B} \to G_{B} = G_{A} \times_{\operatorname{spec} A} \operatorname{spec} B$ induces a morphism of simplicial schemes $B \rho \colon BG_{A}(B)_{B} \to BG_{B}$ and of associated etale homotopy types $(BG_{A}(B)_{B})_{\mathrm{et}} \to (BG_{B})_{\mathrm{et}}$ (cf.
[4]).
In case $(\operatorname{spec} B)_{\mathrm{et}}$ is contractible, there is a natural homotopy equivalence $BG_{A}(B) \simeq (BG_{A}(B)_{B})_{\mathrm{et}}$ where $G_{A}(B)$ is given the discrete topology.
Hence one obtains in this case a natural homotopy class

$$
B G _ {A} (B) \rightarrow \left(B G _ {B}\right) _ {\text {e t}}. \tag {2.2}
$$

We will make use of this map in the following special case.
Assume $A \subset W(\mathbb{F}_p) = W$, the Witt vectors of $\mathbb{F}_p$, and $B = \mathbb{F}_p$ with $A \to B$ the obvious map.
Assume furthermore given an embedding $\varphi \colon W(\mathbb{F}_p) \to \mathbb{C}$.
We have naturally associated a chain of maps

$$
\left(B G _ {\mathbb {F} _ {p}}\right) _ {\text {e t}} \rightarrow \left(B G _ {W}\right) _ {\text {e t}} \leftarrow \left(B G _ {\mathbb {C}}\right) _ {\text {e t}} \rightarrow \left(B G _ {\mathbb {C}}\right) _ {\mathrm {s . e t}} \rightarrow \operatorname {S i n g} B G _ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C}) \tag {2.3}
$$

in the notation of [4; 8.8].
We assume now that $G_W^0$ is reductive.
Then, according to [4; 8.8] the corresponding chain of maps with $G$ replaced by $G^0$ determines homotopy equivalences between the Sullivan completions ( )

Locally finite approximation of Lie groups, I
431

away from $p$.
If we assume moreover that $G_W / G_W^0 = \pi_W$ is a constant group scheme over $W$, then we have a chain of homotopy equivalences

$$
(B \pi_ {\overline {{\mathbb {F}}} _ {p}}) _ {\mathrm {e t}} \rightarrow (B \pi_ {W}) _ {\mathrm {e t}} \leftarrow (B \pi_ {\mathbb {C}}) _ {\mathrm {e t}} \rightarrow (B \pi_ {\mathbb {C}}) _ {\mathrm {s . e t}} \rightarrow \operatorname {S i n g} B \pi_ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C}).
$$

Therefore, the maps in (2.3) induce all homotopy equivalences between the associated Sullivan completions away from $p$.
Using (2.2) we thus obtain for such a $G_W$ a natural homotopy class (depending on $\varphi \colon W \to \mathbb{C}$)

$$
h \varphi \colon B G _ {A} (\overline {{\mathbb {F}}} _ {p}) = B G _ {W} (\overline {{\mathbb {F}}} _ {p}) \rightarrow (B G _ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C})) ^ {\sim / p}.
$$

(2.4) Theorem.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finite component group.
Let $K \subset G$ denote a maximal compact subgroup and $G_{\mathbb{C}}$ the linear algebraic group over $\mathbb{C}$ whose Lie group of complex points is a complex from for $K$.
Let $A$ be a ring of $S$-integers in a number field as in (2.1) such that $G_{\mathbb{C}} = G_A \times_{\operatorname{spec} A} \operatorname{spec} \mathbb{C}$.
Then for almost all primes $p$, there is a locally finite approximation away from $p$

$$
\Phi \colon B G _ {A} (\overline {{\mathbb {F}}} _ {p}) \rightarrow B G.
$$

Furthermore, if $G$ is connected, we may choose $A = \mathbb{Z}$ and $p$ arbitrary.

Proof.
Let $p$ be a prime not lying under a prime of $S$ and assume that $p$ does not divide $|\pi_0(G)|$.
Let $W$ denote the Witt vectors of $\mathbb{F}_p$ and choose an embedding $\varphi \colon W \to \mathbb{C}$ with $A \subset W$.
Consider the natural homotopy class discussed above

$$
h \varphi \colon B G _ {A} (\overline {{\mathbb {F}}} _ {p}) \rightarrow (B G _ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C})) ^ {\sim / p}.
$$

We claim that this map lifts to a unique homotopy class

$$
\bar {\Phi} \colon B G _ {A} (\overline {{\mathbb {F}}} _ {p}) \rightarrow B G _ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C}).
$$

As in the proof of (1.3) it suffices to verify that $\tilde{H}^{*}(BG_{A}(\mathbb{F}_{p}),\mathbb{Q}) = 0$, which clearly holds, and that $\tilde{H}^{*}(BG_{A}(\mathbb{F}_{p}),\mathbb{Z} / p\mathbb{Z}) = 0$.
For the latter it suffices to see that $G_{A}^{0}(\mathbb{F}_{p})$ is $p$-acyclic, since $p$ does not divide $|G_{A}(\mathbb{F}_{p}) / G_{A}^{0}(\mathbb{F}_{p})| = |\pi_{0}(G)|$.
Since the radical $R$ of $G_{A}^{0}$ has a $p$-acyclic group of $\mathbb{F}_{p}$-points (isomorphic to $(\mathbb{F}_p^*)^n$) we may assume $G_{A}^{0}$ semisimple in which case we have already observed that $G_{A}^{0}(\mathbb{F}_{p})$ is $p$-acyclic (cf.
proof of (1.3)).
If we construct $\bar{\Phi}^0\colon BG_A^0 (\mathbb{F}_p)\to BG_{\mathbb{C}}^0 (\mathbb{C})$ in a similar way we obtain a homotopy commutative diagram of fibration sequences

$$
\begin{array}{c}
B G _ {A} ^ {0} (\mathbb {F} _ {p}) \longrightarrow B G _ {A} (\mathbb {F} _ {p}) \longrightarrow B G _ {A} (\mathbb {F} _ {p}) / G _ {A} ^ {0} (\mathbb {F} _ {p}) \\
\Phi^ {0} \Biggl \downarrow \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \Phi \\
B G _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {0} (\mathbb {C}) \longrightarrow B G _ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C}) \longrightarrow B G _ {\mathbb {C}} (\mathbb {C}) / G _ {\mathbb {C}} ^ {0} (\mathbb {C})
\end{array}
$$

where $\Psi$ is constructed as $\bar{\Phi}$, using the constant group scheme $G_A / G_A^0$ instead of $G_A$.
Obviously, $\Psi$ is a homotopy equivalence.
By [5; 2.3], $\Phi^0$ is a $H^{*}(\mathbb{Z} / n\mathbb{Z})$-isomorphism for $n$ prime to $p$.
An easy spectral sequence argument thus shows that $\bar{\Phi}$ satisfies (1.1 a).
Clearly, (1.1 b) holds since $\Psi$ is an equivalence.
We have already observed that $G_A^0 (\mathbb{F}_p)$ is $p$-acyclic.
Moreover,

E.M.
Friedlander and G.
Mislin

every map $G_A^0(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p) \to F$ to a finite group factors through $G_A^0(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)/R(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)$, where $R \subset G_A^0$ denotes the radical.
But $G_A^0(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)/R(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)$ is a product of (abstract) simple groups.
Thus $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{grps}}(G_A^0(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p), F) = \{1\}$ which completes the proof that $\Phi: BG_A(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p) \to BG_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbb{C})$ is a l.f.
approx/$p$.
Since the inclusions $G_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbb{C}) \leftarrow K \to G$ are homotopy equivalences, we obtain an associated l.f.
approx/$p$ $\Phi$ by composing with $BG_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbb{C}) \simeq BK \simeq BG$.

(2.5) Remark.
The map $\Phi: BG_A(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p) \to BG$ constructed in (2.4) is induced by group homomorphisms and base change maps in the following natural way.
There is a chain of group homomorphisms

$$
G _ {A} (\bar {\mathbb {F}} _ {p}) \leftarrow G _ {A} (W) \rightarrow G _ {A} (\mathbb {C}) \leftarrow K \rightarrow G
$$

with $G_A(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)$, $G_A(W)$ discrete and $G_A(\mathbb{C}), K, G$ given their usual Lie group topology, inducing a homotopy commutative diagram

![img-66.jpeg](img-66.jpeg)

The resulting map $BG_{A}(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_{p})\to (BG)^{- / p}$ lifts, as we have seen, in a unique way up to homotopy to $BG$, and this lift $\varPhi:BG_{A}(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_{p})\to BG$ is our locally finite approximation away from $p$.

# 3. Applications to mapping complexes

H.
Miller has shown that $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(B\pi, X)$ is weakly contractible, where $\pi$ is a locally finite group, $X$ a finite dimensional, connected complex, and $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(-, -)$ the mapping complex of pointed maps between pointed spaces [11].
In Theorem 3.1, we provide a natural extension of Miller's Theorem to Lie groups with finitely many components.
(As first observed by B.I.
Gray [7] and then systematically studied by W.
Meier [10] and A.
Zabrodsky [16], there do exist examples of non-trivial maps from the classifying space of a compact Lie group to a finite complex.) We follow the "vanishing" result of Theorem 3.1 with an explicit description in Theorem 3.3 of the homotopy groups of $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(BG, X)$ for somewhat restricted $X$.
Finally, in Theorem 3.5 we identify $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(BG, X)$ for $X$ restricted as in (3.3) and further restricted by the condition that its universal cover be rationally an $H$-space.

The two basic ingredients in the proof of the following theorem are Miller's Theorem and the existence of locally finite approximations (Theorem 1.3). More effort than might be expected is required to prove Theorem 3.1 because the space $X$ is not assumed to be either finite or simply connected.

(3.1) Theorem.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finitely many components, let $X$ be a finite dimensional, connected complex and let $X \to \tilde{X}$ be the Sullivan profinite completion of $X$.
Then $\mathrm{map}_{\bullet}(BG, \tilde{X})$ is weakly contractible.

Locally finite approximation of Lie groups, I
433

Proof.
Write $\hat{X} = \lim_{x \to x_0} X_x$ where each $X_x$ is a connected complex with finite homotopy groups, the limit being taken in the homotopy category.
Because $\pi_i(\mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{X})) = [\Sigma^i BG, \hat{X}] \cong \lim_{x \to x_0} [\Sigma^i BG, X_x]$ for any $i \geq 0$, it suffices to prove that any composition of the form $\Sigma^i BG \to \hat{X} \to X_x$ is null homotopic.
If $d$ denotes the dimension of $X$, then $\hat{X} \to X_x$, being induced from $X \to X_x$, factors through $(s k_d X_x)$, where $s k_d X_x$ is a finite connected complex with finite fundamental group (isomorphic to $\pi_1(X_x)$).
We conclude that it suffices to prove that $\mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{Y})$ is weakly contractible, whenever $Y$ is a connected finite complex with finite fundamental group.
We first verify that $\mathrm{map}_*(BG, (\hat{Y})^\sim) \to \mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{Y})$ is a weak equivalenc where $(\hat{Y})^\sim$ denotes the universal covering space of $\hat{Y}$.
Because $\Sigma^i BG$ is simply connected for $i \geq 1$, it suffices to prove that any map $f: BG \to \hat{Y}$ factors through $(\hat{Y})^\sim$.
Choose any prime $p$ and any l.f.
approx/p $\Phi: B\mathfrak{g} \to BG$ as in (1.3).
By (1.1b), $f$ factors through $(\hat{Y})^\sim$ if $f \circ \phi: B\mathfrak{g} \to \hat{Y}$ is null-homotopic.
Let $F$ denote the homotopy fibre of $Y \to \hat{Y}$; $F$ is connected and $\pi_*(F) \simeq \pi_{*+1}(Y) \otimes \hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z}$ is a $\mathbb{Q}[\pi_1(Y)]$-module.
By (1.2), $\hat{H}^*(\mathfrak{g}, \pi_*(F)) = 0$, so that $f \circ \Phi$ lifts uniquely to a map $B\mathfrak{g} \to Y$ which is necessarily null-homotopic by Miller's Theorem.
Because $Y \to \hat{Y}$ induces an isomorphism on fundamental groups, the universal covering $(\hat{Y})^\sim$ of $\hat{Y}$ is equivalent to $(\bar{Y})^\sim$ which is equivalent to $\prod_l (\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y})$, where the product is indexed by all primes $l$ and where $(\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y})$ is the Bousfield-Kan $\mathbf{Z}/l$-completion.
Since $(\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y})$ is $H^*(, \mathbf{Z}/l)$-local in the sense of Bousfield [1], $\Phi^*: \mathrm{map}_*(BG, (\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y})) \to \mathrm{map}_*(B\mathfrak{g}, (\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y}))$ is an equivalence whenever $p \neq l$.
By (1.3), $p$ can be chosen different from any specified prime $l$, so that the equivalences

$$
\mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{Y}) \leftarrow \mathrm{map}_*(BG, (\hat{Y})^\sim) \rightarrow \Pi \mathrm{map}_*(BG, (\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y})) \tag{3.2}
$$

reduce the proof of the theorem to the proof that $\mathrm{map}_*(B\mathfrak{g}, (\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y}))$ is weakly contractible whenever $p \neq l$.
This is given by [11; Theorems C, D], because $(\mathbf{Z}/l)_\infty(\hat{Y})$ is a nilpotent space with bounded $\mathbf{Z}/q$-homology for every prime $q$.

In order to be able to relate $\mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{X})$ to $\mathrm{map}_*(BG, X)$ we will need the following auxiliary result.

(3.2) Lemma.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finitely many components and let $X$ be a finite dimensional connected complex with universal covering $\hat{X} \to X$.
Then the induced map

$$
\mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{X}) \rightarrow \mathrm{map}_*(BG, X)
$$

is a weak equivalence.

Proof.
It obviously suffices to prove that $\tilde{X} \xrightarrow{u} X$ induces a bijection of connected components

$$
u_*: \pi_0(\mathrm{map}_*(BG, \hat{X})) = [BG, \hat{X}] \rightarrow \pi_0(\mathrm{map}_*(BG, X)) = [BG, X].
$$

Clearly, $u_*$ is monic; it remains to show that every $f \colon BG \to X$ lifts to $\tilde{X}$ or, equivalently, that $f_*: \pi_1(BG) \to \pi_1(X)$ is trivial.
Because a locally finite approximation $\Phi \colon B\mathfrak{g} \to BG$ induces a surjection on fundamental groups, it suffices to

E.M.
Friedlander and G.
Mislin

prove that $(f\circ \Phi)_{*}:\pi_{1}(B\mathfrak{g})\to \pi_{1}(X)$ is trivial.
This is indeed the case, since $X$ is finite dimensional and therefore, by [11, Theorem 10.1], $f\circ \Phi$ is equivalent to the constant map.

(3.3) Theorem.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finitely many components and let $X$ be a finite dimensional connected complex. Then all homotopy groups $\pi_j(\mathrm{map}_*(BG,X))$, $j \geq 1$, are rational vector spaces.
Moreover, if $\pi_i(X)$ is finitely generated for each $i &gt; 1$, then, for $j \geq 1$

$$
\pi_ {j} \left(\operatorname {m a p} _ {*} (B G, X)\right) \cong \prod_ {k &gt; j} H ^ {k - j} (B G, \pi_ {k + 1} (X) \otimes \hat {\mathbf {Z}} / \mathbf {Z})
$$

and each element in $\pi_j(\mathrm{map}_*(BG,X))$ is represented by a phantom map $\Sigma^j BG\to X$ for any $j\geq 0$.

Proof.
Let $Y \to X$ be the universal covering.
Then $\mathrm{map}_*(BG, Y) \cong \mathrm{map}_*(BG, X)$ by (3.2).
Denote by $Y_R$ the Bousfield $H_*(; R)$-localization of $Y$ (cf.
[1]).
Since $Y$ is an arithmetic space in the terminology of [3], there is a pullback diagram

![img-67.jpeg](img-67.jpeg)

We note that $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,\prod Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l})\simeq \prod \mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l})$ is weakly contractible, since by the universal property of $Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l}$, any locally finite approximation $B\mathfrak{g}\to BG$ away from a prime different from $l$ gives rise to a weak equivalence $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l})\simeq \mathrm{map}_{*}(B\mathfrak{g},Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l})$, and the latter is weakly contractible according to Miller's Theorems $C$ and $D$ [11].
Thus $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,Y)\simeq \mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,F),F$ the homotopy fibre of $Y\rightarrow \prod Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l}$, or equivalently of $Y_{\mathbb{Q}}\to (\prod Y_{\mathbf{Z} / l})_{\mathbb{Q}}$.
The homotopy groups of $F$ are all rational vector spaces, and as a consequence, $\Omega F$ is a product of Eilenberg-MacLane spaces.
Therefore for $j\geq 1$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \pi_ {j} \left(\operatorname {m a p} _ {*} (B G, Y)\right) \cong \pi_ {j - 1} \left(\operatorname {m a p} _ {*} (B G, \Omega F)\right) = \left[ \Sigma^ {j - 1} B G, \Omega F \right] \\ = \prod_ {k \geq j} H ^ {k - j + 1} (B G; \pi_ {k + 1} (F)), \\ \end{array}
$$

which is a $\mathbb{Q}$-vektor space.
Assume now that $\pi_i(Y)$ is finitely generated for $i\geq 2$.
Then $\pi_k(F)\cong \pi_{k + 1}(Y)\otimes \hat{\mathbf{Z}} /\mathbf{Z}$ [15; Theorem 3.1], so that

$$
\pi_ {j} \left(\operatorname {m a p} _ {*} (B G, X)\right) \cong \prod_ {k &gt; j} H ^ {k - j} (B G, \pi_ {k + 1} (X) \otimes \hat {\mathbf {Z}} / \mathbf {Z})
$$

for $j\geq 1$

Let $f \colon \Sigma^j BG \to X$ represent an element of $\pi_j(\mathrm{map}_*(BG, X))$ for any $j \geq 0$.
By (3.2) $f$ lifts to $\tilde{f} \colon \Sigma^j BG \to Y$, $Y$ the universal cover of $X$; by (3.1) $\tilde{f}$ determines the null-homotopic map $(\tilde{f})^* \colon \Sigma^j BG \to \tilde{Y}$.
Moreover, $[Z, Y] \to [Z, \tilde{Y}]$ is injective whenever $Z$ is a finite complex because $Y$ is simply connected with finitely generated homotopy groups (cf.
[15]).
We conclude that $\tilde{f}$ and therefore $f$ is null-homotopic when restricted to any finite skeleton of $\Sigma^j BG$.

Locally finite approximation of Lie groups, I
435

We require the following (presumable well known) lemma in order to determine the weak homotopy type of $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,X)$ for $X$ with $\tilde{X}$ rationally an $H$-space.

3.4 Lemma.
Suppose $Y$ is a simple connected space of finite type, whose rationalization $Y_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an $H$-space.
Then the homotopy fibre $F$ of the Sullivan completion $Y \to \tilde{Y}$ is a connected $H$-space, homotopy equivalent to $\prod_{n \geq 1} K(V_n, n)$, where $V_n$ is a rational vector space isomorphic to $\pi_{n+1}(Y) \otimes (\hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z})$.

Proof.
As observed in (3.3), $\pi_j(F) \cong \pi_{i+1}(Y) \otimes \hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z} \cong \pi_{i+1}((\tilde{Y})_{\mathbb{Q}}) / \pi_{i+1}(Y_{\mathbb{Q}})$ and $F$ is the homotopy fibre of $Y_{\mathbb{Q}} \to (\tilde{Y})_{\mathbb{Q}}$.
Since $Y_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an $H$-space and $Y$ is of finite type, all $k$-invariants of $Y$ and $\tilde{Y}$ are torsion classes.
Consequently, $Y_{\mathbb{Q}} \to (\tilde{Y})_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is the product of maps $K(\pi_n(Y_{\mathbb{Q}}), n) \to K(\pi_n((\tilde{Y})_{\mathbb{Q}}, n)$, so that $F$ is homotopy equivalent to $\prod_{n \geq 1} K(\pi_{n+1}(Y) \otimes \hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z}, n)$.

We can now prove the following structure theorem on $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,X)$.
We point out to the reader that $\hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z}$ is a $\mathbb{Q}$-vector space of the same cardinality as the real numbers.

3.5 Theorem.
Let $G$ be a Lie group with finitely many components and let $X$ be a connected finite dimensional complex with $\pi_i(X)$ finitely generated for all $i \geq 2$.
Suppose that the rationalization $(\tilde{X})_{\mathbb{Q}}$ of the universal cover $\tilde{X}$ of $X$ is an $H$-space.
Then there is a weak equivalence

$$
\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG, X) \simeq \prod_{j \geq 0} K(W_j, j)
$$

where $W_j \cong \prod_{k &gt; j} H^{k - j}(BG; \pi_{k+1}(X) \otimes \hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z})$.
In particular, there are no essential maps $BG \to X$ if and only if for every $m \geq 1$ either $H_m(BG)$ or $\pi_{m+1}(X)$ is a finite group.

Proof.
The homotopy groups $\pi_j(\mathrm{map}_*(BG,X)$ for $j\geq 1$ are given by Theorem 3.3. Let $F$ denote the homotopy fibre of the Sullivan completion $Y\to \tilde{Y}$ where $Y$ is the universal cover of $X$.
Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.2 imply that the composition $\mathrm{map}_*(BG,F)\to \mathrm{map}_*(BG,Y)\to \mathrm{map}_*(BG,X)$ is a weak equivalence.
Since $F$ is a connected $H$-space by Lemma 3.4, $F$ admits an $H$-inverse and therefore all connected components of $\mathrm{map}_*(BG,F)$ are weakly equivalent:

$$
\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG, F) \simeq \mathrm{map}_{*}(BG, F)_0 \times K(W_0, 0),
$$

where $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,F)_0$ denotes the connected component of the point map and

$$
W_0 \cong \pi_0(\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG, F)) \cong [BG, F] \cong \prod_{k &gt; 0} H^k(BG, \pi_{k+1}(X) \otimes \hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z}).
$$

Clearly, $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,F)_0$ is a (weak) homotopy commutative and homotopy associative $H$-space since $F$ is, and since all homotopy groups of $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,F)_0$ are $\mathbb{Q}$-vector spaces, the $k$-invariants of $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,F)_0$ all vanish (cf.
[12; Appendix]).
Therefore, $\mathrm{map}_{*}(BG,F)_0 \simeq \prod_{j \geq 1} K(W_j, j)$.

E.M.
Friedlander and G.
Mislin

As a simple example, we see that $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(BS^3,S^5)\simeq K(W_0,0)$, since

$$
W _ {j} \cong \prod_ {k &gt; j} H ^ {k - j} (B S ^ {3}, \pi_ {k + 1} (S ^ {5}) \otimes \hat {\mathbf {Z}} / \mathbf {Z}) = 0 \quad \text{for } j &gt; 0.
$$

Therefore, every component of $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(BS^3,S^5)$ is contractible and the set of components of $\mathrm{map}_{\star}(BS^3,S^5)$ is in one-to-one correspondence with $W_{0} \cong H^{4}(BS^{3},\hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z}) \cong \hat{\mathbf{Z}} / \mathbf{Z} \cong [BS^3,S^5]$.

# $p$-convex Riemannian manifolds

Ji-Ping Sha

Stony Brook, Department of Mathematics, State University of New York, Stony Brook, NY 11794, USA

Let $M$ be an $n$-dimensional compact connected manifolds with non-empty boundary $\partial M$.
By a theorem of Gromov $M$ always carries a riemannian metric with positive sectional curvature.
However, in this theorem there are no assumptions on the behavior of the metric at the boundary.
If we impose some geometric conditions on $\partial M$, then the situation is certainly different.
For instance: If $\partial M$ is not connected, then $M$ can not even carry a metric of positive Ricci curvature for which the boundary has positive mean curvature (cf.
[7]).
Furthermore, it follows from the work of Cheeger-Gromoll-Meyer (cf.
[2, 6]) that only the standard disk can carry a metric of positive sectional curvature whose boundary is convex.

In this paper we shall generalize these results.
For each integer $p$, $1 \leq p \leq (n - 1)$, we shall formulate a notion of “$p$-convexity” at the boundary, and prove that this condition together with non-negative sectional curvature in the interior implies that the manifold is at most $(p - 1)$-dimensional, i.e., is homotopy equivalent to a complex of dimension $\leq (p - 1)$.
This result is “sharp” in the sense that any manifold with this property can be shown to carry such a metric.

Let us now be more specific.

**Definition.** $\partial M$ is called $p$-convex if the sum of any $p$ principal curvatures (with respect to the inward normal vector) at each point is positive.

Here we consider $\partial M$ as a normally oriented hypersurface in $M$, so that its principal curvatures are well defined in the usual way.
Note that “1-convexity” means that the boundary is locally convex, and “$(n - 1)$-convexity” means that the boundary has positive mean curvature.
Our main result is the following.

**Theorem 1.** If $M$ carries a riemannian metric with non-negative sectional curvature and $p$-convex boundary, then $M$ has the homotopy type of a CW-complex of dimension $\leq (p - 1)$.

It should be pointed out that there has been a concept of $p$-convexity in both real and complex geometry for a long time.
It is generally defined by the

438
J.-P.
Sha

requirement that at least $(n - p)$ eigenvalues of the second fundamental form (or the Levi form in the complex case) be positive.
However, with this definition of $p$-convexity, Theorem 1 above is false (a counterexample was shown to us by M.
Gromov).
On the other hand, J.D.
Moore and T.
Schulte recently proved a special case of our theorem for $p = (n - 2)$ (cf.
[10]).

Our stronger notion of $p$-convexity was first suggested by a Bochner-type vanishing theorem that we proved for harmonic forms on riemannian manifolds with boundary.
Our main result here can be applied directly to give vanishing results for harmonic forms.
This is discussed in the appendix to this paper.
Moreover, since the Bochner-Weizenbock-type formulas are of some independent interest, we shall also give a brief derivation of these formulas in the appendix.

The principal idea in the proof of Theorem 1 above is to show the “$p$-convexity” of the distance function to the boundary and then to construct a delicate smoothing.
It is a generally recognized principle that the distance function carries much topological information.
However, it is rather hard to handle due to the complexity of its “critical points”.

In a sequel to this work the author will prove a converse to Theorem 1. We shall show that if a compact $n$-dimensional manifold with boundary is a handle body with handles only of dimension $\leq (p - 1)$, then it supports a riemannian metric of non-negative sectional curvature and $p$-convex boundary.
So Theorem 1 leads to a complete characterization of such manifolds.

This paper represents part of author’s Ph.D.
Thesis written at Stony Brook.
I would like to take this opportunity to thank my advisor Professor Blaine Lawson for his constant inspiration and kind guidance.

Now we begin the proof of the theorem.
In what follows, we always assume that $M$ satisfies the conditions we mentioned above.

## 1. An algebraic lemma

Let $V$ be a real vector space of dimension $d$ with inner product $\langle, \rangle$, and let $A$ be a symmetric linear transformation from $V$ into itself.
We call the quadratic form $\langle A \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ $p$-positive if for all orthonormal sets of $p$ vectors $\{X_1, \ldots, X_p\} \subset V$, we have

$$
\langle A X _ {1}, X _ {1} \rangle + \dots + \langle A X _ {p}, X _ {p} \rangle &gt; 0.
$$

**Lemma 1.1.** The following are equivalent.

i) $\langle A\cdot ,\cdot \rangle$ is $p$-positive;
ii) The sum of any $p$ eigenvalues of $A$ is positive.

**Proof.** That i) $\Rightarrow$ ii) is obvious.

To prove that ii) $\Rightarrow$ i), let $\lambda_1 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$ be the eigenvalues of $A$, with corresponding orthonormal eigenvectors $Y_1, \ldots, Y_d$.
Define $q$ by the condition that $\lambda_q &gt; 0 \geq \lambda_{q+1}$, and note that $q &gt; d - p$.
Now suppose that $X_i = \sum_{j=1}^{d} a_{ij} Y_j$, $i = 1, \ldots, p$ are $p$ arbitrary orthogonal unit vectors.
Then we have that

p
-convex Riemannian manifolds

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{i=1}^{p} \langle A x_{i}, X_{i} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_{j} a_{ij}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_{j} \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{ij}^{2}\right) \\
\geq \lambda_{d-p+1} \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d-p+1} \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{ij}^{2}\right) + \lambda_{d-p+2} \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{i,d-p+2}^{2} + \dots + \lambda_{q} \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{iq}^{2} \\
+ \lambda_{q+1} + \dots + \lambda_{d} \geq \lambda_{d-p+1} \left[ (d-p+1) - (d-p) + \sum_{i=p+1}^{d} a_{i,d-p+2}^{2} \right. \\
\left. + \dots + \sum_{i=p+1}^{d} a_{iq}^{2} \right] + \lambda_{d-p+2} \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{i,d-p+2}^{2} + \dots + \lambda_{q} \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{iq}^{2} + \dots + \lambda_{q+1} \\
+ \dots + \lambda_{d} \geq \lambda_{d-p+1} + \lambda_{d-p+2} + \dots + \lambda_{q} + \lambda_{q+1} + \dots + \lambda_{d} &gt; 0.
\end{array}
$$

Note that we have extended $(a_{ij})$ to a $d \times d$ orthogonal matrix.

#

From this lemma, it is easy to see the following.

**Corollary 1.2.** There exist $\delta_0 &gt; 0$ and $\lambda &gt; 0$ such that for all $y \in \partial M$ and for all sets of $p$ unit tangent vectors $\{Y_1, \ldots, Y_p\} \subset T_y \partial M$ with $|\langle Y_i, Y_j \rangle| &lt; \delta_0$ for $i \neq j$, one has that

$$
a_{1} S(Y_{1}, Y_{1}) + \dots + a_{p} S(Y_{p}, Y_{p}) &gt; \lambda
$$

where $S$ is the second fundamental form of $\partial M$ in $M$, and where $a_1, \ldots, a_p$ are arbitrary real numbers with $|a_i - 1| &lt; \delta_0$ for all $i$.

## 2. The modified boundary distance function

Let $c_{1} &gt; 0$ and $c_{2} &gt; 0$ be two generic constants whose value will be specified later on.
Set

$$
\tau(t) = c_{1} e^{c_{2} t}
$$

and consider the function

$$
\tilde{F} = \tau \circ F: M \to \mathbb{R}
$$

where $F(x) \equiv -\mathrm{dist}(x, \partial M)$ is the negative of the distance to the boundary in $M$.

We call a function $f$ on $M$ uniformly $p$-convex if for any given $C \in \hat{M}$, there exist $\delta &gt; 0$ and $\eta &gt; 0$ such that for all $x \in C$ and for all orthonormal sets $\{X_1, \ldots, X_p\} \subset T_x M$, we have that

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{p} \left[ f \left(\exp_{x} (-t X_{i})\right) + f \left(\exp_{x} (t X_{i})\right) - 2f(x) \right] \geq \eta t^{2}
$$

for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ with $|t| &lt; \delta$.

Our main purpose in this section is to prove the following.

**Theorem 2.1.** $\tilde{F}$ is uniformly $p$-convex.

**Proof.** Suppose $x \in \hat{M}$.
Choose a minimal geodesic $c_{0}(s) = \exp_{x} s X_{0}$, $0 \leq s \leq s_{0} = \mathrm{dist}(x, \partial M)$, from $x$ to $\partial M$.
Let $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{p} \in T_{x} M$ be orthogonal unit vectors.

440
J.-P.
Sha

Suppose the angle between $X_0$ and the linear subspace spanned by $\{X_1, \ldots, X_p\}$ is $\alpha$.
We have $0 \leq \alpha \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$, and there are three cases to consider.

Case I.
Suppose $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{2}$.
Set

$$
c_t^i(s) = \exp_{c_0(s)} t \bar{X}_i
$$

where $\bar{X}_i$ is the vector field generated by parallel translation of $X_i$ along $c_0$.

![img-68.jpeg](img-68.jpeg)
Fig.
1

It is not hard to see that for some $\delta_1 &gt; 0$ there exist smooth functions $a_i(t)$, $1 \leq i \leq p$, with the property that the curves $c_t^i$ are well defined for $0 \leq s \leq a_i(t)$ and have $c_t^i(a_i(t)) \in \partial M$ for each $i$.
A calculation in Fermi coordinates shows that $a_i'(0) = 0$ and $a_i''(0) = -S(X_i, X_i)$.
We then have that,

$$
L(c_t^i) = \int_0^{a_t(t)} \|c_t^i\| \, ds
$$

and

$$
\frac{d}{dt} L(c_t^i) = \|c_t^i(a_i(t))\| \, a_i'(t) + \int_0^{a_t(t)} \frac{\langle V_{\dot{c}_t^i} \bar{X}_i, c_t^i \rangle}{\|c_t^i\|} \, ds,
$$

from which we conclude that

$$
\frac{d}{dt} L(c_t^i)|_{t=0} = 0
$$

and

$$
\frac{d^2}{dt^2} L(c_t^i)|_{t=0} = a_t''(0) - \int_0^{s_0} \langle R(\dot{c}_0, \bar{X}_i) \bar{X}_i, \dot{c}_0 \rangle \, ds \leq -S(\bar{X}_i, \bar{X}_i).
$$

Corollary 1.1 implies that

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{p} \left( -\frac{d^2}{dt^2} L(c_t^i) \right|_{t=0} &gt; \lambda.
$$

Choose $c_1 &gt; \frac{1}{c_2} e^{c_2 d}$, where $d$ is the diameter of $M$.
Then we have $\tau'(-s_0) &gt; 1$, and therefore

$$
\left. \frac{d^2}{dt^2} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{p} \tau(-L(c_t^i)) \right) \right|_{t=0} = \tau'(-s_0) \sum_{i=1}^{p} \left( -\frac{d^2}{dt^2} L(c_t^i) \right|_{t=0} &gt; \lambda.
$$

p
-convex Riemannian manifolds

It follows that there exists $\delta_2 &gt; 0$ such that for $|t| &lt; \delta_2$,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{i=1}^{p} \left[ \tilde{F} \left(\exp_{x} (-t X_{i}) + \tilde{F} \left(\exp_{x} t X_{i}\right) - 2 \tilde{F}(x)\right) \right. \\
\left. \geq \sum_{i=1}^{p} \left[ \tau \left(-L \left(c_{-t}^{i}\right)\right) + \tau \left(-L \left(c_{t}^{i}\right)\right) - 2 \tau \left(-s_{0}\right) \right] \right] &gt; \frac{\lambda}{2} t^{2}.
\tag{1}
\end{array}
$$

Case II.
Suppose $\frac{\pi}{2} &gt; \alpha \geq \alpha_0$ where $\alpha_0$ is a constant such that $|\sin^2 \alpha_0 - 1| &lt; \delta_0$ and $|\langle X_1', X_j' \rangle| &lt; \delta_0$ for $i \neq j$, where $X_i'$ denotes the normalized projection of $X_i$ onto the orthogonal complement $X_0^\perp$ of $X_0$.

For simplicity, we momentarily omit the subscripts $i$ ($1 \leq i \leq p$).

![img-69.jpeg](img-69.jpeg)
Fig.
2

For each sufficiently small $t &gt; 0$, let $a_t$ be the arc-length parametrized minimal goodesic segment from the point $c(t) = \exp_x tX$ to the curve $c_0$.
(See the figure above.) Suppose $a_t(0) = c_0(v_t)$ and $c(t) = a_t(u_t)$.
Let

$$
c_{t}(s) = \exp_{c_{0}(s)} u_{t} \bar{a}_{t}(0), \quad s \geq v_{t}
$$

be a curve from $c(t)$ to $\partial M$, where $\dot{a}_t(0)$ is the parallel translation of $\dot{a}_t(0)$ along $c_0$.
By the argument given in case I, there exists $\delta_1 &gt; 0$ so that for $t &lt; \delta_1$

$$
- L(c_{t}) \geq -s_{0} + v_{t} + \frac{1}{2} s (\bar{a}_{t}(0), \bar{a}_{t}(0)) u_{t}^{2}.
$$

By Rauch's comparison theorem, we have that

$$
u_{t}^{2} \leq v_{t}^{2} + t^{2} - 2 t v_{t} \cos \alpha
$$

$$
t^{2} \leq u_{t}^{2} + v_{t}^{2}.
$$

Consequently, we find that

$$
2 v_{t} t \cos \alpha \leq 2 v_{t}^{2} \quad \text{i.e.} \quad v_{t} \geq t \cos \alpha.
$$

Furthermore, $\frac{u_t}{t \sin \alpha} \to 1$ as $t \to 0^+$, and $\sin \alpha_0 \leq \sin \alpha \leq 1$.
Note also that $\bar{a}_t(0) \to \overline{X}'$ as $t \to 0^+$.
Combining these facts, we get a $\delta_2 &gt; 0$, such that for $t &lt; \delta_2$, we have

$$
- L(c_{t}) \geq -s_{0} + t \cos \alpha + \frac{1}{2} a S (\overline{X}', \overline{X}') t^{2}
$$

where $a$ is some real number with $|a - 1| &lt; \delta_0$.

442
J.-P.
Sha

From this we conclude, after noting that $\frac{d^{+}}{dt} (-L(c_{t}))|_{t = 0} = \cos \alpha$, that

$$
\frac {d ^ {2}}{d t ^ {2}} \left(- L \left(c _ {t}\right)\right) | _ {t = 0} \geq a \left(S \left(\bar {X} ^ {\prime}, \bar {X} ^ {\prime}\right). \right.
$$

On the other hand, let $a_{-t}$ be a minimal geodesic segment from the point $c(-t) = \exp_x(-tX)$ to the point $a_{-t}(u_{-t}) = \exp_x(-t\sin \alpha X')$.
Let

$$
c _ {- t} (s) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} a _ {- t} (s), &amp; 0 \leq s \leq u _ {- t} \\ \exp_ {c _ {0} (s - u _ {- t})} (- t \sin \alpha \bar {X} ^ {\prime}). &amp; s \geq u _ {- t} \end{array} \right.
$$

By the same use of case I arguments and Rauch's comparison theorem, we have that

$$
- L \left(c _ {- t}\right) \geq - s _ {0} - t \cos \alpha + \frac {1}{2} S \left(\bar {X} ^ {\prime}, \bar {X} ^ {\prime}\right) t ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \alpha .
$$

We see that $L(c_{-t})$ is right differentiable and that $\frac{d^{+}}{dt} (-L(c_{-t}))|_{t = 0} = -\cos \alpha$.
This implies that

$$
\frac {d ^ {2 -}}{d t ^ {2}} \left(- L \left(c _ {t}\right)\right) | _ {t = 0} \geq a S \left(\bar {X} _ {i} ^ {\prime}, \bar {X} _ {i} ^ {\prime}\right)
$$

and therefore that

$$
\left. \frac {d ^ {2}}{d t ^ {2}} \left(\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {p} \tau \left(- L \left(c _ {t} ^ {i}\right)\right)\right) \right| _ {t = 0} \geq \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {p} a _ {i} S \left(\bar {X} _ {i} ^ {\prime}, \bar {X} _ {i} ^ {\prime}\right) &gt; \lambda .
$$

The same final arguments as those given in Case I complete the proof of Case II.

Case III.
Suppose $0 \leq \alpha \leq \alpha_0$.
Let $\alpha_i$ denote the angle between $c_0$ and $X_i$.
If $\alpha_i \geq \alpha_0$ for all $i$, then the arguments of Case II apply, so we shall assume that $\alpha_1 &lt; \alpha_0$.
Set

$$
c _ {t} ^ {1} (s) = \exp_ {c _ {0} (s)} a (s) t \bar {X} _ {1}, \quad 0 \leq s \leq s _ {0}
$$

![img-70.jpeg](img-70.jpeg)
Fig.
3

p
-convex Riemannian manifolds

where $a(s)$ is a non-negative smooth function on $0 \leq s \leq s_0$ such that $a(0) = 1$, $a(s_0) = 0$.
Then we have

$$
\frac {d}{d t} L \left(c _ {t} ^ {1}\right) \mid_ {t = 0} = - \cos \alpha \leq - \cos \alpha_ {0},
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {d ^ {2}}{d t ^ {2}} \tau (- L (c _ {t} ^ {1})) | _ {t = 0} = c _ {1} c _ {2} ^ {2} e ^ {- c _ {2} s _ {0}} \left[ \frac {d (- L (c _ {t} ^ {1}))}{d t} \Bigg | _ {t = 0} \right] ^ {2} \\ + c _ {1} c _ {2} e ^ {- c _ {2} s _ {0}} \frac {d ^ {2} (- L (c _ {t} ^ {1}))}{d t ^ {2}} \Bigg | _ {t = 0} \\ \geqslant c _ {1} c _ {2} ^ {2} e ^ {- c _ {2} s _ {0}} \cos^ {2} \alpha_ {0} - c _ {1} c _ {2} e ^ {- c _ {2} s _ {0}} K \\ \end{array}
$$

where $K$ is a positive constant depending only on the manifold $M$.

For the other $X_{i}^{\prime \prime}\mathrm{s}$, we clearly have

$$
\frac {d ^ {2}}{d t ^ {2}} \tau (- L (c _ {t} ^ {i})) | _ {t = 0} \geqslant - c _ {1} c _ {2} e ^ {- c _ {2} s _ {0}} K
$$

where

$$
c _ {t} ^ {i} = \exp_ {c _ {0} (s)} a (s) t \bar {X} _ {i}, \quad 0 \leq s \leq s _ {0}.
$$

By choosing $c_{2} &gt; \frac{(n - 1)K}{\cos^{2}\alpha_{0}}$, $c_{1} &gt; \max \left\{\frac{1}{c_{2}} e^{c_{2}d}, \frac{\lambda}{c_{2}} e^{c_{2}d}(c_{2}\cos^{2}\alpha_{0} - (n - 1)K)^{-1}\right\}$ we get

$$
\left. \frac {d ^ {2}}{d t ^ {2}} \left[ \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {p} \tau (- L (c _ {t} ^ {i})) \right] \right| _ {t = 0} &gt; \lambda .
$$

The same argument as before now gives (1).

To complete the proof of Theorem 2.1 we first observe by careful checking that the $\delta_1, \delta_2$ in the proof can always be chosen uniformly in small open sets.
This implies that $\delta$ can be chosen uniformly on compact subsets of $\mathbb{M}$; and the argument is complete.

## 3. Smooth approximation of $\tilde{F}$

Let $\varphi \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a non-negative $C^\infty$ function which: has support contained in $[-1,1]$, is constant in $[- \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}]$, and has the property that $\int_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n} \varphi(\|v\|) d\mu = 1$, where $\|v\|$ is the euclidean length of $v$ and $d\mu$ is the usual Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}^n$.

There is a collar neighborhood $U \approx \partial M \times [0, t_0]$ of $\partial M$ in $M$.
Choose a $C^\infty$ function $\xi \colon M \to [0,1]$ such that $\xi \equiv 0$ on $\partial M \times \left[0, \frac{t_0}{4}\right]$ and $\xi \equiv 1$ on $\tilde{M} \equiv M$

444
J.-P.
Sha

$$
\begin{array}{l}
- \partial M \times \left[ 0, \frac {t _ {0}}{2} \right). \text{Define } f _ {\varepsilon}, 0 &lt; \varepsilon &lt; \frac {t _ {0}}{4}, \text{by} \\
f _ {\varepsilon} (x) = \frac {1}{\varepsilon^ {n}} \int_ {v \in T _ {x} M} \varphi \left(\frac {\| v \|}{\varepsilon}\right) \cdot \tilde {F} (\exp_ {x} \xi (x) v) d \Omega_ {x} \\
\end{array}
$$

where $\| \cdot \|$ and $d\Omega_{x}$ are the euclidean metric and volume on $T_{x}M$ obtained from the riemannian metric of $M$.

**Lemma 3.1.** For all sufficiently small $\varepsilon &gt; 0$, the function $f_{\varepsilon}$ is $C^\infty$ on all of $M$ and has the property that $f_{\varepsilon}(x) = \tilde{F}(x)$ for $x \in \partial M \times \left[0, \frac{t_0}{4}\right]$.

_Proof._ The second statement is obvious for all $\varepsilon$, so we only need to check the differentiability of $f_{\varepsilon}$ at $x \in \tilde{M}$.
For sufficiently small $\varepsilon &gt; 0$,

$$
f _ {\varepsilon} (x) = \frac {1}{\varepsilon^ {n}} \int \varphi \left(\frac {\| \theta_ {x} ^ {- 1} y \|}{\varepsilon}\right) \cdot \tilde {F} (y) d _ {x} y
$$

where

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\theta_ {x} (v) = \exp_ {x} v \\
d _ {x} y = \theta_ {x} ^ {- 1 ^ {*}} (d \Omega_ {x}).
\end{array}
$$

Standard results about differentiation under the integral sign complete the argument.

#

A little modification of the arguments as in [5] then gives the following.

**Proposition 3.2.** There exists $\eta &gt; 0$ such that for all $x \in \tilde{M}$ and for arbitrary orthonormal vectors $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{p} \in T_{x}M$, one has

$$
\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {p} \frac {d ^ {2}}{d t ^ {2}} f _ {\varepsilon} \left(\exp_ {x} t X _ {i}\right) | _ {t = 0} &gt; \eta
$$

for all sufficiently small $\varepsilon &gt; 0$.

## 4. Completion of the proof

It is a well-known fact that the Morse functions form an open dense subset of $C^\infty(M)$ in the $C^2$ topology.
By §3, we can get a Morse function $\psi: M \to [0,1]$ with only non-degenerate critical points in $\tilde{M}$, such that $\psi^{-1}(1) = \partial M$ and such that the Hessian $H$ at any critical point is $p = \text{positive}$.
This implies that the index of $H$ is $\leq (p - 1)$.
The theorem now follows from standard Morse theory.
In fact we conclude that there exists a CW-complex of dimension $\leq (p - 1)$ which is a deformation retract of $M$.

p
-convex Riemannian manifolds

# Appendix: Harmonic forms on Riemannian manifolds with boundary and bochner-type vanishing theorems

Let $M$ be an $n$-dimensional compact riemannian manifold and $\Delta$ be the Hodge Laplacian on $M$.
If $\partial M = \emptyset$, Hodge's Theorem tells us that the space of harmonic $k$-forms

$$
\mathbb {I} \mathrm {H} ^ {k} \equiv \{w: w \text{ is a } k\text{-form on } M \text{ with } \Delta w = 0 \}
$$

is isomorphic to the real cohomology group $H^{k}(M;\mathbb{R})$.

In the case $\partial M \neq \emptyset$, some elliptic boundary conditions are needed.
Let

$$
\begin{array}{l}
E _ {r} ^ {k} (M) \equiv \{w: w \text{ is a } k\text{-form on } M \text{ such that } i ^ {*} w = 0 \\
\text{and } i ^ {*} \partial w = 0 \}
\end{array}
$$

where $i\colon \partial M\to M$ is the inclusion and $\delta$ is the adjoint of the exterior derivative $d$.
Set

$$
\mathbb {I} \mathrm {H} _ {r} ^ {k} \equiv \{w \in E _ {r} ^ {k} (M): \Delta w = 0 \}.
$$

Then by the generalized Hodge Theorem $\mathbb{H}_r^k \cong H^k(M, \partial M; \mathbb{R})$ (cf.
[3]).
Consequently, as an application of Theorem 1, we have the following.

**Proposition A.** If $M$ has non-negative sectional curvature and $p$-convex boundary, then

$$
\mathbb {I} \mathrm {H} _ {r} ^ {k} = 0
$$

for $k = 0,1,\ldots ,(n - p)$

A different version of Proposition A can be obtained from a Bochner-type vanishing formula.
In fact, it was these formulas that originally suggested to us the idea of "p-convexity".

**Proposition B.** If the curvature operator $R$ of $M$ is non-negative and positive somewhere, and if the sum of any $p$ principal curvatures at each point of $\partial M$ is non-negative, then $\mathbb{H}_r^k = 0$ for $k = 0,1,\ldots ,(n - p)$.

**Proof.** For the convenience of calculation, we use the canonical isomorphism $\varLambda^{*}(M)\cong Cl(M)$ (cf.
[8]).

Recall the general Bochner identity,

$$
\Delta = \nabla^ {*} \nabla + \Re
$$

where $\Re \in \mathrm{Hom}(Cl(M))$ is a symmetric bundle endomorphism with the property that $\Re \geq 0$ (or $&gt;0$) at any point of $M$ where the curvature operator is $\geq 0$ (or $&gt;0$ respectively) (cf.
[4, 8]).

To prove the proposition, we only need to show that the connection Laplacian $\nabla^{*}\nabla$ is non-negative on $\mathbb{H}_r^k$, i.e., we need to show that for any $\varphi \in \mathbb{H}_r^k$, we have

$$
\int_ {M} \langle \nabla^ {*} \nabla \varphi , \varphi \rangle \geq 0.
$$

446
J.-P.
Sha

By a direct calculation using Stoke's theorem,

$$
\int_{M} \langle V^{*} V \varphi, \varphi \rangle = \int_{M} \langle V \varphi, V \varphi \rangle + \int_{\partial M} \langle V_{N} \varphi, \varphi \rangle
$$

where $N$ is the inner unit normal of $M$.
Hence, it will suffice to show that $\langle V_N \varphi, \varphi \rangle \geq 0$ on $\partial M$.

Observe that

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &amp;= \int_{M} \langle \Delta \varphi, \varphi \rangle = \int_{M} d \delta \varphi \Delta * \varphi + \int_{M} \delta d \varphi \Delta * \varphi \\
&amp;= \int_{M} d (\delta \varphi \Delta * \varphi) - (-1)^{k-1} \int_{M} \delta \varphi \Delta d * \varphi + \int_{M} \varphi \Delta * \delta d \varphi \\
&amp;= \int_{M} d (\delta \varphi \Delta * \varphi) + \int_{M} \delta \varphi \Delta * \delta \varphi + (-1)^{k-1} \int_{M} \varphi \Delta d * d \varphi \\
&amp;= \int_{M} d (\delta \varphi \Delta * \varphi) + \int_{M} \delta \varphi \Delta * \delta \varphi + \int_{M} d \varphi \Delta * d \varphi - \int_{M} d (\varphi \Delta * d \varphi) \\
&amp;= (-1)^{n-1} \int_{\partial M} i * \delta \varphi \Delta i^{*} * \varphi + \int_{M} \| \delta \varphi \|^{2} + \int_{M} \| d \varphi \|^{2} - (-1)^{n-1} \int_{\partial M} i^{*} \varphi \Delta i^{*} * d \varphi \\
&amp;= \int_{M} \| \delta \varphi \|^{2} + \int_{M} \| d \varphi \|^{2}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Here we have used the condition that $i^{*} \varphi = i^{*} \delta \varphi = 0$.
We conclude that $d\varphi = 0$, $D\varphi = 0$ where $D \equiv d + \delta$ is the "Dirac operator" on $Cl(M)$ (cf.
[8]).

Fix $x \in \partial M$, and choose a smooth orthonormal tangent frame field $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ in a neighborhood of $x$ such that $e_n = N$ on the boundary, and such that at the point $x$

(i) $V_{e_j} N = -\lambda_j e_j$ for $j = 1, \ldots, (n-1)$;
(ii) $(V_{e_j} e_k)^T = 0$ (and therefore $V_{e_j} e_k = \delta_{j_k} \lambda_k N$) for $j, k = 1, \ldots, (n-1)$;
(iii) and $V_{N} e_{i} = 0$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$.

Suppose that $\varphi$ has the form

$$
\varphi = \sum_{i_{1} &lt; \dots &lt; i_{k} &lt; n} f_{I} e_{i_{1}} \dots e_{i_{k}} + \sum_{j_{1} &lt; \dots &lt; j_{k-1} &lt; n} g_{J} e_{j_{1}} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_{n}.
$$

Then the condition $i^{*} \varphi = 0$ implies that $f_{I}|_{\partial M} = 0$ and hence that $e_{i} f_{I}|_{\partial M} = 0$ for $i = 1, \ldots, (n-1)$ and all $i_{1} &lt; \ldots &lt; i_{k} &lt; n$.

Therefore at $x$ we find that

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &amp;= D \varphi = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_{i} V_{e_{i}} \left( \sum_{i_{1} &lt; \dots &lt; i_{k} &lt; n} f_{I} e_{i_{1}} \dots e_{k} + \sum_{j_{1} &lt; \dots &lt; j_{k-1} &lt; n} g_{J} e_{j_{1}} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_{n} \right) \\
&amp;= \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_{i} \left\{ \sum_{I} \left[ (e_{i} f_{I}) e_{i_{1}} \dots e_{i_{k}} + f_{I} V_{e_{i}} (e_{i_{1}} \dots e_{i_{k}}) \right] \right. \\
&amp;\quad \left. + V_{e_{i}} \left( \sum_{J} g_{J} e_{j_{1}} \dots e_{j_{k-1}}) e_{n} + \left( \sum_{J} g_{J} e_{j_{1}} \dots e_{j_{k-1}}) V_{e_{i}} e_{n} \right) \right. \\
&amp;= (-1)^{k} \sum_{I} (e_{n} f_{I}) e_{i_{1}} \dots e_{i_{k}} e_{n} + \sum_{J} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (e_{i} g_{J}) e_{i} e_{j_{1}} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

p
-convex Riemannian manifolds

$$
\begin{array}{l}
+ (-1)^k \sum_J (e_n g_J) e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} + \sum_J g_J \left[ \sum_{i=1}^n e_i V_{e_i} (e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}}) e_n \right] \\
- \sum_J g_J \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \lambda_i e_i e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_i \right). \tag{*}
\end{array}
$$

Note that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{i=1}^n e_i V_{e_i} (e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}}) e_n = (-1)^{k-1} (\lambda_{j_1} + \dots + \lambda_{j_{k-1}}) e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} \\
\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e_i e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_i (-1)^{k-1} (\lambda_{j_1} + \dots + \lambda_{j_{k-1}}) e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} \\
+ (-1)^k \left( \sum_{\substack{i \notin J \\ i \neq n}} \lambda_i \right) e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}}.
\end{array}
$$

Then we see that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
0 = \text{the coefficient of } e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} \text{ in } (*) \\
= (-1)^k \left[ N g_J - \left( \sum_{\substack{i \notin J \\ i \neq n}} \lambda_i \right) g_J \right].
\end{array}
$$

Consequently we find that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\langle V_N \varphi, \varphi \rangle = \langle \sum_I (N f_I) e_{i_1} \dots e_{i_k} + \sum_J (N g_J) e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_n, \\
\sum_I f_I e_{i_1} \dots e_{i_k} + \sum_J g_J e_{j_1} \dots e_{j_{k-1}} e_n \rangle = \sum_J (N g_J) g_J \\
= \sum_J \left( \sum_{\substack{i \notin J \\ i \neq n}} \lambda_i \right) g_J^2 \geq 0.
\end{array}
$$

This completes our proof.

# The unitary dual of $\mathbf{GL}(n)$ over an archimedean field

David A.
Vogan, Jr.★

Department of Mathematics, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA

# 1. Introduction

Let $\mathbb{F}$ denote one of the three fields $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{C}$, or $\mathbb{H}$.
Write

$$
G = \operatorname {G L} (n, \mathbb {F}) \tag {1.1}
$$

for the group of invertible $n$ by $n$ matrices with entries in $\mathbb{F}$.
We determine explicitly the set $\hat{G}_u$ of equivalence classes of irreducible unitary representations of $G$.
For $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{C}$, the answer has been expected for thirty years: each element of $\hat{G}_u$ is unitarily induced from (one dimensional) unitary characters and certain very simple complementary series.
(A precise statement is in Theorem 6.18. Gelfand and Naimark in [14] overlooked some of the complementary series.
This was pointed out by Stein in [31], and that paper may have created the impression that Gelfand and Naimark missed a great many representations.
However, our result shows that Stein actually found everything that they missed.) For $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{H}$, it is still true that the "building blocks" for constructing all unitary representations are one dimensional unitary characters, and the analogues of Stein's complementary series.
However, one must use not only ordinary induction, but also Zuckerman's derived functors [11, 36].
This does not seem to bode well for a generalization to non-archimedean $\mathbb{F}$, where no analogue of Zuckerman's functors is known.
However, one can view them as implementing certain very special cases of Langlands functoriality; from this point of view, the results make some conjectural sense for any division algebra $\mathbb{F}$ over a local field.
(They may however be too naive, at least in residual characteristic less than or equal to $n$.
Since this work was completed, $M$.
Tadic in [32] has announced a classification of the unitary representations of $\mathrm{GL}(n,\mathbb{F})$ for commutative $p$-adic $\mathbb{F}$.
His results describe everything else in terms of the discrete series, and so avoid such pitfalls.)

If one wishes to use only unitary induction, the set of building blocks must be enlarged when $\mathbb{F}$ is $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{H}$.
Suppose first that $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{R}$.
Then whenever $n$ is even, Speh [29] has described a family of unitary representations of $\mathrm{GL}(n,\mathbb{R})$

★ Sloan Fellow.
Supported in part by NSF grant MCS-8202127

D.A.
Vogan, Jr.
450

parametrized by

$$
(\mathrm{IN} - (0)) \times \mathrm{IR}.
$$

(The last factor is just the central character.) For $n = 2$, they are the discrete series; for $n &gt; 2$, they are not tempered.
When $n$ is a multiple of 4, there are complementary series attached to the series induced from a product of two copies of one of Speh's representations on $\mathrm{GL}(n/2, \mathbb{R}) \times \mathrm{GL}(n/2, \mathbb{R})$.

Next, suppose $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{H}$.
In this case there is a family of unitary representations parametrized by $(\mathrm{IN} - (0)) \times \mathbb{R}$ for every $n$.
When $n$ is 1, it is the discrete series of $\mathrm{GL}(1, \mathbb{H})$.
(Recall that this group is isomorphic to $SU(2) \times \mathbb{R}$.) For even $n$, there are complementary series attached to these representations on $\mathrm{GL}(n/2, \mathbb{H}) \times \mathrm{GL}(n/2, \mathbb{H})$.
Using these extra building blocks, one can obtain all unitary representations by unitary induction.

For other real reductive groups, one expects the unipotent representations to complicate the picture substantially [1, 2].
A representation of $\mathrm{GL}(n,\mathbb{F})$ is special unipotent if and only if it is of the form

$$
\operatorname{Ind}(P \uparrow G)(\chi).
$$

Here $\chi$ is a character of $P$ which is trivial on the identity component of $P$.
(Non-special unipotent representations have yet to be defined.) This paper may therefore (with a little twisted logic) be regarded as evidence that most of the difficulties in treating general reductive groups involve unipotent representations.
At any rate, it is intended as such evidence.

Here is an incomplete outline of some previous results about this problem.
When $n = 2$, $\mathrm{GL}(n, \mathbb{F})$ is (up to center) locally isomorphic to $\mathrm{SO}(d + 1, 1)$, with $d$ equal to the dimension of $\mathbb{F}$ over $\mathbb{R}$.
The unitary duals were determined by Bargmann [3] for $\mathbb{R}$, Gelfand-Naimark [13] for $\mathbb{C}$, and Hirai [18] for $\mathbb{H}$.
For $n = 3$, the case of $\mathbb{C}$ was treated by Tsuchikawa [33], and $\mathbb{R}$ by Vakhutinski [34].
The case of $\mathbb{H}$ is a little easier than $\mathbb{R}$, but I know of no published treatment of it for $n \geq 3$.
For $n = 4$, $\mathbb{R}$ was treated by Speh [30].
For $n = 4$ and 5, $\mathbb{C}$ was treated by Duflo [9].
Partial results are too numerous to discuss completely, but those of Enright [10] are among the most powerful.
In unpublished joint work, Enright and Parthasarathy determined completely the spherical unitary representations of $\mathrm{GL}(n, \mathbb{C})$ with regular infinitesimal character.
The Yale dissertation of S.
Sahi determines the spherical unitary representations which are induced from a character (which, by the results of this paper, is all of them).
The work of Guillemonat [15] is of a similar nature.

To understand the organization of the paper, keep in mind that we have four tasks: to produce a list of representations (construction); to prove that they are all unitary (unitarity); to prove that they are all irreducible (irreducibility); and to prove that any representation not on the list is not unitary (exhaustion).
Crudely put, the main idea is to reduce matters to the case of spherical representations (those which have a vector fixed by a maximal compact subgroup).
Each task therefore has a "spherical" part, and a "reduction" part.

Even to state the result requires carrying out the construction step.
The "spherical construction" (that is, construction of all spherical unitary representations) contains no surprises.
We recall the least familiar aspect of it (Stein's

The unitary dual of
\mathrm{GL}(n)
over an archimedean field

complementary series) in Sect.
2, and complete it in Sect.
3. The reduction part of the construction (in which general unitary representations are constructed from spherical ones) is parametrized by $\hat{K}$ , via the theory of lowest $K$ -types.
$\hat{K}$ is discussed in general terms in Sect.
4, and computed very explicitly for each of the fields in question in Sect.
5. Section 6 completes the reduction construction, and states the main theorem (Theorem 6.18).

The exhaustion argument depends on some complicated but formal properties of $\hat{K}$ , which are discussed in Sect.
7 through 9. With these in hand, we can actually carry out the reduction part of exhaustion; this is done in Sect.
10.

The spherical case (exhaustion, irreducibility, and unitarity) is treated next, with generalities in Sect.
11, and a detailed discussion of each field in Sect.
12 through 14. This is certainly the heart of the paper; the reader wishing to find more than bells and whistles must look for it here.
Sections 15 and 16 are devoted to the reduction arguments for unitarity and irreducibility, respectively.
Section 17 discusses alternative constructions of the representations.

It is a pleasure to thank a few of the mathematicians who have helped in this work.
Birgit Speh taught me the foundations of the subject as she was helping to build them.
Over $\mathbb{C}$ , the critical Proposition 12.2 was suggested by Thomas Enright; I have benefitted from many conversations with him.
The fundamental idea of controlling the signature of a Hermitian form on very special $K$ -types I learned from Enright (although it could be attributed to many people).
I have come to understand it better through its appearance in the thesis of Susana Salamanca, and in conversations with her.
Most importantly, the entire treatment of the complex case here is an extension of unpublished joint work with Dan Barbasch (including the proof of Proposition 12.2); this made everything else possible.

# 2. Stein's complementary series

We continue to write $\mathbb{IF}$ for $\mathbb{IR},\mathbb{C}$ , or $\mathbb{IH}$ .
(For most of the next two sections, it would suffice to require $\mathbb{IF}$ to be a finite dimensional division algebra over a local field.) Assume $n = 2m$ , with $m$ a positive integer.
Write

$$
P = L N = \left\{\left( \begin{array}{l l} A &amp; B \\ 0 &amp; D \end{array} \right) \mid A, D \in \operatorname {G L} (m, \mathbb {I F}), B \in M (m, \mathbb {I F}) \right\} \tag {2.1}
$$

for the indicated maximal parabolic subgroup of $G = \mathrm{GL}(2m, \mathbb{F})$ .
(Here $M(m, \mathbb{F})$ denotes the algebra of all $m$ by $m$ matrices over $\mathbb{F}$ .) The indicated (Levi) decomposition of $P$ is as a semidirect product, with $N$ normal.
More precisely,

$$
L \cong \operatorname {G L} (m, \mathbb {I F}) \times \operatorname {G L} (m, \mathbb {I F}), \tag {2.1}
$$

realized as block diagonal matrices, and

$$
N \cong M (m, \mathbb {I F}) \tag {2.1}
$$

is the unipotent radical of $P$ .

The representations to be constructed are induced from certain characters of $P$ .
To describe these characters, first write

$$
\delta_ {m} \colon \operatorname {G L} (m, \mathbb {I F}) \rightarrow (\mathbb {I R} ^ {\times}) _ {+} \tag {2.2}
$$

D.A.
Vogan, Jr.

for the modular function: $\delta_{m}(g)$ is the Jacobian of the change of variable $v \to g.v$ on $\mathbb{F}^m$, with respect to Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{F}^m$.
(If $\mathbb{F}$ has dimension $d$ over $\mathbb{R}$, then $\mathbb{F}^m$ may be identified with $\mathbb{R}^{dm}$.
In that case, $\delta_{m}(g)$ is the determinant of the $dm$ by $dm$ real matrix representing $g$.
If $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{C}$, then

$$
\delta_{m}(g) = |\det_{\mathbb{C}}(g)|^{2}.
$$

**Definition 2.3.** Fix a one-dimensional unitary character $j$ of $\mathrm{GL}(m,\mathbb{F})$, and a complex number $t$.
Let $P = LN$ be the group defined in (2.1).
Consider the (possibly non-unitary) character

$$
\phi_{2m}(j, t) \colon P \to \mathbb{C}^{x},
$$

defined by

$$
\left[ \phi_{2m}(j, t) \right] ((g, h) n) = [j(gh)] \left[ \delta_{m}(gh^{-1}) \right]^{t}
$$

(for $g$ and $h$ in $\mathrm{GL}(m,\mathbb{F})$, and $n$ in $N$).
Put

$$
\sigma_{2m}(j, t) = \operatorname{Ind}\left(P \uparrow G\right) \left(\phi_{2m}(j, t)\right).
$$

The characters of $\mathrm{GL}(m,\mathbb{F})$ may be identified in a natural way with the characters of $\mathrm{GL}(1,\mathbb{F})$; so $j$ extends to a character (still denoted $j$) of $\mathrm{GL}(2m,\mathbb{F})$.
Clearly

$$
\sigma_{2m}(j, t) \cong \sigma_{2m}(1, t) \otimes j,
$$

so we could consider only the case $j = 1$ for the study of these representations.

**Lemma 2.4.** (cf.
Stein [31]).
In the setting of Definition 2.3,

a) $\sigma_{2m}(j,t)$ is unitary and irreducible for $t$ in $i\mathbb{R}$.
b) There is a nondegenerate Hermitian pairing between $\sigma_{2m}(j,t)$ and $\sigma_{2m}(j,-\bar{t})$.
c) $\sigma_{2m}(j,t)$ and $\sigma_{2m}(j, -t)$ have the same distribution character.
d) When $t$ is $\frac{1}{2}$, $\sigma_{2m}(j,t)$ is reducible.
It contains as a subquotient a representation $\sigma'$ induced from a one-dimensional unitary character of the parabolic subgroup with Levi factor $\mathrm{GL}(m+1,\mathbb{F}) \times \mathrm{GL}(m-1,\mathbb{F})$.
e) $\sigma_{2m}(j,t)$ is irreducible for $|t| &lt; \frac{1}{2}$.

**Proof.** By the remark before the lemma, we may assume that $j$ is trivial.
Irreducibility follows by investigating the restriction of $\sigma_{2m}(1,t)$ to $P$ (see [31]).
Another proof is in Sect.
10 through 14 (particularly Propositions 12.2, 13.4, and 14.2). For (b), let $K$ be a maximal compact subgroup of $G$.
Then we can realize the non-unitarily induced representations as functions in $L^2(K)$, satisfying certain transformation properties under $K \cap P$.
The same calculation proving that $\sigma_{2m}(1,t)$ is unitary for $t$ in $i\mathbb{R}$, shows that the inner product on $L^2(K)$ gives the pairing needed in (b).
For (c), one computes the character as usual.

For (d), embed $\sigma = \sigma(1, \frac{1}{2})$ in a principal series representation $I$ (as can easily be done explicitly – cf.
(11.12)).
Then by inspection, $\sigma'$ occurs in a principal series representation $I'$ with the same character.
Since $I, I'$, $\sigma$, and $\sigma'$ all have unique $K$-fixed vectors, and $\sigma'$ is irreducible (Sect.
11–14 again), it follows that $\sigma'$ is a subquotient of $\sigma$.
That $\sigma'$ is not equal to $\sigma$ follows by inspection of characters.

The validity of (e) with some positive constant $\varepsilon$ in place of $\frac{1}{2}$ follows from the irreducibility of $\sigma_{2m}(1,0)$ (cf.
(a)).
That $\varepsilon$ is at most $\frac{1}{2}$ follows from (d).
For $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{C}$, we will prove the irreducibility of $\sigma_{2m}(1,t)$ for $|t| &lt; \frac{1}{2}$ in Sect.
11, by general

The unitary dual of
\mathrm{GL}(n)
over an archimedean field

nonsense about intertwining operators and the Langlands classification; this can be carried over to any commutative local field $\mathbb{F}$ .
For $\mathbb{H}$ , the argument is more subtle (Sect.
14), and $I$ do not know how to do it for general division algebras.
(For $\mathbb{H}$ , the general nonsense argument gives $\varepsilon \geq \frac{1}{4}$ .) Q.E.D.

Proposition 2.5 (cf.
Stein [31]).
For all real $t$ such that $|t| &lt; \frac{1}{2}$ , and all unitary characters $j$ of $\mathrm{GL}(m, \mathbb{F})$ , the representation $\sigma_{2m}(j, t)$ is infinitesimally equivalent to a unitary representation.

Proof.
We apply Lemma 2.4 and a standard deformation argument.
Lemma 2.4(b) and (c) provide a Hermitian form on each of the representations in question.
The form is non-degenerate for $|t| &lt; \frac{1}{2}$ by Lemma 2.4(e), and positive definite for $t = 0$ by Lemma 2.4(a). It is known (and not hard to prove) that the forms may be chosen to depend continuously on $t$ .
They are therefore positive definite in the whole interval.
Q.E.D.

Definition 2.6. The Stein complementary series of $\mathrm{GL}(2\mathrm{m},\mathbb{F})$ consists of the representations $\sigma_{2m}(j,t)$ , for $0 &lt; t &lt; \frac{1}{2}$ .

Notice that these representations are induced (but not unitarily) from one-dimensional representations.

# 3. The almost spherical principal series

Fix a unitary character

(3.1)(a) $j_{1}:\mathbb{F}^{\times}\to \mathbb{C}^{\times};$

as in the remark after Definition 2.3, this corresponds naturally to a family of characters

(3.1)(b) $j_{m}\colon \mathrm{GL}(m,\mathbb{F})\to \mathbb{C}^{\times},$

characterized by the property that for $m \leq m'$ ,

(3.1)(c) $j_{m^{\prime}}|_{\mathrm{GL}(m,\mathbb{F})} = j_{m}.$

We refer to the collection $(j_m)$ loosely as $j$ .
Define

(3.2) $K(m,\mathbb{F}) =$ standard maximal compact subgroup of $\operatorname {GL}(m,\mathbb{F})$

We will discuss these groups in some detail in Sect.
