# Transcendence and Drinfeld modules

Jing Yu\*

Institute of Mathematics, Academic Sinica, Taipei, Taiwan, ROC

## § 1. Introduction

Notations

$\mathbf{F}_q$ the finite field with $q$ elements, $q$ a power of $p$

$\mathcal{C}$ smooth projective geometrically irreducible curve over $\mathbf{F}_q$

$\infty$ a closed point of $\mathcal{C}$ with degree $d_{\infty}$

$k$ the function field $k(\mathcal{C})$ of $\mathcal{C}$ over $\mathbf{F}_q$

$A$ the ring of functions in $k$ regular on $\mathcal{C} - \{\infty\}$

$k_{\infty}$ the completion of $k$ at $\infty$

$\bar{k}_{\infty}$ the algebraic closure of $k_{\infty}$

It has been ten years since V.G.
Drinfeld introduced in [3] the concept of elliptic $A$-module which we now call Drinfeld $A$-module.
These modules are analogues of elliptic curves for global function field $k$ and we have witnessed a beautiful algebraic theory gradually taking shape.
In [13], we have asked a few natural transcendence questions (conjectures) concerning these modules, having in mind the analogue of the classical work of Siegel-Schneider about elliptic integrals, and based on evidences provided by the old papers of Carlitz and Wade (cf.
[1, 11]).
Here in this article we will prove those conjectures.
We will show that indeed there is an interesting transcendence theory for each algebraic curve over a finite field.

Following Drinfeld, we let $M$ be a lattice in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$, i.e. a finitely generated discrete $A$-module in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ and form

$$
e_{M}(z) = z \prod_{\substack{\omega \in M \\ \omega \neq 0}} \left(1 - \frac{z}{\omega}\right).
$$

This is like the Weierstrass functions or the exponential function.
If the lattice $M$ is of (projective) rank $d$, then the Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_{M}$ associated to $M$ is a ring homomorphism from $A$ into the endomorphism ring of the additive

\* This work was partially supported by NSC, ROC

508
J.
Yu

group $\mathbf{G}_a(\bar{k}_{\infty})$ which has the property that for each $b \in A$ there exist $b_1, \ldots, b_i$ in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ such that

$$
\phi_{M}(b) = b F^{0} + b_{1} F^{1} + \dots b_{i} F^{i}
$$

where $b_{i} \neq 0$ with $i$ equals $d$ times the order of pole of $b$ at $\infty$.
Here $F = F^{1}$ is the generator (Frobenius) of the endomorphism ring of $\mathbf{G}_a$.
If $\phi_M$ is defined over the algebraic closure $\bar{k}$, i.e. all the coefficients of the polynomials $\phi_M(b)$ lie in $\bar{k}$, then all the Taylor coefficients of the entire function $e_M(z)$ lie in $\bar{k}$.
If this is the case we will prove that any non-zero period $\omega$ in the lattice $M$ is transcendental over $k$.
We will show moreover that the function $e_M(\alpha)$ enjoys the property that $e_M(\alpha)$ is transcendental over $k$ for all algebraic $\alpha \neq 0$ in $\bar{k}$.

This result will be deduced from a main theorem about what we call $E_{q}$-functions.
All the functions of Drinfeld with algebraic coefficients are $E_{q}$-functions.
These functions behave like classical $E$-functions satisfying algebraic differential equations.
Our main theorem can be viewed as an analogue of the well-known theorem of Scheider-Lang (cf.
[10, 12]).

**Theorem 4.1.** Let $K/k$ be a finite extension.
Let $f_1, f_2$ be $E_q$-functions relative to $K$ which are algebraically independent over $\bar{k}$.
Then there are only finitely many points $z$ at which $f_1, f_2$ simultaneously assume values in $K$.

Exactly as in the classical situation, one can deduce a sequence of transcendence results from the theorem above.
Among it, the analogue of the Gelfond-Schneider theorem (Hilbert's seventh problem), the transcendence of $j$-values at non-quadratic algebraic point in the "upper-half plane" of Drinfeld, the transcendence of ratios of periods in case the lattice $M$ is without "complex multiplication" and also the transcendence of ratios of periods belonging to lattices of different rank.

The transcendence theory here is certainly non-archimedean and lives in fields of characteristic $\neq 0$.
Nevertheless it is rich and alive!

## § 2. Preliminaries

We denote by $d(a)$ the additive valuation of $a \in k$ which equals $d_{\infty}$ times the order of pole of $a$ at $\infty$.
As usual we extend this valuation to $\bar{k}_{\infty}$.
If $\alpha \in \bar{k} \subset \bar{k}_{\infty}$, the maximum of the valuations of all the conjugates of $\alpha$ is said to be the size of $\alpha$, notes by $\mathcal{X}$.
We define the denominator of $\alpha \in \bar{k}$, den $\alpha$, to be any non-zero element $a$ in $A$ which is of minimal valuation among those having the property that $a\alpha$ is integral over $A$.

Elements in $\bar{k}$ which are integral over $A$ will be called algebraic integers.
We are interested in solving systems of homogeneous linear equations with algebraic integral coefficients.

**Lemma 2.1** (Wade, cf.
[14], Appendix).
Let $K/k$ be a finite extension.
Let $m, n$ be integers with $0 &lt; m &lt; n$ and let

$$
\alpha_{ji} \in K, \quad 1 \leq j \leq m, \; 1 \leq i \leq n
$$

Transcendence and Drinfeld modules

be algebraic integers in $K$ with size at most $B$.
Then there exist algebraic integers $x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}$, not all 0, satisfying

$$
\sum_{i = 1}^{n} \alpha_{ji} x_{i} = 0, \quad j = 1, \dots , m
$$

and

$$
\overline{x_i} &lt; \frac{c n + B m}{n - m}, \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots , n.
$$

Here $C$ denotes a constant depending only on the field $K$.

Proof.
Let $l$ be the inseparable degree of $K$ over $k$.
We consider first the system

$$
\sum_{i = 1}^{n} \alpha_{ji}^{p^l} t_i = 0, \quad j = 1, \dots , m.
$$

Since the coefficients now are in the separable closure of $k$ in $K$, it is standard to solve the system with algebraic integral solutions $y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}$, not all zero, in the separable closure of $k$ in $K$, such that the following hold

$$
\overline{y_i} &lt; \frac{c n + p^l B m}{n - m}.
$$

We let $x_{i} =$ the $p^l$-th root of $y_{i}$.
q.e.d.

Let $f(z)$ be an entire function on the completion of $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ say

$$
f(z) = \sum_{h = 0}^{\infty} b_h z^h, \quad b_n \in \bar{k}_{\infty}.
$$

We define for rational numbers $r$ the following growth function

$$
\mathbf{M}_r(f) = \max_{h \geq 0} (d(b_h) + h r).
$$

The maximum modulus principle in non-archimedean function theory tells us that

$$
\mathbf{M}_r(f) = \sup_{d(z) \leq r} d(f(z)) = \sup_{d(z) = r} d(f(z)).
$$

A corollary is the following lemma, cf.
[14].

Lemma 2.2. Let $R &gt; r$ be rational numbers and $f$ an entire non-zero function.
Then $\mathbf{M}_r(f) \leq \mathbf{M}_R(f) - v_r(R - r)$ where $v_r$ is the number of zeros (counting multiplicities) of $f$ in the disc $d(z) \leq r$.

The order of an entire function, cf.
[14], in characteristic $p$, is defined to be the smallest real number $\rho$ such that for given $\varepsilon &gt; 0$, $\mathbf{M}_r(f) \leq p^{(\rho + \varepsilon)r}$ as long as $r$ is sufficiently large.

Let $K$ be a finite extension field of $k$.
We say that an additive entire function $f$ of finite order is an $E_q$-function with respect to $K$ if we can write $f(z)$ as

$$
f(z) = \sum_{h = 0}^{\infty} b_h z^{q^h}, \quad b_h \in K \text{ with } \overline{b_h} \leqslant 1
$$

510
J.
Yu

and moreover there exists a sequence $a_{h}$ in $A$ such that the following hold

(i) $d(a_{h}) \leqslant h q^{h}$.
(ii) For all $j \leq h$, $a_h b_j$ are algebraic integers in $K$.
(ii) If $q^{h_1} + \ldots + q^{h_s} &lt; q^N$, then $a_{h_1} \ldots a_{h_s} |a_N$.

An important property of these $E_q$-functions is that if $f(z_0)$ is algebraic then all the coefficients of the Taylor expansion of $f(z)$ at $z_0$ are also algebraic.
In fact we have

$$
f(z) = f(z_0) + \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} b_h (z - z_0)^{q^h} = \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} b_h z^{q^h}.
$$

This trivial identity makes the $E_q$-functions behave like classical functions satisfying algebraic differential equations.

Another crucial observation is: In dealing with polynomials in $E_q$-functions, the conditions (i), (ii), (iii) on the sequence $a_h$ provide us with very good estimates of the denominators of Taylor coefficients.

We note finally that if $f(z)$ is an $E_q$-function with respect to $K$ and $\alpha \in K$ then $f(\alpha z)$ is also an $E_q$-function with respect to $K$.

## §3.

Background about Drinfeld modules

We summarize here a few things about Drinfeld modules which we need.
For more background materials, see [2], [3], and papers of D.
Goss e.g. [6].

Let $M$ be a lattice in $\overline{k}_{\infty}$.
This is a torsion-free finitely generated $A$-module, hence is projective and has a fixed rank.
Drinfeld introduced

$$
e_M(z) = z \prod_{\substack{\omega \in M \\ \omega \neq 0}} \left(1 - \frac{z}{\omega}\right).
$$

This function induces an $\mathbf{F}_q$-linear isomorphism from $\overline{k}_{\infty} / M$ onto $\overline{k}_{\infty}$.
One puts the $A$-module structure $*$ on $\overline{k}_{\infty}$ so that this isomorphism becomes an $A$-module isomorphism from $\overline{k}_{\infty} / M$ (with the obvious $A$-module structure).
This is where the Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_M$ comes in:

$$
a * e_M(z) = e_M(a z) = \phi_M(a) (e_M(z)).
$$

For each $a \in A$ we may write $\phi_M(a) = aF^0 + a_1F^1 + \ldots + a_iF^i$, with $i = dd(a)$ and $a_i \neq 0$, i.e. we have the following functional equation for $e_M(z)$

$$
e_M(a z) = a e_M(z) + a_1 e_M(z)^q + \ldots + a_i e_M(z)^{q^i}.
$$

Following Goss [7], we adopt the notations below for $a \in A$:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
[h](a) = a^{q^h} - a, \quad h \geqslant 1 \\
D_h(a) = [h](a) [h - 1]^q(a) \dots [1]^{q^{h-1}}(a) = [h](a) D_{h-1}^q(a) \\
D_0(a) = 1.
\end{array}
$$

Transcendence and Drinfeld modules

If $q^{N} &gt; q^{h_{1}} + \ldots + q^{h_{l}}$, we have the relation, cf.
[7] p.
354,

$$
D_{h_{1}}(a) D_{h_{2}}(a) \dots D_{h_{l}}(a) \mid D_{N}(a).
$$

We may fix non-constant $a$ and then expand $e_M(z)$ into Taylor series

$$
e_{M}(z) = \sum_{h = 0}^{\infty} \frac{B_{h}}{D_{h}(a)} z^{q^{h}}, \quad B_{h} \in A[a_{1}, \dots, a_{l}]
$$

where $B_{0} = 1$, $B_{1} = a_{1}$, $B_{2} = a_{1}^{q + 1} + a_{2}D_{1}^{q}(a), \ldots$,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
B_{h} = a_{1}(B_{h - 1})^{q} + a_{2}(B_{h - 2})^{q^{2}}[h - 1]^{q}(a) + \dots \\
+ a_{l}(B_{h - l})^{q^{l}}[h - 1]^{q}(a) \dots [h - l + 1]^{q^{l - 1}}(a).
\end{array}
$$

This expansion leads to the following essential lemma.

**Lemma 3.1.** Let $\phi_{M}$ be a Drinfeld module of rank $d$ defined over the ring of integers of $K \subset \bar{k}$, with corresponding lattice $M$.
Then the function $e_{M}(z)$ is an $E_{q}$-function with respect to $K$ of order $d \log q / \log p$.

**Proof.** Let $\sigma$ be any embedding of $K / k$ into $\bar{k}$.
We apply $\sigma$ to all the Taylor coefficients of $e_M(z)$.
What we obtain is an entire function which is the Drinfeld function corresponds to the Drinfeld module $\phi_M^\sigma (\phi_M^\sigma (a) = \phi_M(a)^\sigma$ for all $a\in A)$.
This implies that the size of all the coefficients of $e_M(z)$ are bounded.
To see that $e_M(z)$ is an $E_{q}$-function we just note

$$
d(D_{h}(a)) = h q^{h} d(a).
$$

By Theorem 3.2 in [14], $e_M(z)$ has the desired order.
q.e.d.

We say that two Drinfeld $A$-modules $\phi_{M_1}$ and $\phi_{M_2}$ defined over $K$ are isomorphic over $K$ if $M_1 = \alpha_0 M_2$ with $\alpha_0 \neq 0$ in $K$.
In that case $e_{M_1}(z) = \alpha_0 e_{M_2}(\alpha_0^{-1} z)$.

**Lemma 3.2.** Any Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_{M}$ defined over $K \subset \bar{k}$ is isomorphic to one that is defined over the ring of algebraic integers of $K$.

**Proof.** We write $A$ as a finitely generated $\mathbf{F}_q$-algebra with generators $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_s$ and let $d$ be the rank of $\phi_M$.
Then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\phi_{M}(\alpha_{j}) = \alpha_{j} F^{0} + a_{1}(j) F^{1} + \dots + a_{l_{j}}(j) F^{l_{j}}, \quad i_{j} = d d(\alpha_{j}) \\
\phi_{\alpha_{0}^{-1} M}(\alpha_{j}) = \alpha_{j} F^{0} + \alpha_{0}^{q - 1} a_{1}(j) F^{1} + \dots + \alpha_{0}^{q^{l} - 1} a_{l_{j}}(j) F^{l_{j}}.
\end{array}
$$

We just choose $\alpha_0 \neq 0$ in $A$ such that all the polynomials $\phi_{\alpha_0^{-1}M}(\alpha_j)$ in $K\{F\}$ have algebraic integral coefficients.
q.e.d.

The above lemmas imply the following

**Theorem 3.3.** Let $\phi_{M}$ be a Drinfeld $A$-module defined over $\bar{k}$.
Then the associated function $e_{M}(z)$ is an $E_{q}$-function with respect to some finite extension of $k$.

For a given lattice $M \subset \bar{k}_{\infty}$, the set of all elements $\lambda \in \bar{k}_{\infty}$ such that $e_M(\lambda z)$ and $e_M(z)$ are algebraically dependent functions form $a$ field $K_{M}$.
This field is called the field of multiplications of $M$ in [15].

512
J.
Yu

We let $R_{M} \subset \bar{k}_{\infty}$ be the ring consisting of all elements $\alpha$ in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ such that $\alpha M \subset M$.
By an order in a finite extension field $K / k$ we mean a subring of $K$ which contains $A$ and is a finitely generated $A$-module of rank $[K : k]$.
We have shown the following in [15].

**Lemma 3.4.** (i) The field $K_{M}$ is an “imaginary” algebraic extension of $k$ of degree $\leq d$, the rank of $K$.
Here imaginary means $K_{M} \cap k_{\infty} = k$.
(ii) The ring $R_{M}$ is an order in $K_{M}$.

**Lemma 3.6.** Let $M_1, M_2$ be two lattices in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ of different rank.
Then $e_{M_1}(z)$ and $e_{M_2}(z)$ are algebraically independent over $\bar{k}_{\infty}$.

We let $\operatorname{Log}_M(z)$ to denote the compositional inverse of the function $e_M(z)$.
We view this function as multiple-valued on the "plane" $\bar{k}_{\infty}$.
The "logarithm" $\operatorname{Log}_M(z_1)$ is defined to be any inverse image of $z_1$ under the mapping $e_M(z)$.

Finally we consider the case $\mathcal{C} = \mathbf{P}^1(\mathbf{F}_q)$ and $A = \mathbf{F}_q[T]$.
Any rank 2 Drinfeld $A$-module in this case can be described simply by $\phi_M(T)$

$$
\phi_{M}(T) = T F^{0} + g_{1} F^{1} + \Delta F^{2}, \quad \Delta \neq 0.
$$

The isomorphism classes of $\phi_M$ is determined completely by

$$
j = j(M) = g_{1}^{q + 1} / \Delta.
$$

On the other hand the homothetic classes of lattices correspond naturally to points in $\mathcal{H} = \bar{k}_{\infty} - k_{\infty}$.
The group $\mathrm{GL}_2(k_{\infty})$ acts on $\mathcal{H}$ via fractional linear transformations.
In [3], Drinfeld puts a rigid analytic structure on $\mathrm{GL}_2(A) \setminus \mathcal{H}$, so that the modular function $j$ gives a rigid analytic isomorphism

$$
\mathrm{GL}_{2}(A) \backslash \mathcal{H} \cup \{\infty\} \xrightarrow{j} \mathbf{P}^{1}(\bar{k}_{\infty}).
$$

We will study the transcendence of $j$-values in the last section.

## § 4. The main theorem

**Theorem 4.1.** Let $K / k$ be a finite extension.
Let $f_{1}, f_{2}$ be $E_{q}$-functions relative to $K$ which are algebraically independent over $\bar{k}$.
Then there are only finitely many points $z$ at which $f_{1}, f_{2}$, simultaneously assume values in $K$.

**Proof.** We suppose that the functions $f_{i}$, $i = 1,2$, satisfying

$$
\mathbf{M}_{R}(f_{i}) \leq q^{\rho R} \quad \text{for } R \geq R_{0},
$$

and

$$
f_{i}(z) = \sum_{h = 0}^{\infty} b_{hi} z^{q^{h}}.
$$

We let $a_{h1}, a_{h2}$ be 2 sequences in $A$ such that for $i = 1,2$

(i) $d(a_{hi}) \leq C_1 h q^h$.

(ii) For all $j \leq h$, $a_{hi} b_{ji}$ are integral.

(iii) $q^{h_1} + \ldots + q^{h_s} &lt; q^N \Rightarrow a_{h_1 i} \ldots a_{h_s i} |a_{Ni}$.

Transcendence and Drinfeld modules

Since the $E_{q}$-functions are $\mathbf{F}_q$-linear, the points where they simultaneously assume algebraic values form a vector space over $\mathbf{F}_q$.
We assume that this space has infinite dimension and arrive at a contradiction.
By assumption, we may now find a subspace of arbitrary dimension $m$, say $\{\omega_1,\dots ,\omega_{q^m}\}$.

We are going to construct an auxiliary function

$$
\begin{array}{l}
F _ {N} (z) = \sum_ {\lambda_ {1} = 0, \lambda_ {2} = 0} ^ {L (N)} \gamma (\lambda_ {1}, \lambda_ {2}) f _ {1} (z) ^ {\lambda_ {1}} f _ {2} (z) ^ {\lambda_ {2}} \\
= \sum_ {l = 0} ^ {\infty} u _ {t l} (z - \omega_ {t}) ^ {l}, \quad \text{for } t = 1, \dots , q ^ {m}
\end{array}
$$

where $L(N) = q^{\frac{1}{2}(TN + N)}$ and $T \geq 2\rho + 1$ is a fixed odd positive integer.

We require $N$ to be sufficiently large compared with $R_0, T, m$ and the constants $C_1, C_2$ (below).

The $u_{tl}$ are homogeneous linear forms in $\gamma(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$.
We demand that $u_{tl} = 0$ for $t = 1, \dots, q^m$ and $l = 0 \dots q^{TN}$.
All coefficients of this linear system lie in the finite extension $K$ of $k$.
We multiply each equation by the following factor:

$$
\left(\prod_ {i = 1} ^ {2} a _ {(T N) i}\right) \left(\prod_ {t = 1} ^ {q ^ {m}} \prod_ {i = 1} ^ {2} \operatorname {d e n} f _ {i} \left(\omega_ {t}\right)\right) ^ {L (N)}.
$$

The new system has integral coefficients with size bounded by

$$
2 C _ {1} T N q ^ {T N} + C _ {2} p ^ {\frac {1}{2} (T N + N)}.
$$

It follows from Lemma 2.1 that the $\gamma (\lambda_1,\lambda_2)$ can be chosen from algebraic integers not all zero with size at most

$$
3 C _ {1} N T q ^ {T N - N + m}.
$$

We therefore have the following estimate for $R\geq N$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathbf {M} _ {R} \left(F _ {N}\right) \leq 3 C _ {1} N T q ^ {T N - N + m} + 2 q ^ {\rho R + \frac {1}{2} (T N + N)} \\
\leq 2 q ^ {T R}.
\end{array}
$$

We let $S$ to be the largest integer such that $u_{tl} = 0$ for all $l$ up to $q^{TS}$.
Then $S \geq N$.
Moreover we have that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\overline {{u _ {t v}}} \leq 3 C _ {1} N T q ^ {T N - N + m} + C _ {2} q ^ {\frac {1}{2} (T N + N)}, \\
d (\operatorname {d e n} u _ {t v}) \leq 2 C _ {1} T (S + 1) q ^ {T (S + 1)} + C _ {2} q ^ {\frac {1}{2} (T N + N)},
\end{array}
$$

for all $\nu$ with $u_{tv} \neq 0$ and $q^{TS} &lt; \nu \leq q^{TS + T}$.

We now recall the following elementary inequality (cf.
[14], Lemma 2.1)

$$
d (x) \geq - \left(d _ {0} - 1\right) \bar {x} - d _ {0} d (\operatorname {d e n} x)
$$

where $x \neq 0$ is in $\bar{k}$ and $d_0$ denotes the separable degree of $x$ over $k$.
We let $x = u_{tv} \neq 0$, this inequality leads to a contradiction if $m$ is too large compared with $T$ (hence $\rho$), $C_1$, and the separable degree of $K$ over $k$.
All we have to do

514
J.
Yu

is to apply Lemma 2.2 to $R = S$, $r = S/2$.
Thus

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d(u_{tv}) \leq \mathbf{M}_r(F_N) \leq \mathbf{M}_R(F_N) - \frac{S}{2} q^{TS + m} \\
\leq 2 q^{TS} - \frac{S}{2} q^{TS + m} \\
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
d(u_{tv}) \geq -3 d_0 C_1 T(S + 1) q^{TS + T} \\
-3 (d_0 - 1) C_1 T S q^{TS - S + m}. \\
\end{array}
$$

As $N$ (hence $S$) goes to infinity we obtain

$$
q^m \leq 3 d_0 C_1 T q^T. \quad \text{q.e.d.}
$$

## §5.

Applications to Drinfeld modules

**Theorem 5.1.** Let $\phi_M$ be a Drinfeld $A$-module defined over $\bar{k}$ with corresponding lattice $M$.
Then $e_M(\alpha)$ is transcendental for all $\alpha \neq 0$ in $\bar{k} \subset \bar{k}_\infty$.
In particular, the period lattice $M$ is transcendental (i.e. $\omega$ is transcendental for all $\omega \neq 0$ in $M$).

**Proof.** Suppose $e_M(\alpha)$ is algebraic for $\alpha \neq 0$ in $\bar{k}$.
We consider $\phi_M$ as defined over a finite extension $K$ of $k$ which contains $e_M(\alpha)$.
Let $f_1(z) = e_M(z)$ be the first $E_q$-function and $f_2(z) = z$.
Then all the values $f_1(a\alpha), f_2(a\alpha), a \in A$, lie in $K$.
On the other hand, since $e_M(z)$ has a whole lattice of zeros, the functions $f_1$ and $f_2$ are algebraically independent.
This contradicts Theorem 4.1. q.e.d.

Let $H$ be the Hilbert classfield of $A$, i.e. the maximal abelian unramified extension of $k$ which is totally split ("totally real") at $\infty$.
In [9], Hayes shows that there always exists rank 1 Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_M$ which is defined over the ring $\mathcal{O}_H$ of algebraic integers in $H$.
One may write $M = I\pi_\infty$ with $I$ a fractional ideal of $A$.
Our Theorem 5.1 above then shows that this $\pi_\infty$ is transcendental over $k$.
This answers a question raised by D.
Goss in [8], p.
158.

By applying $\operatorname{Gal}(H/k)$ to $\phi_M$, one obtains Drinfeld $A$-modules defined over $\mathcal{O}_H$ whose lattices are $I_j\pi_\infty$, where $I_j$ runs through a complete set of representatives of the ideal classes of $A$.
Goss then deduced in [8] the following important result for any ideal of $A$:

$$
\pi_\infty^{-i} \sum_{\substack{n \in I \\ n \text{monic}}} \frac{1}{n^i} \in H \quad \text{if } i \equiv 0 \bmod (q - 1).
$$

Here one fixes a local uniformizer $w$ at $\infty$ and by "Monic" elements one means those $n$ in $A$ satisfying

$$
d(n w^{d(n)} - 1) &lt; 0.
$$

Thus the situation is completely analogous to that of the values of Riemann zeta functions at even integers.

Transcendence and Drinfeld modules

Theorem 5.2. Let $\phi_M$ be a Drinfeld $A$-module defined over $\bar{k}$, with corresponding lattice $M$.
If $\omega \neq 0 \in M$ and $\alpha \notin K_M$, the field of multiplications of $M$, then $e_M(\alpha \omega)$ is transcendental.

Proof.
Suppose $e_M(\alpha \omega)$ is algebraic.
We consider $\phi_M$ as defined over $K \ni e_M(\alpha \omega)$.
It follows that $f_1(z) = e_M(\alpha z)$ and $f_2(z) = e_M(z)$ are both $E_q$-functions with respect to $K$.
As $\alpha \notin K_M$ these two functions are algebraically independent, but for all $a \in A$, $f_1(a\omega)$ and $f_2(a\omega)$ are in $K$.
q.e.d.

Corollary 5.3. Let $\phi_M$ be a Drinfeld $A$-module defined over $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ with corresponding lattice $M$.
Suppose that $K_M = k$ then $\omega_1, \omega_2 \in M$ are linearly independent over $k$ if and only if they are linearly independent over $\bar{k}$.

For Drinfeld $A$-modules of different ranks we have

Theorem 5.4. Let $\phi_{M_1}$ and $\phi_{M_2}$ be Drinfeld $A$-modules of rank $d_1$ and $d_2$ respectively, with corresponding lattices $M_1$ and $M_2$.
Suppose that both these modules are defined over $\bar{k}$ and $d_1 \neq d_2$.
Then for any $\omega_1 \in M_1 - \{0\}$ and $\omega_2 \in M_2 - \{0\}$ we have that $\omega_1 / \omega_2$ is transcendental.

Proof.
If $\omega_{1} = \alpha \omega_{2}$, with $\alpha \in \bar{k}$, then $e_{M_1}(\alpha z)$ and $e_{M_2}(z)$ are $E_{q}$-functions algebraically independent over $\bar{k}_{\infty}$.
This contradicts Lemma 3.6. q.e.d.

We can also deduce an analogue of Gelfond-Schneider theorem (Hilbert's 7-th problem) for any curve $\mathcal{C}$ and Drinfeld modules $\phi_M$ of arbitrary rank defined over $\overline{k(\mathcal{C})} = \bar{k}$.

Theorem 5.5. Let $\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}$ be two non-zero elements in $\bar{k}$, for $j = 1, 2$, let $\operatorname{Log}_M \alpha_j$ be any determination of the "logarithm" of $\alpha_j$.
If $\operatorname{Log}_M \alpha_1$ and $\operatorname{Log}_M \alpha_2$ are linearly independent over $K_M$ then they are linearly independent over $\bar{k}$.

Proof.
Suppose $\operatorname{Log}_M \beta_2 = \beta \operatorname{Log}_M \alpha_1$ with $\beta \in \bar{k}$ but $\beta \notin K_M$.
Then the $E_q$-functions $e_M(z)$ and $e_M(\beta z)$ takes algebraic values simultaneously at $a \operatorname{Log}_M \alpha_1$, $a \in A$.
But $\beta \notin K_M$ so $f_1$ and $f_2$ should be algebraically independent.
This contradicts our Theorem 4.1. q.e.d.

Finally we specialize to the case $\mathcal{C} = \mathbf{P}^1(\mathbf{F}_q)$ with $A = \mathbf{F}_q[T]$.
We are interested particularly in Drinfeld $A$-modules of rank 2. We have the transcendence of $j$-values at non-quadratic algebraic points in the Drinfeld upper half plane.

Theorem 5.6. If $\alpha \in \bar{k} \cap (\bar{k}_{\infty} - k_{\infty})$ and is not quadratic over $k$, then $j(\alpha)$ is transcendental.

Proof.
Suppose $j(\alpha)$ is algebraic.
We choose a lattice $M$ such that

$$
\phi_M(T) = T F^0 + g_1 F^1 + F^2
$$

and

$$
j(\alpha) = j(M) = g_1.
$$

Thus the corresponding Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_M$ is defined over the field $k(j(\alpha))$.
Let $M = [\omega_1, \omega_2]$ so that $\alpha = \omega_1 / \omega_2$.
The functions $e_M(\alpha z)$ and $e_M(z)$ assume

516
J.
Yu

value 0 at $a\omega_{2}$, $a \in A$.
Since these are $E_{q}$-functions they must be algebraically dependent.
Therefore $\alpha \in K_{M}$ which is a quadratic extension of $k$.
q.e.d.

If $\alpha \notin k_{\infty}$ and is quadratic over $k$ then Gekeler [5] has shown that $j(\alpha)$ is an algebraic integer, i.e. an element in $\bar{k}$ which is integral over $A$.

## Appendix

Drinfeld modules corresponding to lattices in $\bar{k}$

There is an interesting phenomenon revealed by Theorem 5.6 which is common in all curves $\mathcal{C}$ and lattices of all ranks.

Let $K$ be a finite extension of $k$.
We say that $K$ is a $CM_{\infty}$ field over $k$ if there is only one place of $K$ lying above $\infty$.
We call a lattice $M \subset \bar{k}_{\infty}$ an ideal if it is an $\mathcal{O}$-ideal of an order $\mathcal{O}$ in a $CM_{\infty}$ field over $k$ contained in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$.

**Theorem 1.** Let $M$ be a lattice in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ homothetic to one contained in $\bar{k} \subset \bar{k}_{\infty}$.
If the corresponding Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_{M}$ is defined over $\bar{k}$ then $M$ is homothetic to an ideal.

**Proof.** Let $M = (I_1\alpha_1 + \ldots + I_{d-1}\alpha_{d-1} + I_d)\omega$ where $I_1, \ldots, I_d$ are ideals in $A$ and $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{d-1}$ all lie in $\bar{k}$.
The entire functions $e_M(\alpha_1z), \ldots, e_M(\alpha_{d-1}z)$ and $e_M(z)$ are all $E_q$-functions relative to a finite extension of $k$.
We apply Theorem 4.1 to $f_1(z) = e_M(z)$ and $f_2(z) = e_M(\alpha_iz)$.
It follows that all $\alpha_i, i = 1, \ldots, d-1$ must lie in the field of multiplications $K_M$.
This implies that $[K_M: k] = d$ and $M\omega^{-1}$ is an $R_M$-ideal in $K_M$.
The closure of $K_M$ in $\bar{k}_{\infty}$ equals to the $k_{\infty}$-vector space generated by $M\omega^{-1}$.
This space has dimension $d$ over $k_{\infty}$ because $M\omega^{-1}$ is discrete.
Hence $K_M$ is a $CM_{\infty}$ field over $k$.
This completes the proof because we have already shown in Lemma 3.4 that $R_M$ is an order in $K_M$.
q.e.d.

If $M_0$ is an $\mathcal{O}$-ideal of an order $\mathcal{O}$ in a $CM_{\infty}$ field $K$ over $k$, we contend that one can always find a lattice $M \subset \bar{k}_{\infty}$ homothetic to $M_0$ such that the Drinfeld $A$-module $\phi_M$ is defined over a finite extension of $k$.
This follows from Hayes's results.
Indeed, as D.
Hayes showed in [9], one can find a rank one $A_K$-module associated to the ideal $M_0$ which is defined over the Hilbert-class field $H_{\mathcal{O}}$ and which has "everywhere good reduction" if $d_{\infty} = 1$.
The restriction of this Drinfeld $A_K$-module to $A$ is what is needed.

Bertrand, D.
Goss and D.
Hayes, who read a first draft of this paper and offered useful suggestions.

# Conformal complete metrics with prescribed non-negative Gaussian curvature in $\mathbb{R}^2$

Patricio Aviles

Department of Mathematics, University of Illinois, 1409 West Green Street, Urbana, IL 61801, USA

## § 0. Introduction

Recently a great deal of attention has been given to the problem of existence of complete metrics with certain type of curvature prescribed in open manifolds.
(See work of Kazdan-Warner [K-W,2], Schoen-Yau [S-Y], Ni [N,1] Gromov-Lawson [G-L]).
Here we will consider the plane with its standard metric $(\mathbb{R}^2,\delta_{ij})$.
Let $k\colon \mathbb{R}^2\to \mathbb{R}$ be a Hölder continuous function.
Then if $k\leq 0$, $k\not\equiv 0$ and for $|x|$ sufficiently large $-k(x)\leq M|x|^{-b}$, $b &gt; 2$, $M &gt; 0$, $b$, $M$ constants, Ni[N,2] and later McOwen [M] (who gave a nice refinement of Ni's theorem) have proved that there are metrics $g_{ij}$ which are pointwise conformal to the standard metric having $k(x)$ as their curvature, that is if $g_{ij} = e^{2u}\delta_{ij}$, there are $u$'s such that

$$
\Delta u + k (x) e ^ {2 u} = 0 \quad \text {in} \ \mathbb {R} ^ {2} \tag {0}
$$

and moreover the associated metrics are complete.
The Eq.
(0) implies that the Gaussian curvature of $g_{ij}$ is $k(x)$.
If $-k(x)\geq M|x|^{-b}$, as $|x|\to \infty$, $M &gt; 0$, $0 &lt; b &lt; 2$, $M$, $b$ constants, Sattinger [Sa] observed earlier that (0) does not admit solution.

If $k(x)$ is positive in some region then the problem has completely different features, both from the analytic and from the geometric point of view.
Indeed, from the geometric point of view, Cohn-Vossen's formula [B] together with the uniformization theorem implies that a necessary condition for an open 2-manifold to admit a complete metric of positive Gaussian curvature is that the manifold must be homeomorphic to the plane.
Secondly, Bonnet's theorem as well as its generalizations [C] imposes obstructions to an open two manifold admitting a complete metric of positive Gaussian curvature.
Now from the analytic point of view, it is well known that if $k(x)$ is positive then the Eq.
(0) might not have solution, for instance, for compact two manifolds, this is indeed the case, c.f.
[K-W,1].
Therefore, (as in most geometric problems involving the different sign of some prescribed curvature) one needs different ideas to treat it.
It is the aim of this paper to study this case.
We shall prove

P.
Aviles

Theorem A.
Let $k(x)$ be a real valued function defined in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which is Hölder continuous, positive in some region and at infinity the following inequality holds

$$
|k(x)| \leq M |x|^{-b}, \quad b \geq 2, \quad M &gt; 0, b, M \text{ constants.} \tag{1}
$$

Then, there are complete as well as incomplete metrics, $g_{ij}$, which are conformally flat and that have $k(x)$ as their curvature that is if $g_{ij} = e^{2u}\delta_{ij}$, then $u$ satisfies (0).
Moreover, the metrics $g_{ij}$ have finite positive total curvature, $0 &lt; \int_{\mathbb{R}^2}k(x)dA &lt; \infty$, and $u$ has the asymptotic behavior

$$
\frac{(b - c)}{2} \ln |x| - \tilde{C} \leq u(x) \leq \frac{(b - c)}{2} \ln |x| + \tilde{C}. \tag{2}
$$

with $c$, $\tilde{C}$ positive constants, subject to

$$
\max(4, b) \leq c &lt; b + 4. \tag{3}
$$

($dA$ denotes the volume element with respect to $g_{ij}$.)

Under stronger hypotheses on the curvature we may construct complete metrics even when $0 &lt; b &lt; 2$.
Indeed,

Theorem A¹.
Let $k(x)$ be as in Theorem A and assume in addition that $k(x) \geq 0$, it has only finitely many zeros of finite order and

$$
0 &lt; C_1 = \lim_{x \to \infty} \inf k(x) |x|^b \leq \lim_{x \to \infty} \sup k(x) |x|^b = C_2 &lt; \infty, \quad b &gt; 0. \tag{4}
$$

Then, (0) has solutions with the asymptotic behavior (2) with the constant $c$ subject to

$$
\max(2, b) &lt; c &lt; b + \frac{4C_1}{C_2}. \tag{5}
$$

It should be observed that this theorem does not contradict Calabi's generalization of Bonnet's theorem [C], because the corresponding statement reads as follows: If

$$
k(x) \geq \frac{C(\varepsilon)}{(|x|^{(b - c)/2})^{2 - \varepsilon}} \quad 0 \leq \varepsilon &lt; 2 \quad \text{as } x \to \infty,
$$

then the metric $g_{ij}$ is incomplete.
However, this inequality is clearly false in this case.

In view of Theorem A and $\mathbf{A}^1$ it is natural to ask: Does every solution of (0) have the asymptotic behavior (2)? The main results are.

Theorem B.
Let $k \colon \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ be a Hölder continuous function and let us assume that

(I) $k(x)$ at infinity is negative, or
(II) $k(x)$ at infinity is positive.

Let us also suppose that

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) \, dx &gt; 0 \tag{6}
$$

Conformal complete metrics in
\mathbb{R}^2

and

$$
0 &lt; \lim_{x \rightarrow \infty} \inf_{k(x)} |k(x)| |x|^{b} \leq \lim_{x \rightarrow \infty} \sup_{k(x)} |k(x)| |x|^{b} &lt; \infty, \quad b &gt; 2. \tag{7}
$$

The following conclusions hold.

(a) If (I) holds then every solution of (0) has the asymptotic behavior (2) with the constant $c$ subject to

$$
c &gt; b \tag{8}
$$

(b) If (II) occurs and the metric $g_{ij} = e^{2u}\delta_{ij}$ has finite total curvature, i.e. $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2}k(x)e^{2u(x)}dx &lt; \infty$, then (2) and (8) hold.
In particular if $u$ is radially symmetric then $g_{ij}$ has finite total curvature and hence (2) and (8) hold.

It should be stressed that in Theorem B there is no assumption on the sign of $u$.
Also, that if (6) or (7) does not hold then the associated metrics, $g_{ij} = e^{2u}\delta_{ij}$ might spread over the plane in a different manner as the asymptotic behavior of $u$ indicates in the cases above.
We shall illustrate this fact at the end of Sect.
2.

From Theorem B we shall obtain the following intrinsic characterization of completeness.

Theorem C.
Let $g_{ij}$ be a metric pointwise conformal to the standard metric.
If the Gaussian curvature of this metric, $k(x)$, satisfies the properties (I) or (II), (6) and (7).
Then a necessary and sufficient condition for $g_{ij}$ being complete is

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2}k(x)\,dA \leq 2\pi. \tag{9}
$$

In other words, Cohn-Vossen's inequality is necessary and sufficient for completeness.

This paper is organized as follows.
The proof of Theorem A, which is simple is given in Sect.
4. The difficulties lie in proving Theorems $\mathbf{A}^1$ and B.
Section 1 contains a basis a priori estimate which is essential in most of this paper.
Also in Sect.
1 is the proof of Theorem $\mathbf{A}^1$ which is based on the Leray-Schauder fixed point theorem.
This approach was previously used by McOwen [M] in the case of negative curvature.
In this case, however, the a priori estimate needed to apply Leray-Schauder's theorem follows rather easily from the maximum principle.
Sections 2 and 3 contain the proofs of Theorems B and C respectively.

We would like to point out that we have denoted with capital letters (usually $C$, $C_1$, $C_2$) constants depending on prescribed arguments whose values are unimportant and we use small letters (usually $c$, $b$, $l$, $p$, $\delta$) for constants whose values are important.

§ 1. A-Priori estimate and proof of Theorem A¹

We shall first recall some facts about weighted Sobolev spaces ([Ca], [M]).
$M_{s,\delta}^{p}$ is the completion of $C_0^\infty(\mathbb{R}^2)$ in the form

P.
Aviles

$$
\sum_{|\alpha| \leq s} \| (1 + |x|^2)^{(\delta + |\alpha|)/2} D^\alpha \phi \|_{L^p},
$$

$1 &lt; p &lt; \infty, s$ is a non-negative integer, $\delta \in \mathbb{R}, \phi \in C_0^\infty(\mathbb{R}^2)$.
Then we have

Theorem 0. (a) $M_{s',\delta'}^p \subset M_{s,\delta}^p$ if $s' \geq s$, $\delta' \geq \delta$.
If $s' &gt; s$, $\delta' &gt; \delta$ then the inclusion is compact.

(b) $M_{s,\delta}^p \subset C_0(\mathbb{R}^2)$ (continuous function vanishing at infinity) if $s &gt; 2/p$ and $\delta &gt; -2/p$.

(c) If $-2/p &lt; \delta &lt; -2/p + 1$ then the Laplacian, $\Delta$, $\Delta$: $M_{2,\delta}^p \to M_{0,\delta+2}^p$ is an injection with closed range $R = \{f \in M_{0,\delta+2}^p : \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} f = 0\}$, and $\|u\|_{M_{2,\delta}^p} \leq C \|\Delta u\|_{M_{0,\delta+2}^p}$ with $C$ independent of $u$.

Before going into the technical aspects of Theorem A$^1$ we shall briefly explain the ideas involved.
As mentioned in the introduction, it follows from Cohn-Vossen's formula that a necessary condition for the existence of a complete metric, $g_{ij} = e^{2v(x)}\delta_{ij}$, of positive curvature, $k(x)$, is

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2v(x)} dx \leq 2\pi. \tag{C-V}
$$

In Theorem A$^1$, we prove the converse.
That is, if (C-V) holds and the curvature $k(x)$ satisfies some natural decay assumptions, then there exists a solution $v$ of (0) for which the metric $g_{ij} = e^{2v}\delta_{ij}$ is complete.

Because of the Leray-Schauder fixed point theorem (see proof of Theorem A$^1$), it suffices to show that if (C-V) holds and $v$ satisfies (0) (with the decay assumptions on $k(x)$), then the a-priori estimate

$$
k(x) e^{2v(x)} \leq C, \quad x \in \mathbb{R}^2
$$

holds, where $C &gt; 0$ is a constant which depends only on known quantities.
This is accomplished in Lemma A.1.

The ideas on Lemma A.1 might be summarized as follows.
As a general principle we might say that in order to obtain useful information on $v$ we look for a differential inequality which relates a solution of (0) with its "level sets".
This idea has been successfully executed by several authors.
See for example [O] and [U] (proof of Theorem 4.8).

Since the asymptotic behavior of a solution $v$ of (0), is a-priori unknown we work in a bounded domain.
We consider a ball $B \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$.
But the boundary values of $v$ are unknown, hence it is natural to work with $u = v - w$, where $\Delta w = 0$, $w \mid \rho_B = v$.
Clearly, $u$ satisfies

$$
\Delta u + k(x) e^{2w} e^{2u} = 0, \quad u \mid \rho_B = 0. \tag{E}
$$

Because of the term $k(x)e^{2w(x)}$, it is rather difficult to reach any conclusion from (E).
We eliminate this difficulty by introducing a conformal mapping, $f(z)$, from the ball $B$ into a suitable Riemann surface $\mathfrak{R}$, such that $|f'(z)|^2 = \bar{k}(x)e^{-2w}$, and such that $(k(x)/\bar{k}(x))$ is bounded from below and above by suitable constants.
We then consider $\bar{u} = u \circ f$, which satisfies

$$
\Delta \bar{u} + (k(x)/\bar{k}(x)) e^{2\bar{u}} = 0, \quad \bar{u} \mid \rho_{\mathfrak{R}} = 0.
$$

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
523

It is now simple to construct a natural differential inequality (see also [Ba]).
Namely, we consider the standard isoperimetric inequality, $(\mathrm{Length})^2 \geq 4\pi$.
Area, applied to the level sets $\widetilde{U}_{\alpha} = \{\bar{x} \in \mathbb{R} : \bar{u}(\bar{x}) \geq \alpha\}$, $0 \leq \alpha \leq \max_{\bar{x} \in \mathbb{R}} \bar{u}(\bar{x})$.
Motivated by the fact that if $a(\alpha) = |\widetilde{U}_{\alpha}|$ and $|\bar{V}\bar{u}| &gt; 0$ on $\widetilde{U}_{\alpha}$ we have (see c.f.
[T] p.
361)

$$
\frac{da}{d\alpha} = - \int_{\partial \widetilde{U}_{\alpha}} \frac{ds}{|\bar{V}\bar{u}|}
$$

we operate as indicated below in Lemma A.1 to get

$$
\left(- \int_{\widetilde{U}_{\alpha}} e^{2\bar{u}} d\bar{x}\right) \frac{da}{d\alpha} \geq (4\pi - \delta) a(\alpha)
$$

where $\delta &gt; 0$ is a suitable constant.
Finally, we transform this expression into a differential inequality upon replacing $\bar{u}$ by its symmetrization $\bar{u}^*$ (see (1.7)).

After integrating the differential inequality (1.7) we obtain a bound for $\max_{\bar{x} \in \mathbb{R}} e^{2\bar{u}(\bar{x})}$.
Observe that this is expected because if $\bar{u}^*$ is defined in a ball $B_0$ with center at the origin, then it is well-known that

$$
\max_{\bar{x} \in \mathbb{R}} \bar{u}(\bar{x}) = \max_{x \in B_0} \bar{u}^*(x) = \bar{u}^*(0).
$$

It is, however, interesting that after rewriting the expressions in terms of our original function $v$, we can estimate all the terms in terms of known quantities, obtaining the a-priori estimate.
We do not know in what generality this is applicable.

There is one technical difficulty in order to integrate the differential inequality (1.7).
Namely, we need to assert that the function $a(\alpha)$ is absolutely continuous.
If the function $v$ were real analytic this is very simple.
In general, however, this fact needs some explanation.
This is the object of Lemma A.2.
The ideas of Lemma A.2 are rather classical measure theoretical arguments.

The essential tool to show Theorem A$^1$ is the following a-priori estimate.
Let $k(x) \geq 0$ be a Hölder continuous function and suppose $k(x)$ has only finitely many zeros of finite order.
Assume also that there exists $N_0$ such that given $N \geq N_0 &gt; 0$ there exist $C_i(N)$ $i = 1,2$ such that

$$
C_1(N) |x|^{-b} \leq k(x) \leq C_2(N) |x|^{-b} \tag{1.1}
$$

$$
b &gt; 0, |x| \geq N. \text{ Let}
$$

$$
\xi = \lim_{N \to \infty} C_2(N) / C_1(N). \tag{1.2}
$$

Then we have

**Lemma A.1.** Suppose $v \in C^2(\mathbb{R}^2)$ satisfies (0) and

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2v(x)} dx \leq l\pi, \quad l &lt; 4, \tag{1.3}
$$

where $k(x)$ is as above.
If $\xi &lt; 4/l$ then,

$$
k(x) e^{2v(x)} \leq C \tag{1.4}
$$

where $C$ is a positive constant independent of $v$.

P.
Aviles

Proof.
Case $k(x) &gt; 0$.
Because $\xi &lt; 4 / l$ we can select $N_0$ such that (1.1) holds for $|x| &gt; N_0$ and $lC_2C_1^{-1} &lt; 4$, $C_i = C_i(N_0)$ $i = 1,2$.
Let $B(x_0) = \left\{x\colon |x - x_0| &lt; \frac{|x_0|}{2}\right\}$, $|x_0| \geq 2N_0$.
Let $w$ be the harmonic function

$$
\Delta w = 0, \quad w | _ {\partial B (x _ {0})} = v | _ {\partial B (x _ {0})}.
$$

Then $u(x) = v(x) - w(x)$ satisfies

$$
\Delta u + k (x) e ^ {2 w} e ^ {2 u} = 0 \quad \text{in} \ B (x _ {0}).
$$

Let $h(z) = -2^{-1}b\ln |z| + w(z) + 2^{-1}\ln C_2z\in B(x_0)$ and let $h^* (x)$ be a conjugate harmonic to $h(z)$.
Let $z_{1}$ be the analytic function

$$
z _ {1} (z) = \int_ {x _ {0}} ^ {z} \exp \left\{h (\zeta) + i h ^ {*} (\zeta) \right\} d \zeta .
$$

$z_{1}$ defines a 1-1 conformal mapping of $B(x_0)$ into a simply connected Riemann surface $\mathcal{R}$.
This is a well-known fact.
Briefly this goes as follows.
Let $\mathcal{R}$ be the Riemann surface of the analytic function $z_{1}$, (see [J]).
Since $\mathcal{R}$ is a covering surface, $\mathcal{R}$ is simply connected.
So since $z_{1}'(z) \neq 0$ by using the monodromy theorem (see [Ah] p.
134) we can construct a global 1-1 analytic mapping from the Riemann surface $\mathcal{R}$ to the disc $B(x_0)$, say $g$, so that $g \circ z_{1} = \text{identity}$.
This proves the assertion.
On the other hand, the Gaussian curvature of $\mathcal{R}$ is zero (the metric is given by $g_{ij} = \delta_{ij}|z_1'|^2$) and by the classical isoperimetric inequality (see [H-1] and the references therein) we have

$$
L ^ {2} \geq 4 \pi A
$$

where $L$ is the length of a rectifiable Jordan curve $C$ that encloses a simply-connected domain $D$ of area $A$.

Let $D_{\alpha} = z_{1}\{x \in B(x_{0}) \colon (v - w)(x) \geq \alpha\}$ where $0 \leq \alpha \leq \alpha_{0}$, $\alpha_{0} = \max_{x \in B(x_{0})} (v - w)(x)$.
If $\bar{u}(\bar{x}) = u(x)\bar{x} = z_{1}(x)$, $x \in B(x_{0})$ then by Sard's theorem for almost every $\alpha$, $|\bar{V}\bar{u}(\bar{x})| &gt; 0$ on $\partial D_{\alpha}$, and hence $\partial D_{\alpha}$ is a closed rectifiable Jordan curve enclosing a simply connected domain in $\mathcal{R}$.
Applying the isoperimetric inequality to the sets $D_{\alpha}$ we get

$$
\left(\oint_ {\partial D _ {\alpha}} d s\right) ^ {2} \geq 4 \pi \iint_ {D _ {\alpha}} d \bar {x} _ {1} d \bar {x} _ {2}.
$$

Since $|\bar{V}\bar{u} (\bar{x})| &gt; 0$ on $\partial D_{\alpha}$ by Hölder inequality we get

$$
- \oint_ {\partial D _ {\alpha}} \bar {V} \bar {u} \circ \hat {n} d s \cdot \oint_ {\partial D _ {\alpha}} \frac {1}{| \bar {V} \bar {u} |} d s \geq 4 \pi \iint_ {D _ {\alpha}} d \bar {x} _ {1} d \bar {x} _ {2}
$$

where $\hat{n}$ denotes the outward normal on $\partial D_{\alpha}$.
Since $|\bar{V}\bar{u}| \neq 0$ on $\partial D_{\alpha}$, $\partial D_{\alpha}$ is of class $C^2$ and Green's theorem gives

$$
\left(\iint_ {D _ {\alpha}} - \Delta_ {\bar {x}} \bar {u} (\bar {x}) d \bar {x} _ {1} d \bar {x} _ {2}\right) \left(\oint_ {\partial D _ {\alpha}} \frac {1}{| \bar {V} \bar {u} |} d s\right) \geq 4 \pi \iint_ {D _ {\alpha}} d \bar {x} _ {1} d \bar {x} _ {2}.
$$

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
525

Since $\Delta_{\bar{x}}\bar{u} (\bar{x}) + (k(x)e^{2w(x)} / |z_1'(x)|^2)e^{2\bar{u} (\bar{x})} = 0,\quad |z_1'(x)|^2 = C_2e^{2w(x)}|x|^{-b}$ and $k(x)\leq C_2|x|^{-b}$, in $B(x_0)$, we have

$$
\left(\iint_{D_{\alpha}} e^{2 \bar{u} (\bar{x})} d \bar{x}_1 d \bar{x}_2\right) \left(\oint_{\partial D_{\alpha}} \frac{1}{|V \bar{u}|} ds\right) \geq 4 \pi \iint_{D_{\alpha}} d \bar{x}_1 d \bar{x}_2. \tag{1.5}
$$

To obtain information from this relation we shall transform this inequality in an ordinary differential inequality.
For we use symmetrization.
We recall that if $D_{\alpha} = \{\bar{x}\in \mathcal{R}\colon \bar{u} (\bar{x})\geq \alpha \}$ then $D_{\alpha}^{*} = B$ where $B\subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is the ball with center at 0 and measure $(B) = \mathrm{measure}(D_{\alpha})$.
Therefore if

$$
a(t) = \pi r^2(t) = \mathrm{measure}(\{\bar{x}\in \mathcal{R}\colon \bar{u} (\bar{x})\geq t\}),
$$

then

$$
\bar{u}^* (x) = \sup \left\{\beta : x \in D_{\beta}^* \right\} = \beta (\pi | x |^2)
$$

is the inverse of $a(t)$ and $\bar{u}^*$ is a radially symmetric function about the origin.
Therefore

$$
\iint_{D_{\alpha}^*} e^{2 \bar{u}^* (x)} dx_1 dx_2 = \int_0^{r(\alpha)} e^{2 u^* (r)} d(r^2 \pi) = \int_0^{a(\alpha)} e^{2 \beta (a)} da. \tag{1.6}
$$

Hence from (1.5) and (1.6) we obtain that for almost every $\alpha \in [0,\alpha_0]$

$$
\int_0^{a(\alpha)} e^{2 \beta (s)} ds \oint_{\partial D_{\alpha}} \frac{ds}{|V u|} \geq 4 \pi a (\alpha).
$$

Now because of the well-known formula (formula 16 [T] p.
361) $\frac{da}{d\alpha} = -\int_{\partial D_{\alpha}} \frac{ds}{|V \bar{u}|}$ we get

$$
4 \pi a (\alpha) + \frac{da}{d \alpha} \int_0^{a (\alpha)} e^{2 \beta (s)} ds \leq 0 \quad \text{a.e. in} \quad \alpha \in [0, \alpha_0]. \tag{1.7}
$$

On the other hand, because of Lemma A.2 below $a(\alpha)$ is a strictly decreasing absolutely continuous function (hence its inverse $\beta(a)$ is a strictly decreasing continuous function) and hence

$$
f (\alpha) = e^{2 \alpha} a (\alpha) - \int_0^{a (\alpha)} e^{2 \beta (s)} ds + \frac{1}{4 \pi} \left(\int_0^{a (\alpha)} e^{2 \beta (s)} ds\right)^2
$$

is also an absolutely continuous function, also $\frac{\mathrm{df}}{d\alpha}$ exists a.e.
in $\alpha \in [0,\alpha_0]$ $\left(\frac{\mathrm{df}}{d\alpha}\right)$ exists whenever $\frac{da}{d\alpha}$ does and

$$
\frac{\mathrm{df}}{d \alpha} = (2 \pi)^{-1} e^{2 \alpha} \left(4 \pi a (\alpha) + \frac{da}{d \alpha} \int_0^{a (\alpha)} e^{2 \beta (s)} ds\right).
$$

Thus by the fundamental theorem of calculus applied to $f$ ([H-S] p.
286), (1.7) and because $a(\alpha_0) = 0$ we have

$$
f (\alpha) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all} \ \alpha \in [0, \alpha_0].
$$

P.
Aviles

By letting $\alpha = \beta(a)$ in the definition of $f(\alpha)$ we thus obtain

$$
g(a)^{-2}(e^{2\beta(a)}a - g(a)) + \frac{1}{4\pi} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } a \in (0, a(0)]
$$

where

$$
g(a) = \int_{0}^{a} e^{2\beta(s)} \, ds.
$$

Thus

$$
-\frac{d}{da}\left(\frac{a}{g(a)}\right) + \frac{1}{4\pi} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } a \in (0, a(0)].
$$

Since as $a \to 0$ $a / g(a) \to e^{-2\alpha_0}$ by integrating from 0 to $a(0)$ we get

$$
e^{2v(x_0)} \leq \frac{4\pi e^{2w(x_0)} \int_{B(x_0)} C_2 |x|^{-b} e^{2v(x)} \, dx}{a(0)(4\pi - \int_{B(x_0)} C_2 |x|^{-b} e^{2v(x)} \, dx)}
$$

where $a(0) = \int_{B(x_0)} C_2 |x|^{-b} e^{2w(x)} \, dx$.
Hence

$$
k(x_0)e^{2v(x_0)} \leq \frac{C}{m(B(x_0))} \cdot \frac{e^{2w(x_0)}}{\left(\frac{1}{m(B(x_0))} \int_{B(x_0)} e^{2w(x)} \, dx\right)(4\pi - \int_{B(x_0)} C_2 |x|^{-b} e^{2v(x)} \, dx)},
$$

because of (1.3).
But

$$
\int_{B(x_0)} C_2 |x|^{-b} e^{2v(x)} \, dx \leq \frac{C_2}{C_1} \int_{B(x_0)} k(x) e^{2v(x)} \, dx \leq \frac{C_2}{C_1} l\pi &lt; 4\pi,
$$

and since $w$ is harmonic $e^{2w}$ is subharmonic.
Therefore, we obtain by using the mean value theorem that

$$
k(x_0)e^{2v(x_0)} \leq \frac{C}{m(B(x_0))} \leq C'
$$

where $C' &gt; 0$ is a constant independent of $x_0$ as well as $v(x)$.
Hence (1.4) is proved when $|x_0| \geq 2N_0$.

For $|x| \leq 2N_0$ we observe that the uniform continuity of $k(x)$ and the fact that $k(x) &gt; 0$ imply that there exists $\delta &gt; 0$ independent of $x$ so that for every $|x| \leq 2N_0$

$$
\sup_{x \in \Omega_x} k(x) \leq C \inf_{x \in \Omega_x} k(x)
$$

where $C$ is a positive constant such that $Cl &lt; 4$ and $\Omega_x$ is a neighborhood of $x$ such that the measure of $\Omega_x$ is greater than $\delta$.
We can then repeat the process above in $\Omega_x$ to get

$$
k(x)e^{2v(x)} \leq C'
$$

where $C' &gt; 0$ is a constant independent of $|x| \leq 2N_0$ and $v$.

To finish the proof of Lemma A.1, we consider the case where $k(x)$ has finitely many zeros of finite order.
Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ be the zeros of $k(x)$, let $l_1, \ldots, l_n$

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
527

be their orders and write $k(x) = |x - x_i|^{l_i}k_i(x)$ in neighborhoods $N_{i}$ of $x_{i}$ such that

$$
0 &lt; \tilde {C} _ {i} \leq k _ {i} (x) \leq \tilde {\bar {C}} _ {i} \quad \text{in } N _ {i}, \quad i = 1, \dots , n
$$

and

$$
\int_ {N _ {i}} | x - x _ {i} | ^ {l _ {i}} k _ {i} (x) e ^ {2 v} d x \leq \frac {\tilde {\bar {C}} _ {i}}{\tilde {C} _ {i}} l \pi &lt; 4 \pi .
$$

Define $\bar{v} (\bar{x}) = v(x)$, $x\in N_i$, $\bar{x} = (x - x_{i})^{(l_{i} + 2) / 2}$.
Then

$$
\Delta \bar {v} (\bar {x}) + \bar {k} _ {i} (\bar {x}) e ^ {2 \bar {v}} = 0,
$$

with $\bar{k}_i(\bar{x})\neq 0$ if $\bar{x}$ is in the image of $N_{i}$, say $\bar{N}_i$, through the mapping $x\to (x - x_i)^{(l_i + 2) / 2}$.
Since

$$
\int_ {N _ {i}} \bar {k} _ {i} (\bar {x}) e ^ {2 \bar {v} (\bar {x})} d \bar {x} = \int_ {N _ {i}} k (x) e ^ {2 v} d x
$$

we get

$$
\bar {k} _ {i} (\bar {x}) e ^ {2 \bar {v} (\bar {x})} \leq C, \quad \bar {x} \in \bar {N} _ {i},
$$

where $C$ is independent of $\bar{v}$.
Since $\bar{k}_i(\bar{x}) = \frac{k_i(x)4}{(l_i + 2)^2}$ we have

$$
k _ {i} (x) e ^ {2 v (x)} \leq C, \quad x \in N _ {i}.
$$

Hence (1.5) follows by repeating the first case at infinity and say in $B_{2N_0}(0) - \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} N_i$.

We shall now prove that $a(\alpha)$ is an absolutely continuous function.
We shall use the following notations.
Let $v \in C^2(\mathbb{R}^2)$ and consider

$$
Q = \left\{x \in \mathbb {R} ^ {2}: | V v | = 0 \right\};
$$

$$
Q _ {i} = \left\{x \in \mathbb {R} ^ {2}: v _ {x _ {i}} (x) = 0 \right\} \quad i = 1, 2, x = \left(x _ {1}, x _ {2}\right);
$$

$$
B _ {r} (y) = \left\{x \in \mathbb {R} ^ {2}: | x - y | &lt; r \right\}, \quad B _ {r} (0) = B _ {r};
$$

$\mathcal{R}$ is the Riemann surface defined in Lemma A.1 and

$$
\alpha_ {0} = \max  _ {x \in B _ {r} (x _ {0})} u (x) = \max  _ {\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}} \bar {u} (\bar {x}), \quad r = \frac {| x _ {0} |}{2}
$$

where $u,\bar{u}$ are the functions defined in Lemma A.1.

**Lemma A.2.** (a) If $v$ satisfies (0) with $k(x) &gt; 0$ then

$$
\text{measure} (Q \cap B _ {r}) = 0 \quad \text{for all } r &gt; 0.
$$

(b) If $a(\alpha) = \text{measure}(\{\bar{x} \in \mathcal{R}: \bar{u}(\bar{x}) \geq \alpha\})$ then

$$
a (\alpha) = \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) \geq \alpha \}} d \bar {x} = \int_ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha_ {0}} \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = s \}} | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1} \chi_ {\mathcal {R} - Q _ {0}} (\bar {x}) d \mathcal {H} d s
$$

P.
Aviles

where $Q_0 = \{\overline{x} \in \mathcal{R} : |\nabla \overline{u}(\overline{x})| = 0\}$, $\chi_E$ is the characteristic function of $E$ and $\mathcal{H}$ is the 1-dimensional Hausdorff measure.
Thus $a(\alpha)$ is a strictly decreasing absolutely continuous function ([H-S] Theorem 12.34).

Proof.
(a) If for some $r &gt; 0$ (a) does not hold then there exists $x_0 \in Q$ such that for all $\delta &gt; 0$

$$
\text{measure}(Q \cap B_{\delta}(x_0)) &gt; 0. \tag{1.8}
$$

Indeed, since $Q \cap \bar{B}_r$ is a compact set, and since $\bigcup_{x \in Q} B_{s(x)}(x)$, $s(x) &gt; 0$ covers this set, there exist $x_1, \ldots, x_m \in Q \cap \bar{B}_r$ such that $Q \cap \bar{B}_r \subset \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} Q \cap B_{s(x_i)}(x_i)$.
Since measure $(Q \cap \bar{B}_r) &gt; 0$ there exists $j$ so that measure $(Q \cap B_{s(x_j)}(x_j)) &gt; 0$.
But if (1.8) does hold, then for each $x \in Q$ there is $\delta(x) &gt; 0$ such that measure $(Q \cap B_{\delta(x)}(x)) = 0$.
But $\bigcup_{x \in Q} B_{\delta(x)}(x)$ covers $Q \cap \bar{B}_r$ and therefore there exists $x \in Q$ so that $Q \cap B_{\delta(x)}(x)$ has positive measure, a contradiction.
Next, we observe that we may assume that $x_0$ has density 1, that is,

$$
\lim_{\delta \to 0} \text{measure}(Q \cap B_{\delta}(x_0)) / \text{measure}(B_{\delta}(x_0)) = 1.
$$

Indeed, let $\phi = \{x \in Q : \text{density of } x \neq 1\}$.
It is a well-known fact that measure $(\phi) = 0$.
So if in (1.8) we cannot assume that $x_0 \in Q \setminus \phi$, then for all $x \in Q \setminus \phi$ there exists $\delta(x) &gt; 0$ so that measure $(Q \cap B_{\delta(x)}(x)) = 0$.
On the other hand, since measure $(\phi) = 0$ given $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ there exists an open set $O \supseteq \phi$, so that measure $(O) \leq \varepsilon$.
But $Q \cap \bar{B}_r$ is a compact set, hence there exist $x_1, \ldots, x_m \in Q \setminus \phi$ such that

$$
Q \cap \bar{B}_r \subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} B_{\delta(x_i)}(x_i) \cap Q \cup O.
$$

So measure $(Q \cap B_r) \leq \varepsilon$.
Since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary, measure $(Q \cap B_r) = 0$, a contradiction.
Next, we observe that (1.8) and the fact that $x_0$ has density 1 imply that there exist two sectors with vertex at $x_0$ such that

$$
S_1 \cap S_2 = \{x_0\} \tag{1.8}
$$

$$
\text{measure}(Q_i \cap S_j \cap B_{\delta}(x_0)) &gt; 0 \quad \text{for all } \delta &gt; 0, \quad i, j = 1, 2 \tag{1.8}
$$

(see Fig.
1).
Indeed, since $Q = Q_1 \cap Q_2$ it suffices to show that there exist two sectors $S_1, S_2$ with vertex at $x_0$ that satisfies (1.8)' and

$$
\text{measure}(Q \cap S_j \cap B_{\delta}(x_0)) &gt; 0 \quad \text{for all } \delta &gt; 0, \quad j = 1, 2. \tag{1.8}
$$

For consider the sectors

$$
S_k = \left\{(r, \theta) \colon \frac{k\pi}{2} \leq \theta \leq \frac{k+1}{2} \pi \right\}, \quad 0 \leq k \leq 3
$$

where $r, \theta$ denote the standard polar coordinates starting at $x_0$.
So, if (1.8)'' does not hold, then there exists $\delta_0 &gt; 0$ such that for two of the sectors $S_k$, say $S_2, S_3$, we have

Conformal complete metrics in
\mathbb{R}^2

$$
\operatorname {m e a s u r e} \left(Q \cap S _ {2} \cap B _ {\delta} \left(x _ {0}\right)\right) = \operatorname {m e a s u r e} \left(Q \cap S _ {3} \cap B _ {\delta} \left(x _ {0}\right)\right) = 0,
$$

for all $\delta \leq \delta_0$ .
Hence $x_0$ cannot have density 1, a contradiction.

Now from the property (1.8) we have that there are sequences $\{\tilde{y}_n^{(i)}\} \subseteq S_1 - \{x_0\}$ , $\{\tilde{z}_n^{(i)}\} \subseteq S_2 - \{x_0\}$ such that $\tilde{y}_n^{(i)}, \tilde{z}_n^{(i)} \to x_0$ as $n \to \infty$ , and

$$
v _ {x _ {i}} \left(\tilde {y} _ {n} ^ {(i)}\right) = 0 \quad \text {and} \quad v _ {x _ {i}} \left(\tilde {z} _ {n} ^ {(i)}\right) = 0, \quad \text {for} \ i = 1, 2.
$$

Hence from the mean value theorem applied to $v_{x_i}$ , $i = 1,2$ , it follows that we can find sequences (see Fig.

1.

$$
\left\{y _ {n} ^ {(i)} \right\} \subseteq S _ {1} - \left\{x _ {0} \right\} \quad \text {and} \quad \left\{z _ {n} ^ {(i)} \right\} \subseteq S _ {2} - \left\{x _ {0} \right\}, \quad i = 1, 2
$$

as well as directions

$$
\left\{u _ {n} ^ {(i)} \right\} \subseteq S _ {1} \cap \partial B _ {1} \left(x _ {0}\right) \quad \text {and} \quad \left\{w _ {n} ^ {(i)} \right\} \subseteq S _ {2} \cap \partial B _ {1} \left(x _ {0}\right), \quad i = 1, 2
$$

such that

$$
y _ {n} ^ {(i)}, \quad z _ {n} ^ {(i)} \rightarrow x _ {0} \quad \text {as} \quad n \rightarrow \infty , \quad i = 1, 2,
$$

$$
V v _ {x _ {i}} \left(y _ {n} ^ {(i)}\right) \cdot u _ {n} ^ {(i)} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2
$$

and

$$
V v _ {x _ {i}} \left(z _ {n} ^ {(i)}\right) \cdot w _ {n} ^ {(i)} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2.
$$

By compactness there exist subsequences which we shall still call $\{u_n^{(i)}\}$ and $\{w_{n}^{(i)}\}$ so that

$$
u _ {n} ^ {(i)} \rightarrow u ^ {(i)}, u ^ {(i)} \in S _ {1} \cap \partial B _ {1} (x _ {0}) \quad i = 1, 2
$$

and

$$
w _ {n} ^ {(i)} \rightarrow w ^ {(i)}, w ^ {(i)} \in S _ {2} \cap \partial B _ {1} (x _ {0}) \quad i = 1, 2.
$$

Therefore by continuity we get

$$
V v _ {x _ {i}} \left(x _ {0}\right) \cdot u ^ {(i)} = 0 \quad \text {and} \quad V v _ {x _ {i}} \left(x _ {0}\right) \cdot w ^ {(i)} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2.
$$

But by (1.8)' $\{u^{(1)},w^{(1)}\}$ and $\{u^{(2)},w^{(2)}\}$ are pairs of linearly independent vectors, thus

$$
V v _ {x _ {i}} \left(x _ {0}\right) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2.
$$

Hence $\Delta v(x_0) = 0$ , which contradicts the fact that $k(x)e^{2v(x)}$ is a positive function.
