Fig.1

P.
Aviles

(b) Let

$$
D _ {\alpha} = \{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) \geqslant \alpha \}, \quad A _ {k} = \{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | \geqslant k ^ {- 1} \}
$$

and set

$$
\tilde {f} (\bar {x}) = \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) \chi_ {D _ {\alpha}} (\bar {x}) | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1}, \quad \bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}.
$$

By applying the co-area formula of Federer to $\tilde{f} (\bar{x})$ ([Fe], Theorem 3) we obtain

$$
\int_ {D _ {\alpha}} \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) d \bar {x} = \int_ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha_ {0}} \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = s \}} | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1} \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) d \mathcal {H} d s.
$$

Thus by the monotone convergence theorem we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \int_ {D _ {\alpha}} \lim  _ {k \rightarrow \infty} \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) d \bar {x} = \lim  _ {k \rightarrow \infty} \int_ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha_ {0}} \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = s \}} | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1} \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) d \mathcal {H} d s \\ = \int_ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha_ {0}} \lim  _ {k \rightarrow \infty} \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = s \}} | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1} \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) d \mathcal {H} d s \\ = \int_ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha_ {0}} \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = s \}} | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1} \lim  _ {k \rightarrow \infty} \chi_ {A _ {h}} (\bar {x}) d \mathcal {H} d s \\ \end{array}
$$

Now let $\bar{x} \in \mathcal{R}$ , $x \in B(x_0) = B_r(x_0)$ with $r = \frac{|x_0|}{2}$ , and suppose $\bar{x} = z_1(x)$ where $z_1$ is the conformal mapping from $B(x_0)$ to $\mathcal{R}$ defined in Lemma A.1.
From the definition of $\bar{u}$ it is evident that $\bar{x}$ is a critical point of $\bar{u}$ if and only if $x$ is a critical point of $u$ .
Since by part (a)

$$
\operatorname {m e a s u r e} \left(\left\{x \in B \left(x _ {0}\right): V u (x) = 0 \right\}\right) = 0
$$

we also have that

$$
\operatorname {m e a s u r e} \left(\left\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = 0 \right\}\right) = 0.
$$

So from the integral relation given above it follows

$$
a (\alpha) = \int_ {D _ {\alpha}} d \bar {x} = \int_ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha_ {0}} \int_ {\{\bar {x} \in \mathcal {R}: \bar {u} (\bar {x}) = s \}} | V \bar {u} (\bar {x}) | ^ {- 1} \chi_ {R - Q _ {0}} (\bar {x}) d \mathcal {H} d s,
$$

which proves the assertion of the lemma.

Remark.
The idea of using isoperimetric to obtain different kinds of a priori information has been used by several authors, among them we mention Weinberger [W], Payne [P], Yau [Y], Uhlenbeck [U].

We shall now show Theorem $\mathbf{A}^1$ in the following form.

Theorem A¹.
Let $k(x) \colon \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ , $k(x) \geq 0$ , be a Hölder continuous function having finitely many zeros of finite order.
If $k(x)$ satisfies (1.1) and (1.2), then there exist solutions of (0) having the asymptotic behavior (2) with the constant $c$ subject to

$$
\max  (2, b) &lt;   c &lt;   b + 4 \xi^ {- 1}. \tag {1.9}
$$

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
531

We shall now see that (1.4) puts us in the position of using the Leray-Schauder fixed point theorem.

Proof.
We want to define a suitable compact mapping $T: X \to X$ with $X$ being a Banach space to be specified later.
Let us consider the functions

$$
\psi(x) = \frac{(c - b)}{2} \ln(|x|^2 + 1)^\frac{1}{2},
$$

with $c$ as in (1.9).
Then

$$
\Phi = \Delta \psi = (c - b)(|x|^2 + 1)^{-2} \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} \Phi \, dx = (c - b)\pi.
$$

Let

$$
k'(x) = k(x)e^{-2\psi} = \frac{k(x)}{(|x|^2 + 1)^{(c - b)/2}}.
$$

If $X = M_{1,\delta}^p p &gt; 2$, $-2/p &lt; \delta &lt; -2/p + 1$, $X \subset C_0(\mathbb{R}^2)$, then for $v \in X$ there exists $C_v &gt; 0$ so that

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} (\Phi - k'(x)e^{2(v + C_v)}) \, dx = 0. \tag{1.10}
$$

Let $\delta'$ be such that $\delta &lt; \delta' &lt; \min[(c - 2) - 2/p, 1 - 2/p]$.
Then $\Phi - k'(x)e^{2(v + C_v)} \in M_{0,\delta'+2}^p$.
Hence, because of Theorem 0 we can define a continuous map $T: \underset{\mathbf{v} \to W}{X \to X}$, with $\Delta w = \Phi - k'(x)e^{2(v + C_v)}$.
Since $w \in M_{2,\delta'}^p$, and $M_{2,\delta'}^p \hookrightarrow X$ is compact, $T$ is compact.

To conclude that $T$ has a fixed point we need (1.4).
Indeed, assume

$$
\Delta v + t k'(x)e^{2(v + C_v)} = t\Phi, \quad 0 \leq t \leq 1. \tag{1.11}
$$

If $t = 0$ then $v \equiv 0$ because $v \in X \subset C_0(\mathbb{R}^2)$.
So we may assume that $0 &lt; t \leq 1$.
Now because of Theorem 0(c) we get

$$
\| v \|_X \leq \| v \|_{M_{2,\delta}^p} \leq C \| \Delta v \|_{M_{0,2+\delta}^p} \leq C (\Phi, k'(x)) \max \left\{1, \sup_{x \in \mathbb{R}^2} e^{2(v + C_v)} \right\} \tag{1.12}
$$

By (1.11) we have

$$
\Delta \bar{v} + t k''(x)e^{2(\bar{v} + C_v)} = 0 \tag{1.13}
$$

where $\bar{v}(x) = v(x) - t\psi(x)$, $k''(x) = k'(x)e^{2t\psi(x)}$.
Therefore, the form of $k''(x)$ and (1.10) imply that in (1.13) the hypotheses of Lemma A.1 are satisfies and we obtain

$$
t k''(x)e^{2(\bar{v} + C_v)} \leq tC
$$

where $C$ is independent of $\bar{v}$ and $t$.
So

$$
k'(x)e^{2(v + C_v)} \leq C.
$$

Together with (1.12) this implies that $T$ has a fixed point ([G-T] p.
222), say $v$.
Therefore $u = v - \psi + C_v$ satisfies (0).
It is clear from the fact that $v \in C_0(\mathbb{R}^2)$ that $u$ has the asymptotic behavior of the theorem.

P.
Aviles

Remark.
In Sect.
4 we shall see that the hypothesis (1.3) is best possible.
That is if

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2v(x)} dx = 4\pi,
$$

then it could happen that (0) has no solution.

## § 2. Asymptotic behavior

We shall prove Theorem B(a).
We shall first obtain preliminary bounds.

Lemma B.1.
Let $u$ be a solution of (0) with $k$ satisfying the condition stated in (1).
Then at infinity

$$
u(x) \leq (b - 2)/2 \ln |x| + C \tag{2.1}
$$

and

$$
u(x) \geq (b - 2 - c)/2 \ln |x| - C \tag{2.2}
$$

where $c$, $C &gt; 0$ are constants and moreover $C$ is independent of the solution $u$.

Proof.
In the proof of this lemma we shall use the letter $C$ (with or without subindex) to denote several positive constant which are independent of $u$.
Let $B(x_0) = \left\{x \in \mathbb{R}^2 : |x - x_0| &lt; \frac{|x_0|}{2}\right\} |x_0| &gt; 2N$ where $N$ is chosen so that

$$
- k(x) \geq C |x|^{-b} \quad \text{if } |x| &gt; N.
$$

Hence in $B(x_0)$, $\Delta u \geq C_1 |x_0|^{-b} e^{2\bar{u}}$ where $C_1 = C_2^b 3^{-b}$.
But the function

$$
v(r) = \ln C_2 R^{(2 + b)/2} / (R^2 - r^2),
$$

where $R = |x_0| / 2$, $r = |x - x_0|$, $C_2 = 2^{(2 + b)/2} / C_1^{1/2}$, satisfies $\Delta v \leq C_1 |x_0|^{-b} e^{2v}$.
Thus by comparison we obtain (2.1).

We shall prove (2.2).
Consider the auxiliary function

$$
\bar{u}(x) = u \left(\frac{x}{|x|^2}\right) + \frac{(b - 2)}{2} \ln |x|, \quad |x| &lt; 1.
$$

Then

$$
\Delta \bar{u}(x) + \bar{k}(x) e^{2\bar{u}(x)} = 0 \quad \text{if } |x| &lt; 1 - \{0\} \tag{2.3}
$$

where $\bar{k}(x) = k(x / |x|^2) |x|^{-(b + 2)}$.
By adding a positive constant, a fact which is unimportant in what follows, we may assume from (2.1) that $-\bar{u}$ is non-negative.
Therefore for any $B_R(x_0)$ contains in $|x| \leq 1 - \{0\}$ and for any $p &gt; 1$ we get from Theorems 8.17 and 8.18 in [G-T] that

$$
\sup_{x \in B_{R/2}(x_0)} - \bar{u}(x) \leq C R^{-2/p} \left( \int_{B_R(x_0)} (-\bar{u})^p dx \right)^{1/p} + C(R)
$$

and since may assume that $\bar{k}(x)$ is negative we also have

$$
\inf_{x \in B_{R/2}(x_0)} - \bar{u}(x) \geq C R^{-2/p} \left( \int_{B_R(x_0)} (-\bar{u})^p dx \right)^{1/p}
$$

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
533

where $C(R) \leq CR^{2\delta} \|\bar{k}(x)e^{2\bar{u}}\|_{1/(1-\delta)}$, $0 &lt; \delta &lt; 1$.
It follows from (2.1) that $C(R)$ is uniformly bounded.
Hence by a standard covering argument we obtain that for all $0 &lt; R &lt; \frac{1}{2}$

$$
\sup_{|x| = R} -\bar{u}(x) \leq C \inf_{|x| = R} -\bar{u}(x) + C \tag{2.4}
$$

where $C &gt; 1$ is a constant independent of $\bar{u}$ and $R$.

We shall next show that

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} -k(x)e^{2u(x)}dx &lt; \infty. \tag{2.5}
$$

For let $A(r)$ be the average of $u$ on $|x| = R$

$$
A(r) = \int_0^{2\pi} u(r,\theta) \, d\theta \tag{2.6}
$$

where $r$ and $\theta$ denote the standard polar coordinates.
Taking average in (0) we get

$$
-\int_{|x| &lt; r} k(x)e^{2u(x)}dx = r A_r.
$$

So if (2.5) does not hold then for $r \geq r_0$, where $r_0 &gt; 0$ is a real number which is sufficiently large, $A(r) &gt; b \ln r + C$, but this contradicts (2.1).
Next consider $R_0 &lt; \frac{1}{2}$ such that

$$
-\int_{|x| &lt; R_0} \bar{k}(x)e^{2\bar{u}(x)}dx \leq 1. \tag{2.7}
$$

We now claim that if $\psi \in C_0^\infty(B_{R_0}(0))$ and $\psi \equiv 1$ in a neighborhood of the origin then

$$
-\int_{|x| &lt; R_0} V \bar{u} V \psi \, dx + \int_{|x| &lt; R_0} \bar{k}(x)e^{2\bar{u}(x)} \psi \, dx = c_0 \tag{2.8}
$$

where $c_0$ is constant which is independent of $\psi$.
As in Theorem 12 of [S], to see this we consider two functions $\psi$ and $\bar{\psi}$ satisfying the above condition.
Then $\phi = \psi - \bar{\psi}$ has compact support in $B_{R_0}(0) - \{0\}$.
Therefore from (2.3) and (2.5) it follows

$$
\begin{aligned}
&amp; -\int_{|x| &lt; R_0} V \bar{u} V \psi \, dx + \int_{|x| &lt; R_0} \bar{k}(x)e^{2\bar{u}(x)} \psi \, dx \\
&amp; = -\int_{|x| &lt; R_0} V \bar{u} \cdot V \bar{\psi} \, dx + \int_{|x| &lt; R_0} \bar{k}(x)e^{2\bar{u}(x)} \bar{\psi} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the assertion.

We shall now introduce an appropriate test function.
By the subtraction of a constant from $\bar{u}$, a fact which is irrelevant in what follows, we may assume that $-\bar{u} &lt; 0$ on $|x| = R_0$.
Define then

$$
\phi_m(x) = \begin{cases}
0 &amp; \text{if } -\bar{u} \leq 0 \\
-\bar{u} &amp; \text{if } 0 \leq -\bar{u} \leq m \\
m &amp; \text{if } -\bar{u}(x) \geq m
\end{cases}
$$

where $m = \inf_{|x| = r} -\bar{u}(x)$.
Observe that $-\bar{u} \to \infty$ as $|x| \to 0$, because otherwise we would have a sequence $x_n \to 0$ such that $-\bar{u}(x_n) \leq C$, where $C$ is a constant.

P.
Aviles

Then from the definition $-\bar{u}$ and (2.4) we get

$$
u(x) \geq \frac{(b - 2)}{2} \ln |x| + C, \quad \text{at infinity}.
$$

But this estimate contradicts (2.5).
(It should be observe that (2.4) is applicable, because if $C_1 &gt; 0$ is a constant such that $-\bar{u} - C_1 &lt; 0$ on $|x| = R_0$, then from (2.4) we have

$$
\sup_{|x| = R} (-\bar{u} - C_1) \leq C \inf_{|x| = R} (-\bar{u} - C_1) + C[1 + C_1] - C_1
$$

for all $0 &lt; R &lt; \frac{1}{2}$).
Therefore $\phi_m / m$ is an admissible function in (2.8).
Thus

$$
\int_{r \leq |x| \leq R_0} |V\bar{u}|^2 dx \leq m \left( |c_0| - \int_{|x| &lt; R_0} \bar{k}(x) e^{2\bar{u}(x)} \frac{\phi_m}{m} dx \right).
$$

It thus follows from (2.7) and the fact that $-\bar{k}(x) &gt; 0$ that

$$
\int_{r \leq |x| \leq R_0} |V\bar{u}|^2 dx \leq m(|c_0| + 1) \tag{2.9}
$$

To estimate the gradient we shall use capacity.
We recall that the capacity of the ball $|x| &lt; r$, with $r &lt; R_0$ is defined by

$$
\inf_{\psi} \int_{|x| &lt; R_0} |V\psi|^2 dx
$$

where the infimum is taken over all continuously differentiable function $\psi \geq 1$ on $|x| &lt; r$, having compact support in $|x| &lt; R_0$.
It is well known (Lemma 10 of [S]) that this quantity is given by

$$
\inf_{\psi} \int_{|x| &lt; R_0} |V\psi|^2 dx = 2\pi \left( \ln \frac{R_0}{r} \right)^{-1}. \tag{2.10}
$$

Consider $r &lt; R_2 &lt; R_0$ such that for a constant $1 &lt; \beta \leq 2$

$$
0 &lt; -\bar{u}(x) &lt; m \quad \text{if} \quad \frac{R_2}{\beta} &lt; |x| &lt; R_2
$$

and define

$$
\psi_m(x) = \begin{cases}
\max(0, -\bar{u}) &amp; \text{if } |x| \geq R_2 \\
-\bar{u}(x) &amp; \text{if } \frac{R_2}{\beta} &lt; |x| &lt; R_2 \\
\min(m, -\bar{u}(x)) &amp; \text{if } r &lt; |x| \leq \frac{R_2}{\beta} \\
m &amp; \text{if } |x| \leq r
\end{cases}
$$

where $m = \inf_{|x| = r} \bar{u}(x)$.
Since $-\bar{u} &lt; 0$ on $|x| = R_0$, $\psi_m / m$ is an admissible function in (2.10) and we thus obtain

$$
\int_{r &lt; |x| &lt; R_0} |V\bar{u}|^2 dx \geq 2\pi m^2 \left( \ln \frac{R_0}{r} \right)^{-1}.
$$

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
535

It follows from this relation and (2.9) that

$$
m \leq - \frac {(| c _ {0} | + 1)}{2 \pi} \ln r,
$$

since $R_0 &lt; 1$.
So from (2.4) and the definitions of $m$ and $\bar{u}$ it follows that at infinity

$$
u (x) \geq \frac {(b - 2 - c)}{2} \ln | x | - C
$$

where

$$
c = \frac {\left(\left| c _ {0} \right| + 1\right) C}{\pi}. \tag {2.11}
$$

Remark.
From now on we shall assume that the constant $c_{0}$ is non-zero.
The case $c_{0} = 0$ is simpler and it will be treated at the end of the proof of Theorem B(a), p.
20).

We shall now find a relation between the upper and lower estimate.

Lemma B.2.
Let the hypothesis of Lemma B.1 be satisfied.
If the constant $c_0$ in (2.8) is non-zero then, at infinity

$$
u (x) \leq \frac {\left(b - 2 - c _ {1}\right)}{2} \ln | x | + C \tag {2.12}
$$

where $c_{1} = \alpha c$ with $c$ being the constant in (2.2) and where $0 &lt; \alpha &lt; 1$ is a constant and where $C &gt; 0$ is a constant independent of $u$.

Proof.
Since $c_0$ in (2.8) is non-zero exists $R_1, 0 &lt; R_1 &lt; R_0$, such that

$$
- \int_ {| x | &lt; R _ {1}} \bar {k} (x) e ^ {2 \bar {u} (x)} d x \leq \frac {\left| c _ {0} \right|}{2}. \tag {2.13}
$$

We define the test function

$$
X (x) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} 0 &amp; \text{if } | x | &gt; R _ {1} \\ M (\ln | x | / R _ {1}) / \ln (r / R _ {1}) &amp; \text{if } r &lt; | x | &lt; R _ {1} \\ M &amp; \text{if } | x | \leq r \end{array} \right.
$$

where $M = \sup_{|x| = r} (-\bar{u}(x))$, $0 &lt; r &lt; R_1$.
The function $\frac{X(x)}{M}$ is an admissible function for (2.8).
So, since $-\bar{k}(x)$ is positive if $|x| &lt; R_1$, we get by Holder inequality and (2.13) that

$$
\frac {M \left| c _ {0} \right|}{2} \leq \left[ \int_ {r \leq | x | \leq R _ {1}} | V \bar {u} | ^ {2} d x \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ \int_ {r \leq | x | \leq R _ {1}} | V X | ^ {2} d x \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {2.14}
$$

Consider now $r &lt; R_2 &lt; R_1 &lt; R_0$ such that for a constant $1 &lt; \beta \leq 2$

$$
0 &lt; - \bar {u} (x) &lt; M \quad \text{if } \frac {R _ {2}}{\beta} &lt; | x | &lt; R _ {2}
$$

P.
Aviles

and define the test function

$$
\psi_{M}(x) = \begin{cases}
\max(0, -\bar{u}) &amp; \text{if } |x| &gt; R_{2} \\
-\bar{u}(x) &amp; \text{if } \frac{R_{2}}{\beta} &lt; |x| &lt; R_{2} \\
\min(M, -\bar{u}) &amp; \text{if } r &lt; |x| &lt; \frac{R_{2}}{\beta} \\
M &amp; \text{if } |x| \leq r
\end{cases}
$$

where $M = \sup_{|x| = r} -\bar{u}(x)$.
Since we may assume that $-\bar{u} &lt; 0$ on $|x| = R_1$, (otherwise we subtract a constant) we may use $\frac{\psi_M}{M}$ in (2.8) to get from (2.13) that

$$
\int_{r \leq |x| \leq R_{1}} |V\bar{u}(x)|^{2} dx \leq \frac{3}{2} M |c_{0}|.
$$

So using in (2.14) the inequality

$$
ab \leq a^{2} + \frac{b^{2}}{4}
$$

we get

$$
\frac{|c_{0}| M}{2^{3}} \leq \int_{r \leq |x| \leq R_{1}} |VX(x)|^{2} dx.
$$

But a simple calculation shows

$$
\int_{r \leq |x| \leq R_{1}} |VX(x)|^{2} dx = 2\pi M^{2} (\ln R_{1}/r)^{-1}.
$$

So combining these last two relations we get

$$
\frac{|c_{0}|}{2^{4} \pi} \ln (R_{1}/r) \leq M.
$$

Hence it follows from (2.4) that for $|x|$ close to the origin

$$
\frac{|c_{0}|}{2^{4} \pi C} \ln (R_{1}/|x|) - C \leq -\bar{u}(x)
$$

where $C &gt; 1$ is a constant independent of $u$.
So by decreasing the constant in front of $\ln |x|$ to eliminate $R_{1}$ we have that

$$
\frac{-|c_{0}|}{2^{5} \pi C} \ln |x| - C \leq -\bar{u}(x)
$$

for $x$ sufficiently small.
From this relation, the definition of $\bar{u}$ and (2.11), (2.12) is evident.
Indeed $c_{1}, \alpha$ are given by

$$
c_{1} = \frac{|c_{0}|}{2^{4} \pi C}, \quad 0 &lt; \alpha = \frac{1}{2^{4} C^{2}} \left(\frac{|c_{0}|}{|c_{0}| + 1}\right) &lt; 1.
$$

In the next lemma we improve our estimates.

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
537

Lemma B.3.
Let the hypothesis of Lemma B.1 be satisfied.
If the constant $c_0$ in (2.8) is non-zero then $u$ has the asymptotic behavior

$$
\frac{(b - c_2)}{2} \ln |x| - C \leq u(x) \leq \frac{(b - c_2)}{2} \ln |x| + C \tag{2.15}
$$

where $c_2$ is a parameter.

Proof.
Consider the auxiliary function

$$
v = u + \frac{u_0}{D}
$$

where

(i) $u_0(x) = \gamma \ln(1 + |x|^2), \gamma \geq 0$ and where $D$ is a constant chosen so that
(ii) $D \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2u(x)} dx = \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} \Delta u_0(x) dx.$

(Observe that if $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2u(x)} dx = 0$, then $\gamma = 0$).
Consider next the equation

$$
\Delta v = \frac{\Delta u_0}{D} - k(x) e^{2u}(x) = f(x).
$$

Since

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} f(x) dx = 0
$$

and

$$
f(x) \in M_{0,2+\delta}^2
$$

where $-1 &lt; \delta &lt; \min(c_1 - 1, 0)$ with $c_1 &gt; 0$ being the constant given by (2.12), we obtain from Theorem 0(c) that $v \in M_{2,\delta}^2$.
Thus from Theorem 0(b) we get that $v \in C_0(\mathbb{R}^2)$ and hence

$$
- \frac{\gamma}{D} \ln(1 + |x|^2) - C \leq u(x) \leq - \frac{\gamma}{D} \ln(1 + |x|^2) + C.
$$

Making $-\frac{\gamma}{D} = \frac{(b - c_2)}{4}$ we obtain (2.15).

To this end we show Theorem B(a).

Proof of Theorem B(a).
We shall first assume that the constant $c_0$ in (2.8) is non-zero.
From (2.15) we observe that it suffices to show that

$$
c_2 &gt; b.
$$

For multiply (0) by $e^{-2u}$ and integrate by parts to get

$$
\int_{B_R(0)} k(x) dx = -2 \int_{B_R(0)} e^{-2u} |Vu|^2 dx - \int_{S(R)} e^{-2u} \frac{\partial u}{\partial n} ds. \tag{2.16}
$$

On the other hand, an standard gradient estimate (see first formula in the proof of Theorem 4.6 of [G-T]) and (2.15) imply

$$
\left| e^{-2u} \frac{\partial u}{\partial n} \right|_{S(R)} \leq C \left\{ \frac{(\ln R) R^{-(b - c_2)}}{R} + \frac{R}{R^b} \right\}. \tag{2.17}
$$

P.
Aviles

where $C &gt; 0$ is constant independent of $u$.
Hence if $b &gt; c_{2}$, since $b \geq 2$, we have

$$
\left| \int_{S(R)} e^{-2u(x)} \frac{\partial u}{\partial n} \, ds \right| \to 0 \quad \text{as } R \to \infty. \tag{2.18}
$$

Thus from (2.16) it follows that $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) \, dx \leq 0$, which contradicts (6).

To show that $c_{2} = b$ is impossible we proceed as follows.
From Lemma B.3 we notice that $u$ is bounded if and only if

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2u(x)} \, dx = 0.
$$

Hence, since $k(x)e^{2u(x)} \in M_{0,2}^p$ if $p &gt; \max \left(2, \frac{2}{b - 2}\right)$, we get that $u \in M_{2,0}^p$.
In particular we have that $u(x) \to 0$ as $|x| \to \infty$.
So from the gradient estimate used to get (2.17) we now obtain

$$
\left| e^{-2u} \frac{\partial u}{\partial n} \right|_{S(R)} \leq C \left[ \frac{C(R)}{R} + \frac{R}{R^b} \right]
$$

where $C(R) \to 0$ as $R \to \infty$.
So we also obtain (2.18) and thus a contradiction.

If in (2.8) $c_{0} = 0$ then from (2.1) and (2.11) we have that at infinity

$$
(b - 2 - C/\pi)/2 \ln |x| - C \leq u(x) \leq (b - 2)/2 \ln |x| + C
$$

where $C &gt; 0$ is a constant independent of $u$.
So using the gradient estimate that we used to get (2.17) we now obtain

$$
\left| e^{-2u} \frac{\partial u}{\partial n} \right|_{S(R)} \leq C_{1} \left[ \frac{(\ln R) R^{-(b - 2 - C/\pi)}}{R} + \frac{R R^{C/\pi}}{R^b} \right].
$$

Thus if $b &gt; 2 + C/\pi$ then (2.18) holds and therefore we have a contradiction.
If $2 &lt; b \leq 2 + C/\pi$ let us consider the auxiliary function

$$
\bar{v}(\bar{x}) = u(x) \quad \text{where } x = \bar{x}^{\beta}, \quad \beta &gt; C/\pi (b - 2).
$$

Then $\bar{v}(\bar{x})$ satisfies

$$
\Delta \bar{v}(\bar{x}) + \bar{k}(\bar{x}) e^{2\bar{v}(\bar{x})} = 0 \quad \text{in } \mathbb{R}^2
$$

where $\bar{k}(\bar{x}) = \beta^2 k(\bar{x}^\beta) |\bar{x}|^{2(\beta - 1)}$.
Now observe that $\bar{k}(\bar{x})$ satisfies (7) with the constant $b$ being $\bar{b}$,

$$
\bar{b} = \beta b - 2(\beta - 1) &gt; 2 + C/\pi.
$$

It follows from what we have just proved that at infinity

$$
\frac{(\bar{b} - \bar{c}_2)}{2} \ln |\bar{x}| - C_2 \leq \bar{v}(\bar{x}) \leq \frac{(\bar{b} - \bar{c}_2)}{2} \ln |\bar{x}| + C_2
$$

where $\bar{c}_2 &gt; \bar{b}$ and where $C_2 &gt; 0$ is a constant independent of $\bar{v}$.
Rewriting this relation in terms of $u(x)$ we obtain the theorem.

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
539

The proof of part (b) of Theorem B is almost the same as that of part (a).
The basic difference being in Lemma B.1.
For let $\bar{u}(x)$ be the auxiliary function defined in the proof of Lemma B.1.
The hypothesis of finite total curvature implies that one can find a neighborhood of the origin, say $\Omega'$, so that

$$
\int_{\Omega'} \bar{k}(x) e^{2\bar{u}(x)} dx \leq \frac{C_1}{C_2} \pi
$$

with $\bar{k}(x)$ being as in (2.3) and with $C_1, C_2$ being the constants in (4) and (5).
Then by repeating word for word the proof of Lemma A.1 we obtain (2.1).
The proof of (2.2) is obtained with trivial modifications of that when $k(x)$ is negative at infinity.
Observe that in this case, it follows by plugging in (2.8) the text function $\phi_m / m$ defined in Lemma B.1 that $c_0 \neq 0$.
Finally if $u(x)$ is radially symmetric then (0) becomes

$$
ru_r + \int_{|x| &lt; r} k(r) e^{2u(r)} dx = 0 \tag{2.19}
$$

Also from (0) we get that if $v(r) = u(e^r)$, then at infinity $v$ is concave and hence at infinity.
$u$ is either non-decreasing or non-increasing.
In the first case (2.19) implies $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2u(r)} dx \leq 0$ and in the last case, since $b &gt; 2$ and $u$ is radially symmetric we obtain $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(r) e^{2u(r)} dx &lt; \infty$.
Therefore in both cases the total curvature is finite and part (b) of Theorem B applies.

We close this section with examples.

(1) (Negative curvature at infinity.) Let us consider the metric $g_{ij} = (\ln(2 + r^2))^{-2} \delta_{ij}$, with $r = |x|$, $\delta_{ij}$ the Kronecker delta.
Clearly if $g_{ij} = e^{2u} \delta_{ij}$ then $u(r) = -\ln \ln(2 + r^2)$, and a simple calculation shows that $u$ satisfies (0) with $k(r) = -(2 + r^2)^{-2} (4r^2 - 8\ln(2 + r^2))$.
Hence at infinity $k(r)$ is negative and behaves like $-r^{-2}$ (and therefore $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(r) dx = -\infty$).
So (7) is essential to obtain (2) and (8) in part (a) of Theorem B.

(2) (Positive curvature at infinity.) Let us consider the metric $g_{ij} = (\ln(2 + r^2))^2 \delta_{ij}$ where as before $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta.
Thus if $g_{ij} = e^{2u} \delta_{ij}$ then $u(r) = \ln \ln(2 + r^2)$.
Also a simple calculation shows that $u$ satisfies (0) with $k(r) = \ln(2 + r^2)^{-4} (2 + r^2)^{-2} (4r^2 - 8\ln(2 + r^2))$.
Therefore at infinity $k(r)$ is positive and behaves like $r^{-2} (\ln(2 + r^2))^{-4}$.
It is also easy to see that $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(r) dx &lt; 0$.
Hence again if (6) or (7) is not satisfied then the metric $g_{ij}$ may spread-over $\mathbb{R}^2$ in a different way of the assertion of Theorem B(b).

Finally we refers to the papers of Huber [H], Finn [F] and the note of Oleinik [O] in which related questions are treated.

§ 3. Completeness

Proof of Theorem C.
The necessity follows from Cohn and Vossen's inequality cf.
[H], [B].
We shall give a direct proof below.
We prove the sufficiency.

P.
Aviles

Suppose $g_{ij}$ is a metric pointwise conformal to the standard metric.
Then there exists $u \in C^2(\mathbb{R}^2)$ such that $g_{ij} = e^{2u}\delta_{ij}$.
By hypothesis $u$ satisfies (0) with $k(x)$ as in Theorem B.
We shall next distinguish two cases.
Let us first assume that at infinity $k(x)$ is positive.
If as in the proof of Lemma B.1 $A(r)$ denotes the average of $u$ (see (2.6)) then from (0)

$$
r A _ {r} = - \int_ {| x | &lt; r} k (x) e ^ {2 u (x)} d x \geq - \int_ {\mathbb {R} ^ {2}} k (x) e ^ {2 u (x)} d x, \tag {3.1}
$$

where the last inequality follows from the assumption that at infinity $k(x)$ is positive.
Hence integrating from 1 to $r$ and using (9) we get

$$
2 \pi \inf  _ {| x | = r} u (x) \geq A (r) \geq - 2 \pi \ln r + A (1).
$$

But from the asymptotic behavior given by Theorem B(b) we get if $r$ is sufficiently large that

$$
\sup  _ {| x | = r} u (x) \leq \inf  _ {| x | = r} u (x) + C \tag {3.2}
$$

where $C &gt; 0$ is a constant independent of $u$.
So at infinity

$$
u (x) \geq - \ln | x | - C.
$$

Therefore $g_{ij}$ defines a complete metric.

Let us now assume that at infinity $k$ is negative.
Then from (3.1) we have

$$
r A _ {r} = - \int_ {\mathbb {R} ^ {2}} k (x) e ^ {2 u (x)} d x + \int_ {| x | \geq r} k (x) e ^ {2 u (x)} d x.
$$

So using (9) and the asymptotic behavior of $u$ given by Theorem B(a) we obtain

$$
A _ {r} \geq - \frac {2 \pi}{r} - \frac {C}{r ^ {c - 1}}
$$

where $C &gt; 0$ is a positive constant and where $c &gt; b &gt; 2$ is also a constant.
So by integrating from 1 to $r$ and using (3.2), which still holds because of Theorem B(a), we obtain that at infinity

$$
u (x) \geq - \ln | x | - \frac {C}{| x | ^ {c - 2}}.
$$

Hence the metric $g_{ij}$ defines a complete metric.

Remark.
We shall give a direct proof of Cohn and Vossen's formula.
Proceeding exactly as above we conclude.

(i) If at infinity $k(x) &gt; 0$ then for $|x|$ sufficiently large

$$
u (x) \geq - \left(\frac {1}{2 \pi} \int_ {\mathbb {R} ^ {2}} k (x) e ^ {2 u (x)} d x\right) \ln | x |;
$$

(ii) If at infinity $k(x) &lt; 0$ then for $|x|$ sufficiently large

Conformal complete metrics in $\mathbb{R}^2$
541

$$
u(x) \geq - \left( \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2u(x)} dx \right) \ln |x| - \frac{C}{|x|^{c-2}}.
$$

Hence in both cases if $g_{ij}$ is complete then (9) holds.

## § 4. Theorem A and a non-existence example

Proof of Theorem A.
Let $S^2$ be the standard two-sphere that is $S^2$ with the metric $ds^2 = 4dx^2/(1 + |x|^2)^2$.
The idea is to define $k^*(x^*)$ on $S^2$ from $k(x)$ in such a way that $k^*(x^*) \in L^\infty(S^2) \cap C^{0,\alpha}(S^2 - \{(0,0,1)\})$ and one can solve

$$
- \int_{S^2} V u^* V \phi \, ds^2 + \int_{S^2} k^*(x^*) e^{2u^*} \phi \, ds^2 = l \int_{S^2} \phi \, ds^2 \tag{4.1}
$$

where $u^* \in C^{2,\alpha}(S^2 - \{(0,0,1)\}) \cap C^0(S^2)$, $\phi \in C^\infty(S^2)$ and where $l &gt; 0$ is a constant.
For let $x^* \in S^2$ be the image of $x$ under stereographic projection and define

$$
k^*(x^*) = (1 + |x|^2)^{2 - (c - b)/2} k(x)/4,
$$

$k^*(0,0,1) = \lim_{x \to \infty} \sup_{|x|^4 - (c - b)} k(x)$.
Since $c \geq \max(4,b)$, $k^*$ satisfies the above properties.
Now, following Möser (see [K-W, 1]) we minimize

$$
J(v) = \int_{S^2} \left( \frac{1}{2} |Vv|^2 + lv \right) ds^2, \quad v \in H^{1,2}(S^2), \quad 0 &lt; l &lt; 1,
$$

under the constraint

$$
\int_{S^2} k^*(x^*) e^{2v} ds^2 = l 4\pi. \tag{4.2}
$$

Since $\sup_{S^2} k^*(x^*) &gt; 0$, (4.2) is not empty and if $w = v - \bar{v}$, $\bar{v} = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int_{S^2} v \, ds^2$, then we have

$$
J(w) = \int_{S^2} \frac{1}{2} |Vw|^2 ds^2 - l 2\pi \ln \int_{S^2} k^*(x^*) e^{2w} ds^2 + l 2\pi \ln l 4\pi.
$$

Hence using Trudinger's inequality, that is: There exists $\delta$ such that for any $u \in H^{1,2}(S^2)$, $\bar{u} = 0$ and any $\alpha &gt; 0$

$$
\int_{S^2} e^{\alpha |u|} ds^2 \leq \delta \exp \left( \frac{(\alpha \| V u \|_2)^2}{16\pi} \right)
$$

where

$$
\| V u \|_2^2 = \int_{S^2} |Vu|^2 ds^2
$$

(see [K-W, 1] p.
23), the fact that $0 &lt; \sup_{x^* \in S^2} k^*(x^*) &lt; \infty$ one obtains

$$
J(w) \geq \frac{1}{2} (1 - l) \| V w \|_2^2 + \text{constant}.
$$

Therefore $J$ is bounded below and using the compactness lemma (3.7) of [K-W, 1] together with standard regularity results one yields the existence of solutions for (4.1).
Let now

$$
u(x) = u^*(x^*).
$$

P.
Aviles

Then

$$
\Delta_{\mathbb{R}^2} u + \frac{k(x)}{(1 + |x|^2)^{(c - b)/2}} e^{2u} = \frac{l4}{(1 + |x|^2)^2}.
$$

Thus making $l = (c - b)/4$, $v = u - \frac{(c - b)}{2} \ln(|x|^2 + 1)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ we obtain a solution of (0) with the asymptotic behavior (2) and (3).

We shall end this paper constructing an example for Remark 1 in Sect.

1. Let $k^*(\theta, \phi) \in S^2$ be defined by $k^*(\theta, \phi) = 2^{-b}(1 - \cos \phi)^b$, $b &gt; 2$, $0 &lt; \phi \leq \pi$.
   Let $k(x) = (1 + |x|^2)^{-b}$, $x \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
   Let $x = T(0, \phi)$ where $T$ stands for stereographic projection.
   Since $|x|^2 = \frac{1 + \cos \phi}{1 - \cos \phi}$, $k(x) = k^*(\theta, \phi)$.
   We next show that the equation

$$
\Delta u(x) + k(x) e^{2u(x)} = 0 \quad \text{in } \mathbb{R}^2, \quad \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} k(x) e^{2u(x)} dx = 4\pi
$$

has no solution.
From Theorem B(b) we know that at infinity

$$
\frac{(2b - c)}{2} \ln |x| - C \leq u(x) \leq \frac{(2b - c)}{2} \ln |x| + C
$$

where $c &gt; 2b$, $b &gt; 2$.
So $k(x)e^{2u(x)} \in M_{0,2 + \delta}^2$, $-1 &lt; \delta &lt; 0$.
Let us consider now

$$
\psi(x) = \ln(1 + |x|^2).
$$

Then $\Delta \psi(x) = 4(1 + |x|^2)^{-2}$ and $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} \Delta \psi(x) dx = 4\pi$.
Hence

(i) $\Delta \psi(x) - k(x) e^{2u(x)} \in M_{0,2 + \delta}^2$, and
(ii) $\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} (\Delta \psi(x) - k(x) e^{2u(x)}) dx = 0.$

Since

$$
\Delta(u + \psi)(x) = \Delta \psi(x) - k(x) e^{2u(x)},
$$

from Theorem 0(c) we have that $v = (u + \psi) \in M_{2,\delta}^2$.
So from Theorem 0(b), $v \in C_0(\mathbb{R}^2)$.
Thus if $\overline{v}(\theta, \phi) = v(x)$, where $x = T(\theta, \phi)$ then

$$
\Delta_{S^2} \bar{v} + \frac{k^*(\theta, \phi)}{4} e^{2\bar{v}} = 1 \quad \text{on } S^2 - \{(0, 0, 1)\}
$$

and

$$
\bar{v} \text{ is continuous at } (0, 0, 1).
$$

Thus from standard regularity results we then have that

$$
\Delta_{S^2} \bar{v} + \frac{k^*(\theta, \phi)}{4} e^{2\bar{v}} = 1 \quad \text{on } S^2. \tag{4.3}
$$

But from formulae (8.11) in [K-W, 1], (4.3) has no solution, a contradiction.

# On the torsion subgroups of elliptic curves over totally real fields

S.
Kamienny\*

Department of Mathematics, Ohio State University, Columbus Ohio 43210, USA

# § 1. Introduction

It is a well known conjecture that for any number field $K$ there is a uniform bound on the order of the torsion subgroup of the Mordell-Weil group of any elliptic curve $E$ defined over $K$ .
When $K$ is a totally real field we prove this conjecture for a certain collection of elliptic curves, including all elliptic curves obtained by base changing an elliptic curve over $\mathbf{Q}$ .
More precisely, we prove that if $K$ is a totally real number field there is a finite set $S_{k}$ of prime numbers such that if $E$ is a $G$ -curve (see §2) with a $K$ -rational point of order $p$ then $p \in S_{K}$ .
We combine this result with a marvelous theorem of Manin [6] to conclude that there is a uniform bound on the order of the torsion subgroup of any $G$ -curve over the totally real field $K$ .
For some of the primes $p \in S_{K}$ we can actually make Manin's theorem explicit, that is, we can calculate the largest power $p^n$ that can occur as the order of a $K$ -rational torsion point on a $G$ -curve.

# § 2. Modular curves and $G$ -curves

Let $p$ be a prime number $\geq 11$ , and let $Y_0(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ denote the affine curve whose $K$ -rational points classify $\bar{K}$ -isomorphism classes of pairs $(E, C)_{/K}$ where $E_{/K}$ is an elliptic curve and $C$ is a $K$ -rational subgroup of $E$ of order $p$ .
Denote by $X_0(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ the complete curve obtained by adjoining the (rational) cusps 0 and $\infty$ to $Y_0(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ .
Similarly, let $Y_1(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ be the curve whose $K$ -rational points classify isomorphism classes of pairs $(E, P)_{/K}$ where $E_{/K}$ is an elliptic curve, and $P$ is a $K$ -rational $p$ -torsion point of $E$ .
Finally, let $X_1(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ denote the complete curve obtained from $Y_1(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ by adjoining the $p - 1$ cusps.
The curve $Y_1(p)$ is naturally a cyclic cover of $Y_0(p)$ of degree $\frac{p - 1}{2}$ .
The covering map is given by sending an

S.
Kamienny

elliptic curve $E$ and a point $\underline{P}$ to the elliptic curve $E$ and the subgroup $\langle \underline{P} \rangle$ generated by that point.
The covering map extends to a map $\Pi: X_1(p) \to X_0(p)$ that is unramified at the cusps.

Let $\ell$ be a prime different from $p$.
If we reduce the curves $X_{1}(p)$, $X_{0}(p) \bmod \ell$ then the reduced curves retain their interpretations as moduli schemes in characteristic $\ell$.

We denote by $J_0(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ the jacobian of $X_0(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$.
The abelian variety $J_0(p)$ is semi-stable and has good reduction at all primes $\ell$ different from $p$.
If we embed $X_0(p)$ into $J_0(p)$ then the class of $0 - \infty$ generates a $\mathbf{Q}$-rational subgroup $C$ of $J_0(p)$ of order the numerator of $\left(\frac{p - 1}{12}\right)$.
Mazur [7] has shown that $C$ is the torsion subgroup of $J_0(p)(\mathbf{Q})$.
Let $\tilde{J}_{/\mathbf{Q}}$ denote the Eisenstein quotient (see [7], §II.10) of $J_0(p)_{/\mathbf{Q}}$.
Under the natural projection $J_0(p) \to \tilde{J}$ the group $C$ is mapped isomorphically onto the Mordell-Weil group $\tilde{J}(\mathbf{Q})$.

Let $K$ be a number field, and let $K_{G}$ be the smallest extension of $K$ that is galois over $\mathbf{Q}$.
Let $G = \operatorname{Gal}(K_{G} / \mathbf{Q})$, and let $d = [K_{G} : \mathbf{Q}]$ denote the order of $G$.
Finally, let $E$ be an elliptic curve defined over $K$, and let $E_{/K_{G}}$ denote the base change of $E$ to $K_{G}$.
We say that $E_{/K}$ is a $G$-curve if whenever one prime of $K_{G}$ of residue characteristic $q$ divides the conductor of $E_{/K_{G}}$ then all primes of residue characteristic $q$ divide the conductor of $E_{/K_{G}}$.
In other words, the set of primes dividing the conductor of $E$ is invariant under the action of $G$.
Clearly, any curve defined over $\mathbf{Q}$ is a $G$-curve, as are the $\mathbf{Q}$-curves of Gross [3].

## §3.

Torsion points on $G$-curves over totally real fields

In all that follows we assume that $K$ is a totally real field with integer ring $\mathcal{O}$.
The aim of this section is to prove the following.

Theorem 3.1. Let $K$ be a totally real field.
There is a finite set $S_K$ of prime numbers such that if $E_{/K}$ is a $G$-curve with a $K$-rational point of order $p$ then $p \in S_K$.
Explicitly, the set $S_K$ is the union of the sets $A_K, B_K, C_K, D_K,$ and $F_K$ defined below.

$(A_{K})$ Let $f_{2}, f_{3}$ denote the residue class degree of the primes of $K_{G}$ above 2 and 3. Then $A_{K}$ is the set of all primes $p$ such that $p \leq (1 + \sqrt{2^{f_{2}}})^{2}$ and $p \leq (1 + \sqrt{3^{f_{3}}})^{2}$.

$(B_{K})$ Let $e_{p}$ denote the ramification degree of a prime of $K_{G}$ above $p$.
Then $B_{K}$ is the set of all $p$ such that $6 \cdot e_{p} \geq p - 1$.

$(C_{K})$ $C_{K}$ is the set of all primes $p$ such that $\mathrm{num}\left(\frac{p - 1}{12}\right) \leq d$.

$(D_K)$ $D_K$ is the set of all primes $p$ for which $X_0(p)$ is of genus 0, i.e., $p = 2, 3, 5, 7,$ and 13.

$(F_{K})$ $F_{K}$ is the set of all primes $p$ dividing the order of $K_{2}(\mathcal{O})$ (a finite group by Garland's theorem).

Proof.
The proof is closely modeled after Mazur's proof [7] of Ogg's Conjecture.

On the torsion subgroups of elliptic curves

Replacing $K$ by its galois closure $K_G$ we may assume that $K$ is galois over $\mathbf{Q}$.
Let $(E, \underline{P})$ be a pair consisting of an elliptic curve $E_{/K}$, and a $K$-rational $p$-torsion point $\underline{P}$, and assume that $p \notin S_K$.
Let $F = K(\zeta_p)$, and let $L$ be the field generated over $K$ by the $p$-division points of $E$.
Then either $L = F$ or $L/F$ is cyclic of order $p$.
Moreover, the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(F/K)$ on $\operatorname{Gal}(L/F)$ is via the character $\chi^{-1}$, where $\chi$ is the standard cyclotomic character.
To see the latter statement we examine the representation $R$ giving the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(\bar{K}/K)$ on the module $E[p]$ of $p$-division points of $E$.
For a suitable choice of basis of $E[p]$ the image of $R$ consists of matrices of the form $\begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; a \\ 0 &amp; \chi \end{pmatrix}$.
Now $R(\operatorname{Gal}(K(\zeta_p)/K))$ consists of matrices of the form $\begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; \chi \end{pmatrix}$.
That the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(K(\zeta_p)/K)$ on $\operatorname{Gal}(L/F)$ is via $\chi^{-1}$ now follows from the simple matrix calculation

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; \chi(\ell) \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; a \\ 0 &amp; \chi(e) \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; 0 \\ 0 &amp; \chi^{-1}(\ell) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; \chi^{-1}(\ell)a \\ 0 &amp; \chi(e) \end{pmatrix}.
$$

The pair $(E, \underline{P})$ defines a point $x$ on the modular curve $X_1(p)$.
By abuse of notation we will also write $x$ for the projection $\Pi(x)$ of $x$ to $X_0(p)$.
If $\sigma \in G$ then $x^\sigma = (E^\sigma, \underline{P}^\sigma)$ is again a point of $X_1(p)(K)$ and, by projection, we view $x^\sigma$ as a point of $X_0(p)(K)$.
If $s$ is a prime of $\mathcal{O}$ of residue characteristic $\neq p$, and $x = (E, \langle \underline{P} \rangle) \in X_0(p)(K)$, we denote by $x_{/s}$ the image of $x$ on the reduced curve $X_0(p)_{/(\mathcal{O}/s)}$.
If $s$ is a prime of bad reduction for $E$, and $\sigma \in G$, then $s$ is also a prime of bad reduction of $E^\sigma$, since $E$ is a $G$-curve.
When $s$ is a prime of potentially multiplicative reduction we know that $x_{/s} = \infty_{/s}$ if the point $\underline{P}$ does not specialize (mod $s$) to the connected component of the identity $(E_{/s})^0$.

To see this note that the cusp $\infty_{/s}$ is (by [2] Chap.
VI, §§ 5-6) the generalized elliptic curve $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z} \subset \mathbf{G}_m \times \mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})$.
Thus, the subgroup $\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z}$ giving the $\Gamma_0(p)$ structure is not contained in the connected component of the identity.

We break the proof of the Theorem into several steps.
Let $S = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}$.

(1) $E$ is semi-stable over $S$.

Proof.
Let $E_{/\mathcal{O}_s}$ denote the Néron model of $E_{/K_s}$ over $\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_s$.
By the defining property of Néron models the morphism $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/K_s} \to E_{/K_s}$ extends to a morphism of group schemes $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/K_s} \to E_{/K_s}$.
The image $\langle \underline{P} \rangle_{/K_s}$ of this map is the Zariski closure of $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/K_s}$ in $E_{/K_s}$.
Since $\langle \underline{P} \rangle_{/K_s}$ admits a map from the finite flat group scheme $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/K_s}$, which is an isomorphism on generic fibres, we must have that $\langle \underline{P} \rangle_{/K_s}$ is the finite flat group scheme $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/K_s}$.
For the last statement we are using the fact that Raynaud's theorem [8] applies in our situation since $p \notin B_K$.
It follows immediately that the subgroup generated by $\underline{P}_s$ has order $p$.

If $s$ is a point of additive reduction for $E$ then the index of $(E_{/s})^0$ in $E_{/s}$ is $\leq 4$.
Then the subgroup $\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z}$ must be contained in $(E_{/s})^0$.
Thus, the residue characteristic of $s$ must be $p$.
By Serre-Tate [10] there is an extension $\mathcal{K}$ of $K_s$ whose relative ramification index is $\leq 6$, with the property that $E_{/\mathcal{K}}$ has a semistable Néron model $\mathcal{E}$ over $\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{K}}$, the ring of integers of $\mathcal{K}$.
Then we have a map $E_{/\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{K}}} \xrightarrow{\phi} \mathcal{E}_{/\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{K}}}$ that is an isomorphism on generic fibres (by definition of the Néron model).
However, $\phi$ is zero on the connected component of the special

S.
Kamienny

fibre of $E_{/\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{X}}}$.
Thus, $\phi$ is zero on the special fibre of $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{X}}} \subset E_{/\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{X}}}$.
This is impossible by Raynaud's theorem [8] since the absolute ramification index of the prime of $\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{X}}$ is $\leq 6 \cdot e_p &lt; p - 1$.

(2) Let $\phi_2, \phi_3$ denote primes of $\mathcal{O}$ above 2 and 3. Then $\phi_2, \phi_3$ are primes of bad reduction for $E^{\sigma}$ for each $\sigma \in G$.
Moreover, $P^{\sigma}$ does not specialize mod $\phi_i$ ($i = 2,3$) to the connected component of the identity $(E_{/\phi_i}^{\sigma})^0$.

Proof.
If $\phi_i$ is a prime of good reduction for $E^{\sigma}$ then $E_{/\phi_i}^{\sigma}$ is an elliptic curve over $\mathcal{O} / \phi_i$ containing a rational point of order $p$.
By the Riemann hypothesis for elliptic curves over the finite field $\mathcal{O} / \phi_i$ $p$ must be less than $(1 + \sqrt{2^{f_2}})^2$ when $i = 2$, and less than $(1 + \sqrt{3^{f_3}})^2$ when $i = 3$.
Since $p \notin A_K$ this is impossible.
Thus, $E^{\sigma}$ has multiplicative reduction at $\phi_i$.
Suppose that $P^{\sigma}$ does specialize to $(E_{/\phi_i}^{\sigma})^0$.
Over a quadratic extension $\ell$ of $\mathcal{O} / \phi_i$ we have an isomorphism $(E_{/\ell}^{\sigma})^0 \cong \mathbf{G}_{m/\ell^s}$ so that $p$ divides the cardinality of $\ell^*$.
This is impossible since $p \notin A_K$.

(3) If $s$ is any prime of bad reduction for $E$ then $P$ does not specialize to the connected component of the identity $(E_{/s})^0$.

Proof.
By (2) and its proof we may assume that the residue characteristic of $s$ is not 2, 3, or $p$.
Define a map $g: X_0(p)(K) \to J_0(p)(\mathbf{Q})$ by $g(x) = \sum_{\sigma \in G} x^{\sigma} - d \cdot \infty$.
Let $f: X_0(p)(K) \to \tilde{J}(\mathbf{Q})$ be the composition of $g$ with the natural projection $h: J_0(p) \to \tilde{J}$.
By (2) $x_{/\phi_2}^{\sigma} = \infty_{/\phi_2}$ for each $\sigma$, so we have

$$
f (x) _ {/ \phi_ {2}} = h \left(\sum_ {\sigma} x _ {/ \phi_ {2}} ^ {\sigma} - d \cdot \infty_ {/ \phi_ {2}}\right) = 0,
$$

so $f(x)$ has order a power of 2 (recall that $\tilde{J}(\mathbf{Q})$ is a finite group, so $f(x)$ is a torsion point).
However, $f(x)_{/\phi_3} = 0$ by the same reasoning.
Thus, $f(x)$ has order a power of 3, and so $f(x)$ must be zero.

Now, if $s$ is a prime of bad reduction for which $P$ specializes to $(E_{/s})^0$ then $x_{/s} = 0_{/s}$.
Thus, $f(x)_{/s} = h\left(\sum_{\sigma} x_{/s}^{\sigma} - d \cdot \infty_{/s}\right) = h(t \cdot (0 - \infty))_{/s}$, where $t$ is the number of $\sigma \in G$ such that $P^{\sigma}$ specializes to $(E_{/s}^{\sigma})^0$ (the last equality follows from the assumption that $E$ is a $G$-curve).
In particular, $t \leq d$ so $h(t \cdot (0 - \infty))_{/s} \neq 0$ since $p \notin C_K$.
This contradiction proves (3).

(4) $L / F$ is everywhere unramified.

Proof.
If $E$ has good reduction at $s$, and the residue characteristic of $s$ is different from $p$ then $E[p]$ is an étale finite flat group scheme in an open neighborhood of $s$.
So $L / F$ is unramified at a point above $s$.

If $E$ has good reduction at $s$, and the residue characteristic of $s$ is $p$ then $E[p]$ is a finite flat group scheme over $S_{s}$.
Then there is a short exact sequence of finite flat group schemes over $S_{s}$:

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathbf {Z} / p \mathbf {Z} \rightarrow E [ p ] \rightarrow \mu_ {p} \rightarrow 0.
$$

However, $E[p]$ also fits into a short exact sequence

$$
0 \rightarrow E [ p ] ^ {0} \rightarrow E [ p ] \rightarrow E [ p ] ^ {\acute {e} t} \rightarrow 0,
$$

On the torsion subgroups of elliptic curves

where $E[p]^0$ is the largest connected subgroup of $E[p]$, and $E[p]^{\mathrm{ét}}$ is the largest étale quotient.
Clearly, we must have $E[p]^0 = \mu_p$, and this gives us a splitting of the above exact sequences.
If we let $D$ be a decomposition group for $s$, and identify $D$ with the local Galois group $\operatorname{Gal}(\bar{K}_s / K_s)$, then the splitting of our sequences tells us that $D$ acts diagonally on $E[p]$.
Thus, the action of an $s$-inertia subgroup $I \subset \operatorname{Gal}(\bar{K} / K(\zeta_p))$ on $E[p]$ is trivial, i.e. $L / F$ is unramified at the prime above $s$.

If $E$ has bad reduction at $s$ then $E[p]_{/s}$ is a quasi-finite flat group scheme over $S_s$, and fits into a short exact sequence

$$
0 \rightarrow \mathbf{Z} / p \mathbf{Z} \rightarrow E [ p ] \rightarrow \bar{\mu}_p \rightarrow 0,
$$

where $\bar{\mu}_p$ is a quasi-finite flat group with generic fibre isomorphic to $\mu_p$.
Since $(\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z})_{/s}\oplus (E_{/s})^0$ we see that the kernel of multiplication by $p$ on $(E_{/s})^0$ maps injectively to $\bar{\mu}_p$.
Thus, $\bar{\mu}_p$ is actually a finite flat group scheme.
If $\operatorname{char}s \neq p$ then $E[p]$ is étale, and so $L / F$ is unramified at a point above $s$.
If $\operatorname{char}s = p$ then $\bar{\mu}_p = \mu_p$ by Raynaud's theorem [8] (and the assumption $e_p &lt; p - 1$).
Arguing as above we see that $E[p]_{/S_s} = \mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z} \oplus \mu_p$, so that $L / F$ is unramified at the point above $s$.

(5) $L = K(\zeta_p)$

Proof.
We begin by recalling a result (Theorem 13 of [1]) of Coates about $K_2\mathcal{O}$.
We are indebted to Andrew Wiles for pointing out this theorem.

Theorem.
Let $K_n$ denote the unique sub-extension of $K(\zeta_{p^\infty}) / K(\zeta_p)$ of degree $p^n$ over $K(\zeta_p)$, and let $A_n$ denote the ideal class group of $K_n$.
Let $A(1)$ denote the Tate twist of $A = \varinjlim A_n$.

Let $\mathcal{G}$ denote $\operatorname{Gal}(K(\zeta_{p^\infty}) / K)$.
Then the $p$-primary component of $K_2\mathcal{O}$ is isomorphic to $A(1)^{\mathcal{G}}$.
In particular, the unramified $\chi^{-1}p^n$-extensions of $K(\zeta_{p^n})$ are classified by the kernel of multiplication by $p^n$ on $K_2\mathcal{O}$.
Applying this when $n = 1$, and using the assumption that $p \notin F_K$, we see that $L = F$.

Since $L = F$ we have that $E[p] = \mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z} \oplus \mu_p$, where $\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z}$ is the group generated by $P$.
Let $E^{(1)} = E / \mu_p$.
The image $P^{(1)}$ of $P$ in $E^{(1)}$ is a $K$-rational point on $E^{(1)}$.
Applying the above arguments to $(E^{(1)}, P^{(1)})$ we see that $E^{(1)}[p] = \mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z} \oplus \mu_p$, where this $\mathbf{Z} / p\mathbf{Z}$ is the group generated by $P^{(1)}$.
Inductively we define a chain of elliptic curves over $K$, each with a $K$-rational point of order $p$:

$$
E \rightarrow E ^ {(1)} \rightarrow E ^ {(2)} \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow E ^ {(i + 1)} = E ^ {(i)} / \mu_ {p} \rightarrow \dots .
$$

All of the curves of this chain are isogenous, and so have good reduction at the same set of primes.
By Shafarevitch's theorem (see [9], Chap.
IV, 1.4) some of these curves must be isomorphic.
Then $E^{(i)} \approx E^{(j)}$ for some pair of indices $i, j$.
However, the map $E^{(i)} \xrightarrow{\alpha} E^{(j)}$ has kernel that is of multiplicative type, so $\alpha$ cannot be multiplication by a scalar.
Then $E^{(i)}$ is a C.M.
curve and since it is semi-stable, it must have everywhere good reduction, contradicting (2).
Thus, if $p \notin S_K$ then no $G$-curve defined over $K$ can contain a $K$-rational point of order $p$.

S.
Kamienny

Corollary 3.2. Let $K$ be a totally real field.
There is a uniform bound on the order of the torsion subgroup of the Mordell-Weil group of any $G$-curve over $K$.

Proof.
Manin [6] has shown that, for any number field $K$, there is, for each $p$, a bound on the order of the $K$-rational $p$-power torsion in any elliptic curve over $K$.
Combining this with Theorem 3.1 we obtain Corollary 3.2.

§ 4. Making the Uniform Bound Explicit

In this section we assume that $p$ divides the order of $K_2\mathcal{O}$, and that $p$ is not one of the primes contained in $A_K \cup B_K \cup C_K \cup D_K$.
We prove the following.

Theorem 4.1. Let $p^n$ be the largest power of $p$ occurring among the orders of the cyclic subgroups of $K_2\mathcal{O}$.
If $E$ is a $G$-curve with a $K$-rational point $\underline{P}$ or order $p^m$ then $m \leq n$.

Proof.
As in the proof of (3.1) we see that $E$ is semi-stable with bad reduction at $\neq_{2}$ and $\neq_{3}$.
Let $R \colon \operatorname{Gal}(\bar{K}/K) \to \operatorname{GL}_{2}(\mathbf{F}_{p^{m}})$ be the representation giving the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(\bar{K}/K)$ on $E[p^{m}]$.
Then the image of $R$ consists of matrices of the form $\begin{pmatrix} 1 &amp; a \\ 0 &amp; \chi \end{pmatrix}$.

We may assume that $a \neq 0 (\bmod p)$.

(If $a \equiv 0 (\bmod p)$ then $E[p]$ has a submodule isomorphic to $\mu_p$.
Dividing $E$ by this $\mu_p$ we obtain an isogenous elliptic curve $E^{(1)}$ with a $K$-rational point of order $p^m$.
If $E^{(1)}$ has a $\mu_p$ submodule then we define an elliptic curve $E^{(2)} = E^{(1)} / \mu_p$.
Continuing in this manner we see (as in the conclusion of the proof of (3.1)) that either $E$ is a C.M.
curve, or that, for some $i$, the curve $E^{(i)}$ has no $\mu_p$ submodule.
Since $E^{(i)}$ is semi-stable with bad reduction at $\neq_2$ we see that $E^{(i)}$ cannot be C.M.
curve.
Thus, if we replace $E$ with $E^{(i)}$ we may assume that $a$ is nonzero (mod $p$).
Letting $F = K(\zeta_{p^m})$ and $L = K(E[p^m])$ we see that $L$ is a cyclic extension of $F$ of degree $p^m$.
Furthermore, the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(F / K)$ on $\operatorname{Gal}(L / F)$ is via the character $\chi^{-1}$.

We claim that $E[p^m]$ is a finite flat group scheme and, arguing as in (4) of the proof of Theorem (3.1) (using Fontaine's theorem ([7], Chap.

1. in place of Raynaud's theorem), this implies that $L / F$ is everywhere unramified.
   By Coates' theorem, and the comments in (5), we have $p^m \leq p^n$.
   To complete the proof we need only show that $E[p^m]$ is a finite flat group scheme.
   To this end let $s$ be a prime of bad reduction for $E$ such that $\langle \underline{P} \rangle$ does not map injectively to the group of connected components of $E_{/s}$.
   Then $p^{m-1} \cdot \underline{P}$ is a point of order $p$ on $E$ that reduces to the connected component of the identity $(E_{/s})^0$, contradicting (3) in the proof of Theorem (3.1).
   Thus, $\langle \underline{P} \rangle_{/s}$ is a subgroup of order $p^m$ of the group of connected components of $E_{/s}$.
   Then the kernel of multiplication by $p^m$ on $(E_{/s})^0$ maps injectively to $E[p^m] / \langle \underline{P} \rangle$, and so this quotient group scheme is the special fibre of a finite flat group scheme over $S_s$.
   It follows that $E[p^m]$ is also a finite flat group scheme over $S_s$.

# Compact Kähler manifolds with nonnegative curvature operator

Huai-Dong Cao$^{1}$ and Bennett Chow$^{2}$

$^{1,2}$ Department of Mathematics, University of California, San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA

## 1. Introduction

Independently, Siu and Yau [9] and Mori [7] gave solutions to the well-known Frankel conjecture.
They proved

**Theorem 1.** If $M$ is a compact Kähler manifold with positive bisectional curvature, then $M$ is biholomorphic to complex projective space $\mathbb{C}P^n$.

The following conjecture then naturally arose.

**Conjecture.** If $M$ is a compact irreducible Kähler manifold with nonnegative bisectional curvature, then $M$ is either a Hermitian symmetric manifold or biholomorphic to $\mathbb{C}P^n$.

The complete classification of compact Kähler manifolds with nonnegative bisectional curvature would then follow from this conjecture and the splitting theorem of Howard, Smyth and Wu [10].

In [8], Siu showed that a compact irreducible Kähler manifold with nonnegative curvature operator is either Hermitian symmetric or has the real cohomology ring of $\mathbb{C}P^n$.
In this paper we show how, by applying a recent theorem of Hamilton [5], one can give a complete classification of compact Kähler manifolds with nonnegative curvature operator.
This proves a special case of the above conjecture since the condition of nonnegative curvature operator is stronger than that of nonnegative bisectional curvature.

First, recall the following

**Definition** [6, 5].
A Riemannian manifold $M$ has nonnegative curvature operator (denoted $Rm \geq 0$) if the symmetric linear map

$$
Rm: \Lambda^2(M)_p \to \Lambda^2(M)_p,
$$

defined by $Rm(\phi)_{ij} = g^{km}g^{ln}R_{ijkl}\phi_{mn}$ where $\phi \in \Lambda^2(M)_p$, has nonnegative eigenvalues for every $p \in M$.
We say that a Kähler manifold has nonnegative curvature operator if the underlying Riemannian manifold has nonnegative curvature

H.-D.
Cao and B.
Chow

operator.
When $M$ is a Kähler manifold, the curvature operator of the underlying Riemannian manifold restricts to the real $(1,1)$-forms, that is, $Rm: \Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M) \to \Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M)$ and $Rm \equiv 0$ on the subspace of $\Lambda^2(M)$ perpendicular to $\Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M)$.
This is equivalent to the fact the Levi-Civita connection of the underlying Riemannian metric on $M$ restricts to the unitary frame bundle.
If we define the Lie bracket on $\Lambda^2(M)$ as in [5], by

$$
[\phi, \psi]_{ij} = g^{kl} \phi_{ik} \psi_{jl} - g^{kl} \psi_{ik} \phi_{jl} \quad \text{where } \phi, \psi \in \Lambda^2(M),
$$

then $\Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M)$ is a Lie subalgebra of $\Lambda^2(M)$ isomorphic to $u(n)$.
We note that the nonnegativity of the curvature operator of a Kähler manifold $M$ is equivalent to the 1-semipositivity in the dual Nakano sense of $M$ (see [8, p. 74]).

## 2. The main theorem

We now restrict ourselves to the case $M$ is a compact Kähler manifold with nonnegative curvature operator.
Following [4] and [2], we can deform the metric $g$ on $M$ by the complexified Hamilton's equation

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} g_{ij} = -2 R_{ij}, \quad g_{ij}(0) = g_{ij} \tag{*}
$$

where $R_{ij} dz^i \otimes d\overline{z}^j$ is the Ricci tensor of $M$.
As proved in [4], under equation (\*), a solution exists for short time and $M$ remains Kähler.
We recall the following theorem of Hamilton [5].

**Theorem 2.** If $M$ is a compact Kähler manifold with $Rm \geq 0$, then under Eq.
(\*), $Rm \geq 0$ as long as the solution exists.
Moreover, for some time interval $0 &lt; t &lt; \delta$, image $(Rm)$ is a Lie subalgebra of $\Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M) \cong u(n)$ invariant under parallel translation and constant in time.

**Remark.** Although Hamilton [5] stated this theorem in the case $M$ is a Riemannian manifold, when $M$ is Kähler, the theorem is obviously still true.

We also recall the following well-known theorem of M.
Berger [3].

**Theorem 3.** If $M$ is an irreducible compact Riemannian manifold of dimension $n$, then $M$ is either a locally symmetric space or has one of the following as its holonomy group:

1. $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ if $n \geq 2$
2. $U(n/2)$ if $n = 2m &gt; 2$
3. $\mathrm{SU}(n/2)$ if $n = 2m &gt; 2$
4. $\mathrm{Sp}(n/4)\mathrm{Sp}(1)$ if $n = 4m &gt; 4$
5. $\mathrm{Sp}(n/4)$ if $n = 4m &gt; 4$
6. $G_2$ if $n = 7$
7. $\mathrm{Spin}(7)$ if $n = 8$
8. $\mathrm{Spin}(9)$ if $n = 16$

Compact Kähler manifolds with nonnegative curvature operator

For the cases 3), 5), 6) and 7), $M$ is Ricci flat and hence flat provided $M$ has nonnegative curvature operator.
Case 8) was proven to be symmetric and hence either the Cayley plane or disk by D.V.
Alekseevskii [1].
If $M$ is Kähler then $M$ is either Ricci flat or has $U(n/2)$ holonomy (see [8]).

We now have the following consequence of Theorems 1, 2 and 3.

Theorem 4. If $M$ is a compact simply connected irreducible Kähler manifold with nonnegative curvature operator, then $M$ is either isometric to a Hermitian symmetric manifold or biholomorphic to complex projective space $\mathbb{C}P^n$.

Proof.
By Theorem 3, $M$ is either a Hermitian symmetric manifold or has holonomy group $U(n)$.
In the latter case we deform the metric $g$ on $M$ by Eq.
$(\ast)$.
Since the property of having $U(n)$ holonomy group is generic among the class of Kähler metrics.
$M$ will still have $U(n)$ holonomy for short time $t \in [0, \varepsilon)$.
By Theorem 2, for some $t_0 \in [0, \varepsilon)$, the curvature operator of $M$ remains nonnegative and image $(Rm)$ is a Lie subalgebra of $\Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M) \cong u(n)$ invariant under parallel translation.
As remarked in [5], at time $t_0$, image $(Rm)$ is isomorphic to the algebra of the restricted holonomy group of $M$.
This follows from Berger's characterization of the holonomy group by the curvature of a manifold, and the fact that image $(Rm)$ is invariant under parallel translation.
Since $M$ is simply connected, the restricted holonomy group agrees with the full holonomy group.
Hence, when the holonomy group of $M$ is $U(n)$, we have at time $t_0$, image $(Rm)_p = \Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M)_p \cong u(n)$ for every $p \in M$.
This, together with the condition $Rm \geq 0$, implies that $Rm &gt; 0$ when restricted to $\Lambda_{\mathbb{R}}^{1,1}(M)$.
Therefore $M$ has positive bisectional curvature.
By the solution of the Frankel conjecture by Siu-Yau and Mori, $M$ is biholomorphic to $\mathbb{C}P^n$ at time $t_0$.
Since Eq.
$(\ast)$ obviously does not change the complex structure, $M$ is biholomorphic to $\mathbb{C}P^n$ at $t = 0$.
This completes the proof of Theorem 4.

Now by the splitting theorem of Howard, Smyth and Wu [10], we have the main theorem of this paper.

Theorem 5. If $M$ is a compact Kähler manifold with nonnegative curvature operator, then there is a holomorphic, locally isometrically trivial fiber bundle $\pi \colon M \to B$ with fiber $M'$.
Moreover, $B$ is a flat complex manifold and $M' = M_1 \times M_2 \times \ldots \times M_k$, where each $M_i$ is either isometric to an irreducible Hermitian symmetric manifold or biholomorphic to complex projective space.

We would like to thank Professors R.L.
Bryant, R.S.
Hamilton and S.T.
Yau for helpful discussions and their interest.
