# Stable semi-groups of measures as commutative approximate identities on non-graded homogeneous groups

Pawel Głowacki

Mathematical Institute, University of Wrocław, pl.
Grunwaldzki 2/4, PL-50-384 Wrocław, Poland and Department of Mathematics, University of Washington, Seattle, WA 98195, USA

In their monograph “Hardy Spaces on Homogeneous Groups” [1] Folland and Stein have raised the question whether on every homogeneous group $N$ there exists a function $\varphi$ in the Schwartz class $\mathcal{S}(N)$ with the following properties:

(i) $\int_{N}\varphi (x)dx = 1,$

(ii) $\varphi_{t}*\varphi_{s} = \varphi_{s}*\varphi_{t},\qquad t,s &gt; 0,$

where $\varphi_t(x) = t^{-Q}\varphi(\delta_{t^{-1}}x)$ is a dilate of $\varphi$.
The function $\varphi$ (or the family $\{\varphi_t\}$) is then called a commutative approximate identity.
They point out the significance of commutative approximate identities by showing that the Hardy spaces $H^p(N)$ can be characterized by means of the maximal function

$$
M _ {\varphi} f (x) = \sup  _ {t &gt; 0} | f * \varphi_ {t} (x) |,
$$

provided $\varphi$ is a commutative approximate identity.

Folland and Stein [1] have constructed a commutative approximate identity in case when the group $N$ is graded, i.e., when the exponents of homogeneity of the dilations can be taken to be rational.
Their commutative approximate identity is made out of a fundamental solution for $\partial_t + L$ on $N \times \mathbb{R}$, where $L$ is a Rockland operator on $N$.
The existence of Rockland operators follows from a theorem of Helffer and Nourrigat [5].
In case of nongraded groups, however, the method cannot be used since there are no Rockland operators on such groups.

The idea to produce a commutative approximate identity as a stable semigroup of smooth measures is due to Hulanicki, where $\varphi \in \mathcal{S}(N)$ is replaced by $\varphi \in \mathcal{R}(N)$, cf.
Folland and Stein [1], p 253. (Out of such a $\varphi$ a commutative approximate identity in $\mathcal{S}(N)$ is produced by Folland and Stein [1], pp 258-260.) This, in fact, can be done.
The construction for the Heisenberg group endowed with an arbitrary family of dilations was carried out in [2], and for the general case this is presented here.

Still, the construction follows the general idea of Folland and Stein [1].
The difference is that the Rockland operator is replaced by an infinitesimal generator of

P.
Głowacki

a stable semi-group of measures.
In [2] our argument depended substantially on a symbolic calculus for pseudo-differential convolution operators on $N$ and a parametrix construction both of which were adaptations from Melin [9].
The main obstruction to extend the procedure from the Heisenberg group to a larger class of groups has been overcome by replacing pseudo-differential operators by singular integral operators, as Professor E.
M.
Stein suggested in a conversation.
Instead of constructing a parametrix we prove a kind of subelliptic estimate for powers of the infinitesimal generator in which we follow Helffer and Nourrigat [4] very closely.
It should be, however, emphasized that our argument is, by far, shorter and less complicated than that of Helffer and Nourrigat [4, 5], as we avoid penetrating into the structure of subalgebras of the Lie algebra of $N$ associated, according to the Kirillov theory, to unitary representations of $N$.
In fact, we make rather modest use of the representation theory of nilpotent Lie groups since the only representations under consideration (except for the right-regular representation) are those induced by unitary characters from the center of $N$.
The reason for this is that we can benefit from the theory of semi-groups of measures on Lie groups.
A theorem of Janssen [8] plays here a decisive role.

## Notation

$\mathbb{R}, \mathbb{Z}$, and $\mathbb{N}$ will denote the sets of real numbers, integers, and positive, i.e., non-negative integers.

Let $N$ be a finite-dimensional real vector space, and let $U$ be its open subset.
$C(U)$ and $C^\infty (U)$ will denote the spaces of (complex-valued) continuous functions and smooth functions on $U$.
Their subspaces consisting of functions with compact support in $U$ will be denoted by $C_c(U)$ and $C_c^\infty (U)$, respectively.

The pairing of a distribution $T$ on $N$ with a smooth function $f$ will be denoted $\langle T, f \rangle$ whenever it makes sense.
For a smooth function $\varphi$ and a distribution $T$ we shall denote by $\varphi T$ the distribution

$$
f \rightarrow \langle T, \varphi f \rangle , \quad f \in C _ {c} ^ {\infty} (N).
$$

The operator of multiplication by a $\varphi \in C^{\infty}(N)$ will be denoted by $M_{\varphi}$.

The Dirac delta function is defined by

$$
\langle \delta , f \rangle = f (0).
$$

The usual norms in $L^1 (N)$ and $L^{\infty}(N)$ will be denoted by $\| \cdot \|_{L^1}$ and $\| \cdot \|_{L^{\infty}}$.
However, the most frequently used, $L^2$-norm will be denoted simply by $\| \cdot \|$.

The commutator of densely defined operators $A$ and $B$ on a Hilbert space will be denoted by $[A, B]$ whenever it makes sense.
The identity operator will be denoted by $I$.

The letter $C$ occurs in inequalities as a positive constant which is independent of the relevant parameters but may vary from statement to statement, even in the same calculation.

Stable semi-groups of measures
559

# 1. Preliminaries

A family of dilations on a nilpotent Lie algebra $N$ is a one-parameter group $\{\delta_t\}_{t &gt; 0}$ of automorphisms of $N$ determined by

$$
\delta_t e_j = t^{d_j} e_j, \tag{1.1}
$$

where $e_1, \ldots, e_n$ is a linear basis for $N$, and $d_1, \ldots, d_n$ are positive real numbers called the exponents of homogeneity.
The smallest $d_j$ is assumed to be 1. If we regard $N$ as a Lie group with multiplication given by the Campbell-Hausdorff formula

$$
xy = x + y + \frac{1}{2}[x, y] + \dots, \tag{1.2}
$$

then the dilations $\delta_t$ are also automorphisms of the group structure on $N$, and the nilpotent group $N$, equipped with these dilations, is said to be a homogeneous group.
The homogeneous dimension of $N$ is the number $Q$ defined by

$$
d(\delta_t x) = t^Q dx, \quad t &gt; 0,
$$

where $dx$ is a left-invariant Haar measure on $N$.
A distribution $T$ on $N$ is homogeneous of degree $r \in \mathbb{R}$ if

$$
\langle T, f_t \rangle = t^r \langle T, f \rangle \tag{1.3}
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$ and $t &gt; 0$, where

$$
f_t(x) = t^{-Q} f(\delta_{t^{-1}} x), \quad x \in N. \tag{1.4}
$$

We choose and fix once for all a homogeneous norm on $N$, that is, a continuous, positive, and symmetric function $x \to |x|$ which is, moreover, smooth on $N \setminus \{0\}$, homogeneous of degree 1, and which vanishes only for $x = 0$.
(The group identity of $N$ is denoted by 0, and will be referred to as the origin.) Any homogeneous norm satisfies the triangle inequality:

$$
|xy| \leq C(|x| + |y|), \quad x, y \in N, \tag{1.5}
$$

with a constant $C &gt; 0$.

Let

$$
X_j f(x) = \frac{d}{dt} \big|_{t = 0} f(x \cdot t e_j), \tag{1.6}
$$

$$
Y_j f(x) = \frac{d}{dt} \big|_{t = 0} f(t e_j \cdot x)
$$

be, respectively, left- and right-invariant basic vector fields.
We apply the usual notation for higher order derivatives.
If $I = (i_1, \ldots, i_n) \in \mathbb{N}^n$ is a multi-index, we set

$$
X^I f = X_1^{i_1} \dots X_n^{i_n} f, \tag{1.7}
$$

$$
Y^I f = Y_1^{i_1} \dots Y_n^{i_n} f,
$$

and

$$
x^I = x_1^{i_1} \dots x_n^{i_n} \tag{1.8}
$$

P.
Głowacki

for $x = x_{1}e_{1} + \ldots +x_{n}e_{n}\in N$.
We also set

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left| I \right| = i _ {1} d _ {1} + \dots + i _ {n} d _ {n}, \tag {1.9} \\
\left\| I \right\| = i _ {1} + \dots + i _ {n},
\end{array}
$$

and

$$
I! = i _ {1}! i _ {2}! \dots i _ {n}!.
$$

We shall make substantial use of the following Taylor inequality due to Folland and Stein [1], Theorem 1.37.

For a function $f \in C^{\infty}(N), r &gt; 0$, and $x \in N$ let us denote

$$
f ^ {(x)} (y) = f (x y) - \sum_ {| I | \leq r} \frac {1}{I !} X ^ {I} f (x) y ^ {I}, \quad y \in N. \tag {1.10}
$$

(1.11) Proposition.
Let $r &gt; 0$.
There exist constants $C, K &gt; 0$ such that for every $f \in C^{\infty}(N)$ and every $x, y \in N$, $|y| \leq 1$,

$$
\left| f ^ {(x)} (y) \right| \leq C f ^ {(r)} (x) | y | ^ {\bar {r}},
$$

where $\bar{r}$ denotes the smallest number $s$ greater than $r$ with the property that $s = |I|$ for some $I \in \mathbb{N}^n$, and

$$
f ^ {(r)} (x) = \sum_ {I \in W} \sup  _ {| z | \leq K} \left| X ^ {I} f (x z) \right|, \tag {1.12}
$$

where

$$
W = \left\{I \in \mathbb {N} ^ {n}: r &lt;   | I |, \| I \| \leq [ r ] + 1 \right\}.
$$

For $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ let

$$
B _ {\varepsilon} = \left\{x \in N: | x | &lt;   \varepsilon \right\}.
$$

A distribution $T$ which is regular, i.e., smooth away from the origin and satisfies

$$
\langle T, f \circ \delta_ {t} \rangle = t ^ {r} \langle T, f \rangle , f \in C _ {c} ^ {\infty} (N), \quad t &gt; 0, \tag {1.13}
$$

for some $r \in \mathbb{R}$ will be called a kernel of order $r \cdot A$ regular distribution $T$ supported in the unit ball $B_{1}$ which coincides with a kernel of order $r$ in a neighbourhood of the origin will be called a truncated kernel of order $r$.
Thus, according to (1.3) and (1.4), a kernel of order $r$ is homogeneous of degree $-Q - r$.
Let us also remark that if $T$ is a kernel of order $r$, then

$$
T _ {I} = x ^ {I} T \tag {1.14}
$$

is a kernel of order $r - |I|$.
It follows from Proposition (1.11) that if $T$ is a kernel of order $r &gt; 0$, then for $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$

$$
\langle T, f \rangle = \langle D, f \rangle + \lim  _ {\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \int_ {| x | \geq \varepsilon} \left(f (x) - \sum_ {| I | &lt;   r} \frac {1}{I !} X ^ {I} f (0) x ^ {I}\right) T (x) d x, \tag {1.15}
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

where $D$ is a kernel of order $r$ supported at the origin, that is,

$$
D = \sum_{|I| = r} c_I \delta^{(I)}, \tag{1.16}
$$

where $\delta^{(I)} = X^I \delta$.

Let $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(B_1)$ be positive and equal to 1 in a neighbourhood of the origin.
If $T$ is a kernel of order $r &gt; 0$, then for every $0 &lt; \varepsilon \leq 1$ $T$ decomposes to

$$
T = T_\varepsilon + k_\varepsilon, \tag{1.17}
$$

where $T_\varepsilon = (\varphi \circ \delta_{1/\varepsilon}) T$ is a truncated kernel of order $r$ supported in the ball $B_\varepsilon \subseteq B_1$, and $k_\varepsilon$ is a smooth function such that

$$
X^I Y^J k_\varepsilon \in L^1(N) \quad \text{for all} \quad I, J \in \mathbb{N}^n. \tag{1.18}
$$

Note also that, by (1.17), (1.18), if $T_1$ and $T_2$ are kernels of order $r_1 &gt; 0$ and $r_2 &gt; 0$, respectively, then the convolution $T_1 * T_2$ exists, and $T_1 * T_2$ is a kernel of order $r_1 + r_2$.

For a unitary representation $\varrho$ of $N$ on a Hilbert space $H$, we denote by $C^\infty(\varrho)$ the space of $C^\infty$ vectors for $\varrho$.
If $T$ is a compactly supported distribution on $N$, and $f$ is in $C^\infty(\varrho)$, then $\varrho_T f$ defined by

$$
(\varrho_T f, g) = \langle T, \varphi_{f,g} \rangle, \quad g \in H, \tag{1.19}
$$

where $\varphi_{f,g}(x) = (\varrho_x f, g)$, is a vector in $C^\infty(\varrho)$ and so the formula (1.19) defines a linear operator on $H$ with $C^\infty(\varrho)$ for its invariant domain.
Note that (1.19) makes sense also for every kernel $T$ of order $r &gt; 0$ so that, by (1.17), we have

$$
\varrho_T = \varrho_{T_\varepsilon} + \varrho_{k_\varepsilon} \tag{1.20}
$$

for every $0 &lt; \varepsilon \leq 1$.
Consequently, $C^\infty(\varrho)$ is invariant for $\varrho_T$ for $T$ like that.

In particular, the above applies to the right-regular representation

$$
\pi_x f(y) = f(yx) \tag{1.21}
$$

of $N$ on $L^2(N)$.
The space of $C^\infty$ vectors for $\pi$ is equal to

$$
S^\infty(N) = \{f \in C^\infty(N) : X^I f \in L^2(N) \text{ for all } I\}. \tag{1.22}
$$

A family $\{\mu_t\}_{t &gt; 0}$ of positive measures on $N$ is said to be a continuous semi-group of measures if

$$
\mu_t(N) \leq 1, \quad t &gt; 0, \tag{1.23}
$$

$$
\mu_t * \mu_s = \mu_{t+s}, \quad t, s &gt; 0, \tag{1.24}
$$

$$
\lim_{t \to 0} \langle \mu_t, f \rangle = f(0), \quad f \in C_c^\infty(N). \tag{1.25}
$$

If $\{\mu_t\}$ is a continuous semi-group of measures on $N$, then there exists a distribution $P$ on $N$, called the generating functional of $\{\mu_t\}$, such that

$$
\langle P, f \rangle = \lim_{t \to 0} \frac{1}{t} \langle \mu_t - \delta, f \rangle \tag{1.26}
$$

P.
Głowacki

for $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$.
Recall that on $N \setminus \{0\}$ $P$ coincides with a positive Radon measure which is called the Lévy measure of the semi-group $\{\mu_t\}$.
It is easily seen that $P$ is dissipative, that is,

$$
\langle P, f \rangle \leq 0 \tag{1.27}
$$

for real $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$ such that $\sup f(x) = f(0)$.
This is also a sufficient condition for a real distribution $P$ to be a generating functional of a continuous semi-group of measures, and the semi-group is unique.

A continuous semi-group $\{\mu_t\}$ of measures on $N$ is said to be stable (with exponent 1) if

$$
\langle \mu_t, f \rangle = \langle \mu_1, f \circ \delta_t \rangle \tag{1.28}
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$ and $t &gt; 0$.
This means precisely that the generating functional of $\{\mu_t\}$ is homogeneous of degree $-Q - 1$.
It follows immediately from (1.25) and (1.28) that a stable semi-group of measures consists of probability measures.
Note also that the Lévy measure of a stable semi-group of measures on $N$ is smooth, that is, has a smooth density with respect to the Haar measure if and only if the generating functional of the semi-group is a kernel of order 1.

For all the above, concerning continuous semi-groups of measures on Lie groups, the reader is referred to Hunt [7] and e.g. Hulanicki [6].

## 2. Statement of the main result

A function $h \in C^\infty(N)$ belongs to $\mathcal{R}(N)$ if it satisfies

$$
|X^I h(x)| \leq C_I (1 + |x|)^{-Q - 1 - |I|}, \tag{2.1}
$$

$$
|(d/dt)^j X^I h_t(x)| \leq C_{I,j} (t + |x|)^{-Q - |I| - j} \tag{2.2}
$$

for $x \in N, j = 1, 2, \ldots, t &gt; 0$, and all $I \in \mathbb{N}^n$ (cf.
Folland and Stein [1], Chap.
8.B).

We now state our main result.

(2.3) **Theorem.** Let $\{\mu_t\}$ be a non-trivial stable semi-group of symmetric measures on $N$.
If the Lévy measure of $\{\mu_t\}$ is smooth, then there exists a function $h \in \mathcal{R}(N)$ such that

$$
\mu_t(dx) = h_t(x) \, dx
$$

for $t &gt; 0$.
Therefore $h$ is a commutative approximate identity in $\mathcal{R}(N)$.

By (1.15), (1.27), and (1.28), a distribution $P$ is a generating functional of a stable semi-group of symmetric measures with smooth Lévy measure if and only if $P$ is of the form

$$
\langle P, f \rangle = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \int_{|x| \geq \varepsilon} \frac{f(x) - f(0)}{|x|^{Q + 1}} \, \Omega(\delta_{|x|^{-1}} x) \, dx, \tag{2.4}
$$

where $\Omega$ is a positive symmetric $C^\infty$ function on the unit sphere

$$
\Sigma = \{x \in N: |x| = 1\}.
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

Therefore, by Theorem (2.3), any non-zero $\Omega$ like that gives a rise to a commutative approximate identity.

The essential step in proving Theorem (2.3) is the following result.

(2.5) Theorem.
Let $P \neq 0$ be the generating functional of a stable semi-group $\{\mu_t\}$ of symmetric measures.
If the Lévy measure of $\{\mu_t\}$ is smooth, then for every $r &gt; 0$, every kernel $T$ of order $r$, and every integer $m \geq r$ there exists a constant $C$ such that

$$
\left\| \pi_ {T} f \right\| \leq C \left(\left\| \pi_ {P} ^ {m} f \right\| + \left\| f \right\|\right) \tag {2.6}
$$

for $f\in C_c^\infty (N)$

# 3. Basic estimates for the infinitesimal generator

The objective of this section is to prove Theorem (2.5).
The estimates of Theorem (2.5) are very similar to those obtained by Helffer and Nourrigat [4, 5] for Rockland operators.
Our proof is, in fact, an adaptation of some parts of these papers.
The main idea is still to combine the technique of extending estimates by continuity of [4, 5] (cf.
Theorem (3.19) below) with a lemma of Riemann-Lebesgue type (cf.
Lemma (3.29) below).
For the sake of completeness we repeat much of the corresponding argument of Helffer and Nourrigat [4] in detail.

Denote the center of $N$ by $V$, and choose a linear complement $\bar{N}$ to $V$ invariant under the action of the dilations.
Then the corresponding projections:

$$
\sigma \colon N \to \bar {N}, \quad v \colon N \to V
$$

commute with the dilations, and every $x \in N$ can be represented as

$$
x = v (x) + \sigma (x) = v (x) \sigma (x) = \sigma (x) v (x) \tag {3.0}
$$

in a unique way.
It is fairly obvious that $\bar{N}$ with the multiplication $x\circ y = \sigma (xy)$ is a group isomorphic to $N / V$ with $\sigma$ being the canonical homomorphism.
Of course, $\bar{N}$ is then a homogeneous group with the dilations $x\to \delta_t x$, and $x\rightarrow |x|$ is a homogeneous norm on $\bar{N}$.

It is an exercise to show, by using (1.5), that there is a constant $C$ such that

$$
| x | \leq C (| \sigma (x y) | + | y |) \tag {3.1}
$$

for $x\in \bar{N}$, $y\in N$

For a functional $\xi \in \mathbf{V}^*$ we shall denote by $\pi^{\xi}$ the unitary representation of $N$ induced by the character

$$
z \rightarrow e ^ {i \langle z, \xi \rangle}
$$

from the center $V$ of $N$.
$\pi^{\xi}$ can be realized on $L^2 (\bar{N})$ by

$$
\begin{array}{l} \pi_ {a} ^ {\xi} f (x) = e ^ {i \langle v (x a), \xi \rangle} f (\sigma (x a)) \\ = e ^ {i \langle v (x a), \xi \rangle} f (x \circ \sigma (a)), \\ \end{array}
$$

where $a \in N$, $x \in \bar{N}$, and $f \in L^2(\bar{N})$.
Note that $C_c^\infty(\bar{N}) \subseteq C^\infty(\pi^\xi)$ for every $\xi \in V^*$.

P.
Głowacki

For $r &gt; 0$, let $F_r \in L^1(N)$ be defined by

$$
F_r(a) = |a|^{-Q - r + \bar{r}} \chi_{B_1}(a), \quad a \in N,
$$

where $\chi_{B_1}$ is the characteristic function of the unit ball $B_1$, and $\bar{r} &gt; r$ is as in Proposition (1.11).

(3.2) Proposition.
Let $T$ be a truncated kernel of order $r &gt; 0$ and let $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$.
Then there exists a $0 \leq \Psi \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$ such that for every $\xi \in V^*$ and every $f \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$

$$
[\pi_T^\xi, M_\varphi]f = \sum_{0 &lt; |I| \leq r} \frac{1}{I!} M_{\varphi_I} \pi_{T_I}^\xi f + H_{T,\varphi} f,
$$

where $\varphi_I = \pi_{\delta^{(I)}}^0 \varphi$ and

$$
|H_{T,\varphi} f(x)| \leq \Psi(x) \pi_{F_r}^0 |\Psi f|(x), \quad x \in \bar{N}. \tag{3.3}
$$

Proof.
Fix $\xi \in V^*$ and $f \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$, and let

$$
F(a) = e^{(\langle v(a), \xi \rangle} f(\sigma(a)), \quad a \in N. \tag{3.4}
$$

We have

$$
\pi_T^\xi f(x) = \langle T, \lambda_x F \rangle, \tag{3.5}
$$

where $\lambda_x F(a) = F(xa)$.
Therefore, if $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_T^\xi (\varphi f)(x) &amp;= \langle T, \lambda_x (\varphi \circ \sigma) \lambda_x F \rangle \\
&amp;= \sum_{|I| \leq r} \frac{1}{I!} X^I (\varphi \circ \sigma)(x) \langle T_I, \lambda_x F \rangle + \langle T, (\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(x)} \lambda_x F \rangle,
\end{aligned}
$$

where $(\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(x)}$ is the error term in the Taylor expansion of $\varphi \circ \sigma$ about $x$ (cf.
(1.10)).
Thus

$$
\pi_T^\xi M_\varphi f = M_\varphi \pi_T^\xi f + \sum_{0 &lt; |I| \leq r} \frac{1}{I!} M_{\varphi_I} \pi_{T_I}^\xi f + H_{T,\varphi} f,
$$

and it remains to estimate

$$
H_{T,\varphi} f(x) = \langle T, (\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(x)} \lambda_x F \rangle.
$$

By Proposition (1.11),

$$
|\varphi \circ \sigma^{(x)}(a)| \leq C |a|^{\bar{r}} (\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(r)}(x), \quad |a| \leq 1, \tag{3.6}
$$

where $(\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(r)}$ is defined by (1.12).
Due to (3.1), $x$ remains in a bounded set when $\sigma(xz)$ belongs to the support of $\varphi$ and $|z| \leq K$, which implies, by (1.12), that $(\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(r)}$ vanishes outside a compact set.
Since it is also bounded, a simple application of (1.5) shows that there is a $\Psi$ in $C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$ such that

$$
(\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(r)}(x) \leq \Psi(\sigma(xa)) \Psi(x) \tag{3.7}
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

for $x \in \tilde{N}$, $a \in N$, $|a| \leq 1$.
Hence, by Proposition (1.11) and (1.15),

$$
\begin{array}{l}
H_{T, \varphi} f(x) \leq C \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \Psi(x) \int_{\varepsilon \leq |a| \leq 1} \Psi(\sigma(xa)) \varphi(\sigma(xa)) |a|^{\bar{r}} |T(a)| \, da \\
\leq C \Psi(x) \int_{|a| \leq 1} (\Psi f)(\sigma(xa)) |a|^{-Q - r + \bar{r}} \, da,
\end{array}
$$

which implies (3.3) for some larger $\Psi$.

The above proof is valid, in fact, for $V$ being an arbitrary subgroup of the center of $N$ invariant under dilations.
In particular, by letting $V = \{0\}$, we come to the following version of the above proposition for the right-regular representation $\pi$ of $N$ on $L^2(N)$.

(3.8) Proposition.
If $T$ is a truncated kernel of order $r &gt; 0$ and $\varphi$ is a function in $C_c^\infty(N)$, then

$$
[\pi_T, M_\varphi] f = \sum_{0 &lt; |I| \leq r} \frac{1}{I!} M_{\varphi_I} \pi_{T_I} f + H_{T, \varphi} f
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$, where $H_{T,\varphi}$ is a bounded linear operator on $L^2(N)$.

We define dilations on $V^*$ by

$$
\langle z, \delta_t \xi \rangle = \langle \delta_t z, \xi \rangle
$$

for $z \in V, \xi \in V^*, t &gt; 0$, and we choose a homogeneous norm on $V^*$ (still denoted by $|\cdot|$) with respect to these dilations.
For $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\tilde{N})$ we set

$$
\varphi^t = \varphi \circ \delta_t, \quad t &gt; 0.
$$

(3.9) Remark.
Under the notation of Proposition (3.2), for every $0 &lt; t \leq 1$ we have

$$
|H_{T, \varphi^t} f(x)| \leq t \Psi^t(x) \pi_{F_r}^0 |\Psi^t f|(x), \, x \in \tilde{N}, \, f \in C_c^\infty(\tilde{N}).
$$

Proof.
In fact, by (3.7) and (1.12),

$$
\begin{array}{l}
(\varphi^t \circ \sigma)^{(r)}(x) \leq t (\varphi \circ \sigma)^{(r)}(\delta_t x) \\
\leq t \Psi(\delta_t x) \Psi(\sigma(\delta_t x \cdot \delta_t a)) = t \Psi^t(x) \Psi^t(\sigma(xa))
\end{array}
$$

for $x \in \tilde{N}$, $a \in N$, $|a| \leq 1$, and $0 &lt; t \leq 1$.

Now we can follow the argument of the proof of Proposition (3.2).

(3.10) Corollary.
Let $T$ and $\varphi$ be as above.
There exists a function $\Psi \in C_c^\infty(\tilde{N})$ and a constant $C &gt; 0$ such that for every $0 &lt; t \leq 1$ and every $|\xi| \leq t$ we have

$$
\left\| \pi_T^\xi \varphi^t f - \pi_T^0 \varphi^t f \right\| \leq Ct \left( \sum_{\substack{0 &lt; |I| \leq r \\ 0 \leq |J| \leq r}} \| (\varphi_J)^t \pi_{T_I}^\xi f \| + \| \Psi^t f \| \right)
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(\tilde{N})$.

P.
Głowacki

Proof.
Let

$$
\mathcal {V} _ {\xi} (a) = e ^ {- i \langle v (a), \xi \rangle}
$$

for $\xi \in V^{*}$ and $a\in N$ .
Then for $f\in C_c^\infty (\bar{N})$ and $x\in \bar{N}$

$$
\pi_ {T} ^ {\xi} f (x) - \pi_ {T} ^ {0} f (x) = \langle T, \lambda_ {x} (1 - \mathcal {V} _ {\xi}) \lambda_ {x} F \rangle ,
$$

where $F$ is defined by (3.4).
By expanding $\mathcal{V}_{\xi}$ in a Taylor expansion about $x$ , we get

$$
\pi_ {T} ^ {\xi} f (x) - \pi_ {T} ^ {0} f (x) = \sum_ {0 &lt;   | I | \leq r} \frac {1}{I !} \left(X ^ {I} \mathcal {V} _ {\xi}\right) (x) \pi_ {T _ {I}} ^ {\xi} f (x) + K _ {T} f (x), \tag {3.11}
$$

where

$$
K _ {T} f (x) = \left\langle T, \mathcal {V} _ {\xi} ^ {(x)} \lambda_ {x} F \right\rangle , \tag {3.12}
$$

and $\mathcal{V}_{\xi}^{(x)}$ is the remainder term in the expansion (cf.
(1.10)).

Now, let $\varphi \in C_c^\infty (\bar{N})$ .
Since the function

$$
(x, \xi) \rightarrow X ^ {I} \mathcal {V} _ {\xi} (x)
$$

is continuous and satisfies

$$
X ^ {I} \mathcal {V} _ {\delta_ {I} \xi} (x) = t ^ {| I |} X ^ {I} \mathcal {V} _ {\xi} (\delta_ {I} x),
$$

there exists a constant $C &gt; 0$ such that for every $t &gt; 0$ , $|\xi| \leq t$ , and $x$ in the support of $\varphi^t$

$$
\left| X ^ {I} \mathcal {V} _ {\xi} (x) \right| \leq C t ^ {| I |}, \quad I \in \mathbb {N} ^ {n}. \tag {3.13}
$$

Therefore, by Proposition (3.2) and Remark (3.9), there exist a $|\varphi| \leq \Psi \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$ and a constant $C &gt; 0$ such that for every $0 &lt; t \leq 1$

$$
\left\| \left(X ^ {I} \mathcal {V} _ {\xi}\right) \pi_ {T _ {I}} ^ {\xi} \left(\varphi^ {t} f\right) \right\| \leq C t \left(\sum_ {0 \leq | J | \leq r - | I |} \left\| \left(\varphi_ {J}\right) ^ {t} \pi_ {T _ {I} + J} ^ {\xi} f \right\| + \left\| \Psi^ {t} H _ {T, \varphi^ {t}} f \right\|\right)
$$

for $f\in C_c^\infty (\bar{N})$ $|\xi |\leq t$ , and $0 &lt;   |I|\leq r$ .
But, by Remark (3.9),

$$
\left\| H _ {T, \varphi^ {t}} f \right\| \leq C \| \Psi^ {t} f \|
$$

so that, in view of (3.11), it remains to estimate $\| K_T(\varphi^t f)\|$ .
To this end, notice that there exist a positive $\chi \in C_c^\infty (\bar{N})$ such that

$$
\varphi (\sigma (x a)) = \chi (x) \varphi (\sigma (x a))
$$

for $x\in \bar{N}$ $a\in N$ $|a|\leq 1$ , and a constant $C &gt; 0$ such that for every $0 &lt;   t\leq 1$

$$
\mathcal {V} _ {\delta_ {I} \xi} ^ {(r)} (x) \leq C t \tag {3.14}
$$

if $|\xi| \leq t$ and $x \in \sup \chi^t$ (cf.
(3.13) and (1.12)).

Now, by (3.12), we have for $x \in \bar{N}$ and $f \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$

$$
\left| K _ {T} \left(\varphi^ {t} f\right) (x) \right| \leq C t \chi^ {t} (x) \mathcal {V} _ {\xi} ^ {(r)} (x) \int_ {| a | \leq 1} \left| \varphi^ {t} f (\sigma (x a)) \right| | a | ^ {- Q - r + \bar {r}} d a,
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

which, by (3.14), implies the desired estimate

$$
\| K _ {T} (\varphi^ {t} f) \| \leq C \| \varphi^ {t} f \| \leq C \| \Psi^ {t} f \|.
$$

The following lemma is due to Helffer and Nourrigat (cf.
[5], Lemme 4.5.2).

(3.15) Lemma.
Let $N$ be a homogeneous group.
There exist a uniformly discrete subset $\Gamma$ of $N$ and a function $\varphi$ in $C_c^\infty (N)$ such that

$$
\sum_ {z \in \Gamma} | \lambda_ {z} \varphi | ^ {2} = 1. \tag {3.16}
$$

Proof.
When $N$ is abelian, this is well-known.
Suppose by induction, that it holds for the groups $\tilde{N}$ and $V$, as described at the beginning of this section, and let $\varphi_1, \varphi_2$ and $\Gamma_1, \Gamma_2$ be the corresponding functions and sets (respectively).
We set

$$
\varphi (a) = \varphi_ {1} (\sigma (a)) \varphi_ {2} (v (a)), \quad a \in N,
$$

and $\Gamma = \Gamma_1\cdot \Gamma_2$.
Then $\varphi \in C_c^\infty (N)$ and $\varGamma$ is a uniformly discrete set, since $\varGamma_{1}$ is.
Moreover, by (3.0),

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sum_ {u \in \Gamma} \varphi (u a) = \sum_ {u _ {1} \in \Gamma_ {1}} \sum_ {u _ {2} \in \Gamma_ {2}} \varphi_ {1} \left(u _ {1} \circ \sigma (a)\right) \varphi_ {2} \left(u _ {2} + v (a) + v \left(u _ {1} \sigma (a)\right)\right) \\ = \sum_ {u _ {1} \in \Gamma_ {1}} \varphi_ {1} (u _ {1} \circ \sigma (a)) \sum_ {u _ {2} \in \Gamma_ {2}} \varphi_ {2} (u _ {2} + v (a)) = 1, \\ \end{array}
$$

for every $a\in N$.

For $\xi \in V^{*}$ and $f\in C_c^\infty (\tilde{N})$ let

$$
\lambda_ {a} ^ {\xi} f (x) = e ^ {i \langle v (a x), \xi \rangle} f (\sigma (a x)), \quad a \in N, x \in \tilde {N}.
$$

(3.17) Lemma.
Let $\xi \in V^{*}$ and $a, b \in N$.
Then for every $f \in C_c^\infty(\tilde{N})$

Proof.
Since

$$
\lambda_ {a} ^ {\xi} \pi_ {b} ^ {\xi} f = \pi_ {b} ^ {\xi} \lambda_ {a} ^ {\xi} f.
$$

$$
\lambda_ {a} ^ {\xi} \pi_ {b} ^ {\xi} f (x) = e ^ {i \langle v (a x) + v (\sigma (a x) b), \xi \rangle} f (\sigma (a) \circ x \circ \sigma (b))
$$

and

$$
\pi_ {b} ^ {\xi} \lambda_ {a} ^ {\xi} f (x) = e ^ {i \langle v (x b) + v (a \sigma (x b)), \xi \rangle} f (\sigma (a) \circ x \circ \sigma (b)),
$$

it is sufficient to show that

$$
v (a x) + v (\sigma (a x) b) = v (x b) + v (a \sigma (x b))
$$

for $a,b\in N$, $x\in \tilde{N}$.

In fact, by (3.0),

$$
a x b = (a x) b = v (a x) \sigma (a x) b = v (a x) v (\sigma (a x) b) \sigma (\sigma (a x) b)
$$

and, since $v$ is a projection on the center,

$$
a x b = a (x b) = a v (x b) \sigma (x b) = v (x b) a \sigma (x b) = v (x b) v (a \sigma (x b)) \sigma (a \sigma (x b)).
$$

P.
Głowacki

Therefore,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
v(ax) + v(\sigma(ax)b) = v(ax)v(\sigma(ax)b) \\
= v(axb) = v(xb)v(a\sigma(xb)) = v(xb) + v(a\sigma(xb)),
\end{array}
$$

which proves the lemma.
$\square$

We shall need the following result for singular integral operators.

(3.18) **Lemma.** For any truncated kernel $T$ of order 0 there exists a constant $C &gt; 0$ such that for every unitary representation $\varrho$ of $N$ and every $f \in C^{\infty}(\bar{N})$

$$
\|\varrho_T f\| \leq C \|f\|.
$$

**Proof.** See Goodman [3].
$\square$

(3.19) **Theorem.** Let $P$ be a kernel of order $r &gt; 0$ such that for every kernel $T$ of order $0 &lt; s \leq r$ there exists a constant $C$ such that

$$
\|\pi_T^0 f\| \leq C(\|\pi_P^0 f\| + \|f\|), \quad f \in C_c^{\infty}(\bar{N}).
$$

Then there exists a $0 &lt; t_0 \leq 1$ such that for every kernel $T$ of order $0 &lt; s \leq r$ there is another constant $C &gt; 0$ such that

$$
\|\pi_T^\xi f\| \leq C(\|\pi_P^\xi f\| + \|f\|), \quad f \in C_c^{\infty}(\bar{N}), \tag{3.20}
$$

for every $|\xi| \leq t_0$.

**Proof.** Let $\varphi$ and $\Gamma$ be as in Lemma (3.15).
Then for every $\xi \in V^*$ and every $t &gt; 0$,

$$
\sum_{z \in \Gamma} |\lambda_z^\xi \varphi^t|^2 = \sum_{z \in \Gamma} |(\lambda_z \varphi)^t|^2 = 1, \tag{3.21}
$$

where $z_t = \delta_{t-1}z$.
Moreover, since $\Gamma$ is uniformly discrete,

$$
\sup_{x \in \bar{N}} \sum_{z \in \Gamma} |\lambda_z^\xi f^t(x)|^2 \leq C \tag{3.22}
$$

for every $f \in C_c^{\infty}(\bar{N})$, where $C$ does not depend neither on $\xi$ nor on $t$.

By (1.17) and (1.18) we can assume that all the kernels under consideration are truncated.
For a truncated regular kernel $R$, $f \in C_c^{\infty}(\bar{N})$, $\xi \in V^*$, and $t &gt; 0$ denote

$$
E_t^\xi(R, f)^2 = \left\| [M_{\varphi^t}, \pi_R^\xi] f \right\|^2 + \|\pi_R^\xi(\varphi^t f) - \pi_R^0(\varphi^t f)\|^2.
$$

Then

$$
\|\varphi^t \pi_T^\xi\|^2 \leq C\left(\|\varphi^t \pi_P^\xi f\|^2 + \|\varphi^t f\|^2 + E_t^\xi(T, f)^2 + E_t^\xi(P, f)^2\right)
$$

and thus, by (3.21) and Lemma (3.17),

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\|\pi_T^\xi f\|^2 = \sum_{z \in \Gamma} \|(\lambda_z^\xi \varphi^t) \pi_T^\xi f\|^2 = \sum_{z \in \Gamma} \|\varphi^t \pi_T^\xi(\lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f)\|^2 \\
\leq C \sum_{z \in \Gamma} \left( \|\varphi^t \pi_P^\xi(\lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f)\|^2 + \|\varphi^t \lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f\|^2 \right) \\
+ C \sum_{z \in \Gamma} \left( E_t^\xi(T, \lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f) \right)^2 + E_t^\xi(P, \lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f)^2 \\
= C \left( \|\pi_P^\xi f\|^2 + \|f\|^2 + \sum_{z \in \Gamma} \left( E_t^\xi(T, \lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f) \right)^2 + E_t^\xi(P, \lambda_{z_t^{-1}}^\xi f)^2 \right).
\end{array}
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

It remains to estimate the infinite sum on the right-hand side.
But, by Proposition (3.2), Remark (3.9), and Corollary (3.10),

$$
\begin{array}{l}
E_{t}^{\xi}(T, \lambda_{z_{t-1}}^{\xi} f)^{2} \leq Ct \left(\sum_{\substack{0 &lt; |I| \leq s \\ 0 \leq |J| \leq s}} \| \varphi_{J}^{\prime} \pi_{T_{I}}^{\xi}(\lambda_{z_{t-1}}^{\xi} f) \|^{2} + \| \Psi^{\prime} \lambda_{z_{t-1}}^{\xi} f \|^{2}\right) \\
\leq Ct \left(\sum_{\substack{0 &lt; |I| \leq s \\ 0 \leq |J| \leq s}} \| (\lambda_{z_{t}}^{\xi} \varphi_{J}^{\prime}) \pi_{T_{I}}^{\xi} f \|^{2} + \| \lambda_{z_{t}}^{\xi} \Psi^{\prime} f \|^{2}\right),
\end{array}
$$

and a similar estimate holds for $E_{t}(P, \lambda_{z_{t-1}}^{\xi} f)^{2}$.

Consequently, by (3.22),

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{z \in T} \left(E_{t}^{\xi} (T, \lambda_{z_{t-1}}^{\xi} f)^{2} + E_{t}^{\xi} (P, \lambda_{z_{t-1}}^{\xi} f)^{2} \right. \\
\leq Ct \left(\sum_{0 &lt; |I| \leq s} \| \pi_{T_{I}}^{\xi} f \|^{2} + \sum_{0 &lt; |J| \leq r} \| \pi_{P_{J}}^{\xi} f \|^{2} + \| f \|^{2}\right)
\end{array}
$$

so that

$$
\| \pi_{T}^{\xi} f \| \leq C \left(\| \pi_{P}^{\xi} f \| + \| f \|\right) + Ct \left(\sum_{0 &lt; |I| &lt; s} \| \pi_{T_{I}}^{\xi} f \| + \sum_{0 &lt; |J| &lt; r} \| \pi_{P_{J}}^{\xi} f \|\right) \tag{3.23}
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$, where the terms corresponding to $|I| = s$ and $|J| = r$ (if there are any) are eliminated by using Lemma (3.18).

Now, all the kernels $T_{I}, P_{J}$ are of order lower than $r$ so letting $T = T_{I}, P_{J}$ repeatedly and taking $t$ sufficiently small we can get rid of the sums on the right-hand side in (3.23).
This completes the proof.

(3.24) Remark.
Let $P$ satisfy the hypothesis of Theorem (3.19).
Then for every $M &gt; 0$ and every kernel $T$ of order $0 &lt; s \leq r$ there exists a constant $C &gt; 0$ such that (3.20) holds for $|\xi| \leq M$.

Proof.
This is a consequence of Theorem (3.19) and the following equality valid for any kernel of order $s &gt; 0$

$$
\| \pi_{T}^{\hat{\pi}_{I} \xi} f \| = t^{s - \tilde{Q}/2} \| \pi_{T}^{\xi} f^{t^{-1}} \|, \quad f \in C_{c}^{\infty}(\bar{N}), 0 &lt; t \leq 1, \tag{3.25}
$$

where $\tilde{Q}$ is the homogeneous dimension of $\bar{N}$.

Proof of Theorem (2.5).
We proceed by induction.
Consider first the case when $N = \mathbb{R}^n$.
By (1.17) $P$ is then a tempered distribution whose Fourier transform $\hat{P}$ is a continuous homogeneous function on $\mathbb{R}^n$.
Thus, by the Plancherel theorem, it is sufficient to show that $\hat{P}$ does not vanish except for $\xi = 0$.
In fact, if $\hat{P}(\xi) = 0$ for a $\xi \in \mathbb{R}^n$, then $\hat{\mu}_1(\xi) = 1$, and since $\mu_1$ is a probability measure with $\mathrm{supp}\, \mu_1 = \mathbb{R}^n$, $\xi$ must be zero (cf., e.g., [2], (2.3)).

Now we are going to show that our assertion holds for a homogeneous group $N$ provided it holds for $\bar{N} = N/V$, where $V$ is the center of $N$.
We start with the following easy to prove lemma.

(3.26) Lemma.
If $T$ is a kernel of order $r &gt; 0$ on $N$, then $\tilde{T}$, defined by

$$
\langle \tilde{T}, f \rangle = \langle T, f \circ \sigma \rangle, \quad f \in C_{c}^{\infty}(\bar{N}),
$$

P.
Głowacki

is a kernel of order $r$ on $\bar{N}$.
Moreover,

$$
\tilde{\pi}_{\bar{T}} = \pi_T^0,
$$

where $\tilde{\pi}$ denotes the right-regular representation of $\bar{N}$ on $L^2(\bar{N})$.

Let $T$ be a kernel of order $r &gt; 0$ on $N$, and let $m$ be an integer greater or equal to $r$.
We are going to prove the estimate

$$
\left\| \pi_T^r f \right\| \leq C \left( \left\| \pi_{P^m}^r f \right\| + \| f \| \right), \quad f \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N}), \tag{3.27}
$$

for all $\xi \in V^*$ with a constant $C$ independent of $\xi$.
For small $|\xi|$ we can apply Theorem (3.19), but in order to handle the case of large $|\xi|$ we need some more lemmas.

(3.28) Lemma.
The measures $\mu_t$ are absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure on $N$.

Proof.
A theorem of Janssen [8] asserts that if the Lévy measure of a continuous semi-group of measures on a Lie group is absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure and unbounded, then the measures in the semi-group are also absolutely continuous.
Since the Lévy measure $v$ of the semi-group $\{\mu_t\}$ is, by hypothesis, even smooth, it remains to show that it is unbounded.
But this is an easy consequence of homogeneity of $P$ as, by (1.26) and (1.28),

$$
\langle v, f \circ \delta_t \rangle = t \langle v, f \rangle
$$

and so

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} |\langle v, f \circ \delta_t \rangle| = \infty
$$

whenever $f \in C_c^\infty(N \setminus \{0\})$ and $\langle v, f \rangle \neq 0$.
Since $P$ is non-zero, it follows from (2.4) that such an $f$ exists.

(3.29) Lemma.
For every $F \in L^1(N)$

$$
\lim_{|\xi| \to \infty} \|\pi_F^r\|_{\mathrm{op}} = 0,
$$

where $\| \cdot \|_{\mathrm{op}}$ denotes the operator norm on $L^2(\bar{N})$.

Proof.
This is well-known and easy so we omit the proof.

We return to the proof of Theorem (2.5).
By Lemma (3.28), $\mu_t(dx) = h_t(x)dx$ for $t &gt; 0$, where $h_t \in L^1(N)$, so the function $F$ on $N$, defined by

$$
F(x) = - \int_0^\infty e^{-t} h_t(x) \, dt,
$$

is integrable with $\| F \|_{L^1} \leq 1$, and, as it is checked readily, satisfies

$$
F^m * P^m = \delta + F_m,
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

where $F_{m} \in L^{1}(N)$, and $F^{m}$ is the $m$-th convolution power of $F$.
Consequently, we have

$$
\| f \| \leq \| \pi_ {F _ {m}} ^ {\sharp} f \| + \| \pi_ {F} ^ {\sharp} f \|
$$

for $f\in C_c^\infty (\bar{N})$ and $\xi \in V^{*}$, whence, by Lemma (3.29),

$$
\| f \| \leq 2 \| \pi_ {P ^ {m}} ^ {\sharp} f \| \tag {3.30}
$$

for $f\in C_c^\infty (\bar{N})$ and $|\xi |\geq M$$, $M$ being sufficiently large.

Now, by the inductive hypothesis and Lemma (3.26),

$$
\| \pi_ {T} ^ {0} f \| \leq C (\| \pi_ {P ^ {m}} ^ {0} f \| + \| f \|)
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$.
By Remark (3.24), there exists another constant $C &gt; 0$ such that (3.25) holds for $|\xi| \leq 2M$.
This together with (3.30) shows that the estimate

$$
\| \pi_ {T} ^ {\sharp} f \| \leq C \| \pi_ {P ^ {m}} ^ {\sharp} f \|, \quad f \in C _ {c} ^ {\infty} (\bar {N})
$$

holds for $M \leq |\xi| \leq 2M$, whence, by (3.25), it holds also for all $|\xi| \geq M$, and thus (3.27) is valid for all $\xi \in V^*$.

The proof can be completed now by decomposing the right-regular representation $\pi$ into a direct integral of $\pi^{\xi}$ over $V^{*}$.
In fact, for $f\in C_c^\infty (N)$ and $\xi \in V^{*}$ let

$$
f ^ {\xi} (x) = \int_ {V} e ^ {- i \langle x, \xi \rangle} f (z x) d z, \quad x \in \bar {N}.
$$

Then $f^{\xi} \in C_c^\infty(\bar{N})$, and for $a \in N$

$$
\| \pi_ {a} f \| ^ {2} = \int_ {V ^ {*}} \| \pi_ {a} ^ {\sharp} f ^ {\sharp} \| ^ {2} d \xi .
$$

Consequently,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\| \pi_ {T} f \| ^ {2} = \int_ {V ^ {*}} \| \pi_ {T} ^ {\sharp} f ^ {\sharp} \| ^ {2} d \xi \\
\leq C \left(\int_ {V ^ {*}} \| \pi_ {P ^ {m}} ^ {\sharp} f ^ {\xi} \| ^ {2} d \xi + \int_ {V ^ {*}} \| f ^ {\xi} \| ^ {2} d \xi\right) \\
= C \left(\| \pi_ {P ^ {m}} f \| ^ {2} + \| f \| ^ {2}\right), \quad f \in C _ {c} ^ {\infty} (N),
\end{array}
$$

which establishes the theorem.
$\square$

(3.31) Remark.
As for the proofs of Theorem (3.19) and Theorem (2.5), cf.
Helffer and Nourrigat [4], Sect.
3; in particular, Proposition 3.1 and the proof of Proposition 3.3.1.

## 4. Smoothness of the semi-group

The space $S^{\infty}(N)$, as defined in Sect.
1 (cf.
(1.22)), is a Frechet space if endowed with the family of norms

$$
f \rightarrow \| X ^ {I} f \|, \quad I \in \mathbb {N} ^ {n}, \tag {4.1}
$$

P.
Głowacki

and it is easily seen that $C_c^\infty(N)$ is dense in $S^\infty(N)$.
The dual space $S^\infty(N)^*$ can be identified with the space of distributions of finite order on $N$, i.e., with those distributions $T$ which satisfy

$$
| \langle T, f \rangle | \leq C \sum_{|I| \leq m} \| X^I f \|, \quad f \in C_c^\infty(N), \tag{4.2}
$$

for some constants $C$, $m$.
We equip $S^\infty(N)$ with the _-weak topology.
Note that $S^\infty(N)$ is then dense in $S^\infty(N)^_$, and therefore any continuous endomorphism of $S^\infty(N)$ can be uniquely extended to a continuous endomorphism of $S^\infty(N)^*$.
An endomorphism of $S^\infty(N)$ will be called smoothing if it extends to a continuous mapping from $S^\infty(N)^*$ to $S^\infty(N)$.
As a direct consequence of the above definitions, we have

(4.3) Lemma.
(i) If $T$ is a compactly supported distribution, then $\pi_T$ is a continuous endomorphism of $S^\infty(N)$.

(ii) If $k$ is a function in $L^1(N)$ such that for every $I, J \in \mathbb{N}^n$ $X^I Y^J k \in L^1(N)$, then $\pi_k$ is smoothing.

(iii) If $T$ is a kernel of order $r &gt; 0$, then $\pi_T$ is a continuous endomorphism of $S^\infty(N)$ (cf.
(i), (ii), and (1.17), (1.18)).

Let $P$ be a symmetric dissipative kernel of order 1, and let $\{\mu_t\}$ be the semi-group of measures generated by $P$.
According to (1.17),

$$
P = P_1 + k_1, \tag{4.4}
$$

where $P_1$ is a truncated kernel of order 1, and $\pi_{k_1}$ is, by (1.18) and Lemma (4.3), smoothing.
Since $P$ is dissipative, so is $P_1$.
$\pi_P$ and $\pi_{P_1}$ are continuous endomorphisms of $S^\infty(N)$ so it is clear that for every $m \in \mathbb{N}$ $\pi_P^m - \pi_{P_1}^m$ is smoothing and, hence, bounded on $L^2(N)$.
We also have

$$
\| (I - \pi_{P_1}) f \|^2 \geq \| f \|^2 \tag{4.5}
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$, which follows directly from (1.27).
Let $\{v_t\}$ be the semi-group of measures generated by $P_1$, and let

$$
v = \int_0^\infty e^{-t} v_t \, dt \tag{4.6}
$$

be the resolvent for $P_1$.
$v$ is a positive bounded measure and satisfies

$$
v * (\delta - P_1) = (\delta - P_1) * v = \delta.
$$

(4.7) Proposition.
$\pi_v$ is a continuous endomorphism of $S^\infty(N)$.

Proof.
Since for every $m \in \mathbb{N}$ $\pi_{P_1}^m - \pi_P^m$ is bounded on $L^2(N)$ and $C_c^\infty(N)$ is dense in $S^\infty(N)$, it follows from Theorem (2.5), Lemma (4.3), and (4.5) that for every multi-index $I$ there exists an $m$ such that

$$
\| X^I f \| \leq C \| (I - \pi_{P_1})^m f \|, \quad f \in S^\infty(N), \tag{4.8}
$$

which shows that it is sufficient to prove that $\pi_v$ maps a dense subspace of $S^\infty(N)$ into $S^\infty(N)$.

Stable semi-groups of measures

In fact, we are going to show that

$$
\pi_ {v} \left(C _ {c} ^ {\infty} (N)\right) \subset S ^ {\infty} (N). \tag {4.9}
$$

Since $P_{1}$ is of compact support, it follows from a theorem of Hulanicki [6] that

$$
\int (1 + | x |) ^ {m} v (d x) &lt;   \infty \tag {4.10}
$$

for every $m \in \mathbb{N}$ (cf.
Hulanicki [6], Theorem 4.1, Lemma 5.2, Lemma 5.3, and (4.6) above).

Therefore, since every left-invariant differential operator can be represented as a linear combination with polynomial coefficients of right-invariant differential operators,

$$
X ^ {I} (f * \vec {v}) \in L ^ {2} (N)
$$

for every $f\in C_c^\infty (N)$ and every $I\in \mathbb{N}^n$, which completes the proof.

(4.11) Corollary.
If $\mu$ denotes the resolvent for $P$ (cf.
(4.6)), then $\pi_{\mu}$ is a continuous endomorphism of $S^{\infty}(N)$.

Proof.
An easy algebraic computation yields

$$
\pi_ {\mu} = \pi_ {v} \left(I + \pi_ {k _ {1}} \pi_ {\mu}\right),
$$

where, let us recall, $\pi_{k_1}$ is smoothing, and $\pi_{\mu}$ is bounded on $L^2 (N)$.
Thus, the corollary follows immediately from Proposition (4.7).

(4.12) Corollary.
$I - \pi_{P}$ is a topological automorphism of $S^{\infty}(N)$.

Proof.
This is a direct consequence of Theorem (2.5) and Corollary (4.11).
$\square$

Now we are going to consider $\pi_P$ as an unbounded closable linear operator in $L^2 (N)$ with $S^{\infty}(N)$ for its domain.
The closure of $\pi_P$ will be denoted by $\tilde{\pi}_P$.

According to a general theorem of Hunt [7], $\pi_P$ is essentially self-adjoint.
In our case, however, since $P$ is a regular dissipative kernel, we have the following stronger result.

(4.13) Proposition.
$\pi_P$ is essentially self-adjoint.
Moreover, for every $m \in \mathbb{N}$, $S^{\infty}(N)$ is a core for $(\tilde{\pi}_P)^m$.

Proof.
As in (4.5), we have

$$
\left\| \left(I - \pi_ {P}\right) f \right\| \geq \| f \|
$$

for $f\in S^{\infty}(N)$.
This and Corollary (4.12) imply our assertion.

(4.14) Corollary.
The space of $C^\infty$ vectors for $\tilde{\pi}_P$ coincides with $S^{\infty}(N)$.

Proof.
This is a direct consequence of Theorem (2.5) and Proposition (4.13).
$\square$

By Lemma (3.28), the measures $\mu_t$ have densities $h_t \in L^1(N)$.
Now Theorem (2.5) yields:

(4.15) Corollary.
For every $t &gt; 0$, $h_t \in S^\infty(N)$.

P.
Głowacki

Proof.
We shall use the following version of the Sobolev inequality.
There exists a constant $C &gt; 0$ and an integer $M$ such that

$$
|f(0)| \leq C \sum_{|I| \leq M} \|X^I f\| \tag{4.16}
$$

for $f \in S^\infty(N)$.

By the spectral theorem, for every $f \in L^2(N)$ and every $t &gt; 0$ $f * h_t$ is a $C^\infty$ vector for $\tilde{\pi}_P$ so, by Corollary (4.14), $f * h_t$ belongs to $S^\infty(N)$.
Consequently, by (4.16), for every $f \in C_c^\infty(N)$ and every $J \in \mathbb{N}^n$

$$
|\langle X^J f, h_t \rangle| = |X^J(f * h_t)(0)| \leq C \sum_{|I| \leq M + |J|} \|X^I(f * h_t)\|,
$$

whence, by Theorem (2.5),

$$
|\langle X^J f, h_t \rangle| \leq C \left( \| \pi_P^m \pi_{h_t} f \| + \|f\| \right)
$$

provided $m$ is sufficiently large.

But, by the spectral theorem again, $\pi_P^m \pi_{h_t}$ is bounded on $L^2(N)$ so the above shows that the weak derivative $X^J h_t$ is in $L^2(N)$ for every $J \in \mathbb{N}^n$, whence, by the Sobolev lemma, $h_t$ is smooth so that, finally, it belongs to $S^\infty(N)$.

# 5. Decay of the densities at infinity

In this section we prove Theorem (2.3).
The main tools to be used are Theorem (2.5) and Proposition (3.8).

Let $\hat{\pi}$ denote the right-regular representation of the direct product of $N$ and $\mathbb{R}$.
Let $\partial_t$ denote the ordinary differentiation on the real line.

According to (4.1), the topology in $S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$ is determined by the family of norms

$$
f \rightarrow \|\partial_t^j X^I f\|, \quad j \in \mathbb{N}, \, I \in \mathbb{N}^n. \tag{5.1}
$$

In what follows we shall regard distributions on $N$ as distributions on $N \times \mathbb{R}$ supported in $N$ so that if $T$ is a distribution like that, then

$$
\hat{\pi}_T = \pi_T \otimes I.
$$

Throughout this section we assume $\{\mu_t\}$ to be a fixed, non-trivial, stable semigroup of symmetric measures on $N$.
We also assume the Lévy measure of $\{\mu_t\}$ to be smooth.
The generating functional $P$ of $\{\mu_t\}$ is then a non-zero symmetric dissipative kernel of order 1 (cf.
Sect.
1).

We are going to introduce a family of Sobolev spaces on $N \times \mathbb{R}$ associated to the operator

$$
\mathcal{J} = \partial_t - \hat{\pi}_P \tag{5.2}
$$

defined on $S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$, and then, by using the technique of a priori estimates, show that $\mathcal{J}$ has a fundamental solution that allows some differentiability.
From this Theorem (2.3) will follow.
But first, two corollaries to Theorem (2.5).

Stable semi-groups of measures
575

(5.3) **Corollary.** For every $j \in \mathbb{N}$ and every $I \in \mathbb{N}^n$ there exist a constant $C &gt; 0$ and an integer $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that

$$
\left\| \partial_t^j X^I f \right\| \leq C \left\| (I + \mathcal{J})^m f \right\|
$$

for all $f \in S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$.

**Proof.** In fact, for $f \in S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$ we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\| (I + \mathcal{J}) f \|^2 &amp;= \| \hat{\pi}_P f \|^2 + \| \partial_t f \|^2 + \| f \|^2 - 2 \operatorname{Re}(\hat{\pi}_P f, f) \tag{5.4} \\
&amp;\geq \| \hat{\pi}_P f \|^2 + \| \partial_t f \|^2 + \| f \|^2,
\end{aligned}
$$

for

$$
\left(\partial_t f, \hat{\pi}_P f\right) = - \left(\hat{\pi}_P f, \partial_t f\right),
$$

and, by (1.27),

$$
\operatorname{Re}(\hat{\pi}_P f, f) = \operatorname{Re} \int_{\mathbb{R}} \left(\pi_P f(\cdot, t), f(\cdot, t)\right) dt \leq 0.
$$

Now, by iterating (5.4), we get our corollary.
$\square$

(5.5) **Corollary.** $I + \mathcal{J}$ is a topological automorphism of $S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$, and so it extends uniquely to a topological automorphism of $S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})^*$.

**Proof.** By (1.27),

$$
f \rightarrow -\mathcal{J} f(0)
$$

is a generating functional of a continuous semi-group of measures.
Since it is not a regular kernel, Corollary (4.12) cannot be applied directly.
Nevertheless, with some modifications using the fact that $\partial_t$ and $\hat{\pi}_P$ commute, the proof of Corollary (4.12) is valid here as well.
$\square$

For $m \in \mathbb{Z}$ let

$$
S^m(N \times \mathbb{R}) = \left\{ f \in S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})^* : (I + \mathcal{J})^m f \in L^2(N \times \mathbb{R}) \right\},
$$

and let

$$
\| f \|_{(m)} = \| (I + \mathcal{J})^m f \|
$$

for $f \in S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$.

(5.6) **Proposition.** Let $m$ be an integer.

(5.7) $S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ is a Banach space under the norm $\| \cdot \|_{(m)}$, and for every $j \in \mathbb{Z}$ $(I + \mathcal{J})^j$ maps $S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ isometrically onto $S^{m-j}(N \times \mathbb{R})$.
In particular, $S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ is isometric to the Hilbert space $S^0(N \times \mathbb{R}) = L^2(N \times \mathbb{R})$.

(5.8) **The inclusions**

$$
S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R}) \subset S^m(N \times \mathbb{R}) \subset S^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})^*
$$

are continuous and dense.

P.
Głowacki

(5.9) $S^{-m}(N \times \mathbb{R})$ is isomorphic to the dual to $S^{m}(N \times \mathbb{R})$ in the following canonical way: If $T \in S^{-m} \subset (S^{\infty})^{*}$, then the linear form

$$
S^{\infty}(N \times \mathbb{R}) \ni f \rightarrow \langle T, f \rangle
$$

extends uniquely to a continuous linear functional $T_{\Phi}$ on $S^{m}$, and the correspondence $T \to T_{\Phi}$ is isomorphic.

(5.10) If $m \geq 0$, then $f \to \sum_{i+j \leq m} \|\partial_t^j \hat{\pi}_{P^i} f\|$

is a norm in $S^{m}(N \times \mathbb{R})$ which is equivalent to $\| \cdot \|_{(m)}$.

(5.11) For every distribution $T$ of finite order there exists an integer $m_0$ such that $T \in S^{m_0}(N \times \mathbb{R})$.

Proof.
The proposition follows from Corollary (5.3) and Corollary (5.5).
We leave the details to the reader.
$\square$

(5.12) Proposition.
If $T$ is a kernel of order $r \in \mathbb{N}$ on $N$, then for every $m \in \mathbb{Z}$ there exists $C_m &gt; 0$ such that

$$
\left\| \hat{\pi}_T f \right\|_{(m)} \leq C_m \| f \|_{(m + r)}
$$

for $f \in S^{\infty}(N \times \mathbb{R})$.

Proof.
Let $m \geq 0$, and let $i, j \in \mathbb{N}$, $i + j \leq m$.
Then

$$
\left\| \partial_t^j \hat{\pi}_{P^j} \hat{\pi}_T f \right\|^2 = \int_0^{\infty} \left\| \pi_{P^{j*T}} \partial_t^i f(\cdot, t) \right\|^2 dt
$$

for $f \in S^{\infty}(N \times \mathbb{R})$.
But $P^j * T$ is a kernel of order $j + r$ so, by Theorem (2.5), we have

$$
\left\| \pi_{P^{j*T}} \partial_t^i f(\cdot, t) \right\| \leq C \left\| \pi_{P^{j + r}} \partial_t^i f(\cdot, t) \right\|
$$

and, consequently,

$$
\left\| \partial_t^i \hat{\pi}_{P^j} \hat{\pi}_T f \right\| \leq C \left\| \hat{\pi}_{P^{j + r}} \partial_t^i f \right\|,
$$

whence, by (5.10), our assertion follows for $m \geq 0$.
By duality (cf.
(5.9)), it is valid for $m &lt; 0$ too.
$\square$

(5.13) Proposition.
If $T$ is a kernel of order $r + 1$ on $N$, where $r \in \mathbb{N}$, and $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$, then the operator $[\hat{\pi}_T, M_\varphi]$ is bounded as a mapping from $S^{m + r}$ to $S^m$ for every $m \in \mathbb{N}$.

Proof.
By Proposition (3.8), Theorem (2.5), and (5.9),

$$
\begin{aligned}
\| [\hat{\pi}_T, M_\varphi] f \|^2 \\
= \int_{\mathbb{R}} \| [\pi_T, M_{\varphi(\cdot, t)}] f(\cdot, t) \|^2 \\
\leq \int_{\mathbb{R}} \left( \| \pi_{P^r} f(\cdot, t) \|^2 + \| f(\cdot, t) \|^2 \right) dt \\
\leq C \| f \|_{(r)}
\end{aligned}
$$

for $f \in S^{\infty}(N \times \mathbb{R})$ and so the proposition is proved in case $m = 0$.

Stable semi-groups of measures

Assume now that the proposition is true for some positive integer $m$.
We are going to prove it for $m + 1$.
In fact, by using the identities

$$
\partial_{t}[\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] = [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] \partial_{t} + [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\partial_{t}\varphi}],
$$

$$
\hat{\pi}_{P}[\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] = [\hat{\pi}_{P*T}, M_{\varphi}] - [\hat{\pi}_{P}, M_{\varphi}] \hat{\pi}_{T},
$$

we have

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\| [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] f \|_{(m + 1)} \\
\leqslant \| [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] \partial_{t} f \|_{(m)} + \| [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\partial_{t}\varphi}] f \|_{(m)} \\
+ \| [\hat{\pi}_{P*T}, M_{\varphi}] f \|_{(m)} + \| [\hat{\pi}_{P}, M_{\varphi}] \hat{\pi}_{T} f \|_{(m)} \\
+ \| [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] f \|_{(m)} \\
\end{array}
$$

for $f \in S^{\infty}(N \times \mathbb{R})$.

Now, $P$ and $T$ are kernels of order 1 and $r + 1$, respectively, so that $P * T$ is a kernel of order $r + 2$, whence, by the inductive hypothesis and Proposition (5.12),

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\| [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] f \|_{(m + 1)} \\
\leqslant C \left( \| \partial_{t} f \|_{(m + r)} + \| f \|_{(m + r)} + \| f \|_{(m + r + 1)} \right. \\
\leqslant C \| f \|_{(m + r + 1)}. \quad \square \\
\end{array}
$$

By duality, two corollaries follow.

(5.14) Corollary.
If $T$ is a kernel of order 1 on $N$, and $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$, then for every $m \in \mathbb{Z}[\hat{\pi}_T, M_\varphi]$ is a bounded mapping from $S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ to itself.

(5.15) Corollary.
If $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$, then for every $m \in \mathbb{Z}$ $M_\varphi$ is a bounded mapping from $S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ to itself.

Let $U$ be an open subset of $N \times \mathbb{R}$.
We say that a distribution $u$ on $N \times \mathbb{R}$ belongs to $S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ locally on $U$, and write $u \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^m(U)$ if $\varphi u \in S^m(N \times \mathbb{R})$ for every $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)$.

(5.16) Lemma.
Let $U$ be an open subset of $N \times \mathbb{R}$.
For each open $V$ with compact closure in $U$ there exists an $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_V &gt; 0$ such that for every $m \in \mathbb{Z}$ and every distribution $u$ of finite order, $u \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^{m+1}(V)$ provided $u \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^m(U)$ and $(\partial_t - \hat{\pi}_{P_\varepsilon})u \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^m(U)$.

Proof.
Let $\varphi_V \in C_c^\infty(U)$ be equal to 1 in a neighbourhood of $V$, and let $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_V &gt; 0$ be so small that

$$
[\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] = [\hat{\pi}_{T}, M_{\varphi}] M_{\varphi_V}
$$

for those distributions $T$ on $N \times \mathbb{R}$ which are supported in $\{(x, t) : |x| + |t| \leq \varepsilon\}$ and all $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(V)$.
Then

$$
(\partial_{t} - \hat{\pi}_{P_{\varepsilon}}) \varphi u = \varphi (\partial_{t} - \hat{\pi}_{P_{\varepsilon}}) u + (\partial_{t} \varphi) u - [\hat{\pi}_{P_{\varepsilon}}, M_{\varphi}] \varphi_V u
$$

for $u \in S^{\infty}(N \times \mathbb{R})$ and $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(V)$, which, by Corollary (5.14), Corollary (5.15), and Lemma (4.3), gives the assertion of the lemma.
$\square$

P.
Głowacki

Let

$$
\langle H, f \rangle = \int_{0}^{\infty} \langle \mu_{t}, f(\cdot, t) \rangle dt \tag{5.17}
$$

for $f \in C_c^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$.
It is not hard to see that $H$ is a homogeneous distribution on $N \times \mathbb{R}$, and the degree of homogeneity of it is $-Q$.

(5.18) Remark.
If $Q &gt; 1$, then $H$ is of finite order.

Proof.
Since $\mu_t(dx) = h_t(x)dx$, where $h_t \in L^2(N)$ (cf.
Corollary (4.15), and $\|h_t\| = C \cdot t^{-Q/2}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
|\langle H, f \rangle| &amp; \\
\leqslant \left(\int_{0}^{1} + \int_{1}^{\infty}\right) \int_{N} h_{t}(x) |f(x, t)| dx dt &amp; \\
\leqslant \|f\|_{L^\infty} + C \int_{1}^{\infty} t^{-Q/2} \|f(\cdot, t)\| dt &amp; \\
\leqslant \|f_{L^\infty} + \frac{C}{Q - 1} \|f\|.
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, our remark follows by the Sobolev inequality (4.16).

(5.19) Corollary.
If $Q &gt; 1$, then $(\partial_t - \hat{\pi}_P)H = \delta$.

Proof.
This can be checked directly (see Folland and Stein [1], Proposition (1.68)).
In fact, if $f \in C_c^\infty(N \times \mathbb{R})$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \hat{\pi}_{P}H, f \rangle &amp;= \langle H, \hat{\pi}_{P}f \rangle \\
&amp;= \int_{0}^{\infty} \langle \mu_{t}, \pi_{P}f(\cdot, t) \rangle dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left\langle \frac{d}{dt} \mu_{t}, f(\cdot, t) \right\rangle dt \\
&amp;= - \int_{0}^{\infty} \langle \mu_{t}, \partial_{t}f(\cdot, t) \rangle dt + \langle \mu_{t}, f(\cdot, t) \rangle|_{0}^{\infty} \\
&amp;= - \langle H, \partial_{t}f \rangle - \langle \delta, f \rangle = \langle \partial_{t}H, f \rangle - \langle \delta, f \rangle.
\end{aligned}
$$

For a left-invariant differential operator

$$
\partial = \sum_{I} c_{I} X^{I} \neq 0
$$

let us denote by

$$
|\partial| = \max_{c_{I} \neq 0} |I|,
$$

and

$$
\|\partial\| = \max_{c_{I} \neq 0} \|I\|,
$$

Stable semi-groups of measures

respectively, the homogeneous and the algebraic order of $\partial$ (cf.
(1.9)).
Non-zero multiples of the identity are considered to be of order 0 in both the senses.
Additionally, we set

$$
| \partial | = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \| \partial \| = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \partial = 0.
$$

(5.20) Lemma.
Let $T$ be a truncated kernel of order $r$ on $N$, and let $\partial$ be a non-zero left-invariant differential operator on $N$.
Then

$$
[ \partial , \hat {\pi} _ {T} ] = \sum_ {j} \hat {\pi} _ {T _ {j}} \partial_ {j},
$$

where $T_{j}$ are truncated kernels of order $r_{j} &lt; r$ with $\operatorname{supp} T_{j} \subset \operatorname{supp} T$, and $\partial_{j}$ are left-invariant differential operators such that

$$
\| \partial_ {j} \| \leq \| \partial \| \leq | \partial | \leq | \partial_ {j} |,
$$

and the sum is finite.

Proof.
For every $1 \leq j \leq n$

$$
X _ {j} - Y _ {j} = \sum_ {d _ {k} &gt; d _ {j}} W _ {k} ^ {(j)} X _ {k}, \tag {5.21}
$$

where $W_{k}^{(j)}$ is a homogeneous polynomial of degree $d_{k} - d_{j}$ such that $[X_{k}, M_{W_{k}^{(j)}}] = 0$ (see Folland and Stein [1], Chap.
1.D).

We have

$$
[ X _ {j}, \hat {\pi} _ {T} ] = \hat {\pi} _ {(X _ {j} - Y _ {j}) T}
$$

so, by (5.21) and the identity $\hat{\pi}_{X_jT} = -\hat{\pi}_T X_j$

$$
[ X _ {j}, \hat {\pi} _ {T} ] = \sum_ {d _ {k} &gt; d _ {j}} \hat {\pi} _ {W _ {k} ^ {(j)} T} X _ {k}.
$$

The proof can be completed by an obvious induction.

(5.22) Theorem.
For every left-invariant differential operator $\partial$ on $N$, $\partial H \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^{1}(U)$, where $U = N \times \mathbb{R} \setminus \{(0,0)\}$.

Proof.
We make an induction on the homogeneous order of $\partial$ with the algebraic order of $\partial$ being fixed.
We are going to show that if the theorem is true for all $\partial'$ satisfying

$$
\| \partial^ {\prime} \| \leq \| \partial \| \leq | \partial | &lt;   | \partial^ {\prime} |, \tag {5.23}
$$

then it is true also for $\partial$.
Since it is trivially true for $\partial = 0$, the proof will be completed by induction.

Let $\partial$ be a left-invariant differential operator on $N$.
According to (5.11), there is an $m_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$ such that $\partial H$ is in $S^{m_0}(N \times \mathbb{R})$.
(We assume $Q &gt; 1$.
If $Q = 1$, our theorem is obviously true since then $\{h_t\}$ is the Cauchy semi-group on the line.) Therefore it is sufficient to check that for every integer $m \leq 0$, $\partial H \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^{m+1}(U)$ provided $\partial H \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^m(U)$.
In view of Lemma (5.16), this is the case if the following holds:

P.
Głowacki

(5.24) For every open $V$ with compact closure in $U$ $\partial H \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^{m}(U)$ implies $(\partial_t - \hat{\pi}_{P_e}) \partial H \in S_{\mathrm{loc}}^{m}(V)$, where $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_V$ is as in Lemma (5.16).

By Lemma (5.20), Corollary (5.19), and (1.17),

$$
\left(\partial_ {t} - \hat {\pi} _ {P _ {e}}\right) \partial H = \partial \delta + \partial \left(H * \check {k} _ {\varepsilon}\right) + \sum_ {j} \pi_ {P _ {j}} \partial_ {j},
$$

where $P_{j}$ are truncated kernels of order $\leq 0$, $\operatorname{supp} P_{j} \subset B_{\varepsilon}$, and (5.23) holds with $\partial' = \partial_{j}$.
Note also that

$$
\partial (H * \check {k} _ {\varepsilon}) (x, t) = H * \partial \check {k} _ {\varepsilon} (x, t) = h _ {t} * \partial \check {k} _ {\varepsilon} (x),
$$

the last convolution being on $N$, so that, by (1.18), $\partial(H * \check{k}_{\varepsilon})$ is bounded.
If $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(V)$, then $\varphi \partial(H * \check{k}_{\varepsilon}) \in S^0(N \times \mathbb{R})$, and

$$
\varphi \left(\hat {\pi} _ {P _ {j}} \partial_ {j} H\right) = \hat {\pi} _ {P _ {j}} \varphi \partial_ {j} H + \left[ M _ {\varphi}, \hat {\pi} _ {P _ {j}} \right] \varphi_ {V} \partial_ {j} H,
$$

where $\varphi_V$ is as in the proof of Lemma (5.16).
But the operators $\hat{\pi}_{P_j}$ and $[M_{\varphi}\hat{\pi}_{P_j}]$ are bounded in $S^m (N\times \mathbb{R})$, so our inductive hypothesis implies

$$
\left(\partial_ {t} - \hat {\pi} _ {P _ {e}}\right) \partial H \in S ^ {m} (N \times \mathbb {R}),
$$

which proves (5.24), and thus completes the proof of the theorem.

(5.25) Corollary.
$H \in C^{\infty}(N \times (0, \infty))$, and satisfies

$$
\left| \partial_ {t} ^ {m} X ^ {I} H (x, t) \right| \leq C _ {I, m} (t + | x |) ^ {- Q - | I | - m} \tag {5.26}
$$

for all $x\in N$, $t &gt; 0$, $m\in \mathbb{N}$, and $I\in \mathbb{N}^n$.

Proof.
Theorem (5.22) implies that for every multi-index $I$, $X^I H \in L_{\mathrm{loc}}^2(U)$ and $\partial_t X^I H \in L_{\mathrm{loc}}^2(U)$ which, by the Sobolev lemma, proves that $X^I H$ is continuous on $U$ and, consequently, $H$ is smooth with respect to $x$ on $U$.
But, by homogeneity of $H$,

$$
H (x, t) = t ^ {- Q} H \left(\delta_ {t ^ {- 1}} x, 1\right)
$$

for $x \in N$, $t &gt; 0$ so that $H \in C^{\infty}(N \times (0, \infty))$.
Let us remark that this part of the corollary is also an immediate consequence of Corollary (4.15) and the equality

$$
H (x, t) = h _ {t} (x) \tag {5.27}
$$

valid for $x\in N$ and $t &gt; 0$.

Since $X^I H \in C(U)$ and since it is homogeneous of degree $-Q - |I|$, we have

$$
\left| X ^ {I} H (x, t) \right| \leq C _ {I} (t + | x |) ^ {- Q - | I |} \tag {5.28}
$$

for $x \in N, t &gt; 0$.
In view of the Sobolev lemma, (5.27), and homogeneity of $H$, (5.26) will follow when we prove that

$$
\sup  _ {t &gt; 0} \| \varphi X ^ {I} \pi_ {P} ^ {m} h _ {t} \| &lt;   \infty \tag {5.29}
$$

for every $I \in \mathbb{N}^n$, every $m \in \mathbb{N}$, and a suitable $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(N \setminus \{0\})$.
This is true, in fact, for every $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(N \setminus \{0\})$.
To see this, choose a $\tilde{\varphi} \in C_c^\infty(N \setminus \{0\})$ such that $\tilde{\varphi} = 1$ in a

Stable semi-groups of measures

neighbourhood of $\operatorname{supp} \varphi$ .
Then for given $I, m$ , and $\varepsilon &gt; 0$ small enough

$$
\| \varphi X ^ {I} \pi_ {P} ^ {m} h _ {t} \| \leq \| \pi_ {T _ {\varepsilon}} \varphi h _ {t} \| + \| [ M _ {\varphi}, \pi_ {T _ {\varepsilon}} ] \tilde {\varphi} h _ {t} \| + \| \varphi (h _ {t} * \tilde {k} _ {\varepsilon}) \|,
$$

where $T = (-1)^{\| I \|} Y^I P^m = T_\varepsilon + k_\varepsilon$ (cf.
(1.17)) is a kernel of order $|I| + m$ .
Since $T_\varepsilon$ is compactly supported,

$$
\| \varphi X ^ {I} \pi_ {P} ^ {m} h _ {t} \| \leq C \sum_ {| I | \leq M} (\| X ^ {I} \varphi h _ {t} \| + \| X ^ {I} \tilde {\varphi} h _ {t} \| + \| k _ {\varepsilon} \|
$$

for some positive integer $M$ , hence (5.29) follows by (5.28) and the fact that $\tilde{\varphi}$ vanishes in a neighbourhood of the origin.

In case when $m = 0$ , (5.26) can be essentially improved, as the next corollary shows.

(5.30) Corollary.

$$
\left| X ^ {I} H (x, t) \right| \leq C _ {I} t (t + | x |) ^ {- Q - 1 - | I |}
$$

for all $x\in N$ $t &gt; 0$ , and $I\in \mathbb{N}^n$

Proof.
In view of homogeneity of $H$ , it is sufficient to know that for every $I \in \mathbb{N}^n$ and every $0 &lt; t \leq 1$

$$
\sup  _ {| x | + t = 1} \left| X ^ {I} H (x, t) \right| \leq C _ {I} t. \tag {5.31}
$$

But, by continuity, $X^I H$ vanishes for $x \neq 0, t \leq 0$ (see the proof of Corollary (5.25)), and the derivative

$$
\partial_ {t} X ^ {I} H (x, t)
$$

is, according to Corollary (5.25), bounded when $|x| + t = 1$ so (5.31) follows.

Proof of Theorem (2.3).
By Corollary (5.25), Corollary (5.30), and (5.27), the function

$$
h (x) = H (x, 1)
$$

has all the required properties.
