# The measure of non-normal sets

Russell Lyons\*\*\*

Mathématique (Bât.
425), Université de Paris-Sud, F-91405 Orsay Cedex, France

Summary.
It is well-known that almost every number in $[0,1[$ is normal in base 2, in the sense of Lebesgue measure.
Kahane and Salem asked whether the same is true with respect to any Borel measure whose Fourier-Stieltjes coefficients vanish at infinity – in other words, whether the set of non-normal numbers is a set of uniqueness in the wide sense.
We show that this is not the case.
In fact, we give “best-possible” conditions on the rate of decay of $\hat{\mu}(n)$ in order that $\mu$-almost every number be normal.
The techniques include, on the one hand, probability measures with respect to which the binary digits in $[0,1[$ are independent only by blocks, rather than individually, and on the other hand, the strong law of large numbers for weakly correlated random variables.

## 1. Introduction

Recall that a number $x \in \Pi = \mathbb{R} / \mathbb{Z}$ is called normal with respect to the base $q \geq 2$ if, when $x = 0$, $x_1 x_2 \ldots$ is written in base $q$, every digit $0 \leq d &lt; q$ appears equally often:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \# \{n \leq N: x_n = d\} = \frac{1}{q};
$$

every pair of digits $d_1, d_2$ appears equally often:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \# \{n \leq N: x_n = d_1, x_{n+1} = d_2\} = \frac{1}{q^2};
$$

\* This work was partially supported by an NSF Graduate Fellowship, NSF Grant MCS-82-01602, and an AMS Research Fellowship.

\*\* Current address: Department of Mathematics, Stanford University, Stanford CA 94305, USA

R.
Lyons

every triplet appears equally often; and so on.
This is evidently equivalent to the following condition: if $I \subset \mathbb{T}$ is any interval of the form $\left[\frac{a}{q^k}, \frac{a + 1}{q^k}\right](a \in \mathbb{Z})$, then

$$
\lim_{N \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n = 1}^{N} \chi_{I} \left(q^{n - 1} x\right) = \lambda I, \tag{1}
$$

where $\chi_{I}$ is the characteristic function of $I$ and $\lambda$ is Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{T}$.
Since any interval can be approximated by subsets and supersets which are finite unions of intervals of the above form, it follows that $x$ is normal in base $q$ if and only if (1) holds for all intervals $I$.

Now a sequence $\{t_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty} \subset \mathbb{T}$ is said to be uniformly distributed if for all arcs $I \subset \mathbb{T}$,

$$
\lim_{N \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n = 1}^{N} \chi_{I} \left(t_{n}\right) = \lambda I.
$$

Thus, $x$ is normal in base $q$ if and only if $\{q^{n - 1}x\}_{n = 1}^{\infty}$ is uniformly distributed.

Let $r_k(x)$ denote the $k$-th digit of $x$ in base $q$.
Then $\{r_k\}_1^\infty$ are independent identically distributed random variables on $(\mathbb{T}, \lambda)$ and it follows from the strong law of large numbers that $\lambda$-almost every $x$ is normal in base $q$ (Borel [4]).
Weyl [21, §7] generalized this result as follows.
We write $n_k \uparrow \infty$ to mean that $\{n_k\}_{k=1}^\infty$ is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers.

Theorem 1. If $n_k \uparrow \infty$, then for $\lambda$-almost all $x$, the sequence $\{n_k x\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ is uniformly distributed.

We shall rephrase this theorem by means of the following definition.

Definition (Kahane [7], Lyons [12, Chap. II, §2]).
A Borel set $E \subset \mathbb{T}$ is called a non-normal set, or $W^{*}$-set, if there exists a sequence $n_k \uparrow \infty$ such that for every $x \in E$, $\{n_k x\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ is not uniformly distributed.
The maximal $W^{*}$-set corresponding to $\{n_k\}$ is the set

$$
W^{*} \left(\left\{n_{k}\right\}\right) = \left\{x: \left\{n_{k} x\right\} \text{ is not uniformly distributed} \right\}.
$$

It is easily shown that $W^{*}(\{n_{k}\})$ is a Borel set.
We note that our concept of non-normal set is not the same as the complement of a set usually called "normal" (Rauzy [17]); there, the set is always maximal and the sequence $\{n_k\}$ consists of arbitrary real numbers.

With this definition, Theorem 1 becomes

Theorem 1'.
If $E$ is any $W^{*}$-set, then $\lambda E = 0$.

The proof, to be given later, depends on the following fundamental observation.
If $\delta(x)$ denotes the unit mass at $x$, then $\{t_n\}$ is uniformly distributed if and only if $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \delta(t_n) \to \lambda$ weak\*, where we consider the set of complex Borel measures $M(\mathbb{T})$ as the Banach-space dual of the set of continuous functions $C(\mathbb{T})$.
This observation yields

The measure of non-normal sets

Weyl's criterion.
A sequence $\{t_k\} \subset \Pi$ is uniformly distributed if and only if for every non-zero integer $m$,

$$
\lim_{K \to \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} e(m t_k) = 0.
$$

We have written $e(x)$ for $e^{2\pi ix}$.
See [20], [10, pp. 1-3, 7-8] or [22, p. 142] for a complete proof.

Of course, from Theorem 1', it follows that every measure $v \ll \lambda$ annihilates all $W^*$-sets.
By the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma, $v$ belongs to

$$
M_0(\Pi) = \{\mu \in M(\Pi): \lim_{|n| \to \infty} \hat{\mu}(n) = 0\},
$$

where $\hat{\mu}(n) = \int_{\Pi} e(-nt) d\mu(t)$.
Indeed, it is not difficult to see that this condition is necessary in order that a measure annihilate all $W^*$-sets:

**Theorem 2.** If $\mu E = 0$ for all $W^*$-sets $E$, then $\mu \in M_0(\Pi)$.

**Proof.** We show the contrapositive.
Suppose that $\mu \notin M_0(\Pi)$.
Then there exists a sequence $\{n_k\}$ such that either $n_k \uparrow \infty$ or $-n_k \uparrow \infty$ and $\hat{\mu}(n_k)$ converges to a non-zero limit, $\alpha$.
If $t \notin E \equiv W^*(\{|n_k|\})$, then by Weyl's criterion,

$$
\frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} e(-n_k t) \to 0.
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &lt; |\alpha| &amp;= \left| \lim_{K \to \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{1}^{K} \hat{\mu}(n_k) \right| = \left| \lim_{K \to \infty} \int_{\Pi} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{1}^{K} e(-n_k t) d\mu(t) \right| \\
&amp;\leq \lim_{E} \int_{E} 1 \, d|\mu|(t) + \left| \lim_{E \to \infty} \int_{\Pi \setminus E} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{1}^{K} e(-n_k t) d\mu(t) \right| \\
&amp;= |\mu|(E),
\end{aligned}
$$

whence $|\mu|(E) \neq 0$.
For some subset $F \subset E$, $\mu F \neq 0$; $F$ is still a $W^*$-set.

We shall refer to $M_0(\Pi)$ as the class of Rajchman measures.
Kahane and Salem [8, 7] asked if all Rajchman measures annihilate all $W^*$-sets, i.e., whether the converse of Theorem 2 is true.
R.C.
Baker [1, 2] interpreted this as a conjecture and attempted to prove it.
However, as announced in [13, 14], it is false.
Note even $W^*(\{2^{k-1}\})$, the set of non-normal numbers in base 2, is annihilated by all Rajchman measures.
(In other words, $W^*(\{2^{k-1}\})$ is not a set of uniqueness in the wide sense.)

We shall prove the following three theorems, the first two of which are slight extensions of Baker's results [2].

**Theorem 3.** Suppose that $\Phi(n)$ is a non-increasing function on the non-negative integers such that

$$
\sum_{1}^{\infty} \frac{\Phi(n)}{n} &lt; \infty.
$$

R.
Lyons

Then for any positive measure $\mu$ with

$$
|\hat{\mu}(n)| \leq \Phi(|n|),
$$

the $\mu$-measure of every $W^*$-set is 0.

A weaker hypothesis on $\Phi$ is sufficient if we restrict attention to those $W^*$-sets corresponding to quickly growing sequences $\{n_k\}$.
A sequence $\{n_k\}$ is called lacunary if $\inf_{k+1} \frac{n_{k+1}}{n_k} &gt; 1$.
We shall call a $W^*$-set corresponding to such a sequence a lacunary $W^*$-set.
Thus, the set of non-normal numbers in base $q$ is a lacunary $W^*$-set.

**Theorem 4.** Suppose that $\Phi(n)$ is a non-increasing function on the non-negative integers such that

$$
\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{\Phi(n)}{n \log n} &lt; \infty. \tag{2}
$$

Then for any positive measure $\mu$ with $|\hat{\mu}(n)| \leq \Phi(|n|)$, the $\mu$-measure of every lacunary $W^*$-set is 0.

This theorem is the best possible of its form, at least subject to a mild regularity condition which was overlooked in [12–14]:

**Theorem 5.** Suppose that $\Phi(n)$ is a non-increasing function on the non-negative integers such that

$$
\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{\Phi(n)}{n \log n} = \infty \tag{3}
$$

and such that for all sufficiently large $n$,

$$
\Phi(n) \leq n^{1/4} \Phi(n^2). \tag{4}
$$

Then there exists a positive measure $\mu$ concentrated on the set of non-normal numbers base 2 with $|\hat{\mu}(n)| \leq \Phi(|n|)$.

It will be easily seen from the proof that instead of assuming that $\Phi(n)$ is decreasing, it is sufficient to assume only that there exists a constant $C$ such that if $n \leq m \leq n^2$, then $0 &lt; \Phi(m) \leq C \Phi(n)$.
However, it is not clear whether the regularity condition (4) can be lifted.

One may, of course, investigate the size of $W^*$-sets in senses other than their measure.
Erdős and Taylor [5] discuss the cardinality and Hausdorff dimension of $W^*$-sets.
Baire category is examined by Šalát [19] and Lyons [12, Chap. IV, §6].

The notation $W^*$ comes from the closely related concept of $W$-sets [12, 13, 15].
Related questions and other properties of $W^*$-sets are discussed by Lyons [12, 16].

## 2. Rajchman measures which annihilate $W^*$-sets

In order to show that for some measure $\mu \geq 0$, all $W^*$-sets have $\mu$-measure 0, it is necessary and sufficient, by Weyl’s criterion, to establish that for all $m \neq 0$

The measure of non-normal sets

and all $n_k \uparrow \infty$,

$$
\lim_{K \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} e(m n_k x) = 0 \quad \text{a.e.} \ [\mu]. \tag{5}
$$

Actually, since $\{n_k\}$ is arbitrary, it is enough to establish (5) for $m = 1$.
We may also assume that $\mu$ is a probability measure; thus we wish to show that the strong law of large numbers (5) holds for the random variables $\{e(n_k x)\}_k$.
In Weyl's case, $\mu = \lambda$, these variables are orthogonal:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
E \left[ e \left(n _ {k} x\right) \overline{e \left(n _ {l} x\right)} \right] = \int_{\mathbb{R}} e \left(n _ {k} x\right) e \left(- n _ {l} x\right) d \lambda (x) \\
= \hat{\lambda} (- n _ {k} + n _ {l}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l}
1 &amp; \text{if } k = l, \\
0 &amp; \text{if } k \neq l.
\end{array} \right.
\end{array}
$$

Hence the easily-established strong law of large numbers for orthogonal (or uncorrelated) bounded random variables (cf.
[18, Theorem 2.7.1, p. 59]) gives the desired conclusion.

This type of argument in fact applies to a much larger class of measures and will give Theorems 3 and 4. We shall use the following form of the strong law of large numbers for weakly correlated bounded random variables (see Loève [11, pp. 153-4]).

**Theorem 6.** Suppose that $|X_n| \leq 1$ a.s.
and that

$$
\sum_{N = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{N^3} \sum_{1 \leq n, m \leq N} |E[X_n \bar{X}_m]| &lt; \infty. \tag{6}
$$

Then the strong law of large numbers holds:

$$
\lim_{N \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n = 1}^{N} X _ {n} = 0 \quad \text{a.s.} \tag{7}
$$

In particular, (6) holds if

$$
\forall n, m \quad |E[X_n \bar{X}_m]| \leq \Phi(|n - m|), \tag{8a}
$$

where $\Phi$ is a non-negative function satisfying

$$
\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\Phi(n)}{n} &lt; \infty. \tag{8b}
$$

In the sequel, we shall also make use of the well-known principle of Cauchy condensation, stated here for the reader's convenience.

**The principle of Cauchy condensation.** Suppose that $\{a_{n}\}_{n = 1}^{\infty}$ is a sequence of positive numbers satisfying one of the following regularity conditions for some constant $C$:

a) $n \leq m \leq 2n \Rightarrow a_m \leq C a_n$

or

b) $n \leq m \leq 2n \Rightarrow a_m \geq C a_n$.

R.
Lyons

Let $\{n_k\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ be a sequence of positive integers such that $1 &lt; q \leq n_{k+1} / n_k \leq Q &lt; \infty$.
Then

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{a_n}{n} &lt; \infty \Leftrightarrow \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_{n_k} &lt; \infty.
$$

We proceed with the applications of Theorem 6.

Proof of Theorem 3. Let $\mu$ be as indicated and let $n_k \uparrow \infty$.
Without loss of generality, we may assume that $\mu$ is a probability measure.
Since

$$
|E[\overline{e(n_k x)} e(n_l x)]| = |\hat{\mu}(n_k - n_l)| \leq \Phi(|n_k - n_l|) \leq \Phi(|k - l|),
$$

Theorem 6 shows that the strong law of large numbers (5) holds for $\{e(n_k x)\}_k$.
$\square$

Let $\{|\hat{\mu}|^*(n)\}_{n=0}^{\infty}$ denote the non-increasing rearrangement of $\{|\hat{\mu}(n)|\}_{n=0}^{\infty}$.
By using (6) instead of (8), we may show the following stronger theorem.

Theorem 7. If $\mu$ is a positive measure such that

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{|\hat{\mu}|^*(n)}{n} &lt; \infty,
$$

then the $\mu$-measure of every $W^*$-set is 0.

Proof.
Let $n_k \uparrow \infty$ and let $r_N(m)$ denote the number of pairs $(k, l) \in [1, N]^2$ such that $n_k - n_l = m$.
Then $\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_N(m) = N^2$ and $r_N(m) \leq N$ since for each $k$, there can be at most one $l$ such that $n_k - n_l = m$.

Assuming again that $\mu$ is a probability measure, we have

$$
\sum_{1 \leq k, l \leq N} |E[\overline{e(n_k x)} e(n_l x)]| = \sum_{1 \leq k, l \leq N} |\hat{\mu}(n_k - n_l)| = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_N(m) |\hat{\mu}(m)|.
$$

Now the sum of $N^2$ numbers chosen from $\{|\hat{\mu}(m)|\}_{-\infty}^{\infty}$, where each $m$ may be chosen at most $N$ times, is greatest when the $N$ largest numbers are each chosen $N$ times.
Hence the last quantity above is

$$
\leq \sum_{n=-N}^{N} N \cdot |\hat{\mu}|^*(|n|) \leq 2N \sum_{n=0}^{N} |\hat{\mu}|^*(n).
$$

It follows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{N=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{N^3} \sum_{1 \leq k, l \leq N} |E[\overline{e(n_k x)} e(n_l x)]| &amp;\leq 2 \sum_{N=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N} |\hat{\mu}|^*(n) \\
&amp;= 2 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} |\hat{\mu}|^*(n) \sum_{N &gt; n} \frac{1}{N^2} \leq 4 |\hat{\mu}|^*(0) + 2 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{|\hat{\mu}|^*(n)}{n}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Our hypothesis thus guarantees the applicability of Theorem 6. $\square$

The measure of non-normal sets

We stated Theorem 3 in the weaker form so that it is parallel to Theorem 4. The latter theorem, which we now prove, perhaps does not extend in the same way.

Proof of Theorem 4. Extend $\Phi$ to be a non-increasing function on $[0, \infty[$.
Let $\{n_k\}$ be a lacunary sequence, $q = \inf_{k} n_{k+1} / n_k$, and define $\Psi(n) = \Phi(q^n - 1)$.
Then for $k &gt; l$, we have

$$
n_k - n_l \geq q^{k-l} n_l - n_l \geq q^{k-l} - 1,
$$

whence

$$
|\hat{\mu}(n_k - n_l)| \leq \Phi(n_k - n_l) \leq \Psi(k-l).
$$

By the principle of Cauchy condensation and (2), $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{\Psi(n)}{n} &lt; \infty$, whence Theorem 6 again gives the desired conclusion.

It would be interesting to know if Theorems 3 and 4 hold for complex measures $\mu$.
It would also be very interesting to obtain a characterization of the measures which annihilate all $W^*$-sets.
Such a characterization, however, cannot be given in simple terms of the rate of decay of $\hat{\mu}$, even for positive measures, since for any sequence $\Phi(n) \to 0$, there exists a positive function $f \in L^{1}(\lambda)$ such that $\hat{f}(n) &gt; \Phi(n)$ [9, pp. 22, 26].
Theorems $1'$ and 5 show that such a characterization is therefore impossible.
Nevertheless, if $\mu$ is a positive measure with $|\hat{\mu}| \leq |\hat{f}|$ and $f \in L^{p}(\lambda)$ for some $p &gt; 1$, then $\mu$ annihilates all $W^*$-sets.
This follows from the Hausdorff-Young theorem and the following proposition (Baker [1]).

Proposition 8. If $\mu$ is a positive measure and $\hat{\mu} \in l^q$ for some $q &lt; \infty$, then every $W^*$-set is $\mu$-null.

Proof.
Without loss of generality, we may assume that $q &gt; 1$.
If $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$, then Hölder's inequality gives

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{|\hat{\mu}|^*(n)}{n} \leq \left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |\hat{\mu}|^*(n)^q\right)^{\frac{1}{q}} \left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^p}\right)^{\frac{1}{p}} &lt; \infty.
$$

Hence the conclusion follows from Theorem 7.

# 3. Rajchman measures concentrated on $W^*$-sets

Our proof of Theorem 5 turns out to be very similar to the proof that the hypothesis (8b) is the best of its kind when $\Phi(n)$ is assumed to be non-increasing.
For in this case, if (8b) fails, then the principle of Cauchy condensation yields $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \Phi(2^i) = \infty$.
Now without loss of generality, $\Phi(n) \leq 1$.
Consider random variables $X_k = Z_i$ for $2^{i-1} &lt; k \leq 2^i$, where $Z_i$ are independent with $P(Z_i = 0) = 1 - \Phi(2^i)$ and $P(Z_i = 1) = \Phi(2^i)$.
The hypothesis (8a) is easily verified, yet

R.
Lyons

by the Borel-Cantelli lemma, $Z_{i} = 1$ infinitely often almost surely.
Each time $Z_{i} = 1$, we have

$$
\frac {1}{2 ^ {i}} \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {2 ^ {i}} X _ {n} \geq \frac {1}{2 ^ {i}} \sum_ {2 ^ {i} - 1 + 1} ^ {2 ^ {i}} X _ {n} = \frac {1}{2} Z _ {i} = \frac {1}{2},
$$

whence (7) fails.
In order to transfer this type of behavior to the circle, we use the following well-known construction, originally due to Borel [4].

Let $X_{k}$ ($k \geq 1$) be random variables on a probability space $(\Omega, P)$ with values in $\{0, 1\}$ such that

$$
P \left(X _ {k} = 1, \text{ all } k &gt; n\right) = 0 \quad \text{for all } n \geq 1. \tag {9}
$$

The random variable $X = \sum_{k \geq 1} X_k 2^{-k}$ takes values in $[0,1[$ almost surely.
Let $\mu$ be the distribution of $X$ and identify $[0,1[$ with $\Pi$.
For $x \in \Pi$, let $r_k(x)$ denote the $k$-th digit of $x$ in base 2, where we take the representation of $x$ containing infinitely many 0's.
Then $\{r_k\}$ is a sequence of random variables on $(\Pi, \mu)$ with the same joint distribution as that of $\{X_k\}$.
Indeed, for any $n \geq 1$ and any $a_k = 0, 1$ ($k \leq n$), we have that

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mu (\{x: r _ {k} (x) = a _ {k}, k \leq n \}) = \mu ([ \sum_ {k \leq n} a _ {k} 2 ^ {- k}, \sum_ {k \leq n} a _ {k} 2 ^ {- k} + 2 ^ {- n} [) \\
= P \left(\sum_ {k \geq 1} X _ {k} 2 ^ {- k} \in \left[ \sum_ {k \leq n} a _ {k} 2 ^ {- k}, \sum_ {k \leq n} a _ {k} 2 ^ {- k} + 2 ^ {- n} \right]\right) \\
= P \left(X _ {k} = a _ {k}, k \leq n\right) \\
\end{array}
$$

by (9).

Let $\{K_i\}_{i=0}^{\infty}$ be a strictly increasing sequence of integers with $K_0 = 0$.
Write

$$
B _ {i} = \left] K _ {i - 1}, K _ {i} \right] \quad (i \geq 1), \tag {10}
$$

$$
Y _ {i} = \sum_ {k \in B _ {i}} X _ {k} 2 ^ {- k}.
$$

Suppose that $\{Y_i\}_1^\infty$ are independent with distributions $\mu_i$.
Then $\sum_{i\geq 1}Y_i = X$, the distribution of $\sum_{i\leq n}Y_{i}$ is the convolution $\sum_{i = 1}^{n}\mu_{i}$, and hence [3, pp. 284, 288] $\mu$ is the weak\* limit of $\sum_{i = 1}^{n}\mu_{i}$ as $n\to \infty$, which we write simply as

$$
\mu = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {\infty} \mu_ {i}.
$$

This allows the calculation $\hat{\mu}(k) = \prod_{i=1}^{\infty} \hat{\mu}_i(k)$.
Note that (9) is satisfied if and only if for $n \geq 1$, $\prod_{i \geq n} P(Y_i = \sum_{k \in B_i} 2^{-k}) = 0$, which is to say

$$
\sum_ {i \geq 1} [ 1 - \mu_ {i} (\left\{\sum_ {k \in B _ {i}} 2 ^ {- k} \right\}) ] = \infty . \tag {11}
$$

The measure satisfying Theorem 5 will be a measure $\mu$ of the above form.
It is necessary to introduce the blocks $B_{i}$ for the following reason.
If the variables

The measure of non-normal sets

$X_{k}$ themselves are independent (equivalently, $K_{i} = i$), then

$$
\mu = \sum_{k = 1}^{\infty} \left[ p_{k} \delta(0) + q_{k} \delta(2^{-k}) \right],
$$

where $p_k + q_k = 1$ and $p_k = P(X_k = 0)$.

For $\mu$ to be a Rajchman measure, it is necessary and sufficient that $p_k \to \frac{1}{2}$ ([6, pp. 183, 187-188]).
But if $p_k \to \frac{1}{2}$, it follows from the strong law of large numbers that $\mu$-almost all numbers are normal in base 2. Hence, such a $\mu$ cannot satisfy Theorem 5.

The $\mu$ which is in fact required has the following form.

**Lemma 9.** Let $\{K_i\}_{i=0}^{\infty}$ be a strictly increasing sequence of integers with $K_0 = 0$.
Let $\{\varepsilon_i\}_1^{\infty}$ be any sequence of real numbers such that

$$
0 \leq \varepsilon_{i} \leq 1, \quad \sum_{1}^{\infty} \varepsilon_{i} = \infty.
$$

With $B_{i}$ as in (10), let

$$
\mu = \sum_{i = 1}^{\infty} \left\{ \varepsilon_{i} \delta(0) + (1 - \varepsilon_{i}) \sum_{k \in B_{i}} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \delta(0) + \frac{1}{2} \delta(2^{-k}) \right] \right\}. \tag{12}
$$

Then for $2^{K_{i-1}-1} \leq n &lt; 2^{K_{i}-1}$

$$
\left| \hat{\mu}(n) \right| \leq \varepsilon_{i} \varepsilon_{i-1} + \varepsilon_{i} + \varepsilon_{i-1} + 2^{-(K_{i-1} - K_{i-2})} \tag{13}
$$

and $\mu$ is concentrated on the set of $x$ for which

$$
\lim_{K \to \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} \chi_{[0, \frac{1}{2}]}(2^{k-1} x) \geq 1 - \frac{1}{2} Q^{-1}, \tag{14}
$$

where $Q = \lim \inf K_{i+1} / K_i$.

Note that as soon as $\varepsilon_{i} \to 0$ and $\{K_i\}$ is lacunary, (13) and (14) imply that $\mu$ is a Rajchman measure concentrated on the set of non-normal numbers base 2. Observe that if $\{K_i\}$ is hyperlacunary, i.e., $K_{i+1} / K_i \to \infty$, then $\mu$ is even concentrated on the set of $x$ such that

$$
\varlimsup_{K \to \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} \chi_{[0, \frac{1}{2}]}(2^{k-1} x) = 1.
$$

Before proving Lemma 9, we show how Theorem 5 follows.

**Proof of Theorem 5.** It is clearly sufficient to assume that $0 &lt; \Phi(n) \leq 1$ and to find $\mu$ such that $\hat{\mu}(n) = O(\Phi(|n|))$.
Set $K_i = 2^i$.
From two uses of the principle of Cauchy condensation applied to (3), we conclude that $\Sigma \Phi(2^{K_i}) = \infty$.
Put $\varepsilon_i = \Phi(2^{K_{i+1}})$ in (12).
Then (13) implies that $\hat{\mu}(n) = O(\Phi(|n|))$ provided that there exists a constant $C$ such that

$$
2^{-(K_{i-1} - K_{i-2})} \leq C \Phi(2^{K_i}). \tag{15}
$$

R.
Lyons

By (4), there is an $i_0$ such that for all $i \geq i_0$, $\Phi(2^{K_i}) \leq 2^{2^{i-2}} \Phi(2^{K_{i+1}})$.
Let $C$ be such that (15) holds for all $i \leq i_0$.
We shall prove by induction that (15) holds for all $i$.
Indeed, if (15) holds for some $i \geq i_0$, then

$$
\begin{array}{l}
C \Phi \left(2 ^ {K _ {i + 1}}\right) \geq C \cdot 2 ^ {- 2 ^ {i - 2}} \Phi \left(2 ^ {K _ {i}}\right) \geq 2 ^ {- 2 ^ {i - 2}} \cdot 2 ^ {- \left(K _ {i - 1} - K _ {i - 2}\right)} \\
= 2 ^ {- 2 ^ {i - 1}} = 2 ^ {- \left(K _ {i} - K _ {i - 1}\right)},
\end{array}
$$

whence (15) holds for $i + 1$.

Proof of Lemma 9. As before, denote the $k$-th binary digit of $x$ by $r_k(x)$.
Let

$$
\bar {r} _ {k} (x) = 1 - r _ {k} (x) = \chi_ {\{0, \frac {1}{2} \}} \left(2 ^ {k - 1} x\right),
$$

$$
R _ {i} (x) = \sum_ {k \in B _ {i}} r _ {k} (x) / \left(K _ {i} - K _ {i - 1}\right),
$$

and

$$
\bar {R} _ {i} (x) = 1 - R _ {i} (x).
$$

The condition (11) is clearly satisfied, so that $R_{i}$ are independent random variables with

$$
\mu \{x: R _ {i} (x) = 0 \} = \varepsilon_ {i} + (1 - \varepsilon_ {i}) 2 ^ {- (K _ {i} - K _ {i - 1})}.
$$

It follows from the Borel-Cantelli lemma that

$$
\bar {R} _ {i} (x) = 1 \quad \text {infinitely often a.s.} \tag {16}
$$

Therefore

$$
\limsup_{i\to \infty}\frac{1}{K_{i} - K_{i - 1}}\sum_{k = 1}^{K_{i}}\bar{r}_{k}(x)\geq 1\quad \text{a.s.},
$$

whence

$$
\limsup_{i\to \infty}\frac{1}{K_{i}}\sum_{k = 1}^{K_{i}}\bar{r}_{k}(x)\geq 1 - Q^{-1}\quad \text{a.s.}
$$

If $Q &gt; 2$, then we see already that $\mu$ is concentrated on $W^{*}(\{2^{k - 1}\})$.
The argument which follows is necessary only to prove the better estimate (14), which yields such a result for all $Q &gt; 1$.

We claim that

$$
\liminf_{K \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} \bar{r}_{k}(x) \geq \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{a.s.} \tag {17}
$$

For let $Z_{i}$ ($i \geq 1$), $X_{k}^{\prime}$ ($k \geq 1$) be a collection of independent random variables on a probability space $(\Omega, P)$ taking values in $\{0, 1\}$ such that $P(Z_{i} = 0) = \varepsilon_{i}$ and $P(X_{k}^{\prime} = 0) = \frac{1}{2}$.
If $X_{k} \equiv Z_{i} X_{k}^{\prime}$ ($k \in B_{i}$), then $\{X_{k}\}_{k \geq 1}$ has the same joint distribution as $\{r_{k}\}_{k \geq 1}$ has on $(\Pi, \mu)$, whence (17) is equivalent to

$$
\liminf_{K \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} \chi_{\{X_{k} = 0\}} \geq \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{a.s.} [P]. \tag {18}
$$

The measure of non-normal sets

But $\chi_{\{X_k = 0\}} \geq \chi_{\{X_k = 0\}}$ and by the strong law of large numbers,

$$
\lim_{K \to \infty} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} \chi_{\{X_k = 0\}} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{a.s.} \ [P].
$$

Therefore (18), and hence (17), follow.

Consider

$$
\frac{1}{K_i} \sum_{k = 1}^{K_i} \bar{r}_k(x) = \frac{K_{i-1}}{K_i} \left( \frac{1}{K_{i-1}} \sum_{k = 1}^{K_{i-1}} \bar{r}_k(x) \right) + \frac{K_i - K_{i-1}}{K_i} \bar{R}_i(x).
$$

Let $x$ be any point for which (16) and (17) hold.
Given $\delta &gt; 0$, take $i$ so large that

$$
\frac{1}{K_{i-1}} \sum_{k = 1}^{K_{i-1}} \bar{r}_k(x) \geq \frac{1}{2} - \delta
$$

and $\bar{R}_i(x) = 1$.
Then

$$
\frac{1}{K_i} \sum_{k = 1}^{K_i} \bar{r}_k(x) \geq \frac{K_{i-1}}{K_i} \left( \frac{1}{2} - \delta \right) + \left(1 - \frac{K_{i-1}}{K_i} \right) = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{2} + \delta \right) \frac{K_{i-1}}{K_i}.
$$

Letting $i \to \infty$ and then $\delta \to 0$, we conclude that (14) holds for this $x$.
Since (16) and (17) hold almost surely, so does (14).

We now demonstrate (13).
Write $\mu_i = \varepsilon_i \delta(0) + (1 - \varepsilon_i) v_i$, where

$$
v_i = \sum_{k \in B_i} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \delta(0) + \frac{1}{2} \delta(2^{-k}) \right].
$$

Then $\mu_i, \nu_i$ are probability measures and $\mu = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \mu_i$.
Fix $n \geq 1$ and let $i$ be such that $2^{K_{i-1}-1} \leq n &lt; 2^{K_i-1}$.
Since $|\hat{\mu}_j|, |\hat{\nu}_j| \leq 1$ for all $j$, we have

$$
|\hat{\mu}(n)| \leq |\hat{\mu}_{i-1}(n) \hat{\mu}_i(n)| \leq \varepsilon_i \varepsilon_{i-1} + \varepsilon_i + \varepsilon_{i-1} + |\hat{\nu}_{i-1}(n) \hat{\nu}_i(n)|. \tag{19}
$$

Now

$$
\hat{\nu}_{i-1}(n) \hat{\nu}_i(n) = \prod_{K_{i-2} &lt; k \leq K_i} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} e(-n 2^{-k}) \right).
$$

If we multiply this product by $(1 - e(-n2^{-K_i}))$ and use the fact that $(1 + e(u))(1 - e(u)) = 1 - e(2u)$ repeatedly, we find that the product telescopes:

$$
\hat{\nu}_{i-1}(n) \hat{\nu}_i(n) = 2^{-(K_i - K_{i-2})} \frac{1 - e(-n 2^{-K_{i-2}})}{1 - e(-n 2^{-K_i})},
$$

whence

$$
|\hat{\nu}_{i-1}(n) \hat{\nu}_i(n)| \leq 2^{-(K_i - K_{i-2})} / \sin \pi n 2^{-K_i}. \tag{20}
$$

Since $\pi 2^{K_{i-1} - K_i - 1} \leq \pi n 2^{-K_i} &lt; \frac{\pi}{2}$, we have

$$
\sin \pi n 2^{-K_i} \geq \sin \pi 2^{K_{i-1} - K_i - 1} \geq 2^{K_{i-1} - K_i}. \tag{21}
$$

Combining (19), (20) and (21), we obtain (13).
$\square$

R.
Lyons

dissertation.
